probe_reagents.observation: EPR signal (g=2.00525, aN=15.21 G, aH=4.88 G) consistent with carbon-radical spin adduct; PBN-carbon adduct 95 confirmed by HRMS

probe_reagents.name: 1-methylcyclohex-1-ene (46a)
probe_reagents.observation: Sterically hindered substrate allowed isolation of beta-iodo intermediate 97 (28%) along with cyclopropane (32%); 97 could be converted to cyclopropane, confirming iodo-intermediate

probe_reagents.name: ferrocene (Cp2Fe)
probe_reagents.observation: CV: Fc oxidation at E1/2 = +0.57 V vs Ag/Ag+; addition of 1b alone gave no change, but with NaOMe base anodic current increased dramatically and cathodic peak disappeared, indicating catalytic oxidation of deprotonated 1b by Cp2Fe+

probe_reagents.name: no-current control
probe_reagents.observation: No cyclopropanation product without electricity, confirming electrochemical requirement

probe_reagents.name: absence-of-alkene control
probe_reagents.observation: Dimer 93 and oxidative dehydrogenation product 94 of 1b formed (73% combined, 93:94 = 0.19:1), consistent with malonyl radical intermediate

10.1002_anie.202425634.notes: DFT (delta G‡ = 7.3 kcal/mol) supports rapid conversion of beta-iodo intermediate 96 to cyclopropane, explaining why 96 is hard to isolate except with hindered alkenes. Regioselectivity at terminal alkenes (and internal in 89a) is rationalized by formation of the more stable secondary/tertiary C-radical adduct B. Base is generated in situ at the cathode (OH-, pH 8-9), avoiding need for added base.

10.1002_anie.202207059_sup_1.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical iodo-carbocyclization of dimethyl 2-allylmalonate with ethynylbenzene to give an iodo-substituted cyclopentene bearing a phenyl group, mediated by an electrogenerated iodine species in a divided cell.
10.1002_anie.202207059_sup_1.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of iodide (from NaI or NIS) to an electrophilic/radical iodine species that initiates radical addition to the alkyne; the cathode reduces protons/HOAc, not the iodomalonate intermediate (as shown by the divided-cell control with NIS giving no product).
10.1002_anie.202207059_sup_1.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes iodide to I• (or I+), which adds to phenylacetylene to generate a vinyl radical; intramolecular/intermolecular radical cyclization with the allylmalonate forms a carbocyclic C-radical that abstracts/couples with iodine to give the iodo-cyclopentene; cathode reduces HOAc/protons to H2.

key_intermediates.name: vinyl radical from iodine-radical addition to phenylacetylene
cyclopentene product bearing CH2I and Ph

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Added as control to test radical intermediacy in the divided-cell reaction.

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: Added as control to test radical intermediacy.

probe_reagents.name: NIS (as putative iodomalonate precursor in divided cell)
probe_reagents.observation: In divided cell with NIS instead of NaI, no target product was detected, indicating reduction of an iodomalonate intermediate at the cathode is NOT on the productive pathway.

10.1002_anie.202207059_sup_1.notes: Divided-cell experiment with NIS gave no product (n.d.), while the analogous divided-cell run with NaI gave 14% yield, supporting an anodic iodide-oxidation pathway rather than cathodic reduction of an iodomalonate. Page also documents the H-type divided cell apparatus (Figure S1).

10.1002_anie.201510418.rxn_class_summary: Electrocatalytic intramolecular olefin hydroamidation of N-aryl amides via ferrocene-mediated anodic generation of amidyl radicals
10.1002_anie.201510418.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of ferrocene (Fc) to ferrocenium (Fc+), which then oxidizes the deprotonated anilide (amide conjugate base) by outer-sphere SET to give an amidyl radical; cathodic reduction of MeOH generates H2 and MeO- which deprotonates the N-H amide
10.1002_anie.201510418.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes Fc to Fc+ while cathode reduces MeOH to H2 and MeO-; MeO- deprotonates the N-H anilide, and Fc+ then SET-oxidizes the resulting amidate to an amidyl radical (regenerating Fc). The amidyl radical cyclizes onto the tethered olefin, and the resulting C-radical abstracts H from 1,4-cyclohexadiene to give the hydroamidation product.
10.1002_anie.201510418.rate_determining_step: Single-electron transfer from anilide conjugate base to Fc+ (facilitated by less polar mixed THF/MeOH solvent that destabilizes ionic species)

key_intermediates.name: anilide conjugate base (deprotonated N-aryl amide, I)
carbon-centered radical from 5-exo cyclization onto tethered olefin (III)

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of 1a with Fc
probe_reagents.observation: In basic MeOH, Ep/2(1a)=+0.83 V vs SCE >> Ep/2(Fc)=+0.37 V, no electron transfer observed; in THF/MeOH (5:1) the potentials converge to within 60 mV (1a +0.61 V; Fc +0.55 V) and the Fc back-scan disappears, indicating efficient SET

probe_reagents.name: cyclopropyl radical clock substrate 17
probe_reagents.observation: Electrolysis gave ring-opened product 18 (67%), supporting a radical (amidyl) intermediate

probe_reagents.name: 1,4-cyclohexadiene (1,4-CHD)
probe_reagents.observation: Serves as H-atom donor to the cyclized C-radical; omission with substrate 15 led to oxidative termination giving indoline 16

probe_reagents.name: Na2CO3 (base substitution control)
probe_reagents.observation: No electron transfer detected when Na2CO3 was used instead of electrogenerated MeO-, indicating in situ MeO- is critical

10.1002_anie.201510418.notes: Solvent polarity is a key design element: switching from MeOH to THF/MeOH lowers substrate oxidation potential and raises Fc oxidation potential, enabling outer-sphere SET. Chemoselectivity (Scheme 2) shows oxidation occurs only on the more electron-rich PMP-carbamate, consistent with an outer-sphere SET mediator mechanism rather than direct electrolysis. With substrates lacking H-atom donor, an oxidative cyclization (C-radical further oxidized) pathway operates instead of hydroamidation.

10.1002_anie.201801106.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical aziridination of triaryl-substituted alkenes using hexafluoroisopropanol sulfamate (HfsNH2) as a nucleophilic nitrogen source, proceeding via stepwise anodic oxidations and two C-N bond formations
10.1002_anie.201801106.electrochemical_role: Anodic single-electron oxidation of the alkene to a carbon radical cation; a second anodic oxidation of the neutral C-centered radical (after sulfamate addition/deprotonation) to a carbocation; cathodic reduction of protons (assisted by lutidine/LiClO4/MeCN) releases H2
10.1002_anie.201801106.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes the triarylalkene to a carbon radical cation (A); sulfamate nitrogen attacks and lutidine deprotonates to give a neutral β-amido C-radical (B); a second anodic SET oxidizes B to a carbocation (C), which undergoes intramolecular nucleophilic ring closure by the sulfamate nitrogen to deliver the aziridine, while protons are reduced to H2 at the cathode.

key_intermediates.name: neutral β-sulfamyl C-centered radical (B) after nucleophilic addition of HfsNH2 and deprotonation by lutidine
β-sulfamyl carbocation (C) from second anodic oxidation
aziridine product from intramolecular C-N ring closure
Ritter-type nitrilium / dihydroimidazole (7a, 7b-7d) from MeCN trapping of cation (off-cycle trap)

probe_reagents.name: (E)- and (Z)-stilbene (6a)
probe_reagents.observation: Cation generated under the conditions is trapped by MeCN solvent to give the same trans-dihydroimidazole (Ritter-type) 7a in 50%/47% from E/Z isomers, supporting a planar carbocation intermediate

probe_reagents.name: 4-Br, 4-Cl, 4-F stilbenes (6b-6d)
probe_reagents.observation: Also give MeCN-trapped dihydroimidazoles 7b-7d (37-44%), confirming generality of carbocation intermediate

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of 1a, 2a, lutidine, LiClO4
probe_reagents.observation: Oxidation wave at ~+2.0 V vs SCE matches the oxidation potential of alkene 1a alone, indicating SET to anode occurs from the alkene, not from sulfamate

probe_reagents.name: CuI / Rh2(OAc)4
probe_reagents.observation: Nitrene-pathway metal catalysts gave no or only trace aziridine, arguing against a nitrene mechanism

10.1002_anie.201801106.notes: Authors explicitly contrast their alkene-activation pathway with the conventional nitrene/nitrogen-radical pathway (Scheme 1a vs 1b); the failure of CuI and Rh2(OAc)4 nitrene catalysts and the trapping of a planar carbocation (loss of E/Z stereochemistry in stilbene→7a) disfavor a nitrene mechanism. LiClO4-activated 2,6-lutidine or MeCN are proposed to participate in cathodic discharge.

10.1002_anie.200705748.rxn_class_summary: Electroinitiated cation chain reaction forming intramolecular C-C bond between thioacetal and pendant olefin to give tetrahydropyrans bearing an ArS group
10.1002_anie.200705748.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of ArSSAr generates ArS(ArSSAr)+ (an 'ArS+' equivalent) that activates the thioacetal; only a catalytic amount of electricity is required because ArS+ is regenerated in the chain
10.1002_anie.200705748.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes ArSSAr to an 'ArS+' equivalent [ArS(ArSSAr)+]; ArS+ abstracts ArS from thioacetal 6 to give alkoxycarbenium 9, which cyclizes onto the pendant olefin to give cation 10; ArSSAr traps 10 to deliver product 8 and regenerate ArS+, propagating the chain.
10.1002_anie.200705748.rate_determining_step: Intramolecular addition of the alkoxycarbenium ion to the olefin (cyclization of 9 to 10), made feasible by intramolecularization; the trapping of 10 by ArSSAr is concerted-like and accelerated by excess ArSSAr

key_intermediates.name: Alkoxycarbenium ion 9 from thioacetal 6 after ArS+ abstraction of ArS
Cyclized carbocation 10 (tetrahydropyranyl cation after intramolecular olefin addition)

probe_reagents.name: Bu4NBF4 supporting electrolyte (fluoride source probe)
probe_reagents.observation: Fluoride from BF4- attacks cyclized cation giving fluorinated product 7a (81%), proving a free cation intermediate

probe_reagents.name: Bu4NB(C6F5)4 (non-nucleophilic electrolyte)
probe_reagents.observation: Suppresses fluoride attack; ArSSAr instead traps the cyclized cation giving 8a (72-82%), confirming ArSSAr as nucleophile in the chain

probe_reagents.name: Substoichiometric ArS(ArSSAr)+ (20 mol%, 2 mol%)
probe_reagents.observation: Catalytic loading still produces 8a (33-47%), consistent with a chain mechanism; excess ArSSAr boosts yield to 82%

probe_reagents.name: CSI-MS / 1H NMR of ArS(ArSSAr)+B(C6F5)4-
probe_reagents.observation: Direct spectroscopic detection (HRMS m/z 381.0084) of the cationic ArS+ carrier

10.1002_anie.200705748.notes: Exclusive cis-selectivity in 8 (except 8b) suggests that cyclization of 9 to 10 and trapping by ArSSAr occur in a somewhat concerted manner rather than via a fully free, equilibrated cation 10. In-cell (direct) electrolysis (method B) also initiates the chain effectively. The reaction of alkoxycarbenium with a non-stabilized olefin is proposed as the potential bottleneck, rescued by intramolecularity.

10.1002_anie.202406017.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical radical fluoromethylation (CF3/CF2H/CF2Cl) of N-allylbenzamides triggering a Truce-Smiles 1,4-aryl migration to give CF2X-containing β-arylethylamines
10.1002_anie.202406017.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of CF3SO2- (Langlois reagent) to CF3SO2 radical which loses SO2 to give CF3 radical; later a second anodic oxidation converts the carbamoyl radical D to acyl/carbamoyl cation E that is trapped by ROH
10.1002_anie.202406017.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes CF3SO2Na to CF3SO2 radical which extrudes SO2 to give CF3 radical; CF3 adds regioselectively to the allyl alkene generating a β-amino radical that undergoes 5-exo ipso cyclization onto the benzamide arene, then C–C homolysis releases the migrated aryl and a carbamoyl radical, which is anodically oxidized to a carbamoyl cation trapped by ROH to yield the carbamate.
10.1002_anie.202406017.rate_determining_step: Ipso 5-exo cyclization of the β-amino radical onto the arene (TS_BC), competing with 6-endo cyclization; selectivity governed by N-protecting group (t-Bu favors 5-exo, ΔΔE‡ ≈ 3.1 kcal/mol vs 1.6 for Ts)

key_intermediates.name: CF3 radical (from Langlois reagent after SO2 extrusion)
β-amino C-centered radical A (after CF3 addition to alkene)
spirocyclic 6-endo cyclohexadienyl radical C' (off-path dihydroisoquinolinone precursor)
ring-opened aryl-migrated carbamoyl/N-acyl radical C (after C-C cleavage, before decarbonylation)
carbamoyl radical D (after homolytic C-C cleavage)
carbamoyl cation E (after second anodic oxidation)
carbamate cation F / final carbamate product after ROH addition

10.1002_anie.202406017.notes: DFT (B3LYP-D3/def2-SV(P) with DLPNO-CCSD(T)/def2-TZVPP single points) rationalizes why bulky t-Bu N-protection favors s-cis conformer of β-amino radical A enabling 5-exo ipso attack and 1,4-aryl migration, whereas Ts protection allows π-stacking that stabilizes s-trans and promotes competitive 6-endo cyclization to dihydroisoquinolinones. Alternative pathway: 6-endo cyclization gives dihydroisoquinolinones (observed as side products with Ts/OMe protecting groups). A putative carbocation oxidation side pathway from the β-amino radical is also drawn in Scheme 1c. No radical traps (TEMPO/BHT) or KIE explicitly reported in these pages.

10.1002_anie.201200878.rxn_class_summary: Anodic oxidation of methanol at a boron-doped diamond (BDD) electrode generates methoxy radicals, which enable construction of bisdimethyl acetals from dimethoxybenzyl ethers and an electrochemical biomimetic synthesis of the benzofuran neolignan (±)-licarin A from isoeugenol.
10.1002_anie.201200878.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation: MeOH/MeO- is oxidized at the BDD anode to generate methoxy radicals (MeO•); substrates (electron-rich arenes, isoeugenol) are also oxidized at the anode to aryl radical cations under CPE conditions.
10.1002_anie.201200878.electron_flow: At the anode, methanol/methoxide is oxidized by single-electron transfer to give methoxy radicals; in parallel, electron-rich arene substrates undergo anodic SET to radical cations that are trapped by MeOH/MeO-, followed by a second oxidation and loss of a proton or MeOR to deliver bisdimethyl acetals, aldehydes, or (for isoeugenol) radical intermediate 9 that couples/cyclizes to licarin A.

key_intermediates.name: oxocarbenium-type cation (B) en route to bisdimethyl acetal 2
phenoxyl/benzylic radical cation D from a phenol precursor
oxocarbenium E leading to aldehyde 3 by loss of MeOR

probe_reagents.name: DMPO (5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide)
probe_reagents.observation: Electrolysis of MeOH in the presence of DMPO gave an ESR signal matching the DMPO O-methoxy adduct (A_N = 13.37 G, A_H = 7.98 G), confirming generation of methoxy (not hydroxymethyl) radicals; signal intensity followed BDD > Pt > GC anode.

probe_reagents.name: Linear sweep voltammetry of isoeugenol (5)
probe_reagents.observation: Established first and second oxidation potentials used to set CPE potentials (entries 1–6); higher potential on BDD (1.06 V vs SCE) gave the best licarin A yield (40%).

probe_reagents.name: Anode material variation (BDD vs Pt vs GC)
probe_reagents.observation: BDD anode gave the strongest methoxy-radical ESR signal and the highest yield of licarin A (40%), consistent with more efficient MeO• generation at BDD.

10.1002_anie.201200878.notes: The mechanism is supported chiefly by DMPO spin-trapping/ESR detection of methoxy radicals and by anode-material-dependent yields, plus LSV measurements of substrate oxidation potentials; no KIE, DFT, or isotope labeling is reported. Two parallel anodic pathways are proposed: (i) direct SET oxidation of the arene to a radical cation followed by MeOH/MeO- addition (Scheme 1, intermediates A→C→B→2 and D→E→3), and (ii) anodic generation of MeO• that can add to the arene; both can plausibly contribute. For isoeugenol, vicinal byproducts 7 and 8 are rationalized via self-coupling of radical intermediate 9.

10.1002_anie.202300533_sup_2.rxn_class_summary: Iron-catalyzed electrochemical nitration of alkenes, alkynes, aryl boronic acids and arenes (including halo-, decarboxylative, and deborylative variants) using Fe(NO3)3 as both NO2-radical source and redox mediator
10.1002_anie.202300533_sup_2.electrochemical_role: Cathodic reduction of Fe(III) to Fe(II) initiates the cycle; Fe(II) reduces nitrate (or Fe(III)-bound nitrate) to release NO2 radical; the Fe(II) generated is re-oxidized at the anode (along with halide A- to A2) to regenerate Fe(III)
10.1002_anie.202300533_sup_2.electron_flow: Cathode injects an electron into Fe(III) to give Fe(II), which reduces nitrate to NO2 radical; NO2• adds to the alkene/alkyne or aryl pi-system to give a C-centered radical that is intercepted by Fe(III)-X ligand transfer (halonitration), by loss of CO2/Bpin/H+ (decarboxylative/deborylative/aromatic nitration); at the anode, halide is oxidized to X2 and Fe(II) reoxidized to Fe(III), closing the cycle.
10.1002_anie.202300533_sup_2.rate_determining_step: Generation/release of NO2 radical from Fe(III)-nitrate via Fe(II) (electron injection-dependent), consistent with on/off current induction behavior

key_intermediates.name: [FeIII-X] halide complex for ligand transfer (X = Br, Cl)

probe_reagents.name: 1-chloro-4-(1-cyclopropylvinyl)benzene (radical clock)
probe_reagents.observation: Ring-opened bromo-nitroalkene product 4 isolated in 77% (E:Z = 1.1:1), confirming a carbon-centered radical intermediate after NO2• addition

probe_reagents.name: Current on/off
probe_reagents.observation: Conversion proceeds only while current is applied; no dark propagation, indicating continuous electrochemical initiation/turnover of Fe and induction period at low F/mol

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Invoked in decarboxylative mechanism as trapping the α-nitro-β-carboxylate radical, supporting radical intermediate

10.1002_anie.202300533_sup_2.notes: Mechanism is unified across four substrate classes (alkenes, vinyl acids, vinyl boronates, arenes/ArB(OH)2) with the same Fe(III)/Fe(II) electrocatalytic cycle releasing NO2•. For halonitration, the C-radical is captured by Fe(III)-X (radical ligand transfer) and then undergoes base-mediated E2/intramolecular SN2 to give nitroalkenes or nitrolactones (81 & 82). For aryl nitration the cyclohexadienyl radical is oxidized by Fe(III) to a Wheland cation that loses H+ or B(OH)2 (ipso). An alternative direct anodic oxidation of nitrate to NO2• cannot be fully excluded but the Fe-mediated cathodic-initiation pathway is favored.

10.1002_anie.202313830.rxn_class_summary: Cobalt-catalyzed electroreductive alkyl-alkyl bond formation by addition of unactivated primary, secondary, and tertiary alkyl chlorides to conjugated olefins (Michael acceptors) in an undivided cell with a sacrificial Zn anode.
10.1002_anie.202313830.electrochemical_role: Cathodic reduction of Co(II)(salen) to Co(I) at -1.7 V vs Fc/Fc+, which activates the alkyl chloride (by SN2 or XAA depending on substrate/electrolyte); second cathodic event at -2.1 V reduces either Co(III)(alkyl) intermediate or the alkyl-radical-derived adduct to an enolate; Zn (or stainless steel/Co) serves as sacrificial anode.
10.1002_anie.202313830.electron_flow: Cathode reduces Co(II)(L1) to nucleophilic Co(I), which activates R-Cl by SN2 (1° RCl, or 2°/3° in non-coordinating electrolyte) to give Co(III)(alkyl) that homolyzes to R•, or directly by halogen-atom abstraction (2°/3° RCl in LiCl) to give R• plus Co(III)-Cl. The R• adds to the conjugated alkene; the resulting α-EWG radical is reduced at the cathode (or by Co(I)) to an enolate that is protonated by n-BuOH to give product, while the Zn anode supplies the balancing oxidation.
10.1002_anie.202313830.rate_determining_step: C–Cl bond activation by Co(I) (SN2 vs XAA depending on substrate class and electrolyte); for 1° alkyl chlorides, subsequent Co–C bond homolysis of the stable Co(III)(alkyl) is the kinetically limiting step.

key_intermediates.name: Reduced [Co(I)(L1)]- (nucleophilic Co species, B); isolated as [Na+][Co(I)(L2)]-, X-ray characterized
Co(III)(alkyl) intermediate (C, complex 5, e.g. Co(L1)(n-octyl)) formed via SN2 with 1° RCl
Putative [Co(I)Cl(L1)]2- chloride-coordinated super-reduced species mediating XAA with 2°/3° RCl
α-Carbonyl radical adduct after radical addition to acrylate
Enolate (after cathodic reduction of α-carbonyl radical)
Polyacrylate oligomer (off-pathway in absence of proton source)

probe_reagents.name: 5-chloropent-1-ene (radical clock)
probe_reagents.observation: Cyclized:uncyclized = 51%:12%, consistent with free alkyl radical intermediate after C–Cl activation.

probe_reagents.name: D2O (in place of n-BuOH proton source)
probe_reagents.observation: 65% yield with 95% D incorporation at the α-carbonyl position, supporting enolate protonation as the final step.

probe_reagents.name: Isolated Co(III)(alkyl) complex 5 (Co(L1)(n-C6H13))
probe_reagents.observation: Stoichiometric reaction with butyl acrylate gives only 28% of coupling product plus oligomers; CV shows reduction at -2.1 V in KPF6 but stable in LiCl, supporting SN2-derived Co(III)-alkyl pathway for 1° RCl.

probe_reagents.name: [Na+][Co(I)(L2)]- (chemically reduced Co(I))
probe_reagents.observation: Isolated and X-ray characterized; reacts immediately with 2-chlorooctane (color change Co(I) green → Co(III) red, full RCl consumption) — confirms Co(I) as the C–Cl activator.

probe_reagents.name: CV of Co(L1) ± RCl in KPF6 vs LiCl
probe_reagents.observation: In KPF6, 2° RCl gives ECE behavior with second wave at -2.1 V (Co(III)-alkyl reduction); in LiCl, only one catalytic-looking wave at -1.7 V with no Co(III)-alkyl wave, diagnostic of XAA pathway for 2°/3° RCl.

probe_reagents.name: UV-Vis of [Co(I)]- with added LiCl
probe_reagents.observation: No detectable change in E1/2 or speciation, so any chloride-bound super-reduced [Co(I)Cl]2- is invoked only as a minor but highly reactive species.

10.1002_anie.202313830.notes: Two mechanistic pathways for C–Cl activation operate in parallel and are substrate/electrolyte dependent: (i) SN2 by Co(I) giving a Co(III)(alkyl) that homolyzes (operative for 1° RCl in any electrolyte and for 2° RCl in non-coordinating PF6-/BF4-), and (ii) halogen atom abstraction (XAA) by Co(I) (or a chloride-bound [Co(I)Cl]2-) giving R• directly (operative for 3° RCl in all electrolytes and for 2° RCl in LiCl). The XAA pathway bypasses the slow Co–C homolysis step and gives higher yields, which guided ligand design (Co(L1) for 2°, Co(L3) for radical-clock substrates, Co(L5) for 3° RCl). A Zn-only (no current) background reaction contributes ~46% yield but is greatly enhanced by electrolysis with LiCl; acidic proton sources (HFIP) decompose catalyst, while n-BuOH or H2O prevent acrylate oligomerization by protonating the enolate.

10.1002_anie.202011657.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical CoCl2-catalyzed dehydrogenative diarylation and [2+2+2] cycloaddition of alkenes with indoles via radical-polar crossover to give 1,1-bis(indolyl)alkanes and dihydroindolocarbazoles
10.1002_anie.202011657.electrochemical_role: anodic oxidation of indole to indolyl radical cation (A); cobalt-mediated alkene addition gives alkyl radical D which is further anodically oxidized to alkyl cation E; cathode reduces protons to H2
10.1002_anie.202011657.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes indole 2a to a radical cation (A) which deprotonates to an indolyl C3-radical (B); Co catalyst delivers B across the alkene to give a benzylic radical (D), which is anodically oxidized to a benzylic cation (E); electrophilic Friedel-Crafts trapping by a second indole gives diarylation product 3, while intramolecular cyclization of the analogous intermediate F/G with further anodic oxidation furnishes the [2+2+2] carbazole 4; protons are reduced at the Pt cathode to H2.
10.1002_anie.202011657.rate_determining_step: electrophilic alkylation of indole by the benzylic cation in the final indole-trapping step (small KIE kH/kD = 1.27)

key_intermediates.name: benzylic alkyl radical with indolyl substituent (D)
benzylic alkyl cation with indolyl substituent (E)
indolyl cation intermediate F (after electrophilic alkylation of second indole)
dihydro intermediate G en route to carbazole product

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: diarylation product only trace; terindolone 6aa isolated in 27%, consistent with indolyl radical cation formation and electrophilic capture rather than HAT

probe_reagents.name: 1-(1-cyclopropylvinyl)-4-methoxybenzene (1v)
probe_reagents.observation: ring-opened byproduct 4va (24%) formed alongside 3vaa (60%), indicating a carbon-centered radical intermediate

probe_reagents.name: 3-cyclopropyl-1-methylindole (2s)
probe_reagents.observation: no reaction and starting materials recovered; rules out direct generation of indolyl C-radical via cyclopropyl ring opening

probe_reagents.name: 2-cyclopropyl-1-methylindole (2t)
probe_reagents.observation: diarylation product 3att formed in 61% without ring-opening, supports electrophilic (cationic) trapping at indole

probe_reagents.name: 3-deutero-1-methyl-1H-indole (2a-1D)
probe_reagents.observation: small KIE kH/kD = 1.27, supports electrophilic aromatic substitution rather than HAT in final indole trapping

probe_reagents.name: 3-iodo-1-methyl-1H-indole (2u)
probe_reagents.observation: no reaction with TBAI or TBAPF6 electrolytes, indicating iodide oxidation pathway is not operative

probe_reagents.name: TBAI (nBu4NI)
probe_reagents.observation: iodide alone or I2 gave little effect; quaternary ammonium iodide salts crucial; constant-potential experiments show diarylation does not include oxidation of iodide

10.1002_anie.202011657.notes: Authors disfavor a HAT pathway (small KIE) and disfavor iodide-mediated oxidation (no effect of I-/I2 alone, constant-potential control); nBu4NI acts as supporting electrolyte/mediator-like additive lowering anode potential through NaOTf/nBu4NI mixed electrolyte rather than as iodine redox shuttle. Switching to graphite/graphite cell and stronger current diverts the diarylation intermediate F via intramolecular electrophilic alkylation (G) into the [2+2+2] dihydroindolocarbazole product 4.

10.1002_ange.202403461.rxn_class_summary: Photoinduced dearomative [2+2] cycloaddition of quinolines/isoquinolines/phenanthrenes with alkenes enabled by borate complex formation, affording fused 2D/3D 6-6-4-6 frameworks bearing cyclobutane rings.
10.1002_ange.202403461.electron_flow: Deprotonation/addition of nBuLi and HB(pin) (or B(eg)) onto the heteroarene generates an anionic N-borate complex; UV/visible photoexcitation gives a singlet excited state that undergoes ISC to a longer-lived triplet diradical, which adds stepwise to the alkene to form a triplet 1,4-biradical that closes via a minimum-energy crossing point (S0/T1 MECP) to the cyclobutane-fused dearomatized product, which is acylated (AcCl) to deliver the isolated product.
10.1002_ange.202403461.rate_determining_step: Triplet C–C bond formation at the C3 position of the dihydroquinoline borate with the stilbene alkene via 3TS2_3-B* (ΔG‡ = 5.3 kcal/mol); the competing rearomatization via 3TS2_5-B* (13.3 kcal/mol) is suppressed by borate formation.

key_intermediates.name: anionic N-borate complex INT-A/INT-B (1,2-dihydroquinoline lithium borate, IM1-B)
excited triplet borate complex 3IM2-B* (3III*) — 1,2-dihydroquinoline-derived triplet diradical
triplet biradical 3IM3-B* (after C3–C alkene bond formation, benzyl radicals at C4 of DHQ and on stilbene)
closed-shell cyclobutane-fused dihydroquinoline IM4-B (V, product precursor)
lithiated (non-borate) intermediate 3IM2-Li* → rearomatized 3IM5-Li* (off-pathway, yields by-product VI)

probe_reagents.name: transient absorption spectroscopy (ns laser, 355 nm)
probe_reagents.observation: Absorption at ~500 nm with ~10 ns lifetime for both INT-A and INT-B, assigned to excited singlet states; rules out a borate-induced change in triplet lifetime.

probe_reagents.name: N–H / N–Ac analogues (vs N–Li/N–B(pin) borates)
probe_reagents.observation: Only borate (or lithiated) substrates undergo photoexcitation to the productive excited state; neutral N–H/N–Ac substrates do not enter the pathway, and lithiated-only gives rearomatized by-product 5a/6a instead of cycloaddition product 3aa.

probe_reagents.name: control without HB(pin) for isoquinoline 12
probe_reagents.observation: No [2+2] cycloaddition product detected without HB(pin), confirming the borate INT-C is required.

probe_reagents.name: X-ray crystallography (CCDC 2332939) of product 17
probe_reagents.observation: Confirms the dearomative cyclobutane-fused 2D/3D framework structure and diastereochemistry.

10.1002_ange.202403461.notes: Two excited-state pathways were compared computationally: (favored) borate triplet 3IM2-B* → 3TS2_3-B* (5.3) → 3IM3-B* → MECP → IM4-B (cycloadduct, −55.1 kcal/mol); (alternative/off-pathway) borate rearomatization via 3TS2_5-B* (13.3 kcal/mol) giving 3IM5-B*, and lithiated 3IM2-Li* pathway in which 3TS2_5-Li* (7.9) is lower than 3TS2_3-Li* (14.8), explaining why the N–Li intermediate gives only rearomatized by-product. A singlet TS 3TS3_4-B* (39.9 kcal/mol) is ruled out in favor of an S0/T1 MECP for ring closure. Diastereoselectivity is rationalized by approach-direction TS energies (5.3 vs 6.1/7.1/16.6 kcal/mol). Cycloaddition occurs ~10^7 times faster than rearomatization, while rearomatization is ~10^5 times slower for the borate vs the lithiated intermediate.

10.1002_anie.201909922.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical anodic decarboxylative lactonisation of allyl hemioxalate salts with aliphatic carboxylic acids to form γ-butyrolactones via radical cyclisation/cross-coupling
10.1002_anie.201909922.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation (Pt) of the hemioxalate salt to an oxycarbonyl radical via decarboxylation, while the cathode (Pt) reduces protons (from MeOH/NH4+/co-acid) generating methoxide that deprotonates the co-acid; both electrodes contribute productively (paired electrolysis)
10.1002_anie.201909922.electron_flow: Anode oxidises the hemioxalate to an oxycarbonyl radical that loses CO2 and undergoes 5-exo-trig cyclisation onto the pendant alkene, giving a primary C-radical; in parallel, the co-acid carboxylate is anodically decarboxylated to an alkyl radical, and the two radicals recombine at/near the electrode to give the γ-substituted lactone; the cathode reduces protons to H2, generating methoxide that maintains deprotonation of the co-acid.

key_intermediates.name: alkyl radical R• from decarboxylation of co-acid
acyl nitrene/isocyanate from oxamic acid (lactam attempt)
carbamate 20 from methoxide trapping of nitrogen-centred cation

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of hemioxalate vs aliphatic acid
probe_reagents.observation: Hemioxalate 1 is easier to oxidise than the aliphatic co-acid, justifying portionwise addition of 1 to maintain low concentration

probe_reagents.name: carbon graphite electrode (control)
probe_reagents.observation: Unsuitable due to high H overpotential and strong adsorption of organics; supports choice of Pt

probe_reagents.name: deoxygenation/drying of solvent
probe_reagents.observation: No impact on yield, consistent with robust radical pathway at electrode surface

probe_reagents.name: N-tosyl hemioxalate 18 (lactam attempt)
probe_reagents.observation: No exo-cyclisation; instead overoxidation to N-centred cation trapped by MeOH/MeO- to give carbamate 20, indicating cation formation outcompetes radical cyclisation for N-substrates

probe_reagents.name: secondary co-acid (→ lactone 7)
probe_reagents.observation: Lower yield consistent with known anodic overoxidation of secondary alkyl radicals to carbocations

probe_reagents.name: halide-bearing co-acid (→ lactone 10)
probe_reagents.observation: Halogenated chain tolerated, indicating short radical lifetime / rapid recombination near electrode (no halide abstraction)

10.1002_anie.201909922.notes: Main by-product is the R-R homodimer from co-acid radicals; excess (5 equiv) co-acid is used to suppress homo-dimerisation of the cyclised radical. Attempted lactam synthesis from oxamic acids fails because nitrogen lone pair stabilises a cation pathway; even with EWG (Ts, OTf) on N, overoxidation dominates and methoxide traps the cation to give carbamate 20 rather than radical cyclisation product 19.

10.1002_anie.202219166.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical regioselective 1,2-diarylation of alkenes with cyanoarenes and aryl halides via cathodic reduction without transition-metal catalyst
10.1002_anie.202219166.electrochemical_role: Cathodic single-electron reduction of dicyanobenzene (1aa) to its radical anion (DCA), which acts as a redox mediator to reduce aryl halide (ArX) to aryl radical via outer-sphere electron transfer; sacrificial Zn anode is oxidized to Zn(II)
10.1002_anie.202219166.electron_flow: Cathode reduces dicyanobenzene 1aa to its persistent radical anion (DCA); DCA transfers an electron (OSET) to ArX, which fragments to halide and aryl radical; the aryl radical adds anti-Markovnikov to the styrene to give a benzyl radical, which couples with a second DCA and ejects cyanide to give the 1,2-diarylated product. Zn anode is sacrificially oxidized to Zn(2+).
10.1002_anie.202219166.rate_determining_step: Outer-sphere electron transfer from dicyanobenzene radical anion to aryl iodide (ΔG‡ = 6.0 kcal/mol) followed by aryl radical addition to alkene (ΔG‡ = 8.0 kcal/mol via TS-3); radical addition is likely turnover-limiting in the productive cycle

key_intermediates.name: aryl halide radical anion (R2, e.g. iodobenzene radical anion)
benzyl radical adduct (13) from anti-Markovnikov addition of aryl radical to alkene
radical/radical-anion cross-coupling adduct (14) bearing cyano group prior to CN- loss

probe_reagents.name: (1-phenyl-2,2-diphenyl)cyclopropyl alkene (radical clock 8)
probe_reagents.observation: ring-opened diarylation product 9 formed in 56% yield, confirming radical intermediate

probe_reagents.name: DMPO (5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide)
probe_reagents.observation: DMPO-phenyl adduct detected by EPR (g=2.0072, A_N=14.4 G, A_H=20.9 G) and confirmed by HR-MS (190.12202)

10.1002_anie.202219166.notes: Alternative pathway via radical addition of DCA itself to the alkene (TS-1, ΔG‡=33.5 kcal/mol) and subsequent CN- dissociation (TS-2) was computationally ruled out due to steric repulsion and instability of intermediate benzyl radical 11. CV studies show 1aa is easier to reduce than 2aa, and constant-potential experiments at -1.6/-1.7 V (where only 1aa is reducible) still give diarylation, supporting the mediator role of DCA over direct ArX reduction. Final C-C bond formation proceeds via radical/radical-anion cross-coupling between benzyl radical and DCA with loss of CN-.

10.1002_anie.202313273.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical Cu-catalyzed enantioselective azidocyanation of alkenes using TMSN3 and TMSCN to install azide and nitrile groups across the C=C bond
10.1002_anie.202313273.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of azide anion (N3-) to azidyl radical (N3•); Cu(I) is also anodically reoxidized to Cu(II) to close the catalytic cycle; cathode reduces protons to H2
10.1002_anie.202313273.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes N3- (generated from TMSN3/HFIP) to N3•, which adds to the alkene to give a carbon-centered radical; this radical is captured by [LCu(II)(CN)2] to form a Cu(III)-alkyl that undergoes C–CN reductive elimination, delivering the azidocyanation product and Cu(I), which is reoxidized anodically to Cu(II) while the cathode reduces protons to H2.

key_intermediates.name: beta-azido carbon radical from alkene addition
ring-opened homoallyl azide from vinylcyclopropane radical clock (48)

probe_reagents.name: vinylcyclopropane (47)
probe_reagents.observation: Ring-opened azidocyanation product 48 obtained in 15% yield, consistent with a carbon-centered radical intermediate 49

probe_reagents.name: N-allyl amide alkene 50
probe_reagents.observation: Linear azidocyanation product 51 formed (45%) with no oxazoline cyclization product 52, arguing against a cationic intermediate 54 and supporting a radical pathway

probe_reagents.name: stoichiometric Cu(acac)2/L1 without electricity
probe_reagents.observation: No azidocyanation product 2 formed (0% yield, 41% conversion), showing electricity is required

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of Cu(acac)2/L1 with TMSCN and/or TMSN3
probe_reagents.observation: Trace d (with both) mirrors trace b (TMSCN) in the Cu(II/I) region, indicating [Cu–CN] dominates over [Cu–N3]; anodic peak at 0.99 V assigned to N3- → N3• oxidation appears only when TMSN3 is added

probe_reagents.name: anode potential monitoring during preparative electrolysis
probe_reagents.observation: Stable ~1 V potential that surges at end of reaction, consistent with depletion of N3- being oxidized at the anode

10.1002_anie.202313273.notes: Alternative cationic pathway via benzylic carbocation 54 was explicitly considered and ruled out by absence of intramolecular oxazoline cyclization product 52 from amide-tethered alkene 50. HFIP is invoked to promote H2 evolution at the cathode (suppressing undesired cathodic reduction) and to facilitate generation of free N3- from TMSN3; predominance of [Cu–CN] over [Cu–N3] suppresses competing alkene diazidation.

10.1002_anie.201700837.rxn_class_summary: Brønsted acid catalyzed intramolecular [2+2] photocycloaddition of dithiane-substituted alkenes/alkynes under visible-light (405 nm) irradiation via a thionium ion excited state
10.1002_anie.201700837.electron_flow: Brønsted acid catalyst ionizes the dithiane to a thionium ion 7, which absorbs visible light (405 nm) to populate a ππ* singlet state; ring closure onto the tethered alkene followed by intersystem crossing gives a triplet 1,4-biradical 8, whose free rotation around the central C–C bond before spin inversion and C–C bond formation explains loss of alkene stereochemistry to give cyclobutane products.

key_intermediates.name: thionium ion 7 (from Brønsted acid protonation/ionization of dithiane)
ππ* singlet excited state of thionium ion

probe_reagents.name: Z-configured substrate 1j (stereochemical probe)
probe_reagents.observation: Stereo-unspecific [2+2]: cis/trans-6 obtained in 67:33 ratio, indicating loss of alkene geometry consistent with a triplet 1,4-biradical with free C–C rotation

probe_reagents.name: X-ray crystallography of 2g
probe_reagents.observation: Confirmed the relative configuration of the cyclobutane product

probe_reagents.name: UV/Vis absorption of thionium ion (Figure 1)
probe_reagents.observation: Strong absorption indicates a ππ* singlet (S1) is populated upon 405 nm excitation

10.1002_anie.201700837.notes: Alternative singlet [2+2] pathway is implicitly excluded because Z-1j reacts stereo-unspecifically (67:33 d.r.), favoring a triplet 1,4-biradical mechanism over a concerted singlet [2+2]. Dithiane group can subsequently be removed reductively (Raney-Ni → 4) or oxidatively (PhI(OOCCF3)2 → ketone 5/6).

10.1002_anie.201807683.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical dehydrogenative annulation of N-allyl amides with 1,3-dicarbonyl compounds to form pyrrolidines [(4+1)] or tetrahydropyridines [(4+2)] using a phenothiazine organic redox mediator
10.1002_anie.201807683.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of phenothiazine (3, E_p/2 = 0.52 V vs SCE) to its radical cation 3·+, which serves as an outer-sphere SET mediator that oxidizes the deprotonated 1,3-dicarbonyl anion to a C-radical; cathode reduces H2O to H2 and HO- (which deprotonates the 1,3-dicarbonyl)
10.1002_anie.201807683.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes phenothiazine 3 to radical cation 3·+; cathode reduces H2O to H2 + HO-, which deprotonates the 1,3-dicarbonyl to enolate II; 3·+ oxidizes II by SET to C-radical III, which adds across the N-allyl amide alkene to give tertiary radical V; V is further oxidized by 3·+ to a cation that is intramolecularly trapped by the amide carbonyl oxygen, leading after workup to the pyrrolidine (via open-chain 62) or tetrahydropyridine product.

key_intermediates.name: tertiary C-radical V after addition to N-allyl amide alkene

probe_reagents.name: crossover experiment with amides 56 and 57
probe_reagents.observation: Only non-crossed products 14 and 37 formed; no crossover products detected, indicating intramolecular acyl migration

probe_reagents.name: N-Boc analogue 58
probe_reagents.observation: Cyclic carbamate 59 isolated in 45% yield, supporting trapping of cationic intermediate by amide/carbamate carbonyl oxygen

probe_reagents.name: H2-18O
probe_reagents.observation: [18O]-11 obtained in 59% yield with 90% 18O incorporation; LiAlH4 reduction gave diol 61 with no 18O — confirms acyl oxygen of product originates from H2O

probe_reagents.name: isolation of open-chain intermediate 62
probe_reagents.observation: Run in H2O-free tBuOMe/MeOH gave uncyclized amine 62 (53%); resubjection to standard conditions cyclized to pyrrolidine 4 (42%), confirming 62 as on-cycle intermediate

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of 2, 3, and N-allyl amide IV
probe_reagents.observation: E_p/2: 3 = 0.52 V, anion II = 0.31 V, dicarbonyl 2 > 2.0 V, alkene IV = 1.74 V (vs SCE); mediator selectively oxidizes the enolate

10.1002_anie.201807683.notes: Alternative cyclization onto the 1,3-dicarbonyl carbonyl oxygen (instead of amide carbonyl) was considered but ruled out by the 18O-labelling result and the Boc-trapping experiment, which show the product acyl-O derives from water via amide-O attack on cation VI, followed by hydrolysis. Phenothiazine is highlighted as a novel organic redox catalyst operative in aqueous solution, avoiding stoichiometric oxidants and transition metals.

10.1002_anie.202313830_sup_1.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical reductive Giese-type coupling of unactivated alkyl chlorides with electron-deficient olefins (acrylates) catalyzed by cobalt salen complexes
10.1002_anie.202313830_sup_1.electrochemical_role: Cathodic reduction of Co(II/III) salen to a Co(I) nucleophile that activates the alkyl chloride via inner-sphere oxidative addition to form Co(III)-alkyl; a second cathodic reduction of the Giese adduct (alpha-acyl radical) to the enolate is required for product formation. Zn sacrificial anode supplies counter-oxidation.
10.1002_anie.202313830_sup_1.electron_flow: Cathode reduces Co(II) salen to Co(I), which displaces chloride from R-Cl to form a Co(III)-alkyl resting state; thermal Co(III)-C homolysis releases an alkyl radical that adds to acrylate (Giese addition); the resulting alpha-ester radical is reduced (cathodically) to an enolate, which is protonated by nBuOH/H2O to deliver the product.
10.1002_anie.202313830_sup_1.rate_determining_step: Co(III)-C homolysis releasing the alkyl radical (modulated by salen steric bulk; faster with bulky Co(L3) for primary substrates)

key_intermediates.name: enolate (from second reduction of Giese adduct)
acrylate oligomers (polymerization side products without reductant)

probe_reagents.name: 6-chlorohex-1-ene (radical clock)
probe_reagents.observation: Cyclized:uncyclized product ratio 3.6:1 at 5 mol% Co(Me4Salen); ratio decreases with higher catalyst loading (2.8 at 1.1% to 1.9 at 20%), consistent with free alkyl radical intermediate whose lifetime depends on Co concentration

probe_reagents.name: D2O (vs H2O)
probe_reagents.observation: ~95% single deuterium incorporation at alpha-position of ester product, confirming protonation of an enolate intermediate (i.e., second reduction of Giese adduct radical)

probe_reagents.name: Co(III)(L2)-nOctyl stoichiometric
probe_reagents.observation: Heating in presence of butyl acrylate without reductant gives Giese product plus oligomers (radical polymerization); under electroreductive conditions, oligomers suppressed and product yield increases, supporting cathodic reduction of Giese adduct to enolate

probe_reagents.name: Co(L1) added to Co(L3) reactions (inhibition study)
probe_reagents.observation: Increasing loading of less bulky Co(L1) decreases yield with primary electrophiles, implying formation of stable Co(III)-R adduct that retards homolysis

probe_reagents.name: Constant-potential electrolysis at -1.7, -1.9, -2.2 V (CV referenced)
probe_reagents.observation: Product formation and RCl consumption rates independent of applied potential; near-quantitative yield even at mild -1.7 V (E1/2 of catalyst), indicating direct reduction of Co(III)-alkyl is not required—radical generation proceeds via thermal homolysis

10.1002_anie.202313830_sup_1.notes: Steric tuning of salen ligand (Co(L3) = Me4Salen bulky vs Co(L1) = CySalen less bulky) controls Co(III)-C homolysis rate: bulky ligand accelerates homolysis (preferred for primary alkyl chlorides), less bulky for secondary/tertiary. An alternative mechanism in which the Co(III)-alkyl is reduced directly at the cathode to release the radical is ruled out by potential-independence experiments — thermal homolysis is operative. Without cathodic reduction of the Giese adduct radical, acrylate polymerization dominates, indicating that the second cathodic electron (to form the enolate) is essential.

10.1002_anie.202500203_sup_1.rxn_class_summary: Electroreductive Ni-catalyzed cyclopropanation of alkenes using dichloromethane as a carbene precursor (with a related β-methylation side pathway)
10.1002_anie.202500203_sup_1.electrochemical_role: Cathodic reduction of Ni(II)/iPr-PyBOX to Ni(0), which mediates the reduction/activation of dichloromethane to generate a Ni-carbene species; olefin coordination further facilitates DCM reduction
10.1002_anie.202500203_sup_1.electron_flow: Cathode reduces Ni(II)(iPr-PyBOX) to Ni(0); the Ni(0) complex reduces/activates CH2Cl2 to form a Ni-carbene, whose addition across the alkene delivers the cyclopropane; binding of the olefin lowers the DCM reduction onset, enabling catalyst turnover.
10.1002_anie.202500203_sup_1.rate_determining_step: Ni-mediated reduction/activation of dichloromethane (onset shifted from -2.48 V to -1.91 V vs Fc/Fc+ in presence of Ni and olefin)

key_intermediates.name: Cyclopropane product (e.g., 29b) – not on-cycle intermediate for methylation

probe_reagents.name: CD2Cl2 (d2-dichloromethane)
probe_reagents.observation: β-methylation of 25a gives CD2H-incorporated product (48% qNMR), confirming the methylene of DCM is transferred into the methyl product

probe_reagents.name: Cyclopropane substrate 29b (stability test)
probe_reagents.observation: 29b recovered in 61% with 0% methylation product 1c, showing cyclopropane is not an intermediate to the methylated product (off-cycle pathway)

probe_reagents.name: On/off current experiment (substrate 8b)
probe_reagents.observation: Yield increases only while current is on and plateaus when off across 3 cycles, indicating continuous electrolysis is required (no radical chain)

probe_reagents.name: Dichlorodiphenylmethane (CV probe)
probe_reagents.observation: Irreversible cathodic peak at -2.34 V vs Fc/Fc+; direct reduction to tetraphenylethylene unlikely, supporting Ni-carbene homocoupling pathway

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of Ni/iPr-PyBOX ± DCM ± olefin 6a
probe_reagents.observation: DCM reduction onset shifts from -2.48 V (no Ni) to -2.00 V (with Ni) to -1.91 V (with Ni and 6a), supporting Ni-mediated reduction of DCM enhanced by olefin binding

probe_reagents.name: (S)-iPr-PyBOX chiral ligand
probe_reagents.observation: 44% ee in product 1b, indicating a chiral Ni complex is involved in the stereodetermining C-C bond formation

10.1002_anie.202500203_sup_1.notes: Two product manifolds are observed: on-cycle cyclopropanation via a Ni-carbene from CH2Cl2 + alkene, and an off-cycle β-methylation pathway that does NOT proceed through the cyclopropane intermediate (shown by stability of 29b under reaction conditions). CD2Cl2 labeling confirms DCM-derived CH2 is incorporated into both the cyclopropane and methyl products. CV data support that the olefin assists regeneration/turnover of the active Ni species for DCM activation. Alternative direct cathodic reduction of a dichloro intermediate to a free carbenoid is disfavored by CV of dichlorodiphenylmethane.

10.1002_anie.201810870.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical regioselective oxidative dimerization of 4-hydroxystilbenes (phenylpropenoids) to quinone methide dimers (QMDs) via anodic phenol oxidation and phenoxyl radical coupling
10.1002_anie.201810870.electrochemical_role: Anodic single-electron oxidation of the 4-hydroxystilbene phenol (with 2,6-lutidine as proton acceptor) generates a phenoxyl radical (delocalized form 2a-2c); 2,6-lutidine deprotonates the phenol/phenol radical cation to facilitate oxidation at 0.6 V vs Ag/AgCl
10.1002_anie.201810870.electron_flow: Anode removes one electron from the 4-hydroxystilbene (assisted by 2,6-lutidine deprotonation) to give a delocalized phenoxyl radical; two such radicals couple regioselectively at C8-C8' or C3-C8' to furnish the quinone methide dimer, which is the isolated product and serves as a radical-trapping antioxidant by direct addition of peroxyl radicals to its exocyclic C=C followed by aromatization.
10.1002_anie.201810870.rate_determining_step: anodic phenol oxidation / phenoxyl radical generation prior to radical-radical coupling (not explicitly assigned)

key_intermediates.name: phenoxyl radical (resonance forms 2a/2b/2c spread onto C3 and C8')
quinone methide dimer (QMD) from C8-C8' or C3-C8' radical-radical coupling
peroxyl-adduct radical (RTA addition product to QMD exocyclic alkene, aromatizes to stabilized phenoxyl)

probe_reagents.name: CD3OD (deuterated methanol) exchange test on QMD
probe_reagents.observation: no deuterium incorporation into QMD, ruling out tautomerization (mechanism 2) to a phenolic form

probe_reagents.name: PBD-BODIPY (co-autoxidation signal carrier)
probe_reagents.observation: inhibited autoxidation kinetics gave k_inh = 2-3 x 10^6 M^-1 s^-1 and n ~1.3-1.7 for QMDs, far exceeding parent phenols

probe_reagents.name: AIBN initiator / 1-hexadecene co-autoxidation
probe_reagents.observation: established RTA inhibition rate constants and stoichiometries for QMDs vs quadrangularin A analogues

probe_reagents.name: Truncated unsubstituted QMD model (CBS-QB3 computations)
probe_reagents.observation: predicted HAT from C-H k ≈ 6 M^-1 s^-1 (excludes mechanism 1); ΔH = -31.5 kcal/mol for peroxyl addition to exocyclic C=C with computed k matching experimental 4 x 10^6 M^-1 s^-1 (supports direct-addition mechanism 4)

10.1002_anie.201810870.notes: Four mechanistic possibilities for the antioxidant activity of QMDs were evaluated (Figure 4E): (1) HAT via C-H, (2) tautomerization then HAT via O-H, (3) homolysis/recombination of the QMD, and (4) direct addition of peroxyl radical to the exocyclic C=C with aromatization. Mechanisms 1-3 are excluded by k_inh magnitude, lack of D-incorporation in CD3OD, and Keq (1.8 x 10^-10 M) for dissociation respectively. Mechanism 4 (direct peroxyl addition followed by aromatization to a stabilized, O2-unreactive phenoxyl radical) is favored and supported by DFT (CBS-QB3) thermochemistry and rate constant agreement. On the synthesis side, regiocontrol between C8-C8' (e.g. quadrangularin A) and C3-C8' (e.g. hierochin D) coupling arises from radical spin-density distribution on the phenoxyl intermediate.

10.1002_anie.202219166_sup_1.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical reaction of 1,4-dicyanobenzene with aryl iodides and alkenes (e.g., styrenes) via radical intermediates generated under Zn anode / RVC cathode conditions in CH3CN with BnNMe3Cl electrolyte.
10.1002_anie.202219166_sup_1.electrochemical_role: Cathodic reduction generates aryl radical from aryl iodide (and/or radical anion of 1,4-dicyanobenzene); Zn serves as sacrificial anode
10.1002_anie.202219166_sup_1.electron_flow: Cathode reduces aryl iodide (assisted by mediator/radical anion of 1,4-dicyanobenzene) to generate an aryl (phenyl) radical, which adds to the alkene; the Zn anode is oxidized as the sacrificial counter-electrode.

key_intermediates.name: benzyl/alkyl C-radical adduct after radical addition to alkene

probe_reagents.name: (1-(2-phenylcyclopropyl)vinyl)benzene (8)
probe_reagents.observation: Ring-opened product 9 isolated in 56%, consistent with radical addition followed by cyclopropane ring opening

probe_reagents.name: DMPO
probe_reagents.observation: EPR spectrum of DMPO adduct observed (g = 2.0072–2.0073, A_N = 14.40 G, A_H = 20.90 G); HRMS m/z 190.12202 matches DMPO–phenyl adduct C12H16NO (calcd 190.12264), supporting phenyl radical

10.1002_anie.202219166_sup_1.notes: Radical clock with phenylcyclopropyl vinyl substrate gives ring-opened diene product, confirming a carbon-centered radical intermediate. EPR/DMPO trapping with and without added 4-methylstyrene gives the same signal, and HRMS identifies the trapped species as a phenyl radical derived from iodobenzene.

10.1002_anie.202117188_sup_1.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical Cp*Rh-catalyzed C-H alkenylation of 1-naphthols with alkenes under constant current in an undivided cell
10.1002_anie.202117188_sup_1.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation regenerates the active Cp*Rh(III) catalyst from a reduced Rh species formed after C-H activation/alkene insertion/β-H elimination; H2 evolution at Pt cathode closes the redox balance
10.1002_anie.202117188_sup_1.electron_flow: Cp*Rh(III) performs C-H activation on 1-naphthol (non-rate-limiting), alkene inserts and β-H elimination delivers the alkenylated naphthol and a reduced Rh species; the anode reoxidizes Rh back to Rh(III) while protons are reduced to H2 at the Pt cathode.
10.1002_anie.202117188_sup_1.rate_determining_step: Step after the C-H cleavage (likely migratory insertion or β-hydride elimination); C-H activation is not rate-limiting based on kH/kD ≈ 1.1

key_intermediates.name: Cp*Rh(III) naphtholate/aryl complex after C-H metalation
Rh(III)-alkyl after migratory insertion into alkene 2e
Reduced Rh species (Rh(I)) after β-hydride elimination/product release

probe_reagents.name: [D]n-1a (ortho-deuterated 1-naphthol, 90% D)
probe_reagents.observation: Parallel initial-rate experiment gave kH/kD ≈ 1.1, indicating C-H cleavage is not rate-determining

10.1002_anie.202117188_sup_1.notes: Only the KIE portion of the mechanistic studies is shown on this page; the near-unity KIE (1.1) argues against rate-limiting concerted metalation-deprotonation, suggesting a post-C-H-activation step controls the rate.

10.1002_anie.202300533.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemically driven 1,2-halonitration (bromo/chloro-nitration) of alkenes using ferric nitrate as nitryl radical source and LiX as halide source
10.1002_anie.202300533.electrochemical_role: Cathodic single-electron reduction of Fe(NO3)3·9H2O activates nitrate, generating nitryl radical (•NO2) and Fe(II); the injected electron is substoichiometric and acts catalytically (electron as catalyst)
10.1002_anie.202300533.electron_flow: Cathode injects one electron into Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, producing Fe(II) and releasing •NO2 via SET reduction of nitrate (NO3- -> NO3•2- -> •NO2). •NO2 adds to the alkene to give a β-nitroalkyl C-radical, which undergoes inner-sphere radical ligand transfer from an in-situ formed Fe(III)-X intermediate to deliver the C-X bond and regenerate Fe(II); Fe(II) is reoxidized anodically, closing the catalytic cycle with substoichiometric electrons.
10.1002_anie.202300533.rate_determining_step: Single-electron reduction/activation of Fe(NO3)3·9H2O to release •NO2 (initiation); current-controlled, with on/off experiments showing slow continued conversion after current off

key_intermediates.name: NO3 dianion radical (NO3•2-) from pulse radiolysis (λmax 350 nm)
Fe(II) species (regenerated, undergoes anodic reoxidation outside main cycle)

probe_reagents.name: Aryl cyclopropyl alkene (radical clock 3)
probe_reagents.observation: Ring-opened bromo-nitro product 4 obtained in 87% (E:Z 1.1:1), confirming C-radical intermediate after •NO2 addition

probe_reagents.name: Allyl phenyl sulfone (5)
probe_reagents.observation: β-scission/desulfonylation product 6 (41%) consistent with radical intermediate

probe_reagents.name: (E)- and (Z)-stilbene
probe_reagents.observation: Both give product 7 with similar d.r. (10:3), indicating loss of olefin geometry via free C-radical

probe_reagents.name: Cesium nitrate / pulse radiolysis
probe_reagents.observation: Transient at λmax = 350 nm decaying by 2nd-order kinetics, assigned to NO3•2- from one-electron reduction of NO3-

probe_reagents.name: Substituted styrenes (Hammett series: tBu, H, F, Cl, Br, CN, NO2)
probe_reagents.observation: Slightly negative ρ ≈ -0.2, consistent with electrophilic •NO2 addition to alkene

probe_reagents.name: Nitrite (NO2-) detection (Griess-type test)
probe_reagents.observation: NO2- formed only when electricity is passed through Fe(NO3)3 solution, confirming electrochemical generation of •NO2

probe_reagents.name: Fe(OTf)2 / LiNO3 control (test F, I)
probe_reagents.observation: Mixing Fe(II) with nitrate also produces NO2-, supporting Fe(II) role in •NO2 regeneration

probe_reagents.name: On/off current experiments
probe_reagents.observation: Conversion continues during off periods; TON higher with longer off intervals, supporting substoichiometric electron (electrocatalysis) rather than stoichiometric radical chain initiation

10.1002_anie.202300533.notes: Authors frame the electron itself as a substoichiometric catalyst (electrocatalysis, scenario a) but also present the classical Fe(III)/Fe(II) redox cycle (scenario b) as equivalent representation. XPS of precipitate shows Fe(II)/Fe(III) oxide-hydroxide byproducts. Direct cathodic reduction of styrene (~ -1.2 V) is excluded since electrolysis at 0.77 V still works. An alternative inner-sphere pathway where the β-nitroalkyl radical is stabilized by Fe coordination prior to ligand transfer cannot be ruled out. Oxygen-atom transfer from NO3- to Fe(II) as an alternative to SET is also acknowledged.

10.1002_anie.201809454.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical aerobic functionalization of styrenes using O2, where Pt anode gives oxidative C=C cleavage to carbonyls and carbon anode gives oxidative dimerization to 2,5-diaryl tetrahydrofurans.
10.1002_anie.201809454.electrochemical_role: Anodic single-electron oxidation of styrene to its radical cation; cathodic single-electron reduction of O2 to superoxide (O2•-); electrode material dictates which species (O2•- vs neutral styrene) traps the styrene radical cation.
10.1002_anie.201809454.electron_flow: At Pt anode, styrene is oxidized to a radical cation that is trapped by cathodically generated O2•- to give a dioxetane (major) or epoxide (minor), which fragments to aryl ketone + HCHO. At carbon anode, O2•- adsorption is poor, so the styrene radical cation is intercepted by a second neutral styrene to form a distonic dimer cation, which then combines with O2•- and cyclizes to the 2,5-diaryl THF.

key_intermediates.name: styrene oxide / epoxide (2e, minor path for cleavage)
formaldehyde (HCHO, cleavage co-product detected by GC-MS)

probe_reagents.name: 18O2 isotope labeling
probe_reagents.observation: 18O incorporated into both the ketone (81%) and the THF (93%, cis/trans 1:1.1), confirming O2 as the oxygen source in both reactions

probe_reagents.name: isolated epoxide 2e as substrate
probe_reagents.observation: Electrolysis of 2e gave only 88% acetophenone with incomplete conversion, indicating epoxide is a minor (not main) intermediate on the cleavage path

probe_reagents.name: GC-MS detection of HCHO
probe_reagents.observation: Formaldehyde detected, consistent with dioxetane fragmentation

probe_reagents.name: DFT (B3LYP/6-31G+(d,p)) spin density
probe_reagents.observation: Highest spin density of styrene radical cation localized at β-position, rationalizing β-attack by O2•- or by neutral styrene

10.1002_anie.201809454.notes: Authors do not rule out that on Pt the electrode itself adsorbs O2/O2•- and thereby delivers it to the radical cation; a radical–radical coupling pathway is also considered possible for the THF-forming step. Selectivity is explained by Pt favoring O2•- capture of the radical cation while carbon anode favors capture by neutral styrene due to raised HOMO of adsorbed styrene.

10.1002_anie.202425634_sup_1.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical radical cyclopropanation of alkenes with diethyl malonate using a ferrocene mediator to form aryl-substituted cyclopropane-1,1-dicarboxylates
10.1002_anie.202425634_sup_1.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation mediated by ferrocene (Fc/Fc+) generates a malonyl radical from diethyl malonate; the radical adds to the tethered alkene and after further oxidation the resulting carbanion (Int3) undergoes intramolecular SN2-type ring closure displacing iodide
10.1002_anie.202425634_sup_1.electron_flow: Ferrocene is oxidized at the anode to Fc+, which oxidizes diethyl malonate to a malonyl radical; this radical adds across the tethered alkene to give a C-radical that is further oxidized/iodinated and deprotonated to Int3, which closes via SN2-type displacement of iodide to form the cyclopropane.

key_intermediates.name: carbon-centered radical adduct after addition to alkene
TEMPO adduct (compound 91) of the C-radical intermediate
iodinated malonate intermediate Int3 (anion after deprotonation)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: TEMPO adduct 91 (C16H29NO5, HRMS m/z 316.2124) was detected, and TEMPO-malonate adduct 92 was also observed; desired cyclopropane product was not detected, supporting a radical intermediate

10.1002_anie.202425634_sup_1.notes: DFT (SMD(DMA/H2O)/ωB97M-V/def2-QZVPP//IEFPCM/M06-2X-D3/ma-SVP) supports an SN2-type ring closure from Int3 (ΔG = -29.7 kcal/mol, ΔG‡ = 7.3 kcal/mol) as thermodynamically and kinetically facile. Theoretical oxidation potentials were calculated to justify the Fc mediator (vs. SHE, with E_SHE^abs = 4.280 V). Alternative non-mediated direct anodic oxidation pathway was considered but mediator pathway is favored.

10.1002_anie.201913332.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical four-component 1,2-difunctionalization of arylalkenes with carboxylic acids, acetonitrile, and alcohols to give functionalized imides via in situ O-acyl isoamide formation and Mumm rearrangement
10.1002_anie.201913332.electrochemical_role: Anodic single-electron oxidation of styrene to a radical cation initiates the cascade; cathodic reduction of methanol and benzoic acid generates methoxide and carboxylate nucleophiles plus H2
10.1002_anie.201913332.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes styrene to radical cation A; methoxide (from cathodic reduction of MeOH) adds to give benzyl radical B, which is further oxidized at the anode to cation C; MeCN traps C as nitrilium D, which is captured by carboxylate (from cathodic reduction of ArCOOH) to form O-acyl isoamide E that undergoes 1,3-(O->N) Mumm rearrangement to the imide.
10.1002_anie.201913332.rate_determining_step: Not the methoxy or acetonitrile incorporation step (excluded by KIE experiments); likely initial anodic oxidation of styrene

key_intermediates.name: nitrilium ion D (Ritter-type) from MeCN trapping

probe_reagents.name: CD3OD
probe_reagents.observation: Parallel reaction with CH3OH/CD3OD (1:1) gave 3ao/d3-3ao = 1:1, indicating methoxy incorporation is not rate-determining

probe_reagents.name: CD3CN
probe_reagents.observation: Parallel reaction with CH3CN/CD3CN (1:1) gave 3ao/d3-3ao = 1:1, indicating MeCN incorporation is not rate-determining

probe_reagents.name: 18O-labeled 4-chlorobenzoic acid
probe_reagents.observation: 18O is incorporated into the imide oxygen of [18O]-3ak, confirming the acyl-O-to-N migration (Mumm rearrangement) pathway

10.1002_anie.201913332.notes: CV showed styrene has the lowest oxidation potential (1.60 V vs SCE), supporting direct anodic oxidation of the alkene rather than the acid or alcohol as the initiating event. The key novelty is electrochemical generation of an otherwise unstable O-acyl isoamide intermediate that spontaneously undergoes Mumm 1,3-acyl O->N migration.

10.1002_anie.202016620.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical three-component radical 1,2-silyl-oxygenation of electron-deficient alkenes with hydrosilanes and N-hydroxy (N-oxyl) compounds to give β-silyl-oxygenated products.
10.1002_anie.202016620.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of N-hydroxyphthalimide (NHPI) to the phthalimido-N-oxyl radical (PINO), which performs HAT on the Si-H bond to generate the silyl radical; protons are reduced at the cathode to H2.
10.1002_anie.202016620.electron_flow: At the anode, NHPI is oxidized (with proton loss) to PINO, which abstracts H from the hydrosilane to give a silyl radical; the silyl radical adds to the electron-deficient alkene to form a β-silyl C-radical, which is trapped by a second N-oxyl radical to deliver the C-O bond, while protons are reduced to H2 at the cathode.

key_intermediates.name: β-silyl carbon radical (Int-1) from silyl addition to alkene

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: 3.0 equiv TEMPO completely suppressed the reaction; product 4a not detected, consistent with radical intermediates.

probe_reagents.name: vinylcyclopropane (substrate 9, dimethyl/ethyl ester-substituted)
probe_reagents.observation: Ring-opened product 10 isolated in 28% yield, supporting a β-silyl C-radical intermediate (Int-1).

probe_reagents.name: NHPI / Ph3SiH (CV study)
probe_reagents.observation: CV showed NHPI has lower oxidation potential than Ph3SiH; a new oxidation peak appeared for the NHPI + Ph3SiH mixture, supporting NHPI-mediated anodic oxidation/HAT.

10.1002_anie.202016620.notes: Mechanism is mediator-based: NHPI/PINO serves as an HAT mediator at the anode to generate silyl radicals from hydrosilanes, avoiding direct silane oxidation. The N-oxyl moiety plays a dual role — anodic mediator and the oxygen-coupling partner that traps the β-silyl C-radical. Cathodic half-reaction is H2 evolution in an undivided cell.

10.1002_anie.202304434.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical [3+2] cycloaddition of nitromethane-derived dipoles with olefins to construct isoxazoline aldoximes
10.1002_anie.202304434.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of I- (from n-Bu4NI) to I•/I2 and of CF3CO2- to •CF3 + CO2; these radicals abstract H from a cathodically reduced nitromethane-derived intermediate (II). Cathode reduces deprotonated nitromethane (aci-form) cleaving N-O bond.
10.1002_anie.202304434.electron_flow: At the anode, I- is oxidized to I•/I2 and CF3CO2- to •CF3 + CO2; at the cathode, the t-BuOK-generated aci-nitromethane (I) is reduced with N-O bond cleavage to give II. Anodic radicals then perform HAT on II to give dipole III, which self-[3+2] dimerizes to 1,2,5-oxadiazole 2-oxide IV; IV undergoes [3+2] with styrene and PT to deliver the isoxazoline aldoxime.
10.1002_anie.202304434.rate_determining_step: Self-[3+2] cycloaddition of III to form IV (DFT activation barrier ~112 kJ/mol), consistent with the 60 °C requirement; the C(sp3)-H cleavage of nitromethane is NOT rate-determining (secondary KIE 0.74).

key_intermediates.name: intermediate II (formaldehyde oxime anion / CH2=N-O-) after N-O cleavage and reduction
intermediate III: HC=N+(O-)(O-) type nitrile oxide/nitronate radical after HAT
[3+2] cycloadduct V between IV and styrene
intermediate VI after N-O cleavage/PT en route to product

probe_reagents.name: d8-styrene (1a-d8)
probe_reagents.observation: Gave 2a-d8 in 87% yield, confirming styrene H/D incorporation into product

probe_reagents.name: CD3NO2 vs CH3NO2 (intermolecular competition)
probe_reagents.observation: kH/kD = 0.74 (avg of four runs); inverse secondary KIE indicates C(sp3)-H cleavage of MeNO2 is not in the RDS and rehybridization occurs

probe_reagents.name: current on/off control
probe_reagents.observation: No reaction without current; electrical input crucial

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of components
probe_reagents.observation: n-Bu4NI Ep_ox = 0.85 V, CF3CO2Na = 1.12 V, 1,4-dioxane = 2.50 V vs Ag/AgCl; styrene (2.53 V) and 2a (2.60 V) are not directly oxidized

probe_reagents.name: HRMS detection of IV
probe_reagents.observation: 1,2,5-oxadiazole 2-oxide intermediate IV detected by HRMS

probe_reagents.name: CO2 detection experiment
probe_reagents.observation: CO2 evolution confirms anodic decarboxylation of CF3CO2-

probe_reagents.name: Control omission of CF3CO2Na, t-BuOK, n-Bu4NI, dioxane
probe_reagents.observation: Each component necessary; no product without n-Bu4NI (no current) or without both CF3CO2Na and t-BuOK

10.1002_anie.202304434.notes: DFT (M06-2X/6-31+G(d,p), LanL2DZ for I, SMD MeNO2) supports HAT from II being easier with I• and •CF3 than with I2, and characterizes the two [3+2] cycloadditions (self-dimerization of III to IV, then with styrene) as concerted but asynchronous. Inverse secondary KIE (0.74) reflects sp3→sp2 rehybridization at the MeNO2 carbon rather than C-H cleavage in RDS.

10.1039_d2ob01883g.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical monofluoroalkylation/cyclization of N-arylacrylamides with dimethyl 2-fluoromalonate to construct monofluorinated 2-oxindoles via a ferrocene-mediated anodic radical pathway.
10.1039_d2ob01883g.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of Cp2Fe to Cp2Fe+ which acts as a SET mediator to oxidize the fluoromalonate carbanion to a monofluoroalkyl radical; cathode reduces MeOH to MeO- and H2.
10.1039_d2ob01883g.electron_flow: At the anode, Cp2Fe is oxidized to Cp2Fe+; at the cathode MeOH is reduced to MeO- + 1/2 H2. MeO- deprotonates dimethyl 2-fluoromalonate to carbanion 31, which Cp2Fe+ oxidizes via SET to monofluoroalkyl radical 32; radical addition to the acrylamide alkene followed by intramolecular cyclization onto the arene gives radical 33, which is oxidized and deprotonated to deliver the monofluorinated oxindole.

key_intermediates.name: Carbon-centered cyclohexadienyl/benzylic radical 33 after radical addition and cyclization onto arene

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of substrate 1, product 3, mediator Cp2Fe, and fluoromalonate 2
probe_reagents.observation: E_p/2: Cp2Fe 0.46 V, carbanion 31 0.45 V, fluoromalonate 2 1.21 V, substrate 1 1.79 V, product 3 1.64 V (vs Ag/AgCl); Cp2Fe+ can oxidize carbanion 31 but not 2, 1, or 3, supporting mediated SET via the deprotonated malonate.

10.1039_d2ob01883g.notes: Mechanism is proposed based on CV data and literature precedent (refs 13,14); no radical trapping (TEMPO/BHT), KIE, or DFT studies are reported. The same mediated monofluoroalkyl radical is invoked for related transformations in the paper including spirocyclization to 2-azaspiro[4.5]decanes, indole[2,1-a]isoquinoline formation, and 1,2-aryl migration from allyl alcohols to 4-fluorobutanones.

10.1039_d4ob00105b.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical anti-Markovnikov hydroxyselenylation of aryl allyl ethers/allyl benzoates with diaryl diselenides to give β-hydroxy selenides under additive-free constant-current electrolysis
10.1039_d4ob00105b.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of diphenyl diselenide (PhSeSePh) at graphite anode to generate phenylselenyl radical and phenylselenyl cation via a diselenide radical cation; cathode (Pt) reduces protons to H2
10.1039_d4ob00105b.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes PhSeSePh to a diselenide radical cation that fragments into PhSe• and PhSe+; PhSe• adds to the terminal alkene of the allyl ether generating a carbon radical, which is further anodically oxidized to a carbocation; water (present in CH3CN, ~10 equiv by Karl Fischer) attacks the cation to deliver the β-hydroxy selenide while protons are reduced at the Pt cathode to H2.
10.1039_d4ob00105b.rate_determining_step: Anodic oxidation of diphenyl diselenide (Ep ≈ 1.40 V vs Ag/AgCl, lower than substrate at 1.75 V) initiates the cycle and is implicated as the triggering step

key_intermediates.name: β-(phenylselanyl) carbon radical adduct (IV) from PhSe• addition to allyl ether
β-(phenylselanyl) benzylic/secondary carbocation (V) after one-electron anodic oxidation of IV
β-hydroxy selenide product 3 after water addition to V

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Product 3 obtained in only 20% yield; TEMPO–PhSe adduct 59 ([M+H]+ = 314.1004) and TEMPO-trapped selenylated alkyl adduct 60 ([M+H]+ = 462.1906) detected by HRMS, supporting Se- and C-centered radical intermediates

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: 3 formed in 35%; BHT-trapped product 61 ([M+H]+ = 525.2287) detected by HRMS

probe_reagents.name: 1,1-diphenylethylene (DPE)
probe_reagents.observation: Only trace 3; PhSe-DPE radical adduct 62 ([M+H]+ = 337.0473) detected, confirming PhSe•

probe_reagents.name: PhSeCl
probe_reagents.observation: Gave product 3 in 30%, consistent with PhSe+ as a competent intermediate

probe_reagents.name: diphenyl disulfide (PhSSPh)
probe_reagents.observation: No sulfur analogue 65 formed (0%), indicating S-S does not behave like Se-Se under these conditions

probe_reagents.name: O2 (balloon)
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction still produced 3 in 95% with no α-seleno carbonyl byproduct, ruling out molecular O2 as the oxygen source

probe_reagents.name: H2O (Karl Fischer titration)
probe_reagents.observation: ~10 equiv water detected in CH3CN, identified as the source of the hydroxyl group in 3

10.1039_d4ob00105b.notes: Cyclic voltammetry shows 2a (PhSeSePh) oxidizes at 1.40 V while 1a oxidizes at 1.75 V, supporting that the diselenide—not the alkene—is oxidized first (distinct from a substrate-radical-cation pathway). PhSeCl serves as a competent PhSe+ surrogate giving 3 in 30%, supporting cation pathway V→3, while PhSSPh fails, indicating Se-specific reactivity. Water (adventitious from CH3CN) is the verified O source rather than O2.

10.1039_d4ob01483a.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical intramolecular oxyselenenylation of alkenyl alcohols with diaryl diselenides to access selenium-functionalized oxygen-containing heterocycles (benzoxepines/benzodioxepines)
10.1039_d4ob01483a.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of diphenyl diselenide (2a) to a diselenide radical cation that fragments to PhSe• and PhSe+; cathode reduces protons to H2
10.1039_d4ob01483a.electron_flow: At the anode, PhSeSePh is oxidized to its radical cation and fragments to PhSe• (and PhSe+/half equiv PhSeSePh); PhSe• adds to the pendant alkene of the alkenyl alcohol to give a C-radical, which is further oxidized at the anode to a carbocation that is trapped intramolecularly by the tethered hydroxyl, followed by deprotonation; the cathode reduces H+ to H2.

key_intermediates.name: carbon-centered radical adduct (Int II) from PhSe• addition to alkene
carbocation (Int III) after anodic oxidation of C-radical
cyclized oxocarbenium/protonated ether prior to deprotonation

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Only trace 3aa; TEMPO–SePh adduct detected by HRMS, supporting PhSe• radical intermediate

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: Only trace 3aa formed, indicating radical pathway

probe_reagents.name: H2O (added)
probe_reagents.observation: Excess water suppresses product formation

probe_reagents.name: PhSSPh (2f)
probe_reagents.observation: No reaction — PhS• not generated under these conditions

probe_reagents.name: PhTeTePh (2g)
probe_reagents.observation: No reaction — PhTe• not generated

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry
probe_reagents.observation: Diselenide 2a oxidizes at lower potential than substrate 1a, indicating 2a is the species oxidized at the anode

10.1039_d4ob01483a.notes: Authors propose alkene functionalization is initiated by anodically generated PhSe• (not by direct oxidation of substrate), supported by CV showing 2a is more easily oxidized than 1a. Alternative S- and Te-based analogues fail, consistent with selectivity for Se radical generation. Water content critically influences yield, suggesting competing hydrolysis/oxidation side pathways.

10.1039_D1OB00236H.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical oxidative selenocyclization of 2-vinylbenzamides with diphenyl diselenide to form selenium-containing isobenzofuran-imines (3a-type products) in a flow microreactor.
10.1039_D1OB00236H.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of diphenyl diselenide (PhSeSePh, 2a) to a cationic radical intermediate that fragments to PhSe radical and PhSe cation; later, anodic oxidation of cyclized intermediate E to cation F
10.1039_D1OB00236H.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes PhSeSePh (2a) to radical cation A, which fragments to PhSe• (B) and PhSe+ (C). PhSe• adds to the vinyl group of 2-vinylbenzamide 1a to give alkyl radical D, which cyclizes intramolecularly via the amide oxygen to E; E is further oxidized at the anode to cation F, which is deprotonated to give product 3a. Cathode reduces protons to H2.

key_intermediates.name: alkyl radical D (benzylic C-radical bearing SePh after PhSe• addition to vinyl)
cyclized intermediate E (isobenzofuran-type with N-H, after intramolecular O-cyclization of D)
intermediate G (cation adduct from PhSe+ + 1a, path b alternative)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction significantly suppressed; yield of 3a dropped to 10% with 3 equiv

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: Yield of 3a reduced to 30% with 3 equiv

probe_reagents.name: 1,1-diphenylethylene (DPE)
probe_reagents.observation: Yield of 3a reduced to 40% with 3 equiv, supporting radical intermediates

probe_reagents.name: diphenyl diselenide (2a)
probe_reagents.observation: CV shows oxidation peak at 1.84 V, lower than 1a at 2.25 V, indicating 2a is preferentially oxidized at the anode

probe_reagents.name: 2-vinylbenzamide 1a
probe_reagents.observation: CV oxidation peak at 2.25 V, higher than 2a

10.1039_D1OB00236H.notes: Two pathways proposed: favored path a (PhSe• radical addition to alkene → alkyl radical D → cyclization → anodic oxidation to F → product) and alternative path b in which PhSe+ cation C reacts with 1a to give cation G, undergoing intramolecular nucleophilic cyclization to F and then dehydrogenation to 3a. The authors cannot exclude path b.

10.1039_c8ob02485e.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical Co-catalyzed oxidative radical annulation of N-arylacrylamides with phenylhydrazines or potassium alkyltrifluoroborates to give 3,3-disubstituted oxindoles.
10.1039_c8ob02485e.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation generates phenyl or alkyl radicals from PhNHNH2 or R-BF3K with Co(II)/Co(III) redox mediation; cathodic reduction of protons gives H2.
10.1039_c8ob02485e.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes Co(II) to Co(III), which oxidizes phenylhydrazine (losing N2 and H+) or R-BF3K to a carbon radical; this radical adds to the acrylamide alkene to give radical A, which cyclizes onto the N-aryl ring to give cyclohexadienyl radical B; B is oxidized at the anode (via Co(III)) to cation C, which loses H+ to give the oxindole, while protons are reduced at the cathode to H2.

key_intermediates.name: phenyl or benzyl radical from anodic oxidation of PhNHNH2 or R-BF3K
A1/A2: tertiary C-radical from addition of Ph/benzyl radical to acrylamide alkene
B1/B2: cyclohexadienyl (aryl) radical after intramolecular ipso/ortho cyclization onto N-aryl ring
C1/C2: cyclohexadienyl cation after anodic SET oxidation of B

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO (TMEPO)
probe_reagents.observation: Only trace product formed with 1.5 equiv TEMPO in both PhNHNH2 and BnBF3K reactions, indicating radical intermediates.
