10.1039_c8ob02485e.notes: Headspace GC analysis confirmed N2 + H2 byproducts for the phenylhydrazine route and H2 byproduct for the benzyltrifluoroborate route, supporting anodic oxidation/cathodic proton reduction. Co(OAc)2/NaOPiv act as an inner-sphere redox mediator (Co(II)/Co(III) cycle) at the anode.

10.1039_D4OB01626B.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical cascade synthesis of five- and six-membered thiazolidine-2-imines via C–S/C–S(Se) bond formation from isothiocyanates, N-2-en-1-amines, and KSCN/KSeCN
10.1039_D4OB01626B.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of SCN- (or SeCN-) anion to thiocyanyl radical (and its dimer (SCN)2); cathodic reduction of protons to H2
10.1039_D4OB01626B.electron_flow: Amine adds to isothiocyanate to form N-allyl thiourea III; at the anode, SCN- is oxidized to SCN• (and dimerizes to (SCN)2). Path a: SCN• adds to the pendant olefin of III to give C-radical IV, which is further anodically oxidized to cation V and cyclized by intramolecular S-attack with deprotonation to product VII. Path b: (SCN)2 acts as electrophile on III to give cation VI plus SCN-, then cyclization. Cathode reduces protons to H2.

key_intermediates.name: N-alkylurea (unsaturated N-allyl thiourea III, from amine addition to isothiocyanate)
Carbon-centered radical IV (thiocyanyl-added to olefin of III)
Cationic intermediate V (after anodic SET of IV)
Cationic intermediate VI (from III + (SCN)2, ionic path b)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Yield dropped to 12%, indicating involvement of radical pathway

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: Yield dropped to 31%, supporting radical intermediate

probe_reagents.name: Cinnamyl-type aryl-substituted N-2-en-1-amine (42)
probe_reagents.observation: Six-membered thiazolidine-2-imine 43 obtained in 76% yield, consistent with stabilized benzylic radical intermediate

probe_reagents.name: Isolated N-alkylurea 41
probe_reagents.observation: Isolated in 92% under standard conditions in 5 min, confirming initial amine + isothiocyanate addition

probe_reagents.name: KSCN (CV probe)
probe_reagents.observation: Oxidation peak at 1.40 V vs Ag/AgCl, attributed to SCN- → SCN• then dimerization to (SCN)2; thiourea 41 showed no oxidation peak

10.1039_D4OB01626B.notes: Authors propose two parallel pathways: a radical path a (SCN• addition then anodic oxidation to cation) and an ionic path b ((SCN)2 acting as electrophile). Both are stated to operate; favored description in scheme starts with path a. Reaction is metal-, oxidant-, and electrolyte-free, undivided cell with graphite electrodes.

10.1039_D4OB01327A.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical aryl-/alkyl-sulfonylation or alkoxysulfonylation of N-tert-butyl N-allylbenzamides coupled with a Truce–Smiles rearrangement to access β-arylethylamine sulfones, sulfonate esters, and sulfonic acids.
10.1039_D4OB01327A.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation generates the sulfonyl radical (from arylsulfonyl hydrazide via SET/deprotonation/N2 loss) or SO2 radical cation (from NaHSO3 via SO2 release and SET); after radical addition and Truce–Smiles rearrangement, the carbamoyl radical is further oxidized at the anode to a cation trapped by methanol, while protons are reduced at the cathode to H2.
10.1039_D4OB01327A.electron_flow: At the anode, the sulfonyl hydrazide (path a) or NaHSO3 (path b) is oxidized to generate a sulfonyl radical (Ia) or SO2 radical cation (Ib), which adds to the allyl alkene to give a β-amino C-radical II; intramolecular ipso-addition onto the N-aryl group (5-exo-trig) yields spirocyclic radical III, which fragments to a carbamoyl radical IV. IV is oxidized at the anode to cation V, trapped by methanol to deliver the methyl carbamate product, while the cathode reduces protons to H2.

key_intermediates.name: sulfonyl radical Ia (ArSO2• or MeSO2•) from anodic oxidation/N2 extrusion of sulfonyl hydrazide
SO2 radical cation Ib generated from NaHSO3 anodic oxidation
γ-sulfonyl β-amino C-centered radical IIa (from sulfonyl radical addition to alkene)
γ-(methoxysulfonyl) β-amino alkyl radical IIb (from SO2/MeOH trapping of alkene)
spirocyclic cyclohexadienyl radical III (5-exo-trig ipso-cyclization intermediate of Truce–Smiles)
carbamoyl N-centered radical IV after C–C bond cleavage / aryl migration
carbamoyl/acylium-type cation V formed by anodic oxidation of IV

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of 1a, sulfonyl hydrazide 2 and NaHSO3
probe_reagents.observation: Ep/2(1a)=+1.49 V, (4-F-C6H4)SO2NHNH2 = +0.93 V, NaHSO3 = +0.67 V vs Fc+/0 in CH3CN/MeOH; sulfur components are oxidized first, supporting SET initiation at the sulfur reagent rather than the amide.

probe_reagents.name: current off-control (Table 1, entry 15)
probe_reagents.observation: Without applied current, no product forms, confirming the essential role of electrolysis.

10.1039_D4OB01327A.notes: A competing ortho-addition pathway of the alkyl radical onto the N-aryl ring (instead of ipso-substitution) was observed with the 2-thiophenyl substrate 1o, giving dihydrothienopyridinone 4n' (46%) alongside the expected Truce–Smiles product 4n (24%). Two parallel mechanistic pathways are proposed: path a (aryl-/alkyl-sulfonylation from ArSO2NHNH2) and path b (alkoxysulfonylation from NaHSO3 + MeOH); both converge at the radical Truce–Smiles step.

10.1039_d4ob01327a.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical aryl-/alkyl-sulfonylation and alkoxysulfonylation of N-tert-butyl-N-allylbenzamides promoted by a radical Truce–Smiles rearrangement to deliver sulfone- and sulfonate-containing β-arylethylamines.
10.1039_d4ob01327a.electrochemical_role: Anodic SET oxidation of the sulfonyl source (arylsulfonyl hydrazide ArSO2NHNH2 to ArSO2• via -3H+/-N2; or bisulfite/metabisulfite to SO2 then SO2•+); the resulting carbamoyl radical after Truce–Smiles is further anodically oxidized to a carbamoyl cation; cathode performs proton reduction to H2.
10.1039_d4ob01327a.electron_flow: At the anode, ArSO2NHNH2 (or HSO3-) is oxidized by SET (losing 3H+/N2, or losing e-/Na+) to a sulfonyl radical ArSO2• or SO2•+; this radical adds regioselectively to the terminal alkene of the N-allylbenzamide to give a β-amino C-radical, which undergoes 5-exo-trig ipso-cyclization onto the N-aroyl arene (Truce–Smiles) to a spirocyclic radical that fragments by C–C cleavage to a carbamoyl radical; this carbamoyl radical is oxidized at the anode to a carbamoyl cation trapped by MeOH to give the methyl carbamate product, while protons are reduced to H2 at the Ni cathode.

key_intermediates.name: arylsulfonyl (or methyl) radical Ia (XSO2•, X = Ar, Me)
sulfur dioxide radical cation Ib (SO2•+) generated from NaHSO3/Na2S2O5 after SET
γ-sulfonyl β-amino C-centered radical IIa (after addition of ArSO2• to alkene)
γ-methylsulfonate-ester β-amino C-centered alkyl radical IIb (after SO2•+/MeOH trapping of alkene)
spirocyclic ipso-cyclohexadienyl radical III (5-exo-trig Truce–Smiles intermediate)
carbamoyl (amidyl-acyl) radical IV after C–C cleavage / aryl migration
carbamoyl cation V (anodic oxidation of IV)
dihydrothienopyridinone 4n' from competing 6-endo ortho-addition of the alkyl radical onto thiophene

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of 1a, (4-fluorophenyl)sulfonyl hydrazine, and NaHSO3
probe_reagents.observation: Ep/2 = 1.49 V (1a), 0.93 V (ArSO2NHNH2), 0.67 V (NaHSO3) vs Fc+/0 in CH3CN/MeOH 6:4, indicating the sulfur components are oxidized preferentially, initiating the reaction via SET on the sulfonyl source rather than the amide.

probe_reagents.name: control without current (Table 1, entry 15)
probe_reagents.observation: No product (0% yield), confirming that electrolysis is essential to generate the sulfonyl/SO2-derived radical.

probe_reagents.name: thiophene-substrate 1o (probe for ipso vs ortho selectivity)
probe_reagents.observation: Gives 4n in only 24% yield together with the 6-endo ortho-addition byproduct 4n' (46%), supporting a radical aryl-migration pathway competitive with cyclization onto the heteroarene.

10.1039_d4ob01327a.notes: Two parallel pathways are proposed in Scheme 4: path a (aryl-/alkyl-sulfonylation) starts from a sulfonyl radical Ia from ArSO2NHNH2 (or MeSO2 source); path b (methoxy/alkoxysulfonylation) starts from SO2•+ (Ib) released from NaHSO3/Na2S2O5 and trapped by MeOH to install a methyl sulfonate ester. Both converge through the Truce–Smiles spirocyclic intermediate III and a carbamoyl radical/cation (IV→V) trapped by methanol. The bulky N-tBu group is invoked to enforce s-cis amide conformation that favors 5-exo ipso-cyclization over a competing 6-endo ortho-addition (the latter observed only with thiophene substrate 1o, giving dihydrothienopyridinone 4n').

10.1039_c9ob00717b.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical vicinal aminotrifluoromethylation of alkenes using CF3SO2Na as CF3 source and acetonitrile as N-nucleophile to give β-trifluoromethylamines via Ritter-type amination.
10.1039_c9ob00717b.electrochemical_role: Anodic single-electron oxidation of CF3SO2Na releases SO2 and generates CF3 radical; the β-CF3 carbon radical intermediate is further oxidized at the anode to a benzylic carbocation; cathode reduces protons to H2.
10.1039_c9ob00717b.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes CF3SO2- to CF3 radical (losing SO2); CF3 radical adds to the alkene giving a benzylic C-radical, which is further oxidized at the anode to a benzylic carbocation; MeCN attacks the cation and water hydrolyzes the nitrilium (Ritter-type) to the β-CF3 acetamide; cathode reduces 2H+ to H2.

key_intermediates.name: nitrilium ion from MeCN attack on carbocation

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction suppressed (3a only trace); CF3-TEMPO adduct 4 detected by HRMS ([M+H]+ = 226.1416). Without electricity, TEMPO fully recovered and no product, showing CF3 radical is generated anodically rather than by TEMPO oxidation.

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: Yield drops to 12%; CF3-BHT adduct 5 ([M+H]+ = 289.1778) and β-CF3-benzyl-BHT adduct 6 ([M+H]+ = 393.2401) detected, supporting both CF3 radical and β-CF3 C-radical intermediates.

probe_reagents.name: D2O (water)
probe_reagents.observation: In ultra-dry MeCN/CH2Cl2 no D-incorporated product; adding 4 equiv H2O (with CD3CN) gives D3-3a in 72% GC yield ([M+H]+ = 235.1124), proving the amide oxygen comes from water and MeCN is the N source.

probe_reagents.name: CD3CN
probe_reagents.observation: D3-labeled acetamide product confirms MeCN incorporation via Ritter-type pathway.

10.1039_c9ob00717b.notes: CV shows oxidation peak of CF3SO2Na at 0.89 V vs Ag/AgCl and a higher peak at 0.93 V when styrene is co-present, supporting anodic oxidation of the sulfinate rather than the alkene. Analogous CF2H reaction with CF2HSO2Na gives the β-CF2H amide (3a', 54%), consistent with the same SET/Ritter pathway. X-ray of 3j confirms regiochemistry (CF3 on terminal carbon, NHAc on benzylic carbon).

10.1039_D5OB00694E.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical radical trifluoromethylation/cyclization of beta,gamma-alkenyl oximes with CF3SO2Na to give CF3-substituted isoxazolines
10.1039_D5OB00694E.electrochemical_role: Anodic single-electron oxidation of CF3SO2- (Langlois reagent) to generate CF3 radical (with loss of SO2); second anodic oxidation of the C-centered radical adduct to a carbocation that cyclizes; cathodic proton reduction to H2 balances charge
10.1039_D5OB00694E.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes CF3SO2- by one electron to give CF3SO2 radical, which loses SO2 to form CF3 radical; CF3 radical adds to the alkene of the beta,gamma-alkenyl oxime to give a carbon radical, which is further oxidized at the anode to a carbocation that undergoes intramolecular O-cyclization by the oxime hydroxyl to deliver the isoxazoline; protons are reduced to H2 at the Pt cathode.

key_intermediates.name: CF3SO2 radical (A) from anodic oxidation of CF3SO2-
CF3 radical (B) after loss of SO2
C-centered radical adduct on alkenyl oxime (C)
Carbocation intermediate (D) after second anodic oxidation

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: 2.0 equiv almost completely suppressed formation of 3aa, indicating a radical pathway

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: 2.0 equiv suppressed the reaction (trace 3aa), supporting radical intermediates

probe_reagents.name: 1,1-diphenylethylene (DPE)
probe_reagents.observation: Yield of 3aa dropped to <10%; CF3-DPE adduct 7 isolated in 55%, providing strong evidence for a free CF3 radical

probe_reagents.name: Cyclopropyl-bearing alkenyl oxime 8
probe_reagents.observation: Neither cyclic nitrone 9 nor ring-opened product 10 was observed (inconclusive radical clock)

probe_reagents.name: CF3SO2Na (2a) CV
probe_reagents.observation: Irreversible oxidation peak at 1.701 V, consistent with anodic generation of CF3 radical

probe_reagents.name: Alkenyl oxime 1a CV
probe_reagents.observation: Oxidation peak at 2.243 V (higher than 2a); mixture 1a+2a shows onset at 1.947 V with increased current, suggesting CF3SO2- is oxidized first

10.1039_D5OB00694E.notes: Radical clock with cyclopropyl substrate 8 was inconclusive (no nitrone 9 nor ring-opened 10 detected). Mechanism proposes radical-polar crossover (radical addition then anodic oxidation to cation) rather than direct radical cyclization; alternative all-radical cyclization pathway is not explicitly ruled out.

10.1039_d0ob00629g.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical intramolecular oxyselenocyclization of 1-allylnaphthalen-2-ols and allylphenols with diaryl diselenides to give selenylated dihydrofurans.
10.1039_d0ob00629g.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of diphenyl diselenide (PhSeSePh) to a selenium-centered radical cation/PhSe radical; cathode reduces protons to H2.
10.1039_d0ob00629g.electron_flow: Anodic oxidation of PhSeSePh gives a Se-centered radical cation that fragments into PhSe• (Pathway I) or PhSe+ (Pathway II); the radical adds to the allyl alkene to form a C-radical that is further oxidized at the anode to a cation, which is trapped by the pendant naphthol OH (Pathway I); alternatively the PhSe+ forms a seleniranium that is opened by the phenol; protons are reduced at the cathode to H2.

key_intermediates.name: benzylic/naphthyl cation after anodic oxidation of D (E)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: 3.0 equiv TEMPO completely inhibits the reaction, supporting a radical pathway (Pathway I).

probe_reagents.name: PhSeBr (with electrolyte, no current)
probe_reagents.observation: Affords 3a in 86% yield without electricity, supporting a PhSe+/seleniranium pathway (Pathway II).

probe_reagents.name: PhSeBr (no current, no nBu4NClO4)
probe_reagents.observation: Still gives 3a in 45%, consistent with electrophilic Se+ intermediate.

probe_reagents.name: Ar atmosphere control
probe_reagents.observation: Under Ar with standard conditions, 3a obtained in 45% (vs 64% in air), indicating air/O2 plays a role but reaction still proceeds.

10.1039_d0ob00629g.notes: Authors propose two parallel pathways: Pathway I (favored, radical) via anodic generation of PhSe•, alkene addition, second anodic oxidation to cation E, then intramolecular O-attack and deprotonation; Pathway II (cannot be ruled out) via PhSe+ cation forming a seleniranium F opened by the phenol. CV showed Epa = 1.63 V for 2a and 1.77 V for 1a (vs NHE), indicating diselenide is oxidized first. Gram-scale (6 mmol) gives 64% of 3a.

10.1039_d2ob00254j.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical radical diselenylation/cyclization of o-divinylbenzenes with diaryl diselenides to give 1,3-diselenyl-1,3-dihydroisobenzofurans
10.1039_d2ob00254j.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of diphenyl diselenide to a radical cation that fragments into PhSe• and PhSe+; subsequent anodic oxidations of benzylic radicals to cations; cathodic reduction of protons to H2
10.1039_d2ob00254j.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes PhSeSePh to a radical cation that fragments into PhSe• and PhSe+; PhSe• adds to one alkene of the o-divinylbenzene to give benzylic radical II, which is anodically oxidized to cation III and trapped by H2O to give alcohol IV; PhSe• then adds to the second alkene giving radical V, which is again anodically oxidized to cation VI and intramolecularly trapped by the pendant hydroxyl to close the dihydroisobenzofuran; protons are reduced to H2 at the Pt cathode.

key_intermediates.name: benzylic C-radical II from PhSe• addition to one vinyl group
benzylic carbocation III (after anodic oxidation of II)
benzylic alcohol intermediate IV (after H2O attack and deprotonation)
C-radical V from PhSe• addition to remaining vinyl
benzylic cation VI (after anodic oxidation of V)

probe_reagents.name: H2 18O labeling
probe_reagents.observation: 18O incorporated into the dihydroisobenzofuran oxygen, indicating the ring oxygen originates from water

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of 1a and 2a
probe_reagents.observation: PhSeSePh (2a) is oxidized at a significantly lower potential than substrate 1a, supporting initial anodic oxidation of the diselenide

probe_reagents.name: N2 atmosphere control
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction proceeds smoothly under N2, indicating O2 is not required as oxidant

10.1039_d2ob00254j.notes: Authors did not explicitly run radical-trap (TEMPO/BHT) experiments in the shown text; mechanism invokes sequential radical addition / anodic oxidation / nucleophilic trapping cycles (radical-polar crossover). X-ray structure of 3aa (CCDC 2143795) confirms product connectivity and diastereochemistry.

10.1039_d4ob00980k.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical oxidative radical cascade sulfonylation/cyclization of N-aryl 2-vinylbenzamides with sodium sulfinates to access 3-sulfonylisoindolin-1-ones
10.1039_d4ob00980k.electrochemical_role: Anodic single-electron oxidation of sulfinate anion (ArSO2-) to sulfonyl radical (ArSO2•); cathode reduces protons to H2
10.1039_d4ob00980k.electron_flow: At the anode, sulfinate (2b) loses one electron to give sulfonyl radical C (via resonance forms A/B); C adds across the alkene of 1a to form benzylic radical D, whose amide resonance form E undergoes intramolecular cyclization onto nitrogen. A second anodic SET gives cation F, which loses H+ to deliver the isoindolin-1-one product 3ab; the cathode produces H2.

key_intermediates.name: Benzylic C-radical D from sulfonyl addition to styrenyl amide
Cyclized cationic iminol intermediate F (after anodic SET)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: 3 equiv TEMPO under standard conditions suppressed product formation (only trace 3aa); supports radical pathway

probe_reagents.name: 1,1-Diphenylethylene (DPE)
probe_reagents.observation: Sulfonyl-DPE adduct 4 detected by NMR in 68%; product 3aa only trace — confirms free sulfonyl radical

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of substrates
probe_reagents.observation: Oxidation potentials measured (ESI) consistent with anodic oxidation of sulfinate

10.1039_d4ob00980k.notes: Gram-scale (3.5 mmol, 1.05 g of 1a) reaction delivered 3aa in 70% yield, supporting scalability. No DFT or KIE performed; mechanism is proposed by analogy to prior radical sulfonylation literature.

10.1039_D5OB00874C.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical amidochlorination of alkenes with amides using DCE as the chlorine source to give 2-chloroalkylamines under undivided cell conditions
10.1039_D5OB00874C.electrochemical_role: Cathodic reduction of DCE generates chloride anion (and ethylene) with concomitant H2 evolution; anodic oxidation of chloride to Cl2/Cl+ which chlorinates the alkene to a chloronium ion intermediate
10.1039_D5OB00874C.electron_flow: At the cathode, DCE accepts electrons stepwise to release two chloride anions and ethylene while protons are reduced to H2; at the anode, chloride is oxidized (−2e−) to Cl2, which adds to the alkene to form a chloronium ion that is opened by the amide nitrogen to give the 2-chloroalkylamide.

key_intermediates.name: chloride anion (from cathodic cleavage of DCE)
molecular chlorine / Cl+ generated at anode

probe_reagents.name: AgNO3 (chloride trapping)
probe_reagents.observation: White AgCl precipitate formed under standard conditions, confirming chloride anion is generated during electrolysis

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry with DCE, styrene (1a), benzamide (2a), nBu4NCl
probe_reagents.observation: Chloride anion shows oxidation peak at ~1.5 V vs Ag/AgCl; styrene and benzamide show no oxidation; DCE shows reduction at ~−1.5 V vs Ag/AgCl, indicating chloride is easily oxidized at the anode and DCE easily reduced at the cathode

probe_reagents.name: Control without current
probe_reagents.observation: No product formed, confirming electrochemistry is essential

probe_reagents.name: Solvent switch to CH3CN or CH3OH
probe_reagents.observation: No product detected, supporting DCE as the chlorine source

probe_reagents.name: Air vs N2 atmosphere
probe_reagents.observation: Yield drops from 85% to 74% under air, indicating mild sensitivity to O2 but not radical-chain dependent

probe_reagents.name: Downstream cyclization with CH3COONa
probe_reagents.observation: 3aa converts to oxazolines 4a/5a (40%/40%), consistent with a 2-chloroalkylamide structure (also confirmed by X-ray of 3aba)

10.1039_D5OB00874C.notes: Authors favor an ionic (chloronium) pathway rather than a radical chain: anodic Cl− oxidation to Cl2 followed by alkene chloriranium formation and amide opening. The 1:1 regioisomeric ratio (3aaa:3aab) observed in optimization is consistent with attack on a symmetric/loose chloronium-like intermediate. DCE acts both as solvent and as sacrificial chlorine source via 2e− cathodic cleavage to 2 Cl− + ethylene.

10.1039_d4ob00805g.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical selenylation-dearomative ipso-cyclization of N-benzylacrylamides with diaryl diselenides to access selenylated 2-azaspiro[4.5]decadienones
10.1039_d4ob00805g.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of diphenyl diselenide (PhSeSePh) generates a PhSe radical and a selenium cation; cathode reduces the cyclohexadienyl radical cation intermediate to the corresponding carbanion that undergoes demethylation to deliver the spirocyclic dienone
10.1039_d4ob00805g.electron_flow: At the anode, (PhSe)2 is oxidized to a radical cation that fragments to PhSe• and PhSe+; PhSe• adds to the acrylamide alkene to give a C-radical that undergoes ipso-cyclization onto the p-methoxyphenyl ring to form a cyclohexadienyl radical, which is oxidized (at the anode) / reduced at the cathode pathway then loses methyl to furnish the dearomatized spirocyclic dienone.

key_intermediates.name: phenylselenyl radical (PhSe•) from anodic oxidation of (PhSe)2
C-centered radical C from PhSe• addition to acrylamide alkene
cyclohexadienyl radical D after ipso-carbocyclization onto the p-methoxyaryl
cyclohexadienyl cation E after SET at cathode (then demethylation)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Formation of spirocycle 3a was inhibited and a TEMPO-SePh adduct 6 was detected by HRMS, supporting PhSe radical intermediacy

probe_reagents.name: No current (electrolysis off)
probe_reagents.observation: No product formation in the absence of current, confirming that electrochemistry is essential

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of 1a and 2a
probe_reagents.observation: Oxidation peak of (PhSe)2 (2a) at 1.18 V vs 1.72 V for acrylamide 1a, indicating diselenide is oxidized preferentially at the anode

10.1039_d4ob00805g.notes: Reaction fails with 4-NO2 substituent on the N-benzyl aryl (electron-poor arene cannot be dearomatized) and with Me2Se2 (no selenyl radical generated). The cathodic step is drawn as SET to convert dienyl radical D to cation/anion E followed by demethylation; the exact polarity of E (cation after anodic oxidation vs anion after cathodic reduction) is ambiguous in the scheme, but the authors invoke cathodic SET.

10.1055_s-0037-1611791.rxn_class_summary: Diastereodivergent bromoiminolactonization of α-allylmalonamides to bromo-substituted iminolactones, performed either electrochemically (with Zn(OTf)2/2,2'-bipyridine and Et4NBr) or chemically (NBS/Et4NBr).
10.1055_s-0037-1611791.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of bromide (Et4NBr) generates an electrophilic brominating species (bromo cation / Br+ equivalent) that is delivered to the alkene; Et4N+ pairs with this bromo cation to modulate selectivity.
10.1055_s-0037-1611791.electron_flow: Bromide is oxidized at the anode (directly or via Et4NBr) to an electrophilic bromo cation that forms a bromonium ion on the allyl group; intramolecular 5-exo attack by the amide oxygen closes the iminolactone with preferential trans diastereoselectivity.

key_intermediates.name: electrochemically generated bromo cation (Br+ equivalent) complexed with Et4N+
bromonium ion on allyl alkene of α-allylmalonamide

probe_reagents.name: NBS / Et4NBr (chemical control)
probe_reagents.observation: Chemical bromocyclization with NBS/Et4NBr also gave trans-2a' preferentially but with lower dr than the electrochemical conditions, supporting that an Et4NBr–bromo cation complex (rather than Zn(OTf)2/bipy alone) governs selectivity.

probe_reagents.name: oxalic acid hydrolysis of 2a/2a'
probe_reagents.observation: Iminolactones 2a/2a' hydrolyzed cleanly to the corresponding lactones 3a (82%) and 3a' (85%) with retention of stereochemistry.

probe_reagents.name: 4-bromobenzenethiol substitution
probe_reagents.observation: Nucleophilic displacement of the C–Br in 2a/2a' afforded thioether iminolactones 4a (91%) and 4a' (92%), confirming the bromide position and configuration.

10.1055_s-0037-1611791.notes: The authors explicitly state that the role of Zn(OTf)2 and 2,2'-bipyridine in the electrochemical reaction remains unclear; they propose that Et4NBr complexes with an anodically generated bromo cation to control diastereoselectivity. No radical traps, CV, or DFT studies are reported on these pages; mechanistic support is indirect (comparison of chemical vs electrochemical bromination and product derivatization). Asymmetric variants and deeper mechanistic studies are stated to be ongoing.

10.1055_s-0039-1689934.rxn_class_summary: Mn-catalyzed electrochemical three-component chlorophosphinoylation (and azidophosphinoylation) of alkenes via anodically coupled electrolysis, delivering vicinal Cl/P(O)R2 across C=C bonds
10.1055_s-0039-1689934.electrochemical_role: Two parallel anodic events mediated by Mn: (i) oxidation of Cl- to a [Mn(III)-Cl] species (latent Cl radical, persistent); (ii) Mn-mediated oxidation of the secondary phosphine oxide H-P(O)R2 to a P-centered radical. Cathode reduces protons to H2.
10.1055_s-0039-1689934.electron_flow: On the anode, Mn(II)/bipy is oxidized and mediates oxidation of Cl- to [Mn(III)-Cl] (persistent) and oxidation of H-P(O)Ph2 to a transient P-radical I; I adds regioselectively to the alkene to give C-radical III, which abstracts Cl from [Mn(III)-Cl] (II) via persistent-radical-effect-controlled cross-coupling, releasing the chlorophosphinoylation product and [Mn(II)], which is re-oxidized at the anode while protons are reduced to H2 at the Pt cathode.

key_intermediates.name: beta-phosphinoyl C-centered radical (III) after P-radical addition to alkene
[Mn(II)] reduced catalyst form after Cl atom transfer

probe_reagents.name: N,N-diallyl tosylamide (radical clock / cyclization probe)
probe_reagents.observation: Forms cyclized pyrrolidine 26 (57%, dr 1:1), consistent with intermediacy of P- and C-centered radicals

probe_reagents.name: TMSN3 (azide source, nucleophile substitution)
probe_reagents.observation: Replaces LiCl to give azidophosphinoylation product 23 (46%), supporting generality of the ACE scheme with different X-

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of Mn(OTf)2/bipy with LiCl and/or phosphine oxide 2
probe_reagents.observation: Phosphine oxide 2 is hard to oxidize directly; Mn+bipy alone shows weak features; addition of HOAc/NaOAc enhances Mn redox activity; LiCl + Mn gives current enhancement (Mn promotes Cl- oxidation, forming [Mn(III)-Cl]); mixing 2 + LiCl + Mn gives marked catalytic current scaling with [2], consistent with [Mn(III)-Cl] oxidizing 2 to P-radical I

10.1055_s-0039-1689934.notes: Selectivity rationalized by persistent radical effect: transient P-radical I adds to alkene faster than persistent [Mn(III)-Cl] (II); resulting C-radical III then cross-couples with persistent II rather than dimerizing or reacting with another I, ensuring chemoselective heterodifunctionalization over bisphosphinoylation. Alternative direct alkene oxidation (radical cation) pathway is explicitly disfavored due to high oxidation potentials of simple alkenes.

10.1055_s-0043-1775373.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical selenylation/cyclization of ortho-hydroxyaryl alkenes with diselenides to give selenyl-dihydrobenzofurans (and a chromane) bearing a tetrasubstituted carbon center
10.1055_s-0043-1775373.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of the diaryl diselenide (PhSeSePh, 2a) to a radical cation that fragments into PhSe+ (cation B) and PhSe• (radical C); the PhSe+ engages the alkene as a seleniranium electrophile
10.1055_s-0043-1775373.electron_flow: The anode oxidizes PhSeSePh (lower oxidation potential than 1a per CV) to a radical cation that splits into PhSe+ (B) and PhSe• (C); B forms a seleniranium with the alkene (D), and intramolecular phenol attack with loss of H+ delivers the dihydrobenzofuran 3a. In parallel, PhSe• adds to the alkene to give radical E, which is anodically oxidized to D (or PhSe• is oxidized at the anode to regenerate B), while protons are reduced at the cathode to H2.

key_intermediates.name: β-selenyl carbon radical E (from PhSe• addition to alkene)

probe_reagents.name: PhSeCl (stoichiometric, no current)
probe_reagents.observation: Delivers 3a in 91% yield without electricity, supporting a seleniranium (PhSe+) intermediate

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: No effect on yield (3a still 84%), indicating free carbon radicals are not on the dominant pathway

10.1055_s-0043-1775373.notes: Authors favor Path 1 (anodic oxidation of diselenide to PhSe+, then seleniranium cyclization), consistent with PhSeCl control and TEMPO insensitivity. Two alternative pathways are proposed: Path 2 — PhSe• addition to alkene forming radical E, then anodic oxidation to seleniranium D; Path 3 — anodic oxidation of PhSe• to PhSe+ to re-enter the cationic cycle, with cathodic H2 evolution closing the redox balance.

10.1055_s-0036-1589051.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical synthesis of β-hydroxysulfones from α-methylstyrenes and sodium sulfinates via iodide-mediated anodic generation of sulfonyl iodide, sulfonyl radical addition, and oxidation/hydration of a benzylic cation
10.1055_s-0036-1589051.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of iodide (I-) to I2 at the glassy carbon electrode; I2 then reacts with sulfinate (TolSO2Na, 5a) to give the sulfonyl iodide mediator (TolSO2I, intermediate I), which homolyzes to a sulfonyl radical that adds to the styrene; the resulting benzylic radical is further oxidized (loss of e-) at the anode to a tertiary benzylic carbocation
10.1055_s-0036-1589051.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes I- to I2; I2 reacts with sulfinate to give TolSO2I, which homolyzes into a sulfonyl radical (adding to the alkene) and an iodine radical (regenerating I2 catalytic cycle). The resulting benzylic radical loses a second electron at the anode to form a tertiary carbocation, which is trapped by water to deliver the β-hydroxysulfone.

key_intermediates.name: benzylic carbon radical from sulfonyl addition to α-methylstyrene (intermediate II)

probe_reagents.name: electron-rich styrene 7d (3,4-dimethoxy substituted)
probe_reagents.observation: Tolerates dehydration; isolated as allyl sulfone 9 in 91% yield, consistent with intermediate carbocation III that can eliminate when stabilized

probe_reagents.name: simple styrene 10
probe_reagents.observation: Gave β-hydroxysulfone 11 in 90% yield, supporting general applicability of the proposed cation/hydration pathway

10.1055_s-0036-1589051.notes: The mechanism is presented as 'possible' (Scheme 4) and supported indirectly by product distributions: electron-rich substrates give the allyl sulfone via dehydration of the carbocation III, consistent with a cation intermediate. No radical traps (TEMPO/BHT), KIE, CV, or DFT data are reported on these mechanism pages. X-ray crystallography was used to confirm product structure, not mechanism.

10.1055_s-0043-1763695.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical trans-chloroformyloxylation of unactivated cyclic and acyclic alkenes using a diaryl diselenide catalyst, Bu4NCl as chloride source, and DMF as both solvent and formyloxy source
10.1055_s-0043-1763695.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of the diaryl diselenide (or PhSeCl) catalyst generates an electrophilic PhSe+ species that activates the alkene; the chloride/DMF then close the cycle to deliver trans-1,2-functionalization
10.1055_s-0043-1763695.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes the diaryl diselenide/PhSeCl catalyst to a PhSe+ electrophile, which adds to the alkene to form a seleniranium ion; trans-opening by chloride (or by DMF followed by hydrolysis to the formate) installs Cl and the formyloxy group on opposite faces, regenerating the selenium catalyst.

key_intermediates.name: electrophilic phenylselenenyl cation (PhSe+) generated by anodic oxidation
seleniranium ion (bridged episelenonium from alkene + PhSe+)
beta-chloro alkyl phenyl selenide after chloride opening of seleniranium
trans-1,2-chloro formate ester product (DMF-derived formyloxy + Cl across alkene)

probe_reagents.name: water additive (5–50 equiv) screening
probe_reagents.observation: in presence of water chlorohydrins are formed as side products; anhydrous DMF is required to obtain the trans-chloroformyloxylation product (DMF acts as O-nucleophile that is then hydrolyzed to the formate)

probe_reagents.name: diaryl diselenide electronic/steric variants (3a–d)
probe_reagents.observation: screened to probe steric and electronic effects on the selenium catalyst; PhSeCl proved superior, requiring 50 mol% loading

10.1055_s-0043-1763695.notes: Mechanism is proposed by analogy to Denmark's diselenide-catalyzed cis-dichlorination (which invokes a PhSe+ seleniranium with double SN2 by Cl-). Here the electrochemical variant replaces the chemical oxidant with the anode; trans-selectivity arises from single nucleophilic ring-opening by Cl- followed by DMF capture (then hydrolytic workup to formate). With 1,1-disubstituted alkenes vinyl chlorides are obtained, and 1-phenylcyclohex-1-ene gives an allyl chloride, consistent with a cationic seleniranium/carbocation manifold rather than radical addition. No TEMPO/BHT/KIE/CV/DFT studies are reported on these pages.

10.1055_s-0043-1775467.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical oxidative α-functionalization of ketones via anodic oxidation of silyl enol ethers, enabling C-C bond formation with 1,3-dicarbonyls, α-chlorination, and oxidative [3+2] cycloaddition with styrenes
10.1055_s-0043-1775467.electrochemical_role: Anodic single-electron oxidation of the silyl enol ether to a radical cation; a second anodic oxidation of the α-radical intermediate gives an oxocarbenium/oxonium that loses TMS to deliver the α-functionalized ketone. Cathode reduces protons to H2.
10.1055_s-0043-1775467.electron_flow: The anode performs single-electron oxidation of the silyl enol ether (Eox ≈ 1.8 V, lower than malonate at 2.06 V) to give radical cation B; deprotonated malonate attacks the α-carbon to form radical intermediate C, which is oxidized again at the anode to oxonium D; loss of TMS yields the α-functionalized ketone while the cathode evolves H2.

key_intermediates.name: α-carbon radical adduct after nucleophilic attack of deprotonated malonate (C)

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of enol ether 14 vs dimethyl malonate
probe_reagents.observation: Enol ether oxidizes at 1.8 V while Me-malonate oxidizes at 2.06 V, supporting initial anodic oxidation of the enol ether

probe_reagents.name: stoichiometric (1 equiv) Me-malonate experiment
probe_reagents.observation: Formation of ketone dimer side product 18 (confirmed by GCMS), consistent with radical-cation intermediate B reacting with another enol ether

probe_reagents.name: cyclopropyl ring-opening clock (literature precedent, Moeller/Wright)
probe_reagents.observation: Cited prior work establishing enol ether radical cation formation by anodic oxidation

10.1055_s-0043-1775467.notes: Free ketone is not observed in the reaction mixture, arguing against a pathway via ketone enolization; instead the silyl enol ether is the entry point. The mechanism is shown to be generalizable to α-chlorination (with LiCl as Cl source) and oxidative [3+2] cycloaddition with styrene, both presumed to proceed through the same radical-cation intermediate B.

10.1055_a-2025-1822.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical 1,2-bromo-alkoxylation of alkenes using CH2Br2 as bromine source and alcohol as alkoxy source/solvent in an undivided cell to afford β-bromoethers
10.1055_a-2025-1822.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of Br- (released from CH2Br2 C-X cleavage) to Br• radical, which adds to the alkene; the resulting C-radical is further oxidized at the anode to a benzylic carbocation; at the cathode, protons are reduced to H• (H2 evolution) and +CH2Br is reduced to give CH3Br
10.1055_a-2025-1822.electron_flow: C-X bond cleavage of CH2Br2 releases Br- and +CH2Br; anodic oxidation of Br- gives Br• which adds to the alkene to form a benzylic radical; a second anodic oxidation converts this radical to a benzylic carbocation that is trapped by ROH to give an oxonium, which deprotonates to the β-bromoether. At the cathode, H+ is reduced to H• (giving H2) and +CH2Br is reduced to CH3Br.

key_intermediates.name: +CH2Br bromomethyl cation from CH2Br2 C-X cleavage
benzylic C-centered radical from Br• addition to alkene (II)
benzylic carbocation from anodic oxidation of II (III)
alkyl oxonium salt from ROH attack on carbocation (IV)
hydrogen radical H• from cathodic reduction of H+
CH3Br byproduct from H•/+CH2Br combination at cathode

probe_reagents.name: BHT (2,6-di-tert-butyl-4-methylphenol)
probe_reagents.observation: Yield of 1a drastically diminished to 45%; BHT-Br adduct detected by GC-MS (m/z = 299), supporting a free-radical (Br•) intermediate

probe_reagents.name: 1,1-diphenylethylene (substrate)
probe_reagents.observation: CV oxidation peak at 2.48 V vs Ag/AgCl, higher than CH2Br2 (1.93 V), indicating CH2Br2/Br- is oxidized preferentially

probe_reagents.name: CH2Br2
probe_reagents.observation: CV oxidation peak at 1.93 V vs Ag/AgCl, assigned to Br- → Br• oxidation

probe_reagents.name: no-current control
probe_reagents.observation: No product formation without applied current, confirming electrochemical nature

10.1055_a-2025-1822.notes: Cathodic chemistry (H• generation and +CH2Br reduction to CH3Br) is proposed in parallel with the anodic catalytic cycle, making this effectively a paired electrolysis. iPrOH and tBuOH failed as coupling partners, suggesting steric limits on the oxonium/nucleophilic addition step. No KIE or DFT studies were performed.

10.1055_s-0040-1720135_si13.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical triacetoxylation of allylbenzenes (alkylbenzenes bearing a terminal alkene) to give 1,2,3-triacetoxy products via anodic oxidation and acetate trapping
10.1055_s-0040-1720135_si13.electrochemical_role: Anodic single-electron oxidation of the allylarene to an alkene radical cation; subsequent anodic oxidation of a β-acetoxy carbon radical to a cation/acyloxonium; cathode reduces protons to H2
10.1055_s-0040-1720135_si13.electron_flow: The anode performs SET on the allylarene to generate an alkene radical cation that is captured by acetate to give a β-acetoxy benzylic radical; a second anodic oxidation produces a cation that closes to a methyl-substituted 1,3-dioxolan-2-ylium, which is opened by acetate to the 1,2-diacetate, and a further anodic oxidation/deprotonation followed by acetate trapping installs the third OAc. Protons are reduced to H2 at the cathode.

key_intermediates.name: 1,3-dioxolan-2-ylium (acyloxocarbenium) intermediate D with Me at C2
benzylic radical F after further anodic oxidation/H loss

probe_reagents.name: 1,3-diacetoxy isomer 6a (putative intermediate)
probe_reagents.observation: Resubjection of 6a under standard conditions gave 2a in 68%; without electricity 2a was not detected — supports 6a as a competent intermediate requiring anodic oxidation

probe_reagents.name: cinnamyl acetate 7a
probe_reagents.observation: Decomposed under standard conditions, no 2a formed — excludes allylic acetate H as on-cycle intermediate

probe_reagents.name: internal alkene β-methylstyrene 1a'
probe_reagents.observation: Gave 1,2-diacetate 3a in 75% but no triacetate 2a — suggests terminal alkene geometry is required for triacetoxylation

probe_reagents.name: para-CF3 allylbenzene 1p
probe_reagents.observation: Gave 2p (22%) and 1,3-diacetate 3p (6%); electron-poor arene lowers yield, consistent with arene-assisted oxidation

probe_reagents.name: cyclopropyl-substituted allylarene (radical clock)
probe_reagents.observation: No ring-opened products from a vinylcyclopropane substrate; ring-opened acyclic triacetate 2v formed in 7% (1.4:1 dr), consistent with a radical intermediate adjacent to the cyclopropane

probe_reagents.name: in situ GC-MS time course
probe_reagents.observation: GC-MS at 2.5 h detected 1,2-diacetate, 1,3-diacetate (6a), propylbenzene/alkene byproducts, and triacetate 2a, supporting stepwise build-up via diacetate intermediates

10.1055_s-0040-1720135_si13.notes: Authors disfavor a pathway through cinnamyl acetate H (allylic C–H acetoxylation product), since independently prepared 7a decomposes under the conditions and does not yield 2a. The 1,3-diacetate 6a is shown to be a productive on-cycle intermediate (requires current), implicating a 1,3-dioxolan-2-ylium (acyloxonium) opening by acetate. Internal alkene 1a' diverts to the 1,2-diacetate 3a only, indicating the third acetoxylation requires the terminal-alkene-derived radical/cation manifold. A minor amount of ring-opened triacetate from a cyclopropyl probe (2v, 7%) supports radical character at the benzylic position.

10.1055_s-0030-1260794.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical aryl radical cyclization-carboxylation (ERCC) of 2-allyloxybromobenzenes with CO2 using methyl 4-tert-butylbenzoate as an electron-transfer mediator and a sacrificial Mg anode to give 2,3-dihydrobenzofuran-3-ylacetic acids
10.1055_s-0030-1260794.electrochemical_role: Cathodic one-electron reduction of methyl 4-tert-butylbenzoate (4) at Pt cathode generates its radical anion, which selectively transfers one electron to the aryl bromide to produce an aryl radical (avoiding two-electron reduction to the aryl anion); Mg anode is sacrificially oxidized to Mg2+
10.1055_s-0030-1260794.electron_flow: At the Pt cathode, mediator 4 is reduced to its radical anion, which transfers one electron to the aryl bromide to expel Br- and form an aryl radical; the aryl radical undergoes 5-exo-trig cyclization onto the pendant allyl group, the resulting alkyl radical is further reduced at the cathode to a carbanion, which is trapped by CO2 to give a carboxylate (stabilized by Mg2+ from the sacrificial Mg anode).
10.1055_s-0030-1260794.rate_determining_step: mediated single-electron transfer from the radical anion of 4 to the aryl bromide (generation of aryl radical)

key_intermediates.name: radical anion of methyl 4-tert-butylbenzoate (mediator 4 reduced form)
aryl radical from 2-allyloxybromobenzene after Br- loss
aryl anion (off-pathway, direct 2e- reduction without mediator)

probe_reagents.name: methyl 4-tert-butylbenzoate (mediator 4)
probe_reagents.observation: CV alone in DMF shows reversible reduction at -2.9 V vs Ag/Ag+; addition of 0.5 equiv 1a increases reduction peak current and decreases oxidation peak; with 1.0 equiv 1a the oxidation peak disappears, consistent with catalytic mediator behavior generating aryl radicals from ArBr

probe_reagents.name: 2-allyloxybromobenzene (1a)
probe_reagents.observation: Irreversible reduction at -3.2 V vs Ag/Ag+ in DMF (direct reduction much harder than mediated pathway)

10.1055_s-0030-1260794.notes: Without mediator 4, direct two-electron cathodic reduction of ArBr competes to give an aryl anion that is carboxylated before cyclization, leading to benzoic acid byproducts. With alkyne substrate 15, β-selective overcarboxylation of the initially formed α,β-unsaturated acid gives dicarboxylic acid 16 as the sole product. The Mg sacrificial anode is essential to prevent anodic oxidation of carbanion/carboxylate intermediates and to stabilize them as Mg carboxylates.

10.1055_a-2508-9744.rxn_class_summary: Aggregation-induced electrochemical boronation–dihydroxylation of alkenes using water-soluble boronic acids as hydroxyl source in full aqueous media without transition-metal catalyst
10.1055_a-2508-9744.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of the lipophilic alkene aggregated at the hydrophobic electrode surface to generate a carbon-centered radical (then cation after second oxidation); the hydroxyboronate anion (R-B(OH)3-) supplies the hydroxyl via C-O bond formation at the aggregation layer
10.1055_a-2508-9744.electron_flow: Alkene aggregates at the anode where surface tension drives concentration; anode performs single-electron oxidation of the alkene to a radical, which is captured by the hydroxyboronate anion to form the first C-O bond; a second anodic oxidation gives a cation that is trapped by a second hydroxyl from boronate, yielding the cyclic boronate/diol product which diffuses into the aqueous phase.

key_intermediates.name: alkene aggregated layer at lipophilic anode
anodic carbon radical from alkene one-electron oxidation
β-boronate-alkyl radical after first C-O bond formation

10.1055_a-2508-9744.notes: Mechanism is presented as a proposed aggregation model (Scheme 2) emphasizing the well-defined diffusion regime: lipophilic alkene aggregates at the anode while hydrophilic boronic acid is shielded by water; the anionic product gains aqueous solubility and diffuses away, regenerating the aggregation site. Only the mechanism-introduction pages were provided, so no probe experiments (TEMPO, KIE, CV, DFT) are documented here even if performed later in the paper.

10.1055_a-2006-1285.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical reductive alkylation of buta-1,3-dienes with alkyl halides using a sacrificial iron anode and nickel cathode to deliver trans-olefins with high stereoselectivity
10.1055_a-2006-1285.electrochemical_role: Cathodic single-electron reduction of alkyl halide to aliphatic C-centered radical; subsequent cathodic reduction of allyl radical intermediate to allyl carbanion; iron plate serves as sacrificial anode
10.1055_a-2006-1285.electron_flow: At the cathode, the alkyl halide undergoes SET to give an aliphatic C-radical (A), which adds to the 1,3-diene to form an allyl radical (B/C); a second cathodic SET reduces this allyl radical to an allyl carbanion (D), which nucleophilically displaces halide from a second R-X (or is protonated) to deliver the trans-olefin, while the Fe anode is oxidized to Fe(II) as the sacrificial counter-process.
10.1055_a-2006-1285.rate_determining_step: cathodic SET reduction of the alkyl halide to the carbon-centered radical (Ep = -2.58 V vs SCE for 4-iodotetrahydro-2H-pyran)

key_intermediates.name: aliphatic carbon-centered radical (A) from R-X reduction
allyl radical adduct (B) from radical addition to 1,3-diene
allyl carbanion (D) after second cathodic reduction

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of 4-iodotetrahydro-2H-pyran
probe_reagents.observation: Ep = -2.58 V vs SCE, consistent with cathodic reduction generating an aliphatic C-radical

probe_reagents.name: divided-cell experiment
probe_reagents.observation: Product 3a formed in 70% yield in cathode chamber (and 5a in 64%), confirming the productive chemistry occurs at the Ni cathode while Fe anode acts only as sacrificial anode

probe_reagents.name: alkyl halide leaving group variation (I vs Br vs Cl)
probe_reagents.observation: Iodides give highest yields, bromides moderate, chlorides typically 0%, consistent with ease of SET reduction tracking C-X BDE

10.1055_a-2006-1285.notes: Phenyl-substituted 1,3-dienes (4) incorporate only a single alkyl group (giving 5) because the benzylic allyl carbanion D is stabilized by the aryl group and is protonated rather than alkylated by a second R-X; unsubstituted buta-1,3-diene incorporates two alkyl groups (giving 3). The trans-selectivity (>20:1) is rationalized by the thermodynamically favored E-allyl carbanion/radical geometry. No TEMPO/BHT radical trapping experiments are reported; mechanism is supported primarily by CV and divided-cell experiments plus literature precedent.

10.1021_acscentsci.1c00328.rxn_class_summary: Development of a high-throughput electrochemistry platform (HTe-Chem) demonstrated on reductive ketone-olefin coupling, oxidative phenolic C-H/C-H coupling, and reductive coupling of iminium chlorides with cyanopyridines to give hindered primary amines.
10.1021_acscentsci.1c00328.electrochemical_role: Cathodic reduction generates ketyl radicals (from ketone 14) and α-amino radicals (from iminium chlorides 21a-d) plus cyanopyridine radical anions (22-27); in the Waldvogel oxidative C-H/C-H case, anodic ECE oxidation of phenol 17 generates a p-quinone methide that pairs with cathodically generated hexafluoroisopropoxide.
10.1021_acscentsci.1c00328.electron_flow: In the ketone-olefin coupling, the cathode reduces ketone 14 to a ketyl radical (ECEC) which adds to alkene 15; the resulting C-radical is further reduced and protonated to give 16. In the cyanopyridine coupling, the cathode independently generates an α-amino radical and a cyanopyridine radical anion that couple, then lose H+/CN- to deliver the hindered amine. In the Waldvogel paired electrolysis, the anode oxidizes phenol 17 (ECE) to a p-quinone methide while HFIP is deprotonated/reduced to hexafluoroisopropoxide; the two intermediates combine to give 20, which then undergoes TFA-promoted SN with arene 18 to give 19.

key_intermediates.name: C-centered radical after ketyl addition to allylbenzene 15
p-quinone methide electrophile from phenol 17 (ECE oxidation)
α-amino radical from cathodic reduction of iminium chloride 21
cyanopyridine radical anion (proton-coupled cathodic reduction of 22-27)

10.1021_acscentsci.1c00328.notes: Mechanisms are taken from the cited original reports (Baran/Minteer 2020 ref 49; Waldvogel 2018 ref 50; Lehnherr/Rovis 2019 ref 15) rather than newly probed here; this paper's focus is translating those reactions to the HTe-Chem high-throughput platform and screening electrode/current parameters. The benzyl hexafluoroisopropyl ether 20 is isolated as a key intermediate in the Waldvogel pathway, supporting the paired-electrolysis ECE proposal. Lower current densities (0.5 mA, 2 F) were found optimal in the amine coupling to suppress diamine side-products, consistent with the radical-radical coupling pathway.

10.1038_s41467-026-68437-w.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical cobalt-catalyzed asymmetric hydroacylation of 1,6-enynes with biaryl dialdehydes by a desymmetrization strategy, constructing remote dual stereocenters (central/axial, central/planar chirality).
10.1038_s41467-026-68437-w.electrochemical_role: Cathodic reduction of [(S,S)-BDPP]Co(II)Br2 precatalyst to a low-valent chiral Co(I) active species (E1/2 Co(II)/Co(I) = -0.56 V by CV); current is required for catalyst turn-on/regeneration.
10.1038_s41467-026-68437-w.electron_flow: Cathode reduces Co(II)Br2/BDPP to Co(I), which oxidatively cyclizes the 1,6-enyne to a Co(III) metallacycle (sets first stereocenter); migratory insertion of the prochiral dialdehyde sets the second stereocenter, then β-hydride elimination and C-C reductive elimination release the ketone product and regenerate Co(I).
10.1038_s41467-026-68437-w.rate_determining_step: Not C-H cleavage of the aldehyde (kH/kD ≈ 1.0); turnover-limiting step is proposed to be the oxidative cyclization / migratory insertion stage rather than aldehyde C-H activation.

key_intermediates.name: Co(III) metallacycle from enantioselective oxidative cyclization of 1,6-enyne (Int-II)
Co(III) cobaltacycle after migratory insertion of dialdehyde C-H/C=O (Int-III)
Co(III)-alkyl/acyl species prior to β-hydride elimination (Int-IV)
Diene byproduct 10 from enyne + EtOH (cobaltacycle protonation product)

probe_reagents.name: D-1a (90% D-labeled dialdehyde)
probe_reagents.observation: Product D-3a obtained with 90% D incorporation, confirming aldehyde C-H is transferred to the alkene carbon of the product.

probe_reagents.name: D-1a + 1c crossover mixture
probe_reagents.observation: Deuterium incorporated exclusively into D-3a (from D-1a), no crossover to 3q; rules out intermolecular ligand-to-ligand hydrogen transfer (intramolecular pathway).

probe_reagents.name: 1a + D-1a parallel KIE
probe_reagents.observation: kH/kD ≈ 1.0, indicating C-H cleavage of the aldehyde is not rate-limiting.

probe_reagents.name: Ethanol (proton source) with enyne 2a
probe_reagents.observation: Affords diene 10 (31%, 38% e.e.), trapping the cobaltacycle and supporting an oxidative cyclization pathway.

probe_reagents.name: Truncated substrates 11 (no alkene) and 12 (no alkyne)
probe_reagents.observation: No hydroacylation products formed, indicating both π-components are needed for oxidative cyclization (consistent with cobaltacycle Int-II).

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of [(S,S)-BDPP]CoBr2
probe_reagents.observation: Co(II)/Co(I) couple at -0.56 V supports cathodic generation of low-valent Co(I) under the applied current.

probe_reagents.name: Control without current
probe_reagents.observation: No product; electricity is essential, consistent with electrocatalytic Co(I) generation.

probe_reagents.name: X-ray diffraction of 3a and 9a
probe_reagents.observation: Confirms absolute configuration of axial/planar-central chirality products.

probe_reagents.name: DFT rotational barrier of C-C axis in 3a
probe_reagents.observation: 32.7 kcal/mol barrier (t1/2 ≈ 112 years), confirming atropostability.

10.1038_s41467-026-68437-w.notes: An alternative ligand-to-ligand hydrogen transfer (LLHT) pathway was explicitly excluded by the crossover experiment. The catalyst exerts dual stereocontrol in two distinct elementary steps: oxidative cyclization sets the central stereocenter on the pyrrolidine, while migratory insertion into the prochiral dialdehyde sets the remote C-C axial / C-O axial / [2.2]paracyclophane planar / ferrocene planar chirality (desymmetrization). The proposal closely parallels Lautens's Co-catalyzed hydroacylation cycle.

10.1038_s41467-026-68437-w_si1.rxn_class_summary: Cobalt-catalyzed electroreductive [3+2] coupling of biaryl bialdehydes with 1,6-enynes to form pyrrolidine-fused exocyclic alkenyl ketones.
10.1038_s41467-026-68437-w_si1.electrochemical_role: Cathodic reduction of Co(II) precatalyst (with sacrificial Zn anode) generates a low-valent Co(I) species that initiates oxidative addition into the aldehyde C(acyl)-H bond.
10.1038_s41467-026-68437-w_si1.electron_flow: The cathode reduces Co(II) to Co(I), which oxidatively adds into the aldehyde C(acyl)-H bond to give a Co(III)-acyl hydride; sequential migratory insertions across the alkyne and then the alkene of the 1,6-enyne transfer the hydride to the alkyl terminus, and final C-H reductive elimination releases the product and regenerates Co(I). The Zn anode supplies the balancing oxidation.
10.1038_s41467-026-68437-w_si1.rate_determining_step: Migratory insertion / C-H bond-forming step is implicated; oxidative addition of the aldehyde C-H is not rate-limiting (kH/kD ≈ 1.0).

key_intermediates.name: Co(I)L* active catalyst generated by cathodic reduction of [(S,S)-BDPP]CoBr2
Co(III)-acyl hydride from oxidative addition into aldehyde C-H of bialdehyde 1a (intermediate A)
Alkenyl-Co(III) species after migratory insertion of alkyne of 1,6-enyne 2a (intermediate B)
Alkyl-Co(III) species after intramolecular migratory insertion of the pendant alkene (intermediate C/E)
Pyrrolidine-fused alkenyl ketone product 3a after C-H reductive elimination

probe_reagents.name: Mono-deuterated bialdehyde D-1a (90% D at one CHO)
probe_reagents.observation: Product D-3a obtained in 72% yield with 90% D incorporation specifically at the exocyclic CH2D position, confirming transfer of the aldehyde H/D to the terminal sp3 carbon of the enyne-derived pyrrolidine.

probe_reagents.name: Crossover of D-1a with non-deuterated bialdehyde 1c
probe_reagents.observation: D-3a (63%, 90% D) and 3q (66%, 0% D) formed without scrambling, indicating intramolecular H/D transfer with no intermolecular crossover.

probe_reagents.name: Parallel KIE using 1a + D-1a
probe_reagents.observation: kH/kD ≈ 1.0, indicating that cleavage of the aldehyde C-H bond is not the turnover-limiting step.

10.1038_s41467-026-68437-w_si1.notes: An alternative pathway involving Co(I)/Co(III) oxidative addition into the aldehyde C-H, followed by alkyne migratory insertion (B), alkene migratory insertion (C/E via D), and reductive elimination is depicted in Supplementary Figure 6 as the favored cycle consistent with the intramolecular H/D transfer and unity KIE. A radical chain or outer-sphere SET pathway is not invoked.

10.1038_s41467-023-44025-0.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical gold-catalyzed intramolecular aminoarylation/oxyarylation of unactivated alkenes using aryl hydrazines as aryl electrophile surrogates, achieved through an oxidation-relay strategy that enables gold pi-acid activation under anodic conditions.
10.1038_s41467-023-44025-0.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of aryl hydrazine (ArNHNH2.HOTf) generates an aryl radical/cation that oxidizes L-Au(I)Cl directly to [L-Au(III)-Ar], bypassing the L-Au(I)-Ar transmetalation intermediate; cathode performs proton reduction to H2 under acidic conditions (or O2 reduction to HO- under basic conditions).
10.1038_s41467-023-44025-0.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes ArNHNH2.HOTf via denitrogenation to give an aryl radical (or aryl cation), which converts Ph3P-Au(I)Cl directly into [Ph3P-Au(III)-Ar]; the Au(III) species then pi-activates the pendant alkene of the substrate, intramolecular NuH (NHTs or OH) attacks anti to gold, and C(sp3)-C(sp2) reductive elimination delivers the aminoarylation/oxyarylation product while regenerating Au(I); the cathode reduces H+ to H2.
10.1038_s41467-023-44025-0.rate_determining_step: Oxidation of Au(I) to Au(III) (turnover-limiting in the related diaryl-coupling system); in the relay design, oxidation of hydrazine to aryl radical initiates the cycle.

key_intermediates.name: Ph3P-Au(I)Cl (gold catalyst resting state under acidic conditions)
aryl radical (from oxidative denitrogenation of ArNHNH2)
[L-Au(III)-Ar] (key pi-acid active species formed via oxidation relay)
Au(III)-alkene pi-complex (after coordination of pendant alkene)
beta-aryl-Au(III) alkyl intermediate after nucleophilic anti-attack on pi-complex

probe_reagents.name: aryl boronic acid (ArB(OH)2) under EAO conditions
probe_reagents.observation: Under standard EAO conditions gives only diaryl coupling product 4aa (>95%) with 0% alkene aminoarylation, confirming fast transmetalation to L-Au(I)-Ar followed by reductive elimination and no pi-activation pathway.

probe_reagents.name: 1:1 mixture of p-tBu-C6H4-B(OH)2 and p-COOMe-C6H4-B(OH)2
probe_reagents.observation: Dominant homo-coupling of electron-rich p-tBu substrate (60% tBu-tBu, <5% cross, 0% COOMe-COOMe) indicates Au(I)-Ar transmetalation is much faster than Au(III)-Ar transmetalation and is turnover-limiting.

probe_reagents.name: 31P NMR monitoring of catalyst
probe_reagents.observation: Identifies Ph3P-Au(I)-C (alkynyl or aryl) as the gold(I) resting state under EAO conditions.

probe_reagents.name: Ph3P-Au-C6H4-F under MeCN:HOAc, no current
probe_reagents.observation: 100% conversion to H-C6H4-F within 30 min, demonstrating rapid protodemetalation of L-Au(I)-Ar under acidic conditions (rationale for switching to basic conditions for Ar-Ar coupling).

probe_reagents.name: Hydrazine salts with different counterions (HCl, HBF4, HOTf)
probe_reagents.observation: Only the OTf- salt (5a-HOTf) gave the desired aminoarylation 3a (16% then optimized to 92%); HCl and HBF4 salts gave only trace product, indicating the critical role of non-coordinating OTf- anion.

probe_reagents.name: Control without Au catalyst or without current
probe_reagents.observation: No product formation, confirming both gold catalyst and electrochemistry are required.

probe_reagents.name: 1,2-disubstituted E-alkene substrate
probe_reagents.observation: No cyclization observed; alkene recovered unreacted, attributed to steric hindrance blocking Au(III) pi-coordination.

probe_reagents.name: 4-Pyridinyl hydrazine
probe_reagents.observation: No alkene conversion, attributed to rapid decomposition of the in-situ formed pyridinyl radical, supporting an aryl-radical-based initiation.

10.1038_s41467-023-44025-0.notes: Authors explicitly contrast two mechanistic regimes under EAO gold catalysis: (i) the conventional Au(I)/Au(III) transmetalation/reductive-elimination pathway (acidic for alkyne or basic for aryl C-C coupling) which proceeds via [L-Au(I)-Ar] and gives only coupling products with no pi-activation; and (ii) the new 'oxidation relay' in which aryl hydrazine is oxidized first to an aryl radical that directly forges [L-Au(III)-Ar] without going through [L-Au(I)-Ar], thereby unlocking pi-acid reactivity. Aryl cation as the relay species is mentioned as an alternative to aryl radical. A concurrent report by Xie using aryl hydrazines under different conditions gave only C-C coupling without pi-activation, highlighting that bypassing transmetalation is the key design element.

10.1038_s41467-023-44029-w.rxn_class_summary: Electroreductive 1,2-hydroxy fluorosulfonylation of alkenes with FSO2Cl and O2 (air) to deliver beta-hydroxy sulfonyl fluorides via radical chain involving FSO2 radical, peroxy radical, and reduction by B2(OH)4/Et3SiH.
10.1038_s41467-023-44029-w.electrochemical_role: Cathodic reduction of FSO2Cl at Zn cathode generates FSO2 radical (and Cl-); the low local FSO2Cl concentration at the cathode suppresses Cl-atom transfer and favors O2 interception of the benzylic radical. Aluminum sacrificial anode supports the circuit.
10.1038_s41467-023-44029-w.electron_flow: At the Zn cathode, FSO2Cl is reduced to FSO2• (and Cl-); FSO2• adds to styrene to give benzylic radical I, which is trapped by O2 to peroxy radical II; HAT from Et3SiH gives hydroperoxide III, which is reduced by B2(OH)4 through borate intermediate IV/VI and hydrolyzed to the beta-hydroxy sulfonyl fluoride, while Et3Si• regenerates FSO2• via halogen-atom transfer with FSO2Cl to sustain the chain.

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction completely inhibited, supporting a radical pathway.

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction completely inhibited.

probe_reagents.name: 1,1-diphenylethylene (DPE, 58)
probe_reagents.observation: Trapping of FSO2• afforded adduct 59 in 10% yield, supporting FSO2 radical generation.

probe_reagents.name: beta-keto sulfonyl fluoride 4 (resubjection)
probe_reagents.observation: Compound 4 was not reduced to 3 under standard conditions; products 3 and 4 arise via divergent pathways.

probe_reagents.name: constant potential electrolysis
probe_reagents.observation: Optimal at E_cathode = -0.1 V (87%); more reductive potentials (-0.5, -1.0 V) gave lower yields, indicating mild reduction is needed.

probe_reagents.name: N2 atmosphere control
probe_reagents.observation: Under N2, no product formed and styrene was recovered, confirming O2 is essential.

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of Et3SiH and B2(OH)4
probe_reagents.observation: Neither Et3SiH nor B2(OH)4 is directly involved in the electrochemical step (no redox features).

probe_reagents.name: 11B NMR detection of B(OH)3
probe_reagents.observation: B(OH)3 detected, consistent with B2(OH)4 reducing the hydroperoxide via borate intermediate.

probe_reagents.name: HRMS-ESI detection of (Et3Si)2
probe_reagents.observation: Hexaethyldisilane detected, supporting Et3Si• as chain carrier (and competing homocoupling).

10.1038_s41467-023-44029-w.notes: Competing pathways outlined in Fig. 5c (right): in the absence of Et3SiH/B2(OH)4, peroxy radical II undergoes Russell fragmentation to give beta-keto sulfonyl fluoride 4 plus 3; an alternative cathodic reduction of an alkoxy radical to alkoxide is also drawn. A separate EDA-complex (styrene·FSO2Cl) pathway under daylight without electricity also gives product (78%) but with limited scope. Constant cell voltage (not constant current) is required to maintain low local [FSO2Cl] and avoid over-reduction.

10.1038_s41467-023-42106-8_sup_1.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical anti-Markovnikov dihydroxylation of alkenes to 1,2-diols via iodonium intermediates and α-amino radical-mediated C-I activation in tBuOH/H2O
10.1038_s41467-023-42106-8_sup_1.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of iodide to I2 (which acts as the alkene-activating mediator forming an iodonium) and parallel anodic oxidation of in-situ-generated Et3N (from Et4NI decomposition) to an α-amino radical; cathode reduces protons to H2
10.1038_s41467-023-42106-8_sup_1.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes I- to I• which dimerizes to I2; I2 forms an iodonium with the alkene that is opened by water to give a β-iodo alcohol (Int-II). In parallel, Et4NI decomposes to Et3N which is anodically oxidized (-H+/-e-) to an α-amino radical that abstracts the iodine of Int-II; the resulting carbon radical loses an electron at the anode and cyclizes to a protonated epoxide (Int-III), which is opened by H2O to deliver the trans 1,2-diol (with Int-IV epoxide as equilibrium partner). Protons are reduced to H2 at the cathode.
10.1038_s41467-023-42106-8_sup_1.rate_determining_step: Iodine-atom abstraction from Int-II by the α-amino radical (C-I activation) followed by intramolecular cyclization to Int-III

key_intermediates.name: β-iodo alcohol from water opening of iodonium (Int-II)
halogen-bonded / HAT transition state A between Int-II and α-amino radical
free epoxide (Int-IV) in equilibrium with Int-III

probe_reagents.name: compound 4 (β-iodo alcohol) without electricity, with Et3N
probe_reagents.observation: No product 2a; 4 recovered in 90% — electricity required to convert Int-II to product

probe_reagents.name: compound 4 with electricity + Et3N
probe_reagents.observation: 2a formed in 27%, confirming Int-II is on-cycle and electrochemistry drives its conversion

probe_reagents.name: compound 4 with electricity + DIPEA (in place of Et3N)
probe_reagents.observation: 2a in 11%; tertiary amine + current still operative but less efficient

probe_reagents.name: compound 4 with DIPEA, no electricity
probe_reagents.observation: No product; 4 recovered 93%

probe_reagents.name: GC-MS of reaction mixture
probe_reagents.observation: Et3N detected (m/z 86, 58, 101), confirming in-situ decomposition of Et4NI to Et3N

probe_reagents.name: single-crystal X-ray of product 2y (CCDC-2286663)
probe_reagents.observation: trans-diol configuration on cyclohexane confirms anti opening of a protonated epoxide (Int-III/Int-IV) by water

10.1038_s41467-023-42106-8_sup_1.notes: An earlier mechanism draft proposed direct Int-II → Int-III without electron accounting; reviewer 3 pointed out the missing electron loss, and the revised mechanism explicitly invokes anodic oxidation of the carbon radical (after iodine atom transfer to the α-amino radical) to give the cyclic oxiranium Int-III. Int-III and the neutral epoxide Int-IV are in protonation equilibrium; trans-diol stereochemistry from internal alkenes (X-ray of 2y) supports SN2-like anti opening of the protonated epoxide by water. Et4NI serves dual roles: iodide source for I2 mediator and precursor (via Hofmann-type decomposition) to Et3N that supplies the α-amino radical.

10.1038_s41467-023-42106-8.rxn_class_summary: Metal-free electrochemical dihydroxylation of unactivated alkenes using Et4NI/NH4I as iodide mediators and water as the oxygen source, proceeding via in-situ generated iodine and an epoxide intermediate
10.1038_s41467-023-42106-8.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of iodide (I-) to iodine radical/I2, which acts as the alkene-activating mediator; anode also oxidizes Et3N (generated from Et4N+ decomposition) to an α-aminoalkyl radical that abstracts iodine from an alkyl iodide intermediate; cathode evolves H2
10.1038_s41467-023-42106-8.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes I- to I• which dimerizes to I2; I2 forms an iodonium with the alkene that is opened by H2O to give a β-iodo alcohol (4). In parallel, the anode oxidizes Et3N (from Et4N+) to an α-aminoalkyl radical that abstracts the C-I of 4, giving a carbon radical that loses an electron and cyclizes to an epoxide (3i); H2O then opens the epoxide to the diol (2a), while the cathode reduces protons to H2.

key_intermediates.name: iodine (I2) generated from anodic oxidation of I-
β-iodo alcohol (Int-II / compound 4) from water addition to iodonium
α-aminoalkyl radical from electrooxidation of Et3N
β-hydroxy carbon radical (Int-III) after I-atom abstraction
epoxide intermediate (Int-IV / compound 3i) from intramolecular cyclization/ring closure

probe_reagents.name: H2 18-O (isotope labeling)
probe_reagents.observation: Product 2a incorporates 18O in both hydroxyl groups (HRMS m/z 299.1392 vs calc 299.1390), showing both OH groups come from water

probe_reagents.name: I2 (stoichiometric, no electricity)
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction of 1a with I2 in the dark without current gave iodo-alcohols 4 (21%) and 4' (9%), supporting I2 as the alkene-activating mediator

probe_reagents.name: isolated β-iodo alcohol 4 (stoichiometric intermediate)
probe_reagents.observation: Under condition A, 4 gave 2a in 82%; without electricity 4 was recovered (93%), confirming 4 as a key on-cycle intermediate requiring current

probe_reagents.name: isolated epoxide 3i (stoichiometric intermediate)
probe_reagents.observation: 3i gave 2a in 85% under condition A; without electricity 3i was recovered (95%), confirming epoxide as downstream intermediate

probe_reagents.name: Omission of Et4NI; use NH4I/TFA only
probe_reagents.observation: No 2a formed and 4 recovered (92%), indicating Et4N+ (source of Et3N) is required to convert 4 onward

probe_reagents.name: Et3N added to 4 with current
probe_reagents.observation: 2a obtained in 27% with current; no product without electricity, confirming electrooxidation of tertiary amine to α-aminoalkyl radical is required for C-I activation

probe_reagents.name: Et4NBF4 (non-iodide electrolyte) with 4 under current
probe_reagents.observation: Time-course showed 4 decreasing, 3i rising then falling, 2a increasing — demonstrating sequential 4 → 3i → 2a conversion

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of Et4NI at variable scan rates
probe_reagents.observation: Linear ip vs v^1/2 plots (R2 = 0.9753, 0.9983) indicate diffusion-controlled oxidation of iodide

10.1038_s41467-023-42106-8.notes: Both hydroxyl oxygens of the diol originate from water (18O labeling). The mechanism invokes a dual mediator system: iodide for alkene activation and in-situ-generated Et3N (from Et4N+ decomposition) for halogen atom transfer via an α-aminoalkyl radical. The epoxide (Int-IV) and an iodo-cyclic oxocarbenium-type Int-III are proposed to be in equilibrium, with water opening to give the diol. GC-MS detection of Et3N supports the amine-mediator hypothesis.

10.1038_s41467-023-43791-1.rxn_class_summary: Paired electrophotocatalytic C(sp3)-H arylation (two-component) or C(sp3)-H alkylation (three-component, with alkene linker) of alkanes with aryl bromides, using Fe/Ni dual catalysis under blue/purple light
10.1038_s41467-023-43791-1.electrochemical_role: Anodic LMCT-driven oxidation of [FeIIICl4]- (photoexcited) regenerates FeIII from FeII while releasing Cl• that performs HAT on the alkane to give an alkyl radical; cathodic reduction generates and turns over the Ni(I)/Ni(II)/Ni(III) catalytic cycle that couples the alkyl radical with ArBr
10.1038_s41467-023-43791-1.electron_flow: Under irradiation, anodic oxidation regenerates FeIII from FeII while a photoexcited [FeIIICl4]- undergoes LMCT to release Cl•, which abstracts H from the alkane to form an alkyl radical (optionally adding to an electron-poor alkene first). The cathode reduces NiII to NiI, which oxidatively adds ArBr to give NiIII-Ar; the alkyl radical is captured by aryl-NiII (or NiIII) to give a NiIII(Ar)(alkyl) intermediate that reductively eliminates the C(sp3)-C(sp2) product.
10.1038_s41467-023-43791-1.rate_determining_step: Matching of the anodic rate of alkyl radical generation (LMCT/HAT) with cathodic aryl-NiII speciation; selectivity is controlled by rate-matching rather than by a single elementary step

key_intermediates.name: alkyl radical R• (species E) from HAT on alkane
alkyl radical adduct onto EWG-alkene (Giese adduct, species F)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction completely inhibited; cyclohexyl-TEMPO adduct (72) detected by 1H NMR, supporting alkyl radical formation via anodic HAT

probe_reagents.name: Cycloheptyl chloride (alkyl chloride competition)
probe_reagents.observation: Reductive coupling product from alkyl chloride + ArBr (1') not detected; only cyclohexyl arylation product 1 formed (77%), excluding alkyl chloride as a key intermediate

probe_reagents.name: Ni(COD)2 as Ni(0) source
probe_reagents.observation: Only 9% yield, suggesting Ni(0) is not the operative species in the catalytic cycle

probe_reagents.name: Stoichiometric aryl-NiII complex 73 (with 6,6'-Me2-bpy)
probe_reagents.observation: With cyclohexane + FeCl3·6H2O under light gave product 1 in 37%; Ni(I) detected by HRMS, confirming aryl-NiII as catalytic species and Ni(I) intermediate

probe_reagents.name: Aryl-NiII complex 73 as catalyst (10 mol%)
probe_reagents.observation: Delivered product 23 in 46% yield, supporting catalytic competence of Ar-NiII

probe_reagents.name: Light on/off and intensity variation
probe_reagents.observation: No product without light; higher light intensity gives higher rate and selectivity (Fig. 7), consistent with photoinduced LMCT controlling radical generation rate

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of NiBr2·3H2O + bpy
probe_reagents.observation: Shows NiII/NiI reduction wave around -1.1 V vs Ag/AgCl, matching the observed cathode potential during electrolysis

probe_reagents.name: Chronopotentiometry during electrolysis
probe_reagents.observation: Anode held ~0.23 V and cathode ~-1.1 V vs Ag/AgCl, consistent with FeII/FeIII anodic and NiII/NiI cathodic cycles

10.1038_s41467-023-43791-1.notes: The authors emphasize 'rate-matching modulation': current (4 mA for arylation vs 25 mA for three-component alkylation) and light intensity independently tune anodic radical generation vs cathodic Ni speciation to switch selectivity between two- and three-component products. A direct anodic oxidation of Cl-/Br- to halogen radical (without LMCT photoexcitation) is explicitly excluded because the anode potential (~0.23 V) is too low. Alkyl chloride pathway and Ni(0) pathway are excluded by control experiments. The alkyl radical is proposed to be captured either by aryl-NiII (giving NiIII(Ar)(alkyl)) or by NiI followed by oxidative addition of ArBr; the favored radical-chain coupling proceeds via Ar-NiII.

10.1038_s41467-022-32084-8.rxn_class_summary: Electro-oxidative intermolecular 1,2-amino oxygenation of alkenes using sulfonamides and alcohols/acids as N and O sources via nitrogen-centered radicals, with H2 evolution at the cathode.
10.1038_s41467-022-32084-8.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of the deprotonated sulfonamide (N-anion, generated by DBU) to an N-centered radical (NCR); cathodic reduction of protons to evolve H2. DBU appears electroactive and may shuttle electrons (oxidized DBU oxidizes the sulfonamide).
10.1038_s41467-022-32084-8.electron_flow: DBU deprotonates the sulfonamide; the resulting N-anion (or a DBU/sulfonamide complex) is oxidized at the anode to an N-centered radical, which adds anti-Markovnikov to the alkene to give a benzylic C-radical. A second anodic oxidation converts the C-radical to a benzylic cation, which is trapped by the alcohol/carboxylate nucleophile with loss of a proton; cathode reduces 2H+ to H2.

key_intermediates.name: Sulfonamide N-anion (I) after deprotonation by DBU
N-centered radical (NCR, II) from anodic oxidation
C-centered benzylic radical (III) from NCR addition to styrene
Benzylic carbocation (IV) from further anodic oxidation

probe_reagents.name: N-methyl-N-pentyl tosylamide (10) competition substrate
probe_reagents.observation: Under standard conditions with styrene, gave both intermolecular 1,2-amino oxygenation product 10a (15%) and intramolecular Hofmann–Löffler–Freytag cyclization product 10b (41%), supporting an N-centered radical intermediate.

probe_reagents.name: α-cyclopropylstyrene (11) radical clock
probe_reagents.observation: Ring-opened adduct 11a (14%) was formed alongside non-opened 11b (6%), supporting radical addition of NCR to the alkene.

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of 1a, DBU, 2aa and mixtures
probe_reagents.observation: 1a shows no oxidation peak up to 2.5 V; DBU catalytic current increases with added 1a, indicating DBU is electroactive and mediates oxidation of 1a; styrene 2aa is electro-redox active but EDG-styrenes give low yields, so direct alkene oxidation is not the initial step.

probe_reagents.name: NMR titration of 1a with DBU
probe_reagents.observation: NMR supports rapid proton transfer between 1a and DBU prior to electrolysis.

probe_reagents.name: Current on/off control
probe_reagents.observation: No product formed without current, confirming electrochemistry is required.

10.1038_s41467-022-32084-8.notes: Authors note an alternative pathway cannot be ruled out in which the 1a/DBU mixture is directly oxidized at the anode to generate the NCR (rather than discrete deprotonation then oxidation of the N-anion). Styrenes with strong electron-donating groups give low yields, consistent with the NCR-addition pathway rather than direct alkene oxidation. HFIP/TFE-type fluorinated alcohol solvents are critical for NCR lifetime; DBU base is essential for high yields.

10.1038_s41467-023-42566-y.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical hydroalkylation of electron-poor olefins with unactivated alkyl iodides via anodic generation of alpha-aminoalkyl radicals that perform halogen-atom transfer (e-XAT)
10.1038_s41467-023-42566-y.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of Et3N to aminium radical cation followed by alpha-deprotonation generates an alpha-aminoalkyl radical that acts as the XAT mediator; the alkyl radical adds to the Michael acceptor and the resulting alpha-carbonyl C-radical is then reduced at the cathode to a carbanion which is protonated by H2O
10.1038_s41467-023-42566-y.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes Et3N to its aminium radical cation, which loses an alpha-H to give an alpha-aminoalkyl radical; this radical abstracts I from the alkyl iodide (XAT) producing an alkyl radical that adds to the electron-poor olefin, and the resulting alpha-EWG radical is reduced at the Pt cathode to a carbanion that is protonated by H2O.
10.1038_s41467-023-42566-y.rate_determining_step: Halogen-atom transfer from alkyl iodide to the alpha-aminoalkyl radical (driven by exothermic formation of iminium iodide)
