PhB(OH)2 mediates proton/hydroxyl transfer, ending up as the aminoborinic byproduct IV detected by MS.

key_intermediates.name: benzylic carbon radical from styrene + ArS• addition

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Product 3n suppressed (0% yield); TEMPO-trapped adduct 3n' (C24H33NOS, [M+H]+ calcd 384.2356, found 384.2367) detected by ESI-HRMS, confirming a benzylic C-radical intermediate after thiyl radical addition.

probe_reagents.name: PhB(OH)2 (byproduct capture)
probe_reagents.observation: Putative byproduct IV (HO-B(Ph)NMe2, exact mass 149.1012; [M+H]+ 150.1085 found 150.1046) detected by EI-MS and ESI-HRMS, supporting involvement of the boronic acid as a hydroxyl/amine shuttle.

10.1021_acs.orglett.1c03461_sup_1.notes: Only the radical/byproduct trapping portion of the SI was provided; full catalytic cycle and ordering of SET steps are inferred from the TEMPO suppression (radical pathway) and MS identification of the boron-containing byproduct IV. No KIE, CV, or DFT data shown on this page.

10.1021_acs.orglett.5c04052.rxn_class_summary: Continuous-flow electrochemical 1,2-arylheteroarylation of alkenes using aryl bromides and cyanopyridines to give 1,2-diarylethane products
10.1021_acs.orglett.5c04052.electrochemical_role: Cathodic reduction of both the aryl bromide (to aryl radical via debromination) and the cyanopyridine (to a persistent radical anion); concurrent two-substrate cathodic SET enables radical-radical cross-coupling
10.1021_acs.orglett.5c04052.electron_flow: At the cathode, the aryl bromide is reduced and loses bromide to give an aryl radical that adds across the alkene, generating a transient C-radical; in parallel the cyanopyridine is cathodically reduced to a persistent radical anion, and radical-radical cross-coupling between the transient and persistent radicals delivers the product. Et3N serves as the sacrificial reductant oxidized at the anode.

key_intermediates.name: transient benzylic/alkyl carbon-centered radical from aryl radical addition to alkene (35)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Product 4 fully suppressed with 3.0 equiv TEMPO; TEMPO-aryl adduct detected by HRMS, indicating aryl radical intermediate

probe_reagents.name: (1-cyclopropylvinyl)benzene (radical clock 31)
probe_reagents.observation: Ring-opened product 32 isolated in 37% yield, supporting a carbon-centered radical intermediate

probe_reagents.name: methyl 4-bromobenzoate (1)
probe_reagents.observation: CV shows irreversible reduction at -1.86 V (vs Ag/AgCl) assigned to reductive debromination, with a subsequent quasi-reversible wave (Epc = -2.27 V) for the methyl benzoate radical

probe_reagents.name: styrene (2)
probe_reagents.observation: No reduction wave from 0 to -2.5 V; electrochemically inert under conditions

probe_reagents.name: 4-cyanopyridine (3)
probe_reagents.observation: Quasi-reversible redox wave (Epc = -1.81 V, Epa = -1.61 V), characteristic of a persistent radical anion; similar potential to 1 enables concurrent cathodic reduction

10.1021_acs.orglett.5c04052.notes: Aliphatic alkenes are incompatible under the standard conditions. Mechanism relies on the persistent radical effect: cyanopyridine radical anion accumulates as the persistent partner while the alkyl radical is transient, biasing selective cross-coupling over homocoupling. Batch electrolysis gave much lower yields than continuous flow under identical conditions.

10.1021_acs.orglett.2c04045.rxn_class_summary: Palladium-catalyzed electrooxidative hydrofluorination of aryl-substituted alkenes (styrenes and α,β-unsaturated carbonyls) with a nucleophilic fluoride source (Et3N·3HF) and a hydrosilane to give benzylic fluorides
10.1021_acs.orglett.2c04045.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of an η3-benzylpalladium(II) intermediate to a high-valent Pd(III) or Pd(IV) species, which enables C-F bond formation with nucleophilic fluoride; cathode reduces protons to H2 as the counter-reaction
10.1021_acs.orglett.2c04045.electron_flow: Pd(II) inserts into the aryl alkene with hydrosilane to form an η3-benzyl-Pd(II); the anode oxidizes this complex by one or two electrons to a high-valent Pd(III/IV)-benzyl, which undergoes C-F bond formation with fluoride (either via reductive elimination or via a benzyl carbocation trapped by F−), regenerating Pd(II); protons are reduced to H2 at the cathode.
10.1021_acs.orglett.2c04045.rate_determining_step: Anodic oxidation of the η3-benzylpalladium(II) intermediate to the high-valent Pd species (the C-F forming step requires potentials above the onset potential of complex 41)

key_intermediates.name: η3-benzylpalladium(II) species (II) formed from alkene insertion with hydrosilane
high-valent benzylpalladium(III/IV) intermediate (III) generated by anodic oxidation
benzyl carbocation (IV) from heterolysis of high-valent Pd-benzyl, stabilized by HFIP

probe_reagents.name: stoichiometric η3-benzyl Pd(II)-(R)-Tol-BINAP complex 41
probe_reagents.observation: No fluorinated product without electrolysis or at E < Eonset; product 15 formed in 28±5% as a racemate at Ucell = 2.7 V (E > Eonset), supporting that anodic oxidation of the Pd(II)-benzyl is required

probe_reagents.name: chemical oxidants Ag(I), Cu(II), benzoquinone, Oxone, CAN, iodine(III)
probe_reagents.observation: None gave stoichiometric fluorination of 41, indicating the high-valent Pd intermediate is only accessible via anodic oxidation

probe_reagents.name: HFIP cosolvent
probe_reagents.observation: Racemic product from enantiopure Pd complex 41 plus HFIP stabilization argues for a benzyl carbocation pathway from the high-valent Pd

10.1021_acs.orglett.2c04045.notes: Two C-F bond forming scenarios are proposed and cannot be distinguished: (favored) heterolysis of high-valent Pd-benzyl to a benzyl carbocation (IV) that is trapped by fluoride, consistent with the racemic product from enantiopure 41 and the role of HFIP; alternatively inner- or outer-sphere C-F reductive elimination directly from the high-valent Pd(III/IV) intermediate. A low-valent Pd(II)/Pd(0) pathway (pathway II) is ruled out because no product forms without applied potential.

10.1021_acs.orglett.9b00867.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical oxidative 1,2-difunctionalization of alkenes (styrenes) with formyloxylation coupled to chlorination, bromination, or trifluoromethylation
10.1021_acs.orglett.9b00867.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of the halide/sulfinate anion (Br-, Cl-, or CF3SO2- giving CF3•) to a halogen/CF3 radical, which adds to the alkene; Cu(I) co-catalyst is also redox-active
10.1021_acs.orglett.9b00867.electron_flow: Halide or trifluoromethanesulfinate is first oxidized at the anode to X• (or CF3•), which adds to the styrene to give a benzylic C-radical; this radical is further oxidized (anode/Cu) to a benzylic cation that is trapped by formate (from HCOOH/H2O) to install the OCHO group, while protons are reduced at the cathode.

key_intermediates.name: halogen or CF3 radical (X• or CF3•) from anodic oxidation of X- / CF3SO2-
benzylic carbon radical from X•/CF3• addition to styrene
benzylic carbocation after further oxidation of C-radical
formate (HCOO-) nucleophile trapping the benzylic cation to give formyloxy product

probe_reagents.name: radical trapping experiments (see SI Scheme 2)
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction suppressed/intercepted, supporting involvement of radical intermediates

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry
probe_reagents.observation: Indicates Br-, Cl-, or CF3SO2- (without Cu(I)) is oxidized first at the anode

10.1021_acs.orglett.9b00867.notes: Diastereoselective trans-addition on cyclic alkenes suggests a stepwise radical/cation mechanism rather than concerted. Minor β-chlorinated side products from electron-rich styrenes are attributed to over-oxidation/elimination stabilized by push-pull conjugation. Detailed mechanism schemes are in the Supporting Information (Scheme 3).

10.1021_acs.orglett.4c01311.rxn_class_summary: Organoboron-promoted electrochemical chlorosulfonylation of styrenes with sulfonyl chlorides via convergent paired electrolysis to give β-chlorosulfones
10.1021_acs.orglett.4c01311.electrochemical_role: Cathodic reduction of sulfonyl chloride (RSO2Cl) to sulfonyl radical + chloride; anodic oxidation of the released chloride anion to a chlorine radical, which is shuttled by p-ethylphenylboronic acid as a Cl-boron complex
10.1021_acs.orglett.4c01311.electron_flow: Cathode reduces RSO2Cl to a sulfonyl radical plus chloride; the sulfonyl radical adds to the styrene giving benzylic radical P1. The chloride migrates to the anode and is oxidized to Cl•, which is captured by p-ethylphenylboronic acid; this Cl-boron complex then transfers Cl to P1 to deliver the β-chlorosulfone and a dechlorinated borane.

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Product yield dropped from 68% to 9% (1 equiv) and n.d. (2 equiv), indicating radical involvement

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: Product not detected with 2 equiv BHT

probe_reagents.name: DMPO
probe_reagents.observation: EPR signal (g=2.0056, AN=15.9 G, AH=21.7 G) for carbon radical from tosyl addition to p-F-styrene; HRMS adducts 46 (tosyl) and 47 (benzyl) detected

probe_reagents.name: N,N-diallyl-4-methylbenzenesulfonamide
probe_reagents.observation: Chlorinated ring-closing product 48 NOT detected; instead chloro-boron complex 49 was detected by HRMS, supporting Cl• capture by the boronic acid

probe_reagents.name: divided cell (H-cell) control
probe_reagents.observation: No product formed, confirming necessity of paired (undivided) electrolysis

10.1021_acs.orglett.4c01311.notes: CV shows chloride anion (1.42 V) is oxidized preferentially over 4-fluorostyrene (2.45 V vs 1.68 V), and TsCl shows irreversible reduction at -1.63 V; organoboron does not shift redox potentials but increases currents, supporting its role as a Cl-atom shuttle rather than redox mediator. The authors describe the process as ATRA-type within a convergent paired electrolysis framework.

10.1021_acs.orglett.2c02976_sup_1.rxn_class_summary: Nickel-catalyzed electrochemical three-component 1,2-dicarbofunctionalization of styrenes with aryl iodides and 3-iodooxetane via a radical pathway
10.1021_acs.orglett.2c02976_sup_1.electrochemical_role: Cathodic reduction generates Ni(0)Ln from Ni(II); the iron anode is sacrificially oxidized (Fe -> Fe(n+) + ne-); alkyl iodide (3-iodooxetane) undergoes SET to form alkyl radical that adds to styrene
10.1021_acs.orglett.2c02976_sup_1.electron_flow: Cathode reduces Ni(II) to Ni(0), which oxidatively adds ArI to give Ar-Ni(II)-I; this Ni species reduces the alkyl iodide via SET to release an alkyl radical that adds to styrene, generating a benzylic radical that recombines with Ar-Ni(II) to give a Ni(III) intermediate undergoing reductive elimination; the iron anode is sacrificially oxidized to balance charge.

key_intermediates.name: Ni(II)-I (G) reduced back to Ni(0) at cathode

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Target product 4a fully suppressed (0%); TEMPO-oxetanyl adduct 7 isolated in 13% with full system and 31% with only 3a, confirming alkyl radical formation and suggesting Ni(I) generation by disproportionation

probe_reagents.name: DMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Used as spin trap for EPR detection of radical intermediates from 1a and 2a under electrolysis

10.1021_acs.orglett.2c02976_sup_1.notes: Stoichiometric reaction without electric current using Ni(cod)2 still afforded product 4a (10%) and isomer 4a' (40%), supporting feasibility of the Ni(0)/Ni(II)/Ni(III) cycle independent of continuous current. TEMPO trapping with only 3a indicates Ni(I) may form via disproportionation pathway alongside the main cycle.

10.1021_acs.orglett.1c03461.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical phenylboronic-acid-promoted sulfuration-formyloxylation of styrenes with thiophenols in DMF to give beta-formyloxy sulfides, with downstream Selectfluor-mediated oxidation-olefination to (E)-vinyl sulfones
10.1021_acs.orglett.1c03461.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation: thiophenol is oxidized at the anode (easier oxidation by CV) to a thiyl radical that adds to styrene; the resulting benzylic radical is further oxidized at the anode to a benzylic carbocation; protons are reduced to H2 at the cathode
10.1021_acs.orglett.1c03461.electron_flow: At the anode, PhSH undergoes 1e- oxidation to PhS•, which adds to styrene to give a benzylic radical; a second anodic 1e- oxidation furnishes a benzylic carbocation that is trapped by DMF to form an iminium, hydrolyzed (by H2O generated in situ from PhB(OH)2) to the beta-formyloxy sulfide; protons are reduced at the cathode to H2.

key_intermediates.name: thiyl radical (PhS•) in equilibrium with disulfide PhSSPh
benzyl radical I from thiyl addition to styrene
iminium intermediate III from DMF addition to cation II
beta-formyloxy sulfide 3a (product of hydrolysis of III)
fluorosulfoxonium I-5 / hydroxysulfurane I-6 / sulfone I-7

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Product 3n not formed (0%); TEMPO-SAr adduct 3n' detected by ESI-MS, supporting thiyl-radical intermediate

probe_reagents.name: H2O (2 equiv added)
probe_reagents.observation: Gave 3a in 68% with small amount of beta-hydroxy sulfide 5a (6%), indicating excess water is detrimental and supporting in situ water generation via PhB(OH)2

probe_reagents.name: Hu's conditions (HCO2H/CuCl/Bu4NPF6)
probe_reagents.observation: Only trace 3a and 26% hydroxylation 5a; conditions not applicable for the sulfuration-formyloxylation

probe_reagents.name: Selectfluor / HF / HCO2H (on sulfoxide 8a)
probe_reagents.observation: No vinyl sulfone 4g formed (0%), ruling out simple acid-mediated elimination

probe_reagents.name: DABCO or CsF (on sulfoxide 8a)
probe_reagents.observation: Near-quantitative conversion of 8a to vinyl sulfone 4g (>98%), supporting an E2-type elimination in the olefination step

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry
probe_reagents.observation: Thiophenol is much more easily oxidized than styrene; two one-electron oxidation events observed, consistent with sequential anodic oxidations

probe_reagents.name: ESI-MS detection of I-2 and byproduct IV
probe_reagents.observation: Mass-spec detection of ammonium intermediate I-2 and PhB(OH)(NMe2) byproduct IV supports proposed cycle

10.1021_acs.orglett.1c03461.notes: Authors favor a stepwise radical/cation (anodic two-step oxidation) pathway: thiyl radical addition then anodic oxidation to a benzyl cation trapped by DMF (iminium III), hydrolyzed to product. PhB(OH)2 acts as a Lewis-acid additive that removes excess Me2NH/H2O (forming byproduct IV) and provides in situ water for iminium hydrolysis. Alternative: direct nucleophilic addition/elimination between iminium III and PhB(OH)2 to deliver the formate group. For the downstream Selectfluor oxidation-olefination of 3a to vinyl sulfone 4a, an E2-type elimination from sulfone I-7 is invoked (supported by DABCO/CsF base-promoted elimination of 8a).

10.1021_acs.orglett.9b03306.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical intermolecular [2+1+n] annulation of alkenes with diazo compounds and tert-butyl nitrite to give isoxazolines (with styrenes) or 5,6-dihydro-4H-1,2-oxazines (with allylbenzenes), where diazo compounds serve as radical acceptors
10.1021_acs.orglett.9b03306.electrochemical_role: Cathodic reduction of tBuONO generates an electron-rich nitroso radical (·NO) and tert-butoxide; the resulting C-centered benzylic radical is oxidized at the anode to a benzylic cation (radical-polar crossover); paired electrolysis with H2 evolution avoided by using tBuONO as sacrificial species
10.1021_acs.orglett.9b03306.electron_flow: Cathode reduces tBuONO to give ·NO and tBuO-/tBuO·; ·NO adds to the diazo compound with loss of N2 to give an α-acyl C-radical (I), which adds to the alkene to give a benzylic/alkyl radical (II or V); for allylbenzenes, 1,2-aryl migration gives the more stable benzyl radical VI; anodic oxidation of the C-radical to a cation, tautomerization to the oxime, and intramolecular O-attack/dehydration closes the isoxazoline or 1,2-oxazine ring.

key_intermediates.name: nitroso radical (·NO) from homolysis/heterolysis of tBuONO
α-oxime-ester C-radical I (·NO addition to diazo with N2 loss)
benzylic radical II from addition of I to styrene
benzylic cation III after anodic oxidation of II
alkyl radical V (from addition of I to allylbenzene)
more-stable benzyl radical VI after 1,2-aryl migration
nitrile oxide 6 (alternative anodic oxidation product of I, not observed)

probe_reagents.name: H-type divided cell experiment
probe_reagents.observation: Product 3a still formed in 45% GC yield in divided cell (vs 75% undivided), indicating tBuONO undergoes homolytic cleavage independently and confirming both anodic and cathodic events contribute

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of tBuONO with/without HFIP
probe_reagents.observation: HFIP lowers reduction potential of tBuONO to -0.78 V vs Ag/AgCl, rationalizing why HFIP facilitates nitroso radical generation

probe_reagents.name: allylbenzene substrates (radical clock / migration probe)
probe_reagents.observation: 1,2-aryl migration observed to give 5,6-dihydro-4H-1,2-oxazines (R2=H, alkyl), proving radical pathway rather than 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition (which would give isoxazoline 7, not observed)

probe_reagents.name: absence of nitrile oxide [3+2] product 7
probe_reagents.observation: Only [2+1+3] product 5 formed, not the cycloaddition product 7 expected from nitrile oxide 6, ruling out anodic oxidation of I to nitrile oxide / 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition pathway

10.1021_acs.orglett.9b03306.notes: Authors explicitly rule out the alternative 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition pathway (anodic oxidation of radical I to nitrile oxide 6 followed by [3+2] with alkene) on the basis that allylbenzenes give 1,2-oxazines via aryl migration rather than the cycloaddition product 7. HFIP additive critical for lowering tBuONO reduction potential. Paired electrolysis: anode oxidizes benzylic radical, cathode reduces tBuONO — no sacrificial H2 evolution needed.

10.1021_acs.orglett.1c02991.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical chemoselective oxo-azidation or oxo-hydroxyphthalimidation of styrenes using O2 as the oxygen source, with TMSN3 or NHPI as the second functional group source.
10.1021_acs.orglett.1c02991.electrochemical_role: For oxo-azidation: anodic oxidation of styrene generates a styrene radical cation that is attacked by azide. For oxo-hydroxyphthalimidation: NHPI is deprotonated and anodically oxidized to phthalimide N-oxyl (PINO) radical, which adds to styrene as a mediator-type pathway.
10.1021_acs.orglett.1c02991.electron_flow: Oxo-azidation: anode oxidizes styrene (Ep = 1.66 V vs Ag/AgCl) to its radical cation, which is attacked by N3- to give an α-azido benzylic radical that is trapped by O2 and converted to the α-azido aryl ketone. Oxo-hydroxyphthalimidation: NHPI is deprotonated then anodically oxidized to PINO, which undergoes radical addition to styrene; the resulting carbon radical is trapped by O2 to furnish the α-hydroxyphthalimide ketone.

key_intermediates.name: α-azido benzylic peroxyl radical (after O2 trap)

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of styrene 1c, TMSN3, NHPI (and NHPI + NaOMe / 2,6-lutidine)
probe_reagents.observation: Styrene 1c shows oxidation peak at Ep = 1.66 V vs Ag/AgCl; TMSN3 and neat NHPI show no oxidation in this window, excluding generation of azide radical and indicating styrene is oxidized first in the azidation case. In the presence of NaOMe or 2,6-lutidine, NHPI becomes more easily oxidized than styrene, supporting PINO formation as the initiating step in the hydroxyphthalimidation.

probe_reagents.name: control experiments (see SI)
probe_reagents.observation: Support the divergent mechanism (radical cation pathway for azidation vs PINO-mediator pathway for hydroxyphthalimidation); cyclic styrene and phenylpropene fail, consistent with requirement for terminal aryl-substituted alkene.

10.1021_acs.orglett.1c02991.notes: Two divergent mechanisms are proposed for the same overall oxo-difunctionalization platform: (i) substrate-initiated radical cation pathway with nucleophilic azide for TMSN3, and (ii) mediator-initiated PINO radical pathway for NHPI. Molecular O2 is incorporated as the ketone oxygen in both cases. No DFT, KIE, EPR, or TEMPO trapping reported in the main text; mechanism is supported mainly by CV and additive (NaOMe / 2,6-lutidine) experiments referencing Stephenson's prior work on PINO.

10.1021_acs.orglett.1c02661_sup_1.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical Y(OTf)3-catalyzed selenofunctionalization of styrenes and thiophenes with diphenyl diselenide to give 1,1-diaryl-2-phenylselenyl products (4a) and 2-phenylselenyl thiophenes (5a)
10.1021_acs.orglett.1c02661_sup_1.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of PhSeSePh (or PhSe-) to generate an electrophilic PhSe+ species (or PhSe radical), which is activated/Lewis-acid-promoted by Y(CF3CO2)3 to engage the styrene/thiophene
10.1021_acs.orglett.1c02661_sup_1.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes PhSeSePh (assisted by Y(CF3CO2)3 as Lewis acid) to an electrophilic PhSe+ equivalent, which adds to styrene forming a seleniranium ion; nucleophilic attack by 2-ethylthiophene at the benzylic position opens the seleniranium to give 4a, while direct electrophilic selenylation of 2-methylthiophene affords 5a. Protons are reduced at the Pt cathode to H2.

key_intermediates.name: benzylic carbocation after thiophene attack on seleniranium

10.1021_acs.orglett.1c02661_sup_1.notes: Only the gram-scale procedure and a time-course (kinetic) study were shown on this page; mechanistic probes (radical traps, CV, intermediate isolation) are not visible here, so the mechanism is inferred from the reaction setup (electrochemistry, Y(III) Lewis acid, diselenide, styrene/thiophene). A radical pathway via PhSe• cannot be excluded from this page alone.

10.1021_acs.orglett.5c00005.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical bromide-mediated aminooxygenation of N-vinylbenzamides with pyrazoles to give 2-aryl-4,5-dihydrooxazoles bearing a pyrazolyl group
10.1021_acs.orglett.5c00005.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of Br- to Br2 (bromide acts as redox mediator); Br2 then reacts with the enamide N-H to generate an N-Br intermediate which homolyzes/ionizes to N-centered radical and ultimately a carbocation; cathode reduces protons to H2
10.1021_acs.orglett.5c00005.electron_flow: Br- is anodically oxidized (2e-) to Br2, which brominates the enamide N-H to give N-Br intermediate A; loss of Br- and a further 1e- anodic oxidation generates an N-acyliminium cation D that cyclizes to oxazolinium E/F; pyrazole adds to the cation and deprotonation furnishes product 3, while the cathode reduces protons to H2.

key_intermediates.name: vinyl-stabilized N-centered radical (intermediate C, from homolysis of N-Br)

probe_reagents.name: 1,1-diphenylethylene (DPE)
probe_reagents.observation: Product 3 yield dropped to 37%; radical adduct 46 and hydrolysates 47 and 48 detected by HRMS, consistent with N-centered/vinyl radical intermediate C

probe_reagents.name: pyrazole (carbocation trap)
probe_reagents.observation: Carbocation-trapped product 49 plus hydrolysates 47 and 50 detected by HRMS in absence of nBu4NBF4, supporting cation D

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of nBu4NBr / 1 / 2
probe_reagents.observation: Br- oxidizes at 0.36 and 0.80 V; substrate 1 and pyrazole 2 show no oxidation peaks 0-0.80 V; catalytic current observed when 1 is added to nBu4NBr, supporting Br- as the mediator

10.1021_acs.orglett.5c00005.notes: Authors invoke both radical (C/B) and cationic (D/E/F) intermediates connected by further anodic oxidation (radical-polar crossover). HRMS-detected adducts are the principal evidence; no DFT, KIE, or EPR reported. Trace benzamide observed during scale-up also pointed toward involvement of C/D.

10.1021_acs.orglett.3c00121_sup_1.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical [2+1] cyclopropanation (with diazo compounds) and [2+2] cycloaddition (with styrenes) of alkenes proceeding via anodically generated alkene radical cations
10.1021_acs.orglett.3c00121_sup_1.electrochemical_role: Anodic single-electron oxidation of the electron-rich alkene to an alkene radical cation; subsequent cathodic reduction of a downstream radical cation closes the cycle (paired electrolysis in undivided cell)
10.1021_acs.orglett.3c00121_sup_1.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes the electron-rich alkene A to radical cation B; B is intercepted by a nucleophile (diazo compound for [2+1], styrene for [2+2]) to give a new radical cation (D→E after N2 loss, or H); cathodic reduction then delivers the neutral cyclopropane F or cyclobutane I.

key_intermediates.name: alkene radical cation B (from anodic oxidation of styrene/anethole A)
distonic radical cation D from addition of diazo compound C to B (retains N2)
1,3-distonic radical cation E after N2 extrusion (precursor to cyclopropane)
cyclopropane product F (after cathodic reduction of E)
acyclic radical cation H from addition of styrene G to B ([2+2] pathway)
cyclobutane product I (after cathodic reduction of H)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Complete suppression of both [2+1] and [2+2] reactions (0% yield) when added at the start, supporting a radical (radical cation) intermediate

probe_reagents.name: E- vs Z-stilbene (cis/trans olefin probe)
probe_reagents.observation: Both E- and Z-stilbene give exclusively trans-cyclopropane (E: 82%, Z: 67% yield), consistent with a freely rotating open-chain radical cation intermediate rather than concerted cycloaddition; trans-olefin reacts faster

probe_reagents.name: Divided (H-cell) vs undivided cell
probe_reagents.observation: [2+1] drops to <10% in H-cell (vs 79% undivided) — requires cathodic reduction step; [2+2] gives 19% in H-cell (vs 78% undivided), suggesting a partial radical-chain contribution for [2+2]

10.1021_acs.orglett.3c00121_sup_1.notes: Stereochemical scrambling (E/Z → same trans-cyclopropane) is taken as evidence for a stepwise radical-cation mechanism rather than a concerted [2+1]/[2+2]. Authors explicitly do not exclude a radical-chain pathway in which radical cations E/H are reduced by another molecule of alkene A instead of at the cathode; the chain contribution appears more significant for the [2+2] (H-cell yield 19%) than for the [2+1] (H-cell yield <10%). A minor proton-migrated (ring-opened α,β-unsaturated ester) byproduct (<6%) is observed in the [2+1] reaction, consistent with deprotonation of the 1,3-distonic radical cation E competing with cyclization/reduction.

10.1021_acs.orglett.5c00929.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical formal C-C cleavage of 1,3-diaryl-1,3-diketones with aryl alkenes and water to give 1,4-ketoalcohol benzoate esters via anodic oxidation
10.1021_acs.orglett.5c00929.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation: deprotonated 1,3-diketone enolate is oxidized at the anode to an α-keto C-centered radical; a second anodic oxidation converts the alkene-adduct radical to a benzylic cation; cathode reduces protons/water to H2
10.1021_acs.orglett.5c00929.electron_flow: Base/anode deprotonates and oxidizes the 1,3-diketone to an α-keto radical, which adds to the styrene to give a benzylic radical; a second anodic oxidation forms a benzylic cation that is trapped by water, and the resulting hemiketal rearranges with C-C cleavage to deliver the 1,4-ketoalcohol ester while the cathode evolves H2.

key_intermediates.name: benzylic C-radical adduct (III) from radical addition to styrene
benzylic alcohol intermediate (V) after water trapping
dimer 7 from coupling of α-keto radicals (in absence of alkene)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction strongly inhibited; TEMPO adduct 6 of the α-keto radical isolated in 44% yield, supporting an α-keto C-radical intermediate

probe_reagents.name: Omission of alkene (styrene 2a)
probe_reagents.observation: Dimer 7 of two α-keto radicals isolated in 23%, confirming generation of the α-keto radical

probe_reagents.name: H2 18-O labeled water
probe_reagents.observation: 18O is incorporated into the ester/alcohol of 3a (HRMS), showing water is the oxygen source

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of 1a vs 2a
probe_reagents.observation: 1a oxidizes at 2.03 V (vs SCE), lower than styrene 2a at 2.20 V, so the diketone is preferentially oxidized at the anode

10.1021_acs.orglett.5c00929.notes: Authors note that concerted proton-coupled electron transfer (loss of H+ and e- simultaneously) from 1a cannot be fully excluded as an alternative to stepwise deprotonation/oxidation to form the α-keto radical (II). Substrate scope (failure of 3ae-3ah) suggests aryl substitution on both diketone and alkene is required, consistent with stabilization of radical/cation intermediates.

10.1021_acs.orglett.4c01311_sup_1.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical three-component sulfonylation/arylation of styrenes with TsCl and arylboronic acids via a radical pathway
10.1021_acs.orglett.4c01311_sup_1.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation generates a sulfonyl radical (Ts•) from TsCl (or tosylate), which adds to the styrene to form a benzylic carbon radical; the aryl group from the arylboronic acid is then installed, likely via further anodic oxidation/transmetalation
10.1021_acs.orglett.4c01311_sup_1.electron_flow: Anodic oxidation generates a sulfonyl radical from TsCl; this Ts• adds across the styrene alkene to give a benzylic C-radical, which is then coupled with the aryl group derived from the arylboronic acid to deliver the 1,2-sulfonyl-aryl product.

key_intermediates.name: benzylic carbon radical from Ts• addition to p-F-styrene
TEMPO adduct of benzylic radical (m/z 390.1539 found 390.1552)
DMPO spin adduct of benzylic carbon radical (EPR g=2.0056, AN=15.9 G, AH=21.7 G)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction suppressed; TEMPO-Ts adduct (calc 268.1007, found 268.1003) and TEMPO-benzylic carbon radical adduct (calc 390.1539, found 390.1552) detected by HRMS

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction inhibited, supporting radical pathway

probe_reagents.name: DMPO
probe_reagents.observation: EPR spin adduct observed (g=2.0056, AN=15.9 G, AH=21.7 G), consistent with a carbon-centered radical (benzylic radical after Ts• addition)

10.1021_acs.orglett.4c01311_sup_1.notes: A separate Cl-radical trapping experiment using N,N-diallyl-4-methylbenzenesulfonamide with Et4NCl probes whether Cl• is generated under electrolysis (suggesting a possible parallel chlorine radical pathway from TsCl/chloride oxidation). Mechanism is consistent with anodic generation of Ts•, radical addition to styrene, then arylation via the arylboronic acid; the exact step for C–C bond formation with ArB(OH)2 (radical vs radical-polar crossover with oxidation of benzylic radical to cation) is not unambiguously assigned by the probes shown.

10.1039_D3EE02948D_sup_1.rxn_class_summary: EPR spin-trapping experiments using DMPO to detect carbonate radical (CO3•−) and peroxycarbonate radical (CO4•−) intermediates generated in the electrochemical/catalytic system.
10.1039_D3EE02948D_sup_1.electrochemical_role: DMPO spin trap captures reactive oxygen-centered radicals (CO3•− and CO4•−) generated in the system; the radicals are presumed to form via oxidation of (bi)carbonate/peroxycarbonate species, enabling EPR detection of the radical intermediates.
10.1039_D3EE02948D_sup_1.electron_flow: CO3•− or CO4•− adds to the α-C of the DMPO nitrone to give a transient DMPO-OCO2 or DMPO-OOCO2 adduct; subsequent hydrolysis releases HCO3− and yields the EPR-detectable DMPO-OH or DMPO-OOH spin adduct.

key_intermediates.name: DMPO-OCO2 adduct (carbonate addition to DMPO nitrone carbon)
DMPO-OH adduct (final EPR-active species after hydrolysis)
DMPO-OOH adduct (final EPR-active species after hydrolysis)

probe_reagents.name: DMPO (5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide)
probe_reagents.observation: Forms DMPO-OH adduct from CO3•− and DMPO-OOH adduct from CO4•−, both detected by EPR, confirming the presence of carbonate and peroxycarbonate radicals.

10.1039_D3EE02948D_sup_1.notes: The DMPO-OH and DMPO-OOH adducts observed by EPR are commonly attributed to •OH and •OOH directly; here the authors propose they arise via nucleophilic addition of CO3•−/CO4•− followed by hydrolytic loss of HCO3−, an alternative interpretation that should be considered when assigning EPR signals.

10.1021_acscatal.0c01841.rxn_class_summary: Visible-light photocatalytic dearomative [2+2] cycloaddition of N-(omega-alkenoyl)indoles with alkenes via energy transfer to give cyclobutane-fused indoline polycycles
10.1021_acscatal.0c01841.electron_flow: Ir(III) photocatalyst is excited by visible light and transfers triplet energy to the N-acylindole substrate, generating a triplet diradical localized at indole C2/C3; this attacks the tethered alkene in a tail-to-tail fashion to give a triplet 1,4-biradical, which crosses to the open-shelled singlet surface (MECP) and closes to the cyclobutane-fused indoline product. No net SET between PC and substrate occurs (substrate redox potentials lie outside PC range).
10.1021_acscatal.0c01841.rate_determining_step: C-C bond formation via tail-to-tail transition state TS1 (14.5 kcal/mol barrier from 1a-T1; head-to-head TS1' at 91.9 kcal/mol is disfavored by 14.9 kcal/mol)

key_intermediates.name: Triplet excited indole substrate 1a-T1 (sensitized via EnT from Ir photocatalyst)
Open-shelled singlet biradical INT1-OSS (after MECP1 spin crossing)

probe_reagents.name: 2,5-dimethylhexa-2,4-diene (triplet quencher)
probe_reagents.observation: Standard reaction strongly inhibited, yield drops to 2%, supporting a triplet energy-transfer mechanism

probe_reagents.name: Trifluoroethanol (CF3CH2OH) titration monitored by 13C NMR
probe_reagents.observation: Downfield shift and line broadening of the acyl carbonyl 13C signal upon adding TFE, indicating H-bonding between solvent and substrate carbonyl that lowers triplet energy

probe_reagents.name: Stern-Volmer quenching with 1a
probe_reagents.observation: Photocatalyst emission efficiently quenched by N-(omega-alkenoyl)indole 1a

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of 1a
probe_reagents.observation: E(ox) = 1.70 V vs SCE; no reductive peak observed - outside redox window of Ir PC (E1/2(III*/II)=1.21 V, E1/2(III*/IV)=-0.89 V), ruling out photoinduced electron transfer

10.1021_acscatal.0c01841.notes: Two competing cyclization modes were computed: favored tail-to-tail (TS1 77.0 kcal/mol, via INT1/INT1-OSS/TS2 to product 2a at 9.6 kcal/mol) vs disfavored head-to-head (TS1' 91.9 kcal/mol, via INT1'/INT1'-OSS/TS2'). Three undetected diastereomers (2a-A', 2a-B, 2a-B') were also located on the PES and shown to be kinetically/thermodynamically inaccessible. Mechanism involves two minimum energy crossing points (MECP1 48.4, MECP2 80.1 kcal/mol) between triplet and open-shelled singlet surfaces. H-bonding from CF3CH2OH solvent to the acyl carbonyl is proposed to lower the substrate triplet energy, enabling efficient EnT from Ir(dFCF3ppy)2(dtbbpy)PF6.

10.1021_acscatal.8b03302.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical oxidative alkoxysulfonylation of alkenes with sulfonyl hydrazines and alcohols to give beta-alkoxy sulfones
10.1021_acscatal.8b03302.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of sulfonyl hydrazine to a sulfonyl radical (via diazo intermediate, losing N2); second anodic oxidation of the beta-sulfonyl carbon radical to a benzyl cation; cathodic reduction of ROH/H+ to H2 and alkoxide
10.1021_acscatal.8b03302.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes the sulfonyl hydrazine (with -3H+/-3e-) to a diazo species that loses N2 to give a sulfonyl radical, which adds to the styrene to form a benzyl radical; this radical is further oxidized at the anode to a benzyl cation that is trapped by the alcohol and deprotonated, while protons and ROH are reduced at the cathode to H2 and alkoxide.

key_intermediates.name: sulfonyl diazo intermediate (R-SO2-N=N*) after anodic oxidation and deprotonation
beta-sulfonyl benzyl carbon radical from addition to styrene
beta-sulfonyl benzyl cation after second anodic oxidation

probe_reagents.name: alpha-cyclopropyl styrene (radical clock 2m)
probe_reagents.observation: Both direct beta-alkoxy sulfone 5m (41%) and ring-opened product 5m' (40%) isolated, indicating intermediacy of a carbon radical that undergoes cyclopropyl ring-opening

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of 1a and 2a
probe_reagents.observation: Oxidation peak of 1a at 1.91 V and 2a at 2.19 V (vs Ag/AgCl), indicating 1a (sulfonyl hydrazine) is oxidized preferentially at the anode

10.1021_acscatal.8b03302.notes: Catalyst-free, additive/oxidant-free electrolysis with carbon anode and Ni cathode in undivided cell; N2 and H2 are the only byproducts. Mechanism proposed on the basis of CV and radical clock evidence; no DFT, TEMPO trapping, or KIE reported.

10.1021_acscatal.8b03209.rxn_class_summary: Mn-catalyzed electrochemical chloroalkylation of alkenes using malononitriles/cyanoacetates and NaCl via anodically coupled electrolysis forming vicinal C-C and C-Cl bonds
10.1021_acscatal.8b03209.electrochemical_role: Two parallel anodic oxidations mediated by Mn: (i) Mn(III) oxidizes the malononitrile (with H+/e- transfer assisted by acetate) to a transient C-centered radical, and (ii) Mn(II) oxidizes Cl- to generate a persistent [Mn(III)]-Cl chlorine-atom-transfer species; cathode reduces HOAc to H2 and OAc-
10.1021_acscatal.8b03209.electron_flow: At the anode, Mn(II)(bpy)/OAc is oxidized to Mn(III), which in parallel oxidizes the deprotonated malononitrile to a transient free C-radical IV and oxidizes Cl- to a persistent [Mn(III)]-Cl. IV adds to the alkene to form transient radical V, which abstracts Cl from [Mn(III)]-Cl (atom transfer) to deliver the chloroalkylation product; HOAc is reduced to H2 at the Pt cathode generating OAc- that returns to the anodic compartment as ligand/base.

key_intermediates.name: benzylic C-radical adduct after IV addition to alkene (V)

probe_reagents.name: cyclopropyl-substituted alkene 36
probe_reagents.observation: Ring-opening occurred after first C-C bond formation to give product 37 in 79%, confirming C-centered radical intermediate

probe_reagents.name: chlorinated malononitrile 45
probe_reagents.observation: Formed in 53% without alkene; using 45 in place of 2 gave predominantly dichloride 4 (61%) with only 32% 3, ruling out Kharasch-type pathway

probe_reagents.name: divided-cell electrolysis with NaOAc in anodic chamber
probe_reagents.observation: Restored chloroalkylation reactivity (76% at N=1.0), excluding cathode-assisted radical formation (alt. path 2)

probe_reagents.name: NaCN vs NaCl competition
probe_reagents.observation: Only chloroalkylation product (82%) observed; no cyanoalkylation, excluding free carbocation intermediate that would react with more nucleophilic CN-

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry / DPV of Mn(OTf)2/bpy with HOAc/NaOAc/Cl-/2
probe_reagents.observation: New anodic wave at ~500 mV indicates Mn(II)/Mn(III) facilitated by OAc ligand; current enhancement on adding Cl- assigned to [Mn(II)]-Cl oxidation to [Mn(III)]-Cl

10.1021_acscatal.8b03209.notes: Two alternative pathways considered and excluded: (1) Kharasch-type Mn-catalyzed radical addition of pre-formed alpha-chloromalononitrile 45 to the alkene (ruled out by product distribution when 45 is used in place of 2, and by the no-current control); (2) cathode-assisted reduction of 45 to radical IV (ruled out by divided-cell experiment). A radical-polar crossover pathway via benzylic cation V+ and Cl- attack was also considered but disfavored by lack of acetoxylation/Ritter products and by NaCN/NaCl competition giving exclusive C-Cl product. Selectivity is rationalized by the persistent radical effect: transient IV adds fast to alkene; persistent [Mn(III)]-Cl then cross-couples with transient V.

10.1021_acscatal.0c03808.rxn_class_summary: Visible-light/Ir-photosensitized energy-transfer dearomatization of indole-tethered O-methyl oximes leading divergently to indoline-fused azetidines via [2+2] cycloaddition or to benzindolizidines via 1,5-hydrogen atom transfer.
10.1021_acscatal.0c03808.electron_flow: Ir(dFppy)3 is excited by blue LEDs and transfers triplet energy to the indole-tethered oxime, generating a triplet diradical localized on the indole C2/C3; intramolecular C-N bond formation with the oxime gives a triplet 1,4-diradical that undergoes ISC (MECP1) to the open-shelled singlet diradical, which either closes by radical-radical combination (TS2, low barrier, kinetic [2+2] azetidine) or undergoes 1,5-HAT (TS3, higher barrier, thermodynamic benzindolizidine); for C3-alkyl substrates the [2+2] adduct is re-opened under light back to the diradical and funneled to the HAT product.
10.1021_acscatal.0c03808.rate_determining_step: For the divergence, the C-C bond-forming [2+2] closure proceeds via TS2 (41.6 kcal/mol, ΔG‡ ≈ 3.4 kcal/mol from INT1-OSS, kinetic product) while 1,5-HAT via TS3 (55.5 kcal/mol, ΔG‡ ≈ 17.3 kcal/mol) is the higher-barrier, thermodynamically controlled step; reversible visible-light-promoted C-N homolysis of the [2+2] adduct ultimately controls product distribution.

key_intermediates.name: Triplet diradical of indole-oxime substrate (3a-T1) after energy transfer, spin density at C2/C3 with elongated C2-C3 (1.51 Å)
Cyclized triplet diradical INT1 (C2-N(oxime) bond formed, radicals on C3 and on oxime carbon)
Open-shelled singlet diradical INT1-OSS (after ISC via MECP1)

probe_reagents.name: Stern-Volmer quenching with substrate 1a vs indoline analogue 1a'
probe_reagents.observation: 1a efficiently quenches Ir(dFppy)3* luminescence (Ksv = 54750), while 1a' does not (slope ≈ -250), indicating energy/electron transfer requires the indole chromophore.

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of 1a
probe_reagents.observation: Redox potentials inconsistent with photoredox SET; supports energy-transfer mechanism via triplet state.

probe_reagents.name: Dark control / no photosensitizer control
probe_reagents.observation: 0% yield without light or without Ir(dFppy)3, confirming photosensitizer- and light-dependence.

probe_reagents.name: Isolation of [2+2] adduct 4f' and time-course 1H NMR monitoring of 3f
probe_reagents.observation: 4f' isolated and X-ray characterized (CCDC 2011330); under light it is reconsumed to regenerate 3f and accumulate 4f, demonstrating reversible [2+2] and irreversible 1,5-HAT.

probe_reagents.name: 4f' stability test in absence of light/Ir(dFppy)3
probe_reagents.observation: 4f' is stable without light or photosensitizer, showing that C-N homolysis is a visible-light-promoted process.

probe_reagents.name: DFT calculations at (U)B3LYP/6-31G(d,p)
probe_reagents.observation: Located T1 (3a-T1 53.0), TS1/TS1' (62.7/64.5), INT1 (38.4), MECP1 (42.2), INT1-OSS (38.2), TS2 (41.6, [2+2]) and TS3 (55.5, 1,5-HAT); supports kinetic vs thermodynamic divergence.

10.1021_acscatal.0c03808.notes: Two competing pathways from the same open-shelled singlet diradical INT1-OSS: [2+2] cycloaddition (kinetic, favored for R'=H/Me without C3-alkyl, gives indoline-fused azetidines) vs 1,5-HAT (thermodynamic, requires C3-alkyl substituent providing the transferable C-H, gives benzindolizidines). Selectivity is switched by substrate substitution (C3-H vs C3-alkyl) and by reaction time / dilution; the [2+2] adduct is a kinetically formed but photochemically reversible intermediate that funnels to the HAT product. Mechanism proceeds via energy transfer (triplet sensitization), not photoredox SET, as supported by Stern-Volmer and CV. Bridged indene-fused substrate 3o gives only [2+2] product (4o') because the geometry precludes 1,5-HAT.

10.1021_acscatal.3c01244.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical cobalt-catalyzed cross-electrophile coupling of aryl/alkyl bromides with chlorogermanes (Me3GeCl) to form Csp2-Ge and Csp3-Ge bonds (germylation).
10.1021_acscatal.3c01244.electrochemical_role: Cathodic reduction of the bpy-Co(III)(acac) precatalyst to generate low-valent Co(0)/Co(I) species that activate Me3GeCl and Ar/Alkyl-Br sequentially; cathode also regenerates active Co(0) from Co(I) after reductive elimination.
10.1021_acscatal.3c01244.electron_flow: The cathode reduces bpy-Co(III) to Co(0), which preferentially undergoes oxidative addition with Me3GeCl to give a Ge-Co(II)-Cl species; further cathodic reduction gives Ge-Co(I), which engages Ar-Br by oxidative addition or alkyl-Br by inner-sphere halogen abstraction/radical recombination to a Co(III)(GeMe3)(R) species. Reductive elimination forms the C-Ge bond and releases Co(I), which is reduced back to Co(0) at the cathode.

key_intermediates.name: Ge-Co(II)-Cl intermediate from oxidative addition of Me3GeCl (B)
Ge-Co(I) species after cathodic reduction of B (C)
Co(III) intermediate bearing Ar/Alkyl and GeMe3 (D)
Co(I) species (E) released after reductive elimination
alkyl radical from inner-sphere halogen abstraction

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Product 3a still formed in 62% yield (vs 93% without TEMPO); no TEMPO-GeMe3 adduct observed, suggesting germanium radical is not a free intermediate.

probe_reagents.name: 1,1-diphenylethylene (DPE)
probe_reagents.observation: Product 3a obtained in 72%; Me3Ge-CH2-CHPh2 type trapping product not detected, indicating no free Ge radical.

probe_reagents.name: Radical clock alkyl bromide (allyl ether-tethered bromide)
probe_reagents.observation: Cyclized/germylated products 5t (36%) and 5u (39%) obtained, demonstrating that alkyl radicals are involved in the Csp3 germylation.

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of [bpy-Co(III)](acac)3, Co(acac)2/3, and electrophiles
probe_reagents.observation: Reduction peaks at -0.60 (Co(III)/Co(II)), -1.60 (Co(II)/Co(I)), -1.83 V (Co(I)/Co(0)) vs SCE; electrophile reductions (Ar-Br -2.62, alkyl-Br -2.64, Me3GeCl -2.81 V) are more negative than Co reductions. Co(I)/Co(0) peak current grows with added electrophiles and reversibility is lost, indicating Co(0) reacts preferentially with Me3GeCl.

10.1021_acscatal.3c01244.notes: An alternative pathway is proposed (and shown in Figure 2e) in which Co(0) first reacts with Ar/Alkyl-Br to give Co(II)-R (F), then cathodic reduction to Co(I)-R (G), followed by SN2-type oxidative addition of Me3GeCl to give Co(III) (H) and reductive elimination. CV data indicate the main pathway (Me3GeCl activation first) is preferred because Co(0) shows the largest current enhancement with added germanium chloride and the Co(I)/Co(0) and Co(II)/Co(I) reversible waves vanish upon addition of electrophiles.

10.1021_acscatal.1c05557.rxn_class_summary: Electrocatalytic oxidative hydrofunctionalization of alkenes (hydroalkoxylation, hydroacyloxylation, hydroarylation, semipinacol rearrangement, deallylation) via a CoSalen-mediated Co(II/III/IV) HAT cycle.
10.1021_acscatal.1c05557.electrochemical_role: Anode oxidizes Co(II) to Co(III) (and later alkyl-Co(III) to alkyl-Co(IV)); cathode reduces MeOH to H2 and methoxide, which activates the silane to generate Co(III)-H for HAT to the alkene.
10.1021_acscatal.1c05557.electron_flow: Anodic oxidation of Co(II) gives Co(III), which is converted to Co(III)-H by silane/methoxide; Co-H performs HAT to the alkene to give a C-radical that is trapped by Co(II) to form an alkyl-Co(III); a second anodic oxidation gives an electrophilic alkyl-Co(IV) that is intercepted by a nucleophile (alcohol, carboxylate, arene) near the anode, releasing product and regenerating Co(II).

probe_reagents.name: CD3OD solvent labeling
probe_reagents.observation: Very low D incorporation in product (<2% D) when CD3OD used vs Ph2SiD2 (70-80% D), confirming hydride comes from silane and indicating H/D scrambling via reversible HAT.

probe_reagents.name: aryl cyclopropane radical clock (14)
probe_reagents.observation: Ring-opened trapping product (15) observed in 40% yield, supporting a discrete C-radical intermediate from HAT.

probe_reagents.name: Chiral CoSalen (3b, 3d)
probe_reagents.observation: Catalyst-controlled enantioselectivity (8% ee with 3b; -31% ee with 3d; up to -20% ee with RVC anode) indicates C-Nu bond formation occurs at a Co-bound intermediate (Co(IV)) rather than a free cation.

probe_reagents.name: Electrolyte variation (n-Bu4NBF4 vs LiClO4)
probe_reagents.observation: More hydrophobic NBu4BF4 gives lower methanol-trapping selectivity (1.9:1) vs LiClO4 (>20:1), consistent with Co(IV) trapping near the electrode within the EDL.

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of 3a
probe_reagents.observation: Reversible oxidation at E1/2 = -0.17 V vs Fc+/Fc; reduction wave disappears upon addition of KOMe, indicating methoxide is needed for silane activation/Co-H formation.

10.1021_acscatal.1c05557.notes: Authors favor an ECEC mechanism with a discrete cationic Co(IV)-alkyl (24) trapped by nucleophile near the anode. Catalyst-controlled stereoselectivity and electrolyte (EDL) effects rule out a free carbocation diffusing into bulk. Side pathways include alkene isomerization and hydrogenation, attributed to back-HAT from the alkyl radical to Co(II) or deprotonation of Co(IV) complex 24. 1,1-disubstituted alkenes give predominantly isomerization, ascribed to disfavored Co(IV) formation.

10.1021_acscatal.0c02607.rxn_class_summary: Enantioselective copper-catalyzed intramolecular alkene carboamination and carboetherification of alkenylsulfonamides/alcohols using O2 (air or 10% O2) as the sole stoichiometric oxidant
10.1021_acscatal.0c02607.electron_flow: Cu(II)·L coordinates the tethered heteroatom nucleophile and alkene; intramolecular C-X bond formation generates a primary carbon radical with concomitant reduction to Cu(I); the C-radical adds onto the pendant arene and rearomatizes to give the carbocyclized product, while Cu(I) is reoxidized to Cu(II) by O2 to close the catalytic cycle.

key_intermediates.name: Cu(II)-bis(oxazoline) complex coordinated to alkene/sulfonamide substrate
primary carbon radical formed after intramolecular C-X bond formation (X = O, NSO2Ar) onto alkene
cyclized C-radical adding intramolecularly onto aryl ring of N-tosyl/aryl group
Cu(I) species reoxidized by O2 to Cu(II)

probe_reagents.name: K2CO3 additive control
probe_reagents.observation: Little conversion (entry 8); proposed to sequester protons required for O2 -> H2O reduction, impeding Cu reoxidation

probe_reagents.name: O2 partial-pressure variation (1 atm O2 vs air vs 10% O2)
probe_reagents.observation: Lower O2 (air/10%) suppresses competitive intermolecular trapping of C-radical by O2 (aldehyde 4, lactam 5) and improves sultam 2 yield

probe_reagents.name: Anaerobic control (no O2, entry 7)
probe_reagents.observation: Mostly starting material with traces of sultam 2 and aminochloride 3; confirms O2 is required for catalytic turnover (Cu(I)->Cu(II) reoxidation)

probe_reagents.name: Achiral vs chiral bis(oxazoline) ligand screen (L1 vs L2)
probe_reagents.observation: CuCl·L1 gives racemic sultam in good yield under air/10% O2; Cu(OTf)2·L2 under O2 gives poor reactivity and ee with competing aminooxygenation/aminochlorination

10.1021_acscatal.0c02607.notes: Mechanism is inferred by analogy to the authors' prior MnO2-oxidant carboamination/carboetherification (ref 13) and TEMPO/O2 aminooxygenation (ref 10/11a) systems shown in Scheme 1A/B. The paper focuses on demonstrating O2 (air) as terminal oxidant in place of MnO2 or TEMPO/O2; detailed mechanistic probes (TEMPO trapping, radical clocks, KIE, DFT) are not performed on this page. Side pathways (aminooxygenation to aldehyde 4, gamma-lactam 5, aminochlorination 3) are diagnostic of a discrete C-radical intermediate. Counterion (OTf vs Cl/carboxylate) and oxidant identity strongly influence whether enantioselective carboamination or racemic/competing pathways dominate.

10.1021_acscatal.3c02072_sup_1.rxn_class_summary: Cobalt-catalyzed electrochemical C-H activation/annulation of 8-aminoquinoline benzamides with allenylphosphine oxides to form isoquinolinone-type products
10.1021_acscatal.3c02072_sup_1.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of Co(II) to Co(III) at the graphite felt anode; cathodic H2 evolution at Pt; the high-valent Co species mediates C-H metalation/migratory insertion/C-N bond formation
10.1021_acscatal.3c02072_sup_1.electron_flow: The GF anode oxidizes the Co(II)/benzamide complex to a Co(III) species, which undergoes carboxylate-assisted (NaOPiv) C-H metalation of the benzamide; migratory insertion of the allene followed by C-N reductive elimination yields the product, while protons are reduced to H2 at the Pt cathode.
10.1021_acscatal.3c02072_sup_1.rate_determining_step: C-H metalation is partially rate-influencing but not fully rate-limiting (small KIE ~1.2 suggests C-H cleavage is not solely rate-determining)

key_intermediates.name: Cobalt(III) complex bearing chiral oxazoline ligand L3, benzamide-derived cyclometalated aryl, and 4-methoxypyridine coordinated (compound 45)
Co(II) precatalyst Co(OAc)2·4H2O ligated by L3
Cyclometalated Co(III)-aryl after concerted metalation-deprotonation (CMD) of benzamide C-H

probe_reagents.name: [D5]-benzamide 1 (intermolecular competition with non-deuterated 1)
probe_reagents.observation: PH/PD ≈ 1.2 from 1H NMR of product, indicating C-H cleavage is not the rate-determining step

probe_reagents.name: 4-methoxypyridine (as donor ligand to trap Co(III) intermediate)
probe_reagents.observation: Stoichiometric electrolysis (1.0 equiv Co, 1.0 equiv L3, 1.0 mA, 12 h) furnished isolable Co(III) complex 45 in 12% yield, supporting a Co(III) cyclometalated intermediate

10.1021_acscatal.3c02072_sup_1.notes: Only two mechanistic probes shown on this page (KIE and isolation of a Co(III) intermediate via stoichiometric electrolysis). The small KIE (~1.2) argues against C-H activation being rate-determining; the isolated Co(III) complex 45 supports an inner-sphere electrocatalytic Co(II)/Co(III) cycle with carboxylate-assisted CMD.

10.1021_acscatal.9b02348.rxn_class_summary: Copper-electrocatalyzed C-H alkynylation/annulation of benzamides bearing 8-aminoquinoline directing group with terminal alkynes (or alkynyl carboxylic acids) to form isoindolones, with H2 as the sole byproduct.
10.1021_acscatal.9b02348.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of Cu(I)/Cu(II) intermediates to Cu(III) drives carboxylate-assisted C-H activation and reductive elimination; cathode reduces protons to H2
10.1021_acscatal.9b02348.electron_flow: Substrate coordinates to Cu(II); anodic oxidation gives Cu(III) carboxylate which performs CMD C-H activation on the benzamide; ligand exchange with terminal alkyne and reductive elimination delivers the ortho-alkynylated arene and Cu(I); Cu(I) is reoxidized to Cu(II) at the anode (PCET) while protons are reduced to H2 at the cathode; the alkynylated intermediate cyclizes to the isoindolone.
10.1021_acscatal.9b02348.rate_determining_step: Not C-H cleavage (KIE ≈ 1.2 and no H/D scrambling); likely the anodic Cu oxidation / subsequent organometallic step

key_intermediates.name: Cu(III) cyclometalated intermediate 7 after CMD C-H activation
Cu(III)-alkynyl/aryl complex 8 after ligand exchange with terminal alkyne
Cu(I) species (CuOPiv) 9 released after reductive elimination
isoindolone product 3 from intramolecular hydroamidation/cyclization of 4

probe_reagents.name: para-substituted benzamide competition (1l CF3 vs 1i Me; 2g CF3-aryl alkyne vs 2b tolyl alkyne)
probe_reagents.observation: Electron-poor substrates react preferentially (3la/3ia = 3.2; 3ag/3ab = 2.3), inconsistent with SET-based anilide oxidation and consistent with concerted metalation-deprotonation

probe_reagents.name: CD3OD cosolvent
probe_reagents.observation: <5% D incorporation into recovered 1a and into product 3aa; H/D exchange not observed, indicating C-H cleavage is irreversible and not rate-limiting

probe_reagents.name: [D5]-1a (perdeuterated benzamide)
probe_reagents.observation: Parallel in operando React-IR gave kH/kD ≈ 1.2, supporting facile (non-rate-limiting) C-H scission

probe_reagents.name: independently prepared ortho-alkynylated benzamide 4
probe_reagents.observation: Delivered 3aa in 81% under electrolysis and 80% without electricity, confirming 4 as on-cycle intermediate and that cyclization is non-electrochemical

probe_reagents.name: headspace GC
probe_reagents.observation: Detected H2 as sole gaseous byproduct, consistent with cathodic proton reduction

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of Cu(OAc)2 with NaOAc and 1a
probe_reagents.observation: Cu(II)+1a shows oxidation at 0.95 V vs SCE (assigned to Cu(II)→Cu(III)); CuOAc reoxidation at 0.05 V; 1a itself shows no competitive oxidation, ruling out substrate SET

probe_reagents.name: in operando React-IR kinetics
probe_reagents.observation: Monitored growth of 3aa (1721 cm-1) and decay of 1a, enabling initial-rate KIE determination

10.1021_acscatal.9b02348.notes: Authors explicitly exclude a substrate SET-oxidation (radical cation) pathway based on Hammett-type competition (electron-poor benzamides faster) and CV (no oxidation of 1a in the operating window), distinguishing this Cu electrocatalysis from prior anilide SET functionalizations. The protocol is extended to decarboxylative C-H/C-C scission with alkynyl carboxylic acids 11 (Scheme 8), presumed via in situ decarboxylative generation of a Cu-acetylide that enters the same Cu(I)/Cu(III) cycle. Final isoindolone formation is a thermal (non-electrochemical) intramolecular hydroamidation of intermediate 4.

10.1021_acscatal.3c02072.rxn_class_summary: Cobalt-catalyzed enantioselective electrochemical C-H annulation of benzamides/diarylphosphinic amides with allenes to afford C-N axially chiral isoquinolinone/phosphinamide products
10.1021_acscatal.3c02072.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation selectively oxidizes Co(II) to Co(III) catalyst, enabling chiral cobaltacycle formation without chemical oxidant; Co(III)/Co(I)/Co(II) redox cycle supported by CV
10.1021_acscatal.3c02072.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes Co(II)/L complex to Co(III), enabling concerted metalation-deprotonation (CMD) C-H activation to form chiral cobaltacycle; allene migratory insertion followed by C-N reductive elimination releases product and Co(I), which is reoxidized at the anode back to Co(III)/Co(II).
10.1021_acscatal.3c02072.rate_determining_step: Not C-H cleavage (KIE PH/PD ≈ 1.2 indicates C-H activation is not rate-determining); likely allene insertion or subsequent reductive elimination

key_intermediates.name: Co(III) cobaltacycle from benzamide with 4-methoxypyridine and chiral oxazoline ligand L3 (intermediate 45)
Co(III) cobaltacycle from diarylphosphinic amide with L2 and 4-methoxypyridine (intermediate 46)
Co(I) species generated after C-N bond-forming reductive elimination

probe_reagents.name: [D5]-benzamide substrate (d-substrate)
probe_reagents.observation: Intermolecular competition gave PH/PD ≈ 1.2, indicating C-H cleavage is not the rate-determining step

probe_reagents.name: 4-methoxypyridine
probe_reagents.observation: Stabilized Co(III) cobaltacycles 45 and 46, allowing their isolation

probe_reagents.name: Isolated cobaltacycle 45
probe_reagents.observation: Stoichiometric reaction with allene 2 + NaOPiv delivered product 3 in 52% yield, 99% ee, supporting catalytic relevance

probe_reagents.name: Isolated cobaltacycle 46
probe_reagents.observation: Stoichiometric reaction with allene 2 + NaOPiv gave product 31 in 41% yield, 99% ee

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of Co(OAc)2/NaOPiv/L3 ± substrate 1
probe_reagents.observation: Co(OAc)2/NaOPiv shows no relevant redox; addition of L3 produces clear oxidation waves (mono- and bis-L3 Co species); reversible wave at 0.8 V vs SCE becomes less reversible upon adding substrate 1, supporting Co(III)/Co(I)/Co(II) catalytic pathway

probe_reagents.name: Thermal racemization of product 3
probe_reagents.observation: Racemization barrier 39.4 kcal/mol, t1/2 ≈ 2.7×10^8 years at 25 °C — confirms configurational stability of C-N axis

10.1021_acscatal.3c02072.notes: 4-Methoxypyridine is used as an ancillary ligand to stabilize otherwise unstable Co(III) cobaltacycle intermediates for isolation/characterization. The authors explicitly favor a Co(III)/Co(I)/Co(II) electrocatalytic cycle in which anodic oxidation replaces a stoichiometric chemical oxidant; this is also implemented in a continuous-flow electrochemical reactor enabling decagram-scale synthesis without supporting electrolyte.

10.1021_acscatal.0c01413.rxn_class_summary: Enantioselective visible-light [2+2] photocycloaddition between aryl enones and alkenes, organocatalyzed by a chiral primary-secondary diamine via an intramolecular charge-transfer iminium ion intermediate
10.1021_acscatal.0c01413.electron_flow: Acid-promoted condensation of diamine 3d with enone gives iminium A-1 bearing a tethered naphthyl donor; visible light populates a CT excited state B-2 (naphthyl->iminium SET) that thermally equilibrates with the iminium-localized singlet B-1, which adds to alkene 2a to form 1,4-biradical C-1, ring-closes to cyclobutyl iminium D-1, and hydrolyzes to release cyclobutane 4 and regenerate catalyst. With Ru(bpy)3Cl2, an external triplet energy transfer to the iminium gives B-1^T and a longer-lived triplet biradical, raising dr.

key_intermediates.name: Iminium ion A-1 (condensation of enone 1a with diamine catalyst 3d, with pendant naphthyl donor)
Singlet charge-transfer excited state B-2 (CT^S, S1, naphthyl->iminium SET)
Singlet iminium-localized excited state B-1 (LE^S, S2), reactive species for [2+2]
Singlet 1,4-biradical C-1 (tertiary radical at iminium C, primary radical on alkene-derived carbon)
Alternative biradical C-1' (primary radical at iminium side, tertiary on alkene side) from CT excitation, 20.6 kcal/mol higher in energy
Triplet iminium-localized excited state B-1^T (accessed only with Ru(bpy)3 sensitization via triplet energy transfer)
Triplet 1,4-biradical (longer-lived, gives higher dr after ISC)

probe_reagents.name: Ru(bpy)3Cl2·6H2O (triplet photosensitizer)
probe_reagents.observation: Switches mechanism to triplet pathway; dr increases from 2:1 (singlet, direct CT excitation) to 5:1 for 4k, while er remains comparable, supporting iminium intermediacy in both regimes

probe_reagents.name: Catalysts 3g/3h lacking naphthyl donor
probe_reagents.observation: No bathochromic CT absorption band; negligible conversion under 456 nm without sensitizer, confirming the intramolecular CT transition is responsible for visible-light photoactivity

probe_reagents.name: Achiral diamine 3g + Ru(bpy)3Cl2 control (Scheme 1a)
probe_reagents.observation: Triplet sensitization gives 71% conversion of 4a (vs 10% without Ru), demonstrating that the iminium can be triplet-sensitized; racemic since catalyst is achiral

probe_reagents.name: Bulky achiral diamine 3i + Ru(bpy)3Cl2 control (Scheme 1b)
probe_reagents.observation: 36% conversion to racemic 4a vs 5% without Ru, again supporting triplet-sensitized iminium reactivity

probe_reagents.name: (Z)-4-phenyl-3-buten-2-one
probe_reagents.observation: Gives same cyclobutane 4a (84%, 80:20 er) via organocatalyzed Z->E isomerization, consistent with iminium intermediate

probe_reagents.name: X-ray of hydrazone 5a derived from 4a
probe_reagents.observation: Establishes absolute configuration of cyclobutane product

probe_reagents.name: O2 (air) tolerance test
probe_reagents.observation: Air did not inhibit the reaction; full conversion under air, arguing against a long-lived freely diffusing triplet/radical in the CT pathway

10.1021_acscatal.0c01413.notes: Two excited-state pathways are considered: (favored) singlet iminium-localized excited state B-1 (LE^S, S2) reacting via a singlet biradical C-1 with concerted-or-stepwise [2+2], and an alternative CT excited state B-2 (S1) that is discarded as unproductive because (i) its derived biradical C-1' is 20.6 kcal/mol less stable than C-1 and would give wrong regiochemistry, and (ii) ISC from singlet iminium to triplet is slow by El-Sayed's rule. The CT state's role is to provide the visible-light absorption band that then funnels via thermal S1<->S2 equilibrium into the reactive LE singlet. With external Ru(bpy)3Cl2 (ET=46 kcal/mol matching the singlet-triplet gap of A-1), a parallel triplet-energy-transfer pathway operates, giving a longer-lived triplet biradical and higher dr (5:1 vs 2:1 for 4k). Solvent polarity tunes the CT^S/LE^S equilibrium. TDDFT (CAM-B3LYP/6-31G*) on M06-2X/6-311G** structures supports HOMO(naphthyl)->LUMO(iminium) CT assignment; no pi-stacking between donor and acceptor is required.

10.1021_acscatal.2c05186.rxn_class_summary: Electrocatalytic 1,2-diazidation of alkenes mediated by a second-sphere H-bonding manganese amidoporphyrin complex using NaN3 as the azide source
10.1021_acscatal.2c05186.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of [Mn(L1)N3] (and/or free N3-) generates an azidyl radical and a higher-valent diazidomanganese(IV) species that delivers the second azide; cathode reduces protons to H2
10.1021_acscatal.2c05186.electron_flow: [Mn(L1)Cl] undergoes ligand exchange with N3- to form [Mn(III)(L1)N3], which is anodically oxidized (and/or free N3- is oxidized) to release an azidyl radical that adds to the alkene; the resulting C-radical then abstracts a second azide via azidyl transfer from the anodically generated [Mn(IV)(L1)(N3)2] to deliver the 1,2-diazide, while protons are reduced to H2 at the Pt cathode.

key_intermediates.name: Carbon-centered β-azido alkyl radical (II) after N3• addition to alkene

probe_reagents.name: Tetrabutylammonium azide (TBAN3)
probe_reagents.observation: Oxidation wave at Ep/2 = +453 mV vs Fc+/0; mixing with [Mn(L1)Cl] gave a new redox peak at E1/2 = +155 mV assigned to [Mn(L1)N3], showing ligand exchange lowers MnIII oxidation potential by >600 mV

probe_reagents.name: [Mn(L1)Cl] complex
probe_reagents.observation: CV shows two overlapping one-electron oxidations above +600 mV; UV-vis titration with NaN3 confirms quantitative formation of [Mn(L1)N3]

probe_reagents.name: DFT model [Mn(L3)N3] and [Mn(L3)(N3)2]
probe_reagents.observation: B3LYP-D3(BJ)/def2-SVP calculations identify two N-H···N H-bonds between amide groups and Mn-bound azide (QTAIM BCPs); supports second-sphere stabilization of key intermediates

probe_reagents.name: Chiral D2-symmetric amidoporphyrin Mn catalysts [Mn(L4)Cl] and [Mn(L5)Cl]
probe_reagents.observation: [Mn(L5)Cl] gave 13% ee in diazidation of 4-tert-butylstyrene, implying second azidyl transfer occurs from the chiral Mn azide complex rather than via free diffusion

10.1021_acscatal.2c05186.notes: Authors consider two parallel initiation pathways leading to the same azidyl radical: (i) anodic oxidation of [Mn(L1)N3] to [Mn(L1)(N3)2] followed by homolytic fragmentation, and (ii) direct anodic oxidation of free N3- (both feasible since anode potential reaches +420 mV vs Fc+/0). The second C-N bond is proposed to form by azidyl group transfer from [Mn(IV)(L1)(N3)2] to the transient carbon radical rather than by radical-radical coupling, which is statistically disfavored. Overall cycle formulated as MnIII/MnIV. Second-sphere amide N-H···N3 H-bonds are invoked to stabilize Mn-azide intermediates and may reduce N3 nucleophilicity (no SN2/E2 side products with tosylate substrate 13).

10.1021_acscatal.4c02320.rxn_class_summary: Photoelectrochemical carboamidation of styrenes with unactivated alkanes via TBADT HAT, radical addition, oxidative radical-polar crossover, and Ritter-type amidation
10.1021_acscatal.4c02320.electrochemical_role: Anode reoxidizes the reduced TBADT (H+[W10O32]5-/2H+[W10O32]6-) back to [W10O32]4- and oxidizes the benzylic radical intermediate to a benzyl cation (oxidative radical polar crossover); cathode evolves H2
10.1021_acscatal.4c02320.electron_flow: Photoexcited TBADT abstracts a C-H from the alkane to give an alkyl radical that adds to the styrene, forming a benzylic radical; the anode (assisted by trace wO) oxidizes this radical to a benzyl cation (oxidative RPC), which is trapped by R2CN (Ritter) and hydrolyzed to the amide. The reduced TBADT is reoxidized at the anode while protons are reduced to H2 at the cathode.

key_intermediates.name: alkyl radical R1• from HAT on alkane
nitrilium ion from R2CN trapping benzyl cation

10.1021_acscatal.4c02320.notes: Cell potential was deliberately kept below the styrene oxidation potential to suppress direct anodic SET of the alkene, ensuring the HAT-initiated pathway dominates over previously reported styrene radical-cation pathways (e.g., Lambert's TAC+ chemistry). Mechanism shown is the proposal in Figure 1c; additional probe/control experiments likely appear later in the paper but are not in the provided page.

10.1021_acscatal.1c05557_sup_1.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical cobalt-catalyzed hydrofunctionalization (hydromethoxylation/hydroalkoxylation) of alkenes proceeding via metal-hydride hydrogen atom transfer (MHAT) and radical-polar crossover
10.1021_acscatal.1c05557_sup_1.electrochemical_role: Cathodic reduction regenerates the active Co(II/I) catalyst; Co-H is generated from Ph2SiH2 and delivers H• to alkene (MHAT); the carbon radical is further oxidized (anodically or via Co) to a carbocation that is trapped by MeOH/ROH
10.1021_acscatal.1c05557_sup_1.electron_flow: Electrochemically generated low-valent Co reacts with Ph2SiH2 to form a Co-H; HAT to alkene gives a carbon radical, which is oxidized to a carbocation and trapped by the alcohol nucleophile (MeOH) to give the ether; Co is regenerated cathodically.

probe_reagents.name: CD3OD (deuterated methanol)
probe_reagents.observation: Product 13 (cyclized THF) showed <2% D incorporation at the methyl-derived position, indicating the H delivered to the alkene comes from Ph2SiH2 (Co-H) and not from the alcohol solvent

probe_reagents.name: (1-cyclopropyl-1-phenyl)alkene / vinylcyclopropane 14
probe_reagents.observation: Ring-opened product 15 (homoallyl methyl ether, 40%) obtained, consistent with a carbon-centered radical intermediate undergoing cyclopropane ring opening before C-O bond formation

probe_reagents.name: n-Bu4NBF4 electrolyte
probe_reagents.observation: Electrolyte identity affects hydromethoxylation efficiency (Figure 5e), supporting electrochemical involvement

10.1021_acscatal.1c05557_sup_1.notes: Additional mechanistic probes include CV studies (III-A), attempts at asymmetric catalysis (III-D) which failed — consistent with a free (non-Co-bound) carbocation/radical intermediate after MHAT, supporting the radical-polar crossover pathway rather than a Co-alkyl inner-sphere ionization.

10.1038_s42004-022-00697-1.rxn_class_summary: Electroreductive hydrodifluoromethylation of unactivated, conjugated, and electron-rich alkenes (and alkynes) using sulfonyl-CF2H reagents with H2O or PhSH as H-source, via a two-pronged radical-polar crossover or HAT mechanism
10.1038_s42004-022-00697-1.electrochemical_role: Cathodic single-electron reduction of ArSO2CF2H (Ph- or 2-pyridyl-sulfonyl difluoromethane) cleaves the S-C bond to release CF2H radical; sacrificial Zn anode is oxidized to Zn(2+) to balance charge
10.1038_s42004-022-00697-1.electron_flow: Cathode delivers one electron to ArSO2CF2H, fragmenting it into ArSO2– and a CF2H radical that adds to the alkene to give carbon radical D; for EWG/aryl alkenes D is further reduced at the cathode to carbanion E which is protonated by H2O (path A, ECEC radical-polar crossover), whereas for alkyl/EDG alkenes D abstracts H from PhSH (path B, HAT). The Zn anode is sacrificially oxidized to Zn(2+).

key_intermediates.name: phenyl- or 2-pyridyl-sulfonyl difluoromethane (CF2H radical precursor, A)
carbon-centered (often benzylic) radical D from CF2H addition to alkene
carbanion E from further cathodic reduction of D (path A, radical-polar crossover)
thiyl radical PhS• (from PhSH after HAT, path B)
vinyl radical intermediate from CF2H addition to alkyne (Int-10v / pre-16)

probe_reagents.name: D2O instead of H2O
probe_reagents.observation: 58% D incorporation at the benzylic position, consistent with protonation of carbanion E by water (path A)

probe_reagents.name: piperidine-d11
probe_reagents.observation: Only 10% D incorporation, ruling out amine α-C–H as the dominant H source

probe_reagents.name: vinylcyclopropane 6a (R1=Ph)
probe_reagents.observation: Ring-opened product 8a formed (36%); fast benzyl radical opening (k~10^8 s^-1) outcompetes reduction

probe_reagents.name: vinylcyclopropane 6b (R1=H)
probe_reagents.observation: Cyclopropane intact (7b, 99%); slower opening (k~10^5 s^-1) shows reduction of benzylic radical (Int-8) is faster than ring-opening

probe_reagents.name: diallyl tosylamide 13
probe_reagents.observation: Cyclized pyrrolidine 14 obtained (88%, dr 3:1), confirming radical intermediacy

probe_reagents.name: voltaic profile monitoring (Eanode, Ecathode during electrolysis)
probe_reagents.observation: Ecathode ≈ –1.8 V matches onset of reduction of 2 (Ered = –2.2 V, Eonset ≈ –1.7 V); also overlaps reduction of benzylic radical 4→carbanion 5 (Ered ≈ –2.0 V), supporting radical-polar crossover

probe_reagents.name: control without H2O
probe_reagents.observation: Yield drops to 2%, confirming H2O is the proton source in path A

probe_reagents.name: control without applied current
probe_reagents.observation: No reaction, confirming requirement for electrochemical SET

probe_reagents.name: 2-pySO2CF2H (2-py reagent, less negative Ered)
probe_reagents.observation: Required for highly conjugated/electron-poor alkenes (e.g. 1,3-butadiene 9v) because milder reduction potential enables RPC of stabilized radical Int-10v

10.1038_s42004-022-00697-1.notes: Two pathways are explicitly proposed depending on alkene electronics: path A (radical-polar crossover, cathodic reduction of benzylic/EWG-stabilized radical D to carbanion E, then protonation by H2O) is favored for aryl/EWG-substituted alkenes with PhSO2CF2H or 2-pySO2CF2H; path B (HAT from PhSH) is favored for aliphatic/electron-rich alkenes where Ered(D/E) is too negative. Sacrificial Zn anode is essential (Mg and Al gave only 7% and 9% yield). Choice of CF2H precursor (Ph vs 2-py sulfonyl) tunes the reduction potential to match substrate radical Ered. Double hydrodifluoromethylation of arylalkynes proceeds via first HAT to vinyl radical, then RPC for the second addition, giving 1,1,3,3-tetrafluoropropan-2-yl groups.

10.1038_s42004-022-00697-1_si1.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical hydrodifluoromethylation of alkenes (and double hydrodifluoromethylation of alkynes) using PhSO2CF2H as a CF2H radical source with a sacrificial Zn anode and carbon cathode in MeCN/piperidine with H2O as proton source.
10.1038_s42004-022-00697-1_si1.electrochemical_role: Cathodic single-electron reduction of the (hetero)aryl sulfone PhSO2CF2H (E_red = -2.3 V vs Fc/Fc+) generates a CF2H radical with extrusion of ArSO2-; the Zn anode is sacrificially oxidized (E_ox = +0.01 V). A second cathodic SET reduces the benzylic radical adduct (E_red = -2.1 V) to a carbanion, which is protonated by H2O (or piperidinium).
10.1038_s42004-022-00697-1_si1.electron_flow: Cathode reduces PhSO2CF2H by SET to release sulfinate and a CF2H radical, which adds to the alkene to give a benzylic radical; a second cathodic SET reduces this radical to a benzylic carbanion that is protonated by H2O (or piperidine/PhSH HAT in the alkyne case). The Zn anode is oxidized to Zn2+ to balance charge.

key_intermediates.name: CF2H radical (from sulfone fragmentation after SET)
benzylic C-radical from CF2H addition to alkene
benzylic carbanion (after second SET at cathode)
vinyl radical (in alkyne variant, after first CF2H addition)

probe_reagents.name: D2O (10 equiv) instead of H2O
probe_reagents.observation: 58% D incorporation at the benzylic (proton-quench) position of product 3-d, consistent with carbanion protonation by water.

probe_reagents.name: Radical clock 6a (1-phenyl-1-vinylcyclopropane, R1=Ph)
probe_reagents.observation: Ring-opened product 8a obtained in 36%; closed product 7a not observed (k_Ph ~ 10^8 s^-1), indicating a C-radical intermediate fast enough to ring-open.

probe_reagents.name: Radical clock 6b (vinylcyclopropane, R1=H)
probe_reagents.observation: Closed cyclopropane product 7b in 99%; ring-opened 8b not observed (k_H ~ 10^5 s^-1), consistent with radical addition but slower clock not outrunning trapping.

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry / voltammetric profile
probe_reagents.observation: Anode plateaus at ~ +0.01 V (Zn0 -> Zn2+); cathode operates near -2.1 to -2.3 V, matching reduction of PhSO2CF2H (-2.3 V) and of the benzylic radical (-2.1 V).

10.1038_s42004-022-00697-1_si1.notes: For the alkyne double hydrodifluoromethylation (Supplementary Figure 7), PhSH is invoked as a HAT mediator to convert the initial vinyl radical to an alkene that undergoes a second CF2H radical addition before cathodic reduction/protonation; this HAT step is specific to the alkyne variant and not required in the alkene case. The 58% (not 100%) D incorporation suggests competing proton sources (piperidine/piperidinium) in addition to D2O.

10.1016_j.gresc.2022.10.008.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical trifunctionalization of styrenes with diaryl disulfides (or thiophenols), NCS, and water to install two C-Cl bonds, one C-S bond, and one S=O bond (chloro-sulfoxidation/chlorination) in one pot.
10.1016_j.gresc.2022.10.008.electrochemical_role: Cathodic reduction of NCS generates chloride and succinimidyl anion; anodic oxidation of disulfide/thiophenol generates thiyl radical and also oxidizes chloride to Cl• and the chlorothiolation intermediate 4b to a radical cation that is trapped by water.
10.1016_j.gresc.2022.10.008.electron_flow: At the cathode NCS is reduced to Cl– (and succinimidyl anion → succinimide); at the anode disulfide/thiophenol is oxidized to ArS• which adds to the styrene to give a benzylic radical trapped by Cl• to form chlorothioether 4b; anodic oxidation of 4b's sulfur generates a radical cation that is captured by H2O to give α-chloro sulfoxide 5, which is further anodically oxidized to a benzylic radical IV and coupled with Cl• to deliver the trifunctionalized product.

key_intermediates.name: thiyl radical (ArS•) from anodic oxidation of disulfide/thiophenol
benzylic carbon radical II from ArS• addition to styrene
sulfur radical cation III from anodic oxidation of 4b
second benzylic C-radical IV from anodic oxidation of 5
chloride anion / Cl• (from NCS reduction and anodic oxidation)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction (both initial trifunctionalization from 1h and second chlorination of 5) suppressed; TEMPO-adduct 7 with intermediate 5 detected by HRMS, consistent with C-radical IV; supported by EPR.

probe_reagents.name: H2-18O
probe_reagents.observation: 18O-labeled product 3aa obtained in 69% yield (HRMS confirmed), proving the S=O oxygen originates from water.

probe_reagents.name: dry MeCN (water exclusion)
probe_reagents.observation: No trifunctionalization or 5 formed from 1h+2a in anhydrous MeCN, confirming water is required for the first sulfoxidation; however, second chlorination of pre-formed 5 still proceeds in dry MeCN (64%).

probe_reagents.name: chlorothioether 4a
probe_reagents.observation: Under standard conditions 4a gave 3ad in 67%, supporting chlorothiolation as an early intermediate.

probe_reagents.name: α-chloro sulfoxide 5
probe_reagents.observation: Treatment of 5 with NCS under standard conditions gave 3ha in 91%, confirming 5 as a key on-cycle intermediate.

probe_reagents.name: NBS (replacing NCS)
