probe_reagents.observation: Bromination product 6 obtained in 70% yield (d.r. 3:1), supporting the halogen-radical pathway for the second halogenation.

10.1016_j.gresc.2022.10.008.notes: Water serves both as solvent and as the oxygen source for the sulfoxide S=O; the mechanism proceeds via sequential bifunctionalization (chlorothiolation) then anodic oxidation/water addition then radical chlorination, i.e., a stepwise radical/radical-cation (radical-polar crossover) sequence rather than concerted trifunctionalization.

10.3762_bjoc.11.21.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical cathodic reductive hydrodimerization of methyl cinnamate (and related phenylacrylates) on a boron-doped diamond (BDD) electrode to give neolignan-type C-C coupled dimers.
10.3762_bjoc.11.21.electrochemical_role: Cathodic one-electron reduction of the methyl cinnamate (phenylacrylate) C=C to a benzylic/stabilized radical anion (after protonation, a benzylic radical), which dimerizes to the hydrodimer.
10.3762_bjoc.11.21.electron_flow: The BDD cathode delivers one electron to methyl cinnamate (1a) generating a radical anion that is protonated to a benzylic radical at the β-carbon; two such radicals couple to give the hydrodimer 2, while undesired hydrolysis to 1b is suppressed by using a phosphate-buffered catholyte.

key_intermediates.name: radical anion / benzylic radical of methyl cinnamate after 1e- reduction and protonation

probe_reagents.name: electrode material variation (GC, Pt, Mg vs BDD)
probe_reagents.observation: Pt and Mg cathodes gave only H2 evolution and recovery of 1a; GC gave dimer 2 (34%, dr 74:26) plus cyclic product E-3 (25%) and 41% 1a; BDD selectively produced hydrodimer 2 in 85% yield.

probe_reagents.name: phosphate buffer (catholyte)
probe_reagents.observation: Suppresses hydrolysis of ester 1a to 1b and improves selectivity for dimer 2.

10.3762_bjoc.11.21.notes: On other cathodes (Hg, Cu, Pb, Zn, Sn, Ag) the major products from cinnamate reductions are reported to be cyclic Dieckmann-type products (type 3), whereas BDD uniquely favors the open-chain hydrodimer 2. The proposed mechanism (Figure 1, right) is drawn but not interrogated with radical traps, KIE, CV, or DFT in this paper.

10.1016_j.cclet.2021.06.008.rxn_class_summary: Catalyst- and oxidant-free electrochemical trifluoromethylation/cyclization of N-arylacrylamides to 3-CF3-substituted oxindoles using TfNHNHBoc (CF3SO2NHNHBoc) as the CF3 source
10.1016_j.cclet.2021.06.008.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of TfNHNHBoc (reagent 1) to a diazene intermediate I that fragments to release •CF3; cathode reduces protons to H2
10.1016_j.cclet.2021.06.008.electron_flow: TfNHNHBoc is oxidized at the graphite anode (2e-) to diazene I, which fragments (loss of SO2, N2, tBu•) to give •CF3; the radical adds to the acrylamide alkene to form C-radical II that undergoes intramolecular cyclization onto the aryl ring to give cyclohexadienyl radical III; III is further anodically oxidized to cation IV, which loses a proton to give oxindole 3, while H+ is reduced to H2 at the Pt cathode.

key_intermediates.name: diazene CF3SO2-N=N-Boc (I) from 2e- anodic oxidation of TfNHNHBoc
trifluoromethyl radical (•CF3) generated after loss of SO2, N2, tBu•
alkyl C-centered radical II from •CF3 addition to acrylamide alkene
cyclohexadienyl radical III from intramolecular ipso/ortho radical cyclization onto arene
cyclohexadienyl cation IV from anodic oxidation of III
trifluoromethylated oxindole product 3 after deprotonation of IV

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: 3 equiv. BHT fully suppressed product formation, indicating a free-radical intermediate

10.1016_j.cclet.2021.06.008.notes: CV showed reagent 1 oxidizes at 1.45 V vs SCE, lower than substrate 2a (~2.03 V), supporting that 1 is oxidized first at the anode. Control with no electricity gave 0% yield. Gram-scale (7 mmol) reaction gave 3a in 55% yield. No oxindole product was observed without electricity.

10.1016_j.cclet.2025.110822_sup_1.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical three-component oxysulfonyl-triazolylation of alkenes with N-sulfonyl-1,2,3-triazoles and water (or other nucleophiles) to give β-hydroxy N2-alkylated triazoles via radical-polar crossover
10.1016_j.cclet.2025.110822_sup_1.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of the nucleophile (H2O/NuH) generates a nucleophile-derived radical (•Nu, e.g. •OH); cathode reduces protons to H2
10.1016_j.cclet.2025.110822_sup_1.electron_flow: At the anode, the nucleophile (H2O) is oxidized to a nucleophile radical (•OH) which adds to the 1,1-diaryl alkene to give a stabilized β-functionalized C-radical; this C-radical attacks N2 of the N-sulfonyl-1,2,3-triazole, expelling a sulfonyl radical (•SO2R2) to deliver the N2-alkylated triazole product, while at the cathode protons are reduced to H2.

key_intermediates.name: β-hydroxy carbon radical from addition of •Nu to 1,1-diphenylethylene
radical adduct on N-sulfonyl triazole (N2-attack intermediate prior to SO2R loss)
sulfonyl radical •SO2R2 released after triazole N–S homolysis

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO (TMEPO)
probe_reagents.observation: Desired product 4 fully suppressed (n.d.), consistent with a radical pathway

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: Product 4 suppressed; BHT–triazolylmethyl adduct 53 isolated in 76%, supporting a triazolyl/C-radical intermediate

probe_reagents.name: (E)- and (Z)-β-methylstyrene (54)
probe_reagents.observation: Both E- and Z-isomers give 55 in similar yield with dr ≈ 1:1, indicating loss of olefin geometry through a free C-radical intermediate

probe_reagents.name: NH-triazole (56) and 1H-triazole isomer (57)
probe_reagents.observation: NH-triazoles give product 4 in 81% and 78%, suggesting N-sulfonyl group is not strictly required and triazolyl radical/anion may be operative

10.1016_j.cclet.2025.110822_sup_1.notes: Anode potential monitoring shows a gradual rise to ~2.25 V during electrolysis; a potential-controlled experiment (Table S2) shows that fixed Vanode from 2.0–2.6 V gives only trace–15% product, indicating constant-current conditions are important for productive turnover. The favored mechanism involves anodic generation of •Nu followed by addition to the alkene and radical substitution at N2 of the triazole with loss of •SO2R2; an alternative would be direct anodic oxidation of the triazole to a triazolyl radical, but the BHT trapping of a triazolylmethyl-type adduct and NH-triazole competence are consistent with the radical-addition/N–S cleavage pathway shown in Figure S3.

10.1016_j.cclet.2020.09.041.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical intramolecular aminoazidation of 2-vinylanilines (N-Ts) with NaN3 to give 3-azido-indolines via an iodonium-ion intermediate
10.1016_j.cclet.2020.09.041.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of iodide (I-) to molecular I2, which then forms an iodonium ion with the styrenyl alkene; the cathode reduces protons to H2
10.1016_j.cclet.2020.09.041.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes I- to I2; I2 adds to the styrene alkene of 1a forming an iodonium ion, which is opened by intramolecular nucleophilic attack of the tosylamide nitrogen to give a 3-iodo-indoline after deprotonation; azide then displaces iodide via SN2 to give the 3-azido-indoline, while the cathode reduces protons to H2.
10.1016_j.cclet.2020.09.041.rate_determining_step: Slow electrochemical generation of I2 from I- (matching the rate of iodonium formation with intramolecular amine cyclization to suppress competing direct amine addition)

key_intermediates.name: molecular iodine (I2) generated at anode from I-
cyclized indolinium cation C (after intramolecular N-attack)
3-azido-indoline product 3a (after SN2 by azide on D)

probe_reagents.name: BHT (2,6-di-tert-butyl-4-methylphenol)
probe_reagents.observation: Addition of BHT did not decrease the yield (3a obtained in 72%), indicating no radical intermediate is involved

probe_reagents.name: I2 (stoichiometric, no electricity)
probe_reagents.observation: 1 equiv I2 with HOAc in CH3CN:H2O at 25 °C gave 3a in 47%, supporting I2 as the active species but showing direct I2 addition is less efficient than slow electrogeneration

10.1016_j.cclet.2020.09.041.notes: Authors argue that under direct I2 catalysis, rapid iodonium formation outpaces intramolecular amine cyclization (aniline competes via nucleophilic addition first), lowering yield; slow electrogeneration of I2 from I- matches the cyclization rate and suppresses this side pathway. No radical pathway is supported by the BHT experiment.

10.1016_j.cclet.2019.04.023.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical vicinal oxysulfonylation of olefins with sulfinic acids and O-nucleophiles (water, alcohols, or carboxylic acids) to give β-hydroxy/alkoxy/acyloxy sulfones and β-sulfonyl lactones
10.1016_j.cclet.2019.04.023.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of sulfinic acid (PhSO2H) to sulfonyl radical; subsequent anodic oxidation of the benzylic radical intermediate to a benzylic cation; cathode reduces protons to H2
10.1016_j.cclet.2019.04.023.electron_flow: Anode first oxidizes sulfinic acid to a sulfonyl radical (I), which adds to the styrene to give a benzylic radical (II); a second anodic SET converts II to a benzylic cation (III), which is trapped by an O-nucleophile (H2O, ROH, or RCO2H) to deliver the β-oxysulfone, while protons are reduced to H2 at the Pt cathode.

key_intermediates.name: benzylic carbon radical from sulfonyl addition to styrene
benzylic carbocation after second anodic oxidation

10.1016_j.cclet.2019.04.023.notes: Mechanism proposed based on relative redox potentials (styrene higher than sulfinic acid) rather than radical-trap or DFT experiments. X-ray structure (CCDC 1893818) of product 3b confirms regiochemistry. Intramolecular variant with tethered alcohol/carboxylic acid gives cyclic ethers and lactones, consistent with cation trapping by internal nucleophile.

10.1016_j.cclet.2025.110822.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical oxidative 2,1-difunctionalization (azolation/hydroxyazolation, diamination, oxoamination, fluoroamination) of alkenes using N-mesyl-1,2,3-triazole as triazole radical precursor and various nucleophiles
10.1016_j.cclet.2025.110822.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of in situ generated NH-1,2,3-triazole (from hydrolysis of N-mesyl-triazole) to an N-centered triazolyl radical; concomitant cathodic reduction of protons evolves H2; the relayed C-radical is further anodically oxidized to a carbocation
10.1016_j.cclet.2025.110822.electron_flow: N-mesyl-triazole hydrolyzes to NH-triazole (2H-tautomer dominant); the anode oxidizes the deprotonated triazole to an N-centered radical that adds to the alkene, generating a benzylic C-radical that is further oxidized at the anode to a carbocation, which is trapped by the nucleophile (H2O, ROH, NH3, AcOH, Py·HF); protons are reduced at the cathode to H2.
10.1016_j.cclet.2025.110822.rate_determining_step: anodic oxidation of NH-1,2,3-triazole to the N-centered radical (CV onset ~2.18 V vs SCE, matching anode potential ~2.25 V)

key_intermediates.name: C-centered benzylic radical E (after addition to alkene)
benzylic carbocation F (after second anodic oxidation)
alkene radical cation (E', Path A, disfavored)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction completely suppressed; no desired product formed

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction suppressed; BHT-triazolyl adduct 53 isolated in 76% yield, confirming triazole N-radical

probe_reagents.name: DMPO
probe_reagents.observation: EPR signals (AN1=13.23 G, AN2=4.58 G, AH=14.70 G, g=2.0060) consistent with N-centered triazolyl radical spin adduct 53a

probe_reagents.name: (E)- and (Z)-β-methylstyrene 54
probe_reagents.observation: Both stereoisomers gave product 55 with identical dr ~1:1, indicating a free carbocation intermediate trapped by water

probe_reagents.name: 1H-1,2,3-triazole 56 / 2H-1,2,3-triazole 57
probe_reagents.observation: Both gave N2-selective product 4 in 81% and 78% yield, confirming NH-triazole as the key intermediate and 2H-tautomer as the productive form

probe_reagents.name: Michael addition test on alkenyl product 47
probe_reagents.observation: No product formed with any nucleophile, excluding nucleophile-first radical pathway

10.1016_j.cclet.2025.110822.notes: Two pathways were considered (Fig. 6): Path A invokes alkene radical cation (E') as the initial oxidized species followed by triazole radical/nucleophile addition; this is disfavored because alkene 2 has a higher oxidation potential than NH-triazole. Path B (favored) starts with anodic oxidation of NH-triazole to N-centered radical, addition to alkene, second anodic oxidation of the C-radical to a carbocation, and nucleophilic trapping. The 2H-tautomer (B') is responsible for the high N2-regioselectivity via steric effects. Reaction is highly sensitive to potential window (2.5 V optimal; <2.2 V gives only trace, >2.6 V gives decomposition on Pt).

10.1016_j.cclet.2023.109058.rxn_class_summary: electrochemical 8-endo selenocyclization of N-tosyl-arylalkene-tethered butenyl amines with diselenides to give seleno-benzo[b]azocines
10.1016_j.cclet.2023.109058.electrochemical_role: anodic oxidation of diaryl diselenide (PhSe)2 to generate seleno-radical and seleno-cation; the cyclized carbon radical intermediate B is further anodically oxidized to a cation that is deprotonated to yield product; cathode reduces DCM (or H+) to balance charge
10.1016_j.cclet.2023.109058.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes (PhSe)2 to PhSe+/PhSe•; the seleno-radical adds to the terminal alkene of the butenyl group giving carbon radical A, which undergoes intramolecular 8-endo cyclization onto the styryl alkene to give benzylic radical B; B is then oxidized at the anode to a cation and deprotonated to give the benzo[b]azocine product, while the cathode reduces DCM (or H+) for charge balance.
10.1016_j.cclet.2023.109058.rate_determining_step: intramolecular 8-endo radical cyclization via TS3-4 (ΔG‡ = 10.7 kcal/mol), favored over 6-endo TS3-4' by 3.5 kcal/mol

key_intermediates.name: carbon-centered radical A (PhSe added to terminal carbon of butenyl group)
8-endo cyclized carbon radical B (benzylic radical on benzo[b]azocine ring)
cation Int4 (post-oxidation of B, prior to deprotonation)
Int5 / Int3' (alternative radical isomers, off-pathway for 6-endo cyclization)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: reaction completely inhibited; 0% of 3a

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: reaction completely inhibited; 0% of 3a, 1a recovered

probe_reagents.name: stilbene
probe_reagents.observation: only 17% 3a; radical-trapping adduct 5 (PhCH=CHSePh) detected by HRMS, supporting seleno-radical intermediate

probe_reagents.name: divided cell experiment
probe_reagents.observation: 3a obtained in 43% yield, indicating reaction proceeds at the anode without cathodic involvement in product-forming step

10.1016_j.cclet.2023.109058.notes: DFT (Fig. 3) compared four radical-addition transition states; addition at terminal butenyl carbon (TS1-2) and diphenylethylene (TS1-2a) had lower barriers, with TS1-2b/c disfavored due to geometric distortion of sp2 to pseudo-sp3 carbon centers. Competing 6-endo cyclization to a thermodynamically favorable quinoline (via TS3-4', Int4') is kinetically and thermodynamically disfavored relative to 8-endo (Int4 more stable by 18.7 kcal/mol). Cyclization of alternative isomer Int5 to Int6 was found to be kinetically and thermodynamically unfavorable. CV showed similar oxidation potentials for 1a (2.00 V) and 2a (1.98 V), but mechanism favors oxidation of diselenide first based on control experiments.

10.1016_j.cclet.2021.10.016.rxn_class_summary: Applied-potential-controlled electrochemical vicinal fluorosulfenylation and fluorosulfoxidation of alkenes using thiols and Et3N·3HF, proceeding through a thiyl radical / disulfide / episulfonium ion pathway.
10.1016_j.cclet.2021.10.016.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of thiophenol to a thiyl radical (and further anodic oxidation of the in-situ generated disulfide back to thiyl radical); a second anodic oxidation event generates the episulfonium ion intermediate, and a higher applied potential further oxidizes the fluorosulfide to the fluorosulfoxide (with CCl4/H2O as the oxygen-transfer system).
10.1016_j.cclet.2021.10.016.electron_flow: ArSH is anodically oxidized (with H2 evolution at cathode) to ArS•, which dimerizes to ArSSAr; further anodic oxidation regenerates ArS• that adds to the alkene to give a β-thio carbon radical, which after another anodic oxidation forms an episulfonium ion (alternatively, the alkene reacts with an anodically generated bis(arylthio)sulfonium ion). Fluoride from Et3N·3HF opens the episulfonium to give the fluorosulfide, and at higher cell potential CCl4/H2O-mediated anodic oxidation converts it to the fluorosulfoxide.

probe_reagents.name: diaryl disulfide (96) in place of ArSH
probe_reagents.observation: Gave fluorosulfide 3 (67%) under condition A and fluorosulfoxide 63 (51%) under condition B, supporting disulfide as a viable intermediate.

probe_reagents.name: vinylcyclopropane (radical clock, 97)
probe_reagents.observation: Ring-opened products 98 (15%) and 99 (44%) observed, indicating radical intermediates.

probe_reagents.name: indene (100) and 1,2-dihydronaphthalene (102)
probe_reagents.observation: Stereospecific trans-fluorosulfenylation (101, 50%; 103, 59%) consistent with an episulfonium ion intermediate.

probe_reagents.name: (E)- and (Z)-stilbene (104)
probe_reagents.observation: Both isomers gave fluorosulfide 105 with the same stereochemistry; Z→E isomerization observed prior to fluorosulfenylation, consistent with reversible thiyl-radical addition.

probe_reagents.name: H2^18O labeling
probe_reagents.observation: O-18 incorporation in fluorosulfoxide 63 scaled with amount of H2^18O added, indicating H2O (not O2) is the oxygen source for sulfoxidation.

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of 39 and 62
probe_reagents.observation: Distinct oxidation potentials of fluorosulfide vs. fluorosulfoxide rationalize selectivity via 'dialled-in' applied potentials (Ecell 1.8 V for sulfenylation; CCE 20 mA, Eanode 1.10–1.95 V for sulfoxidation).

probe_reagents.name: rigorously oxygen-free conditions
probe_reagents.observation: Yield of fluorosulfoxide 63 unchanged, ruling out O2 as oxygen source.

10.1016_j.cclet.2021.10.016.notes: Two pathways to the episulfonium ion are proposed: (path a, favored) anodic oxidation of the β-thio carbon radical from ArS• addition to the alkene; (path b) reaction of the alkene with an anodically generated aryl-bis(arylthio)sulfonium ion [ArS(ArSSAr)]+. Cathodic reduction of ArSSAr to ArS• is mechanistically possible but deemed non-essential given the undivided cell setup. Attempts to access the fluorosulfone (I) at higher potentials (2.8–3.8 V) only led to decomposition. CCl4 is proposed to deliver Cl−/Cl• species that facilitate oxidation of fluorosulfide to fluorosulfoxide, with H2O as the oxygen source.

10.1039_D1NJ04819H.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical oxidative iodocyclization of N-allylcarboxamides to 2-aryl-4-(iodomethyl)oxazolines using LiI as both electrolyte and halogen source
10.1039_D1NJ04819H.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of iodide (I-) to molecular iodine (I2), which acts as the active halogenating mediator; cathodic reduction of H2O generates H2 and hydroxide
10.1039_D1NJ04819H.electron_flow: At the graphite anode, iodide is oxidized to I2, which adds to the alkene of the N-allylamide to form an iodiranium-type intermediate; the amide oxygen attacks intramolecularly to deliver the oxazoline with loss of H+, while the Pt cathode reduces water to H2 and OH-.

key_intermediates.name: molecular iodine (I2) generated in situ from anodic oxidation of iodide
iodonium/iodiranium intermediate I from alkene + I2 addition on N-allylbenzamide
cyclized oxazolinium cation (post intramolecular O-attack, pre-deprotonation)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Addition of 2.0 equiv TEMPO decreased conversion to 53%; authors infer formation of a TEMPO-iodine adduct that suppresses I2 availability

10.1039_D1NJ04819H.notes: CV experiments show a clear oxidation feature only when LiI is present (not with substrate 1a alone), supporting iodide oxidation as the key anodic event rather than direct substrate oxidation. The mechanism is described as ionic (iodonium / nucleophilic cyclization) rather than radical; the TEMPO effect is attributed to scavenging of iodine/I2 rather than a carbon radical intermediate.

10.1134_s1023193511100144.rxn_class_summary: Electrosynthesis of substituted cyclopropanecarboxylic acid esters via cathodic reduction of methyl trichloroacetate (MTCA) or methyl dichloroacetate (MDCA) in the presence of acrylonitrile (AcN), yielding methyl 1-chloro-2-cyanocyclopropanecarboxylate.
10.1134_s1023193511100144.electrochemical_role: Cathodic reduction: two-electron dissociative reduction of MTCA (C–Cl cleavage) at Hg cathode generates a stabilized dichlorocarbanion (CCl2C(O)OMe-); alternatively, cathodic reduction of residual water generates OH-/Et4NOH which deprotonates MDCA to the same anion (hidden indirect deprotonation, pathway II).
10.1134_s1023193511100144.electron_flow: At the Hg cathode, MTCA accepts 2e- with loss of Cl- to give the CCl2CO2Me carbanion, which undergoes Michael addition to acrylonitrile; the resulting carbanion displaces an intramolecular Cl- to close the cyclopropane ring. For MDCA on steel/Hg, cathodic reduction of H2O gives OH- (then Et4NOH via Et4NCl), which deprotonates MDCA to the same CCl2CO2Me anion that then follows the Michael/ring-closure sequence.
10.1134_s1023193511100144.rate_determining_step: Initial C–Cl dissociative electron transfer (for MTCA) or cathodic water discharge / deprotonation of MDCA (for MDCA pathway II)

key_intermediates.name: Michael adduct anion from carbanion + acrylonitrile (CCl2(CO2Me)-CH2-CH(-)-CN)
tetraethylammonium hydroxide (Et4NOH) as in situ base
linear adduct 'm' EtO2C-CCl2-CH2-CHCl-CN (claimed in ref [6], not observed here)

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of MDCA on Pt in Et4NClO4/MeCN
probe_reagents.observation: Reverse-scan anodic peak for Cl- oxidation observed, indicating that on low-H-overpotential cathodes MDCA reduction still proceeds with C–Cl cleavage rather than C–H deprotonation.

probe_reagents.name: solvent variation (DMF vs MeCN) and electrolyte (Et4NCl vs LiCl)
probe_reagents.observation: Substitution of LiCl for Et4NCl lowers current efficiency; MeCN on steel recovers cyclopropane yield comparable to Hg/DMF, supporting Et4NOH as in situ base (pathway II).

probe_reagents.name: cathode material variation (Hg vs steel)
probe_reagents.observation: On steel cathode no cyclopropane 'b' (which would indicate cathodic dechlorination of MDCA) is detected, supporting the deprotonation pathway II for MDCA.

probe_reagents.name: 1H NMR and GC-MS product analysis
probe_reagents.observation: Cyclopropane 'a' identified (M+ = 125); no linear product 'm' detected, contradicting the halogenation mechanism proposed in ref [6].

10.1134_s1023193511100144.notes: Two pathways are proposed for cyclopropane 'a' formation: Pathway I (favored for MTCA) — direct 2e cathodic dissociative reduction of MTCA to the CCl2CO2Me carbanion, Michael addition to acrylonitrile, intramolecular SN2 ring closure. Pathway II (favored for MDCA, especially on steel) — 'hidden indirect deprotonation' where cathodic water reduction generates OH-, which together with Et4NCl yields Et4NOH that deprotonates MDCA to the same carbanion. The earlier mechanism 'm' from ref [6], invoking MTCA as a halogenating agent to give a linear chloronitrile, is rejected because product 'm' was not detected by 1H NMR or GC-MS in this work.

10.1021_acsomega.2c06689.rxn_class_summary: Transition-metal-free oxidative decarboxylative radical difluoroarylmethylation/cyclization of N-methacryloyl-2-arylbenzimidazoles with α,α-difluoroarylacetic acids to give difluoroarylmethyl-substituted benzimidazo[2,1-a]isoquinolin-6(5H)-ones
10.1021_acsomega.2c06689.electron_flow: Persulfate thermally homolyzes to SO4•-, which oxidatively decarboxylates the α,α-difluoroarylacetic acid to a difluorobenzyl radical A; A adds across the methacrylamide alkene to give tertiary radical B, which undergoes intramolecular homolytic aromatic substitution onto the 2-aryl benzimidazole to form cyclohexadienyl radical C; SET oxidation of C by another SO4•-/S2O8 2- gives arenium cation D, and rearomatization by loss of H+ delivers the product.

key_intermediates.name: α,α-difluorobenzyl radical (A) from decarboxylation of α,α-difluorophenylacetic acid by persulfate
tertiary C-radical B from addition of A to the methacryloyl alkene
cyclohexadienyl radical C from intramolecular ipso/ortho cyclization onto the 2-aryl ring
Wheland-type arenium cation D after SET oxidation of C
sulfate radical anion SO4•- (from thermolysis of S2O8 2-)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Product 3aa not formed; TEMPO–CF2Ar adduct 4 detected by LC-MS, supporting a radical pathway

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction suppressed; desired product 3aa not observed

probe_reagents.name: Phenylacetic acid (2r) in place of α,α-difluorophenylacetic acid
probe_reagents.observation: Decarboxylative coupling still proceeds to give 3ar in 68% yield, indicating the CF2 group has little influence on the transformation

10.1021_acsomega.2c06689.notes: Mechanism is proposed by analogy to prior persulfate-mediated decarboxylative radical cyclizations; no DFT, KIE, EPR, or CV evidence is reported. X-ray crystallography of product 3ea confirms regiochemistry but does not directly probe the mechanism.

10.1021_acsomega.9b02442.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical alkoxysulfonylation of α-substituted styrenes with sodium arenesulfinates and alcohols to give β-alkoxysulfones via radical-polar crossover
10.1021_acsomega.9b02442.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of sodium 4-methylbenzenesulfinate to a sulfonyl radical; subsequent anodic oxidation of the benzylic C-radical adduct to a benzylic cation; cathode reduces protons to H2
10.1021_acsomega.9b02442.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes ArSO2Na to a sulfonyl radical (via O-radical tautomer), which adds to the styrene C=C to give a benzylic radical; this radical is further oxidized at the anode to a benzylic cation that is trapped by methanol, while the cathode reduces protons to H2.

key_intermediates.name: benzylic C-radical adduct (C) from sulfonyl radical addition to styrene
benzylic carbocation (D) after anodic oxidation of C
protonated β-alkoxysulfone (E) after MeOH attack on D

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Addition of 3 equiv TEMPO completely inhibited the reaction (0% yield of 3a) with α-methylstyrene 1a largely recovered, indicating a radical pathway

10.1021_acsomega.9b02442.notes: Competing hydroxysulfonylation (with water as nucleophile) is observed when isopropanol is used, lowering yield to 29%. Mechanism is proposed based on TEMPO inhibition plus literature precedent (refs 13b, 14, 15); no DFT, CV, or KIE reported.

10.1002_ajoc.201300017.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemically initiated catalytic dithiolation (addition of ArSSAr) to alkenes and alkynes via a cationic chain mechanism mediated by an arylbis(arylthio)sulfonium ion ArS(ArSSAr)+.
10.1002_ajoc.201300017.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of ArSSAr at -78 °C generates and accumulates a catalytic 'cation pool' of ArS(ArSSAr)+ (1), which then initiates a non-electrochemical cation chain with the alkene and additional ArSSAr.
10.1002_ajoc.201300017.electron_flow: Anode removes one electron from ArSSAr to build up a catalytic pool of ArS(ArSSAr)+ (1); 1 transfers ArS+ to the alkene forming thiiranium A, which is opened by another ArSSAr to give sulfonium B, which fragments to dithiolated product C and regenerates 1, propagating a cation chain without further electron transfer.

key_intermediates.name: arylbis(arylthio)sulfonium ion ArS(ArSSAr)+ (1), chain carrier / initiator
thiiranium ion A from ArS+ transfer to alkene
β-arylthio sulfonium ion B (open-chain sulfonium after ArSSAr attack on A)

probe_reagents.name: Bu4NB(C6F5)4 vs Bu4NBF4 (counteranion probe)
probe_reagents.observation: Yields drop sharply with BF4- (≤26%) vs B(C6F5)4- (71-79%); less nucleophilic/less stabilizing B(C6F5)4- keeps cations A and B electrophilic enough for ArSSAr attack, supporting cationic chain.

probe_reagents.name: Catalytic electricity (0.06 F/mol) experiment
probe_reagents.observation: Substoichiometric charge (ca. 6 mol% of 1 generated) still drives full conversion, consistent with a chain process with 1 as chain carrier.

probe_reagents.name: Et3N quench
probe_reagents.observation: Added to quench cationic intermediates and terminate the reaction.

10.1002_ajoc.201300017.notes: Mechanism builds on prior stoichiometric ArS(ArSSAr)+ 'cation pool' chemistry (thiiranium intermediates trapped by hard/soft nucleophiles). No radical traps, KIE, CV, or DFT reported on these pages; mechanistic support comes from catalytic-charge experiment, counteranion effect, and anti-selective addition consistent with thiiranium opening. Alternative pathways (e.g., radical chain) are not explicitly excluded.

10.1002_ajoc.202300485.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical oxidative sulfenocyclization of unsaturated carboxylic acids with diaryl disulfides to give thio-substituted lactones
10.1002_ajoc.202300485.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of diphenyl disulfide (PhSSPh) to generate a thiyl radical (PhS•) and a sulfenyl cation (PhS+); cathode reduces protons to H2
10.1002_ajoc.202300485.electron_flow: PhSSPh is preferentially oxidized at the anode (CV: oxidation peak of 2a at 1.86 V, lower than 1a at 2.37 V) to give PhS• and PhS+; the electrophilic PhS+ adds to the alkene of the unsaturated carboxylic acid to form a sulfonium intermediate, which undergoes intramolecular nucleophilic attack by the carboxylate followed by deprotonation to give the lactone, while protons are reduced to H2 at the cathode.

key_intermediates.name: thiiranium/sulfonium adduct of alkene with carboxylic acid (C)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction inhibited; product yield reduced to 30%, indicating a radical intermediate

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction inhibited; product yield reduced to 27%, consistent with radical pathway

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of 1a, 2a, and mixture
probe_reagents.observation: Oxidation peak of 2a at 1.86 V vs Ag/AgCl, lower than 1a (2.37 V), showing disulfide is preferentially oxidized at anode

10.1002_ajoc.202300485.notes: The mechanism is proposed as a radical/cationic crossover initiated by anodic oxidation of the disulfide rather than the alkene-COOH substrate, supported by CV. Method extends to δ-lactones (from δ,ε-unsaturated acids) and tellurated γ-lactones using PhTeTePh.

10.1002_ajoc.202100607.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical oxidative selenylation/cyclization of 4-arylpent-4-enoic acids with diaryl (or dialkyl) diselenides to afford selenated gamma-lactones in an undivided cell with Pt electrodes and LiClO4/MeCN.
10.1002_ajoc.202100607.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of PhSeSePh to its radical cation, which fragments into PhSe+ and PhSe radical; the alkenoic acid is also oxidizable (Eox = 2.4 V) and a radical-addition intermediate is further oxidized at the anode to a carbocation. Protons are reduced to H2 at the cathode.
10.1002_ajoc.202100607.electron_flow: Path a (ionic): anode oxidizes PhSeSePh to a radical cation that fragments to PhSe+ and PhSe•; PhSe+ forms a seleniranium with the alkene, and intramolecular carboxylate attack closes the gamma-lactone. Path b (radical): PhSe• adds to the alkene to give a C-radical, which is anodically oxidized to a carbocation, then trapped by the pendant carboxylate with deprotonation; protons are reduced to H2 at the cathode.

key_intermediates.name: seleniranium ion (IV) from PhSe+ addition to alkene of 1a
beta-selenyl carbon radical (V) from PhSe• addition to alkene
beta-selenyl carbocation (VI) from anodic oxidation of V

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Yield of 3a decreased to 44% but reaction not fully inhibited, implicating a partial radical pathway.

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: Yield of 3a reduced to 67%; partial inhibition consistent with radical involvement.

probe_reagents.name: 1,1-diphenylethene
probe_reagents.observation: Yield of 3a reduced to 65%; partial inhibition consistent with radical involvement.

probe_reagents.name: cyclopropyl-substituted styrene (16)
probe_reagents.observation: Ring-opened/rearranged product 17 obtained in 41% (E:Z = 1.3:1), supporting a radical addition pathway.

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of 1a and 2a
probe_reagents.observation: Oxidation peaks at 2.4 V (1a) and 1.7 V (2a), indicating diselenide is preferentially oxidized at the anode.

probe_reagents.name: PhSSPh (disulfide 14) in place of diselenide
probe_reagents.observation: Only trace lactone 15 formed, showing the chemistry is specific to Se radical/cation chemistry.

10.1002_ajoc.202100607.notes: Authors explicitly propose two parallel pathways (path a, ionic via seleniranium from PhSe+; path b, radical via PhSe• addition then anodic oxidation to a cation). They state they cannot rule out the radical pathway; both converge on the same carbocation-like intermediate trapped by the carboxylate. Control with disulfide failed (only trace product), and substrate scope shows alkyl diselenides also work, supporting a general Se electrophile/radical mechanism.

10.1002_ajoc.202200531.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical N-centered radical addition/semipinacol rearrangement of alkenyl cyclobutanols with sulfonimides to give beta-amino cyclic ketones (ring-expanded cyclopentanones).
10.1002_ajoc.202200531.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of dibenzenesulfonimide (via PCET with n-Bu4NOAc base) generates an N-centered sulfonimidyl radical; a second anodic SET oxidizes the C-radical adduct to a carbocation that triggers 1,2-alkyl shift.
10.1002_ajoc.202200531.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes the sulfonimide N-H via PCET (with n-Bu4NOAc as base) to give an N-centered radical I; I adds to the alkene of the alkenyl cyclobutanol giving C-radical II; anodic SET oxidizes II to cation III, which undergoes 1,2-alkyl (ring) migration (semipinacol) with loss of TMS to give the beta-amino cyclopentanone; protons are reduced to H2 at the cathode.

key_intermediates.name: carbon-centered radical from addition to alkene (beta-amino tertiary C-radical bearing OTMS cyclobutane)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Only trace product observed; reaction inhibited, supporting radical intermediates.

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: 0% product; reaction completely inhibited.

probe_reagents.name: 1,1-diphenylethylene (DPE)
probe_reagents.observation: Coupling product 21 isolated in 82% yield, consistent with trapping of N-centered sulfonimidyl radical.

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of 1a and 2a
probe_reagents.observation: Oxidation peaks at 2.22 V (alkenyl cyclobutanol 1a) and 1.68 V (sulfonimide 2a); sulfonimide is oxidized preferentially.

10.1002_ajoc.202200531.notes: Mechanism proposed by analogy to prior N-centered radical addition/semipinacol work (Li/Zhang with NFSI, Xiao with aminopyridinium); electrochemistry replaces stoichiometric external oxidants. CV indicates sulfonimide (1.68 V) is oxidized before the alkene (2.22 V), supporting initial N-H oxidation rather than alkene radical cation formation.

10.1002_ajoc.201900020.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical fluoroalkynylation of aryl alkenes using fluoride ions and alkynyltrifluoroborate salts to give propargylic fluorides
10.1002_ajoc.201900020.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of the aryl alkene to a radical cation; cathodic reduction of HF to H2 and F-
10.1002_ajoc.201900020.electron_flow: Anode performs one-electron oxidation of the aryl alkene to a radical cation (I), which is trapped by the alkynyltrifluoroborate to give a C-centered radical (II); a second anodic SET oxidizes II to benzylic carbocation III, which is selectively captured by fluoride (delivered from cathodic reduction of HF) rather than by the bulky organotrifluoroborate, giving the propargylic fluoride.

key_intermediates.name: benzylic carbocation (III) after second SET oxidation

10.1002_ajoc.201900020.notes: Mechanism proposed by analogy to the authors' previous work (ref 12); no radical traps, CV, KIE, or DFT reported in this communication. Selectivity for F- vs organotrifluoroborate attack on cation III is rationalized by steric demand. A minor side product (1,2-difluoroethane-1,1-diyl)dibenzene (9:1 with 20) suggests competing difluorination via the same cation intermediate.

10.1007_s10593-021-02966-8.rxn_class_summary: Electrocatalytic cascade assembly of benzylidenebarbiturates and cyclohexane-1,3-diones in MeOH with NaBr mediator to give dihydro-spiro[1-benzofuran-2,5'-pyrimidines]
10.1007_s10593-021-02966-8.electrochemical_role: Cathodic reduction of MeOH generates methoxide (base) and H2; anodic oxidation of Br- generates Br2 which acts as the in-solution oxidant/halogenating mediator
10.1007_s10593-021-02966-8.electron_flow: Cathode reduces MeOH to MeO- (and H2), which deprotonates the 1,3-dione to anion A; A undergoes Michael addition to benzylidenebarbiturate 1 giving enolate B; anodically generated Br2 brominates B at the barbiturate alpha-carbon; a second methoxide deprotonation gives enolate C, whose dione oxygen displaces bromide intramolecularly to forge the dihydrofuran spirocycle, regenerating Br-.

key_intermediates.name: Michael adduct enolate anion B from addition of A to benzylidenebarbiturate
alpha-Bromo Michael adduct (after bromination of B by Br2)
Enolate anion C (after second deprotonation by methoxide)
Spirobarbituric dihydrofuran 3 (final product via intramolecular O-alkylation/SN2 displacement of Br)

10.1007_s10593-021-02966-8.notes: Mechanism proposed without explicit mechanistic probes (no TEMPO, KIE, or CV reported in shown pages); structures of products confirmed by 1H-13C HMBC. Br- functions as a redox mediator and methoxide as an in situ generated base; both base and halogen are present only in catalytic concentrations (2.4 F/mol passed).

10.3762_bjoc.18.112.rxn_class_summary: Radical cation Diels–Alder reaction of arylidene cycloalkanes with 2,3-dimethyl-1,3-butadiene under TiO2 photoelectrochemical or electrochemical conditions to give spirocyclic cyclohexenes
10.3762_bjoc.18.112.electrochemical_role: Anodic single-electron oxidation of the electron-rich arylalkene (anethole/arylidene cycloalkane) to its radical cation, which acts as the dienophile; the cyclohexene radical cation intermediate is then reduced back (at cathode or by another substrate molecule in a chain) to give the neutral cycloadduct
10.3762_bjoc.18.112.electron_flow: Anode (or photoexcited TiO2 hole) oxidizes the electron-rich styrene/arylidene cycloalkane to a radical cation, which undergoes stepwise [4+2] addition with 2,3-dimethyl-1,3-butadiene to give a cyclohexene radical cation; back-electron transfer (from cathode, TiO2 conduction-band electron, or a neutral substrate in a chain) regenerates the neutral Diels–Alder adduct.

key_intermediates.name: distonic cyclohexene radical cation (after C–C bond formation with diene)

probe_reagents.name: β-methylstyrene (non-substituted dienophile control)
probe_reagents.observation: Productive under TiO2 photoelectrochemical conditions (61%) but 0% under simple electrochemical conditions, showing photoelectrochemistry enables otherwise unreactive substrates

probe_reagents.name: β-methylanethole (1)
probe_reagents.observation: Trace under TiO2/UV (9%) but 43% under electrochemistry; β-substitution introduces steric hindrance disfavoring the radical cation cycloaddition

probe_reagents.name: arylidene cyclopropane (4)
probe_reagents.observation: Totally unreactive under both conditions, attributed to instability/strain of the proposed tertiary radical cation

probe_reagents.name: arylidene cyclobutane (5)
probe_reagents.observation: Spiro adduct 9 formed in 61% (photo) / 66% (EC), showing reduced steric hindrance vs gem-dimethyl analog enables reaction

probe_reagents.name: arylidene cyclopentane (6) and cyclohexane (7)
probe_reagents.observation: Less productive (10: 0–5%; 11: 31–56%), indicating ring-size dependent steric/electronic effects

10.3762_bjoc.18.112.notes: Mechanism (Figure 1) is the established radical cation Diels–Alder pathway adopted from prior literature; no direct mechanistic probes (TEMPO, KIE, CV, DFT) were performed in this work. The study focuses on substrate scope to test steric/electronic effects of β-substitution. TiO2 photoelectrochemistry is invoked as advantageous because both single-electron oxidation and reduction occur at the same semiconductor surface, enabling a productive chain/back-ET pathway not accessible under simple constant-potential electrolysis for some substrates.

10.1016_j.tetlet.2021.153514.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical C-H halogenation (chlorination and bromination) of electron-deficient arenes/heterocycles (quinones, coumarins, quinoxalin-2(1H)-ones, 1,3-diketones) using HX as halide source in an undivided cell
10.1016_j.tetlet.2021.153514.electrochemical_role: Cathodic reduction of benzoquinone to hydroquinone, and anodic oxidation of chloride to Cl2 (electrophilic chlorinating species); after SEAr on hydroquinone, anodic reoxidation regenerates the quinone product
10.1016_j.tetlet.2021.153514.electron_flow: Cathode reduces the quinone (with H+) to the hydroquinone while the anode oxidizes Cl- to Cl2; Cl2 electrophilically chlorinates the electron-rich hydroquinone, and the chlorinated hydroquinone is then reoxidized at the anode to deliver the halogenated quinone product.

key_intermediates.name: molecular chlorine (Cl2) generated by anodic oxidation of chloride

probe_reagents.name: BHT (2,6-di-tert-butyl-4-methylphenol)
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction completely suppressed; BHT-Cl adduct 3a detected by HRMS, but authors interpret results as inconsistent with a free-radical pathway

probe_reagents.name: N,N-diallyl-4-methylbenzenesulfonamide
probe_reagents.observation: Gave dichlorinated open-chain product 3b in 54% yield with no cyclization, arguing against a radical addition pathway and supporting an electrophilic (ionic) chlorination

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry
probe_reagents.observation: CV analysis supports cathodic reduction of quinone and anodic oxidation of chloride, consistent with the proposed paired mechanism

10.1016_j.tetlet.2021.153514.notes: Although BHT shut down the reaction and a BHT-Cl adduct was detected (which could suggest radical involvement), the lack of cyclization in the diallylsulfonamide probe led the authors to favor an electrophilic (ionic) chlorination by anodically generated Cl2 on a cathodically generated hydroquinone, rather than a free-radical chain. A no-current control gave no reaction, confirming the electrochemical requirement.

10.1016_j.tetlet.2023.154410.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical radical relay cyclization of benzimidazole-tethered alkenes with sodium triflinate (Langlois reagent) to access 5-(2,2,2-trifluoroethyl)-5,6-dihydrobenzimidazo-isoquinolines
10.1016_j.tetlet.2023.154410.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of CF3SO2- (Langlois reagent) generates CF3SO2• which extrudes SO2 to give CF3•; a downstream cyclohexadienyl-type radical intermediate is also oxidized at the anode to a cation prior to rearomatization
10.1016_j.tetlet.2023.154410.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes CF3SO2- to CF3SO2•, which loses SO2 to give CF3•; CF3• adds to the tethered alkene of 1a to form alkyl radical A, which cyclizes onto the arene to give cyclohexadienyl radical B; B is oxidized at the anode (or by O2/HOO•) to cation C, then deprotonation yields aromatic product 3 with concomitant H2 evolution at the cathode.

key_intermediates.name: CF3SO2• (sulfonyl radical from anodic oxidation of Langlois reagent)
Carbon-centered alkyl radical A from CF3 addition to the pendent alkene
Cyclohexadienyl radical B from intramolecular radical addition onto the aryl ring
Wheland-type arenium cation C after anodic oxidation of B

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Product 3 not detected (N.D.), consistent with radical intermediates

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: Product 3 not detected, supporting a radical pathway

probe_reagents.name: 1,1-diphenylethylene (DPE)
probe_reagents.observation: Ph2C=CHSO2CF3 adduct detected by GC-MS (m/z 312.00); product 3 not formed, trapping CF3SO2•/CF3• indicates sulfonyl/trifluoromethyl radical intermediates

probe_reagents.name: N2 atmosphere control
probe_reagents.observation: Yield dropped from 80% (air) to 43% under N2, indicating O2 participates as a terminal oxidant/H-abstractor (HOO•/O2•-) in rearomatization

probe_reagents.name: No current control
probe_reagents.observation: No reaction without applied current, confirming electrochemical initiation

probe_reagents.name: Unactivated N-pentenyl substrates 26 and 27
probe_reagents.observation: 0% product — aryl ring acceptor is required for the radical cyclization step

10.1016_j.tetlet.2023.154410.notes: Cyclic voltammetry (Fig. 1) shows that 2 + CF3SO3H produces a new oxidation feature at lower potential than 1a, supporting that anodic oxidation of the Langlois reagent (not the benzimidazole alkene) initiates the cycle. The authors note that direct H-atom abstraction from cyclohexadienyl radical B (by O2•-/HOO•) to deliver 3 cannot be ruled out as an alternative to the anodic oxidation/deprotonation pathway through cation C.

10.1016_j.tet.2022.132651.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical fluorosulfonylation of 1,1-disubstituted alkenes with sulfonyl hydrazides and Et3N·HF to give vicinal fluorinated sulfones
10.1016_j.tet.2022.132651.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of sulfonyl hydrazide (after deprotonation) to generate a sulfonyl radical (with N2 release); subsequent anodic oxidation of the benzylic radical adduct to a benzylic cation; cathodic reduction generates H2
10.1016_j.tet.2022.132651.electron_flow: At the anode, the sulfonyl hydrazide is deprotonated and oxidized with loss of N2 to give a sulfonyl radical A, which adds to the alkene to form benzylic radical B; B is further oxidized at the anode to benzylic cation C, which is trapped by fluoride from Et3N·HF to deliver product 4, while H+ is reduced at the cathode to H2.

key_intermediates.name: sulfonyl radical A (e.g., tosyl radical) from anodic oxidation/deprotonation/N2 loss of sulfonyl hydrazide
benzylic radical B from sulfonyl radical addition to 1,1-diarylalkene
benzylic cation C from anodic oxidation of radical B
vicinal fluorinated sulfone product 4 after fluoride capture
alkenyl sulfone (tested as putative intermediate, ruled out)

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: Sulfonyl-BHT adduct isolated in 41%; product 4aa yield drops to 40%, supporting sulfonyl radical intermediacy

probe_reagents.name: cyclopropyl-substituted alkene 1y (radical clock)
probe_reagents.observation: Cyclopropane ring-opened product 4aa formed in 25%; closed product 4ya 0%, consistent with radical addition pathway

probe_reagents.name: alkenyl sulfone 1z (putative intermediate test)
probe_reagents.observation: Under standard conditions gave 0% of 4aa, excluding hydrofluorination of alkenyl sulfone as the pathway

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of 1a, 2a, 3a and mixtures
probe_reagents.observation: Sulfonyl hydrazide 2a oxidizes at 1.92 V (and 2.41 V) vs Ag/AgCl, lower than 1,1-diphenylethylene 1a (2.26 V), indicating preferential anodic oxidation of the hydrazide

10.1016_j.tet.2022.132651.notes: An alternative pathway via initial formation of an alkenyl sulfone followed by hydrofluorination was explicitly tested with 1z and ruled out (0% product). CV data establish that the sulfonyl hydrazide, not the alkene, is oxidized first at the anode.

10.1016_j.tet.2008.07.098.rxn_class_summary: Nickel-catalyzed electrochemical conjugate addition of ortho-functionalized aryl bromides to Michael acceptors with sacrificial iron anode, followed by base-promoted cyclization to dihydro/tetrahydronaphthalenes.
10.1016_j.tet.2008.07.098.electrochemical_role: Cathodic reduction of Ni(II) (with 2 e-) generates a low-valent nickel species that undergoes oxidative addition into the Ar-Br bond; the sacrificial iron anode releases Fe(II) ions that act as a Lewis acid activating the Michael acceptor and the resulting carbanion/enolate intermediate.
10.1016_j.tet.2008.07.098.electron_flow: At the cathode, Ni(II) is reduced by 2 e- to Ni(0), which oxidatively adds into the Ar-Br bond; the resulting aryl-nickel transfers the aryl group to the activated olefin, generating an α-EWG carbanion stabilized by Fe(II) (released by anodic dissolution of the iron anode), which either is protonated by EtOH to give the conjugate adduct or cyclizes onto a tethered CN/COCH3 group to give the bicyclic product.

key_intermediates.name: ArNi species from oxidative addition of o-(cyanomethyl)aryl bromide
Intramolecular ketenimine-type cyclized anion (from CN attack by benzylic carbanion)

probe_reagents.name: ethanol (proton source)
probe_reagents.observation: Addition of 1-4 equiv EtOH suppresses cyclization and favors the open-chain conjugate addition product (48-62% isolated); Fe(II) from anode enhances EtOH acidity.

probe_reagents.name: stainless steel anode (replacing Fe)
probe_reagents.observation: With non-sacrificial stainless-steel anode (runs 4-5), no Fe(II) Lewis acid is released and conjugate addition product is not observed (only cyclized 21, 22 formed), supporting the role of Fe(II) released from the sacrificial anode.

probe_reagents.name: excess acrylonitrile (4 equiv)
probe_reagents.observation: With o-acetyl aryl bromide 13 and acrylonitrile, double alkylation at the benzylic position is observed (compound 23), consistent with a benzylic carbanion intermediate (Scheme 7).

10.1016_j.tet.2008.07.098.notes: Two competing pathways are proposed depending on conditions: (i) Ni-mediated aryl transfer followed by intramolecular cyclization of the α-EWG carbanion onto the ortho CN or COCH3 group gives bicyclic dihydro/tetrahydronaphthalenes (Scheme 6); (ii) when EtOH is added, the intermediate carbanion is protonated, giving the acyclic conjugate addition product that can be cyclized in a second step with NaOEt. Scheme 7 shows a competing benzylic dialkylation pathway with excess acrylonitrile. Fe(II) from the sacrificial iron anode is explicitly invoked as a Lewis acid activating both the Michael acceptor and EtOH.

10.1016_j.tetlet.2021.153567.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical oxidative bromolactonization of 4-aryl-pent-4-enoic acids with NaBr to give bromomethyl-substituted gamma-butyrolactones
10.1016_j.tetlet.2021.153567.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of bromide to bromine radical (and subsequently to a bromocation equivalent), which initiates alkene functionalization; cathode reduces protons to H2
10.1016_j.tetlet.2021.153567.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes Br- to Br•, which adds to the terminal alkene of the pentenoic acid to give a benzylic C-radical (I); a second anodic SET oxidizes I to a benzylic cation (II), which is trapped intramolecularly by the carboxylate to release H+ and deliver the bromomethyl gamma-lactone, while the cathode reduces protons to H2.

key_intermediates.name: Bromine radical (Br•) generated by anodic oxidation of Br-
Carbon-centered radical I from Br• addition to alkene of 4-phenylpent-4-enoic acid
Benzylic carbocation II from anodic oxidation of radical I

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: Reduced yield of product, consistent with radical intermediate

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Reduced yield of product, consistent with radical intermediate

probe_reagents.name: Molecular Br2 (no current)
probe_reagents.observation: Only trace product 3a formed, indicating that free Br2 is not the active brominating intermediate

probe_reagents.name: No-electricity control
probe_reagents.observation: No product formed without applied current, confirming electrochemical initiation

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of 1a and NaBr
probe_reagents.observation: Oxidation peaks observed at 1.76 V (1a) and 0.63 V (NaBr), indicating Br- is oxidized preferentially at the anode

10.1016_j.tetlet.2021.153567.notes: Authors explicitly rule out molecular Br2 as the active intermediate based on the Br2-only control giving trace product. Mechanism is a radical-polar crossover (Br• addition then anodic oxidation to cation, then carboxylate cyclization), supported by radical-trap suppression and CV data but no KIE or DFT.

10.1016_j.tetlet.2021.153602.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical halo-heterocyclization of N-allylbenzamides using 1,2-dichloroethane (DCE) as a chloride shuttle to construct 5-halomethyl-2-oxazolines, 2-thiazolines, 1,3-oxazines and isoxazolines
10.1016_j.tetlet.2021.153602.electrochemical_role: Cathodic reduction of DCE generates a chloroethyl radical/carbanion that fragments to ethylene and Cl-; the released Cl- is then anodically oxidized to electrophilic Cl+ which initiates chloro-cyclization of the N-allylbenzamide (DCE acts as an e-shuttle halogenating reagent in a paired electrolysis).
10.1016_j.tetlet.2021.153602.electron_flow: At the cathode, DCE is reduced (2 e-) to a chloroethyl radical (A) then carbanion (B), which beta-eliminates Cl- and releases ethylene. The liberated Cl- migrates to the anode where it is oxidized to electrophilic Cl+; this Cl+ activates the alkene of the N-allylbenzamide (intermediate C), the amide oxygen cyclizes onto the chloronium (intermediate D), and loss of H+ delivers the 5-chloromethyl-2-oxazoline product 2aa.

key_intermediates.name: chloroethyl radical from one-electron reduction of DCE (A)
electrophilic Cl+ from anodic oxidation of Cl-
chloriranium/chloronium complex C of N-allylbenzamide with Cl+

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of 1aa, DCE, and Et4NBF4 in MeCN
probe_reagents.observation: No oxidation wave for 1aa in 0-2.0 V, ruling out direct anodic oxidation of the alkene to a radical cation; DCE shows a reduction peak at 0.95 V and an oxidation peak at 1.41 V (Cl- generated from DCE reduction is oxidized at the anode).

10.1016_j.tetlet.2021.153602.notes: An alternative mechanism via direct anodic oxidation of the alkene 1aa to a radical cation was explicitly excluded by CV (no oxidation of 1aa in 0-2.0 V). The mechanism is supported mainly by CV and analogy to prior work; no radical traps, KIE, or DFT calculations are reported on these pages.

10.1016_j.tet.2008.12.068.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical fluoro-selenenylation of α,β-unsaturated esters and related electron-deficient olefins using PhSeSePh and Et3N·5HF as fluoride source in an undivided cell to give vicinal β-fluoro-α-phenylselenyl (and regioisomeric α-fluoro-β-phenylselenyl) esters.
10.1016_j.tet.2008.12.068.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of PhSeSePh in the presence of fluoride generates an electrophilic [PhSeF] species, which adds to the olefin to form a seleniranium (episelenonium) cation that is opened by fluoride.
10.1016_j.tet.2008.12.068.electron_flow: At the anode, PhSeSePh is oxidized in the presence of F− (from Et3N·5HF) to give an electrophilic [PhSeF] equivalent; this adds to the α,β-unsaturated ester to form a seleniranium ion, which is opened regioselectively by F− at the carbon best able to stabilize positive charge (α-PhSe stabilization or α-alkyl stabilization), delivering the vicinal F/SePh adduct.

key_intermediates.name: α-PhSe-stabilized β-carbocation intermediate (more stable for 1a, leading to 2a)
β-PhSe / α-carbocation intermediate A (minor for 1a, leading to 3a)
Seleniranium ion from crotonate (1c) opened to α-cation stabilized by α-PhSe (intermediate B)
Seleniranium from methacrylate (1b) opened to β-cation stabilized by α-methyl (more stable, leading to 3b)

10.1016_j.tet.2008.12.068.notes: Regiochemistry is rationalized by relative stability of the cationic intermediate arising from seleniranium opening: with simple acrylate (1a) α-PhSe stabilization dominates giving 2a as major (β-F, α-SePh); with α-methyl substrates (methacrylate 1b) the tertiary β-carbocation stabilized by the α-methyl is favored, inverting regioselectivity toward 3b (α-F, β-SePh); crotonate (1c) gives exclusively 2c. No radical traps, CV, or DFT reported in these pages; mechanism is inferred from product distributions and substituent effects.

10.1016_j.tetlet.2019.04.009.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical oxidative sulfonylation/semipinacol ring-expansion of 1-(1-arylvinyl)cyclobutanols with arylsulfonyl hydrazides to give β-sulfonyl cyclopentanones.
10.1016_j.tetlet.2019.04.009.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of iodide (NaI) to I2, which oxidizes ArSO2NHNH2 to a sulfonyl iodide that fragments to an arylsulfonyl radical; the resulting carbon radical from radical addition is further oxidized at the anode to a carbocation. Cathode reduces water to H2/hydroxide.
10.1016_j.tetlet.2019.04.009.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes I- to I2, which converts ArSO2NHNH2 into ArSO2I and then an ArSO2• radical; the sulfonyl radical adds to the vinyl group of the cyclobutanol, the resulting C-radical is oxidized at the anode to a benzylic cation, which undergoes 1,2-alkyl (semipinacol) ring expansion of the cyclobutanol to give the cyclopentanone product. Cathode reduces water to H2 and hydroxide.

key_intermediates.name: tertiary carbon radical from ArSO2• addition to alkenyl cyclobutanol (II)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Only trace 3a formed with 3 equiv TEMPO, consistent with a radical intermediate.

probe_reagents.name: Ts-I (preformed sulfonyl iodide)
probe_reagents.observation: Ts-I with electrolysis gave 3a in 51%; even without current Ts-I afforded 3a in 35%, supporting ArSO2I as the on-cycle sulfonyl radical precursor.

10.1016_j.tetlet.2019.04.009.notes: Desulfonation of 3l with Mg/MeOH gives α-methyl cyclopentanone 10, supporting the β-sulfonyl ketone connectivity. NaI acts as a redox mediator; without current, Ts-I still gives some product, indicating the electrochemistry mainly regenerates the I2/ArSO2I oxidant and oxidizes the C-radical to the cation for semipinacol rearrangement.

10.1016_j.tet.2023.133707.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical diazolation of multisubstituted alkenes with pyrazoles to form N,N'-ethylene-bridged bispyrazoles, with H2 as the sole byproduct
10.1016_j.tet.2023.133707.electrochemical_role: Anodic single-electron oxidation of the alkene to an alkene radical cation; cathodic reduction of protons to H2
10.1016_j.tet.2023.133707.electron_flow: The anode first oxidizes the alkene to a radical cation A, which is trapped by pyrazole and deprotonated to give C-radical B; a second anodic oxidation converts B to cation C, which is captured by a second pyrazole and deprotonated to deliver the bispyrazole, while protons are reduced to H2 at the cathode.
10.1016_j.tet.2023.133707.rate_determining_step: Initial anodic oxidation of the alkene (suggested by CV: oxidation peak of alkene 1a shifts from ~1.5 V to ~1.1–1.3 V vs SCE in presence of pyrazole)

key_intermediates.name: alkenyl radical cation A (from one-electron oxidation of alkene 1)
β-pyrazolyl carbon radical B (after nucleophilic addition of pyrazole to A and deprotonation)
carbocation intermediate C (from second anodic oxidation of B)
N,N'-ethylene-bridged bispyrazole product 3 (after second pyrazole addition and deprotonation)

probe_reagents.name: 1,1-diphenylethylene (1r)
probe_reagents.observation: No reaction, indicating a second substituent / specific substitution pattern on the alkene is required

probe_reagents.name: stilbene (1q)
probe_reagents.observation: Only trace product, supporting substrate-scope/mechanistic constraints

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of 1a, 2a, and 1a+2a
probe_reagents.observation: 2a alone inert; 1a oxidizes at Ep/2 ≈ +1.5 V vs SCE; mixture shifts onset to ~1.1 V and Ep/2 to +1.3 V, indicating pyrazole promotes alkene oxidation

10.1016_j.tet.2023.133707.notes: Mechanism is proposed based mainly on CV and literature precedent; no radical-trap (TEMPO/BHT), KIE, or DFT studies were reported. Gram-scale experiment (0.865 g, 55%) demonstrates scalability. Alternative pathways (e.g., direct two-electron oxidation to a vinyl cation, or initial pyrazole oxidation) are not explicitly ruled out, though CV shows pyrazole 2a is inert in the operating window.

10.1016_j.tet.2017.10.047.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical synthesis of vinyl sulfones from sulfonyl hydrazides and styrenes using KI as redox mediator in an undivided cell
10.1016_j.tet.2017.10.047.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of iodide to I2 (and to p-toluenesulfonyl iodide A); the sulfonyl iodide A is reduced at the cathode to sulfinate anion B which is then re-oxidized at the anode to sulfonyl radical C; benzyl radical D is further oxidized at the anode to benzyl cation E. H2O is reduced at the cathode to H2 and OH-.
10.1016_j.tet.2017.10.047.electron_flow: At the anode, iodide is oxidized to I2 which converts TsNHNH2 into sulfonyl iodide A; A is reduced at the cathode via ECE to sulfinate B which is then anodically oxidized to sulfonyl radical C. C adds to styrene to give benzyl radical D, which is anodically oxidized to cation E; loss of H+ (or HI via iodosulfone F) yields the vinyl sulfone.

probe_reagents.name: p-toluenesulfonyl iodide A (isolated/prepared)
probe_reagents.observation: Independently prepared; its CV shows irreversible cathodic reduction (~-0.75 V vs SCE) consistent with ECE reduction to sulfinate

probe_reagents.name: sodium p-toluenesulfinate (TsNa)
probe_reagents.observation: CV shows irreversible oxidation at +0.78 V; current matches 1e- (vs ferrocene) confirming single-electron oxidation to sulfonyl radical C

probe_reagents.name: ferrocene
probe_reagents.observation: Used as 1e- reference to calibrate the electron count of the sulfinate oxidation

probe_reagents.name: β-hydroxysulfone 4ao
probe_reagents.observation: Electrolysis under reaction conditions did NOT give vinyl sulfone 3ao, ruling out dehydroxylation pathway

probe_reagents.name: p-methoxystyrene / α-methylstyrene
probe_reagents.observation: Yielded β-hydroxysulfones 4, supporting intermediacy of stabilized benzyl cation E

10.1016_j.tet.2017.10.047.notes: Two pathways from cation E are proposed: (i) direct deprotonation to vinyl sulfone, or (ii) iodide trapping to β-iodosulfone F followed by HI elimination. With electron-rich styrenes, E is trapped by water to give β-hydroxysulfones (off-pathway, control experiment shows these do not dehydrate to vinyl sulfones). Hypoiodite/iodate species formed from I2 + cathodically generated OH- may also participate in oxidizing the hydrazide.

10.1016_j.tet.2021.132535.rxn_class_summary: Electroreductive linear dimerization of electron-deficient vinylarenes to 1,4-diarylbutanes in an undivided cell with carbon electrodes using n-Bu4NBr/CH3CN-DMF, where CH3CN serves as the H-atom source.
10.1016_j.tet.2021.132535.electrochemical_role: Cathodic single-electron reduction of the electron-deficient vinylarene generates a radical anion that adds to a second vinylarene; after H-atom abstraction from CH3CN the resulting carbanion is re-oxidized at the anode to a radical, which abstracts a second H from CH3CN to give the saturated dimer.
10.1016_j.tet.2021.132535.electron_flow: Cathode reduces vinylarene 6a to radical anion 15a, which adds across another 6a to give distonic intermediate 16a; HAT from CH3CN furnishes carbanion 17a, which is oxidized at the anode to radical 18a; a second HAT from CH3CN delivers the 1,4-diarylbutane 12a.

key_intermediates.name: distonic radical anion from radical addition to second vinylarene (16a)
benzylic carbanion after H-atom transfer from CH3CN (17a)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Product 12a not detected under standard conditions with 2.0 equiv TEMPO, consistent with a radical intermediate.

probe_reagents.name: D2O
probe_reagents.observation: Addition of D2O (0.1 mL) gave 12a in 62% yield with no deuterium incorporation, indicating H does not come from water.

probe_reagents.name: CD3CN
probe_reagents.observation: Replacing CH3CN with CD3CN gave [D]-12a in 60% yield with 100% D incorporation at the benzylic positions, demonstrating CH3CN is the H-atom source.

10.1016_j.tet.2021.132535.notes: Authors note that other mechanistic pathways cannot be ruled out. Strong electron-withdrawing substituents favor reaction by stabilizing the intermediate anion 17a, consistent with the proposed cathodic reduction initiation. A cross-coupling variant (4-vinylpyridine 6y + acrylate 6aa → 12aa, 52%) is also demonstrated.

10.1016_j.tet.2007.10.104.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical carboxylation of cinnamate esters with CO2 at a metallic ring cathode with sacrificial Mg anode, affording benzylmalonate (dicarboxylic) and monocarboxylic ester products.
10.1016_j.tet.2007.10.104.electrochemical_role: Cathodic one-electron reduction of the cinnamate ester C=C to a radical anion, which attacks CO2; a second cathodic one-electron reduction generates a carbanion that is carboxylated again or protonated.
10.1016_j.tet.2007.10.104.electron_flow: Cathode delivers one electron to the cinnamate ester to form a radical anion, which either adds to CO2 (major) or is protonated to a benzylic radical (minor); a second cathodic electron reduces these to carbanions that are trapped by CO2 (giving dicarboxylate) or by H+ (giving monocarboxylate), while the Mg anode is sacrificially oxidized.
10.1016_j.tet.2007.10.104.rate_determining_step: First one-electron reduction of the cinnamate ester to the radical anion

key_intermediates.name: benzylic radical from protonation of B (C)
carboxylated radical anion D (from B + CO2)
α-ester radical E (from C + CO2, after e-)
carbanion F (from second reduction of D)
monocarboxylic acid product G (from E pathway)

probe_reagents.name: CO2 saturation in CV experiment
probe_reagents.observation: First reduction peak shifts positive and current increases (ip/ip0 ≈ 1.76–1.96), indicating reaction of the electrogenerated radical anion with CO2.

10.1016_j.tet.2007.10.104.notes: Two competing branches diverge at the radical anion B: nucleophilic addition to CO2 (major, leading to F and then dicarboxylic acid I or monocarboxylic acid H) vs protonation to benzylic radical C (minor, giving monocarboxylic acid G after further reduction/CO2 capture). Authors argue carbanion F is sterically hindered, so H+ trapping outcompetes a second CO2 addition, accounting for the mixture of mono- and dicarboxylic products.

10.1016_j.tet.2016.10.058.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical asymmetric oxidative dimerization of cinnamic acid L-proline amides to furofuran bislactones, precursors of natural lignans (yangambin, sesamin, eudesmin).
10.1016_j.tet.2016.10.058.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation (Pt anode) of the electron-rich aryl alkene of the cinnamoyl-proline substrate to an aryl/alkene radical cation, which dimerizes and cyclizes to the furofuran bislactone.
10.1016_j.tet.2016.10.058.electron_flow: At the Pt anode, the electron-rich arylcinnamate is oxidized by SET to a radical cation; radical-cation coupling/dimerization followed by intramolecular trapping of the carboxylate oxygens onto the resulting cationic centers builds the furofuran bislactone with the proline auxiliary controlling stereochemistry. Aldehyde 3 (ArCHO) is a competing fragmentation product.

key_intermediates.name: dimeric bis(radical cation)/cyclized cationic intermediate leading to furofuran bislactone

probe_reagents.name: L-proline t-butyl ester auxiliary
probe_reagents.observation: Acts as chiral auxiliary; bislactones 2a-d obtained with up to >95% e.e., demonstrating diastereoselective radical-cation coupling.

probe_reagents.name: temperature variation (rt, 0, -20, -40 °C)
probe_reagents.observation: Lower temperature gave higher yield and e.e. (52% yield, 91% e.e. at -40 °C for 1a).

probe_reagents.name: supporting electrolyte screen (Et4NClO4, Et4NOTFA, n-Bu4NPF6, n-Bu4NHSO4, halide salts)
probe_reagents.observation: Halide-containing electrolytes were ineffective; non-coordinating salts gave comparable modest yields with 1c.

probe_reagents.name: protic acid screen (TFA, TfOH, HSO3F, MsOH, HClO4, HBF4·Et2O)
probe_reagents.observation: Only TFA (and trace with TfOH) afforded bislactone, implicating need for a non-nucleophilic Brønsted acid co-solvent to stabilize cationic intermediates.

probe_reagents.name: PbO2/TFA chemical oxidation control
probe_reagents.observation: PbO2 in TFA/CH2Cl2 at -78 °C also produced 2a (64%, >95% e.e.), supporting a SET/radical-cation pathway analogous to the anodic process.

10.1016_j.tet.2016.10.058.notes: Mechanism is proposed by analogy to Ronlan-type anodic phenolic/styrene oxidative coupling; no TEMPO/BHT trapping, KIE, CV, or DFT reported. Aldehyde 3 (ArCHO) is observed as a C-C cleavage side product, consistent with fragmentation of the radical-cation/dimeric cation manifold. More electron-rich arenes (trimethoxy, methylenedioxy) gave better yields, consistent with easier anodic SET, opposite to a prior racemic chemical synthesis by Yuzikhin.

10.6023_cjoc202105046.rxn_class_summary: ArI-mediated electrochemical aziridination of electron-deficient alkenes using acetyl hydrazide-type N-source in an undivided cell
10.6023_cjoc202105046.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of ArI to ArI(OAc)2 (hypervalent iodine, I(III)) which serves as the active mediator; cathodic reduction generates H2 and regenerates acetate, closing the electron cycle
10.6023_cjoc202105046.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes ArI to ArI(OAc)2 (A), which exchanges an OAc for the N-nucleophile (Fg-NH2) to give iodine(III) intermediate B bearing I-O and I-N bonds; B partitions into acetoxyamine 6 (releasing ArI) or iodonitrene 7 (releasing HOAc), and both deliver nitrogen to the alkene to give the aziridine in a stepwise manner. At the cathode, protons are reduced to H2, regenerating acetate.

key_intermediates.name: ArI(OAc)(NHFg) (iodine(III) species with I-O and I-N bonds, intermediate B)

probe_reagents.name: HRMS detection of reaction mixture
probe_reagents.observation: Species with masses matching compound 6 (HRMS 221.0568) and compound 7 (394.9892) both detected, supporting two coexisting pathways via acetoxyamine and iodonitrene

10.6023_cjoc202105046.notes: Two parallel pathways are proposed (via acetoxyamine 6 and via iodonitrene 7), both leading to stepwise aziridination; the authors do not designate one as exclusively favored. Mechanism cites ref [10c] for the stepwise aziridination mode. No radical traps, KIE, or DFT reported on these pages.

10.6023_cjoc202108006.rxn_class_summary: Ni-catalyzed electrochemical reductive cross-coupling of trifluoromethyl alkenes with alkyl halides or alkyl-substituted redox-active esters to give gem-difluoroalkenes via allylic defluorinative alkylation
10.6023_cjoc202108006.electrochemical_role: Cathodic reduction of NiBr2(diglyme) to Ni(0) active catalyst; cathode also reduces alkyl-Ni(II) species (or benzylic radical) to enable β-fluoride elimination; Zn serves as sacrificial anode
10.6023_cjoc202108006.electron_flow: Cathode reduces Ni(II) precatalyst to Ni(0), which undergoes SET to alkyl halide/RAE generating R• and Ni(I)X; R• adds to the CF3-alkene to form an α-CF3 benzylic radical that is either captured by Ni(I) to form alkyl-Ni(II) (Pathway I) or further cathodically reduced to a benzylic anion (Pathway II); β-fluoride elimination then delivers the gem-difluoroalkene, with Zn anode supplying the balancing oxidation.

key_intermediates.name: alkyl radical (R•) from SET to alkyl halide or RAE
α-trifluoromethyl benzylic radical (after radical addition to CF3-alkene)
alkyl-Ni(II)X species (benzylic radical captured by Ni(I))
benzylic anion (from cathodic reduction, Pathway II)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction strongly inhibited (yield drops from 77% to <2% with 0.5–3.0 equiv TEMPO); R-TEMPO adduct (0.076 mmol) isolated, confirming alkyl radical intermediate

probe_reagents.name: (Bromomethyl)cyclopropane (2u)
probe_reagents.observation: Only ring-opened product 4bu (25%) obtained, consistent with cyclopropylcarbinyl radical ring-opening

probe_reagents.name: 6-iodohex-1-ene (alkenyl iodide 2v)
probe_reagents.observation: Cyclized product 4bva and acyclic 4bvb obtained in 5:1 ratio (61% yield), supporting a 5-exo-trig radical cyclization

probe_reagents.name: Acrylamide-containing CF3-alkene 1l (competition)
probe_reagents.observation: Mono- and dialkylation products formed at the CF3-alkene site; acrylamide untouched, showing higher reactivity of trifluoromethyl alkene toward the alkyl radical

10.6023_cjoc202108006.notes: Authors propose two divergent pathways after radical addition: (Pathway I) capture of α-CF3 benzylic radical by Ni(I) to form an alkylnickel that undergoes β-F elimination, or (Pathway II) cathodic reduction of the benzylic radical to a carbanion followed by β-F elimination. A Ni(I)/Ni(III) catalytic manifold cannot be excluded. Chemoselectivity observed: alkyl-RAE > alkyl bromide in competition (substrate 4am), but aliphatic (non-aryl) CF3-alkenes are unreactive under standard conditions.

10.1002_cjoc.200790177.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical dicarboxylation of styrene with CO2 at a sacrificial Mg anode to give 2-phenylsuccinic acid
10.1002_cjoc.200790177.electrochemical_role: Cathodic one-electron reduction of styrene to its radical anion; sacrificial Mg anode supplies Mg2+ to stabilize carboxylate intermediates
10.1002_cjoc.200790177.electron_flow: Cathode delivers one electron to styrene forming a radical anion B; B attacks CO2 yielding the monocarboxylated radical C, which is further reduced at the cathode to the benzyl carbanion D; D attacks a second CO2 to give the dicarboxylate E. Mg anode is oxidized to Mg2+, which pairs with the carboxylates.
10.1002_cjoc.200790177.rate_determining_step: Heterogeneous one-electron transfer to styrene (α = 0.21, ∂Ep/∂lg v ≈ 70.7 mV/decade, indicating slow heterogeneous electron transfer)

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of styrene with/without CO2
probe_reagents.observation: Without CO2: irreversible peak at -2.64 V (vs SCE). With 0.28 mol/L CO2: original peak shifts to less negative potentials and a new reduction peak appears at more negative potentials, supporting sequential SET/CO2 addition pathway.

10.1002_cjoc.200790177.notes: Mechanism is supported primarily by CV data and consistent with general electrocarboxylation literature; no radical traps or isotope labeling were employed. The Mg2+ generated at the sacrificial anode is invoked to stabilize/precipitate the carboxylate intermediates and shift equilibria toward product. Lower temperatures favor carboxylation by increasing CO2 solubility.

10.1002_cjoc.202400127.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical multicomponent cascade of indole-tethered alkenes with CF3SO2Na and n-Bu4NI to assemble iodomethyl spiropyrrolidinyl-oxindoles via iodide-mediated radical trifluoromethylsulfonyloxylation, spirocyclization, and water coupling.
10.1002_cjoc.202400127.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of iodide (I-) to I2 acts as the mediator that oxidizes CF3SO2Na to the CF3S(O)O• radical; additional anodic oxidations convert the spirocyclic C-radical to a cation and oxidize the hemiaminal to the oxindole carbonyl.
10.1002_cjoc.202400127.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes I- to I2, which oxidizes CF3SO2- to CF3S(O)O•; this O-centered radical adds to the tethered alkene (A), 5-exo-trig spirocyclization onto the indole gives C3-radical B, which is anodically oxidized to indoleninium C and deprotonated to indolenine D; a second anodic oxidation furnishes sulfonate ester E, SN2 displacement by I- yields iodomethyl indolenine 5, water addition gives hemiaminal F, and final anodic oxidation delivers the oxindole 3a.
10.1002_cjoc.202400127.rate_determining_step: Radical addition of CF3S(O)O• to the alkene via TS1 (ΔG‡ = 27.8 kcal/mol), the highest barrier on the computed profile.

key_intermediates.name: Intermediate A: C-radical from O-addition of CF3S(O)O• to alkene of 1a
Intermediate B: spirocyclic indolyl C3-radical after 5-exo cyclization
Intermediate C: cationic indoleninium after anodic oxidation of B
Intermediate D: indolenine bearing CH2-OS(O)CF3 group
Intermediate E: sulfonic ester after second anodic oxidation of D
Intermediate 5: iodomethyl indolenine (SN2 product from E + I-)
Intermediate F: hemiaminal from H2O addition to 5
I2 (iodine, mediator from anodic oxidation of I-)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Product 3a only in trace; TEMPO–SO2CF3 adduct 4 detected by ESI-MS, confirming CF3S(O)O• radical.

probe_reagents.name: Reaction without CF3SO2Na
probe_reagents.observation: Only trace 3a, demonstrating CF3SO2Na is essential as the radical source.

probe_reagents.name: Reaction under N2 (no air)
probe_reagents.observation: Only trace 3a; suggests O2 or aerobic conditions contribute (control).

probe_reagents.name: N-alkyl indole substrates (1q, 1r; R = Me, Et)
probe_reagents.observation: 0% yield of spiroproduct, supporting indoleninium/indolenine intermediate requiring free N–H for tautomerization.

probe_reagents.name: Intermediate 5 detection (2 h reaction)
probe_reagents.observation: Iodomethyl indolenine intermediate 5 detected by ESI-MS, supporting proposed pathway.

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of n-Bu4NI, CF3SO2Na, 1a
probe_reagents.observation: Oxidation peak for n-Bu4NI at 0.71 V; CF3SO2Na at 1.90 V; no oxidation peak for 1a — reaction is triggered by iodide oxidation (mediator).

10.1002_cjoc.202400127.notes: Alternative 6-endo spirocyclization (TS2′, ΔG‡ = 25.6 kcal/mol) was computed but disfavored vs 5-exo TS2 (21.8 kcal/mol), explaining regioselectivity for the 5-membered spirocycle. An alternative direct SN2 on intermediate D by iodide was ruled out by DFT (TS3′ = 35.7 kcal/mol) in favor of prior anodic oxidation to sulfonic ester E followed by SN2 (TS3 = 26.0 kcal/mol). Direct CF3• incorporation (trifluoromethyl spiroproduct) was not observed — O- rather than C-radical addition operates.

10.1002_cjoc.202300174.rxn_class_summary: Electrophotocatalytic reductive three-component 1,2-diarylation of alkenes with aryl halides and cyanoaromatics (isocyanopyridines) in an undivided cell, mediated by phenanthrene and 4-DPAIPN photocatalyst.
10.1002_cjoc.202300174.electrochemical_role: Cathodic reduction of phenanthrene mediator generates phenanthrene radical anion, which reduces ArX to the aryl halide radical anion that fragments to aryl radical; anodic oxidation of DABCO/formate provides sacrificial electrons (replacing sacrificial metal anode).
10.1002_cjoc.202300174.electron_flow: Cathode reduces phenanthrene to its radical anion, which performs outer-sphere SET to ArX, giving an aryl halide radical anion that mesolytically cleaves to aryl radical; the aryl radical adds to the styrene to give a benzylic radical that couples with the photoreduced cyanoaromatic radical anion (formed by SET from PC*– or CO2•–), and decyanation delivers the 1,2-diaryl product.
