DABCO/formate are oxidized at the anode to regenerate the catalysts.
10.1002_cjoc.202300174.rate_determining_step: Formation of the aryl halide radical anion via SET from phenanthrene radical anion (TS_I, ΔG‡ = 31.6 kcal/mol in DFT model with iodobenzene)

probe_reagents.name: current on/off control
probe_reagents.observation: No 4aaa formed without electric current; electrocatalysis essential.

probe_reagents.name: light on/off control
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction proceeds without light or 4-DPAIPN but in lower yields; photoredox significantly improves yield.

probe_reagents.name: phenanthrene omission
probe_reagents.observation: Yield drops sharply without phenanthrene, indicating its role as cathodic mediator.

probe_reagents.name: DABCO omission / amine swap (DIEA, Et3N, quinuclidine)
probe_reagents.observation: Without DABCO yield falls to 7%; other amines give 15–58%, indicating DABCO acts as sacrificial electron donor.

probe_reagents.name: HCO2Na omission
probe_reagents.observation: Yield drops to 11%, indicating formate acts as reductant/H source via CO2•–.

probe_reagents.name: NaOAc omission
probe_reagents.observation: Yield drops to 42%, supporting role in halide/cyanide removal.

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of substrates and additives
probe_reagents.observation: 1a Ep/2red = -2.79 V; 2a -2.46 V; 3a -1.97 V; phenanthrene -2.58 V (shifted to -2.29 V with 2a, -2.15 V with 3a); DABCO/HCO2Na/NaOAc/4-DPAIPN mixture shows reduced potential -0.96 V for 2a, demonstrating cooperative reductive activation.

10.1002_cjoc.202300174.notes: An alternative photoredox-only pathway is also proposed in which reductive quenching of PC* by DABCO yields PC•–, which together with CO2•– (from HCO2Na/DABCO•+) reduces the aryl halide directly; both the electrocatalytic (phenanthrene-mediated) and photoredox pathways operate cooperatively. DFT shows direct dissociation of CN– to pyridyl radical R4 is highly endergonic (+25.6 kcal/mol), ruling out a free pyridyl radical pathway; instead, radical-radical coupling between benzylic radical and cyanoaromatic radical anion followed by decyanation (TS_IV, 7.9 kcal/mol) is favored. Overall process is exergonic by 82.1 kcal/mol.

10.6023_cjoc202004051.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical selenylation/cyclization of N-allylthioamides with diselenides to form selenyl-substituted 4,5-dihydrothiazoles
10.6023_cjoc202004051.electrochemical_role: Cathodic reduction of diaryl diselenide cleaves Se-Se bond to give a selenyl radical and a selenide anion; the selenide anion is reoxidized at the anode to a selenyl radical, which adds to the alkene; the resulting C-radical is further oxidized at the anode to a carbocation.
10.6023_cjoc202004051.electron_flow: Diselenide is reduced at the cathode to a selenyl radical A and selenide anion B; B is reoxidized at the anode back to A; A adds to the allyl C=C of the thioamide generating a β-selenyl C-radical C, which is anodically oxidized to carbocation D; intramolecular 5-exo-trig attack of the thiocarbonyl S followed by deprotonation furnishes the dihydrothiazole, with cathodic H+ reduction (½ H2) closing the redox balance.

key_intermediates.name: Alkyl C-radical from selenyl addition to allyl thioamide (C)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Only trace 3a formed in the presence of 2 equiv TEMPO, supporting a radical pathway

probe_reagents.name: Cyclopropyl-substituted allyl thioamide (1x, radical clock)
probe_reagents.observation: 3x obtained in 54% yield with the cyclopropyl group intact, excluding an intramolecular thiyl radical cyclization/selenylation mechanism

probe_reagents.name: Diphenyl diselenide (cyclic voltammetry)
probe_reagents.observation: Reduction peak observed at -0.51 V, indicating facile cathodic reduction of 2a

10.6023_cjoc202004051.notes: An alternative pathway involving intramolecular thiyl radical cyclization followed by selenylation was explicitly excluded by the cyclopropyl radical-clock experiment (ring-opening not observed).

10.1002_adsc.202200847.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical intramolecular cyclization of 2-alkenyl-substituted arenes (benzoic acids, benzamides, phenols, anilines) to give phthalides, isoindolinones, benzofurans, and indoles via anodic oxidation of the alkene to a radical cation followed by heteroatom cyclization.
10.1002_adsc.202200847.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of the styrenyl alkene to a radical cation that is intercepted by a tethered nucleophile (CO2-, CONHR, OH, NHTs); for 2-alkenylbenzoic acid 1a a prior cathodic reduction generates the carboxylate (Int-1a) with H2 evolution before anodic oxidation.
10.1002_adsc.202200847.electron_flow: For the phthalide manifold, the cathode first reduces the carboxylic acid (H2 evolution) to its carboxylate, which is then anodically oxidized at the alkene to a radical cation; 5-exo (or 6-endo in HFIP) O-cyclization gives a benzylic radical that is further oxidized at the anode to a cation and trapped by MeOH/methoxide. For benzamide, phenol, and N-Ts aniline substrates, direct anodic oxidation of the styrene gives a radical cation, intramolecular N- or O-attack with loss of H+ affords a cyclic C-radical, which is oxidized again to a cation (or in the benzamide case undergoes HAT with MeCN) and loses H+ to give the aromatic isoindolinone/benzofuran/indole; in the benzofuran case tosylate is incorporated then β-eliminated.

key_intermediates.name: benzylic cation (Int-1d) trapped by methoxide to give phthalide 2a

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: All four reactions inhibited; no TEMPO adduct detected by MS.

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: Reactions inhibited; trace product only; no BHT adduct detected.

probe_reagents.name: acetonitrile-d3
probe_reagents.observation: 50% D incorporation into the CH2 of isoindolinone 5a, supporting HAT from MeCN to Int-4b.

probe_reagents.name: (E)-stilbene
probe_reagents.observation: Gives vic-bis(tosyloxy)stilbene 12 in 23% under standard conditions with Et4NOTs, supporting tosylate trapping of an alkene radical cation.

probe_reagents.name: Koser reagent (PhI(OH)OTs, 13)
probe_reagents.observation: Stoichiometric addition to 7a (no current) afforded 2-phenylbenzofuran 8a in 31%, mimicking the proposed tosyloxy-cation intermediate.

probe_reagents.name: 2,4,6-collidine
probe_reagents.observation: Non-oxidizable base; addition to 1a generates a new oxidation wave at 1.10 V vs SCE, indicating that the deprotonated carboxylate is the species being anodically oxidized.

probe_reagents.name: current off (no electrolysis)
probe_reagents.observation: No reaction without electric current, confirming the electrochemical origin of the radical intermediates.

10.1002_adsc.202200847.notes: Selectivity between 5-exo (phthalide 2a, in MeOH) and 6-endo (isochromanone 3a, in HFIP) cyclization of Int-1b is unexplained; both Path A and Path B are retained as possibilities. For benzofuran formation, Path B (tosylate-assisted) is favored over Path A (direct cyclization of Int-7a) because Et4NOTs dramatically raises yield (12% → 62%) and stilbene/Koser control experiments support OTs incorporation, but Path A cannot be excluded. The unexpected formation of isoindolin-1-one 5h in DMSO (a poor H-atom donor) suggests an alternative non-HAT pathway in that case. Chemoselectivity between C–N and C–O bond formation for 2-alkenylbenzamides in MeOH (giving iminoisobenzofurans 6) vs MeCN (giving isoindolinones 5) is not yet explained.

10.1002_adsc.202001583_sup_1.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical bromocyclization of N-aryl acrylamides with KBr to form bromo-substituted indolo[2,1-a]isoquinolines
10.1002_adsc.202001583_sup_1.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of bromide (Br-) from KBr to generate Br radical (Path A) or Br2/bromonium (Path B); cathodic reduction of protons to H2
10.1002_adsc.202001583_sup_1.electron_flow: At the anode, Br- is oxidized to Br• (or further to Br2); in Path A, Br• adds to the acrylamide olefin to give C-radical E, which undergoes 6-endo-trig cyclization onto the pendant aryl to give radical F, then a second anodic oxidation (or Br2) yields arenium cation G, which loses H+ to afford 4a. Meanwhile, protons are reduced at the cathode to H2.

key_intermediates.name: bromine radical (Br•) from anodic oxidation of Br-
C-centered radical E from Br• addition to olefin of 1a
cyclohexadienyl radical F after 6-endo-trig cyclization onto aryl ring
arenium cation G after further anodic oxidation (or Br2 oxidation) of F
bromonium ion H from Br2 addition to olefin (Path B)
Wheland/arenium intermediate I after electrophilic cyclization (Path B)

10.1002_adsc.202001583_sup_1.notes: Two pathways are proposed. Favored Path A is a radical cascade (Br• addition / 6-endo-trig / oxidation / deprotonation), analogous to the authors' sulfonyl-radical cyclization to indolo[2,1-a]isoquinolines. Alternative Path B is an electrophilic bromocyclization via a bromonium ion H and arenium I. Mechanism is proposed based on prior literature analogy rather than dedicated probe experiments shown on this page.

10.1002_adsc.202201155_sup_1.rxn_class_summary: Cobalt-catalyzed electrochemical C–H/N–H annulation of 8-aminoquinoline benzamides with alkenes/alkynes to form isoquinolinones
10.1002_adsc.202201155_sup_1.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation regenerates the active high-valent Co(III)/Co(IV) species from Co(II) after C–H metalation, enabling catalytic turnover
10.1002_adsc.202201155_sup_1.electron_flow: Co(II)(acac)2 coordinates the benzamide N–H (deprotonated by NaOPiv) and undergoes CMD-type ortho C–H metalation; alkene insertion and C–N reductive elimination give the isoquinolinone with release of Co(I/II), which is reoxidized at the GF anode to regenerate the active Co(III) catalyst.
10.1002_adsc.202201155_sup_1.rate_determining_step: Not the C–H cleavage step (kH/kD = 1.2 indicates C–H activation is not rate-limiting)

probe_reagents.name: [D5]-benzamide 1a
probe_reagents.observation: Parallel reactions gave 3aa (86%) and [D4]-3aa (80%) with kH/kD = 1.2, indicating C–H cleavage is not rate-determining

probe_reagents.name: D-labeled substrate (H/D exchange)
probe_reagents.observation: H/D exchange experiment performed to probe reversibility of C–H metalation

10.1002_adsc.202201155_sup_1.notes: Mechanism inferred from standard Co(III)/8-aminoquinoline-directed C–H activation paradigm combined with anodic reoxidation; only KIE and H/D exchange probes shown in the provided pages. No radical traps or CV data presented here.

10.1002_adsc.202500174.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical radical-polar crossover (RPC) coupling of ortho-vinyl benzamides with C-H acidic malonate-type esters to assemble multi-ester functionalized benzoxazines and iminoisobenzofurans.
10.1002_adsc.202500174.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation: base (tBuOK) deprotonates the C-H acidic ester to a stabilized carbanion, which is oxidized at the graphite anode to an electrophilic C-centered radical; after radical addition to the alkene, the resulting benzylic radical is further oxidized at the anode to a benzylic carbocation (RPC). Cathode reduces protons to H2.
10.1002_adsc.202500174.electron_flow: tBuOK deprotonates the malonate ester; the carbanion is oxidized at the anode to an electrophilic radical, which adds to the styrenyl alkene of the o-vinyl benzamide to give a benzylic radical; this radical is oxidized again at the anode to a benzylic cation (radical-polar crossover), which is trapped intramolecularly by the amide oxygen (after amide tautomerization) to give the O-heterocycle, while the cathode reduces protons to H2.

key_intermediates.name: benzylic C-radical adduct from radical addition to o-vinyl benzamide
benzylic carbocation after second anodic oxidation (RPC)

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of substrate 1 (o-vinyl benzamide)
probe_reagents.observation: No oxidation observed between 0 and 1.30 V vs SCE, indicating alkene 1 is not directly oxidized.

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of substrate 2 (C-H acidic ester)
probe_reagents.observation: Shows oxidation in the accessible window; anodic oxidation of the deprotonated C-H nucleophile is strongly preferred, supporting the RPC pathway with 2 as the species oxidized at the anode.

10.1002_adsc.202500174.notes: Mechanism is supported primarily by CV comparison of the two substrates plus literature precedent; no radical trap (TEMPO/BHT), KIE, or DFT experiments are reported on these pages. The amide acts as a tethered O-nucleophile, with amide isomerization (imidic acid tautomer) invoked to enable O-attack on the benzylic cation; depending on the N-substituent the product is a benzoxazine or an iminoisobenzofuran.

10.1002_adsc.202401108_sup_1.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical iodo-trifluoroethoxylation of terminal alkenes using an aryl iodide as iodine source and TFE as nucleophile/solvent
10.1002_adsc.202401108_sup_1.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of the aryl iodide (2-iodo-1,3-dimethoxy-5-methylbenzene) generates an electrophilic/radical iodine species that adds across the alkene; TFE then traps the resulting cation/intermediate
10.1002_adsc.202401108_sup_1.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes the electron-rich aryl iodide to release an electrophilic iodine equivalent that adds to the alkene; TFE acts as the oxygen nucleophile to capture the resulting cationic/radical intermediate and deliver the β-iodo trifluoroethyl ether.

key_intermediates.name: aryl iodide-derived I-centered radical or iodonium
β-iodo carbon radical or iodiranium ion from alkene

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: Under standard electrolysis with 2.5 equiv BHT, yield of 3a dropped to 19% and 73% of alkene 2a was recovered, consistent with a radical intermediate

probe_reagents.name: N-iodosuccinimide (NIS)
probe_reagents.observation: Replacing the aryl iodide with NIS under non-electrochemical conditions (DCM/TFE, rt, 25 h, no current) gave 3a in 58% yield (2:1), supporting that an electrophilic I+ source can drive the iodo-etherification independent of electricity

10.1002_adsc.202401108_sup_1.notes: The NIS control suggests an I+ / iodiranium (ionic) pathway is viable, while BHT suppression supports a radical component — consistent with a radical/polar crossover mechanism in which anodic oxidation of ArI generates an iodine radical that adds to the alkene, followed by oxidation to a cation trapped by TFE.

10.1002_adsc.202200597.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical anodic oxyselenation of N-acyl allylamines with diaryl diselenides via anchimeric-assisted acyl migration to give β-acyloxy-γ-selenyl amides and selenyl carbamates (oxazolidinones).
10.1002_adsc.202200597.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of PhSeSePh (diphenyl diselenide) to generate electrophilic PhSe+ (with H2 evolution at the Pt cathode); PhSe+ acts as the active selenating electrophile on the alkene.
10.1002_adsc.202200597.electron_flow: The graphite anode oxidizes PhSeSePh to PhSe+ (with PhSe• as a transient species recombining to PhSe+), while protons are reduced to H2 at the Pt cathode. PhSe+ electrophilically adds to the allylamine alkene to form a seleniranium ion, which is opened by intramolecular anchimeric attack of the N-acyl carbonyl oxygen; nucleophilic addition of water and subsequent fragmentation deliver either an acyl-migration product (6) or a cyclic carbamate (7).

key_intermediates.name: electrophilic PhSe+ (phenylselenenyl cation) generated at anode
seleniranium ion (Int. A) from PhSe+ addition to allylamine alkene
5-exo cyclized oxocarbenium / 1,3-dioxolan-2-ylium-type intermediate from anchimeric assistance of acyl carbonyl oxygen (Int. B)
hemiorthoester-type intermediate after water addition (Int. C)
Int. C1 (R = Ph or alkyl acyl) leading to acyl-migration product 6 via loss of NTs
Int. C2 (R = tBuO or BnO) leading to oxazolidinone 7 via loss of tBuOH/BnOH

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Standard reaction with 3 equiv TEMPO still gave 6a in 71% yield, indicating a non-radical (cationic) pathway.

probe_reagents.name: Crossover substrates 4c (p-OMe-benzoyl) and 4o (p-CF3-benzoyl)
probe_reagents.observation: No crossover products observed (only 6c 87% and 6o 91%), indicating intramolecular acyl migration.

probe_reagents.name: H2 18O (isotope-labeled water)
probe_reagents.observation: Product 6a' incorporated 18O at the carbonyl oxygen (96% yield, detected by NMR), supporting water-promoted acyl migration through an orthoester-like intermediate.

probe_reagents.name: Regiochemical probe substrates (cinnamyl/internal alkene variants)
probe_reagents.observation: Regioselectivity controlled by both Markovnikov's rule and Baldwin's 5-endo over 6-exo rule; when in conflict, Baldwin's rule dominates (6z10:6z11 = 1:2).

probe_reagents.name: N-acetyl-N-benzoyl allylamine
probe_reagents.observation: Only benzoyl migration product 6z12 obtained (30%), with acetate migration only in trace; chemoselectivity attributed to greater nucleophilicity of benzoyl carbonyl oxygen.

probe_reagents.name: Cyclohex-2-en-1-amine derivative
probe_reagents.observation: Highly diastereoselective anti-oxyselenation product 6z13 obtained (84%, dr > 20:1); consistent with seleniranium opening via half-chair transition state.

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of PhSeSePh and substrates
probe_reagents.observation: Supports anodic oxidation of PhSeSePh as the initial electron-transfer event (see SI).

10.1002_adsc.202200597.notes: A radical pathway (PhSe• addition) is explicitly ruled out by TEMPO compatibility; the authors favor a cationic seleniranium/anchimeric-assistance mechanism. Divergence between acyl-migration products (6, when R = aryl/alkyl acyl) and cyclic carbamate products (7, when R = OtBu or OBn) is rationalized by which leaving group (NTs vs. alkoxide as ROH) is thermodynamically more favorable from intermediate C. The product distribution (6 vs 7) and regioselectivity in unsymmetrical alkenes is jointly governed by Markovnikov and Baldwin 5-endo > 6-exo preferences, with Baldwin dominating when they conflict.

10.1002_adsc.201901115.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical iodide-mediated intramolecular selenocyclization of unsaturated alcohols (and acids) with diaryl diselenides to give selenomethyl-substituted cyclic ethers and lactones
10.1002_adsc.201901115.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of iodide (I-) to I+ (via 2e- loss), which acts as electrophilic mediator generating an iodonium ion on the alkene; cathode reduces I2 back to I- and protons to H2, closing the redox cycle
10.1002_adsc.201901115.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes I- to I+ (2e- loss); I+ activates the alkene of the unsaturated alcohol to form an iodonium cation (A), which undergoes intramolecular O-cyclization with loss of H+ to give iodo-ether B; B is displaced by PhSe- (from PhSeSePh, releasing 1/2 I2) to deliver the selenoether product, and the cathode reduces I2 to I- and H+ to H2.

key_intermediates.name: iodo-cyclic ether intermediate B (after intramolecular O-attack and deprotonation)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Product 3b obtained in 75% yield under standard conditions; no inhibition, arguing against a radical pathway

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: Product 3b obtained in 74% yield; no inhibition, arguing against a radical pathway

probe_reagents.name: I2 (no current)
probe_reagents.observation: With I2 (1 equiv) and no current, 3b formed in 50% yield, supporting I+ as the active electrophilic species

probe_reagents.name: NH4I without current
probe_reagents.observation: No reaction between 1b and 2a with NH4I in the absence of current — confirms electrochemical activation needed

probe_reagents.name: divided cell experiment
probe_reagents.observation: Only trace 3b in divided cell, proving cathodic reduction of I2 back to I- is essential for catalytic turnover

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of NH4I, 1a, 2a and mixtures
probe_reagents.observation: NH4I shows two oxidation peaks at 0.38 V (I-/I2) and 0.67 V (I3-/I2); curves unchanged upon addition of 1a or 2a, indicating I- is oxidized first and substrates are not directly oxidized

10.1002_adsc.201901115.notes: A radical mechanism was explicitly considered but excluded based on TEMPO/BHT non-inhibition and the I2-only control giving product. The divided-cell experiment establishes paired electrolysis: anodic I- oxidation coupled with cathodic I2/H+ reduction is required for catalytic turnover.

10.1002_adsc.9600.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical multi-component hydroxy-thiocarbamation of electron-deficient alkenes with KSCN and H2O to give β-hydroxy primary S-thiocarbamates
10.1002_adsc.9600.electrochemical_role: Anodic single-electron oxidation of SCN- to thiocyanyl radical (SCN•); the benzylic radical adduct is further oxidized at the anode to a carbocation; protons are reduced at the cathode to H2
10.1002_adsc.9600.electron_flow: SCN- is oxidized at the anode to SCN•, which adds to the electron-deficient alkene to give a benzylic C-radical; a second anodic SET produces a benzylic cation that is trapped by H2O to give a β-hydroxy thiocyanate; HFIP-assisted hydration of the SCN group then yields the primary S-thiocarbamate while protons are reduced to H2 at the cathode.

key_intermediates.name: Benzylic carbon radical from SCN• addition to α-phenylacrylate, intermediate II
HFIP H-bond-activated thiocyanate adduct, intermediate V

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: Target product 4a suppressed to trace; HRMS detected BHT-SCN adduct and BHT-carbon radical adduct, confirming SCN• (I) and benzylic radical (II)

probe_reagents.name: D2O
probe_reagents.observation: D-labeled 4a detected by HRMS; N-H/O-H of carbamoyl and hydroxyl originate from water

probe_reagents.name: H2 18O
probe_reagents.observation: 18O-labeled 4a detected by HRMS; both hydroxyl and carbamoyl oxygens come from H2O

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of 1a and KSCN
probe_reagents.observation: KSCN onset oxidation +0.383 V vs SCE, much lower than α-phenylacrylate (+1.693 V), indicating SCN- is preferentially oxidized at the anode

probe_reagents.name: Pd/C hydrogenation of N-phenylmaleimide using cell-evolved H2
probe_reagents.observation: N-phenylsuccinimide 6a obtained in 59% yield, confirming cathodic H2 evolution

10.1002_adsc.9600.notes: HFIP (solvent) is proposed to activate the SCN group via H-bonding (intermediate V), enabling nucleophilic hydration of the nitrile-like SCN to deliver the primary S-thiocarbamate; no DFT or KIE reported.

10.1002_adsc.202300949.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical intramolecular oxyselenenylation of N-allylureas with diaryl diselenides to access selenomethyl-substituted cyclic isoureas (5- to 7-membered) via C-O/C-Se bond formation
10.1002_adsc.202300949.electrochemical_role: Anodic single-electron oxidation of diphenyl diselenide (PhSeSePh) generates the electrophilic phenylselenenyl cation (PhSe+); cathode reduces protons to H2
10.1002_adsc.202300949.electron_flow: At the anode, PhSeSePh loses two electrons to give 2 equiv of PhSe+; PhSe+ adds to the alkene of the N-allylurea forming a seleniranium-type cation B, which is opened by intramolecular nucleophilic attack of the urea oxygen, followed by loss of H+. The cathode reduces 2H+ to H2.

key_intermediates.name: phenylselenenyl cation (PhSe+) generated by anodic SET on PhSeSePh
seleniranium/thiiranium-like ion (B) from PhSe+ addition across the allyl C=C of the N-allylurea
selenomethyl-substituted cyclic isourea product 3a after intramolecular O-attack and deprotonation

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction proceeded smoothly with 1 equiv TEMPO giving 3a in 80% yield, indicating no free radical pathway

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction proceeded smoothly with 1 equiv BHT giving 3a in 79% yield, no inhibition

probe_reagents.name: phenyl hypochloroselenoite (PhSeCl, 5a)
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction of 1a with PhSeCl under standard conditions without electrolysis afforded 3a in 13% yield, supporting involvement of an electrophilic PhSe+ species

10.1002_adsc.202300949.notes: CV shows PhSeSePh (2a) oxidizes at ~1.0 V vs Ag/AgCl while 1a oxidizes at ~1.3 V, confirming the diselenide (not the urea/alkene) is the species oxidized at the anode. Authors initially describe the pathway as a 'cationic radical mechanism' based on TEMPO/BHT not inhibiting and the PhSeCl experiment, but the proposed cycle (Figure 3) is fundamentally a two-electron electrophilic selenocyclization via PhSe+ (cation-initiated), with no discrete C-centered radical invoked.

10.1002_adsc.202001583.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical radical cascade cyclization of 2-aryl-N-acryloyl indoles with arylsulfonyl hydrazides (or KBr) to access sulfonyl- and bromo-substituted indolo[2,1-a]isoquinolinones via iodide/bromide mediation.
10.1002_adsc.202001583.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of iodide (or bromide) to molecular I2 (or Br radical); I2 then activates arylsulfonyl hydrazide to release arylsulfonyl radical; cathode reduces protons to H2.
10.1002_adsc.202001583.electron_flow: At the anode, two iodide anions lose 2 e- to form I2, which converts arylsulfonyl hydrazide into Ts-I and releases a sulfonyl radical; this radical adds to the acrylamide olefin, undergoes 6-endo-trig cyclization onto the 2-aryl ring, and the resulting cyclohexadienyl radical is further oxidized (anodically or by I2) to a cation that deprotonates to give the aromatic product, while protons are reduced at the cathode to H2.

key_intermediates.name: molecular iodine (I2) generated by anodic oxidation of 2 I-
C-centered radical B from sulfonyl radical addition to acryloyl alkene
cyclohexadienyl radical C from 6-endo-trig cyclization onto 2-aryl ring
arenium cation D after oxidation of C (by anode or I2)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction fully inhibited, indicating a radical pathway.

probe_reagents.name: PBN (N-tert-butyl-α-phenylnitrone)
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction fully inhibited under standard conditions.

probe_reagents.name: I2 (stoichiometric, no current)
probe_reagents.observation: Product 3a formed in 48% yield without electricity, supporting I2 as the active mediator.

probe_reagents.name: NIS
probe_reagents.observation: 3a formed in 45% yield without current, consistent with electrophilic iodine species mediating the reaction.

probe_reagents.name: Ts-I (4-methylbenzenesulfonyl iodide)
probe_reagents.observation: 3a formed in 41% yield without current, supporting Ts-I as an on-cycle intermediate.

10.1002_adsc.202001583.notes: X-ray structure (CCDC 2050276) confirms product connectivity for 4a. For the bromo variant, an analogous cycle is proposed in which a Br radical is generated anodically from KBr and adds to the alkene to initiate the same 6-endo-trig cascade. No KIE, CV, EPR, or DFT data were reported.

10.1002_adsc.202200847_sup_1.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical transformation of styrene-type substrates studied by cyclic voltammetry to identify anodic oxidation potentials of substrate, mediator, and intermediates.
10.1002_adsc.202200847_sup_1.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation: substrates 1a, 4a, 7a, 9a and the mediator Et4NOTs are oxidized at the anode; CV identifies oxidation onsets (1a: 1.14 V; 4a: 1.49 V; 7a: 1.22 V with shifts upon addition of Et4NOTs and H2SO4; 9a: 1.53 V) and cathodic features for 1a (-0.46, -0.90 V) and 4a (-2.06 V).
10.1002_adsc.202200847_sup_1.electron_flow: The anode performs single-electron oxidation of the styrenyl substrate (and/or the OTs mediator), generating a radical cation/oxidized mediator that propagates the reaction; CV peaks indicate accessible oxidation events at ~1.1–1.5 V vs SCE, with reductive features for some substrates at negative potentials.

key_intermediates.name: substrate-derived radical cation (from anodic SET on styrene/arene substrate)

probe_reagents.name: 2,4,6-collidine
probe_reagents.observation: CV of 1a + 2,4,6-collidine shows altered oxidation/reduction features (oxidation onset ~1.14 V; reductive peaks at -0.46 and -0.90 V), consistent with base-assisted oxidation of 1a.

probe_reagents.name: Et4NOTs
probe_reagents.observation: Addition to 7a shifts/enhances anodic features (1.07, 1.22, 1.39, 1.89 V), indicating mediator oxidation and interaction with substrate.

probe_reagents.name: H2SO4
probe_reagents.observation: Further addition to 7a + Et4NOTs modifies the CV response, suggesting protonation affects redox behavior.

10.1002_adsc.202200847_sup_1.notes: Only cyclic voltammetry data (Figure S1) is provided on this page; CVs were recorded in 0.1 M Bu4NPF6 in anhydrous DCE (for 1a) or MeCN (for 4a, 7a, 9a) with glassy carbon working, Pt counter, and 3 M KCl Ag/AgCl reference electrodes at 100 mV/s, with potentials converted to SCE by subtracting 0.04 V. No radical traps, KIE, DFT, or isolated intermediates are presented on the supplied page.

10.1002_adsc.202200470.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical synthesis of iodohydrins (and ethers) from alkenes using ammonium iodide as a non-toxic iodine source via an acetonitrile-stabilized iodonium intermediate.
10.1002_adsc.202200470.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of iodide (I-) to iodine radical and then to a CH3CN-stabilized I+ (iodonium-like) species; cathodic reduction of H+ to H2
10.1002_adsc.202200470.electron_flow: At the anode, I- is oxidized in two successive one-electron steps to give an acetonitrile-stabilized I+ species, which adds to the alkene to form an iodonium ion; Markovnikov nucleophilic attack by water (or alcohol) delivers the iodohydrin/ether, while 2H+ are reduced at the cathode to H2.

key_intermediates.name: iodine radical (from first anodic oxidation of I-)
open carbocation (from iodonium ring-opening, invoked for norbornene rearrangement)

probe_reagents.name: molecular iodine (I2) in place of NH4I
probe_reagents.observation: Gave the iodohydrin in moderate yield (36%), lower than with NH4I, indicating in-situ I2 cannot be ruled out but is less productive than CH3CN-stabilized I+

probe_reagents.name: norborn-2-ene (radical clock / cation probe)
probe_reagents.observation: Gave rearranged products 39 and 40 in 64:36 ratio, consistent with a cationic (iodonium/carbocation) intermediate rather than a radical pathway

probe_reagents.name: current interruption (45 min then 24 h without current)
probe_reagents.observation: No additional product formed, ruling out post-electrolysis chemistry from cathodically released metal salts

probe_reagents.name: NH4Br control
probe_reagents.observation: Formed bromohydrin; CV showed similar oxidation waves to NH4I

probe_reagents.name: NH4Cl control
probe_reagents.observation: No reaction; CV showed oxidation at higher potential, explaining non-reactivity

probe_reagents.name: styrene CV alone and with NH4I
probe_reagents.observation: No oxidation/reduction of styrene up to +1.2 V; adding NH4I gave no current increase, ruling out direct styrene oxidation / iodine-mediated styrene oxidation

10.1002_adsc.202200470.notes: Authors cannot fully exclude direct reaction of in-situ generated I2 with alkene, but argue CH3CN-stabilized I+ pathway is dominant based on lower yield with I2 and CV data. Stereochemistry of product 15 and norbornene rearrangement (39/40) support an iodonium/carbocation pathway rather than a radical mechanism.

10.1002_adsc.9600_sup_1.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical hydroxy-thiocarbamation of electron-deficient alkenes (α-arylacrylates) with KSCN and H2O to give β-hydroxy primary S-thiocarbamates
10.1002_adsc.9600_sup_1.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of SCN- to thiocyanate radical (SCN•) which adds to the alkene; cathode reduces H2O/protons to evolve H2 (confirmed by Pd/C-catalyzed hydrogenation of N-phenylmaleimide using the evolved H2)
10.1002_adsc.9600_sup_1.electron_flow: SCN- is oxidized at the anode to SCN•, which adds to the β-carbon of the α-arylacrylate to give a stabilized benzylic radical; further anodic single-electron oxidation generates a benzylic cation that is trapped by H2O to install the OH, while the cathode reduces protons to H2. The pendant SCN is then hydrolyzed to the primary S-thiocarbamate.

key_intermediates.name: benzylic C-radical from SCN• addition to α-phenylacrylate
benzylic cation after further anodic oxidation
β-hydroxy primary S-thiocarbamate product 4a (after SCN hydrolysis)

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: Only trace 4a formed; BHT-SCN adduct (HRMS m/z 300.1400, calc 300.1393) and BHT-trapped benzylic radical adduct (HRMS m/z 462.2076, calc 462.2073) detected, supporting SCN• and a C-centered radical intermediate

probe_reagents.name: D2O
probe_reagents.observation: D-incorporation into OH and NH2 of 4a (D-4a, HRMS 281.0643 vs calc 281.0646), confirming H2O is the proton/H source for OH and NH

probe_reagents.name: H2-18O
probe_reagents.observation: 18O incorporated into both the OH and the C=O of the thiocarbamate of 4a (18O-4a, HRMS 282.0549 vs calc 282.0542), confirming H2O is the oxygen source for hydroxyl and for carbamate carbonyl (SCN hydrolysis)

probe_reagents.name: α-phenylacrylate 1a
probe_reagents.observation: CV onset oxidation +1.693 V vs SCE

probe_reagents.name: KSCN
probe_reagents.observation: CV onset oxidation +0.383 V vs SCE — much easier to oxidize than 1a, indicating SCN- is the species oxidized at the anode

probe_reagents.name: N-phenylmaleimide (H2 probe)
probe_reagents.observation: Headspace H2 from electrolysis reduces N-phenylmaleimide to N-phenylsuccinimide 6a in 59% yield with Pd/C, confirming cathodic H2 evolution

10.1002_adsc.9600_sup_1.notes: Authors depict two BHT adducts (I: BHT-SCN from direct trapping of SCN•; II: BHT-trapped benzylic radical after SCN• addition to alkene), supporting a stepwise radical addition / radical-polar crossover pathway. The primary S-thiocarbamate (-SC(=O)NH2) arises from hydrolysis of the intermediate thiocyanate (-SCN) by H2O, as evidenced by 18O incorporation into the carbamate carbonyl.

10.1002_adsc.201801173.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical 1,2-dimethoxylation of olefins via anodic oxidation through a cation-initiated (ionic-like) pathway, with divergence to semipinacol-rearranged acetals for less-stabilized carbocations
10.1002_adsc.201801173.electrochemical_role: Anodic single-electron oxidation of the alkene to a radical cation; a second anodic oxidation of the β-methoxy carbon radical generates the key carbocation, while H2 is evolved at the cathode from MeOH reduction
10.1002_adsc.201801173.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes the alkene (1) by SET to a radical cation i1, which is attacked by MeOH to give a β-methoxy C-radical i2; a second anodic SET gives carbocation i3, which is trapped by a second MeOH (path I, stabilized cations) or undergoes 1,2-aryl/alkyl migration to i4 before MeOH trapping (path II); protons reduced at cathode to H2.
10.1002_adsc.201801173.rate_determining_step: Not the nucleophilic addition of MeOH (excluded by KIE studies); most likely the initial anodic oxidation of the alkene

key_intermediates.name: β-methoxy carbon-centered radical (i2) from MeOH addition to radical cation
β-methoxy carbocation (i3) after second anodic oxidation

probe_reagents.name: radical-clock substrate 1aa (cyclopropyl-substituted alkene)
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction proceeded smoothly to 2aa without any ring-opening/ring-expansion products, arguing against a long-lived C-radical and supporting an ionic-like pathway

probe_reagents.name: deuterated MeOH (KIE experiment)
probe_reagents.observation: KIE indicated that nucleophilic addition of MeOH is not the rate-determining step

probe_reagents.name: MeOH / 1a / 1al cyclic voltammetry
probe_reagents.observation: No oxidation peak for MeOH in the operating window; clear oxidation peaks for olefins 1a and 1al, showing alkene is oxidized preferentially and excluding methoxyl radical generation

10.1002_adsc.201801173.notes: Two divergent pathways from the same cationic intermediate i3: path I direct MeOH trapping for stabilized (tri/diaryl) cations giving 1,2-dimethoxylation; path II semipinacol 1,2-migration for less stable (1,2-disubstituted) cations giving acetal products (Scheme 3). Authors explicitly exclude a methoxyl-radical pathway based on CV. A radical-cation intermediate is retained at the start, so a brief radical-polar crossover step is involved, but overall the productive C–O bond formation is described as ionic/cation-initiated.

10.1002_adsc.200800064.rxn_class_summary: Enantioselective organocatalytic epoxidation of alkenes using iminium salt catalysts with electrochemically generated persulfate or percarbonate as terminal oxidant
10.1002_adsc.200800064.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation at boron-doped diamond electrode generates persulfate (from sulfate) or percarbonate (from carbonate) in situ; the peroxy species then acts as terminal oxidant on the iminium catalyst rather than electrons being transferred to the substrate directly
10.1002_adsc.200800064.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes carbonate or sulfate in aqueous solution to percarbonate or persulfate; the peroxy anion adds to the chiral iminium catalyst to give a pair of diastereomeric oxaziridinium species, which then transfer oxygen to the alkene to deliver the epoxide and regenerate the iminium.

key_intermediates.name: peroxy adduct of iminium / oxaziridinium precursor (XOO-iminium)

probe_reagents.name: hydrogen peroxide control (no carbonate/sulfate)
probe_reagents.observation: H2O2 alone does not induce epoxidation with iminium catalysts, indicating the active oxidant is the peroxy anion (percarbonate/persulfate), not H2O2 generated by equilibrium

probe_reagents.name: hydrodynamic voltammetry at Pt microelectrode
probe_reagents.observation: anodic limiting current is mass-transport controlled and proportional to peroxide concentration, used to monitor in situ peroxide generation

probe_reagents.name: commercial sodium percarbonate comparison
probe_reagents.observation: electrochemically generated persulfate gives ee comparable to commercial persulfate; electrochemically generated percarbonate gives good conversion but lower ee than persulfate, implying different diastereofacial selectivity in oxaziridinium formation

10.1002_adsc.200800064.notes: Authors propose two possible origins for the ee difference between percarbonate and persulfate: (i) different concentrations of free H2O2 in the equilibria (disfavored, since H2O2 alone is inactive), or (ii) different diastereofacial selectivity in the addition of the peroxy anion to the iminium, generating different ratios of diastereomeric oxaziridinium intermediates (favored explanation, Figure 2). Counter-ion role of the oxaziridinium is noted as unclear.

10.1002_adsc.202300949_sup_1.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical selenocyclization of N-allyl-N'-tosylureas with PhSeSePh to form 2-amino-oxazoline products bearing a CH2SePh group
10.1002_adsc.202300949_sup_1.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of PhSeSePh generates electrophilic PhSe+ (or PhSe radical/PhSe+ equivalent) that adds to the allyl alkene, triggering intramolecular cyclization by the urea oxygen
10.1002_adsc.202300949_sup_1.electron_flow: PhSeSePh is oxidized at the carbon anode to a PhSe+ electrophile (with reduction of protons at the Pt cathode); PhSe+ activates the allyl C=C as a seleniranium ion, which is opened by intramolecular attack of the urea carbonyl oxygen to give the 2-iminooxazoline 3a.

key_intermediates.name: PhSe electrophile (from anodic oxidation of diphenyl diselenide)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction proceeded under standard conditions with TEMPO (1 equiv.) giving 3a in 80% yield, indicating no free C-radical intermediate

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: 3a obtained in 79% (1 equiv. BHT) and 81% (0.5 equiv. BHT), again consistent with a non-radical (ionic) pathway

10.1002_adsc.202300949_sup_1.notes: Radical-trap experiments (TEMPO, BHT) do not inhibit the reaction, arguing against a free-radical chain and favoring an ionic seleniranium/cation-initiated cyclization following anodic generation of PhSe+. Optimal conditions: C(+)|Pt(-) undivided cell, n-Bu4NBF4, MeCN/H2O (7:1 mL), 10 mA, r.t., 2 h.

10.1002_adsc.202201155.rxn_class_summary: Cobalt-catalyzed electrochemical C-H/N-H annulation of 8-aminoquinoline benzamides with alkenes to give dihydroisoquinolinone derivatives
10.1002_adsc.202201155.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation regenerates the active Co(III) catalyst from Co(I)/Co(II); cathode reduces protons to H2. The electrode shuttles electrons via the cobalt catalyst rather than directly oxidizing substrate.
10.1002_adsc.202201155.electron_flow: Co(II) binds the benzamide N-H, is anodically oxidized to Co(III), then performs BIES C-H metalation; alkene coordinates and migratory insertion gives a 7-membered cobaltacycle that reductively eliminates product and Co(I), which is reoxidized at the anode while protons are reduced at the cathode to H2.
10.1002_adsc.202201155.rate_determining_step: C-H activation step (BIES-type concerted metalation-deprotonation), supported by KIE = 1.2 and highly unsymmetrical transition state

key_intermediates.name: Co(I) species released after reductive elimination

probe_reagents.name: CD3OD (H/D exchange)
probe_reagents.observation: <5% D incorporation in recovered 1a and in product 3aa, indicating C-H metalation is likely irreversible

probe_reagents.name: [D5]-1a deuterated benzamide
probe_reagents.observation: kH/kD = 1.2, suggesting C-H cleavage is involved but with a highly unsymmetrical TS

probe_reagents.name: Competition between 1b (Me) and 1h (CF3) benzamides
probe_reagents.observation: 3ba:3ha = 1.7:1, electron-rich amide reacts faster, consistent with BIES pathway

probe_reagents.name: Three-component coupling with benzoyl chloride 9 + 8-aminoquinoline 10 + styrene 2a
probe_reagents.observation: Only 15% of 3aa obtained, indicating covalently pre-installed quinoline directing group is essential

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of 1a, Co(acac)2, NaOPiv mixtures
probe_reagents.observation: Oxidation peaks at 1.11 V and 1.61 V appear only with 1a+Co+NaOPiv, supporting formation/oxidation of a Co(III)-amide intermediate rather than direct substrate oxidation (1a alone at 1.66 V; 2a at 1.94 V)

10.1002_adsc.202201155.notes: Authors derivatize product 3aa (Pd/C-H2 to 4; LiAlH4 to 5) to demonstrate synthetic utility. The covalently bound 8-aminoquinoline directing group is mechanistically essential — a stepwise three-component variant fails (15%). No quenching of CV signal by alkene 2a argues against alkene oxidation as a productive step.

10.1002_adsc.202401522.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical catalyst-free radical 1,2-alkylarylation of allylic alcohols with malonate-type C–H nucleophiles bearing two or three EWGs, via umpolung of the C–H nucleophile to an electrophilic carbon radical followed by neophyl-type aryl migration.
10.1002_adsc.202401522.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of the deprotonated malonate carbanion to a tertiary carbon radical; cathode reduces protons to H2.
10.1002_adsc.202401522.electron_flow: Na2CO3 deprotonates the triester 2a to carbanion 38, which is oxidized at the graphite anode (single electron) to tertiary C-radical 39; 39 adds to allylic alcohol 1a giving radical 40, which undergoes ipso-radical cyclization onto the aryl group to spirocyclohexadienyl radical 41, ring-opens via aryl 1,2-migration to 42, and a final anodic electron-proton transfer delivers ketone 3 while the Ni-foam cathode reduces 2H+ to H2.

key_intermediates.name: malonate-derived carbanion (38) from base deprotonation of 2a
tertiary electrophilic carbon radical (39) after anodic SET
benzylic/homoallylic C-radical adduct (40) from radical addition of 39 to allylic alcohol 1a
spirocyclohexadienyl radical (41) from intramolecular aryl ipso-cyclization (neophyl rearrangement)
rearranged tertiary carbon radical / α-hydroxy benzyl-type species (42) after aryl migration
ketone product 3 after electron-proton transfer (oxidation of 42)

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of 1a vs 2a
probe_reagents.observation: No oxidation peak for allylic alcohol 1a within the CV window; 2a shows clear oxidation around 1.1 V vs Ag/AgNO3, supporting preferential anodic oxidation of the malonate component.

probe_reagents.name: electron-poor vs electron-rich aryl migration selectivity (products 22–25)
probe_reagents.observation: Preferential migration of more electron-poor aryl groups is consistent with a radical (neophyl-type) rearrangement mechanism rather than a cationic one.

10.1002_adsc.202401522.notes: No TEMPO/BHT trapping, KIE, or DFT reported on these pages; mechanistic support rests on CV (selective oxidation of malonate) and the aryl-migration electronic trend characteristic of radical neophyl rearrangement. 2-Arylmalonates failed, suggesting steric/electronic limits on carbanion oxidation or radical addition. Cathodic H2 evolution makes this effectively a paired electrolysis.

10.1002_adsc.201901262.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical intramolecular oxy-chalcogenation of N-allylbenzamides and allyl oximes with diaryl dichalcogenides to form selenylated/thiolated oxazolines and isoxazolines via tandem C-O and C-chalcogen bond formation
10.1002_adsc.201901262.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of PhSeSePh (peak 1.66 V vs Ag/AgCl) to PhSe+ cation and PhSe radical; also direct anodic oxidation of the amide substrate (1.32 V) to a delocalized N/O-centered radical; cathodic reduction generates H2 and reduces PhSeSePh (-1.44 V) to PhSe radical + PhSe anion (oxidized back to PhSe radical)
10.1002_adsc.201901262.electron_flow: At the anode, PhSeSePh is oxidized to PhSe+ (cation) and PhSe• (radical), while the amide/oxime substrate can also be directly oxidized to a heteroatom-centered radical; PhSe+ adds to the allyl alkene to give a cation cyclized by the amide/oxime oxygen (ionic pathway I), or the substrate-derived radical cyclizes and couples with PhSe• (radical pathway II); the cathode reduces protons to H2 and PhSeSePh to PhSe•/PhSe-.

key_intermediates.name: seleniranium/β-selenyl carbocation C from PhSe+ addition to allyl alkene
delocalized amidyl/oxy radical E from oxidation of N-allylbenzamide 1a
TEMPO adduct 13 from trapping of allyl/carbon radical of oxime 6a

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO (with amide 1a)
probe_reagents.observation: Product 3aa formation diminished to 21% yield, indicating partial radical pathway

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO (with oxime 6a)
probe_reagents.observation: Selenylation shut down (only trace of 7aa); TEMPO-carbon adduct 13 isolated in 78% yield, supporting radical intermediate for oxime

probe_reagents.name: Controlled potential electrolysis at 1.00, 1.55, 1.77 V (0.5 F/mol)
probe_reagents.observation: No product at 1.00 V; 27% 3aa at 1.55 V (only reduction of 2a + oxidation of 1a feasible → radical pathway active); 39% at 1.77 V exceeds the 33% maximum for pathway II alone, indicating ionic pathway I also operates

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry
probe_reagents.observation: PhSeSePh oxidation peak at 1.66 V and reduction peak at -1.44 V vs Ag/AgCl; amide 1a oxidation at 1.32 V; oxime 6a oxidation at 1.13 V (lower, consistent with dominant radical pathway for isoxazolines)

10.1002_adsc.201901262.notes: Two parallel mechanisms proposed: Pathway I (ionic, PhSe+ addition then O-cyclization, 1.0 F/mol) and Pathway II (radical, substrate oxidation/cyclization then PhSe• coupling, 1.5 F/mol). For oxazolines from amides both pathways operate; for isoxazolines from oximes the radical pathway dominates as evidenced by the high yield of TEMPO adduct 13 and the lower oxidation potential of the oxime. Only sub-stoichiometric diselenide is needed because PhSe is recycled between anode and cathode.

10.1002_adsc.202300205.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical oxidative difluoroethylation/cyclization of alkenyl carboxylic acids, alcohols, and amides with sodium difluoroethylsulfinate (NaSO2CF2Me) to give difluoroethylated lactones, dihydroisobenzofurans, and iminoisobenzofurans.
10.1002_adsc.202300205.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of the difluoroethylsulfinate anion (MeCF2SO2-) generates MeCF2 radical via loss of SO2; subsequent radical adduct is further oxidized at the anode to a carbocation that is trapped by the intramolecular nucleophile; cathode reduces protons to H2.
10.1002_adsc.202300205.electron_flow: Anode first oxidizes MeCF2SO2- (lower oxidation potential than substrate, confirmed by CV) to release SO2 and generate MeCF2 radical, which adds to the alkene to give benzylic radical A; A is oxidized again at the anode to carbocation B, which undergoes intramolecular nucleophilic cyclization by the pendant CO2H/OH/CONHPh group, while the cathode reduces protons to H2.

key_intermediates.name: MeCF2 radical (1,1-difluoroethyl radical) from anodic oxidation of MeCF2SO2- and loss of SO2
Benzylic C-radical intermediate A from MeCF2 addition to alkene
Benzylic carbocation intermediate B from anodic oxidation of A

probe_reagents.name: BHT (2,6-di-tert-butyl-4-methylphenol)
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction inhibited, supporting a free radical pathway.

probe_reagents.name: 1,1-Diphenylethylene (DPE)
probe_reagents.observation: Yield of 3a decreased to 13%; radical trapping adduct 12a isolated, confirming MeCF2 radical.

probe_reagents.name: Difluoroethyl sulfone (alternative MeCF2 source)
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction proceeded poorly; sulfone reduction not compatible with the electrochemically driven CF2Me radical cyclization.

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of 1a, 2, and 1a+2
probe_reagents.observation: NaSO2CF2Me (2) oxidized at ~0.70 V vs. 1.3 V for substrate 1a, indicating sulfinate is oxidized first; current increase in 1:1 mixture; controlled-potential electrolysis at 0.70 V sufficient to drive reaction.

10.1002_adsc.202300205.notes: Method also extends to cyclopropyldifluoromethylation using sodium cyclopropyldifluoromethylsulfinate (75–80% for benzoxazines). Sulfone-based MeCF2 precursor is ineffective, suggesting the sulfinate-specific anodic oxidation is essential. No KIE or DFT data reported.

10.1002_adsc.202400153_sup_1.rxn_class_summary: Photoelectrochemical thiocyanation/cyclization of N-alkenylacrylamides with KSCN to form SCN-functionalized pyrrolinones (3a) using Mes-Acr+ photocatalyst under 455 nm light and constant current electrolysis in Bu4NOAc/HFIP.
10.1002_adsc.202400153_sup_1.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation regenerates the excited-state-reduced photocatalyst (or oxidizes SCN-/acetate); light excites Mes-Acr+ which oxidizes thiocyanate to a thiocyanyl radical that adds to the acrylamide alkene, triggering radical cyclization.
10.1002_adsc.202400153_sup_1.electron_flow: Photoexcited Mes-Acr+* oxidizes SCN- to •SCN, which adds to the acrylamide alkene; the resulting carbon radical cyclizes onto the pendant enamide, and the cyclized radical is oxidized (anodically and/or by photocatalyst) to a cation that is deprotonated to give the pyrrolinone, while the anode reoxidizes the reduced acridine radical to close the catalytic cycle.

key_intermediates.name: carbon-centered radical after SCN addition to acrylamide alkene

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Product 3a fully suppressed (0% under both conditions A and B); TEMPO-SCN adduct detected by HRMS, supporting •SCN intermediate.

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: Product 3a fully suppressed (0% under both conditions A and B), consistent with radical pathway.

probe_reagents.name: light/current on-off (control without photocatalyst)
probe_reagents.observation: Yield grows during 'on' periods and plateaus during 'off' periods for both light and current toggling, indicating both photo- and electro-input are continuously required.

probe_reagents.name: no-light / no-electricity controls
probe_reagents.observation: Without light: no product. Without electricity: only ~8% yield vs ~70% under full conditions, showing electrochemistry is essential.

10.1002_adsc.202400153_sup_1.notes: Two condition sets (A: with Mes-Acr+ photocatalyst; B: PC-free photoelectrosynthesis) are studied; on/off experiments in Figure S10 are for the PC-free variant (conditions B), showing that simultaneous photo- and electro-input are needed. Reaction profiles (Figure S9) confirm both light and current are required for high yield under conditions A.

10.1002_adsc.202100161.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical alkoxy- and hydroxy-bromination of styrenes to bromohydrins and bromohydrin ethers using KBr as bromine source in DMSO/H2O or MeCN/ROH with AcOH additive.
10.1002_adsc.202100161.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of bromide to Br2 / [Br]+ which acts as the electrophilic mediator that activates the styrene; no direct SET to the alkene (CV shows styrene is not oxidized under these conditions).
10.1002_adsc.202100161.electron_flow: Bromide is oxidized at the anode to Br2 / [Br]+; this electrophile (optionally relayed through a DMSO-Br adduct C) adds to the styrene to give bromonium A or benzylic sulfonium B, which is opened by H2O (or ROH) to give the bromohydrin (or its ether); protons are reduced to H2 at the cathode.

key_intermediates.name: molecular bromine / [Br]+ electrophile generated at anode
cyclic bromonium ion A from styrene + [Br]+
alkoxysulfonium / sulfonium salt B (DMSO-trapped benzylic cation)
DMSO-Br complex C ([Br]+ bound to DMSO)
vicinal dibromide 4 (from Br- attack on bromonium A)

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of KBr, styrene 1a, and their mixture
probe_reagents.observation: Only Br- is oxidized; styrene 1a shows no oxidation wave; addition of 1a to KBr decreases the reverse reduction peak, consistent with chemical consumption of electrogenerated Br2/[Br]+ by the alkene.

probe_reagents.name: control with isolated epoxide 3 under reaction conditions
probe_reagents.observation: Epoxide 3 does not convert to bromohydrin 2a, ruling out epoxide as on-cycle intermediate.

probe_reagents.name: AcOH vs TFA vs KOH additive screen
probe_reagents.observation: 5 eq AcOH gives 86% 2a with no epoxide; adding KOH or removing AcOH increases epoxide 3 (up to 48%) and suppresses 2a, showing acid controls hydroxide concentration and prevents epoxide formation.

probe_reagents.name: TFA substitution for AcOH
probe_reagents.observation: Lower 76% yield of bromohydrin ether 6a vs 87% with AcOH.

probe_reagents.name: high current density operation (j=53.3 mA/cm^2) with visible Br2 evolution
probe_reagents.observation: Bulk Br2 observed dripping from electrodes, supporting two-electron Br-/Br2 mediator pathway over a Br-radical chain.

10.1002_adsc.202100161.notes: A radical pathway (anodic Br- -> Br•, addition to styrene, anodic oxidation of benzyl radical to cation, then nucleophile capture) is explicitly considered but disfavored based on CV and observation of free Br2 at high current density. Two electrophile delivery modes are proposed in parallel: direct attack of [Br]+/Br2 on styrene to give bromonium A, and DMSO-mediated delivery via complex C giving sulfonium B; both converge on nucleophilic opening by H2O/ROH. Side products include vicinal dibromide 4 (Br- opening of A), epoxide 3 (high OH-), and diol 5 (hydrolysis of 3).

10.1002_adsc.201801492.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical three-component 1,2-aminosulfonylation of alkenes with sulfinates and amines to form 2-sulfonylethan-1-amines
10.1002_adsc.201801492.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of sulfinate (PhSO2Na) to sulfonyl radical; subsequent anodic oxidation of the benzylic C-radical adduct to a benzylic cation; cathode reduces protons to H2
10.1002_adsc.201801492.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes sulfinate to a sulfonyl radical which adds to the alkene giving a benzylic C-radical; this radical is further oxidized at the anode to a benzylic cation that is trapped by the amine nucleophile, while the cathode reduces protons to H2.

key_intermediates.name: benzylic C-radical adduct B (after sulfonyl radical addition to alkene)
benzylic cation C (from anodic oxidation of B)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Only trace 4aaa; >95% of 1a and 3a recovered — reaction suppressed, supporting radical pathway

probe_reagents.name: BHT (2,6-di-tert-butyl-4-methylphenol)
probe_reagents.observation: Yield of 4aaa dropped to 21%, consistent with radical intermediates

10.1002_adsc.201801492.notes: Byproducts 5aa (vinyl sulfone via deprotonation of cation C) and 6aa (PhNHSO2Ph, from sulfonyl radical + amine) arise from high reactivity of intermediates A and B. Use of NH3 or NH3·H2O gave no product (4aan trace). Mechanism supported by CV (Figure S1, SI).

10.1002_adsc.202001192.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical tandem cyclization of N-arylacrylamides with diaryl diselenides to form 3-(arylselanyl)oxindoles via selenyl radical addition/cyclization.
10.1002_adsc.202001192.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of PhSeSePh generates PhSe• radical (A) and PhSe+ cation (B); cathode reduces H+ to H2.
10.1002_adsc.202001192.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes PhSeSePh to give PhSe• (path I) and PhSe+ (path II); PhSe• adds to the acrylamide alkene to form a C-radical that cyclizes onto the arene; further anodic oxidation and deprotonation yield the oxindole. In parallel, PhSe+ adds to the alkene to give a cation that cyclizes and loses H+ to the same product. Cathode reduces protons to H2.

key_intermediates.name: tertiary C-radical from PhSe• addition to acrylamide (C)

10.1002_adsc.202001192.notes: Two parallel pathways proposed: favored radical path (path I) via PhSe• and ionic path (path II) via PhSe+ — authors state the ionic path cannot be ruled out. Electrooxidation nature was confirmed by prior control experiments referenced on this page but probe reagent details (e.g., TEMPO trapping) are not shown on the provided page.

10.1002_adsc.70132_si.rxn_class_summary: electrochemical three-component thiomethoxylation of vinyl ethers with diaryl disulfides and methanol via thiyl-radical addition/radical-polar crossover
10.1002_adsc.70132_si.electrochemical_role: anodic oxidation of PhSSPh (or thiolate) to generate PhS• thiyl radical that adds to the vinyl ether
10.1002_adsc.70132_si.electron_flow: Anodic oxidation generates a PhS• thiyl radical from PhSSPh; PhS• adds to the vinyl ether to give an α-alkoxy carbon radical, which is further oxidized (anodically) to an oxocarbenium ion that is trapped by MeOH to furnish the α-methoxy thioether product.

key_intermediates.name: alpha-alkoxy carbon radical from PhS• addition to ethyl vinyl ether

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Target product formed only in trace amounts; TEMPO-SPh adduct (C15H24NOS+, m/z 266.1571, calcd 266.1573) detected by HRMS, confirming a thiyl-radical intermediate.

10.1002_adsc.70132_si.notes: Only the radical-trapping control experiment is shown on this page; trapping captures the PhS• rather than the carbon radical, so the downstream cation step (radical-polar crossover with MeOH) is inferred from product structure rather than directly probed here.

10.1002_adsc.202401225.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical dual trifluoromethylation/cyclization of 2-aryl-N-acryloyl indoles with CF3SO2Na to afford bis-trifluoromethylated indolo[2,1-a]isoquinolinones.
10.1002_adsc.202401225.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of CF3SO2Na to a trifluoromethanesulfonyl radical that loses SO2 to give CF3 radical; later anodic oxidations of cyclized C-radicals generate cationic intermediates that lose H+ to rearomatize/form product.
10.1002_adsc.202401225.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes CF3SO2− to CF3SO2• which extrudes SO2 to give CF3•; CF3• adds to the acryloyl alkene of 1a, the resulting C-radical cyclizes onto the indole C3 to give radical B, which is anodically oxidized to cation C and deprotonated to neutral mono-CF3 intermediate 5; a second CF3• adds to the styrene-like alkene of 5 to give benzylic radical D, anodic oxidation to cation E followed by deprotonation furnishes 3a.
10.1002_adsc.202401225.rate_determining_step: Intramolecular radical cyclization of A onto the indole (TS2, ΔG‡ = 19.1 kcal/mol).

key_intermediates.name: carbon radical A (CF3 adduct on acryloyl C=C)
cyclized C3-indolyl radical B (after intramolecular radical addition onto indole C3)
benzylic radical D after second CF3• addition

probe_reagents.name: 1,1-diphenylethylene (DPE)
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction completely suppressed; DPE-CF3SO2 adduct 4 detected by ESI-MS, implicating CF3SO2• as a radical intermediate.

probe_reagents.name: no-current control
probe_reagents.observation: Without electricity essentially no reaction occurred; electricity is indispensable.

probe_reagents.name: reaction quench at 2 h
probe_reagents.observation: Mono-CF3 intermediate 5 detected by ESI-MS, supporting stepwise dual trifluoromethylation.

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of 2a vs 1a
probe_reagents.observation: CF3SO2Na oxidation onset at 1.80 V vs Ag/AgCl, lower than 1a (1.97 V), indicating preferential anodic oxidation of CF3SO2− to generate CF3•.

10.1002_adsc.202401225.notes: Mechanism supported by DFT (wB97XD/def2TZVPP, CPCM-MeCN) including TS1 (CF3• addition, 10.5 kcal/mol), TS2 (radical cyclization, 19.1 kcal/mol, rate-determining), TS3 (second CF3• addition, 9.1 kcal/mol), and Hirshfeld spin-density analysis tracking spin-center migration from CF3 carbon to β-carbon during each addition/cyclization step. Single-crystal X-ray of 3a confirms regiochemistry. Substrate with CHF2SO2Na failed, suggesting sensitivity to radical stability of the perfluoroalkyl source.

10.1002_adsc.202400153.rxn_class_summary: Electrophotocatalytic carbothiocyanation of N-alkenylacrylamides with KSCN to give thiocyanato-4-pyrrolin-2-ones, either acridinium-catalyzed (conditions A) or self-photocatalyzed by the diene substrate/product (conditions B).
10.1002_adsc.202400153.electrochemical_role: Anodic single-electron oxidation of KSCN (or of the reduced photocatalyst) to regenerate the oxidized PC; the excited PC* (acridinium under A, or the substrate/product 1a/3a acting as self-PC under B) oxidizes SCN- to SCN radical, and the anode reoxidizes the reduced PC•- closing the cycle, with concomitant cathodic H+ reduction to H2.
10.1002_adsc.202400153.electron_flow: Photoexcited PC (acridinium under A, or 3-aza-1,5-diene 1a/pyrrolinone 3a as self-PC under B) oxidizes SCN- to SCN•, generating PC•-; SCN• adds regioselectively to the acrylamide alkene to give radical I, which undergoes intramolecular radical cyclization onto the enamine to give radical II; II is oxidized (SET to PC* or anode) to cation III, which deprotonates to product 3a, and PC•- is reoxidized at the anode while protons are reduced to H2 at the cathode.

key_intermediates.name: C-centered radical adduct I from SCN addition to acryloyl alkene
cyclized C-centered radical II (ring-closed via enaminic double bond)
cationic intermediate III after oxidative SET

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction shut down under both conditions A and B; TEMPO-SCN adduct detected by ESI-HRMS, supporting SCN• intermediacy.

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction completely suppressed.

probe_reagents.name: 1,1-diphenylethylene (DPE)
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction shut down; with NaSMs instead of KSCN, DPE-Ms adduct 6a was isolated (58% A, 62% B), supporting free radical S-centered intermediate.

probe_reagents.name: CuCl2
probe_reagents.observation: Electron scavenger; completely suppressed product formation, consistent with SET pathway.

probe_reagents.name: p-dinitrobenzene (pDNB)
probe_reagents.observation: SET scavenger; significant yield decrease (to 30% A, 41% B).

probe_reagents.name: CCl4
probe_reagents.observation: Electron scavenger; yield decreased (43% A, 24% B).

probe_reagents.name: thiocyanogen (SCN)2 / NCSSCN
probe_reagents.observation: Reacted with 1a to give 3a in only 44% (A/B), 9% (Mes-Acr+/blue LED), 8% (365 nm UVA); less reactive than KSCN/anodic conditions, suggesting NCSSCN is not the key productive intermediate.

10.1002_adsc.202400153.notes: Two operative regimes are proposed: (A) Mes-Acr+ acts as photocatalyst at 455 nm with electrolysis; (B) PC-free, where 3-aza-1,5-diene 1a and product 3a self-photocatalyze under 365 nm UVA. Stern-Volmer shows KSCN quenches Mes-Acr+* (not 1a), while 1a* and 3a* are quenched by KSCN. Computed triplet energies (1a 50.6, 3a 54.3, NCSSCN 28.9 kcal/mol) suggest EnT to NCSSCN is exergonic, but chalcone dimerization control rules out EnT photocatalysis by 1a/3a, so 1a/3a operate as SET photocatalysts. Low quantum yields (Φ=0.009 A, 0.037 B) and on/off experiments argue against long radical chains. Alternative NCSSCN-mediated pathway considered but disfavored. H2 evolution at cathode confirmed.

10.1002_adsc.202401108.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical 1,2-iodotrifluoroethoxylation (β-iodohydrofluoroether synthesis) of alkenes using 2-iodo-1,3-dimethoxy-5-methylbenzene as iodine source and TFE as nucleophile
10.1002_adsc.202401108.electrochemical_role: Anodic SET oxidation of the electron-rich iodoarene (1a) to a radical cation (I), which after a second oxidation/H+ loss releases I+ equivalent; cathode performs hydrogen evolution (HER) to balance charge
10.1002_adsc.202401108.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes electron-rich iodoarene 1a by SET to a radical cation, which loses H+/further electron to generate an aryl-I+ species; this iodonium transfers I+ to the alkene forming an iodiranium ion that is opened regioselectively by trifluoroethanol (Markovnikov), while the cathode reduces protons to H2.

key_intermediates.name: Biaryl/passivated dimer (II) from coupling of two radical cations of 1a
Iodonium ion (I+ equivalent) released from oxidized iodoarene
Open β-iodo carbocation (for substrates showing rearrangement/Markovnikov selectivity)

probe_reagents.name: BHT (2,6-di-tert-butyl-4-methylphenol)
probe_reagents.observation: Yield of 3a dropped from baseline to 19%; 73% of alkene 2a recovered, indicating radical intermediates involved

probe_reagents.name: I2 (molecular iodine as alternative I source)
probe_reagents.observation: Only 17% of 3a obtained, showing I2 is a poor surrogate

probe_reagents.name: Bu4NI (tetrabutylammonium iodide)
probe_reagents.observation: No product formed, ruling out direct oxidation of iodide

probe_reagents.name: NIS (N-iodosuccinimide) as I+ source
probe_reagents.observation: 58% yield of 3a but regioselectivity dropped to 2:1, consistent with iodonium pathway but different selectivity

probe_reagents.name: PhI(OAc)2 / PhI(OCOCF3)2 with BF3·OEt2 (chemical oxidation of 1a)
probe_reagents.observation: Formed diiodinated arenes 1c/1d (20% and 60%), supporting SET oxidation of 1a to radical cation

probe_reagents.name: Control without electricity
probe_reagents.observation: <1% product, confirming necessity of current

10.1002_adsc.202401108.notes: Regioselectivity follows Markovnikov, consistent with electrophilic addition via iodonium; products from norbornene (3o, 3o', 3o'') and rearranged products (3f, 3n') suggest open carbocation character competing with bridged iodonium. CV of 1a shows two oxidation waves; no redox features for alkene 2a, supporting iodoarene (not alkene) as the species oxidized at the anode. Passivation of the GC anode by biaryl dimer II is invoked to explain solution color change. Radical-cation iodoarene mediator mechanism is proposed in line with Kita/Waldvogel/Powers precedents.

10.1002_adsc.202200421.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical oxidation-induced oxyphosphorylation of alkenes and alkynes with water to give β-ketophosphonates via acetate-mediated HAT generation of P-radicals
10.1002_adsc.202200421.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of acetate (nBu4NOAc) generates an acetoxyl radical mediator that abstracts H from P(O)–H; carbon radical intermediate is further anodically oxidized to a carbocation
10.1002_adsc.202200421.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes acetate to AcO•, which abstracts H from Ar2P(O)H to give a P-centered radical; this P• adds to the alkene to form a β-phosphoryl C-radical, which is further oxidized at the anode to a carbocation, trapped by water, and oxidized to the ketone (β-ketophosphonate), with H2 evolved at the cathode.
10.1002_adsc.202200421.rate_determining_step: Not P–H cleavage (KIE = 1.26); rate-limiting step likely occurs after HAT (e.g., radical addition or subsequent oxidation)

key_intermediates.name: acetoxyl radical (AcO•) from anodic oxidation of acetate
phosphorus-centered radical (P•) from HAT on diarylphosphine oxide
β-phosphoryl carbon radical (C) from P• addition to alkene
β-phosphoryl carbocation (D) from anodic oxidation of carbon radical
β-hydroxyphosphonate (E) from water attack on carbocation

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction suppressed; product not observed, indicating radical intermediates

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: BHT-P adduct 1e isolated in 61% yield, confirming P-radical intermediate

probe_reagents.name: 1,1-diphenylethylene (DPE)
probe_reagents.observation: Product 2e detected by HRMS; reaction suppressed, supporting radical pathway

probe_reagents.name: deuterated P(O)–H substrate 1b-d
probe_reagents.observation: KH/KD = 1.26 (parallel experiments); P–H cleavage not rate-limiting

probe_reagents.name: H2 18O
probe_reagents.observation: 18O incorporated into β-ketophosphonate (2c'), confirming water as oxygen source (86% yield)

probe_reagents.name: isolated β-hydroxyphosphonate (D/E intermediate)
probe_reagents.observation: Subjected to standard conditions gave 2c in 75%, supporting path a (hydroxyl intermediate oxidized to ketone)

probe_reagents.name: β-acetoxyphosphonate (path b intermediate)
probe_reagents.observation: Under standard conditions + DTPB, no 2c formed; rules out path b via acetate adduct

probe_reagents.name: nBu4NOAc / 1b cyclic voltammetry
probe_reagents.observation: 1b not directly oxidized at anode; nBu4NOAc oxidizes more easily (Ep=2.35 V); new peaks upon mixing indicate electron transfer between them

10.1002_adsc.202200421.notes: Two pathways considered after carbocation D: (path a, favored) water nucleophilic attack to give β-hydroxyphosphonate E, then anodic oxidation to β-ketophosphonate; (path b, ruled out) acetate trapping of D to give β-acetoxyphosphonate F. Path b excluded by independent synthesis showing the acetoxy intermediate does not convert to product. 18O labeling confirms water (not acetate/O2) as the oxygen source.

10.1002_adsc.70132.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical oxidant- and metal-free alkoxylation-thiolation of vinyl ethers using diaryl disulfides as arylthio sources and alcohols as alkoxy sources to give alpha-thionyl acetals
10.1002_adsc.70132.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of diaryl disulfide (PhSSPh) to arylthio radical and arylthio cation; subsequent anodic oxidation of beta-thio carbon radical to carbocation; cathode performs proton reduction to H2 (and can reduce PhS+ back to PhS radical that dimerizes to PhSSPh)
10.1002_adsc.70132.electron_flow: PhSSPh is oxidized at the anode to give PhS• (A) and PhS+ (A'); PhS• adds to the vinyl ether 1a to form a C-centered radical B, which is further oxidized at the anode to oxocarbenium-type cation C; methanol attacks C and deprotonation (assisted by 4-CF3-pyridine) delivers product 4a, while protons are reduced to H2 at the cathode.

key_intermediates.name: beta-(arylthio) alpha-alkoxy carbon radical (PhS-CH2-C•(OEt)) — intermediate B
alpha-alkoxy carbocation bearing beta-SPh (PhS-CH2-C+(OEt)) — intermediate C

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction inhibited with only trace 4a; TEMPO-SPh adduct detected by HRMS (m/z 266.1571), confirming arylthio radical intermediate

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of 1a, 2a, 4-CF3Py
probe_reagents.observation: Neither 1a nor 4-CF3Py show oxidation peaks in the window; 2a oxidizes at 1.41 V, lowered to 1.32 V upon addition of 4-CF3Py, indicating disulfide 2a is the species oxidized at the anode and 4-CF3Py acts only as a base

10.1002_adsc.70132.notes: 4-(Trifluoromethyl)pyridine functions as a mild, electrochemically stable base that assists deprotonation rather than as a redox mediator. An alternative fate proposed for cation A' is reaction with vinyl ether 1a directly to form C, or cathodic reduction/dimerization back to PhSSPh, constituting a partially paired electrolysis cycle.

10.1002_celc.201901502.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical iodocyclisation of 2-allylphenols to 2-(iodomethyl)dihydrobenzofurans via in situ anodic generation of I2 and Zn(II) Lewis acid from a zinc-coated graphite electrode.
10.1002_celc.201901502.electrochemical_role: Dual anodic oxidation: first oxidation of Zn(0) coating on graphite to Zn(II) (releases Lewis acid), then anodic oxidation of iodide (via triiodide) to I2 as the on-demand iodinating species.
10.1002_celc.201901502.electron_flow: At the anode, Zn(0) on the graphite electrode is first oxidised to Zn(II) (releasing a dry Lewis acid), then iodide is oxidised via I3- to I2; Zn(II) binds the deprotonated 2-allylphenol to give a bis(aryloxy)zinc complex which coordinates I2 and undergoes I–I/Zn–O metathesis to deliver an iodosyl-aryloxy species that intramolecularly iodocyclises the pendant alkene to the dihydrobenzofuran.
10.1002_celc.201901502.rate_determining_step: Formation/transfer of the transient iodosyl(aryloxy)zinc intermediate (ArO–I) that delivers I+ intramolecularly to the alkene; cyclisation of the unsubstituted (non-ring-iodinated) allylphenol is faster than that of the ring-iodinated congener.

key_intermediates.name: Zn(II) Lewis acid (released from Zn-coated graphite anode)
transient iodosyl(aryloxy)zinc / iodophenoxy intermediate (species 2) — ArO-I tethered to Zn
HI (formed in situ; drives reverse deiodination of aryl ring)

probe_reagents.name: allylbenzene (no phenol)
probe_reagents.observation: No reaction with I2/NaI or with I2/NaI/ZnCl2 — alkene alone is not iodocyclised; rules out free I+ acting on alkene.

probe_reagents.name: 2,4,6-trimethylphenol (external phenol, no tethered alkene)
probe_reagents.observation: With allylbenzene + I2/NaI/ZnCl2 + mesitol, ~10% of intermolecular iodoether 1 isolated, showing the phenol/Zn complex is required to activate iodine.

probe_reagents.name: K2CO3 (added base)
probe_reagents.observation: Addition of base did not accelerate cyclisation; instead gave predominantly arene-iodinated products, arguing against a free cycloiodonium pathway and implicating the Zn-phenoxide–I intermediate.

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of allylbenzene
probe_reagents.observation: No redox events within solvent window — alkene is not directly oxidised electrochemically.

probe_reagents.name: 4-iodophenol (model for ring-iodinated intermediate)
probe_reagents.observation: Under electrochemical conditions with MeSO3H (HI mimic), the C–I bond is labile (deiodination observed), supporting reversible aryl iodination.

probe_reagents.name: 4-iodoanisole (alkylated analogue)
probe_reagents.observation: No deiodination — C(aryl)–I in the absence of free phenol OH is not labile, showing the OH is required for the reverse deiodination equilibrium.

probe_reagents.name: MeSO3H (used to mimic HI)
probe_reagents.observation: Mimics in situ HI; together with the iodophenol experiments shows iodinated allylphenol can revert to allylphenol + I2, explaining lack of ring-iodinated by-products.

probe_reagents.name: charge–time monitoring during electrolysis
probe_reagents.observation: Two-stage charge profile: initial Zn(0)→Zn(II) oxidation, then 3 I- → I3- (-2e-), confirming the dual oxidative process of Scheme 1.

10.1002_celc.201901502.notes: Authors explicitly rule out two alternative mechanisms: (i) direct interaction of Zn(II) with I2 to generate the iodinating species (excluded by Scheme 4a–c control experiments showing alkene + I2/ZnCl2 alone is unreactive), and (ii) a free cycloiodonium-ion pathway intercepted by zinc phenoxide (excluded because added K2CO3 did not accelerate cyclisation and instead gave arene iodination). Favored pathway (Scheme 5): bis(aryloxy)zinc complex coordinates I2, undergoes I–I/Zn–O metathesis to a tethered ArO–I (iodosylaryl) species (intermediate 2), which delivers I+ intramolecularly to the allyl alkene; reversible deiodination via HI prevents accumulation of ring-iodinated arenes and the cyclised dihydrobenzofuran is itself deactivated toward further iodination, accounting for the high selectivity. No TEMPO/BHT or DFT studies were performed; mechanism is supported mainly by control experiments, CV, and charge–time electrochemical profiling.

10.1002_celc.201900172.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical oxidative 1,2-migration of α,α-disubstituted allyl alcohols to α-exo-methylene ketones using calcium or magnesium halide salts as halogen mediators
10.1002_celc.201900172.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of halide (Cl- or Br-) from CaX2/MgX2 generates an electrophilic halogen species (X+) that activates the allyl alcohol; cathode reduces MeOH to H2 and methoxide
10.1002_celc.201900172.electron_flow: Halide (from CaX2 or MgX2) is oxidized at the anode to an electrophilic X+ species, which adds to the allyl alcohol alkene; 1,2-migration with loss of H+ delivers an α-halo ketone; cathodic reduction of MeOH produces methoxide which (or added base) effects dehydrohalogenation to the α-exo-methylene ketone.

key_intermediates.name: electrophilic halogen species (X+) generated at anode from halide mediator
halonium ion / halogenated alkoxide adduct of allyl alcohol (X added across C=C with adjacent O-H)
α-halo ketone (after 1,2-migration and loss of H+)
α-exo-methylene ketone (final product after base-mediated dehydrohalogenation)

10.1002_celc.201900172.notes: Mechanism is proposed in Scheme 1 without direct mechanistic probes shown on these pages; halide-mediator role inferred from screening of supporting electrolytes (Et4NBF4 inactive; chloride/bromide salts active). Calcium and magnesium halides outperform alkali metal chlorides, and mixed MeOH/MeCN solvent suppresses electrode fouling from insoluble Ca species.

10.1002_celc.202101013.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical oxidative selenocyclization of N-(2-vinylphenyl)benzamides with diaryl/dialkyl diselenides to afford selenium-containing benzoxazines.
10.1002_celc.202101013.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of diphenyl diselenide (PhSeSePh) to a cationic radical that fragments into PhSe• and PhSe+; further anodic oxidation of a carbon-centered radical intermediate to a benzylic carbocation; cathodic reduction of H+ to H2 and PhSe+ back to diselenide.
10.1002_celc.202101013.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes PhSeSePh (Eox ~1.70 V, lower than substrate 1a at 1.90 V) to a radical cation that splits into PhSe• and PhSe+; PhSe• adds to the styrenyl alkene of 1a giving a benzylic C-radical, which is further oxidized at the anode to a carbocation, then intramolecular O-nucleophilic attack by the amide oxygen and deprotonation furnishes the benzoxazine; at the cathode, H+ is reduced to H2 and PhSe+ is reduced back to ½ PhSeSePh.

key_intermediates.name: benzylic carbon radical from PhSe• addition to styrene

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Only trace product formed; reaction inhibited, supporting radical pathway.

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: Only trace product; reaction significantly inhibited.

probe_reagents.name: 1,1-diphenylethylene (DPE)
