probe_reagents.observation: Only trace product; reaction inhibited, consistent with radical intermediate.

10.1002_celc.202101013.notes: CV shows PhSeSePh is oxidized preferentially over substrate 1a, supporting selenide-first oxidation rather than direct alkene oxidation. Mechanism is a radical-polar crossover with paired electrochemistry (productive anodic oxidations and H2-evolving cathode).

10.1002_celc.201900753.rxn_class_summary: HFIP-promoted electrochemical oxidative [3+2] annulation of 1,3-dicarbonyl compounds with alkenes to give 2,3-dihydrofurans with H2 evolution
10.1002_celc.201900753.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of the in situ generated 1,3-dicarbonyl enolate (e.g., sodium acetylacetonate) to an electrophilic C-centered radical; subsequent second anodic oxidation of the benzylic radical adduct to a benzylic cation; H2 evolved at the Pt cathode
10.1002_celc.201900753.electron_flow: Base (NaOAc) deprotonates the 1,3-dicarbonyl to its enolate, which is anodically oxidized to an electrophilic α-C-radical; this radical adds to the styrene to give a benzylic radical that is further oxidized at the anode to a benzylic cation, stabilized by an HFIP cluster, which tautomerizes and undergoes intramolecular nucleophilic O-attack by the enol oxygen to close the dihydrofuran, while protons are reduced to H2 at the Pt cathode.

key_intermediates.name: benzylic radical after addition to styrene (III)
enol tautomer (VI) prior to intramolecular O-cyclization

probe_reagents.name: DMPO (spin trap)
probe_reagents.observation: EPR signal (g=2.0063, AN=14.56, AH=22.44) consistent with trapping of an electrophilic C-centered radical

probe_reagents.name: sodium acetylacetonate (2a')
probe_reagents.observation: Used as substrate instead of acetylacetone gave 3a in 81% GC yield, supporting enolate as the species oxidized at the anode

probe_reagents.name: DMSO (as co-solvent in place of HFIP)
probe_reagents.observation: Replacing HFIP with DMSO still gave 3a in 35% GC yield, supporting stabilization of a cationic intermediate by coordinating solvent

probe_reagents.name: acetylacetone vs sodium acetylacetonate (CV)
probe_reagents.observation: Ep = 2.75 V (acetylacetone) vs 1.14 V (Na-acetylacetonate) vs Ag/AgCl; styrene Ep = 2.61 V — only the enolate is oxidizable under operating potential

probe_reagents.name: controlled-potential electrolysis
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction proceeds only when working electrode potential >0.7 V, matching the ~0.75 V onset oxidation of the acetylacetonate anion

10.1002_celc.201900753.notes: Authors emphasize HFIP acts as a 'stabilizer' for the cationic intermediate IV via H-bonded cluster; DMSO control experiment supports cation stabilization as a general role. No product is obtained without electricity, without NaOAc base, or without HFIP, indicating all three (anodic oxidation, enolate formation, and cation stabilization) are required. Alternative pathway via direct oxidation of styrene radical cation is ruled out by CV (styrene oxidation potential too high relative to operating conditions).

10.1038_s41596-018-0010-0.rxn_class_summary: Mn-catalyzed electrochemical 1,2-diazidation of alkenes using NaN3 as the azide source under anodic oxidation conditions
10.1038_s41596-018-0010-0.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation regenerates [Mn(III)]–N3 from [Mn(II)]; Mn mediator lowers the potential required to oxidize N3- to an azidyl radical equivalent that is delivered to the alkene
10.1038_s41596-018-0010-0.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes [Mn(II)] to [Mn(III)]-N3, which delivers an azidyl radical to the alkene generating a C-centered radical; a second [Mn(III)]-N3 (regenerated by anodic oxidation with N3-) transfers a second azide to the carbon radical to give the 1,2-diazide.

key_intermediates.name: beta-azido carbon-centered radical (from azidyl addition to alkene)
[Mn(II)] reduced manganese species (resting after azidyl transfer)

probe_reagents.name: cell potential variation (Ecell 1.9-2.3 V)
probe_reagents.observation: Lowering Ecell from 2.3 V to 1.9 V with 10 mol% MnBr2·4H2O improves selectivity (16:17 from 3:1 to 7:1) and isolated yield (from <10% to 60%), consistent with controlled anodic oxidation of Mn/N3-

10.1038_s41596-018-0010-0.notes: Mechanism is proposed (Fig. 2d) without explicit radical traps, KIE, EPR, or DFT shown on these pages. A major byproduct (17, a triazide) suggests overoxidation/competing radical pathways at higher potentials. The Mn catalyst plays a dual role: lowering the oxidation potential of N3- and chemoselectively delivering azidyl first to the alkene and then to the C-radical, accounting for tolerance of oxidatively sensitive FGs (alcohols, aldehydes, sulfides, amines, ferrocenes).

science.adg2866.rxn_class_summary: Enantioselective electrochemical cobalt-catalyzed aryl C-H activation enabling C-C, C-N, and C-O bond-forming annulations of carboxylic amides and phosphinamides coupled to the hydrogen evolution reaction at the cathode.
science.adg2866.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of the cyclometalated Co(III) species to higher-valent Co(IV)/Co(III) intermediates that drive C-N/C-O/C-C bond formation via oxidation-induced reductive elimination; protons reduced at the Pt cathode to give H2 (HER) as the terminal sink, with cobalt acting as the inner-sphere electrocatalyst.
science.adg2866.electron_flow: Co(II) is oxidized in situ and undergoes CMD-type C-H metalation of the benzamide to give a chiral cyclometalated Co(III) species; anodic single-electron oxidation generates a Co(IV) (or Co(III/IV/II) manifold) intermediate that, after alkene/alkyne coordination and migratory insertion, undergoes oxidation-induced reductive elimination to forge the C-C/C-N/C-O bond, while protons are reduced at the Pt cathode to H2.
science.adg2866.rate_determining_step: Electron transfer (anodic oxidation of the cyclometalated Co(III) intermediate); C-H cleavage and migratory insertion are not rate-limiting (kH/kD ≈ 1.0; no order in maleimide).

key_intermediates.name: Cyclometalated Co(III) complex C1 (CPA1-ligated, C-H activated benzamide)
Co(III)-CPA1 chiral cobaltacycle (point of enantioinduction via inner-sphere coordination of chiral phosphoric acid)
Higher-valent Co(IV) intermediate generated by anodic oxidation
Migratory-insertion alkyl-Co intermediate after alkene insertion

probe_reagents.name: Deuterated benzamide [D]5-1
probe_reagents.observation: kH/kD ≈ 1.0 in parallel experiments, indicating C-H cleavage is not rate-determining.

probe_reagents.name: Stoichiometric cyclometalated Co(III) complex C1
probe_reagents.observation: Isolated and fully characterized; required electricity for efficient and selective product formation, supporting its role as on-cycle intermediate.

probe_reagents.name: Variation of maleimide concentration (kinetic order)
probe_reagents.observation: No marked correlation of initial rate with maleimide concentration; migratory insertion deemed fast and not rate-limiting.

probe_reagents.name: Variation of current (product formation rate)
probe_reagents.observation: Direct correlation between current and product formation rate, identifying electron transfer as rate-determining.

probe_reagents.name: Nonlinear effect study (varying ee of CPA1)
probe_reagents.observation: Linear relationship between ee of CPA1 and ee of product 3, ruling out self-association or multiligand catalysts.

probe_reagents.name: No-current control
probe_reagents.observation: No product formation in absence of electricity, confirming electrochemical turnover.

probe_reagents.name: Ag2CO3 as chemical oxidant control
probe_reagents.observation: Very low enantioselectivity obtained, highlighting the unique role of anodic oxidation for enantiocontrol.

science.adg2866.notes: Authors invoke a Co(III/IV/II) catalytic manifold supported by HRMS and UV-vis spectroelectrochemistry. Inner-sphere coordination of CPA1 at cobalt is proposed as the origin of enantioinduction, rather than an outer-sphere chiral counteranion mechanism. HER occurs at the Pt (or stainless steel) cathode as the sole stoichiometric byproduct; pivalic acid additive prevents cathode passivation.

10.1021_jacs.0c03993.rxn_class_summary: Photoinduced palladium-catalyzed three-component 1,2-carbofunctionalization (aminoalkylation, oxyalkylation, dialkylation) of 1,3-dienes with alkyl iodides and N/O/C-nucleophiles via a π-allyl Pd radical-polar crossover pathway
10.1021_jacs.0c03993.electron_flow: Blue-light photoexcited LnPd(0) performs single-electron transfer to the alkyl iodide, generating an alkyl radical and LnPd(I)-I; the alkyl radical adds to the terminal carbon of the 1,3-diene to give an allyl radical that combines with Pd(I) as π-allyl Pd radical A, which undergoes radical-polar crossover (favored by the weakly coordinating NTf2- anion from AgNTf2) to cationic π-allyl Pd(II) species B, where the amine/O-/C-nucleophile attacks to deliver the 1,2-product and regenerate LnPd(0).

key_intermediates.name: hybrid alkyl radical / LnPd(I)-X species (C) formed via SET to R3-I
alkyl radical R3• adding to terminal diene carbon
π-allyl Pd(I) radical intermediate A (allyl radical / Pd(I) hybrid)
cationic π-allyl Pd(II) intermediate B (after radical-polar crossover, stabilized by triflimide as weakly coordinating anion)
Xantphos π-allyl Pd complex with iodide counterion (82)
Xantphos π-allyl Pd complex with triflimide counterion (83)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: With TEMPO added to the standard reaction, the aminoalkylation product 4a was suppressed (0%) and a TEMPO-alkyl adduct 80 was isolated in 22% NMR yield, consistent with a free alkyl radical intermediate.

probe_reagents.name: Reaction without AgNTf2 (counterion control)
probe_reagents.observation: Without AgNTf2, aminoalkylation product 4a forms in 44% NMR yield along with 34% of allyl-radical dimerization byproduct 81, indicating that the triflimide anion suppresses radical dimerization by stabilizing the cationic π-allyl Pd species.

probe_reagents.name: Stoichiometric Xantphos π-allyl Pd complexes 82 (X=I) and 83 (X=NTf2) with TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Under blue LED, both complexes gave TEMPO-allyl trapping product 84 (82: 74%; 83: 59%), but under thermal/dark conditions only 82 reacted (50%) while 83 was unreactive (0%), supporting that NTf2- stabilizes the π-allyl Pd complex against homolysis and shifts the equilibrium toward the ionic π-allyl Pd cation.

10.1021_jacs.0c03993.notes: The mechanism hinges on a radical ⇌ ionic π-allyl Pd equilibrium (A ⇌ B); weakly coordinating triflimide (from AgNTf2) shifts this toward the electrophilic cationic π-allyl Pd intermediate B, enabling nucleophile attack and suppressing radical dimerization. Photoexcitation is required for SET to alkyl iodide; thermal reactivity of iodide complex 82 with TEMPO suggests some Pd-allyl homolysis is accessible thermally, but productive turnover requires light. No DFT, KIE, or radical clock experiments are reported.

10.1021_jacs.4c08945.rxn_class_summary: Cu/bpy-catalyzed three-component carboamination of alkenes with α-halo carbonyls and aryl amines proceeding via an ATRA intermediate and a Lewis-acid-assisted oxocarbenium intermediate
10.1021_jacs.4c08945.electron_flow: N-methylaniline (with K3PO4) reduces [Cu^II(dtbbpy)2]2+ to [Cu^I] via inner-sphere SET, generating an aminyl radical that dimerizes to hydrazine; [Cu^I] then reduces the α-bromoester to give a C-radical and [Cu^II]–Br, the radical adds to the alkene, and [Cu^II] returns Br to form the ATRA adduct; Cu acts as a Lewis acid on the C–Br bond to assist intramolecular SN2 by the ester carbonyl, generating the oxocarbenium (closing the Cu cycle) which is then opened by the amine nucleophile.
10.1021_jacs.4c08945.rate_determining_step: Oxocarbenium (33) formation via Cu-assisted intramolecular SN2 of the ATRA adduct (mech 2′); for electron-rich vinyl arenes, ATRA formation can become rate-limiting.

key_intermediates.name: α-ester carbon-centered radical (13) from reduction of α-bromoester by Cu(I)
benzylic radical adduct 14 (from radical addition to alkene)
Lewis-acid (Cu)-coordinated benzylic bromide (45) en route to SN2 cyclization
N-centered aminyl radical (•NHMePh / [•NHMePh]+) from inner-sphere reduction of Cu(II)
γ-aminoester carboamination product 16 (C1 attack) or γ-iminolactone 17 (C4 attack) or γ-lactone (C5 attack)

probe_reagents.name: Hammett series of para-substituted styrenes (OMe, tBu, Me, H, Cl, CO2Me, CF3)
probe_reagents.observation: Nonlinear Hammett plot: ρ = −0.2 for electron-rich (ATRA rate-limiting) and ρ = −1.2 for electron-neutral/deficient arenes (oxocarbenium formation rate-limiting); excludes free carbocation (Mechanism A would require ρ ≈ −5 to −6).

probe_reagents.name: Hammett study on uncatalyzed amination of ATRA adducts
probe_reagents.observation: ρ = −3.1, consistent with SN2 cyclization to charged oxocarbenium (mech 2), not SN1.

probe_reagents.name: Thorpe-Ingold probe: α,α-dimethyl (35) vs α-methyl (43) ATRA adducts
probe_reagents.observation: kMe2/kH = 2.6 (uncatalyzed) and 9.2 (Cu-catalyzed); supports cyclization at/before RDS, rules out mech 1′ (free cation).

probe_reagents.name: Enantiomerically enriched ATRA adduct 37* (92% ee)
probe_reagents.observation: Uncatalyzed amination is stereospecific (90% ee product), supporting SN2; Cu-catalyzed amination gives racemic product with full epimerization of starting material, indicating reversible Cu(I)-mediated reduction to benzylic radical.

probe_reagents.name: p-MeO-styrene (38)
probe_reagents.observation: 7% carboarylation product 40 observed (Friedel–Crafts/EAS on aniline), indicating partial SN1/asynchronous SN2 character with electron-rich styrenes.

probe_reagents.name: Crossover with p-CF3-styrene (36) added to 37
probe_reagents.observation: Formation of di-insertion adduct 47 and tri-insertion 48 (by LCMS), confirming reversible C–Br homolysis of ATRA adduct.

probe_reagents.name: Zn(OTf)2 (nonredox Lewis acid)
probe_reagents.observation: Accelerates SN2 cyclization (kZn/kbackground = 337 for 53a); reaction remains stereospecific (>95% ee retained from 37*), supporting Lewis-acid-assisted SN2 (mech 2′) and excluding mech 3′/4′ as dominant for benzylic systems.

probe_reagents.name: EPR of [Cu^II(dtbbpy)2]2+ and intermediates
probe_reagents.observation: Axially symmetric Cu(II) signal shifts to rhombic upon N-methylaniline addition (ligation), then a second isotropic signal localized on N appears (aniline-derived radical); supports inner-sphere reduction of Cu(II) by aniline.

probe_reagents.name: UV–vis monitoring of Cu(II) precatalyst 27 with K3PO4 + N-methylaniline
probe_reagents.observation: Loss of 750 nm band and appearance of MLCT at 432 nm consistent with reduction to [Cu^I(dtbbpy)2]+.

probe_reagents.name: HRMS detection of hydrazine (PhMeN–NMePh)
probe_reagents.observation: Hydrazine detected stoichiometrically and catalytically, confirming dimerization of aminyl radical formed upon Cu(II) reduction.

probe_reagents.name: DFT calculations
probe_reagents.observation: ATRA adduct 35 is 19.5 kcal/mol downhill from 26 + styrene; ΔG‡ = 20.1 kcal/mol for background SN2 cyclization to 33; clamshell conformation of α,α-dimethyl radical favored by 1.34 kcal/mol; cyclic acetal radical 32 is 23.4–23.8 kcal/mol uphill (eliminating mech 4′); free cation 30 is 7.2 kcal/mol less stable than oxocarbenium 33.

probe_reagents.name: Kinetic order determination
probe_reagents.observation: rate = kobs[26]^1[19]^1[27]^1[24]^0.4; fractional order in amine implies multiple operative pathways.

probe_reagents.name: Independent synthesis and time-course tracking of ATRA adduct 35
probe_reagents.observation: ATRA adduct builds up then is consumed as carboamination product 1 grows in, confirming it as a kinetically competent on-cycle intermediate.

probe_reagents.name: Cl3CBr (radical precursor lacking carbonyl)
probe_reagents.observation: Does not give carboamination, supporting the requirement of an ester carbonyl for oxocarbenium formation.

probe_reagents.name: Evans chiral auxiliary halide 23
probe_reagents.observation: Olefin-electronics-dependent dr (1.3:1 OMe, 3:1 H, 8:1 CF3; ρ = 0.8 on log dr); consistent with dynamic kinetic transformation governed by competing benzylic-radical racemization vs. oxocarbenium formation.

probe_reagents.name: α-bromoisobutyrate 18 + styrene + N-methyl-p-toluidine
probe_reagents.observation: γ-lactone 22 (84%) plus t-butyl/N-alkyl byproducts detected by GCMS, supporting oxocarbenium intermediate (SN1/E1 at C5).

10.1021_jacs.4c08945.notes: Three mechanisms were considered for oxocarbenium formation: (A) oxidation of benzylic radical 29 to free cation 30 then cyclization — ruled out by modest Hammett ρ and DFT (cation 7.2 kcal/mol less stable than oxocarbenium); (B/favored) atom transfer to ATRA adduct 31 followed by Lewis-acid (Cu)-assisted intramolecular SN2 (mech 2′) — supported by Hammett, Thorpe–Ingold, Zn(OTf)2 surrogate, stereospecificity, and DFT; (C) 5-endo-trig radical cyclization to cyclic acetal radical 32 then oxidation (mech 3′/4′) — eliminated by high DFT energy of 32 and TS 51 (37 kcal/mol). Product selectivity at C1 (γ-aminoester) vs C4 (iminolactone) vs C5 (lactone) of oxocarbenium 15 is controlled by amine class and styrene electronics: N-alkyl anilines and e-deficient primary anilines → carboamination (Pathway A); e-rich primary anilines with e-neutral/deficient or unactivated alkenes → iminolactone (Pathway B); secondary amines with unactivated alkenes → lactone (Pathway C). Lewis-acid co-catalysts (Zn(OTf)2) and neopentyl α-bromoesters extend scope to α-olefins.

10.1021_jacs.7b01016.rxn_class_summary: Metal- and reagent-free electrochemical intramolecular oxidative amination of tri- and tetrasubstituted alkenes to give allyl-substituted cyclic carbamates, ureas, and lactams via anodically generated nitrogen-centered radicals
10.1021_jacs.7b01016.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of the amide N-H substrate generates a nitrogen-centered radical (NCR); a second anodic oxidation of the cyclized C-radical produces a carbocation that loses a proton to give the allylic product
10.1021_jacs.7b01016.electron_flow: The anode oxidizes the alkene-tethered amide (after N-H deprotonation) to a nitrogen-centered radical I, which undergoes intramolecular cyclization onto the hindered alkene to give a carbon-centered radical II; II is further oxidized at the anode to a carbocation III, which loses a proton to regenerate a terminal C=C, yielding the cyclic allyl carbamate/urea/lactam. Protons are reduced to H2 at the Pt cathode.

key_intermediates.name: N-centered radical (amidyl radical) from anodic oxidation/deprotonation of the tethered amide N-H
Carbon-centered radical from 5-exo cyclization onto the tri/tetrasubstituted alkene
Carbocation (intermediate III) from anodic oxidation of the C-radical

10.1021_jacs.7b01016.notes: Authors note that the alternative pathways for C-radical II (H-abstraction reduction; nucleophilic trapping of cation III; nonselective/undesired proton elimination from III) are challenges that are overcome here, with regioselective deprotonation occurring at the terminal position to give the less stable but kinetically favored terminal alkene rather than the thermodynamically more stable tetrasubstituted enamine. Redox potentials (Ep/2 vs SCE in MeCN): substrate 1 = 1.23 V, product 2 = 1.39 V, rationalizing absence of overoxidation. Only the proposal portion of the paper is shown; mechanistic probe experiments (if any) are not visible on this page.

10.1021_jacs.5c09249.rxn_class_summary: Visible-light energy-transfer-catalyzed dearomative [2π+2σ] cycloaddition of thiophenes with bicyclo[1.1.0]butanes (BCBs) and dearomative [2π+2π] cycloaddition of oxazoles/thiazoles with electron-deficient alkenes
10.1021_jacs.5c09249.electron_flow: No net electron transfer: photoexcited Ir photocatalyst (*fac-Ir(dFppy)3 or Ir-F) transfers triplet energy to the azole/thiophene substrate, generating a triplet biradical that adds across the alkene/BCB; the resulting triplet 1,4-diradical undergoes ISC and radical-radical recombination (with optional ring opening and 4π-electrocyclization for thiophenes) to deliver the cycloadduct.

key_intermediates.name: triplet excited state of 2-benzoylthiophene (1a*) with spin density highest at C5 (0.69) and C2 (0.51)
triplet 1,4-diradical I from C5 addition of 1a* to BCB 2a (cyclobutyl radical + thiophene-centered radical)
ring-opened diradical II after thiophene C-S/C-C cleavage at C4
singlet diene intermediate III formed after ISC and radical-radical recombination
tricyclic product 3a from disrotatory photochemical 4π-electrocyclization of III
triplet excited state of 2-phenyloxazole (5a*) with spin density at C5 (0.61) and C4 (0.34)
triplet 1,4-diradical IV from C5 addition of 5a* to acrylonitrile
2-oxa-4-azabicyclo[3.2.0]hept-3-ene cycloadduct 6a after ISC and radical-radical recombination

probe_reagents.name: thioxanthone (low triplet-energy photocatalyst control)
probe_reagents.observation: No product formation with TXT for thiophene substrate, consistent with required ET > 60 kcal/mol; only partial activity (39%) in oxazole series

probe_reagents.name: 4-fluorophenyl-substituted thiophene 1ab
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction stops at the diene stage giving 3ab, confirming intermediate III

probe_reagents.name: quantum yield measurement
probe_reagents.observation: Φ = 0.13 (thiophene) and Φ = 0.09 (oxazole), ruling out radical chain mechanism

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of 1a, 1b, 5a, 7a vs Ir photocatalyst potentials
probe_reagents.observation: Substrate oxidation (~1.9 V vs SCE) and reduction (~ -2.36 V vs SCE) are not thermodynamically accessible to excited Ir-F (PC*/PC- = +1.21, PC+/PC* = -0.89 V), ruling out photoredox/SET pathway

probe_reagents.name: Stern-Volmer luminescence quenching
probe_reagents.observation: Thiophene 1a (Ksv ≈ 150) and oxazole 5a (Ksv ≈ 13.9) quench *Ir; BCB 2a and acrylonitrile 4b do not, supporting substrate activation via energy transfer

probe_reagents.name: UV-vis absorption
probe_reagents.observation: Only the Ir photocatalyst absorbs at 405/450 nm; direct excitation of substrate gives no product, ruling out direct photoexcitation

probe_reagents.name: Mulliken spin-population (DFT) analysis on 1a* and 5a*
probe_reagents.observation: Highest spin density at C5 (0.69 in 1a*, 0.61 in 5a*) rationalizes regioselectivity of radical addition

probe_reagents.name: X-ray crystallography of 6a, 8a', 8c
probe_reagents.observation: Confirms exo-diastereomer connectivity of cycloadducts

10.1021_jacs.5c09249.notes: Photocatalyst triplet energy threshold (>60 kcal/mol) correlates with yield; thioxanthone (ET = 65.5) inactive for thiophenes but partially active for oxazoles. For thiophenes, the diradical I undergoes thiophene ring cleavage (C4-radical drives C-S cleavage) to diene III, then disrotatory 4π-electrocyclization gives the 2-thiabicyclo[3.2.0] product; for oxazoles, no ring-opening, direct biradical closure gives [2π+2π] cycloadduct. An unusual product 3j from 4-phenyl-2-acetothiophene is proposed to form via photochemically allowed suprafacial 1,3-sigmatropic shift or stepwise 1,3-migration of the styrenic cyclobutene. Esters and Weinreb amides on thiophene are unreactive (insufficient ET lowering).

10.1021_jacs.3c04864_sup_1.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical allylation and allenylation of aldehydes (benzaldehyde) with allylarenes/propargylarenes via cathodically generated allylic/propargylic carbanions under Co-salen/DABCO catalysis
10.1021_jacs.3c04864_sup_1.electrochemical_role: Cathodic reduction generates a Co(II)-salen / Co(III)-H species that deprotonates the weakly acidic allyl/propargyl C-H of the substrate (HER-type), delivering an allylic or propargylic carbanion that adds to the aldehyde; sacrificial anode oxidation balances charge
10.1021_jacs.3c04864_sup_1.electron_flow: Cathode reduces Co(II)-salen / generates Co(III)-H that abstracts a proton from the weakly acidic benzylic/propargylic C-H of the substrate (HER-like, mediated), producing an allylic or propargylic carbanion that nucleophilically adds to benzaldehyde to give the homoallylic alcohol (3a) or α-allenyl alcohol (5a); the anode supplies the balancing oxidation.
10.1021_jacs.3c04864_sup_1.rate_determining_step: Proton transfer in the HER/deprotonation step generating the carbanion (consistent with observed secondary KIE in parallel experiments)

key_intermediates.name: alkoxide adduct (3a/5a-alkoxide after C-C bond formation)

probe_reagents.name: cyclopropyl-substituted substrates (radical clock 3bm-3br, 5bd, 5be)
probe_reagents.observation: No ring-opening products observed; cyclopropyl group retained in all products, arguing against radical intermediates and supporting an ionic (carbanion) pathway

probe_reagents.name: HOAc
probe_reagents.observation: Suppresses reaction to trace yield — acidic additive preferentially undergoes HER over substrate deprotonation

probe_reagents.name: MeOH
probe_reagents.observation: Reduces yield (32%/12% for 3a/5a Cond II) — competing HER of acidic additive

probe_reagents.name: H2O
probe_reagents.observation: Reduces yield (40%/25%) — competing HER consistent with carbanion mechanism

probe_reagents.name: 1,1-diphenylethylene (DPE)
probe_reagents.observation: Only marginal effect on yield (68-73%), inconsistent with radical chain

probe_reagents.name: P(OEt)3
probe_reagents.observation: Significant yield erosion (21%/48% / 50%) attributed to nucleophilic addition of carbanion to P(OEt)3, supporting carbanion intermediate

probe_reagents.name: D2O
probe_reagents.observation: No D incorporation in allylation product 3a; 0.48 D incorporated at allene carbon of 5a — propargylic carbanion has lower reactivity and undergoes reversible H/D exchange at cathode, whereas allylic carbanion adds to aldehyde faster than exchange

probe_reagents.name: 4a-d5 (CD3-labeled propargylarene)
probe_reagents.observation: 5a-d4 product shows 0.25 D at benzylic position and 0.72 D at allene terminus (D depleted from CD3 source) — supports H/D exchange via carbanion

probe_reagents.name: 1be-d2 / 4a-d5 KIE substrates
probe_reagents.observation: Parallel KIE kH/kD = 1.3/1 (allyl) and 1.5/1 (propargyl) — secondary KIE consistent with proton transfer in HER; intermolecular competition gives kH/kD = 3.3/1 and 4.3/1, discrepancy attributed to rapid H/D exchange of the carbanion

10.1021_jacs.3c04864_sup_1.notes: DFT (PBE0-D3(BJ)/ma-def2-TZVPP//B3LYP-D3(BJ)/Def2-SVP, SMD DMF) supports nucleophilic addition of allyl/allenyl carbanion to PhCHO: for allylation, the E-pathway is favored (TS1(E) 4.3 kcal/mol; 3a(E) -2.8 kcal/mol) over Z and over a homoallyl regioisomer (TS2 8.9 kcal/mol giving 3a'); for allenylation, addition through the central allenyl C is preferred (TS3 ~1.5-2.6 kcal/mol) giving 5a over the propargylic isomer 5a'. A radical pathway via Co(III)-H HAT was considered but excluded by radical clock and trap experiments; the authors note radical-like Co(III)-H species may still be affected by radical scavengers, explaining the partial yield drop with P(OEt)3.

10.1021_ja910586v.rxn_class_summary: Intramolecular anodic olefin coupling reaction forming substituted pyrrolidine and piperidine rings via oxidation of electron-rich olefin tethered to a tosylamide nucleophile, trapped by methanol
10.1021_ja910586v.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of electron-rich olefin (enol ether, thioenol ether, ketene dithioacetal, styrene, allylsilane, or diene) to a radical cation at an RVC anode; second anodic oxidation of the cyclized C-radical to a cation that is trapped by methanol
10.1021_ja910586v.electron_flow: The anode removes one electron from the electron-rich olefin to give a radical cation; the pendant tosylsulfonamide nitrogen (or its lithiated anion) attacks intramolecularly to give a cyclic C-radical, which is then oxidized at the anode to a cation that is trapped by methanol to deliver the cyclic amino ether product.
10.1021_ja910586v.rate_determining_step: Intramolecular cyclization of the nitrogen nucleophile onto the olefin-derived radical cation must outcompete deprotonation/elimination; final methanol trapping of cation 19 also affects yield

key_intermediates.name: cyclic carbon-centered radical (18) formed by intramolecular attack of tosylsulfonamide nitrogen on radical cation
stabilized cyclic cation (19) from oxidation of radical 18
deprotonation/elimination ketene acetal byproduct (16) leading to overoxidation product 12
lithiated (deprotonated) sulfonamide anion / N-centered radical (alternative pathway)

probe_reagents.name: LiOMe (replacing 2,6-lutidine as base)
probe_reagents.observation: Raising pH by using LiOMe dramatically improved cyclization yields (e.g., 9a: 20%->70-82%; 9b: 54%->85-90%), consistent with deprotonated sulfonamide acting as better nucleophile or N-radical precursor

probe_reagents.name: Methyl substitution at beta-carbon of olefin (9d vs 9c)
probe_reagents.observation: Removing the acidic beta-H suppressed elimination/overoxidation pathway, supporting deprotonation of radical cation 15 as side reaction

probe_reagents.name: gem-dimethyl substrate 29a vs mono-methyl 29b
probe_reagents.observation: gem-Dimethyl group blocks elimination and raised six-membered ring yield to 81%, vs 35% for 29b, implicating elimination from allylic position as competing pathway

probe_reagents.name: Trisubstituted allylsilane substrate 34
probe_reagents.observation: A1,3-strain forces allylic H out of overlap with the radical cation pi-system, slowing elimination and giving 71% cyclized product as single diastereomer

probe_reagents.name: Electrolyte/solvent variation (Et4NOTs vs LiClO4; MeOH vs MeOH/THF)
probe_reagents.observation: Higher [MeOH] near anode (lower electrolyte conc., LiClO4) improves trapping of cation 19, supporting the proposed cation-trapping final step

probe_reagents.name: NOESY on 11e
probe_reagents.observation: Single diastereomer assigned; consistent with TS minimizing steric interactions between methyl and dithiane groups

10.1021_ja910586v.notes: Two indistinguishable pathways are proposed for the role of LiOMe: (i) deprotonated sulfonamide anion attacks the olefin radical cation as a better nucleophile, or (ii) N-centered radical is generated by oxidation at nitrogen and adds to the olefin; the authors note these can equilibrate by fast intramolecular electron transfer and both converge on cyclic C-radical 18. Major side pathway is deprotonation of the radical cation to give a neutral alkene that undergoes a second oxidation/MeOH trapping to give overoxidized products (e.g., 12, 13, 22, 28). Six-membered ring formation is intrinsically slower and more susceptible to elimination.

10.1021_jacs.4c02975.rxn_class_summary: Enantioselective Paternò–Büchi [2+2] photocycloaddition between 3-alkoxyquinolones and aryl α-ketoesters (benzoylformates) sensitized by a hydrogen-bonding chiral Ir photocatalyst to give chiral oxetanes.
10.1021_jacs.4c02975.electron_flow: No net electron transfer: the chiral Ir photocatalyst is excited by visible light and, while H-bonded to the quinolone, transfers triplet energy by Dexter mechanism preferentially to the unbound aryl α-ketoester within the solvent cage; the triplet (n,π*) ketoester then attacks the bound quinolone alkene to form a triplet 1,4-biradical that closes to the oxetane (triplet rebound), with chirality conferred by the catalyst–quinolone H-bond.
10.1021_jacs.4c02975.rate_determining_step: Partition between productive in-cage reaction of triplet ketoester with H-bonded quinolone (high ee) versus cage escape to give racemic background reaction; cage escape is the selectivity-determining branch.

key_intermediates.name: Ground-state H-bonded complex between chiral Ir photocatalyst (pyrazole N–H) and 3-isopropoxyquinolone substrate (2)
Free triplet aryl α-ketoester (e.g. 3e*, (n,π*) triplet with oxyl radical character) generated by Dexter energy transfer within the solvent cage
Triplet 1,4-biradical from C–O bond formation between triplet ketoester oxygen and quinolone alkene (Paternò–Büchi biradical)
Free (cage-escaped) triplet ketoester reacting with unbound quinolone (racemic pathway)
Triplet quinolone 2* generated by competitive Dexter energy transfer to bound quinolone (transposed Paternò–Büchi pathway)

probe_reagents.name: Adamantyl benzoylformate 3e (bulky, non-H-bond-accepting α-ketoester)
probe_reagents.observation: NMR titration shows Ka < 10 with catalyst 1f (no ground-state binding) while still being quenched (KSV = 4000 M^-1, KD = 3700 M^-1), supporting a triplet rebound (dynamic) pathway rather than static binding.

probe_reagents.name: Quinolone substrate 2
probe_reagents.observation: NMR isotherm fits 1:2 binding (Ka1 = 280, Ka2 = 180) with catalyst 1f; strong static + dynamic quenching (KSV = 9400 M^-1, KD = 7100 M^-1, with static component KS = 280 M^-1) showing pre-association of quinolone.

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of 2 and 3e
probe_reagents.observation: Eox > +1.75 V and Ered < -1.4 to -1.7 V vs SCE for both substrates, outside the photocatalyst window (+1.23 / -1.13 V), ruling out single-electron transfer and supporting energy transfer.

probe_reagents.name: Triplet energy comparison (ET of 1f = 63 kcal/mol; 2 = 55; 3e = 61 kcal/mol)
probe_reagents.observation: Dexter energy transfer thermodynamically feasible to both substrates, consistent with competing quenching pathways.

probe_reagents.name: Time-resolved emission (Stern–Volmer) of 1f with 2 and 3e
probe_reagents.observation: Single-exponential decays give KD values that quantify dynamic quenching by each component; supports collisional energy transfer.

probe_reagents.name: Air/oxygen exposure
probe_reagents.observation: Dissolved O2 quenches triplet ketoester (longer-lived for bulkier 3e), lowering ee — consistent with a free triplet ketoester intermediate whose lifetime governs cage escape.

probe_reagents.name: Slow addition (syringe pump) of quinolone 2
probe_reagents.observation: Reducing [2] suppresses competitive Dexter quenching by quinolone and the transposed Paternò–Büchi pathway, raising ee to 86–98%.

probe_reagents.name: Coumarin analog lacking N–H
probe_reagents.observation: Failed to give Paternò–Büchi product, confirming essential role of H-bonding to the catalyst pyrazole N–H.

10.1021_jacs.4c02975.notes: Favored mechanism is 'triplet rebound': catalyst H-bonds the quinolone in the ground state but selectively sensitizes the unbound ketoester, which then reacts back with the bound quinolone inside the solvent cage to give high ee. Two competing pathways erode ee: (i) cage escape of triplet ketoester followed by reaction with free quinolone (racemic), and (ii) Dexter energy transfer to bound quinolone giving triplet 2* that reacts with ground-state ketoester (a 'transposed Paternò–Büchi' pathway with low ee). Sensitivity of ee to ketoester substitution is attributed to changes in triplet lifetime and cage escape efficiency. SET is excluded by mismatched redox potentials.

10.1021_jacs.9b13305.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical hydrocarboxylation of aryl alkenes (styrenes) with CO2 to give β-aryl carboxylic acids, including formation of all-carbon quaternary centers from β,β-disubstituted alkenes
10.1021_jacs.9b13305.electrochemical_role: Cathodic reduction of the surface-adsorbed styrene to a radical anion; subsequent CO2 addition and a second cathodic electron transfer give a carbanion that is protonated by water
10.1021_jacs.9b13305.electron_flow: At the cathode, the aryl alkene adsorbs and accepts one electron to form a benzylic radical anion; CO2 adds to the benzylic position to give a carboxylated benzylic radical, which is reduced by a second electron and protonated by trace water (or D2O) to deliver the β-carboxylic acid.

key_intermediates.name: surface-adsorbed styrene radical anion (benzylic radical anion)
β-carboxylate benzylic radical (after CO2 addition to radical anion)
benzylic carbanion (after second electron transfer, dissociated from electrode)
ring-opened homoallyl/benzyl radical from cyclopropane clock 1u

probe_reagents.name: cyclopropane radical clock (substrate 1k, 1o)
probe_reagents.observation: No ring opening observed (k_open ~6.1e4 s-1 < k_closure 5.4e6 s-1); benzylic radical reversibly closes, consistent with short-lived benzylic radical

probe_reagents.name: phenyl-substituted cyclopropane clock 1u
probe_reagents.observation: Irreversible ring opening (k ~2e8 s-1) gives ring-opened acid 2u (5:1 vs 2u'); without CO2, ring-opened reduced product 6' dominates (16:1 vs 6), supporting a radical intermediate

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: No TEMPO adduct isolated; no carboxylation or reduction observed (inconclusive trapping)

probe_reagents.name: D2O
probe_reagents.observation: >95% D incorporation at the benzylic position of [D]2a, plus ~10% deuterated ethylbenzene; confirms protonation by water at benzylic carbanion

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of 4-CF3-styrene 1e
probe_reagents.observation: Reduction onset unchanged upon CO2 saturation, but cathodic current drops from -149 to -65 µA, indicating surface-adsorbed radical anion that is intercepted by CO2 rather than polymerizing

10.1021_jacs.9b13305.notes: Two mechanistic routes were considered (Table 3): route (a) direct reduction of CO2 to CO2•– which adds to the alkene, vs route (b) reduction of the surface-adsorbed alkene to a radical anion that then reacts with CO2. The authors favor route (b) based on CV data showing alkene reduction and current attenuation under CO2, but note that the biased potential prevents fully ruling out direct CO2 reduction (route a). Unactivated cyclopropane substrates (1k, 1o) do not ring-open because benzylic radical ring-closure outcompetes opening — this is consistent with but does not by itself prove a radical intermediate; the phenyl-cyclopropane 1u clock provides the decisive radical evidence.

10.1021_ja202880n.rxn_class_summary: Integrated electrochemical-chemical oxidation of diarylmethanes, methylarenes, and aryl-substituted alkenes to carbonyl compounds (ketones, aldehydes, 1,2-diketones, and cyclized ketones) via anodically generated carbocations trapped by DMSO to form alkoxysulfonium ions, followed by Et3N-mediated Kornblum-type elimination.
10.1021_ja202880n.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation generates either a diarylcarbenium ion (cation pool, Method A, in CD2Cl2 at -78 °C) or a radical cation of an electron-rich arene/alkene (Method B, in DMSO/CD2Cl2) that is trapped in situ by DMSO to form an alkoxysulfonium ion; substrate is preferentially oxidized over DMSO because of its lower oxidation potential.
10.1021_ja202880n.electron_flow: Anode removes one or two electrons from the substrate to give a benzylic/diarylcarbenium cation (or, for activated arenes/alkenes, a radical cation that is further oxidized after nucleophilic capture); DMSO traps the cationic carbon as an alkoxysulfonium ion, which Et3N then deprotonates to expel Me2S and yield the carbonyl product (Kornblum-type collapse).

key_intermediates.name: 4-methoxybenzyl-type alkoxysulfonium ion (8, in situ trapped, d6-DMSO)
dialkoxysulfonium ion from stilbene (13) — bis-DMSO adduct of stilbene dication equivalent
cyclized alkoxysulfonium ion from alkene bearing pendant nucleophile (e.g., 24 from tosylamide-tethered styrene 23)
radical cation of styrene/diene precursor (proposed for cyclization substrates, e.g., 32•+)
distonic radical cation after intramolecular alkene cyclization

probe_reagents.name: low-temperature 1H NMR characterization of cationic intermediates 2, 3, 8, 13, 24
probe_reagents.observation: Distinctive downfield H_a signals (e.g., 9.99 ppm for diarylcarbenium 2; 6.58 ppm for alkoxysulfonium 3; 5.17 ppm for 8; 5.93 ppm for 13; 6.13/3.68 ppm for cyclized 24) confirm accumulation of the proposed carbocation and alkoxysulfonium intermediates.

probe_reagents.name: rotating disk electrode (RDE) voltammetry of substrates
probe_reagents.observation: Eox values (e.g., 1.96 V for 1, 1.38 V for 7, 1.16 V for diene 32 vs 1.54 V for monoene 40) rationalize choice between Method A vs B and support rapid cation-radical cyclization for tethered dienes.

probe_reagents.name: comparison of Eox(32)=1.16 V vs Eox(40)=1.54 V (intramolecular vs single alkene)
probe_reagents.observation: Lower oxidation potential of the diene indicates that the initial radical cation undergoes rapid intramolecular cyclization onto the second alkene before further oxidation.

10.1021_ja202880n.notes: Two operationally distinct modes: (A) 'cation pool' generation of stable diarylcarbenium at -78 °C in CD2Cl2 followed by DMSO addition, used when substrate Eox > Eox(DMSO)=1.76 V; (B) in situ DMSO trapping during electrolysis, used when substrate Eox < 1.76 V (e.g., 4-methoxytoluene, stilbenes, tethered alkenes), which also avoids accumulation of unstable cations (e.g., 4-methoxybenzyl cation). For cyclization substrates, the favored mechanism (Figure 4) is anodic one-electron oxidation to a radical cation, rapid intramolecular addition onto a second alkene/nucleophile to give a distonic radical cation, then a second anodic oxidation and DMSO trapping to give the cyclic alkoxysulfonium; Et3N then gives the cyclic ketone. The DMSO/Et3N step is mechanistically the Swern/Kornblum-type collapse of an alkoxysulfonium.

10.1021_jacs.0c10899.rxn_class_summary: Electroreductive activation of chlorosilanes (Si-Cl) to generate silyl radicals that add to alkenes, enabling disilylation, hydrosilylation, allylic silylation, and silacycle synthesis via a radical-polar crossover mechanism
10.1021_jacs.0c10899.electrochemical_role: Cathodic single-electron reduction of R3Si-Cl (~ -0.5 V vs Mg0/2+) cleaves the strong Si-Cl bond to generate a silyl radical; Mg sacrificial anode provides Mg2+ that facilitates Si-Cl reduction by binding Cl-; a second cathodic reduction reduces the benzylic radical to a benzylic anion
10.1021_jacs.0c10899.electron_flow: Cathode reduces R3Si-Cl (rate-determining) to its radical anion, which fragments to Cl- and a silyl radical; the silyl radical adds to the alkene giving a benzylic radical (Int-1) that is further reduced at the cathode to a benzylic anion (Int-2); Int-2 attacks a second R3Si-Cl (or H+/intramolecular electrophile) to form the second C-Si bond, while Mg is oxidized at the sacrificial anode.
10.1021_jacs.0c10899.rate_determining_step: Cathodic single-electron reduction of TMSCl (global rate-determining step, GRDS); first C-Si bond formation (silyl radical addition to alkene) is the rate-determining chemical step (RDCS)

probe_reagents.name: vinyl cyclopropane (substrates 30, 31)
probe_reagents.observation: Ring opening observed giving products 32/33 (73%, 55%), confirming intermediacy of carbon radical Int-3

probe_reagents.name: allyl ether 34 (allyl-OBn substrate)
probe_reagents.observation: Converted to allylsilane 35 (71%) with BnOTMS detected by GC-MS, supporting benzylic anion Int-4 that triggers β-elimination of BnO- leaving group

probe_reagents.name: CD3CN (deuterated acetonitrile)
probe_reagents.observation: Deuterosilylation product 45 obtained, confirming H+/D+ from MeCN as the proton source for the carbanion Int-2

probe_reagents.name: 13C kinetic isotope effect
probe_reagents.observation: 13C KIE consistent with nucleophilic silyl radical addition to alkene being rate-determining chemical step

probe_reagents.name: substituted styrenes (Hammett/MLR)
probe_reagents.observation: Two-parameter MLR fit (lg(krel) = 0.12 + 1.2σ + 0.73σ*, R2=0.96) shows both electronic and radical stability effects on rate, consistent with nucleophilic silyl radical addition

probe_reagents.name: TMSOAc (very strong Si-O bond substrate)
probe_reagents.observation: Even Si-O bond (120-140 kcal/mol) was activated giving 31% product, demonstrating reach of cathodic potential

10.1021_jacs.0c10899.notes: An alternative 2e- pathway in which TMSCl is reduced to silyl anion TMS- (analogous to Li/Na chemistry, Scheme 1B) was considered but ruled out based on CV, MLR, KIE, and DFT data. Control experiments also exclude pathways involving electrogenerated Mg0 (no conversion with Mg powder or Mg0 pre-generation); Mg2+ is not required but lowers Si-Cl reduction overpotential by Cl- coordination. Overall ECEC sequence (electrochemical-chemical-electrochemical-chemical) with zero-order in substrate and first-order in current.

10.1021_jacs.3c07146_sup_1.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical coupling involving anodic oxidation of arenes/thiophenes/styrenes, studied by cyclic voltammetry to establish oxidation potentials of substrates
10.1021_jacs.3c07146_sup_1.electrochemical_role: Anodic single-electron oxidation of electron-rich (hetero)arenes and styrenes to their radical cations at a glassy carbon electrode (E_p/2 ranging from 1.26 V to 2.19 V vs SCE)
10.1021_jacs.3c07146_sup_1.electron_flow: The anode performs single-electron oxidation of the electron-rich (hetero)aromatic substrate to a radical cation; subsequent chemistry (e.g., deprotonation to a benzylic radical) is probed by radical trapping experiments.

probe_reagents.name: benzylic radical trap (TEMPO or similar, as indicated by 'Benzylic radical trapping experiment' section)
probe_reagents.observation: Section header indicates a benzylic radical trapping experiment was performed to evidence a benzylic radical intermediate (details on subsequent pages not shown)

10.1021_jacs.3c07146_sup_1.notes: Only the CV portion and the header of a benzylic radical trapping experiment are visible. CV data (E_p/2: compound 49 = 1.26 V; compound 2 = 1.70 V; compound 47 = 1.46 V; compound 1 = 2.00 V; compound 3 = 1.71 V; 2-acetylthiophene = 2.19 V) rank substrate oxidation potentials, supporting that electron-rich heteroaromatics/styrenes are oxidized first under anodic conditions. Probe reagent identity for the benzylic radical trapping experiment is not visible on this page.

10.1021_jacs.3c02961.rxn_class_summary: Visible-light-mediated ortho-[2π+2σ] dearomative photocycloaddition of bicyclic aza-arenes (quinolines, isoquinolines, etc.) with bicyclobutanes (BCBs) to give bicyclo[2.1.1]hexane (BCH)-fused dihydroquinolines
10.1021_jacs.3c02961.electron_flow: Photoexcited Ir-F* sensitizes the quinoline by triplet energy transfer to give an activated diradical, which adds across the BCB central σ-bond via a concerted TS1 to form a triplet 1,4-biradical; ISC at a minimum-energy crossing point (favoring the ortho [2-2] geometry) and radical–radical recombination deliver the BCH-fused dihydroquinoline. In a parallel two-photon-initiated chain, *Ir-F oxidizes the excited quinoline to a radical cation that adds to BCB; cyclization and SET to ground-state quinoline propagate the chain (Brønsted/Lewis acids lower triplet energy and TS1, boosting the chain).
10.1021_jacs.3c02961.rate_determining_step: Concerted BCB addition transition state TS1 (C–C bond formation with BCB σ-bond cleavage), ΔG‡ = 18.2 kcal/mol; lowered to 12.3 (H+) and 16.4 (Sc(OTf)3) kcal/mol by acid additives

key_intermediates.name: TS1 — concerted C–C bond formation / BCB σ-bond cleavage (triplet)
Quinoline radical cation (B/C in chain mechanism)
BCH radical cation G (cyclized chain carrier)

probe_reagents.name: Sc(OTf)3 (Lewis acid additive)
probe_reagents.observation: Bathochromic shift in UV/vis enabling direct excitation at 450 nm; lowers triplet energy of quinoline by 10.5 kcal/mol and TS1 by 1.8 kcal/mol; raises quantum yield from 0.20 to 8.8 (with Ir-F) indicating chain propagation

probe_reagents.name: p-TsOH·H2O (Brønsted acid)
probe_reagents.observation: Decreases triplet energy by 3.2 kcal/mol and TS1 by 5.9 kcal/mol; Φ = 7.7

probe_reagents.name: Pyrazole (nucleophile trap)
probe_reagents.observation: Trapping experiments with 6-methoxyquinoline support intermediacy of a quinoline radical cation generated by Ir-F oxidation (biphotonic pathway)

probe_reagents.name: Light-intensity variation
probe_reagents.observation: Initial rate ∝ P^0.85 (450 nm) and ∝ P^1.0 (415 nm), inconsistent with purely biphotonic (P^2) — supports parallel one-photon EnT and two-photon chain pathways

probe_reagents.name: Stern–Volmer quenching study
probe_reagents.observation: Excited *Ir-F is quenched by quinoline 1a but not by BCB 2a

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of 1a
probe_reagents.observation: Ground-state SET between *Ir-F and quinoline is thermodynamically unfavorable, supporting EnT for the main cycle; only triplet sensitizers (~60 kcal/mol) that are also strongly oxidizing initiate the chain

probe_reagents.name: Photocatalyst screen / triplet-energy correlation
probe_reagents.observation: Yield correlates with increasing triplet-state energy of photocatalyst

probe_reagents.name: Thermal control (MeCN, 100 °C, no light)
probe_reagents.observation: No background thermal reactivity — rules out ground-state pathway

10.1021_jacs.3c02961.notes: Two mechanisms are proposed to operate in parallel. (1) Favored under standard conditions: triplet energy transfer from *Ir-F to the bicyclic aza-arene, concerted-like BCB addition through TS1 to a triplet biradical IM4-T1, then ISC via a [2-2] minimum-energy crossing point (MECP) to give the ortho cycloadduct IM5; the alternative [2-4] MECP would give the para isomer IM6 (less stable by ~10 kcal/mol and higher in MECP energy), rationalizing ortho selectivity. Regioselectivity correlates with computed C8 spin density (0.76 on 7-OMe quinoline). (2) Two-photon-initiated radical-chain pathway: *Ir-F oxidizes excited quinoline to a radical cation (C), which adds to BCB at C3 (highest spin density at C8), cyclization gives BCH radical cation G, and SET to ground-state quinoline propagates the chain. Acids (Brønsted H+ or Lewis Sc(OTf)3) coordinate the quinoline N, lower triplet energy and TS1, and dramatically enhance the chain (Φ up to 8.8). The exact role of acid in chain propagation is acknowledged as speculative.

10.1021_jacs.2c07726.rxn_class_summary: Photosensitized dearomative para-cycloaddition of quinolines with alkenes/allenes via Lewis acid activation and triplet energy transfer, proceeding through a stepwise biradical pathway.
10.1021_jacs.2c07726.electron_flow: Blue-light excitation of [Ir(dFCF3ppy)2dtbbpy]PF6 produces the Ir triplet which undergoes Dexter triplet-triplet energy transfer to the BF3-activated quinoline, lowering its ET; the triplet quinoline biradical adds to the alkene to give a triplet 1,5-biradical, which ISCs to an open-shell singlet biradical that either fragments back (reversible) or recombines (selectivity-determining) to the closed-shell para cycloadduct, with a second EnT photo-eroding any ortho adduct.
10.1021_jacs.2c07726.rate_determining_step: Intramolecular radical recombination of the open-shell singlet biradical (OSS->CSS via TP-B) is the selectivity-determining step; radical addition is reversible and not selectivity-determining.

key_intermediates.name: Triplet BF3-quinoline complex (3A, T1) generated by Dexter energy transfer from [Ir-F]*
Triplet 1,5-biradical (3B) after radical addition of triplet quinoline to alkene
Open-shell singlet biradical (1B / OSS) formed by ISC, long-lived enough to allow bond rotation
Closed-shell singlet cycloadduct (1C, para product) after radical recombination
Ortho cycloadduct (1D), photolabile, can be re-sensitized and fragment back

probe_reagents.name: E-2D-labeled homoallyl MOM ether (alkene 59)
probe_reagents.observation: Recovered alkene was scrambled to 1:1 E/Z and products 60a/60b formed 1:1, indicating reversible radical addition and that recombination is rate/selectivity-determining.

probe_reagents.name: 13C natural-abundance KIE (Singleton method) on 5-Me-quinoline and 5-OAc-quinoline
probe_reagents.observation: Strong 13C KIE at C5 (1.017 for 5-Me-quinoline; 1.019 for 5-OAc-quinoline) consistent with C-C bond formation at C5 in the selectivity-determining recombination step.

probe_reagents.name: Stern-Volmer quenching with quinoline vs quinoline-BF3 vs 1-hexene
probe_reagents.observation: Quinoline and quinoline-BF3 quench [Ir-F]* with similar efficiency; 1-hexene does not quench, showing EnT occurs to the quinoline (Lewis acid not strictly required for EnT).

probe_reagents.name: Control without BF3 using 2D-alkene 59
probe_reagents.observation: <2% product and no E/Z scrambling of alkene; BF3 is needed to lower the radical addition barrier.

probe_reagents.name: Cyclopropyl-quinoline trap (6-Cl-quinoline giving fused product 66; 5-CO2Me-quinoline giving cyclobutane 73)
probe_reagents.observation: Ring-opened/rearranged products consistent with a second EnT to an ortho cycloadduct, C-Cl or C-C homolysis, and recombination.

probe_reagents.name: X-ray crystallography of regioisomers 13 and 14
probe_reagents.observation: Confirmed identity of 8-to-5 vs 5-to-8 regioisomers.

10.1021_jacs.2c07726.notes: An alternative mechanism in which the first intermolecular radical addition is selectivity-determining was excluded by the 2D-alkene scrambling experiment and 13C KIE. DFT mapped triplet (T), open-shell singlet (OSS), and closed-shell singlet (CSS) surfaces, with two transition points (TP-A back to starting materials, TP-B to cycloadduct). Lewis acid plays a dual role: minor lowering of ET for EnT (Stern-Volmer shows EnT possible without LA) and major lowering of the radical addition barrier by increasing quinoline electrophilicity. Regiochemistry (5-to-8 vs 8-to-5) is controlled by biradical dipole stabilization (solvent polarity) and hyperconjugative stabilization (substituent position); endo selectivity is driven by London dispersion interactions with the pyridine ring (DFT-D3 vs non-dispersion-corrected comparison). Ortho adducts are photolabile (lower ET than [Ir-F]) and undergo a second EnT, funneling the reaction toward para products.

10.1021_jacs.5c01506.rxn_class_summary: Photoexcited Gd(III)-catalyzed enantioselective dearomative [2π+2σ] cycloaddition of naphthalenes with bicyclo[1.1.0]butanes to give chiral tricyclic BCHs
10.1021_jacs.5c01506.electron_flow: Gd(III) chelates the acylpyridine directing group of the naphthalene and red-shifts its absorption; visible-light excitation gives the singlet S1 complex, which undergoes ISC to a triplet that adds across the central C–C σ-bond of the BCB to form a triplet 1,4-biradical, followed by a second ISC and radical recombination to close the cyclobutane and deliver the dearomatized product after ligand exchange with fresh naphthalene.

key_intermediates.name: Excited S1 singlet Gd-bound naphthalene complex (1*I)
Excited T1 triplet Gd-bound naphthalene complex (3*I)
Triplet 1,4-biradical from radical addition of 3*I to BCB 2a (intermediate II)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: 3 equiv suppressed formation of 3a (trace yield), consistent with radical intermediates

probe_reagents.name: O2
probe_reagents.observation: 1 atm O2 reduced 3a to trace, supporting triplet/radical involvement

probe_reagents.name: hexa-2,4-diene
probe_reagents.observation: 0.5 equiv → 70% yield; 1 equiv → 52% yield; triplet quencher diminishes yield, supporting triplet naphthalene intermediate

probe_reagents.name: acetyl-naphthalene 10 (R=Me)
probe_reagents.observation: No reaction without the 2-acylpyridine bidentate DG, confirming necessity of bidentate Gd coordination

probe_reagents.name: benzoyl-naphthalene 11 (R=Ph)
probe_reagents.observation: No reaction, confirming bidentate DG required

probe_reagents.name: enantioenriched ligand L1 (variable ee)
probe_reagents.observation: Linear correlation (slope 0.962, R^2=0.9968) between ee of L1 and ee of 3a, indicating a single Gd/L1 species delivers enantioinduction (no nonlinear effect)

10.1021_jacs.5c01506.notes: Light on/off experiments show reaction halts in the dark, consistent with a photocatalytic (not radical-chain) regime. UV-vis shows that only the Gd(III)+L1+naphthalene+BCB combination absorbs significantly in the visible region, supporting selective excitation of the Gd-bound naphthalene complex rather than free BCB or naphthalene. Linear effect study argues against aggregated Gd species. A singlet-only pathway is disfavored because triplet quenchers (O2, TEMPO, hexa-2,4-diene) strongly suppress the reaction; the authors propose ISC to a triplet biradical followed by a second ISC before ring closure.

10.1021_jacs.4c08290.rxn_class_summary: Photosensitized de novo deracemization synthesis of azaarene-functionalized cyclobutanes via enantioselective [2+2] photocycloaddition/photocycloreversion catalyzed by a chiral pyrenyl Brønsted acid
10.1021_jacs.4c08290.electron_flow: The chiral pyrenyl Brønsted acid C7 hydrogen-bonds to the racemic cyclobutane to form two diastereomeric ground-state complexes with different absorption energies; selective photoexcitation of the matched (lower-energy) complex drives [2+2] photocycloreversion back to the olefins, while the mismatched complex persists, enriching one enantiomer. SET between *pyr and the cyclobutane is thermodynamically feasible but ruled out as the productive pathway; energy-transfer/sensitized cycloreversion via a biradical-like excited cyclobutane is invoked.
10.1021_jacs.4c08290.rate_determining_step: Photocycloreversion of the matched *Cat-P_S diastereomeric complex (kinetic differentiation between the two diastereomeric excited complexes)

key_intermediates.name: racemic cyclobutane (±)-cycloadduct from [2+2] cycloaddition of azaarene-substituted alkene and vinylpyridine
diastereomeric complex *Cat-P_R (chiral Brønsted acid C7 H-bonded to one cyclobutane enantiomer, higher excitation energy)
diastereomeric complex *Cat-P_S (chiral Brønsted acid C7 H-bonded to the other cyclobutane enantiomer; bathochromically shifted, lower excitation energy, undergoes faster cycloreversion)
excited-state pyrenyl chromophore of C7 (*pyr) acting as photosensitizer/energy donor
regenerated olefin feedstocks (indolyl enone 1a and 2-vinylpyridine 2a) after cycloreversion of the matched diastereomer

probe_reagents.name: (PhO)2P(O)OH (achiral phosphoric acid in place of C7)
probe_reagents.observation: 64% yield of (±)-3a with 17% 1a and 18% 2a; no enantioenrichment, showing chirality of catalyst is required

probe_reagents.name: pyrene (in place of C7)
probe_reagents.observation: 63% (±)-3a, 20% 1a, 20% 2a; pyrene alone enables cycloreversion but without enantiocontrol

probe_reagents.name: (±)-C7 (racemic catalyst with enantiopure 3a)
probe_reagents.observation: Only 12% yield of (±)-3a from optically pure 3a, confirming matched-enantiomer cyclobutane is preferentially cleaved

probe_reagents.name: ent-3a vs 3a UV/vis with C7
probe_reagents.observation: Larger bathochromic shift for C7·ent-3a than C7·3a complex, evidencing thermodynamic differentiation of the two diastereomeric H-bonded complexes

probe_reagents.name: Stern–Volmer quenching of *pyr by 3a and by 3zm
probe_reagents.observation: 3a quenches *pyr (consistent with feasible SET, E*(S*/S•+) = -1.86 V vs E_p(3a) = -1.68 V), but 3zm (E_p = -2.46 V) does not quench yet still undergoes deracemization, arguing against SET as the productive cycloreversion pathway

probe_reagents.name: O2 tolerance test
probe_reagents.observation: Deracemization proceeds under O2 (E_red = -1.23 V), which would outcompete 3a for *pyr reduction if SET were operative — supporting energy-transfer/sensitization mechanism

probe_reagents.name: ee(C7) vs ee(3a) linear correlation
probe_reagents.observation: Linear correlation indicates a single molecule of C7 is involved in the stereocenter-ablating step

probe_reagents.name: Time-course / three-path kinetic study (1a+2a, (±)-3a, 3a 95% ee)
probe_reagents.observation: All three entry points converge to the same enantioenriched 3a, confirming reversible [2+2]/cycloreversion with kinetic differentiation

10.1021_jacs.4c08290.notes: Authors explicitly consider and disfavor a SET-based cycloreversion mechanism (*pyr oxidizing the cyclobutane) in favor of an energy-transfer/sensitization pathway operating through two diastereomeric H-bonded chiral-acid·cyclobutane complexes with distinct excitation energies (thermodynamic differentiation) and distinct cycloreversion rates (kinetic differentiation). Achiral phosphoric acid and free pyrene both promote racemic cycloreversion but give no ee, underscoring that the chiral pyrenyl Brønsted acid couples chromophore and chiral H-bond donor in one catalyst. Deracemization differs from classical photochemical deracemization in that two C(sp3)–C(sp3) bonds are simultaneously broken/reformed, enabling control of ≥2 stereocenters.

10.1021_jacs.0c11025.rxn_class_summary: Iron-catalyzed intermolecular aminoazidation of alkenes affording unprotected primary 2-azidoamines from hydroxylamine-derived aminating reagent and an azide source
10.1021_jacs.0c11025.electron_flow: An iron catalyst reductively cleaves the N–O bond of the hydroxylamine-derived reagent to generate an N-centered radical (or iron nitrenoid) that adds to the alkene; the resulting carbon-centered radical is then trapped by an iron-coordinated azide to deliver the 2-azidoamine.
10.1021_jacs.0c11025.rate_determining_step: C–N bond-forming addition of the N-centered radical/nitrenoid to the alkene (product-selective step inferred from Hammett ρ = -0.51)

key_intermediates.name: iron-bound aminating species from reductive cleavage of hydroxylamine reagent (putative iron nitrenoid or N-centered aminyl radical)
carbon-centered radical from aminyl radical addition to alkene
iron-coordinated azide (Fe-N3) that delivers azido group to C-radical

probe_reagents.name: phenyl cyclopropyl alkene (slow radical clock, 5a)
probe_reagents.observation: Ring not opened; intact product 5b (14%) plus methoxylated 5c (45%) in MeOH — radical short-lived

probe_reagents.name: fast radical clock (5d, phenyl vinyl cyclopropane)
probe_reagents.observation: Ring opens readily to give 5e (51%, 7:1 E/Z), confirming a C-centered radical intermediate

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: No TEMPO-adduct isolable, but azidoamine 5g yield drops from 67% to 16% with 1.0 equiv TEMPO — consistent with a short-lived radical

probe_reagents.name: MeOH solvent trap
probe_reagents.observation: Methoxylated byproduct 5c only forms at benzylic position (5a); non-benzylic alkene (5f) gives no methoxy adduct — argues against a free carbocation

probe_reagents.name: (E)- and (Z)-oct-4-ene (5h)
probe_reagents.observation: Stereoconvergent: both give similar dr of 5i (4:1 from E; 3:1 from Z); small amount of isomerized (E)-5h detected — supports stepwise radical addition with reversible C–N bond formation

probe_reagents.name: para-substituted styrenes (Hammett study)
probe_reagents.observation: ρ = -0.51 against σp (R² = 0.93), minor positive charge buildup; consistent with radical rather than carbocationic amination

10.1021_jacs.0c11025.notes: Authors explicitly contrast a radical-based mechanism vs a carbocationic one and favor the radical/iron-nitrenoid pathway based on (i) lack of MeOH trapping at non-benzylic positions, (ii) small negative Hammett ρ, (iii) radical-clock results, (iv) stereoconvergence of E/Z alkenes, and (v) TEMPO suppression. Compared to their prior aminochlorination, the lack of stereoretention here suggests a slightly longer-lived C-radical. Alternative mechanism mentioned: free carbocation pathway (ruled out except at benzylic positions where solvent trapping competes).

10.1021_jacs.3c12894.rxn_class_summary: photoredox-catalyzed dearomative [2π+2σ] cycloaddition of phenols with bicyclo[1.1.0]butanes to give bicyclo[2.1.1]hexane-fused cyclic enones
10.1021_jacs.3c12894.electron_flow: Visible-light-excited acridinium [PC1]* is reductively quenched by either the BCB (Path A, oxidation to radical cation I) or the phenol (Path B, oxidation to phenoxyl radical II); both pathways converge via Friedel–Crafts-type strain-release C–C bond formation to a common benzylic radical III, which undergoes 5-exo-trig cyclization to α-carbonyl radical IV, finally reduced by [PC1]•− to regenerate the photocatalyst and close the cycle.

key_intermediates.name: benzylic/ring-opened radical intermediate III (common to both paths)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: product 3a obtained in <5% yield, indicating involvement of radical intermediates

10.1021_jacs.3c12894.notes: Two mechanistic pathways are proposed and both are considered operative: Path A (oxidation of BCB 2a, E1/2 = 1.66 V vs SCE, thermodynamically slightly favored) and Path B (oxidation of phenol 1a, E1/2 = 1.70 V vs SCE, kinetically favored due to excess phenol). Stern–Volmer quenching shows BCB quenches [PC1]* ~1.4× more efficiently than phenol. Quantum yield Φ = 0.19 supports a closed catalytic (non-chain) cycle. UV–vis confirms PC1 is the only species absorbing at 450 nm, excluding direct substrate excitation.

10.1021_jacs.0c13077.rxn_class_summary: Photoredox/nickel dual-catalyzed three-component HAT-mediated dicarbofunctionalization (DCF) of alkenes, coupling an aryl halide, an activated alkene, and a C-H precursor (ether/alcohol/amide) via a diaryl ketone HAT catalyst.
10.1021_jacs.0c13077.electron_flow: Photoexcited diaryl ketone (triplet) abstracts a hydrogen atom from the C-H precursor to give a ketyl radical and an alpha-heteroatom alkyl radical; this radical undergoes Giese addition to the activated alkene, and the resulting stabilized radical is captured by a Ni(0)/Ni(II) species, oxidative addition/isomerization gives a Ni(III) aryl-alkyl intermediate that reductively eliminates the DCF product, and SET from the ketyl radical to Ni(I) regenerates both ground-state catalysts.
10.1021_jacs.0c13077.rate_determining_step: Selectivity-determining (kinetic) step is the competition between radical metalation to tetrahedral 3C (A-TS-B', 11.5 kcal/mol) versus Giese addition to the acrylate (A-TS-D, ~isoenergetic); HAT from C-H precursor by triplet ketone is the productive radical-forming step.

key_intermediates.name: Excited triplet diaryl ketone (benzophenone derivative) diradical II
Carbon-centered alkyl radical IV (alpha to heteroatom from C-H precursor)
Giese radical adduct VI (alkyl radical after addition to activated alkene)
Ni(III) intermediate X/E (productive tetrahedral, after radical capture)

probe_reagents.name: i-PrOH-d7 (deuterated isopropanol)
probe_reagents.observation: Intermolecular competition i-PrOH vs i-PrOH-d7 gave 3.6:1 ratio of 40 to 40-d6, consistent with exothermic radical C-H abstraction

probe_reagents.name: Irradiation control without benzophenone catalyst 1
probe_reagents.observation: No DCF product formed at 390 or 300 nm in absence of 1, ruling out direct alkene photoexcitation or bromide photolysis

probe_reagents.name: Ni(dtbbpy)(NO3)2 with 4-iodobenzonitrile (no Br source)
probe_reagents.observation: Good yield of DCF product obtained, ruling out bromide-radical-mediated HAT

probe_reagents.name: 1-Cyclopropylethanol (radical clock)
probe_reagents.observation: Major product (77, 44%) is two-component ring-opened cross-coupling with acrylonitrile; with t-Bu acrylate, cyclopropane-containing DCF 75 (38%) and [3+2] adduct 76 (15%) observed, evidencing alpha-cyclopropyl radical ring opening competitive with Giese addition

probe_reagents.name: CPME (cyclopentyl methyl ether) selectivity probe
probe_reagents.observation: Innate HAT selectivity favors secondary alpha-alkoxy radical; minor primary alpha-alkoxy radical CC byproduct observed

probe_reagents.name: MgCl2 (chaotrope to disrupt H-bonding)
probe_reagents.observation: Addition of MgCl2 restored high selectivity for hydroalkylation of acrylonitrile vs adamantyl acrylate with i-PrOH, attributed to disruption of H-bond between i-PrOH and acrylate

10.1021_jacs.0c13077.notes: Two competing Ni pathways were computed: blue pathway (radical addition to singlet square-planar 1C at axial position, A-TS-B, 12.8 kcal/mol) versus favored green pathway (radical addition to triplet tetrahedral 3C at equatorial position, A-TS-B', 11.5 kcal/mol). Alternative Ni(0)/Ni(I)/Ni(III) and Ni(0)/Ni(II)/Ni(III) cycles are both energetically feasible and converge on singlet 1C. DCF vs two-component CC selectivity is kinetically controlled by the relative energies of metalation (A-TS-B') vs Giese addition (A-TS-D), strongly modulated by hydrogen bonding between alpha-hydroxy radicals and acrylate carbonyl (locks hydroxyl orientation in Ni(III) intermediate 66 toward C=O, favoring reductive elimination/DCF). Tertiary radicals favor exclusive Giese/DCF (no direct metalation). DLPNO-CCSD(T)/def2-TZVPP//(U)B3LYP-D3/def2-SVP-CPCM(benzene) used for energetics.

10.1021_jacs.0c11896.rxn_class_summary: Visible-light photoredox-catalyzed redox-neutral carbocarboxylation of activated alkenes using α-amino acids (or alkyl carboxylic acids) as bifunctional reagents that provide both the alkyl radical and, via in situ released CO2, the carboxyl group to access γ-aminobutyric acids (GABAs).
10.1021_jacs.0c11896.electron_flow: Photoexcited *[Ir(III)] oxidizes the in situ–formed carboxylate 2a' (SET) to give an α-amino radical D and CO2 plus [Ir(II)]; D adds to the styrene 1a forming a stabilized benzylic radical E, which is reduced by [Ir(II)] back to [Ir(III)] (closing the cycle) to give a benzylic carbanion F that nucleophilically attacks the in situ–released CO2, furnishing carboxylate G and, upon workup, GABA product 3aa.
10.1021_jacs.0c11896.rate_determining_step: Not explicitly assigned; kinetic and Stern–Volmer data indicate that productive carboxylation competes with irreversible protodecarboxylation of 3aa to 3aa' (3aa is quenched ~5× more slowly than 2a, favoring decarboxylation of 2a).

key_intermediates.name: benzylic radical E (from radical addition of D to styrene 1a)
benzylic carbanion F (after SET reduction of E by [Ir(II)])
carboxylate G (after nucleophilic addition of F to in situ CO2)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Formation of 3aa and 3aa' strongly suppressed; α-amino radical–TEMPO adduct 7 detected by HRMS, supporting an α-amino radical intermediate.

probe_reagents.name: D2O
probe_reagents.observation: Up to 93% deuterium incorporation at the benzylic position of 3aa', consistent with a benzylic carbanion intermediate F.

probe_reagents.name: Aryl aldehydes (R-C6H4-CHO, R = CN, CF3, COOMe)
probe_reagents.observation: Three-component coupling delivers β-hydroxy products 8–10 in 86–92% yield, trapping the benzylic carbanion.

probe_reagents.name: α-(Trifluoromethyl)styrene 11
probe_reagents.observation: Radical/polar crossover defluorinative alkylation gives gem-difluoroalkene 12 (43%), consistent with carbanion-mediated β-fluoride elimination.

probe_reagents.name: 13C-labeled 2b (13COOH)
probe_reagents.observation: [13C]-3ab formed with 92% 13C incorporation, demonstrating that the carboxyl C of the product originates from the carboxylic acid starting material (via decarboxylation/CO2 reuptake).

probe_reagents.name: 3aa' as substrate (control)
probe_reagents.observation: Subjecting 3aa' + 2a to standard conditions gives no 3aa (only 3% rsm), excluding 3aa' as a productive intermediate for 3aa formation.

probe_reagents.name: 3aa decarboxylation control
probe_reagents.observation: 3aa decarboxylates to 3aa' (41%) under standard conditions in absence of 2a; presence of 2a strongly inhibits this decarboxylation (rsm 3aa = 90%).

10.1021_jacs.0c11896.notes: Stern–Volmer quenching of Ir[dF(CF3)(ppy)]2(dtbbpy)PF6 (λmax = 470 nm) shows carboxylate 2a' (formed with CsF) quenches much faster than 3aa, establishing the carboxylate (not the carboxylic acid) as the SET partner and rationalizing chemoselective decarboxylation of 2a over product 3aa. The CO2 in the productive step is in situ–generated and present at low concentration; the irreversible decarboxylation of 2a' and slower decarboxylation of carboxylate G are invoked to explain net productive carboxylation despite reversible CO2 addition to F. An alternative pathway in which 3aa' is the precursor to 3aa was explicitly excluded by control experiment (Scheme 2E).

10.1021_jacs.2c06273.rxn_class_summary: Photoelectrochemical (PEC) bromide-mediated epoxidation of alkenes on a BiVO4 photoanode using NaBr as redox mediator in H2O/CH3CN
10.1021_jacs.2c06273.electrochemical_role: Photogenerated holes at the BiVO4 photoanode oxidize Br- to Br2 (mediator); Br2 disproportionates in water to give the electrophilic [Br]+ (HBrO) that initiates alkene activation. Cathode performs HER, producing OH- needed for ring closure.
10.1021_jacs.2c06273.electron_flow: Light excites BiVO4; holes at the photoanode oxidize Br- to Br2, which disproportionates with H2O to HBrO/[Br]+ and HBr. [Br]+ adds to the alkene C=C to give a bromonium, water opens it to bromohydrin, and OH- (from cathodic HER) closes it to the epoxide while regenerating Br-.
10.1021_jacs.2c06273.rate_determining_step: Photoanodic oxidation of Br- to Br2 (mediator turnover), as suggested by current-limited epoxide accumulation under light-on/off control

probe_reagents.name: proton exchange membrane (two-compartment cell)
probe_reagents.observation: Anodic reaction stalls at bromohydrin 3 because OH- from the cathode cannot reach the anolyte, confirming bromohydrin as the on-cycle intermediate prior to OH--assisted cyclization

probe_reagents.name: dark control (no illumination)
probe_reagents.observation: No epoxide formed in the dark at 0.8 V; epoxide accumulates only under light, showing PEC (photo-driven) origin

probe_reagents.name: NaBr omission control
probe_reagents.observation: Only 8% epoxide yield without NaBr and water oxidation dominates; with NaBr, water oxidation is suppressed and epoxidation is selective

probe_reagents.name: NaClO4 substitution for NaBr
probe_reagents.observation: O2 evolution observed (water oxidation) instead of epoxidation, confirming Br- as the essential mediator

probe_reagents.name: GC headspace analysis
probe_reagents.observation: H2 quantified at cathode with ~100% FE; no O2 detected in sealed H-cell with NaBr, confirming suppression of OER

10.1021_jacs.2c06273.notes: Mechanism is adapted from established halide-mediated EC epoxidation (refs 28, 31). NaBr plays a dual role: redox mediator for epoxidation and hole scavenger that stabilizes BiVO4 against photocorrosion (loss of V5+). Dibromide (5) and diol (6) side pathways are drawn in Scheme 1 but not observed experimentally. Non-unity FE (65%) attributed to accumulation of unreacted Br2/HBrO rather than side reactions.

10.1021_ja016885b.rxn_class_summary: Electrocatalytic intermolecular formal [2+2] cycloaddition between aryl enol ethers and alkenes (including allyl-TMS and nonactivated alkenes) to give substituted cyclobutanes via anodic oxidation
10.1021_ja016885b.electrochemical_role: Anodic single-electron oxidation of the electron-rich (4-methoxyphenyl) enol ether to its radical cation, which initiates C-C bond formation with a neutral alkene; the product radical cation is regenerated/turned over by intermolecular disproportionation with starting enol ether (chain-like, sub-stoichiometric charge: ~0.5 F/mol)
10.1021_ja016885b.electron_flow: The anode oxidizes the 4-methoxyphenyl enol ether to a radical cation, which adds across the allyl-TMS (or other alkene) to give an acyclic distonic radical cation; ring-closure forms a cyclobutane radical cation, which is reduced back to neutral product by intermolecular electron transfer (disproportionation) from another enol ether, propagating the chain.
10.1021_ja016885b.rate_determining_step: Initial anodic SET oxidation of the methoxyphenyl-substituted enol ether (governed by Eox; only the 4-MeO-aryl substrate 3 reacts, not n-pentyl 1 or phenyl 2)

key_intermediates.name: open-chain distonic radical cation after C-C bond formation with allyl-TMS (β-silyl carbocation / C-radical hybrid)
cyclobutane radical cation bearing methoxyphenyl group (precursor to 5)

probe_reagents.name: on/off current interval electrolysis (stair-step potential application)
probe_reagents.observation: Product accumulates only during current application and is essentially constant during off intervals over 16 h, consistent with electrochemical initiation rather than a long-lived non-electrochemical chain

probe_reagents.name: dark control (electrolysis without light)
probe_reagents.observation: Product 5 forms equally under dark conditions, ruling out a photochemical [2+2] pathway

probe_reagents.name: pre-electrolyzed electrolyte then substrate addition (no current)
probe_reagents.observation: No product formed, excluding a long-lived electrogenerated oxidant doing the chemistry

probe_reagents.name: oxidation potential screen of enol ethers 1-3
probe_reagents.observation: Only 3 (Eox = 1.39 V, 4-MeO-C6H4) gives cycloadduct; 1 (1.48 V) and 2 (1.43 V) give none, showing the methoxyphenyl group is required to access the radical cation under these conditions

probe_reagents.name: nOe analysis of 5
probe_reagents.observation: All-trans stereochemistry of the cyclobutane established, consistent with stepwise (radical cation) rather than concerted [2+2]

probe_reagents.name: o-methoxyphenyl variant 12
probe_reagents.observation: Also gives cycloadducts 13/14, supporting role of methoxyaryl in intramolecular disproportionation step

10.1021_ja016885b.notes: Authors explicitly invoke intramolecular disproportionation between the cyclobutane radical cation and the pendant methoxyphenyl group to regenerate the enol-ether radical cation (Scheme 5), explaining catalytic turnover at ~0.5 F/mol. A concerted [2+2] is excluded by trans stereochemistry and by the dark control. Reaction fails without the electron-rich aryl group, indicating the aryl substituent is essential both for accessible Eox and for the redox relay step.

10.1021_jacs.3c08839_sup_1.rxn_class_summary: Decarboxylative coupling of aliphatic carboxylic acids with allylic/vinyl electrophiles proceeding through an alkyl radical intermediate
10.1021_jacs.3c08839_sup_1.electrochemical_role: anodic oxidation/decarboxylation of carboxylate to generate alkyl radical
10.1021_jacs.3c08839_sup_1.electron_flow: Carboxylic acid is deprotonated and anodically oxidized to release CO2 and an alkyl radical; the alkyl radical undergoes characteristic radical rearrangement (cyclopropyl ring opening or 5-exo-trig cyclization) before coupling with the allylic/vinyl electrophile partner.

key_intermediates.name: alkyl radical from decarboxylation of 2-cyclopropylacetic acid (cyclopropylcarbinyl)
ring-opened homoallyl radical from cyclopropylcarbinyl rearrangement
hex-5-enyl radical from decarboxylation of hept-6-enoic acid

probe_reagents.name: 2-cyclopropylacetic acid (radical clock)
probe_reagents.observation: ring-opened product 64 isolated in 57% yield, consistent with cyclopropylcarbinyl radical intermediate

probe_reagents.name: hept-6-enoic acid (radical clock)
probe_reagents.observation: 5-exo-trig cyclization product 66 with (E)-4-bromobut-3-en-1-yl benzoate dominates over direct coupling 67 (66:67 = 15:1), 67% combined yield

probe_reagents.name: hept-6-enoic acid with allylic tosylate S18
probe_reagents.observation: cyclization product S19 isolated in 52% yield, confirming radical intermediate with tosylate electrophile

10.1021_jacs.3c08839_sup_1.notes: Only radical clock probes shown on this page (Section 5.1); the high ratio of cyclized to uncyclized product (15:1) indicates a relatively long-lived/free alkyl radical rather than a tightly bound metal-alkyl species.
