10.1021_ja0171759.rxn_class_summary: Cathodic reduction of an electrochemically generated N-acyliminium 'cation pool' to a carbon-centered α-amido radical, which adds to activated olefins (e.g., methyl acrylate) to forge C–C bonds (formal C–H addition to C=C).
10.1021_ja0171759.electrochemical_role: Two-stage electrolysis in a divided cell: anodic oxidation of N-(methoxycarbonyl)pyrrolidine at -72 °C generates an acyliminium cation pool; subsequent cathodic one-electron reduction of the acyliminium cation produces an α-amido carbon radical that couples with the olefin, and a second cathodic reduction of the adduct radical gives a carbanion trapped by proton.
10.1021_ja0171759.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes the carbamate by 2e-/-H+ to give an acyliminium cation pool; at the cathode, one-electron reduction yields an α-amido radical that adds to the electron-deficient olefin (path A); the resulting electron-poor adduct radical is reduced by a second electron to a carbanion, which is protonated by added TfOH to give the cross-coupled product.

key_intermediates.name: α-amido carbon radical (3) from one-electron reduction of 2
α-amido carbanion (5) from two-electron reduction of 2
adduct radical after addition to methyl acrylate (8)
adduct carbanion (9) from further one-electron reduction of 8

probe_reagents.name: trifluoromethanesulfonic acid (TfOH) as proton source / carbanion trap
probe_reagents.observation: Increasing TfOH (0→50 equiv) raised yield of cross-coupled 6 from 34% to 88% and suppressed 2:1 adduct 7; yield of reduced 1 did not increase with excess TfOH, ruling out path B (carbanion 5 attacking olefin).

probe_reagents.name: zinc metal (chemical reductant control)
probe_reagents.observation: Chemical 2e- reduction gave reduced product 1 (54%, major) with only 16% homocoupled 4, indicating that under chemical reduction the carbanion pathway dominates, whereas electrochemical reduction favors the radical coupling pathway.

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of cation 2
probe_reagents.observation: Acyliminium cation 2 shows reduction wave at -0.85 V vs Ag/AgCl in Bu4NBF4/CH2Cl2, supporting feasibility of cathodic one-electron reduction.

probe_reagents.name: 1H NMR control with TfOH
probe_reagents.observation: TfOH did not appreciably affect iminium cation 2 by 1H NMR, excluding protonation/decomposition of the cation by the proton source.

10.1021_ja0171759.notes: Two mechanistic pathways were considered: favored path A involves one-electron cathodic reduction of acyliminium 2 to radical 3, radical addition to acrylate, then a second one-electron reduction to carbanion 9, and protonation. Alternative path B (two-electron reduction to carbanion 5 followed by Michael addition) was excluded because excess TfOH did not increase the yield of simple reduction product 1, which would be expected if 5 were a key intermediate. A competing homocoupling of radical 3 produces dimer 4, and oligomerization gives a 2:1 adduct 7; both are minimized at high TfOH (50 equiv) and 5 equiv acrylate.

10.1021_jacs.1c01487.rxn_class_summary: Nickel-electrocatalyzed single-electron reduction/activation of alkyl iodides to generate carbon-centered radicals, where ligand-controlled metal-ligand cooperativity dictates outer-sphere vs inner-sphere electron transfer and thus downstream radical selectivity (e.g., cyclization vs dimerization of a 5-hexenyl iodide).
10.1021_jacs.1c01487.electrochemical_role: Cathodic two-electron reduction of [Ni(tpyPY2Me)]2+ ([Ni-1]2+) to spin-delocalized [Ni-1], which acts as an OSET mediator to reduce alkyl iodide RI to R• + I-; alternatively, one-electron cathodic reduction of [Ni(PY5Me2)]2+ ([Ni-2]2+) to metal-localized [Ni-2]+, which activates RI via ISET (halogen atom abstraction) to form [(Ni-2)(I)]+ and R•.
10.1021_jacs.1c01487.electron_flow: For [Ni-1]2+: cathode delivers two sequential electrons to give spin-delocalized [Ni-1], which transfers an electron to RI by dissociative OSET, releasing R• and I-; R• rapidly rebounds onto [Ni-1]+ to form [(Ni-1)(R)], sequestering the radical and enabling selective onward chemistry. For [Ni-2]2+: cathode delivers one electron to give metal-localized [Ni-2]+, which abstracts I• from RI in an inner-sphere step to give [(Ni-2)(I)]+ plus a free R• at high concentration, leading to indiscriminate substrate activation and dimerization.
10.1021_jacs.1c01487.rate_determining_step: Homogeneous single-electron transfer from reduced Ni species to alkyl iodide (step k1: dissociative OSET cleavage of R-I for [Ni-1]; halogen atom abstraction step for [Ni-2]+).

key_intermediates.name: [Ni(tpyPY2Me)]2+ ([Ni-1]2+), Ni(II) resting state with redox-active polypyridyl ligand
[Ni-1]+, one-electron reduced species with spin delocalized over Ni and tpyPY2Me ligand (π*+dz2)
[Ni-1], doubly reduced spin-delocalized species (triplet, ligand-based π*), active OSET reductant
[(Ni-1)(R)] alkyl-Ni adduct formed by radical rebound, sequesters R•
[Ni(PY5Me2)]2+ ([Ni-2]2+), Ni(II) resting state with redox-innocent pentapyridine ligand
[Ni-2]+, one-electron reduced Ni(I), metal-localized dz2 spin, ISET activator
[(Ni-2)(I)]+, Ni(III)-iodide formed by halogen atom abstraction from RI
Primary alkyl radical R• (e.g., 5-hexenyl radical from substrate 11)

probe_reagents.name: Dimethyl 2-butyl-(iodomethyl)malonate (3, activated primary RI)
probe_reagents.observation: Activated by [Ni-1] at -1.94 V (0.58 V positive of direct reduction); k1 ~700 M-1 s-1; log k1–E1/2 plot linear with Ar•- mediators, consistent with OSET.

probe_reagents.name: Cyclohexyl iodide (CyI, secondary RI)
probe_reagents.observation: Activated ~5-fold slower than 3 by [Ni-1]; k1 matches reported value for 2-iodobutane; log k1–E1/2 linear, consistent with OSET. With [Ni-2]+, activated ~100-fold faster than by analogous Ar•- (inconsistent with OSET, supporting ISET).

probe_reagents.name: Organic radical-anion mediators 4–10 (e.g., aromatic nitriles, fluoranthene, anthracene derivatives)
probe_reagents.observation: Span ~400 mV in E1/2; log k1 vs E1/2 is linear for RI activation, defining the OSET Hush–Marcus baseline that [Ni-1] follows but [Ni-2]+ deviates from by ~10–100×.

probe_reagents.name: 5-Hexenyl iodide substrate 11 (radical clock)
probe_reagents.observation: Under [Ni-1]2+ catalysis (-2.03 V, 1 F/mol): 31% cyclized H-quenched 13 and 17% iodocyclized 14, indicating discrete radical intermediate; under [Ni-2]2+ (-1.91 V): 12% 13 and 38% dimer 15, indicating higher free-radical concentration and dimerization.

probe_reagents.name: CyI as exogenous halogen atom donor (selectivity probe)
probe_reagents.observation: Added to [Ni-1]2+/11 electrolysis: 75% iodocyclized 14 with only 1 F/mol, demonstrating selective activation of 11 over CyI and trapping of cyclized radical by I• transfer. With [Ni-2]2+/11+CyI: indiscriminate activation, 3 F/mol consumed, complex product mixture.

probe_reagents.name: NaOTf (counterion / coordination probe)
probe_reagents.observation: Addition to [Ni-1]2+/11 electrolysis switches selectivity to 46% dimer 15, supporting that OTf- blocks [(Ni-1)(R)] formation and releases free R•, implicating Ni-R rebound in controlling selectivity.

probe_reagents.name: Pre-electrogenerated [Ni-1] then add 11 with no applied bias (e-[Ni-1] control)
probe_reagents.observation: <4% cyclized products, ruling out non-Faradaic ATR chain pathway and confirming electrocatalytic turnover.

probe_reagents.name: Independently synthesized [Ni(PY5Me2)(I)]+
probe_reagents.observation: Exhibits E1/2 = -1.75 V and supports catalysis from this entry point, giving the same dimerization-favoring product distribution as [Ni-2]2+; confirms [(Ni-2)(I)]+ as a catalytically viable intermediate in the ISET cycle.

probe_reagents.name: Ar 8 (organic radical anion comparison reductant)
probe_reagents.observation: CPE of 11 with 8 gives exclusive dimer 15, showing that absent Ni-R rebound, free-radical fate is dimerization.

10.1021_jacs.1c01487.notes: Two divergent mechanisms are explicitly contrasted in one paper. Favored pictures: (i) [Ni-1] (tpyPY2Me) activates RI by dissociative OSET with subsequent radical rebound to give [(Ni-1)(R)], controlled radical release; (ii) [Ni-2]+ (PY5Me2) activates RI by ISET halogen atom abstraction to give [(Ni-2)(I)]+ and free R•. Alternative OSET pathway without radical rebound for [Ni-1] is ruled out by saturation of catalytic current with [RI] and by OTf--induced switch to dimerization. SN2-type activation for [Ni-1] is ruled out by agreement of k1 with Hush–Marcus OSET line for organic Ar•- mediators. DFT (ωB97X-D) shows [Ni-1]+ has mixed π*+dz2 SOMO (35% Ni), [Ni-1] is a triplet with ligand-based π* electron (spin delocalized), while [Ni-2]+ has a purely dz2 metal-localized SOMO, rationalizing ISET vs OSET selectivity through metal–ligand cooperativity.

10.1021_jacs.4c03087.rxn_class_summary: Photoenzyme-catalyzed enantioselective [2+2] cycloaddition of indoles using a thioxanthone-based photosensitizer embedded in an engineered protein scaffold (CMP4.0)
10.1021_jacs.4c03087.electron_flow: Visible-light excitation of the protein-bound thioxanthone (3c) produces a long-lived triplet state whose nπ* character disfavors H-abstraction; triplet–triplet energy transfer to the π-stacked indole 1a generates an excited indole/biradical that undergoes enantioselective [2+2] cycloaddition within the chiral active site.
10.1021_jacs.4c03087.rate_determining_step: Unimolecular photochemical step on bound substrate (kcat linearly dependent on light intensity); Michaelis–Menten kinetics with KM = 36.69 µM and kcat = 11.28 min⁻¹

key_intermediates.name: thioxanthone photosensitizer 3c triplet excited state (T1, nπ*/ππ* mixed)
triplet biradical from energy transfer to indole substrate 1a
CMP4.0·1a ground-state complex (π–π stacked at 3.9 Å, enantioface-selective)

probe_reagents.name: Michaelis–Menten kinetic analysis
probe_reagents.observation: Saturation kinetics for CMP1.0 (kcat/KM = 209.47) and CMP4.0 (kcat/KM = 306.88 mM⁻¹ min⁻¹); kcat linear in light intensity → unimolecular photochemical RDS

probe_reagents.name: Photosensitizer comparison (CMP4.0 405 nm vs CMP4.0′ 365 nm vs free 3c 405 nm)
probe_reagents.observation: Bound 3c in CMP4.0 gives much higher yield than free 3c, confirming active-site catalysis

probe_reagents.name: UV-vis and photoluminescence spectroscopy
probe_reagents.observation: PL of CMP4.0 at 451 nm vs free 3c at 471 nm; quantum yield 17% vs 7%; longer τ2 (6144 ns vs 536 ns) indicating stabilized excited state in the protein

probe_reagents.name: Time-resolved emission decay (kinetic traces)
probe_reagents.observation: Double-exponential fit: CMP4.0 τ1=139.95 ns, τ2=6144.62 ns vs 3c τ1=2.95 ns, τ2=536.54 ns — protein matrix dramatically lengthens triplet lifetime

probe_reagents.name: X-ray crystallography (PDB 8ZF1) + docking
probe_reagents.observation: Two photosensitizers 5.8 Å apart; substrate 1a π-stacked at 3.9 Å with one face shielded, rationalizing enantioinduction

10.1021_jacs.4c03087.notes: Authors invoke energy transfer rather than SET; the nπ*-character triplet of thioxanthone is invoked to explain higher photostability vs benzophenone-based photoenzymes (which suffer H-abstraction via ππ* triplets). Alternative SET/radical-cation pathway is not explicitly probed by TEMPO/BHT trapping or EPR; mechanism inferred largely from photophysics, crystal structure, docking, and kinetics. Loading reduced to 0.25 mol% with >350 turnovers and ≥95:5 e.r.

10.1021_jacs.3c10109.rxn_class_summary: Ni-catalyzed enantioselective electroreductive cross-coupling (eRCC) of acrylates with aryl halides and alkyl bromides to give alpha-aryl carbonyls in an undivided cell with sacrificial Fe anode
10.1021_jacs.3c10109.electrochemical_role: Cathodic reduction (Ni foam) regenerates low-valent Ni species (Ni(II)->Ni(I) and Ar-Ni(III)->Ar-Ni(II)); Fe sacrificial anode is oxidized to Fe2+ to balance charge
10.1021_jacs.3c10109.electron_flow: Cathode reduces Ni(II) precatalyst to Ni(I) (A), which undergoes oxidative addition with ArCl to give Ar-Ni(III); cathodic reduction yields Ar-Ni(II) (B). In parallel, Ni(I) activates alkyl bromide by SET to release an alkyl radical that adds to the acrylate giving radical C, which is captured by B to form Ar-Ni(III)-alkyl D; reductive elimination delivers the product and Ni(I) (E), while Fe anode is oxidized to Fe2+.

key_intermediates.name: Ar-Ni(III) species (oxidative addition adduct of ArCl to Ni(I))
alkyl radical (tert-butyl radical from t-BuBr via SET with Ni(I))
alpha-ester C-radical intermediate C (alkyl radical addition to acrylate)
Dimeric Ni/BiIM species Ni-1 (two BiIM ligands per Ni, characterized by XRD)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Product 4a not formed (0% yield), consistent with radical intermediates

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: Product 4a not formed (0% yield)

probe_reagents.name: Mn
probe_reagents.observation: No product without electric current; underscores requirement of electrochemistry

probe_reagents.name: Zn
probe_reagents.observation: No product without electric current

probe_reagents.name: TDAE (tetrakis(dimethylamino)ethylene)
probe_reagents.observation: No product as chemical reductant alone

probe_reagents.name: tert-butyl bromide (3a) added to NiBr2.glyme/L1 in CV
probe_reagents.observation: Reduction peak of Ni(II)/Ni(I) considerably increased, indicating SET from Ni(I) to alkyl bromide generates alkyl radicals

probe_reagents.name: aryl chloride 1a added in CV
probe_reagents.observation: Oxidation peak at -0.59 V disappears with increasing 1a; consistent with oxidative addition of ArCl to Ni(I)

probe_reagents.name: Controlled potential electrolysis
probe_reagents.observation: No product at -1.0 V; 68% yield/94% ee at -1.8 V; 27% at -2.2 V, supporting Ni(I) (not Ni(0)) as active species

probe_reagents.name: Electrolysis without catalyst
probe_reagents.observation: No radical addition product detected (Fig 1G), excluding direct cathodic generation of alkyl radicals

probe_reagents.name: Nonlinear effect study with chiral L1
probe_reagents.observation: Positive nonlinear effect (ee_prod > ee_L1), indicating a dimeric Ni species with two BiIM ligands is involved in the enantio-determining step

10.1021_jacs.3c10109.notes: Authors favor Ni(I)/Ni(III) cycle (rather than Ni(0)/Ni(II)) based on CV showing oxidation peak disappearance upon ArCl addition and constant-potential electrolysis at -1.87 V (matching Ni(II)/Ni(I) couple). Positive nonlinear effect suggests a dimeric Ni-BiIM2 species participates in the stereo-determining step; a dimeric Ni species (Ni-1) was crystallographically characterized. Fe anode is sacrificial (oxidized to Fe2+). Chemical reductants (Mn, Zn, TDAE) fail under otherwise identical conditions, highlighting unique role of cathodic reduction.

10.1021_jacs.2c05126.rxn_class_summary: Cu-electrocatalyzed 1,2-diazidation of alkenes using TMSN3 as azide source under anodic oxidation
10.1021_jacs.2c05126.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of Cu(II) azide to Cu(III) azide, which homolyzes to release an azide radical that initiates alkene azidation; Cu(I) azide is reoxidized at the anode
10.1021_jacs.2c05126.electron_flow: At the anode, Cu(II) azide is oxidized to Cu(III) azide which releases an azide radical; the N3• adds reversibly to the alkene to give a β-azido C-radical that is captured by Cu(II)(N3)2 via inner-sphere ligand transfer to deliver the second N3 and regenerate Cu(I), which is reoxidized at the anode (with H2 evolution at the cathode).

key_intermediates.name: ring-opened homoallylic radical from cyclopropane probe 43
cyclized pyrrolidinyl radical 46 from diene probe

probe_reagents.name: vinylcyclopropane 41 (radical clock)
probe_reagents.observation: Ring-opened (E)-1,4-diazide 42 formed in 15%, supporting a β-azido C-radical intermediate 43

probe_reagents.name: N-tosyl allyl-methallyl amine 44 (radical cyclization probe)
probe_reagents.observation: Cyclized pyrrolidine 45 obtained in 21% (2:1 dr), supporting alkyl radical intermediate 46

probe_reagents.name: N-allyl benzamide 47 (carbocation probe)
probe_reagents.observation: Only acyclic diazide 48 (59%) formed; no oxazoline 49 from cation cyclization, arguing against carbocation 51

probe_reagents.name: jasmone-type Z-alkene 52 (Z/E scrambling probe)
probe_reagents.observation: Recovered alkene 52 isomerized from 10:1 Z/E to 1:1.1 Z/E at 73% conversion, indicating reversible N3• addition to the alkene

probe_reagents.name: stoichiometric Cu(acac)2 without electricity
probe_reagents.observation: <10% conversion, no diazide 2 formed; Cu(II) azide alone is not competent — higher-valent Cu(III) needed

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of Cu(acac)2 with TMSN3/K3PO4 ± alkene
probe_reagents.observation: Cu(II/III) wave observed; addition of alkene did not increase Cu(II/III) peak current, arguing against fast direct ligand transfer from Cu(III) to alkene

10.1021_jacs.2c05126.notes: Authors do not exclude a minor pathway where N3• is generated by direct anodic oxidation of azide. For aryl alkenes, a benzylic carbocation pathway may operate due to easier oxidation of benzylic radicals, even though alkyl alkenes (probe 47) show no carbocation behavior. Reversible azide radical addition to alkenes (jasmone E/Z scrambling) is explicitly invoked.

10.1021_jacs.4c02284.rxn_class_summary: Photoredox/nickel dual-catalyzed three-component 1,2-dialkylation of unactivated alkenes with alkyl halides and hypervalent iodine(III) reagents via bimolecular homolytic substitution (SH2).
10.1021_jacs.4c02284.electron_flow: Visible-light excitation of 4-CzIPN generates a triplet that triplet-triplet energy-transfers to the iodine(III) reagent, homolyzing I-O to release a nucleophilic 1° alkyl radical (i, +CO2, +PhI); i abstracts iodine from the electrophilic alkyl iodide (XAT) to give an electrophilic alkyl radical ii, which adds regioselectively to the unactivated alkene to give congested radical iii; meanwhile i is selectively captured by Tp*Ni(II) to form Tp*Ni(III)(alkyl) v, which undergoes outer-sphere SH2 with iii to forge the second C(sp3)-C(sp3) bond and release Ni(I)/Ni(II) for turnover.
10.1021_jacs.4c02284.rate_determining_step: Outer-sphere bimolecular homolytic substitution (SH2) of secondary C-radical iii at the Tp*Ni(III)-alkyl species v to forge the second C(sp3)-C(sp3) bond (proposed key selectivity-determining step).

key_intermediates.name: carboxyl radical from I-O homolysis of iodine(III) reagent (fragments to CO2 + alkyl radical)
nucleophilic primary alkyl radical (i, e.g. methyl radical from PhI(OAc)2)
electrophilic alkyl radical ii (e.g. •CH2CF3 from ICH2CF3 via XAT)
sterically congested secondary C-radical iii from addition of ii to alkene
Tp*Ni(III)(Me)(R) complex (v) formed by radical capture of i by Ni(II)

probe_reagents.name: exogenous haloarene (4-bromoiodoarene)
probe_reagents.observation: Haloarene fully recovered and reaction unaffected (84% 4a), arguing against low-valent Ni(0)/Ni(I) that would oxidatively insert into Ar-X.

probe_reagents.name: methyl iodide (in place of PhI(OAc)2)
probe_reagents.observation: No dialkylation detected, showing the second C(sp3)-C(sp3) bond does not form via Ni insertion into Me-I and that the Me source must come from the iodine(III) reagent.

probe_reagents.name: dicumyl peroxide (alternative methyl radical source)
probe_reagents.observation: Affords 4a in 14% yield, supporting that photolytically generated Me• can engage in XAT with ICH2CF3.

probe_reagents.name: PhI(O2CEt)2 with ICH2CN
probe_reagents.observation: Gives product 5m (45%) along with EtI (110%), confirming chemoselective XAT in which the ethyl radical from the I(III) reagent abstracts iodine from the electrophilic alkyl iodide.

10.1021_jacs.4c02284.notes: Authors explicitly disfavor a low-valent Ni(0)/Ni(I) oxidative addition pathway based on the haloarene control. Polarity matching (nucleophilic 1° radical preferentially abstracts I from electrophilic EWG-CH2-I) and steric differentiation by Tp*Ni (preferring the unhindered 1° alkyl radical) are invoked to rationalize chemoselectivity. Ring-opening of α-pinene/caryophyllene oxide substrates serves as an implicit radical clock supporting cyclobutylcarbinyl-radical intermediacy after initial electrophilic radical addition. No KIE, EPR, DFT, or CV reported in the main text.

10.1021_jacs.3c06794_sup_1.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical 1,2-dialkylation of alkenes with a tertiary alkyl bromide (tBuBr) and a primary alkyl bromide via radical addition/cathodic reduction to a carbanion, followed by SN2 alkylation.
10.1021_jacs.3c06794_sup_1.electrochemical_role: Cathodic reduction generates a tert-butyl radical from tBuBr; after radical addition to the alkene, the resulting benzylic/stabilized C-radical is further reduced at the cathode to a carbanion, which then undergoes SN2 with the primary alkyl bromide. Mg sacrificial anode balances charge.
10.1021_jacs.3c06794_sup_1.electron_flow: Cathode reduces tBuBr to tBu radical, which adds to the alkene; the resulting stabilized C-radical is reduced again at the cathode to a carbanion that performs SN2 on the primary alkyl bromide. The Mg anode is sacrificially oxidized to balance charge.

key_intermediates.name: tert-butyl radical from cathodic reduction of tBuBr
benzylic/stabilized C-radical after tBu addition to alkene
ring-opened homoallyl radical from cyclopropane radical clock 59

probe_reagents.name: (2-phenylcyclopropyl)ethylene radical clock 59
probe_reagents.observation: Ring-opened product 60 (1-chloro-9,9-dimethyldec-6-en-4-yl)benzene isolated in 18% (7:1 E/Z), confirming a carbon radical intermediate after tBu addition.

probe_reagents.name: D2O
probe_reagents.observation: Product 61 obtained in 8% yield with 82% D incorporation at the benzylic position, consistent with a carbanion intermediate quenched by D2O.

10.1021_jacs.3c06794_sup_1.notes: DFT (M06-2X/6-311+G(d,p), SMD(DME)) supports exergonic, mostly irreversible tBu• addition to π-conjugated alkenes (ΔG‡ ≈ 10.4–12.6 kcal/mol) and an SN2 mechanism for the second C–C bond formation with the primary alkyl bromide; the radical/anion-stabilizing nature of group Z dictates reactivity, with vinyl silanes/phosphines/sulfides being less reactive.

10.1021_jacs.3c00753.rxn_class_summary: PPTS-promoted divergent conversion of a grayanane-type intermediate 11 into 1-epi-33 and 33 via competing Grob fragmentation/carbonyl-ene and E1 elimination pathways, applied to total syntheses of grayanoids
10.1021_jacs.3c00753.electron_flow: PPTS hydrogen-bonds to 11 (INT1) and promotes heterolytic C1-OAc cleavage through TS1 to give the ion pair INT2; INT2 either undergoes deprotonation-triggered Grob fragmentation of the C1-C5 bond (TS2) to macrocycle 10A, which then collapses by concerted transannular carbonyl-ene cyclization (TS4) to 1-epi-33, or isomerizes to INT3 and undergoes E1 elimination (TS3) to give 33.
10.1021_jacs.3c00753.rate_determining_step: Either Grob fragmentation via TS2 (ΔG‡ = 35.9 kcal/mol) for the 1-epi-33 pathway or E1 elimination via TS3 (ΔG‡ = 37.7 kcal/mol) for the 33 pathway; the close barriers rationalize the observed ~2.5:1 dr

key_intermediates.name: INT1, hydrogen-bonded complex of 11 with PPTS (pyridinium tosylate and acetate counterion network)
INT2, ion-paired oxocarbenium/carbocationic intermediate after C-OAc heterolysis via TS1
INT3, higher-energy isomeric ion pair in equilibrium with INT2, leading to E1 elimination
10A, macrocyclic Grob fragmentation product bearing Δ1,10-alkene
10B, reactive conformer of 10A leading to 1-epi-33 via TS4
10C, reactive conformer of 10A leading to 5-epi-33 via TS5 (disfavored)
33, E1 elimination/Prins-type product from INT3 via TS3

probe_reagents.name: BF3·Et2O (Lewis acid screen)
probe_reagents.observation: Substantial decomposition of 11 at 0 °C in CH2Cl2

probe_reagents.name: TsOH·H2O
probe_reagents.observation: <5% of both 1-epi-33 and 33 in toluene at 110 °C

probe_reagents.name: PPTS (pyridinium p-toluenesulfonate)
probe_reagents.observation: Best conditions: 2.5:1 1-epi-33:33 in 80% combined yield at 110 °C, toluene

probe_reagents.name: Py·HCl
probe_reagents.observation: Diminished yield (18% of 33), supporting role of tosylate counterion

probe_reagents.name: 2,6-lutidine·TsOH
probe_reagents.observation: Low yields (9% 1-epi-33, 20% 33); ortho-substitution on pyridinium disrupts H-bond network

probe_reagents.name: 3,5-lutidine·TsOH
probe_reagents.observation: Diminished yields (16% 1-epi-33, 24% 33), implicating meta-H involvement

probe_reagents.name: para-substituted pyridinium tosylate variants (Table S2 entries 25, 26)
probe_reagents.observation: Electron-deficient pyridinium tunes acetate basicity and biases pathway selectivity, validating the H-bond-weaved counterion model

probe_reagents.name: DFT (ωB97X-V-SMD(toluene)/def2-TZVPP//B3LYP-D4-CPCM(toluene)/def2-SVP)
probe_reagents.observation: Located INT1/INT2/INT3 and TS1-TS5; predicts ΔG‡(TS2)=35.9 vs ΔG‡(TS3)=37.7 kcal/mol and ΔΔG‡(TS5-TS4)=4.0 kcal/mol explaining absence of 5-epi-33

10.1021_jacs.3c00753.notes: Alternative pathway considered: direct PPTS-promoted Prins cyclization of 10A to 33 was computed to be kinetically unfavorable (ΔG‡ = 37.7 kcal/mol, Figure S3), so 33 is assigned exclusively to the E1 elimination route from INT3. The diastereoselectivity of the transannular carbonyl-ene step is rationalized by an eclipsed (disfavored, TS5, leads to 5-epi-33) vs staggered (favored, TS4, leads to 1-epi-33) transition state with torsional strain developing as the C6-H/C5-O dihedral rotates from -23.1° to +22.1°. The hydrogen-bond-weaved PPTS-acetate counterion is invoked as the key organizing element enabling pathway divergence.

10.1021_jacs.9b05572.rxn_class_summary: Visible-light photocatalytic remote double functionalization of 5-aryl-1,2,4-oxadiazolines to spiro-azalactams via energy-transfer-generated iminyl radicals and singlet oxygen.
10.1021_jacs.9b05572.electron_flow: fac-Ir(ppy)3 is photoexcited and undergoes triplet-triplet energy transfer in parallel to both the oxadiazoline 1a (giving 1a*) and to O2 (giving 1O2); 1O2 inserts at C5 of 1a* to form excited hydroperoxide D*, whose homolytic N–O cleavage releases HOO• and an iminyl radical A that performs ipso attack on the pendant arene, with the resulting spirocyclic radical E trapped by O2/HOO• at the para position to give 3a after loss of water.
10.1021_jacs.9b05572.rate_determining_step: Homolytic N–O bond cleavage of the excited hydroperoxy intermediate D* generating iminyl radical A (DFT TS1 N–O ≈ 24.9 kcal/mol on the favored pathway).

probe_reagents.name: 18O2 isotopic labeling
probe_reagents.observation: Product 3a exclusively contained 18O, confirming O2 as the oxygen source incorporated at the para position.

probe_reagents.name: SmI2 (chemical one-electron reductant)
probe_reagents.observation: No 3a formed (0%); 1a recovered (94%), excluding a reductive SET pathway via radical anion B.

probe_reagents.name: Ceric ammonium nitrate (CAN)
probe_reagents.observation: Gave only 2a (20%) and trace 3a, ruling out oxidative SET pathway via radical cation C.

probe_reagents.name: Benzil (triplet sensitizer, ET = 2.57 eV)
probe_reagents.observation: Afforded 3a (32%), supporting triplet-triplet energy-transfer activation of 1a.

probe_reagents.name: 1,3-Diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF)
probe_reagents.observation: Bleaching of DPBF absorbance confirmed 1O2 generation by fac-Ir(ppy)3; ΦΔ = 0.95.

probe_reagents.name: DABCO (1O2 quencher)
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction completely suppressed (0% 3a), confirming requirement of singlet oxygen.

probe_reagents.name: NaN3 (1O2 quencher)
probe_reagents.observation: Yield reduced to 33%, consistent with 1O2 involvement.

probe_reagents.name: Sterically hindered 2,6-disubstituted substrate 1b
probe_reagents.observation: No 3b formed; ipso attack sterically disfavored, supporting iminyl radical ipso cyclization.

probe_reagents.name: Chemical 1O2 sources (NaOH/Na2MoO4/H2O2 or Ca(OH)2/H2O2)
probe_reagents.observation: No 3a formed (1a recovered 97%), showing 1a* (not ground-state 1a) is required.

probe_reagents.name: Benzoyl peroxide (PhCO2)2
probe_reagents.observation: Only 10% 3a with 31% 2a side product, demonstrating need for visible-light photocatalysis.

probe_reagents.name: Light on/off and photon flux variation
probe_reagents.observation: No dark reactivity; nonlinear photon-flux dependence consistent with two-photon excitation of both 1a and O2.

probe_reagents.name: Ferrioxalate actinometry
probe_reagents.observation: Quantum yield 16–18% (<50%), excluding radical chain propagation.

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of 1a / fac-Ir(ppy)3
probe_reagents.observation: Eox(1a)=+1.24 V, Ered(1a)=−2.16 V vs SCE vs E*red(Ir)=+0.16 V and E*ox(Ir)=−1.62 V — both SET directions thermodynamically forbidden.

10.1021_jacs.9b05572.notes: Authors favor dual energy-transfer (Dexter to 1a, confirmed by ln(ET rate) ∝ [1a]^(−1/3); Förster-like to O2) producing 1a* and 1O2 that combine to D*, then homolytic N–O cleavage to iminyl radical A and ipso arene attack. An alternative pathway (ortho-hydroperoxidation of spiro radical E followed by [1,3]-sigmatropic shift via intermediate F) is mentioned as plausible based on DFT spin densities (para 0.605 vs ortho 0.415). Electron-transfer pathways via radical cation C or radical anion B are explicitly excluded by redox potentials and by SmI2/CAN control experiments.

10.1021_jacs.2c08951.rxn_class_summary: Electrophotocatalytic regiodivergent 1,2-aminooxygenation of aryl olefins to oxazolines using water or urethane as the first nucleophile and acetonitrile as the second (Ritter-type) nucleophile
10.1021_jacs.2c08951.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of trisaminocyclopropenium (TAC) catalyst to its radical dication, which upon photoexcitation becomes a strongly oxidizing species (E*red = 3.3 V vs SCE) that performs SET oxidation of the aryl alkene to a radical cation
10.1021_jacs.2c08951.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes TAC+ to TAC2+•, which is photoexcited to a super-oxidant that performs SET on the aryl alkene to give a radical cation; nucleophilic addition of water (or urethane) yields a benzylic radical that is further oxidized (by TAC2+• or the anode) to a benzylic cation; acetonitrile traps it as a nitrilium that cyclizes via the pendant hydroxyl (or carbamate carbonyl) to the oxazoline, with H2 evolved at the cathode.

key_intermediates.name: TAC radical cation (catalyst, one-electron oxidized form)
beta-hydroxy benzylic radical (65, after water addition)
beta-carbamate benzylic radical (70, after urethane addition)
Oxazoline product 68 (from water pathway, Ritter cyclization)
Oxazoline product 71 (from urethane pathway, carbamate O cyclization)

probe_reagents.name: excess water (stoichiometry probe)
probe_reagents.observation: Large excess of water diverts the reaction to 1,2-diol 69 and produces benzaldehyde, indicating water can outcompete MeCN as the second nucleophile; minimizing water restores selectivity for oxazoline 68

probe_reagents.name: methyl carbamate (alternative nucleophile)
probe_reagents.observation: Replaces water in the first nucleophilic addition step, giving the methyl analogue of 11 and supporting the radical-cation trapping step

probe_reagents.name: urethane (ethyl carbamate)
probe_reagents.observation: Outcompetes acetonitrile in the first addition, leading to regiodivergent oxazoline 71 via carbamate-oxygen cyclization, consistent with the proposed cation/radical-cation intermediates

probe_reagents.name: cyclic olefin substrate
probe_reagents.observation: Gave substantial diamination byproduct alongside oxazoline, indicating competing pathways for constrained substrates

10.1021_jacs.2c08951.notes: Regiodivergence is controlled simply by choice of first nucleophile (water vs urethane); both pathways share initial alkene radical-cation generation by photoexcited TAC dication. With cyclic olefins a competing diamination pathway is observed. Excess water diverts to diol/benzaldehyde, showing the second nucleophilic addition is the selectivity-determining step. No KIE, radical-trap (TEMPO/BHT), or DFT data are reported on these pages.

10.1021_ja806259z.rxn_class_summary: Anodic oxidative cyclization of tosyl-sulfonamide-tethered enol ethers, thioenol ethers, allylsilanes, and styrenes to form proline (pyrrolidine) derivatives via olefin radical cation intermediates trapped by the nitrogen nucleophile
10.1021_ja806259z.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation (RVC anode, Pt cathode) of the electron-rich olefin (enol ether/thioenol ether/styrene/allylsilane) to a one-electron olefin radical cation; LiOMe/n-BuLi deprotonates the sulfonamide N-H to enhance nucleophilicity for intramolecular trapping
10.1021_ja806259z.electron_flow: Anode removes one electron from the electron-rich olefin to give a radical cation (resonance: olefin radical cation with N-anion, or N-centered radical with olefin); intramolecular C-N bond formation gives a C-centered radical (7), which is oxidized at the anode to a cation (8), then trapped by methanol to give the proline product.
10.1021_ja806259z.rate_determining_step: Intramolecular cyclization of the radical cation by the deprotonated sulfonamide nitrogen (rate enhanced by LiOMe-mediated N-H deprotonation); under fast cyclization, methanol trapping of cation 8 must also be rapid to preserve yield

key_intermediates.name: olefin radical cation of enol/thioenol ether substrate (6)
C-centered cyclized radical (7) after C-N bond formation
alpha-alkoxy/thio carbocation (8) formed by second anodic oxidation of 7
methoxylated pyrrolidine product (4) after MeOH trapping and deprotonation

probe_reagents.name: methanol vs 30% methanol/THF solvent variation
probe_reagents.observation: Switching from MeOH to 30% MeOH/THF dropped yield of 4g from 83% to 28%, indicating that rapid trapping of cation 8 by MeOH is required after fast cyclization

probe_reagents.name: LiOMe (basic additive)
probe_reagents.observation: Improved cyclization yields by deprotonating the sulfonamide N-H, increasing N nucleophilicity

probe_reagents.name: less polar thioenol ether substrate (3b/3f)
probe_reagents.observation: Less polar radical cation gave higher yields, supporting a discrete radical cation intermediate

probe_reagents.name: lowered electrolyte concentration (0.03 M Et4NOTs)
probe_reagents.observation: Suppressed over-oxidation/elimination side path; raised yield of 4c to 50% and 4d to 73%

probe_reagents.name: allylsilane substrate 9a
probe_reagents.observation: Anodic oxidation of allylsilane initiated cyclization in 90% yield, consistent with radical cation generation on electron-rich olefin

probe_reagents.name: styrene substrate 9b
probe_reagents.observation: Oxidation of styrene moiety triggered cyclization (88%), supporting generality of olefin radical cation mechanism

10.1021_ja806259z.notes: Two resonance descriptions are given for intermediate 6: (a) olefin radical cation trapped by a nitrogen anion, or (b) a nitrogen-centered radical adding to the electron-rich olefin; the authors treat these as resonance forms rather than distinct mechanisms. Product 4g is formed as a single diastereomer, consistent with prior cyclizations using alcohol nucleophiles and ketene dithioacetals, suggesting a well-defined stereochemistry-determining cyclization transition state. No TEMPO/BHT trapping, KIE, or DFT studies are reported on these pages.

10.1021_jacs.9b03296.rxn_class_summary: Enantioselective electrocatalytic cyanophosphinoylation of vinylarenes, installing C-P and C-CN bonds across the alkene using a chiral Cu/serine-derived bisoxazoline (sBOX) catalyst.
10.1021_jacs.9b03296.electrochemical_role: Anode oxidizes Cu(I)(sBOX)(CN) to Cu(II)(sBOX)(CN)+, which then oxidizes the secondary phosphine oxide (Ph2P(O)H) to a P-centered radical, regenerating Cu(I); two such anodic events per turnover deliver the P• and the Cu(II)-CN that engages the carbon radical.
10.1021_jacs.9b03296.electron_flow: At the anode, Cu(I)(sBOX)(CN) is oxidized to Cu(II)(sBOX)(CN)+, which oxidizes Ph2P(O)H to a P-centered radical that adds to the vinylarene; the resulting benzylic radical is captured by another Cu(II)(sBOX)(CN) via single-electron oxidative addition to form a Cu(III) species, which undergoes enantiodetermining C-CN reductive elimination, releasing Cu(I) that is reoxidized at the anode while H+ is reduced to H2 at the Pt cathode.

key_intermediates.name: benzylic C-centered radical from P• addition to vinylarene
pentacoordinated Cu(III)(sBOX)(CN)(alkyl) intermediate prior to reductive elimination

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of Cu/sBOX, +TMSCN, +Ph2P(O)H
probe_reagents.observation: Catalytic anodic current at ~0.15 V increases upon addition of CN- and further upon addition of phosphine oxide 2, consistent with Cu(II)(sBOX)(CN) catalytically oxidizing the phosphine oxide to a P-centered radical.

probe_reagents.name: direct anodic oxidation control of Ph2P(O)H on carbon anode
probe_reagents.observation: Direct oxidation of diphenylphosphine oxide is difficult (>0.3 V), supporting the need for the Cu mediator.

probe_reagents.name: controlled-potential electrolysis at 0.19 V
probe_reagents.observation: Product 3 formed in 94% ee, consistent with a Cu(I/II)(sBOX)(CN) mediated electrocatalytic cycle.

probe_reagents.name: ligand scaffold variation (BOX vs sBOX with ester ancillary group)
probe_reagents.observation: Adding ester second-sphere ancillary coordination sites raised ee from 84% (11:1 er) to 95% (39:1 er), supporting a rigidified pentacoordinated Cu(III) reductive-elimination TS.

10.1021_jacs.9b03296.notes: Mechanism proposed by analogy to Stahl/Liu Cu-catalyzed C-H cyanation (ref 15d) in which a pentacoordinated Cu(III)(BOX)(CN)2(alkyl) undergoes enantiodetermining C-CN reductive elimination; here one CN is conceptually replaced by an intramolecular ester carbonyl coordination from the sBOX ligand. Authors note the enantioinduction mode by Cu(sBOX) (steric vs electrostatic stabilization by the planar ester ancillary group) is still under investigation. No TEMPO/BHT trapping, KIE, or DFT explicitly reported on these pages.

10.1021_jacs.3c04073.rxn_class_summary: Metal-free photoinduced energy-transfer enabled amino(hetero)arylation and aminosulfonylation of alkenes using bifunctional oxime ester reagents, with NaHSO3 as SO2 source for the sulfonylation variant
10.1021_jacs.3c04073.electron_flow: Visible light excites 2-iPrTX, which sensitizes the oxime ester a1 via triplet energy transfer; the excited a1* fragments its N-O bond, releasing CO2 to give a persistent iminyl radical and a transient (hetero)aryl radical. The aryl (or, with NaHSO3, the in-situ-formed arylsulfonyl) radical adds to the alkene, and the resulting carbon-centered radical undergoes radical-radical cross-coupling with the iminyl radical (or chain transfer with another a1) to deliver the β-(hetero)arylethylamine product.

key_intermediates.name: excited oxime ester a1* (triplet) generated by EnT from 2-iPrTX*
transient (hetero)aryl radical (e.g., 2-pyridyl radical, species 126) after CO2 loss
benzylic/alkyl C-centered radical 127 from aryl radical addition to alkene
alkyl-sulfonyl C-radical 129 after sulfonyl radical addition to alkene

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Addition of 2.0 equiv TEMPO completely inhibited the reaction of a1 with styrene (only trace 1), supporting radical intermediates

probe_reagents.name: Allyl-tethered oxime ester a24 (radical clock / 5-exo-trig probe)
probe_reagents.observation: Gave cyclized product 123 in 60% via 5-exo-trig radical addition/cyclization, confirming aryl and iminyl radical intermediates

probe_reagents.name: Diethyl diallylmalonate b77 (ring-closing diene probe)
probe_reagents.observation: Ring-closed product 124 formed (15%, 2.8:1 d.r.), consistent with radical addition/cyclization pathway

probe_reagents.name: Crossover oxime esters a1 + a25
probe_reagents.observation: GC-MS detected all four crossover combinations of pyridyl/4-F-aryl with Ph2C=N/Ar2C=N, showing radical-radical coupling occurs outside the solvent cage

probe_reagents.name: Thermal radical initiators (AIBN, DTBP, BPO) in the dark at 90 °C
probe_reagents.observation: No product 1 formed (0% yield), indicating that photoinduced EnT (not simple thermal radical initiation) is required

probe_reagents.name: UV-vis absorption of a1, b1, 2-iPrTX
probe_reagents.observation: Only 2-iPrTX absorbs near the 390 nm excitation wavelength, consistent with it being the sole light-absorbing species and EnT operation

probe_reagents.name: Quantum yield measurement
probe_reagents.observation: Φ = 2.5 (aminoarylation) and 1.4 (aminosulfonylation), >1, suggesting a free radical chain mechanism contributes

10.1021_jacs.3c04073.notes: Mechanism proceeds via energy transfer (EnT) from photoexcited 2-iPrTX to the oxime ester rather than single-electron transfer (photoredox SET); UV-vis shows only 2-iPrTX absorbs at 390 nm and quantum yields >1 indicate a contributing radical chain. The iminyl radical acts as the persistent radical (persistent-radical effect) while the (hetero)aryl radical is transient. For aminosulfonylation, NaHSO3 generates SO2 that is trapped by the pyridyl radical to give an arylsulfonyl radical before alkene addition. Termination of the C-radical with iminyl can occur either by radical-radical coupling with 125 or by H/N transfer with another a1, consistent with the observed crossover and chain quantum yield.

10.1021_ja00028a042.rxn_class_summary: Electron-transfer-induced valence isomerization of 2,2'-distyrylbiphenyl into 9,10-dibenzyl-9,10-dihydrophenanthrene or a cyclobutane via reductive bis-benzylic dianion intermediates.
10.1021_ja00028a042.electrochemical_role: Cathodic reduction (heterogeneous on K/Li, homogeneous via mediator dianions such as anthracene or stilbene, or electrochemically in DMA/THF) sequentially injects electrons into 2,2'-distyrylbiphenyl to form radical anion 1•-, dianion 1^2-, and higher anions which undergo skeletal rearrangement.
10.1021_ja00028a042.electron_flow: Cathode (or alkali metal / mediator dianion) delivers a first electron to 1 giving stable 1•-, then a second electron yields dianion 1^2- which rapidly rearranges (C-C bond formation at the benzylic/styryl carbons) into the bis-benzylic dianion 3^2- (kinetically as 3b^2-, equilibrating to 3a^2-); protonation of 3^2- gives dihydrophenanthrene 6, whereas oxidative quenching of 3b^2- (via 2b•-) closes the second C-C bond to give cyclobutane 2b. Overall observed as a two-electron EEC(DISP) process with disproportionation of 1•- driven by ion pairing.
10.1021_ja00028a042.rate_determining_step: Follow-up rearrangement of dianion 1^2- to 3^2- (k ≈ 0.2 s^-1 in DMA at -40 °C; 0.04 s^-1 in DMA/TBABr), fastened at the dianion (or trianion) stage.

key_intermediates.name: radical monoanion of 2,2'-distyrylbiphenyl (1•-) with intact frame
bis-benzylic dianion 3^2- (9,10-dihydrophenanthrene-type, trans-configured benzyl substituents), conformers 3a^2- and 3b^2-
cyclobutane dianion 3b^2- precursor to 2b upon reoxidation
phenanthrene and stilbene radical anion/dianion fragmentation products
all-trans cyclobutane 2b, product from oxidation of 3b^2-

probe_reagents.name: CdCl2 (oxidative quench)
probe_reagents.observation: Reoxidation of anions traps 3^2- as dihydrophenanthrene 6 (up to 100%); also generates cyclobutane 2b from 3b^2-.

probe_reagents.name: Iodine (oxidative quench)
probe_reagents.observation: Slow reoxidation yields stereoselectively the all-trans cyclobutane 2b (25-28%) alongside 6.

probe_reagents.name: Methylene bromide / 1,2-dibromoethane
probe_reagents.observation: Quenching gives only starting material recovered (no alkylation of dianion); indicates fast SET from dianion.

probe_reagents.name: 1,3-dibromopropane
probe_reagents.observation: 28% mono- and diadducts 7a/7b formed, evidencing benzylic carbanion character of 3^2-.

probe_reagents.name: Ethanol / methanol (protonation)
probe_reagents.observation: Fast protonation of 3^2- gives 6 quantitatively; protonation of 1^4- gives tetrahydro 10.

probe_reagents.name: Anthracene (mediator)
probe_reagents.observation: Anthracene dianion (E = -2.55 V) delivers one electron to 1 giving radical monoanion; product distribution (30% SM, 2-7% 6, 10-13% 11) confirms accessibility of mono- vs dianion stages.

probe_reagents.name: Stilbene (mediator)
probe_reagents.observation: Stilbene dianion (-2.67 V) cleanly drives 1 to 3^2-, isolated as 6 after CdCl2 quench.

probe_reagents.name: tert-butyl-substituted analog 5
probe_reagents.observation: Solubility/steric probe: gives tetraanion 5^4- cleanly observable by NMR; second bond formation to cyclobutane sterically hindered, so only DHP 8 (no [2+2] adduct 9) forms.

probe_reagents.name: 15-crown-5
probe_reagents.observation: Sequestration of alkali cations suppresses ion-pair-driven follow-up reactions, supporting ion-pair acceleration of 1^2- → 3^2-.

probe_reagents.name: TBABr / TBAPF6 vs alkali salts (supporting electrolyte)
probe_reagents.observation: Switching to TBA+ reduces ion pairing; ΔE between first two reductions drops to ~90 mV (vs ~220 mV with alkali), slowing 1^2- → 3^2- ~5x and shifting product distribution.

10.1021_ja00028a042.notes: Authors explicitly rule out an intramolecular [2+2] cycloaddition pathway (concerted or radical-anion mediated) for forming cyclobutane 2 under reductive conditions: 2b only appears upon reoxidation of 3b^2-. The voltammetric two-electron behavior at the first reduction is interpreted as an EEC(DISP) mechanism (Scheme III) rather than a true ECE, with disproportionation 2·1•- ⇌ 1 + 1^2- driven by ion pairing. Two conformers 3a^2- (rotatable, charges far apart, thermodynamic) and 3b^2- (fixed, charges close, kinetic) are invoked; only 3b^2- ring-closes stereoselectively to trans-2b. Side pathway at high T/low charge: fragmentation of 2 to phenanthrene + stilbene; at high charge: over-reduction to inert tetraanion 1^4- trapped as tetrahydro 10. Contrasts with photochemical [2+2] which gives all-trans 2b and thermal which gives all-cis 2a.

10.1021_jacs.0c08532.rxn_class_summary: electroreductive carbofunctionalization of radical-acceptor alkenes with alkyl bromides (carboformylation, anti-Markovnikov hydroalkylation, carbocarboxylation, and allylic alkylation) via a radical-polar crossover mechanism
10.1021_jacs.0c08532.electrochemical_role: cathodic reduction of alkyl bromide to alkyl radical (ECEC); cathode subsequently reduces the C-centered adduct radical to a benzylic carbanion; Mg sacrificial anode provides the counter oxidation
10.1021_jacs.0c08532.electron_flow: Cathode reduces R-Br to R• (dissociative SET); R• adds to the radical-acceptor alkene in a polarity-matched fashion to give a benzylic radical; this radical is more easily reduced than the starting alkene, so a second cathodic SET produces a benzylic carbanion which is trapped by an external electrophile (DMF → CHO, MeCN/Et2NH → H, CO2 → CO2H, or undergoes E1cb on allyl ethers). The Mg anode is oxidized sacrificially.
10.1021_jacs.0c08532.rate_determining_step: cathodic dissociative single-electron reduction of the alkyl bromide to generate R•

key_intermediates.name: isopropyl radical (Int-3, from cathodic reduction of iPrBr at ~ -2.0 V)
benzylic C-centered radical adduct (Int-1/Int-6) from radical addition to styrene
benzylic carbanion (Int-2/Int-8) from second cathodic reduction at ~ -1.6 V
alkoxide/hemiaminal adduct from carbanion addition to DMF (formyl donor)
β-alkoxy carbanion Int-9/Int-10 in allyl ether substrates undergoing E1cb β-elimination

probe_reagents.name: iPrMgBr (preformed Grignard)
probe_reagents.observation: slow addition of iPrMgBr to styrene 7 in DMF/MeCN gave ≤2% of difunctionalization products, ruling out direct carbanion addition to styrene (Pathway B)

probe_reagents.name: tBuLi
probe_reagents.observation: same as above: no productive difunctionalization, disfavoring carbanion-to-alkene addition pathway

probe_reagents.name: enantioenriched chiral secondary alkyl bromide 130 (98% ee)
probe_reagents.observation: product 131 formed in 52% yield as 1.4:1 dr with both diastereomers fully racemic, consistent with a free alkyl radical intermediate and ruling out an SN2 pathway via alkene radical anion (Pathway C)

probe_reagents.name: cyclopropane radical clock (vinylcyclopropane 132)
probe_reagents.observation: ring-opened product 133 obtained in 66% with E/Z > 20:1 as the only observable product, confirming a radical addition step

probe_reagents.name: no-electricity control / no-electricity + Mg powder
probe_reagents.observation: 2-3% conversion with no product, demonstrating that the reaction requires electrochemistry and is not driven by Mg(0)

probe_reagents.name: divided cell experiment
probe_reagents.observation: yield drops from 89% to 45% with increased byproducts, indicating that Mg salts generated at the anode play a beneficial role

probe_reagents.name: Co(salen) mediator test
probe_reagents.observation: 2 mol% Co(salen) did not improve the reaction (55% vs 71%), suggesting direct electron transfer to iPrBr is operative rather than mediated SET

10.1021_jacs.0c08532.notes: Authors explicitly compare three mechanisms: Pathway A (ECEC radical-polar crossover initiated by alkyl halide reduction, favored), Pathway B (EEC: 2e- reduction of R-Br to R-, then carbanion adds to alkene; ruled out by Grignard/RLi control), and Pathway C (ECEC initiated by alkene radical anion reduction; ruled out by chiral alkyl bromide racemization). Reduction potentials (iPrBr ~ -2.0 V, styrene ~ -2.6 V, benzylic radical ~ -1.6 V vs SCE) are most consistent with Pathway A. The β-elimination side reactivity (E1cb) on allyl ether substrates was leveraged to discover a new allylic alkylation reaction. Standard conditions use LiOTf or TBAPF6 electrolyte, DMF solvent (also CHO source) or MeCN/Et2NH or CO2-saturated DMF, Mg sacrificial anode, graphite cathode, undivided cell, constant current 10 mA, 4 F/mol.

10.1021_jacs.8b03302.rxn_class_summary: Visible-light photocatalytic dearomative cascade of 1-naphthol arenes bearing tethered alkenes, giving either [2+2] cycloadducts or benzocyclobutene products via catalyst-controlled sequential triplet energy transfer
10.1021_jacs.8b03302.electron_flow: Blue-light excites Ir(III) photocatalyst to its triplet state, which transfers triplet energy to naphthol 1a giving 1a*; intramolecular [2+2] cycloaddition with the pendant alkene yields styrene 2a. With PC III (higher ET), a second EnT to 2a generates triplet 2a*, which undergoes vinylcyclobutane ring-opening to diradical 4 that ISCs and recombines to benzocyclobutene 3a.
10.1021_jacs.8b03302.rate_determining_step: Second energy transfer from 3*Ir(III) to styrene 2a (slow with PC II, fast with PC III), governing divergence between 2a and 3a

key_intermediates.name: Ir(III) triplet excited state photocatalyst (3*Ir(III), T1)
Triplet styrene 2a* (second EnT-activated intermediate, selective with PC III)
1,n-Diradical intermediate 4 from vinylcyclobutane C-C bond homolysis
Benzocyclobutene product 3a (after ISC and radical-radical recombination)

probe_reagents.name: Stern-Volmer quencher 1a (naphthol substrate)
probe_reagents.observation: Both PC II and PC III are efficiently quenched by 1a (linear S-V), supporting EnT activation of 1a

probe_reagents.name: Stern-Volmer quencher 2a (styrene intermediate)
probe_reagents.observation: Only PC III is effectively quenched by 2a; PC II shows poor quenching, rationalizing catalyst-controlled divergence

probe_reagents.name: Thermal (dark) control
probe_reagents.observation: No reaction in absence of light/photocatalyst, precluding a thermal nonphotocatalytic pathway

probe_reagents.name: Reaction monitoring by 1H NMR
probe_reagents.observation: With PC II, 1a converts cleanly to 2a; with PC III, 2a forms transiently then converts to 3a, consistent with sequential EnT

10.1021_jacs.8b03302.notes: DFT-computed triplet energies (ET) and redox potentials of 1a (ET=59.8 kcal/mol) and 2a (ET=55.9 kcal/mol) combined with catalyst ET values (PC I 57.8, PC II 49.2, PC III 60.1 kcal/mol) rationalize catalyst-selective EnT. Crystal structures of 2a and 3a confirm connectivity/stereochemistry. The benzocyclobutene-forming step is proposed via a triplet vinylcyclobutane rearrangement (biradical 4) followed by ISC; an alternative concerted di-π-methane-like pathway is not explicitly invoked. No reaction without a carbonyl EWG on the naphthol, presumably needed to lower arene triplet energy.

10.1021_jacs.2c05126_sup_1.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical Cu-catalyzed 1,2-diazidation of alkenes with TMSN3, probed here by radical-clock (vinylcyclopropane ring-opening, 1,6-diene cyclization) and carbocation-trapping experiments.
10.1021_jacs.2c05126_sup_1.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation regenerates Cu(II)/Cu(III)-N3, which delivers an azidyl radical to the alkene; the resulting carbon radical is further oxidized to a carbocation (or trapped by a second Cu-N3) to install the second azide.
10.1021_jacs.2c05126_sup_1.electron_flow: Cu-azide delivers an N3 radical to the alkene to give a β-azido C-radical; for substrates that can stabilize a cation, this radical is further oxidized (anodically or by Cu(III)) to a carbocation that is trapped by azide, while less-stabilized radicals couple directly with a second Cu-N3.

key_intermediates.name: β-azido carbon radical from N3 addition to alkene
ring-opened homoallyl/allyl carbon radical from vinylcyclopropane (radical clock)
cyclized pyrrolidinyl carbon radical from 1,6-diene (radical clock)
β-azido tertiary carbocation (from methallyl benzamide substrate)

probe_reagents.name: (1-phenyl-2-vinylcyclopropane) 41
probe_reagents.observation: Ring-opened 1,5-diazidopent-3-en-1-ylbenzene (42) isolated in 15% (E/Z 5:1), confirming a free carbon radical intermediate.

probe_reagents.name: N-allyl-N-(2-methylallyl)tosylamide 44
probe_reagents.observation: 5-exo-trig cyclization gives 3,4-bis(azidomethyl)-3-methyl-1-tosylpyrrolidine (45) in 21%, dr 2:1, supporting a radical cyclization prior to second azidation.

probe_reagents.name: N-(2-methylallyl)benzamide 47
probe_reagents.observation: Affords N-(2,3-diazido-2-methylpropyl)benzamide (48) in 59% without amide-cyclization product, consistent with a tertiary β-azido carbocation rapidly trapped by azide rather than by the pendant amide.

10.1021_jacs.2c05126_sup_1.notes: Pages shown are SI mechanistic probes (sections b 'Radical clock experiments' and c 'Investigation of carbocation intermediates'). Both a radical and a carbocation pathway are operative: most substrates proceed through radical-polar crossover, while non-stabilized radicals likely undergo direct Cu-mediated azidation without going through a cation.

10.1021_jacs.3c01137_sup_1.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical anti-Markovnikov aminofunctionalization of alkenes via vinyl thianthrenium salt intermediates trapped by phthalimide nucleophiles
10.1021_jacs.3c01137_sup_1.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of thianthrene to thianthrene radical cation, which adds to the alkene to generate an alkyl-thianthrenium salt that serves as the electrophilic intermediate; subsequent steps (amine addition, byproduct formation) are non-electrochemical
10.1021_jacs.3c01137_sup_1.electron_flow: Thianthrene is anodically oxidized to its radical cation, which reacts with the alkene to give an alkyl- (or vinyl-) thianthrenium electrophile; methylamine then attacks the adjacent phthalimide carbonyl rather than displacing thianthrene cleanly, triggering loss of TT, intramolecular O-cyclization, ring-opening by a second equivalent of MeNH2, and finally collapse to release the aminoalcohol plus N-methyl iminoisoindolinone byproduct.

key_intermediates.name: alkyl thianthrenium salt (from terminal alkene + methylamine substrate)
phthalimide-derived tetrahedral hemiorthoamide adduct (amine addition to phthalimide C=O)
oxazoline/oxazolidine intermediate from intramolecular O-attack after loss of thianthrene (TT)
bis-amidine / amidinium intermediate after second methylamine addition and ring opening

probe_reagents.name: isolated vinyl thianthrenium salt 33
probe_reagents.observation: Treatment of isolated 33 with KNPhth (4 equiv) + HNPhth (1 equiv) gave alkyl-thianthrenium 2 in 74% yield with no enamide 34; with KNPhth alone (5 equiv), 2 dropped to 15% and enamide 34 formed in 38% (3:1 Z:E), implicating 33 as a kinetically competent intermediate and showing HNPhth suppresses elimination

probe_reagents.name: trans-4-octene (internal alkene substrate scope probe)
probe_reagents.observation: Electrolysis with thianthrene gave vinyl thianthrenium salt (59% trans, 8% cis at 5 min); after 18 h with KPhth, only 5–7% aminofunctionalization product and 28–33% allylic phthalimide formed, indicating internal alkenes do not productively undergo aminofunctionalization

10.1021_jacs.3c01137_sup_1.notes: Figure S2 proposes a detailed off-pathway sequence accounting for observed aminoalcohol and N-methyl iminoisoindolinone byproducts when methylamine is the nucleophile: amine addition to the phthalimide carbonyl (rather than SN2 at the C–S bond) leads to TT loss, intramolecular oxazolidine formation, second amine addition, and ring fragmentation. Figure S5 shows HNPhth additive is critical to suppress E1-type elimination of the alkyl thianthrenium to enamide 34. Figure S6 demonstrates that internal alkenes fail because allylic phthalimide formation dominates over aminofunctionalization.

10.1021_jacs.0c05183.rxn_class_summary: Radical-mediated three-component difunctionalization of alkenes with diazo compounds, delivering hydroalkylation (via photoredox/thiol HAT) or azidoalkylation (via Fe catalysis with TMSN3/TBHP), bypassing cyclopropanation.
10.1021_jacs.0c05183.electron_flow: For hydroalkylation: photoexcited Ru(II)* oxidizes Hantzsch ester (PCET) generating Ru(I) and 3a'; Ru(I) reduces the diazo compound, which loses N2 and is protonated to give α-ester radical I that adds to the alkene to form radical II; thiol-mediated HAT from 3a' delivers the C–H bond of product. For azidoalkylation: Fe(II)/TBHP generates tBuO•/Fe(III)X2OR, which reduces the diazo via PCET to radical I; addition to alkene gives radical II; Fe(III)X2N3 (formed by ligand exchange with HN3 from TMSN3) transfers N3 to radical II, regenerating Fe(II).

key_intermediates.name: Ru(I) reductant generated after oxidation of Hantzsch ester
alpha-ester carbon radical I (•CH(CO2Et)R) from diazo after loss of N2 and protonation
benzylic alkyl radical II after addition to styrene
Fe(II)X2 / Fe(III)X2N3 redox couple in azidoalkylation
tBuO• / tBuOO• radicals from Fe + TBHP
α-azido benzyl radical 12-C (from addition to α-phenyl vinyl azide)
iminyl radical 12-N after loss of N2 from 12-C

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: 3.0 equiv TEMPO fully suppresses both hydroalkylation (4a: 97%→0%) and azidoalkylation (6a: 73%→0%), consistent with radical intermediates.

probe_reagents.name: α-cyclopropylstyrene (radical clock 8)
probe_reagents.observation: Ring-opened products 9 (88%, E:Z 90:10) and 10/10' observed, confirming radical addition/ring-opening.

probe_reagents.name: D2O (6.5 equiv) in MeCN
probe_reagents.observation: Dideuterated product 4a' formed (D at α and γ positions), indicating water/protic solvent provides H atoms in the photocatalytic system.

probe_reagents.name: 2-propanol-d8 solvent
probe_reagents.observation: In azidoalkylation, mixture of 6a/6a'/6a'' (8:37:55) indicates partial deuterium incorporation; H also from TBHP/H2O.

probe_reagents.name: preformed cyclopropane 11 (from carbene + 1,1-diphenylethylene)
probe_reagents.observation: Cyclopropane 11 does NOT open to give 4bh or 6q under either set of conditions, ruling out a cyclopropane intermediate.

probe_reagents.name: α-phenyl vinyl azide 1cr
probe_reagents.observation: Trapping gives ketone 12 (47%) via iminyl radical 12-N (confirmed by GC-MS of imine 12'), supporting the radical addition pathway; requires light and photocatalyst but not thiol.

probe_reagents.name: Control: omission of Ru(bpy)3Cl2, light, or thiol
probe_reagents.observation: No or trace product without photocatalyst, light, or Hantzsch ester; replacing Ru with Eosin Y fails – establishes role of each component.

10.1021_jacs.0c05183.notes: Two complementary catalytic manifolds share a common α-ester carbon radical generated from the diazo via PCET (Ru(I)-mediated for hydroalkylation; Fe(II)/TBHP-mediated for azidoalkylation). Authors explicitly exclude a free-carbene/cyclopropane pathway (preformed cyclopropane does not open). For azidoalkylation, azide delivery is proposed via inner-sphere ligand transfer from Fe(III)X2N3 to alkyl radical II rather than free N3• addition.

10.1021_jacs.4c05288.rxn_class_summary: Gd(III)/chiral PyBox-catalyzed visible-light-promoted asymmetric [4+2] dearomative cycloaddition of 1-(2-carbonylpyridine)-substituted naphthalenes with styrenes
10.1021_jacs.4c05288.electron_flow: Gd(III)/PyBox coordinates the 2-carbonylpyridine-directed naphthalene to lower its excitation energy; blue-light excitation of this complex gives a singlet that undergoes ISC to a triplet naphthalene biradical, which adds enantioselectively to styrene to form a triplet 1,4-biradical, then a second ISC and radical recombination close the [4+2] ring.

key_intermediates.name: triplet 1,4-biradical from Si-face addition of styrene to triplet naphthalene (III)

probe_reagents.name: O2 (triplet quencher)
probe_reagents.observation: yield dropped to 26% (ee retained 96%), consistent with triplet intermediate

probe_reagents.name: 2,5-dimethylhexa-2,4-diene (10)
probe_reagents.observation: dose-dependent inhibition (5% yield at 100 mol%); supports triplet pathway

probe_reagents.name: benzil (triplet sensitizer, ET ~54 kcal/mol)
probe_reagents.observation: racemic 3aa in low yield (17%), poor dr/rr — direct triplet sensitization does not reproduce selective reaction

probe_reagents.name: [Ir(ppy)2(dtbbpy)]PF6 (ET 49.2 kcal/mol)
probe_reagents.observation: 23% yield, racemic, low selectivity

probe_reagents.name: [Ir(dFCF3ppy)2(dtbbpy)]PF6 (ET 61.8 kcal/mol)
probe_reagents.observation: 54% yield, racemic, low selectivity — rules out simple energy transfer mechanism

probe_reagents.name: (E)- vs (Z)-β-methylstyrene (2ac)
probe_reagents.observation: (E)-2ac gives 3aac (3:1 dr, 95% ee) with recovered 2ac >20:1 E/Z; (Z)-2ac gives 3aac' (1.4:1 dr, 60% ee) with recovered 2ac 0.8:1 E/Z — partial Z→E isomerization indicates reversible radical addition through a biradical, not energy transfer

10.1021_jacs.4c05288.notes: Job's plot established 1:1 Gd:1a binding stoichiometry; UV-vis shows a bathochromic shift (ε = 59 M^-1 cm^-1 at 448 nm) for the Gd(III)/L4/1a complex enabling visible-light absorption that the free naphthalene lacks. A linear nonlinear-effect plot (ee of 3aa vs ee of L4) implies one ligand per enantiodetermining TS. An alternative energy-transfer mechanism via free triplet naphthalene was excluded because external triplet sensitizers gave racemic, low-selectivity product and Z/E isomerization of styrene argues for reversible biradical addition rather than triplet energy transfer. Control experiments confirmed Gd(OTf)3, L4, and light are all essential.

10.1021_ja4092648.rxn_class_summary: Low-temperature electrochemical generation of DMSO-stabilized halogen (Br+, I+) and chalcogen (ArS+, ArSe+) cation pools that add across alkenes/dienes/allenes to give α-halo or α-chalcogeno carbonyl compounds (and cyclized N-heterocycles from tethered tosylamides) after triethylamine treatment
10.1021_ja4092648.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of Bu4NBr, Bu4NI, ArSSAr, or ArSeSeAr at -78 °C in the presence of DMSO generates DMSO-coordinated cation pools (X+/DMSO); these electrophilic cations then react with alkenes (no further electron transfer at the electrode during C-C bond formation)
10.1021_ja4092648.electron_flow: At -78 °C the anode oxidizes the halide or dichalcogenide to a cationic X+ that is stabilized by one or two DMSO molecules, forming a persistent cation pool; this electrophilic X+ then adds to an alkene to give a three-membered halonium/chalcogenonium ion, DMSO opens it (anti, back-side attack) to give a β-functionalized alkoxysulfonium ion, and triethylamine-induced Swern–Moffatt-type elimination delivers the α-halo or α-chalcogeno carbonyl product.

key_intermediates.name: three-membered ring bromonium/halonium ion from X+ + alkene

probe_reagents.name: methanol quench of alkoxysulfonium intermediate (E)- and (Z)-1-phenyl-1-propene (2d)
probe_reagents.observation: (E)-2d gave 5d-Br (72%) and (Z)-2d gave the diastereomer 5d'-Br (75%); anti stereochemistry indicates back-side attack of DMSO on the bromonium ion

probe_reagents.name: thermal-stability assay of X+/DMSO solutions (warming then cooling, reaction with 2b)
probe_reagents.observation: Relative stability order Br+ < I+ < PhS+ < PhSe+, correlating inversely with electronegativity of X

probe_reagents.name: Br2 used in place of electrogenerated 1-Br
probe_reagents.observation: Br2/DMSO also delivers α-bromo ketones (entry 6), supporting Br+/DMSO as the active species

probe_reagents.name: NBO/DFT analysis of three-membered ring onium ions
probe_reagents.observation: Charge on benzylic carbon increases Se < S < I < Br, rationalizing why DMSO attacks the benzylic carbon for Br but the terminal/other carbon for Se, explaining regiochemical switch between 1-Br and 1-Se with 2d

10.1021_ja4092648.notes: Regioselectivity with unsymmetrical alkenes (e.g., 1-phenyl-1-propene 2d) inverts between Br+ (attack at benzylic C → 4d-Br) and Se+ (attack at internal C → 4d'-Se), attributed to electronegativity-driven charge distribution on the halonium/chalcogenonium intermediate (supported by NBO/DFT). 1,3-Dienes undergo selective 1,2-addition at the internal carbon (no 1,4-product), and allenes react at the terminal carbon. Tethered tosylamide alkenes (2g) and 1,6-dienes (2h) give cyclized pyrrolidines/piperidines as single diastereomers, indicating that the cation-pool intermediates also initiate alkene cyclization. The Swern–Moffatt-like final step destroys alkene stereochemistry in the carbonyl product, but the MeOH-trapping experiment preserves and reveals the anti-addition stereochemistry of the X+/DMSO step.

10.1021_jacs.7b09388.rxn_class_summary: Mn-catalyzed electrochemical 1,2-dichlorination of alkenes using MgCl2 as a nucleophilic chlorine source via Mn-mediated Cl atom transfer.
10.1021_jacs.7b09388.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of Mn(II)-chloride complex to a Mn(III)-Cl (MnIIIClX2) species that acts as a Cl atom transfer agent; cathode reduces protons to H2.
10.1021_jacs.7b09388.electron_flow: At the RVC anode, Mn(II)-Cl is oxidized to a Mn(III)-Cl species which transfers a Cl atom to the alkene to give a beta-chloroalkyl radical; a second Mn(III)-Cl delivers the second Cl atom to close vicinal dichlorination, while the Pt cathode reduces H+ to H2.
10.1021_jacs.7b09388.rate_determining_step: Cl atom transfer from Mn(III)ClX2 to the alkene (radical addition step, slower than electrochemical turnover of catalyst, per CV current enhancement at low scan rates).

key_intermediates.name: Mn(II)-Cl complex (resting state, gives quasi-reversible CV wave at 0.75 V)
Mn(III)ClX2 (X = Cl or OAc) - Cl atom transfer agent
beta-chloro carbon-centered radical from Cl* addition to alkene
free Cl* (ruled out as dominant; Mn-bound Cl preferred for chemoselectivity)
Cl2 / chloronium / 1,3-dioxolium (from benzoate anchimeric assistance) - alternative Cl2 pathway

probe_reagents.name: N-tosyl diallylamine (radical clock 32)
probe_reagents.observation: Cyclization to pyrrolidine product 33 (71%) under electrocatalysis; no cyclization with Cl2, supporting C-radical intermediate.

probe_reagents.name: Vinyl cyclopropane derivative 36
probe_reagents.observation: Ring-opening to 37 (86%, E/Z 8:1) under electrocatalysis; no ring-opening with Cl2.

probe_reagents.name: Cl2 (substitute for MgCl2/electricity)
probe_reagents.observation: Cl2-mediated reactions gave different (syn) diastereoselectivity, different chemoselectivity (chlorolactonization of 40, oxidation of sulfide/amine), ruling out electrochemically generated Cl2 as the active chlorinating agent.

probe_reagents.name: Cinnamyl benzoate 28 (stereochemical probe)
probe_reagents.observation: Electrocatalytic gives anti-dichloride 11 (dr 12:1); Cl2 gives syn-29 via 1,3-dioxolium - opposite stereochemistry confirms distinct mechanisms.

probe_reagents.name: MgCl2 / Mn(OTf)2 CV
probe_reagents.observation: Quasi-reversible wave at 0.75 V assigned to Mn(II)/Mn(III)-Cl couple; current enhancement upon addition of 4-tert-butylstyrene indicates catalytic Cl transfer.

10.1021_jacs.7b09388.notes: The alternative mechanism considered and ruled out is anodic generation of Cl2 followed by electrophilic Cl2 addition; this is excluded by (i) opposite diastereoselectivity vs Cl2 conditions, (ii) opposite chemoselectivity with oxidatively sensitive groups (sulfide 38, amine 39, 4-pentenoic acid 40 -> lactonization with Cl2 but dichlorination electrochemically), and (iii) positive radical clock outcomes only under electrocatalysis. Authors propose the Mn-bound Cl* preserves radical character while suppressing promiscuous free Cl* reactivity (HAT).

10.1021_jacs.3c08839.rxn_class_summary: Electrophotochemical dual transition metal (Ce/Ni) catalyzed decarboxylative alkenylation of alkyl carboxylic acids with vinyl halides to form alkenes.
10.1021_jacs.3c08839.electrochemical_role: Anode oxidizes Ce(III) to photoredox-active Ce(IV) carboxylate (71), which undergoes LMCT under 400 nm light to generate alkyl radical and CO2; cathode reduces Ni(II) precatalyst to active mononuclear Ni(I) (75) and later reduces Ni(III) adduct (76) to benzoate-bound Ni(II) (73).
10.1021_jacs.3c08839.electron_flow: At the anode, benzoate-ligated Ce(III) is oxidized to Ce(IV) carboxylate which, upon 400 nm irradiation, undergoes LMCT to extrude CO2 and release an alkyl radical. At the cathode, the Ni precatalyst is reduced to Ni(I), oxidatively adds the vinyl bromide to Ni(III), is reduced by one electron to Ni(II), captures the diffusing alkyl radical, and undergoes reductive elimination (assisted by anodic single-electron oxidation) to release the alkene.
10.1021_jacs.3c08839.rate_determining_step: Radical capture of the alkyl radical by the persistent vinyl-Ni(II) intermediate at the counter electrode (described as the most challenging step).

key_intermediates.name: primary alkyl radical from cyclopropane ring-opening (Int-2)
nickel oligomers (reduced from precatalyst, reactivated by Phth-K)
vinyl benzoate side product (70, C-O reductive elimination)

probe_reagents.name: 2-cyclopropylacetic acid (radical clock 62)
probe_reagents.observation: Ring-opened linear 1,5-diene product 64 formed in 57% yield, confirming radical decarboxylation and primary alkyl radical intermediate (Int-2).

probe_reagents.name: alkene-tethered carboxylic acid 65
probe_reagents.observation: Intramolecular cyclization product 66 favored over direct alkenylation product 67, consistent with a free alkyl radical intermediate.

probe_reagents.name: dimeric Ni complex 69
probe_reagents.observation: Stoichiometric use without Phth-K gave <10% product and decomposition; with Phth-K gave 69% — supports phthalimide reactivating Ni oligomers into active mononuclear Ni.

probe_reagents.name: controlled-potential electrolysis at 0.2 V vs Fc+/0
probe_reagents.observation: 33% conversion / 25% yield of product 3, matching CV-assigned Ce(III)/Ce(IV) oxidation potential facilitated by benzoate coordination.

probe_reagents.name: omission controls (no Ce, no Ni, no light, no current, no Phth-K, no 4)
probe_reagents.observation: Each component required; without Phth-K or benzoate, product forms initially but decomposes, indicating additives protect the alkene.

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of CeCl3 ± benzoate 4
probe_reagents.observation: New oxidation wave at ~0.2 V vs Fc+/Fc upon adding 4, assigned to benzoate-ligated Ce(III)/Ce(IV).

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of Ni(bpy)3Br2 ± benzoate 4
probe_reagents.observation: Ni(II)/Ni(I) couple at -1.6 V made more reversible (ipa/ipc 0.3 → 0.7), showing benzoate stabilizes Ni species.

10.1021_jacs.3c08839.notes: Side product 70 (vinyl benzoate) arises from anodic single-electron oxidation of alkyl-vinyl-Ni(II) 73 to Ni(III), followed by C-O reductive elimination (path b); this side reaction provides evidence that the cathodically generated Ni(II) species 73 diffuses to the anode compartment. Br2 generated by anodic oxidation of bromide may also mediate this oxidation. Phth-K functions as a ligand/promoter that converts inactive Ni oligomers into catalytically active mononuclear Ni, especially with electron-rich/neutral vinyl bromides. Asymmetric variant achievable by replacing bpy with chiral bis-oxazoline ligand 58.

10.1021_ja00184a056.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical reductive double cyclization of β-dicarbonyl enol phosphates to bicyclo[n.1.0]alkanes (cyclopropane-fused carbocycles)
10.1021_ja00184a056.electrochemical_role: Cathodic reduction of β-dicarbonyl enol phosphate substrate 1; one-electron reduction gives radical anion 3, two-electron reduction gives vinyl anion 4 (resonance form of singlet carbene 4a), both with loss of diethyl phosphate anion
10.1021_ja00184a056.electron_flow: Cathode reduces β-dicarbonyl enol phosphate 1 by one electron with loss of diethyl phosphate to give vinyl radical 3a; intramolecular radical cyclization gives monocyclic radical 5, a second radical cyclization forms bicyclic radical 6, which is further reduced at the cathode to anion 8 and protonated to deliver the bicyclo[n.1.0]alkane 7. A competing pathway via two-electron reduction to singlet carbene 4a (≡ vinyl anion 4b) is excluded by retention of alkene stereochemistry in probe substrates.

key_intermediates.name: radical anion 3a/3b (from one-electron reduction of enol phosphate, loss of phosphate)
vinyl anion 4b / singlet carbene 4a (from two-electron reduction)

probe_reagents.name: cis/trans-disubstituted alkene substrates (examples 6 and 7 of Table I)
probe_reagents.observation: Double cyclization proceeded with retention of alkene stereochemistry, excluding free singlet carbene (3a/4a) as the reactive intermediate; an intermediate that permits isomerization (e.g., free vinyl anion) is also ruled out since complete equilibration does not occur

probe_reagents.name: ethyl carbonate analogue of enol phosphate (example 5 substrate)
probe_reagents.observation: Under identical electrochemical conditions gave 43% of the same bicyclic product, showing the leaving group can be varied and supporting a reductive C–O cleavage step

10.1021_ja00184a056.notes: Authors explicitly state mechanism is complex and not rigorously defined. Favored pathway is stepwise radical cyclization from radical anion 3a → 5 → 6 → reduction to 7. Alternatives considered and disfavored: (i) singlet carbene addition via 3a or 4a (excluded by stereoretention in examples 6/7); (ii) two-step anionic Michael route via vinyl anion 4b → 8 → intramolecular Michael; (iii) direct H-atom abstraction by 6 to give 2; (iv) reduction of 5 to 8 prior to second cyclization (disfavored due to short lifetime/ring closure outcompeting bond rotation). Loss of stereoselectivity in final protonation in example 8 indicates protonation of carbanion 8 is hindered from both cyclopentyl faces. Footnote 13 notes vinyl anions like 4b do not add intramolecularly to unactivated 1,2-disubstituted alkenes, further arguing against the anionic pathway.

10.1021_jacs.8b13192.rxn_class_summary: Aminoxyl (CHAMPO)-catalyzed electrochemical 1,2-diazidation of alkenes mediated by a metastable CHAMPO-N3 charge-transfer complex as an azidyl group-transfer agent.
10.1021_jacs.8b13192.electrochemical_role: Anode oxidizes the aminoxyl radical (CHAMPO) to the oxoammonium cation (CHAMPO+), which combines with N3- to form a metastable CHAMPO-N3 charge-transfer complex; this CTC serves as the source of free N3 radical and as a radical azidyl group-transfer agent. Cathode reduces water to H2 + OH-.
10.1021_jacs.8b13192.electron_flow: At the anode, CHAMPO is oxidized to CHAMPO+ which associates with N3- to give a CHAMPO-N3 CTC; endergonic fragmentation releases N3• that adds to the alkene to form a β-azido C-radical (I), which then undergoes inner-sphere azidyl group transfer from a second CHAMPO-N3 to give the 1,2-diazide and regenerate CHAMPO.
10.1021_jacs.8b13192.rate_determining_step: Endergonic fragmentation of the CHAMPO-N3 CTC to free N3• and CHAMPO (first C-N bond formation is slower than the second).

probe_reagents.name: Vinylcyclopropane radical clock (7)
probe_reagents.observation: Ring-opened azide 8 formed in 61% (E:Z >20:1); no direct diazidation — supports radical intermediate.

probe_reagents.name: N-allyl-N-methallyl tosylamide (9/10)
probe_reagents.observation: 5-exo-trig cyclization to pyrrolidine diazide observed (11/12), consistent with radical cyclization rather than cation.

probe_reagents.name: N-allyl benzamide (13, carbocation trap)
probe_reagents.observation: Gives diazide 14 (71%) cleanly; oxazoline 15 (cation-cyclization product) not observed — excludes carbocation pathway.

probe_reagents.name: Camphor-derived alkene (16)
probe_reagents.observation: Gives only 11% diazide 17 plus 42% rearranged 1,3-azidoalcohol 18 (via norbornyl cation); shows cationic pathway operates only when carbocation is stabilized.

probe_reagents.name: 4-Methoxy-α-methylstyrene (1g)
probe_reagents.observation: Major diazide 2g (79%) plus minor azidoalcohol 20 (16%); cation capture by H2O is minor, supporting predominantly radical pathway.

probe_reagents.name: Pre-formed TEMPO-azidooxygenation adduct (3g/3k)
probe_reagents.observation: Electrolysis of 3 instead of alkene gave no diazide (>85% 3 recovered); rules out oxidation/mesolytic cleavage of azidooxygenation product as on-pathway.

10.1021_jacs.8b13192.notes: Two alternative cationic pathways were considered and disfavored: (A) direct anodic or CHAMPO+ oxidation of radical I to carbocation II followed by N3- capture, and (B) oxidation/mesolytic cleavage of the azidooxygenation byproduct 3 to II. Both are ruled out by radical clock, carbocation-trap (amide 13), and control electrolysis of 3. Cationic pathway can operate for substrates that form especially stabilized carbocations (e.g., camphor alkene 16, p-MeO styrene 1g). Catalyst design (CHAMPO vs TEMPO/ACT) suppresses β-H elimination of the oxoammonium and prolongs CTC lifetime; the dark-red color of the CTC serves as a visual UV-vis indicator of catalyst integrity.

10.1021_jacs.0c03130.rxn_class_summary: Copper-catalyzed three-component asymmetric radical 1,2-carboalkynylation of alkenes with terminal alkynes and alkyl halides using a chiral cinchona-derived N,N,P-ligand to deliver chiral alkynes
10.1021_jacs.0c03130.electron_flow: L1Cu(I)-acetylide (II) reduces alkyl halide R2-X by inner-sphere SET to generate an alkyl radical ·R2 and L1Cu(II)-acetylide (III); ·R2 adds to the terminal alkene to give a benzylic radical IV, which combines with Cu(II) species III to form Cu(III) intermediate VI that reductively eliminates the C(sp3)-C(sp) bond, regenerating L1Cu(I).
