10.1021_jacs.0c03130.rate_determining_step: C(sp3)-C(sp) bond-forming reductive elimination from Cu(III) intermediate VI is proposed as the enantiodetermining step; kinetic orders (first order in alkene, catalyst, and alkyl halide; saturation in alkyne) are consistent with turnover-limiting steps involving alkene, catalyst, and R-X but not free alkyne

key_intermediates.name: L1Cu(II)-acetylide (III) generated after SET to alkyl halide
benzylic/prochiral alkyl radical IV stabilized by delocalization into the (hetero)aromatic ring (after addition to alkene)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction inhibited, supporting a radical pathway

probe_reagents.name: radical clock substrate 82 (cyclopropane-containing)
probe_reagents.observation: Ring-opened product 83 obtained under standard conditions, consistent with an alkyl radical intermediate

probe_reagents.name: Control: reaction without L1
probe_reagents.observation: No reaction without L1; with L1, copper phenylacetylide + 3a gave product 39 in 80% yield, 96% ee, implicating the ligated Cu-acetylide as the reductant of alkyl halides

probe_reagents.name: Tertiary alkyl bromide 3b vs chloride 3c
probe_reagents.observation: Both gave the same enantioselectivity, arguing against outer-sphere/free SET stereochemistry differences

probe_reagents.name: L2CuI NMR binding study
probe_reagents.observation: 1H NMR of L2Cu(I) complex characterized binding mode of N,N,P-ligand to Cu(I)

10.1021_jacs.0c03130.notes: Enantiodiscrimination is rationalized by two pentacoordinated Cu(III) transition states: the re-TS is favored over si-TS due to steric clash between the (hetero)aryl group on substrate and the P-3,5-di-tert-butylphenyl group of the ligand, giving R-configured products. Authors note no solid experimental evidence yet for the stereochemical model and that DFT/further experiments are ongoing. Direct radical-Cu(II) coupling at quaternary centers is suppressed by steric hindrance, consistent with observed selectivity. Aggregated Cu(I)-acetylide (V) is proposed as an off-cycle resting form.

10.1021_jacs.3c06794.rxn_class_summary: electrochemical three-component cross-electrophile dialkylation of alkenes with tertiary and primary alkyl bromides via radical-polar crossover under sacrificial Mg anode conditions
10.1021_jacs.3c06794.electrochemical_role: Cathodic reduction selectively reduces the more easily reduced tertiary alkyl bromide to a tertiary alkyl radical; after radical addition to the alkene, the resulting stabilized benzylic/α-boryl radical is further reduced at the cathode to a carbanion. The Mg sacrificial anode is oxidized to Mg2+ to balance charge.
10.1021_jacs.3c06794.electron_flow: At the cathode, the less-negative-potential tertiary alkyl bromide is reduced first to a tertiary alkyl radical, which adds to the radical-acceptor alkene; the resulting stabilized radical undergoes a second cathodic reduction (radical-polar crossover) to a carbanion that performs SN2 on the sterically accessible primary alkyl bromide, while the Mg anode is sacrificially oxidized.

key_intermediates.name: tertiary alkyl radical (e.g., tert-butyl radical) from cathodic reduction of tertiary alkyl bromide
benzylic/α-boryl radical adduct after tertiary radical addition to alkene (intermediate E)
stabilized benzylic/α-boryl carbanion (intermediate F) from second cathodic reduction
primary alkyl bromide electrophile (e.g., 1-bromo-3-chloropropane) attacked by carbanion via SN2
Mg2+ from sacrificial anode (forms MgBr2/Mg(ClO4)2 passivation layer in THF)

probe_reagents.name: vinylcyclopropane radical clock (substrate 59, Ph-cyclopropyl-vinyl)
probe_reagents.observation: Ring-opened dialkylation product 60 obtained in 18% yield (7:1 E/Z), supporting intermediacy of carbon radical E after tert-butyl radical addition

probe_reagents.name: D2O
probe_reagents.observation: Addition of D2O (5 equiv) under standard conditions gave deuteroalkylation product 61 (8% NMR yield) with 82% D incorporation at the benzylic position, supporting carbanion intermediate F

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of tBuBr vs n-alkyl bromide
probe_reagents.observation: tBuBr Ep/2 = -2.77 V vs Fc+/0; primary alkyl bromide 3 Ep/2 = -3.00 V vs Fc+/0 (0.23 V more difficult), rationalizing selective reduction of the tertiary halide

probe_reagents.name: chlorotrimethylsilane CV
probe_reagents.observation: Onset potential < -3 V vs Fc+/0; too hard to reduce, so chlorosilanes act as electrophiles trapping the carbanion rather than as radical precursors

10.1021_jacs.3c06794.notes: DFT supports facile tert-butyl radical addition to styrene/vinyl boronate (ΔG‡ ≈ 12.1–12.6 kcal/mol) and accessible SN2 of carbanion F on primary bromide (ΔG‡ ≈ 14.2–14.5 kcal/mol); computed E° of tert-butyl radical is -3.54 V vs Fc+/0 (too negative to reduce directly), whereas the stabilized benzylic/α-boryl radical E reduces at -2.63 to -2.80 V, explaining why an anion/radical-stabilizing Z group on the alkene is essential for radical-polar crossover. Solvent choice (DME vs THF) is critical: DME dissolves Mg salts and suppresses anode passivation observed in THF, raising yield from 8% to 79–80%. Deuteration via H-atom transfer from D2O to radical E is ruled out on BDE grounds (O-D 118.3 vs C-D 89.3 kcal/mol), favoring protonation of carbanion F.

10.1021_ja0172215.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical aziridination of olefins using N-aminophthalimide as nitrene precursor under metal-free anodic oxidation conditions
10.1021_ja0172215.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of N-aminophthalimide to a nitrogen-centered species that transfers to olefin; protons reduced at platinum cathode to H2
10.1021_ja0172215.electron_flow: At +1.80 V (vs Ag wire) on platinum anode, N-aminophthalimide undergoes one-electron oxidation (CV shows anodic peaks at +1.35 and +1.68 V) generating an N-centered oxidized intermediate that, with assistance of NEt3/AcOH, transfers a nitrene-equivalent to the olefin; protons are reduced at the cathode to H2.

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of N-aminophthalimide
probe_reagents.observation: Two irreversible one-electron oxidations at +1.35 V and +1.68 V vs Ag/AgCl

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of cyclohexene
probe_reagents.observation: Only -1.0 μA anodic current at +1.68 V vs -152 μA for N-aminophthalimide, indicating olefin oxidation is kinetically disfavored (overpotential)

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of Pb(OAc)2
probe_reagents.observation: Pb(II)/Pb(IV) oxidation at +1.60 V vs Ag/AgCl, enabling catalytic Pb variant as control

10.1021_ja0172215.notes: A related Pb(OAc)4-catalyzed (10 mol%) electrochemical variant was demonstrated, where Pb(II) is reoxidized to Pb(IV) at +1.60 V and acts as mediator; the metal-free pathway is favored as the main contribution and proceeds at slightly higher potential (+1.80 V) where direct N-aminophthalimide oxidation dominates over olefin background oxidation. Exact nature of the transferred nitrogen species (free singlet/triplet nitrene vs metal-free aminyl radical) is not definitively assigned.

10.1021_jacs.1c04652.rxn_class_summary: Pulse radiolysis and spectroelectrochemical study of bipyridine-ligated Ni(I) bromide intermediates relevant to Ni-catalyzed cross-coupling, characterizing Ni(0)/Ni(II) comproportionation and Ni(I) oxidative addition with aryl iodides.
10.1021_jacs.1c04652.electrochemical_role: Cathodic two-electron reduction of (dtbbpy)NiBr2 generates Ni(0), which rapidly comproportionates with remaining Ni(II) to give the catalytically relevant Ni(I) monomer; pulse radiolysis alternatively delivers a single solvated electron to (dtbbpy)NiBr2 to directly generate [(dtbbpy)NiBr].
10.1021_jacs.1c04652.electron_flow: A solvated electron (from pulse radiolysis) or cathode reduces (dtbbpy)NiBr2; under bulk electrolysis a 2e- reduction yields Ni(0) which comproportionates with Ni(II) to give two Ni(I)Br monomers, which then undergo rapid oxidative addition with aryl iodides to give Ni(III)(Ar)(Br)(I).
10.1021_jacs.1c04652.rate_determining_step: Oxidative addition of aryl iodide to [(dtbbpy)NiBr] (ΔG‡298K = 10.4 ± 0.4 kcal/mol; ΔH‡ = 5.8 kcal/mol, ΔS‡ = -15.5 cal/mol/K)

probe_reagents.name: 4-bromobenzotrifluoride
probe_reagents.observation: No significant decay of Ni(I) signal; upper limit kOA < 10^4 M-1 s-1 for aryl bromide OA

probe_reagents.name: ethyl 4-iodobenzoate
probe_reagents.observation: Significant Ni(I) signal quenching, kOA = 1.18 × 10^5 M-1 s-1

probe_reagents.name: 1,3-dicyanobenzene
probe_reagents.observation: Used as electron-transfer mediator to ensure efficient Ni(I) generation at high aryl iodide concentrations

probe_reagents.name: iodobenzene (vs synthesized [(dtbbpy)NiBr]2 dimer)
probe_reagents.observation: Monomer reacts rapidly (kOA = 2.2 × 10^4 M-1 s-1); pre-formed dimer shows no reaction with PhI over 16 h by 1H NMR

probe_reagents.name: panel of 4-substituted aryl iodides (OMe, H, OCF3, CO2Et, CN)
probe_reagents.observation: Hammett ρ = +3.1 (R2 = 0.91); 18-fold rate increase across σ = -0.27 to +0.66, consistent with electron-rich Ni(I) attacking electrophilic arene

10.1021_jacs.1c04652.notes: Two competing pathways for Ni(I) generation are distinguished: (i) direct 1e- reduction (pulse radiolysis) vs (ii) 2e- cathodic reduction followed by Ni(0)/Ni(II) comproportionation (bulk electrolysis) — both converge on the same [(dtbbpy)NiBr] monomer (matching UV-vis at 430, 620, 860 nm). Dimerization to [(dtbbpy)NiBr]2 occurs on the minute timescale and shunts catalyst into an unreactive off-cycle resting state, with implications for cross-coupling reactions run at high Ni concentrations. Eyring analysis provides experimental benchmark for DFT studies of Ni(I) OA barriers in Ni/photoredox catalysis. The OA could in principle proceed via Ni(I)/Ni(III) (favored here) or Ni(0)/Ni(II) (alternative complicated by comproportionation).

10.1021_jacs.3c09875.rxn_class_summary: Dual NHC/photoredox-catalyzed three-component coupling of imides, pyrocarbonates, and styrenes to form sterically congested beta-2,2-amino esters via imidyl-radical addition and radical-radical cross-coupling with an NHC-stabilized alkoxycarbonyl radical.
10.1021_jacs.3c09875.electron_flow: K2CO3 deprotonates Az-1 to give free NHC, which adds to diethyl pyrocarbonate to form ester azolium IV; excited PC-1* reduces IV to the NHC-stabilized alkoxycarbonyl radical V, generating PC-1 radical cation, which then oxidizes the imide anion (or performs PCET on the imide) to deliver imidyl radical II. II adds to styrene to give benzylic radical III, which couples with V; collapse of alkoxide VI releases the free NHC and the beta-2,2-amino ester product.

key_intermediates.name: benzylic C-radical III from imidyl addition to styrene
alkoxide adduct VI (radical-radical coupling product before NHC release)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Product formation suppressed (0% of 4a); TEMPO-alkoxycarbonyl adduct 7 detected by ESI-HRMS, supporting an NHC-stabilized alkoxycarbonyl radical intermediate.

probe_reagents.name: control: no NHC / no photocatalyst / no light / no base
probe_reagents.observation: Each omission gives 0% yield, indicating all four components are essential.

probe_reagents.name: Stern-Volmer quencher screen (1a, 2a, 3a, 1a+K2CO3, ester azolium IV)
probe_reagents.observation: 2a and 3a do not quench PC-1*; 1a and 1a+K2CO3 give only minor quenching; ester azolium IV quenches strongly, consistent with oxidative quenching of PC-1* by IV.

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of imide 1a
probe_reagents.observation: Confirms base-enabled oxidation of imide 1a is accessible, but does not uniquely define the photoredox step.

10.1021_jacs.3c09875.notes: Authors favor oxidative quenching (PC-1* reduces ester azolium IV first, then PC-1 radical cation generates imidyl radical from imide anion via SET or directly from neutral imide via PCET). A reductive quenching pathway (PC-1* oxidizes 1a first) cannot be fully excluded given the small Stern-Volmer quenching observed with 1a. An alternative pathway in which radical III is reduced to a benzylic anion that then attacks pyrocarbonate 2 was considered but disfavored based on reported reduction potentials of benzylic radicals versus 4CzIPN (PC-1). Robustness data show sensitivity to O2 and water, consistent with radical intermediates.

10.1021_jacs.7b03195.rxn_class_summary: Ni-catalyzed three-component reductive 1,2-dicarbofunctionalization of alkenes with aryl iodides and tertiary alkyl iodides using TDAE as organic reductant
10.1021_jacs.7b03195.electron_flow: TDAE reduces Ni(II) to Ni(0), which undergoes oxidative addition into ArI to give ArNi(II) (A); in parallel a Ni(I) species reduces the tertiary alkyl iodide via SET to release an alkyl radical (C) that adds to the alkene giving radical D, which recombines with A to form Ni(III) intermediate B that reductively eliminates the dicarbofunctionalized product and Ni(I), the latter being re-reduced by TDAE.

key_intermediates.name: ArNi(II)-I (A), from oxidative addition of ArI to Ni(0)
Tertiary alkyl C-centered radical (C) from Ni(I)-mediated activation of alkyl iodide
Alkyl-CH2-CHR• radical adduct (D) after addition of alkyl radical to alkene
Ni(III)(Ar)(alkyl-CH2-CHR) species (B) from radical recombination with A
Ni(I) species regenerating Ni(0)/Ni(II) with TDAE
Secondary alkyl iodide (E) — proposed but ruled out as intermediate

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: significantly reduced reaction efficiency, consistent with radical intermediates

probe_reagents.name: 1,1-diphenylethylene
probe_reagents.observation: significantly reduced reaction efficiency

probe_reagents.name: 1,4-cyclohexadiene
probe_reagents.observation: significantly reduced reaction efficiency

probe_reagents.name: Radical clock alkyl iodide 38 (hexenyl-type)
probe_reagents.observation: with unactivated alkene, 5-exo cyclization product 39 favored over intermolecular addition to allyl acetate (17%); with acrylonitrile, intermolecular addition product 40 obtained in 45% without 39 — confirms C-centered radical and kinetic competition

probe_reagents.name: Preformed secondary alkyl iodide 41
probe_reagents.observation: no reactivity under standard conditions, ruling out secondary alkyl iodide as on-cycle intermediate (path II excluded)

probe_reagents.name: Stoichiometric ArNi(II) complex 43
probe_reagents.observation: with acrylonitrile, tBuI and TDAE gave product 24 in 55% yield, confirming ArNi(II) competence

probe_reagents.name: Ni(dtbbpy)I2 + 2-iodotoluene + TDAE
probe_reagents.observation: formed ArNi(II) complex 42, demonstrating TDAE reduces Ni(II) and enables oxidative addition

probe_reagents.name: Control without ArI / without Ni
probe_reagents.observation: no reaction in absence of Ni or ArI; without alkyl iodide no reaction; demonstrates all three components required

10.1021_jacs.7b03195.notes: Authors favor Path I (radical addition to alkene then capture by ArNi(II) to give Ni(III), then reductive elimination). Path II (in situ secondary alkyl iodide E undergoing oxidative addition) is explicitly ruled out by NMR/GC-MS monitoring and by unreactivity of preformed secondary iodide 41. Alternative ArAlkNi(III) pathway via direct recombination of tertiary alkyl radical with A or oxidative addition of ArI to AlkNi(I) is disfavored due to absence of Alk-Ar direct coupling products. Unactivated olefins fail — a coordinating/directing group is required.

10.1021_jacs.3c01137.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical 1,2-aminofunctionalization (including diamination) of terminal alkenes via anodically generated thianthrenium-based dicationic adducts that undergo regiospecific single substitution by phthalimide and subsequent functionalization with a second nucleophile
10.1021_jacs.3c01137.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of thianthrene (TT) generates a thianthrenium radical cation that adds to the alkene to form a dicationic bis-sulfonium adduct; the electrochemistry is upstream of the productive C-N bond-forming substitution steps
10.1021_jacs.3c01137.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes thianthrene to its radical cation, which adds across the alkene to give a dicationic bis-sulfonium adduct; phthalimide anion then deprotonates/eliminates this adduct to an alkenyl thianthrenium salt, adds regiospecifically at the β-carbon to form a sulfur ylide, and is protonated by HNPhth (generated in the elimination) to deliver the β-phthalimido monosulfonium product, which is finally displaced by a second nucleophile.

key_intermediates.name: alkenyl thianthrenium salt (33 / II) formed by E1cb-type elimination of dicationic adduct by phthalimide anion
β-phthalimido sulfur ylide intermediate (III) from phthalimide addition at β-position of alkenyl thianthrenium
allylic phthalimide byproduct (34, 3:1 Z/E) formed under proton-deficient conditions

probe_reagents.name: isolated alkenyl thianthrenium salt 33
probe_reagents.observation: Resubjection to KNPhth (4 equiv) + HNPhth (1 equiv) gave 1,2-phthalimidosulfonium 2 in 74% yield with no allylic byproduct 35; with KNPhth (5 equiv) only, 2 dropped to 15% and allylic byproduct 34 formed in 38% (3:1 Z:E), establishing 33 as on-pathway and showing proton balance controls selectivity

probe_reagents.name: time-course 1H NMR monitoring
probe_reagents.observation: After addition of KNPhth, dicationic adduct 32 is rapidly consumed to alkenyl thianthrenium 33, which is then converted to 2, consistent with sequential elimination then addition

probe_reagents.name: X-ray crystallography of 1,2-phthalimidosulfonium 2
probe_reagents.observation: Crystal structure confirms regiochemistry of internal phthalimide addition / external sulfonium

probe_reagents.name: ammonia surrogate screen (TBAN3, pyridine, Ph2C=NH, KNPhth)
probe_reagents.observation: Only KNPhth gives high yield (~80%) of internal addition product A; other nucleophiles favor terminal isomer B or eliminated/allylic products, highlighting KNPhth's unique regioselectivity

10.1021_jacs.3c01137.notes: Authors explicitly contrast with the alternative direct SN2-like substitution of the dicationic adduct, which would not explain the high internal selectivity; instead, an elimination/re-addition pathway through alkenyl thianthrenium 33 is favored because alkenyl sulfonium salts are more electrophilic at the β-position. Proton balance (HNPhth generated in the elimination step) is essential — its absence diverts the reaction to allylic substitution product 34, indicating a competing allylation pathway analogous to reported alkenyl thianthrenium allylation chemistry.

10.1021_jacs.8b08592.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical 1,2-carbohydroxylation of arylalkenes via anodic oxidation, adding an alkynyl/alkenyl group (from organotrifluoroborate) and OH (from H2O) across the C=C bond
10.1021_jacs.8b08592.electrochemical_role: Anodic single-electron oxidation of arylalkene to a radical cation, which is trapped by the organotrifluoroborate; the resulting C-radical is further anodically oxidized to a benzylic cation that is trapped by H2O. At the cathode, H2O is reduced to H2 and HO-.
10.1021_jacs.8b08592.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes the arylalkene (Ep/2 = 1.57 V vs SCE) to a radical cation, which is intercepted by the organotrifluoroborate through a concerted C-C bond formation/BF3 release, yielding a benzylic radical; this radical is further oxidized at the anode to a carbocation that is trapped by H2O, while at the cathode H2O is reduced to H2 + HO-.

key_intermediates.name: benzylic C-centered radical (D) after concerted C-C bond formation/BF3 loss
benzylic carbocation (E) from anodic oxidation of D

probe_reagents.name: photoredox control (Acr+-Mes ClO4- / [Co] cat., blue LED)
probe_reagents.observation: 0% of carbohydroxylation product 3; instead 58% aldehyde 77 formed, showing distinct selectivity vs electrochemistry

probe_reagents.name: CAN (cerium ammonium nitrate, chemical oxidant)
probe_reagents.observation: 0% of 3; benzophenone formed (25%) at 42% conversion, indicating alkene preferentially reacts with H2O under homogeneous SET oxidation

probe_reagents.name: 1,1-diphenylpropene (trisubstituted alkene)
probe_reagents.observation: Gave 27% hydroxy-alkynylation 32 plus 51% vicinal diol 33, 10% epoxide, and 9% benzophenone, consistent with a benzylic cation intermediate trapped competitively by H2O

probe_reagents.name: DFT calculation on radical cation + alkenyltrifluoroborate
probe_reagents.observation: Low activation barrier for a concerted C-C bond formation/C-B cleavage, supporting concerted (not stepwise) coupling that preserves alkene stereochemistry

10.1021_jacs.8b08592.notes: Alternative stepwise mechanism (C-C bond formation followed by SET oxidation and C-B cleavage, as proposed for enamine radical cations with alkenyltrifluoroborates, ref 9c) was considered but ruled out because alkenyltrifluoroborate stereochemistry is fully retained in product, favoring a concerted C-C/C-B step. Selectivity for H2O over a second equivalent of organotrifluoroborate at the cation E is rationalized by steric hindrance.

10.1021_jacs.2c09248.rxn_class_summary: Visible-light triplet energy transfer enabled intermolecular [2π+2σ] cycloaddition of aryl/heteroaryl ketoesters (benzoylformate esters) with bicyclobutanes (BCBs) to directly assemble polysubstituted 2-oxabicyclo[2.1.1]hexanes via a biradical/aryl-migration cascade
10.1021_jacs.2c09248.electron_flow: Ir-F photocatalyst is excited by visible light and transfers triplet energy to the benzoylformate ester, generating a 1,2-(C,O)-diradical (A); this triplet diradical adds across the central C–C σ-bond of the BCB to give the most stabilized 1,5-diradical (B), which after ISC closes by radical–radical coupling to the 2-oxa-BCH (C). A second excited ketoester then abstracts the backbone C–H to give radical D, which undergoes aryl 1,2-migration through cyclohexadienyl intermediate E to the more stable benzyl radical F, finally quenched by HAT from an H-donor.
10.1021_jacs.2c09248.rate_determining_step: Addition of the triplet excited benzoylformate ester onto the BCB to form the 1,5-diradical B is the regioselectivity-determining (and likely rate-determining) step

key_intermediates.name: Triplet excited benzoylformate ester (1,2-diradical, species A)
1,5-diradical from addition of excited ketoester onto BCB (species B)
Closed-shell oxa-bicyclic intermediate after ISC and radical-radical coupling (species C)
Backbone C-H abstracted carbon radical (species D)
Bridged aryl-migration transition state / cyclohexadienyl-type intermediate (species E)
Stabilized α-ester benzyl-type radical after aryl migration (species F)
Norrish type II product (off-pathway, from o-Me substrate 3r)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction suppressed; TEMPO-adducts detected by ESI-HRMS, supporting a stepwise radical pathway

probe_reagents.name: Styrene (alternative radical acceptor in place of ketoester)
probe_reagents.observation: Under 365 nm or standard conditions with BCB 2a, no cycloadduct detected, indicating BCBs do not initiate the radical pathway

probe_reagents.name: Direct 365 nm UV excitation (no photocatalyst)
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction of 1a + 2a still gave 3a in 50% yield, consistent with sensitization of the ketoester triplet

probe_reagents.name: o-Methyl benzoylformate ester (substrate probe for Norrish II)
probe_reagents.observation: Gave Norrish type II product 7 (39%) rather than cycloadduct 3r (n.d.), demonstrating accessible triplet ketone biradical reactivity

probe_reagents.name: Stern–Volmer quenching with 1a and 2a
probe_reagents.observation: Both substrates quench excited *[IrF] at similar rates

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of 1a and 2a
probe_reagents.observation: Redox potentials outside the range of *[IrF], ruling out single-electron transfer

probe_reagents.name: UV/vis of 1a + 2a mixture
probe_reagents.observation: No evidence for EDA complex formation, supporting energy-transfer mechanism

10.1021_jacs.2c09248.notes: Authors favor an energy-transfer (triplet sensitization) mechanism over single-electron transfer or EDA-complex pathways, based on Stern–Volmer, CV and UV/vis data. Regioselectivity is rationalized by formation of the most stabilized 1,5-diradical B and by the aryl-migration step that yields the more stabilized benzyl radical F; a reversible HAT step accounts for selective C–H abstraction at the backbone position. Substrate scope (only disubstituted BCBs; o-Me ketoester gives Norrish II 7) and X-ray structures (3a, 3h, 3q, 3v, 3z, 6) support the proposed connectivity. Mechanism explicitly noted by the authors as 'remains to be elucidated'.

10.1021_jacs.1c01967.rxn_class_summary: Electrophotocatalytic acetoxyhydroxylation (1,2-dioxygenation) of aryl-substituted olefins using a triaminocyclopropenium (TAC) photocatalyst under anodic electrolysis with AcOH as the oxygen nucleophile
10.1021_jacs.1c01967.electrochemical_role: Anode oxidizes the ground-state TAC catalyst (TAC+ -> TAC2+•); photoexcitation of TAC2+• generates a strongly oxidizing excited state TAC*2+• that performs single-electron oxidation of the aryl olefin to its radical cation, regenerating TAC+
10.1021_jacs.1c01967.electron_flow: TAC+ is oxidized at the anode to TAC2+•, which absorbs a photon to give a potent excited-state oxidant TAC*2+• that performs SET on the aryl alkene to give an alkene radical cation and regenerate TAC+. Nucleophilic addition of AcOH followed by deprotonation gives a β-acetoxy C-radical, which is further oxidized (loss of a second electron, anode-mediated) to an acetoxonium/dioxolenium cation that is opened by water to deliver the β-hydroxy acetate.

key_intermediates.name: α-aryl-β-acetoxy carbon radical (9) after AcOH addition and -H+

probe_reagents.name: no light control
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction suppressed (<5% yield), indicating photoexcitation of the oxidized TAC catalyst is required

probe_reagents.name: no catalyst control
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction suppressed (<5% yield) at 1.5 V; at 3.0 V direct electrolysis gave only ~40% with substantial overoxidation byproducts (aldehyde/ketone/diacetyl acetal), demonstrating the selectivity benefit of the electrophotocatalytic cycle

probe_reagents.name: no electrolysis control
probe_reagents.observation: 0% yield, confirming anodic oxidation is required to access the active TAC2+• state

probe_reagents.name: Differentiated diene substrate 50 (one styrenyl, one non-styrenyl olefin)
probe_reagents.observation: Exclusive functionalization of the styrenic olefin to give 51 in 75%, consistent with a mechanism initiated by single-electron oxidation of the more easily oxidized aryl alkene

probe_reagents.name: 1,3-diene substrates
probe_reagents.observation: Only 1,2-dioxygenation observed; no 1,4- or tetraoxygenated products, consistent with localized radical cation reactivity

10.1021_jacs.1c01967.notes: Mechanism proposed in Figure 1C contrasts the electrophotocatalytic cycle with simple direct anodic oxidation, which leads to overoxidation side products (aldehydes, ketones, diacetyl acetals). The selectivity (chemoselective styrenic olefin oxidation in diene 50; clean 1,2- vs 1,4-dioxygenation in dienes; tolerance of electron-rich heterocycles like furan/pyrrole/indole) supports a discrete alkene radical cation rather than a strongly oxidizing electrode surface as the active oxidant. Direct mechanistic probes such as CV, radical traps (TEMPO/BHT), or KIE are not explicitly reported on this page; conclusions rest on control experiments (no light, no catalyst, no current, direct electrolysis) and chemoselectivity arguments.

10.1021_jacs.9b12129.rxn_class_summary: Visible-light photosensitized intramolecular [2+2] cycloaddition of N-allylcarboxamide-tethered indoles (and related heterocycles) to give cyclobutane-pyrrolidinone-fused tetracyclic scaffolds via dearomative energy transfer
10.1021_jacs.9b12129.electron_flow: Visible light excites the photosensitizer to its singlet then triplet state via ISC; triplet-triplet energy transfer (~60 kcal/mol) to the indole substrate 4 generates a triplet diradical 4*, which undergoes stepwise intramolecular C-C bond formation (preferentially 5-exo-trig at C2 to give INT-A) followed by radical-radical combination to close the cyclobutane and deliver tetracyclic 1.
10.1021_jacs.9b12129.rate_determining_step: Stepwise C-C bond formation from triplet diradical 4* via 5-exo-trig closure (lower activation energy than 7-endo-trig) is energetically favored per DFT

key_intermediates.name: triplet photosensitizer (PS*) generated via ISC after hv absorption
triplet excited-state substrate 4* (diradical) formed by energy transfer from PS*
INT-A: 1,4-diradical from 5-exo-trig attack of C2 radical onto tethered olefin (favored pathway)
INT-B: 1,4-diradical from 7-endo-trig attack of C3 radical onto tethered olefin (disfavored)

10.1021_jacs.9b12129.notes: DFT shows triplet-singlet (T1-S0) free energy gap of the substrate must be below the PS triplet energy (<60 kcal/mol) for productive energy transfer; C2-tethered carboxamide substrates qualify (delta-G 58.2-58.7 kcal/mol for indoles IV-VI; indenes VII/VIII and benzofuran IX also viable at 53.7-58.5 kcal/mol), whereas unsubstituted indole (65.0 kcal/mol) is unreactive. Alternative 7-endo-trig pathway via INT-B is calculated but disfavored relative to 5-exo-trig via INT-A.

10.1021_jacs.3c07146.rxn_class_summary: Photoelectrocatalytic asymmetric heteroarylcyanation of aryl alkenes with thiophenes and TMSCN via dual acridinium photoredox / chiral copper catalysis under anodic oxidation.
10.1021_jacs.3c07146.electrochemical_role: Anode regenerates the ground-state acridinium photocatalyst from the acridine radical (Mes-Acr-Ph•) and re-oxidizes Cu(I) to Cu(II); cathode reduces protons to H2.
10.1021_jacs.3c07146.electron_flow: Photoexcited [Mes-Acr-Ph]+* oxidizes the thiophene to a radical cation, which adds to the aryl alkene at the α-position to form a distal radical cation; deprotonation gives a benzylic radical that is captured by chiral Cu(II)(CN)2 to form a Cu(III) intermediate that undergoes enantiodetermining reductive elimination to deliver the chiral nitrile. The anode reoxidizes [Mes-Acr-Ph]• and Cu(I) back to their active states while the cathode evolves H2.
10.1021_jacs.3c07146.rate_determining_step: Enantiodetermining reductive elimination from Cu(III)(CN)2-alkyl complex IV (stereochemistry-determining step; not explicitly assigned as rate-limiting)

key_intermediates.name: distal benzylic/heteroarene-stabilized radical cation (II) from alkene addition

probe_reagents.name: allyl sulfone 51 (methyl 2-((phenylsulfonyl)methyl)acrylate)
probe_reagents.observation: Benzylic allylation product 52 isolated in 17% yield alongside 24% of 3, confirming a benzylic radical intermediate.

probe_reagents.name: electron-rich aryl alkene 47 (2-methoxystyrene)
probe_reagents.observation: Alkene 47 (Ep/2ox = 1.46 V) did not react with thiophene 2 (1.70 V) and decomposed; pairing 47 with more electron-rich thiophene 49 restored reactivity, supporting that the heteroarene must be more easily oxidized than the alkene.

probe_reagents.name: 2-acetylthiophene
probe_reagents.observation: Higher oxidation potential (2.19 V) than alkene 1 (2.00 V); failed to yield product 12, consistent with required photooxidation of the heteroarene.

probe_reagents.name: thiophene 2 and alkene 1 (Stern-Volmer)
probe_reagents.observation: Both quench [Mes-Acr-Ph]+*, but thiophene 2 quenches much more efficiently, indicating heteroarene is the principal SET partner.

10.1021_jacs.3c07146.notes: An alternative initial addition mode (II') in which the thiophene radical cation adds at the β-position of the alkene is considered but disfavored because pathway via II benefits from more extensive cation delocalization; for carbonyl-substituted alkenes (compounds 17-19), regiochemistry inverts to position (b) to avoid placing the cation adjacent to the carbonyl. Stern-Volmer data show alkene also quenches the excited state but less efficiently, so a minor alkene-first oxidation pathway cannot be strictly excluded.

10.1021_jacs.3c04864.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical isomerizing allylation and allenylation of aldehydes with unactivated alkenes and alkynes via cobalt-salen-mediated hydrogen evolution generating allylic/propargylic carbanions
10.1021_jacs.3c04864.electrochemical_role: Cathodic reduction of Co(II)-salen to Co(I), which deprotonates the allylic/propargylic C-H substrate to form Co(III)-H; further SET reduction gives Co(II)-H that releases H2, shifting the dissociation equilibrium to slowly release the carbanion nucleophile (indirect HER)
10.1021_jacs.3c04864.electron_flow: Cathode reduces Co(II)-salen to Co(I), which deprotonates the weakly acidic allylic/propargylic C-H of the alkene/alkyne to give Co(III)-H and a carbanion; a second electron converts Co(III)-H to Co(II)-H, releasing H2 and regenerating Co(II). The slowly released carbanion then undergoes nucleophilic addition to benzaldehyde via the kinetically and thermodynamically favored TS1(E) to give the linear (E)-allylation or allenylation product.
10.1021_jacs.3c04864.rate_determining_step: Deprotonation/proton transfer of the allylic (or propargylic) C-H bond during the HER step (supported by secondary KIE in parallel experiments and larger KIE in intermolecular competition)

probe_reagents.name: Cyclopropyl-substituted allyl/alkyne and aldehyde substrates (radical clock)
probe_reagents.observation: Products 3bm-3br and 5bd-5be obtained with cyclopropyl ring intact; no ring-opening or cyclization, excluding a radical pathway

probe_reagents.name: Allylbenzene-d2 (1be-d2)
probe_reagents.observation: Parallel kH/kD = 1.3, intermolecular competition kH/kD = 3.3; secondary KIE consistent with H/D exchange of carbanion and proton transfer in HER

probe_reagents.name: 1-Phenyl-1-butyne-d5 (4a-d5)
probe_reagents.observation: Parallel kH/kD = 1.5, intermolecular competition kH/kD = 4.3

probe_reagents.name: NaH in DMF (stoichiometric base mimic)
probe_reagents.observation: With allylbenzene + PhCHO gives isomerizing allylation product 3a in 62% yield (linear E); fails for 4a (alkyne) due to lower acidity, supporting carbanion intermediate for allylation

probe_reagents.name: Acidic additives (HOAc, MeOH, H2O)
probe_reagents.observation: Addition of acid dramatically decreases yield (trace-40%) due to competing HER of the acid, supporting an anionic/HER-driven mechanism

probe_reagents.name: DABCO (CV probe)
probe_reagents.observation: DABCO is cathodically inert but enhances Co(II)/Co(III) and Co(I)/Co(II) redox peaks of salen catalyst I; required additive for catalysis

10.1021_jacs.3c04864.notes: Two mechanisms are proposed: (favored) indirect HER via Co(II)-salen mediator generating Co(I)/Co(III)-H/Co(II)-H that deprotonates substrate, and (alternative) direct HER where the cathode reduces protons from the weakly acidic substrate directly. CV shows substrate (allylbenzene Ered = -1.78 V) is more reducible than aldehydes (-1.96 to -2.74 V), but the cathode potential is held more positive than aldehyde reduction to avoid the side reaction (PhCHO -> PhCH2OH or pinacol). DFT (PBE0+D3(BJ)/ma-def2-TZVPP//B3LYP/Def2-SVP) shows TS1(E) is kinetically (4.3 vs 6.0/8.9 kcal/mol) and thermodynamically (-2.8 vs -0.5/+3.3 kcal/mol) favored, rationalizing the unusual linear (E)-selectivity over the branched product.

10.1021_jacs.1c12762.rxn_class_summary: Electrocatalytic intramolecular cyclopropanation of active methylene cyanoamides bearing tethered alkenes using a phenothiazine organocatalyst (PT-1) in TFE to give cyclopropane-fused quinolinones and pyrrolidinones.
10.1021_jacs.1c12762.electrochemical_role: Anodic single-electron oxidation of phenothiazine catalyst PT-1 to its persistent radical cation [PT-1]•+, which serves as the active oxidant; cathodic reduction of CF3CH2OH generates CF3CH2O- (base) and H2.
10.1021_jacs.1c12762.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes PT-1 to [PT-1]•+; cathodically generated CF3CH2O- deprotonates the active methylene substrate, and [PT-1]•+ oxidizes the resulting carbanion to an alpha-cyano C-radical, which undergoes exo-trig cyclization; the cyclized alkyl radical couples with another [PT-1]•+ to give a sulfonium intermediate that is opened by CF3CH2O- in a Corey-Chaykovsky-like SN2 to deliver the cyclopropane and regenerate PT-1.

key_intermediates.name: phenothiazine radical cation [PT-1]•+ (SOMO with significant S character)
deprotonated cyanoamide anion (70 -> anion after CF3CH2O- deprotonation)
alpha-cyano carbon radical 71 (electrophilic C-radical on cyanoamide)
cyclized alkyl radical 72 (after 5-exo/exo-trig radical cyclization onto pendant alkene)
sulfonium adduct 73 (C-S bond between alkyl radical 72 and [PT-1]•+)
substrate-catalyst adduct 68 (analog of 73 from methylated substrate 67) detected by HRMS at m/z 608.2032
cyclopropane product 74 + regenerated PT-1 (after SN2-like displacement by CF3CH2O-)

probe_reagents.name: cis/trans-alkene stereoisomers 64 and 65
probe_reagents.observation: Both isomers gave similar yields and a mixture of diastereomers (1.7:1 and 1.3:1 dr), indicating loss of alkene stereochemistry and a stepwise radical cyclization rather than concerted carbene-like addition.

probe_reagents.name: alpha-methylated cyanoamide 67
probe_reagents.observation: Blocks the cyclization step after C-S bond formation; substrate-catalyst sulfonium adduct 68 detected by HRMS (608.2032, calc 608.2036), supporting sulfonium intermediate 73.

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of 1, PT-1, and PT-1 + 1 + LiOMe
probe_reagents.observation: Catalytic current observed only with both amide 1 and base present, showing base-assisted electrocatalytic oxidation of substrate by [PT-1]•+.

probe_reagents.name: DFT calculation of [PT-1]•+ SOMO and exo- vs endo-trig cyclization
probe_reagents.observation: SOMO is distributed across the tricyclic ring with significant density on sulfur (rationalizing C-S coupling); exo-trig cyclization is kinetically favored over endo even for 1,1-disubstituted alkenes.

10.1021_jacs.1c12762.notes: Authors emphasize that efficient radical cyclization of 71 is essential — otherwise the open-chain alpha-cyano radical can couple with [PT-1]•+ to form undesired sulfur ylides. The C-S/SN2 termination is explicitly analogized to the Corey-Chaykovsky cyclopropanation. The corresponding adduct 69 from non-methylated substrate 1 was not observed by HRMS, consistent with fast turnover. No exogenous base is needed because cathodic reduction of TFE continuously generates CF3CH2O-.

10.1021_jacs.1c07082.rxn_class_summary: Visible-light/photosensitizer-induced dearomative [5+2] (and divergent [2+2], interrupted [5+2], [5+4]) cycloaddition of indole/pyrrole-tethered vinylcyclopropanes (VCPs) via energy-transfer sensitization to triplet biradicals.
10.1021_jacs.1c07082.electron_flow: Visible-light excitation of the Ir photosensitizer to its triplet state transfers energy (Dexter, not SET) to the indole core of the substrate, generating a triplet indole; intramolecular C2–Cβ bond formation furnishes a triplet 1,4-biradical that undergoes cyclopropane ring-opening to a triplet 1,7-biradical, which crosses to the open-shell singlet via MECPs; the Z-1,7-biradical recombines to the [5+2] product, while the E-1,7-biradical (in HFIP) is protonated/deprotonated through a zwitterion to give the interrupted [5+2] product, and C3-substituted substrates close instead to [2+2] cyclobutane-fused indolines.
10.1021_jacs.1c07082.rate_determining_step: Intersystem crossing / ring-opening through TS2a-T1 or TS2b-T1 (~35–38 kcal/mol) connecting the triplet 1,4- to 1,7-biradicals; final C–C ring closure via TS3 (32.0 kcal/mol) on the OSS surface for [5+2].

key_intermediates.name: Triplet excited indole-tethered VCP (1a-T1) generated by energy transfer from Ir photosensitizer
Triplet 1,4-biradical INT1a-T1/INT1b-T1 (C2-C/β-C bond formed, radicals at C3 of indoline and cyclopropane carbon)
Triplet 1,7-biradical INT2-E-T1 / INT2-Z-T1 (after cyclopropane ring-opening, E or Z olefin)
Open-shell singlet 1,4-biradical INT1a-OSS / INT1b-OSS (after ISC via MECPs)
Open-shell singlet 1,7-biradical INT2-Z-OSS (productive for [5+2] ring closure via TS3)
Open-shell singlet 1,7-biradical INT2-E-OSS (unreactive toward [5+2]; routed to interrupted [5+2] via zwitterion)
Zwitterionic intermediate (indolyl cation / malonate anion) formed from E-1,7-biradical with HFIP/H2O as proton shuttle

probe_reagents.name: 2,5-dimethylhexa-2,4-diene (triplet quencher)
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction largely inhibited, supporting involvement of triplet intermediates.

probe_reagents.name: Stern–Volmer quenching with substrate 1a vs indoline analogue 1a'
probe_reagents.observation: Excited PS is quenched by indole-tethered 1a but not by the indoline analogue 1a', consistent with energy transfer to the indole chromophore.

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of 1a
probe_reagents.observation: Redox potentials of 1a do not match those of the photosensitizer, excluding a photoredox SET pathway.

probe_reagents.name: HFIP-d2 (deuterated solvent)
probe_reagents.observation: 63% D incorporation at the malonate α-position of 6a-d, supporting a zwitterionic intermediate with HFIP as proton shuttle.

probe_reagents.name: HFIP/D2O (5:1)
probe_reagents.observation: 42% D incorporation in 6b-d, consistent with proton transfer through a zwitterion.

probe_reagents.name: Sc(OTf)3 additive (for 7-azaindole substrate)
probe_reagents.observation: Stoichiometric Sc(OTf)3 restores reasonable yield (73%) of 2ad by bidentate coordination to the 7-azaindole.

probe_reagents.name: DFT calculations [(U)B3LYP-D3(BJ)/def2-TZVP//def2-SVP]
probe_reagents.observation: Map triplet and OSS biradical surfaces, MECPs, and TS energies; rationalize [5+2] vs [2+2] vs interrupted [5+2] divergence.

10.1021_jacs.1c07082.notes: Mechanism is energy-transfer (Dexter) photosensitization, explicitly distinguished from photoredox SET (CV mismatch, Stern–Volmer with indoline analogue). Divergent pathway selection is governed by the fate of the open-shell singlet 1,7-biradical: Z-isomer → [5+2]; E-isomer → interrupted [5+2] via zwitterion (favored in HFIP); C3-substituted indoles → [2+2] (via INT1-OSS ring closure through TS4a/b); pyrrole-tethered VCPs switch to [5+4] cycloaddition with C5 of pyrrole. Direct [2+2] from INT1a/b-OSS is a competitive but thermodynamically disfavored pathway for indole substrates (PRO-2+2a/b only −5.6/−5.2 vs 2a at −21.2 kcal/mol).

10.1021_jacs.4c01690.rxn_class_summary: Photoinduced palladium-catalyzed three-component asymmetric carboamination of 1,3-dienes with C(sp3)-H substrates and amines to give chiral allylic amines
10.1021_jacs.4c01690.electron_flow: Blue-light excited Pd(0)Ln* reduces ArBr by single-electron transfer to give an aryl radical and LnPd(I)Br; the aryl radical abstracts a C(sp3)-H hydrogen (HAT) to generate an alkyl radical, which adds to the 1,3-diene to give an allyl radical that recombines with Pd(I) to form a pi-allyl Pd(II) complex, finally undergoing nucleophilic allylic substitution by the amine to release product and regenerate Pd(0).
10.1021_jacs.4c01690.rate_determining_step: Intermolecular hydrogen atom transfer (HAT) from the C(sp3)-H substrate to the aryl radical (KIE = 1.9)

key_intermediates.name: aryl radical / Pd(I) pair from ArBr SET reduction
alkyl radical (from HAT on C(sp3)-H substrate, e.g. benzyl radical)
allyl radical intermediate III from radical addition to 1,3-diene

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction suppressed; TEMPO-aryl adduct 101 and TEMPO-benzyl adduct 102 detected by HRMS, supporting aryl and benzyl radical intermediates

probe_reagents.name: trans-1-phenyl-2-vinylcyclopropane (103, radical clock)
probe_reagents.observation: Ring-opened product 104 formed in 31% yield, 64% ee, supporting radical addition to the alkene

probe_reagents.name: D8-toluene (D8-1a)
probe_reagents.observation: Parallel KIE kH/kD = 1.9, implicating HAT in the turnover-limiting step

probe_reagents.name: Ir(ppy)3 (photosensitizer swap)
probe_reagents.observation: No product formed when used in place of Pd catalyst, indicating Pd is essential beyond simple photosensitization

probe_reagents.name: dark / no light control
probe_reagents.observation: No product even at 100 °C, confirming photoexcitation requirement

probe_reagents.name: omission of ArBr or Pd/L
probe_reagents.observation: No product, confirming both Pd catalyst and aryl bromide oxidant are required

10.1021_jacs.4c01690.notes: Bulky 2,6-di-tert-butyl-substituted aryl bromides are used to suppress direct aryl-radical addition to the diene (side product 105), favoring the HAT pathway. Pi-allyl Pd(II) intermediate IV detected by HRMS. Chirality is set by chiral phosphine ligand (e.g., BINAP/xyl-BINAP) during nucleophilic attack on the pi-allyl Pd(II) complex.

10.1021_jacs.4c09621.rxn_class_summary: Decarboxylative C(sp2)-C(sp3) cross-coupling of carboxylic acids with aryl iodides via Fe/Ni dual metallaphotoredox catalysis under 390 nm irradiation.
10.1021_jacs.4c09621.electron_flow: Photoexcited Fe(III) carboxylate undergoes inner-sphere LMCT to give a carboxyl radical and Fe(II); decarboxylation releases an alkyl radical that adds to (L)Ni(I)X to form (L)Ni(II)(Alkyl)X, which is reduced by Fe(II) to (L)Ni(I)(Alkyl); oxidative addition of Ar-I gives (L)Ni(III)(Aryl)(Alkyl) that reductively eliminates the cross-coupled product and regenerates (L)Ni(I)X.

probe_reagents.name: (4-tBuBpy)Ni(II)(Aryl)(NPhth) (44)
probe_reagents.observation: With FeCl3 + light gave only 2% cross-coupled product and ~6% Ar-NPhth, indicating Ni(II)-aryl is not the productive on-cycle intermediate.

probe_reagents.name: (4-tBuBpy)Ni(II)(Alkyl)(NPhth) (46)
probe_reagents.observation: Formed cross-coupled product under various conditions even without light, supporting Ni(II)-alkyl as competent intermediate; bialkyl side products implicate reductive elimination forming Ni(0).

probe_reagents.name: KNPhth (potassium phthalimide)
probe_reagents.observation: Addition of 5 equiv with FeCl3/acid/base gave only 13% product, distinct from MacMillan's 67%, arguing against Ni(II)-aryl pathway.

probe_reagents.name: FeCl2 vs FeCl3 control (no light)
probe_reagents.observation: No coupled product without irradiation; only Ar-H formed, confirming light-driven Fe-mediated decarboxylation is essential.

probe_reagents.name: X-ray crystallography of 46
probe_reagents.observation: Structural characterization of Ni(II)(Alkyl)(NPhth) (CCDC 2371033).

10.1021_jacs.4c09621.notes: Authors explicitly consider and disfavor an alternative pathway proceeding via Ni(0)/Ni(II)(Aryl)X oxidative addition followed by radical capture; their data instead support radical capture by Ni(I)X to give Ni(II)(Alkyl)X, then reduction to Ni(I)(Alkyl) prior to Ar-I oxidative addition. Open questions remain regarding exact Fe speciation, the radical-capture step, and roles of iodide, Zn, and 4-ethylpyridine additives. Fe activation is via inner-sphere LMCT rather than outer-sphere SET typical of Ir photocatalysts.

10.1021_jacs.4c10431_sup_1.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical anodic oxidation of an α-methylstyrene-type substrate leading to benzylic/allylic hydroxylation (formation of 2) versus deprotonation to a diene-type product (3), with TEMPO trapping evidence for a radical-polar crossover via a benzylic cation intermediate INT2.
10.1021_jacs.4c10431_sup_1.electrochemical_role: Anodic single-electron oxidation of the styrene substrate generates a styrene radical cation (INT1); after TEMPO radical rebound a closed-shell benzylic cation (INT2) is formed, which is then trapped by H2O (hydroxylation) or deprotonated by lutidine (diene formation). A second anodic oxidation of 2 leads onward to ketone product 4.
10.1021_jacs.4c10431_sup_1.electron_flow: Anode removes one electron from the styrene to give a radical cation INT1; TEMPO couples to the terminal carbon (TS_radical, 1.7 kcal/mol) producing the benzylic cation INT2 (-23.8 kcal/mol), which partitions between H2O addition (benzylic vs terminal hydroxylation, giving 2 or 2') and lutidine-mediated deprotonation to diene 3; subsequent anodic oxidation of 2 generates INT3 that collapses to ketone 4 with release of TMP+.
10.1021_jacs.4c10431_sup_1.rate_determining_step: On the productive surface, the lutidine/H2O-assisted ionic addition/deprotonation step from INT2 (TS-OH-benzyl 11.3, TS-diene 13.7, TS-OH-term 14.3 kcal/mol) is rate-limiting; the prior TEMPO radical-rebound is nearly barrierless (1.7 kcal/mol).

key_intermediates.name: oxidized bis-aminoxy/oxocarbenium INT3 en route to 4

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: DFT shows near-barrierless radical rebound (TS_radical = 1.7 kcal/mol) onto the styrene radical cation to give the strongly stabilized adduct INT2 (-23.8 kcal/mol), supporting a stepwise radical-then-cation (radical-polar crossover) pathway over a direct ionic addition (TS_ionic = 19.6 kcal/mol).

probe_reagents.name: 2,6-lutidine
probe_reagents.observation: Acts as Brønsted base in the deprotonation pathway (TS-diene, 13.7 kcal/mol) to deliver diene product 3, and as proton shuttle with H2O in the hydroxylation TSs.

probe_reagents.name: H2O
probe_reagents.observation: Nucleophile that traps cationic INT2 at the benzylic position (TS-OH-benzyl 11.3 kcal/mol, leading to 2 at -26.4) preferentially over the terminal position (TS-OH-term 14.3, leading to 2' at -19.5).

10.1021_jacs.4c10431_sup_1.notes: Two competing mechanistic surfaces were computed: (i) a 'radical trapping / radical-polar crossover' route via TEMPO addition to the styrene radical cation forming closed-shell cation INT2, and (ii) a direct 'charge annihilation' ionic route via TS_ionic (19.6 kcal/mol) leading to 2'. The radical-polar crossover (favored) is far lower in energy. From INT2, three downstream pathways were compared: benzylic hydroxylation (favored, TS = 11.3, giving 2), deprotonation to diene 3 (TS = 13.7), and terminal hydroxylation (TS = 14.3, giving 2'). Subsequent oxidation of 2 generates ketone 4 with extrusion of TMP+ (Figure S19). NPA charge analysis on INT2 (C1 +0.21, C2 +0.37, C3 +0.21) rationalizes site selectivity.

10.1021_jacs.7b13387.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical chlorotrifluoromethylation (and bromotrifluoromethylation) of alkenes via anodically coupled electrolysis combining two parallel oxidative events
10.1021_jacs.7b13387.electrochemical_role: Anode performs two parallel oxidations: direct oxidation of CF3SO2- (Langlois reagent) to generate transient CF3 radical, and oxidation of Mn(II)-Cl to a persistent [Mn(III)]-Cl radical chlorinating species; cathode reduces protons to H2
10.1021_jacs.7b13387.electron_flow: At the anode, CF3SO2- is oxidized (Ep/2 = 800 mV) losing SO2 to give CF3 radical, while in parallel Mn(II)-Cl is oxidized (E = 780 mV) to persistent [Mn(III)]-Cl; CF3 radical (transient) rapidly adds to the alkene to give a C-centered radical (I), which is then trapped by the persistent [Mn(III)]-Cl via atom transfer to deliver the chloro-trifluoromethylated product and regenerate Mn(II). Protons are reduced to H2 at the Pt cathode.
10.1021_jacs.7b13387.rate_determining_step: Likely the anodic oxidation events (cell potential rises to 1.3-1.6 V to oxidize both CF3SO2- and Mn(II)-Cl); product-forming radical addition (path A) is fast and not rate-limiting

key_intermediates.name: benzylic/alkyl C-centered radical (I) after CF3 addition to alkene

probe_reagents.name: vinyl cyclopropane (25)
probe_reagents.observation: Ring-opened product 26 obtained (40%, E/Z 10:1), consistent with C-radical intermediate after CF3 addition and before chlorination

probe_reagents.name: bisallyl tosylamine (27)
probe_reagents.observation: 5-exo-trig cyclized pyrrolidine 28 (82%, dr 3.5:1), confirming radical intermediate, excluding cationic ECEC pathway

probe_reagents.name: MgCl2 vs LiCl swap
probe_reagents.observation: LiCl gave diminished 2a yield with substantial dichloride 2d (12%), indicating chemoselectivity depends on solubility/Mn coordination

probe_reagents.name: Mn omission control
probe_reagents.observation: No 2a formed without Mn(OAc)2, demonstrating essential role of Mn-Cl persistent radical

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of [Mn(III)]-Cl/[Mn(II)]-Cl
probe_reagents.observation: Reversible redox waves indicate lifetime of [Mn(III)]-Cl >10^2 s (persistent radical character)

10.1021_jacs.7b13387.notes: Mechanism leverages persistent-radical effect: transient CF3 radical (spin on C) adds to alkene faster than the persistent [Mn(III)]-Cl (path A > path C), and cross-coupling of C-radical with persistent Mn-Cl (path D) outcompetes radical-radical homocoupling (path B), giving selective hetero-difunctionalization. Alternative ECEC (carbocation) mechanism ruled out by radical clock results since required carbocations would be primary/destabilized. Side product 2d (vicinal dichloride) arises from competing Mn-mediated dichlorination pathway; dimerization of I suppressed by high local concentration of [Mn(III)]-Cl in the anodic diffusion layer.

10.1021_jacs.8b00391.rxn_class_summary: Ferrocene-mediated electrochemical difluoromethylarylation of alkynes using CF2HSO2NHNHBoc to construct fluorinated dibenzazepines via a 7-membered ring homolytic aromatic substitution
10.1021_jacs.8b00391.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of Cp2Fe to Cp2Fe+ which acts as a single-electron-transfer mediator to oxidize the deprotonated sulfonyl hydrazide (I) to an N-radical (II) and then diazene (III); MeOH is reduced at the cathode to H2 and MeO-
10.1021_jacs.8b00391.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes Cp2Fe to Cp2Fe+, which oxidizes the deprotonated sulfonyl hydrazide to give an N-radical and then a diazene that fragments to CF2H radical with loss of SO2/N2; CF2H radical adds to the alkyne to give a vinyl radical, which undergoes 7-ortho cyclization onto the pendant arene, and the cyclohexadienyl radical is reoxidized (by Cp2Fe+) and deprotonated to give the dibenzazepine product.
10.1021_jacs.8b00391.rate_determining_step: 7-ortho vinyl-radical cyclization onto the arene (HAS, kinetically favored over 6-ipso and 1,5-HAT by DFT)

key_intermediates.name: Vinyl radical IV (from CF2H addition to alkyne)
Cyclohexadienyl C-radical V (after 7-ortho cyclization onto arene)

probe_reagents.name: Vinylcyclopropane radical clock (alkene 49)
probe_reagents.observation: Ring-opened product 50 bearing CF2H group obtained in 69% yield, evidencing CF2H radical intermediacy

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of reagent 1 with base
probe_reagents.observation: Cp2Fe+ oxidizes reagent 1 in presence of Na2HPO4 or LiOMe (more efficient with LiOMe)

probe_reagents.name: Control: no Cp2Fe
probe_reagents.observation: Yield drops to 18% (<5% isolated); mediator essential

probe_reagents.name: Control: no electricity
probe_reagents.observation: No reaction; electrochemistry required

probe_reagents.name: Alternative oxidants (tBuOOH/CuSO4; K2S2O8; Ir(ppy)3/hv)
probe_reagents.observation: 0% of product 3 under non-electrochemical oxidative conditions, demonstrating necessity of electrochemical mediation

10.1021_jacs.8b00391.notes: DFT compared three cyclization modes of vinyl radical IV: path a (7-ortho HAS, ΔG = -9.2, ΔG‡ = 10.7), path b (6-ipso, ΔG = -5.4, ΔG‡ = 14.4), and path c (1,5-HAT, ΔG = -13.5, ΔG‡ = 15.6); 7-ortho is kinetically favored. In an undivided cell, Cp2Fe+ may be partially reduced at the cathode, lowering current efficiency. Reagent 1 (CF2HSO2NHNHBoc) is introduced as a new air-stable CF2H radical precursor.

10.1021_jacs.5c12057.rxn_class_summary: Photoinduced chiral N,N'-dioxide/metal Lewis acid-catalyzed asymmetric dearomative [2+2] photocycloaddition of quinolines and indoles with bicyclo[1.1.0]butanes (BCBs) to give chiral dihydroquinoline-fused and indoline-fused bicyclo[2.1.1]hexanes (BCHs).
10.1021_jacs.5c12057.electron_flow: Chiral N,N'-dioxide/Zn(II) (or Tb(III)) Lewis acid binds the 2-acyl quinoline (or 2-acyl indole) substrate, lowering its triplet energy and inducing a bathochromic shift so that 400 nm light selectively excites the LA-substrate complex to a long-lived triplet diradical; this diradical adds to the strained central C-C bond of the BCB to form a chiral cyclobutyl/aryl 1,4-biradical (regio- and enantio-determining step), which then ring-closes to give the BCH product and regenerates the catalyst. SET is excluded by Rehm-Weller analysis.
10.1021_jacs.5c12057.rate_determining_step: First C-C bond formation between the photoexcited triplet substrate-LA complex and the BCB terminal carbon (TS1-Si1), which is also the regio- and enantio-determining step.

key_intermediates.name: Triplet excited quinoline-LA complex (long-lived triplet diradical on quinoline C5-C8/C2-C3 of indole)
Carbon-centered radical adduct after first C-C bond formation between BCB terminal carbon and quinoline C5 (INT2-Si1, cyclobutyl/quinolinyl 1,4-biradical bound to Zn)
Closed-shell dihydroquinoline-fused BCH product coordinated to Zn (INT3-Si1)
Indole/Tb(III)/L4-TqCPh3 triplet diradical (τ = 0.04 s) for indole substrates

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction completely inhibited; ESI-HRMS detected [A1+B1+TEMPO+H]+ (m/z 468.2856) and [A1+TEMPO+H]+ (m/z 327.2068), supporting radical intermediates from both quinoline and BCB.

probe_reagents.name: Isoprene / 1,3-diene triplet quencher
probe_reagents.observation: Yield of C1 reduced to trace, consistent with triplet excited-state pathway.

probe_reagents.name: O2 (air balloon)
probe_reagents.observation: Yield dropped from 80% to 32% (ee retained 98%), consistent with triplet quenching.

probe_reagents.name: Naphthalene (3 equiv.)
probe_reagents.observation: Yield reduced to 57%, consistent with triplet energy transfer quenching.

10.1021_jacs.5c12057.notes: SET (photoredox) mechanism was explicitly excluded: excited-state reduction potential of A1/Zn/L3-RaPr2(CPh3) is +1.17 V vs SCE, incompatible with BCB oxidation (+1.83 V vs SCE). Fluorescence lifetime increased from 3.76 to 4.21 ns and bathochromic shift on LA addition support selective excitation of the LA-bound complex. Quantum yield Φ = 0.04 rules out radical chain, supporting a closed catalytic cycle. Linear ligand-ee vs product-ee shows one ligand per active Zn. Regioselectivity (C5 vs C8 on quinoline; C3 vs C2 on indole) is controlled by the steric profile of the chiral pocket (extended/open/partly open) tuned by ligand bulk; DFT shows TS1-Si2 (C8) is 1.7 kcal/mol higher than TS1-Si1 (C5) due to steric clash with the ramipril framework, and Re-face is blocked by aryl ligand groups (TS1-Re1 disfavored by 1.2 kcal/mol). For indole/Tb system, C3 attack favored over C2 by 2.5 kcal/mol (48.3 vs 50.8 kcal/mol).

10.1021_jacs.4c17657.rxn_class_summary: Photoredox/pyridine N-oxide co-catalyzed regioselective carbohydroxylation and aminohydroxylation of unactivated alpha-olefins with water as the hydroxyl source
10.1021_jacs.4c17657.electron_flow: Photoexcited acridinium PC2* performs single-electron oxidation of pyridine N-oxide 1b to generate an electrophilic N-oxy radical, which adds to the terminal carbon of the alpha-olefin to give a more substituted C-radical; this radical undergoes Giese addition to benzalmalononitrile (or DIAD), and the resulting adduct radical is reduced by PC2-radical-anion (regenerating PC2) to a carbanion that is protonated by TFA, forming an N-alkoxypyridinium that undergoes SN-type substitution by water (TFA-activated) to deliver the primary alcohol and regenerate pyridine N-oxide.
10.1021_jacs.4c17657.rate_determining_step: Nucleophilic substitution of pyridine N-oxide from the N-alkoxypyridinium by water (TS_Sub, DFT free-energy barrier 24.3 kcal/mol), with water activated by trifluoroacetate counterion

key_intermediates.name: tertiary carbon-centered radical from anti-Markovnikov N-oxy radical addition to alpha-olefin (Int1)
Giese adduct radical with benzalmalononitrile (post C-C bond formation)
stabilized malononitrile carbanion (Int3) after SET reduction by PC2-reduced
N-alkoxypyridinium trifluoroacetate intermediate (Int4) after protonation by TFA

probe_reagents.name: H2-18O (95 atom% 18O)
probe_reagents.observation: Carbohydroxylation of 2-ethyl-1-butene gave primary alcohol with 18O/16O = 92:8 (78% yield), confirming water as the hydroxyl source via nucleophilic substitution of N-alkoxypyridinium

probe_reagents.name: 1,6-heptadiene (radical clock / intramolecular trap)
probe_reagents.observation: Cyclization/carbohydroxylation gave disubstituted cyclopentane 32 in 52% yield, evidencing a discrete carbon radical intermediate

probe_reagents.name: Stern-Volmer fluorescence quenching with 1b
probe_reagents.observation: Excited PC2* quenched by 2,6-dichloropyridine N-oxide 1b (Ksv = 34.2) but not appreciably by alpha-olefin (Ksv = 1.8) or benzalmalononitrile, supporting initial SET to 1b

probe_reagents.name: tert-butanol (alternative nucleophile)
probe_reagents.observation: Gave carboetherification product 33 (42%), trapping the N-alkoxypyridinium electrophile

probe_reagents.name: acetic acid / acetate buffer
probe_reagents.observation: Gave carboesterification product 34 (56%), consistent with polar substitution on N-alkoxypyridinium

probe_reagents.name: 4-pentenoic acid (intramolecular carboxylate)
probe_reagents.observation: Delivered gamma-substituted delta-lactone 35, proof-of-principle for cyclization via N-alkoxypyridinium substitution

10.1021_jacs.4c17657.notes: Two pathways were considered for alcohol formation from the N-alkoxypyridinium: (favored) water nucleophilic substitution regenerating pyridine N-oxide (supported by catalytic 1b loading and 18O labeling), vs (alternative) reductive N-O bond cleavage which would consume 1b stoichiometrically. Competing ortho-alkylation of pyridine N-oxide (product 3) and allylic C-H functionalization (product 4 with 2,6-dibromopyridine N-oxide 1d; product 35 from cyclohexene) are side pathways suppressed by using ortho-chlorinated 1b. Direct SET oxidation of the alpha-olefin is excluded because olefin oxidation potential (>+2.50 V vs SCE) exceeds PC2* (+2.15 V).

10.1021_jacs.1c06700.rxn_class_summary: Copper-catalyzed diazidation of enamides, enecarbamates, aminocyclopropanes, and aminocyclobutanes with PIDA/TMSN3 (or Selectfluor/TMSN3), followed by one-pot nucleophilic substitution of the α-amino azide (masked iminium) to access orthogonally protected 1,2-, 1,3-, and 1,4-diamines.
10.1021_jacs.1c06700.electron_flow: CuTc is oxidized by Selectfluor (or PIDA) to a high-valent Cu(III), which performs SET oxidation of the aminocyclopropane to an aminium radical; β-scission opens the ring to a distal C-radical that is azidated by a Cu(II)-N3 complex, regenerating Cu(I); a second oxidation gives an iminium that is trapped by azide (or by an added C-nucleophile in the one-pot variant). For enamides, an azide radical addition to the electron-rich alkene is proposed instead.

key_intermediates.name: Cu(III) species formed by oxidation of CuTc with Selectfluor (or PIDA)
aminium radical cation (I) from SET oxidation of aminocyclopropane
distal carbon radical (II) after β-scission/ring-opening of cyclopropane
iminium ion (III) after azidation of II and further oxidation
α-amino azide / 1,3-diazide product (e.g., 6, 13a, 18) acting as masked iminium
[(BPhen)Cu(II)(N3)(μ-N3)]2 (Stahl complex) demonstrated as competent azide-transfer species with CuTc

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: With 5b, 6% TEMPO adduct 15 isolated and 94% 5b recovered; even without TMSN3 a TEMPO-water adduct forms, showing azide is not required for ring-opening.

probe_reagents.name: Methyl acrylate
probe_reagents.observation: Radical relay gave 1,5-diazide 16 in 9%, consistent with a free carbon radical intermediate after β-scission.

probe_reagents.name: Cyclopropyl/cinnamyl substrate 17 (radical clock + alkene)
probe_reagents.observation: With PIDA/TMSN3, 92% alkene 1,2-diazidation (18) with no cyclopropane ring-opening; redox potential of the alkene is lower than that of the aminocyclopropane, so π-bond is oxidized preferentially.

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of 5b, 5m, 5p, 5r and CuTc
probe_reagents.observation: E1/2red of aminocyclopropanes (+1.67 to +2.23 V vs SCE) exceeds Selectfluor (+0.33, +0.79 V), so direct oxidation by Selectfluor is unfeasible; CuTc shows Cu(I)/Cu(II) at 0.86 V and Cu(II)/Cu(III) at 2.28 V, and a mixture of 5b+CuTc shows a new oxidation feature supporting Cu-mediated oxidation.

probe_reagents.name: Chiral CN-BOX ligand with 5xb
probe_reagents.observation: No asymmetric induction observed, suggesting a freely diffusing radical intermediate.

probe_reagents.name: Selectfluor (variation of CuTc loading)
probe_reagents.observation: Increasing CuTc loading raised 6xa:20 (azide vs fluoride) ratio from 85:15 to 96:4, implicating copper in azide group transfer rather than free F/N3 radicals.

probe_reagents.name: [(BPhen)Cu(II)(N3)(μ-N3)]2 (Stahl complex)
probe_reagents.observation: Not catalytically competent alone, but transferred azide in the presence of CuTc, supporting a Cu-N3 azide transfer step.

10.1021_jacs.1c06700.notes: Favored mechanism for aminocyclopropanes: Cu(III)-mediated SET oxidation → aminium radical → β-scission → C-radical → Cu(II)-N3 azide transfer → iminium → azide trapping. Alternatives considered but not preferred: (i) rebound mechanism in which Cu(III)-N3 oxidizes substrate and directly delivers N3; (ii) free azide radical recombination with carbon radical. For enamide/enecarbamate substrates, the mechanism is proposed to differ — addition of an azidyl radical to the electron-rich enamide alkene rather than substrate SET, as in prior styrene diazidation work.

10.1021_ja3034075.rxn_class_summary: Copper-catalyzed enantioselective intramolecular alkene carboetherification (and hydroetherification) of pentenols to form fused tetrahydrofurans via oxycupration/radical aryl addition.
10.1021_ja3034075.electron_flow: Cu(II)-bisoxazoline binds the alkene of the pentenol and undergoes cis-oxycupration to give an alkyl-Cu(II) THF intermediate; homolysis of the C-Cu(II) bond releases Cu(I) and a primary carbon radical, which adds to the tethered arene to give an aryl/cyclohexadienyl radical that is reoxidized (MnO2 reoxidizes Cu(I) to Cu(II)) and rearomatized to yield the fused bicyclic product.
10.1021_ja3034075.rate_determining_step: C-C bond formation (radical addition of primary C-radical to the arene) is implicated as slow relative to H-atom abstraction by 1,4-cyclohexadiene

key_intermediates.name: primary carbon radical from C-Cu(II) homolysis (29)

probe_reagents.name: 1,4-cyclohexadiene (H-atom donor)
probe_reagents.observation: Intercepts the carbon radical to give 2,3-trans tetrahydrofuran 27 in 82% yield (and 26 in 85% yield, 76% ee) instead of carboetherification product, confirming a carbon radical intermediate and that HAT is faster than C-C bond formation.

probe_reagents.name: Deuterium-labeled 3-p-methoxybenzyl-4-penten-1-ol (10a-D)
probe_reagents.observation: Carboetherification gave 1.8:1 trans/cis 11a-D:12a-D, each as 1:1 diastereomeric mixture at the deuterated carbon, indicating loss of alkene stereochemistry consistent with an sp2 (radical) intermediate.

probe_reagents.name: Regiochemical probe (ortho vs para arene addition)
probe_reagents.observation: Ortho-substituted product formed as major (ortho:para = 1.9:1), consistent with radical (not electrophilic/Friedel-Crafts) addition to the arene.

probe_reagents.name: Chiral Cu(OTf)2/(R,R)-Ph-Box catalyst variation
probe_reagents.observation: Enantioselectivity (up to ~75% ee carboetherification, 76% ee hydroetherification) supports Cu involvement in the alkene oxycupration step.

10.1021_ja3034075.notes: Authors considered alternative C-C bond-forming mechanisms: (i) radical addition to arene (favored), (ii) electrophilic aromatic substitution via organocopper(III) (direct or via reductive elimination), and (iii) Friedel-Crafts on a primary carbocation (rejected as too high energy). The 2,3-trans selectivity in hydroetherification is rationalized via TS-D placing the allylic substituent pseudo-equatorial; diminished ee in carboetherification is attributed to reversible ring-opening/ring-closing of the carbon radical prior to C-C bond formation. MnO2 serves as terminal oxidant to regenerate Cu(II) from Cu(I).

10.1021_jacs.7b04363.rxn_class_summary: Visible-light-excited asymmetric intermolecular [2+2] photocycloaddition of α,β-unsaturated 2-acyl imidazoles (and related N,O-chelating substrates) with alkenes catalyzed by a chiral-at-rhodium Lewis acid (Δ-RhS).
10.1021_jacs.7b04363.electron_flow: Substrate coordinates to chiral Rh(III) via N,O-chelation, red-shifting absorption; visible light directly excites the Rh-substrate complex to S1, which undergoes ISC to a triplet state localized on the enone alkene; this triplet adds to the alkene cosubstrate to form a 1,4-biradical that ring-closes (after ISC) to the chiral cyclobutane, then dissociates from Rh.

key_intermediates.name: Rh-bound substrate singlet excited state S1 (II)
Rh-bound substrate triplet excited state T1 (III)
Rh-coordinated 1,4-triplet biradical from addition to alkene (IV)

probe_reagents.name: benzil (triplet sensitizer)
probe_reagents.observation: Racemic [2+2] product 3a obtained with benzil as sensitizer in place of Δ-RhS, supporting a triplet pathway.

probe_reagents.name: 2,5-dimethylhexa-2,4-diene (triplet quencher)
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction 1a + 2a → 3a significantly inhibited, consistent with a triplet intermediate.

probe_reagents.name: (E)- vs (Z)-1p substrate diastereomers
probe_reagents.observation: Both (E)- and (Z)-1p give the same diastereomer of 3p with >20:1 d.r. and 99% ee, indicating configurational lability (a biradical/triplet) at the alkene during cycloaddition.

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry / redox potentials of RhS-1a
probe_reagents.observation: Excited-state potentials (E[Rh]+/[Rh]* = -0.78 V; E[Rh]*/[Rh]- = +0.88 V) insufficient to oxidize/reduce styrene (Ep red = -2.91 V; E1/2 ox = +2.05 V), excluding photoredox/SET.

probe_reagents.name: DFT on RhS-1a T1 state
probe_reagents.observation: Spin density localized on the alkene carbons of the coordinated enone, consistent with alkene-localized triplet reactivity in [2+2].

probe_reagents.name: UV/Vis of RhS, 1a, RhS-1a
probe_reagents.observation: RhS-1a complex shows red-shifted absorption into the visible, demonstrating direct visible-light activation of the Lewis acid/substrate complex.

10.1021_jacs.7b04363.notes: A photoredox/SET pathway was explicitly excluded based on excited-state redox potentials. Alternative mechanisms considered include triplet sensitization of free substrate (ruled out because Rh-substrate complex absorbs visible light directly and gives enantioselective product, whereas benzil sensitization gives racemate). The favored mechanism is direct visible-light excitation of the Rh-bound substrate followed by ISC to an alkene-localized triplet that undergoes stepwise biradical [2+2] cycloaddition under chiral Lewis acid control.

10.1021_jacs.4c13682.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical metal-free 1,2-syn-cyano-hydroxylation of alkenes via a telescoped 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition with a sulfonyl-nitrile oxide followed by cathodic SET-induced radical ring-opening (sew & cut)
10.1021_jacs.4c13682.electrochemical_role: Cathodic SET reduction of the C=N (oxime) moiety of the 3-sulfonyl-isoxazoline cycloadduct generates a radical anion that expels arylsulfinate to form an imidoyl radical, which undergoes β-scission; anode performs oxidation of Et3N (or 1,4-cyclohexadiene) as sacrificial reductant to close the circuit
10.1021_jacs.4c13682.electron_flow: In situ generated sulfonyl-nitrile oxide undergoes metal-free 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition with the alkene to give an isoxazoline cycloadduct; cathodic single-electron reduction of its C=N bond yields a radical anion that ejects arylsulfinate to form an imidoyl radical, which fragments by radical β-scission of the N–O bond to release a nitrile and an oxygen-centered radical, the latter being further reduced at the cathode and protonated to deliver the 1,2-syn-cyano-hydroxylation product.
10.1021_jacs.4c13682.rate_determining_step: Radical β-scission of the imidoyl radical X (isoxazoline ring-opening) following SET reduction and sulfinate loss

key_intermediates.name: radical anion IX (cathodic SET adduct of cycloadduct)
α-oxy radical VIII from radical Brook rearrangement (with C5-silyl substrate)

probe_reagents.name: cyclopropene-derived cycloadduct 18 (radical clock)
probe_reagents.observation: Electrolysis afforded β-hydroxy aldehyde 19 (70%) and over-reduced alcohol 20 (28%); no β-hydroxy-imine or β-hydroxy-amine (excluding path A) and no tertiary-radical product 21/22 from cyclopropane opening (excluding path C), consistent with path B: SET to C=N, sulfinate loss, then fast isoxazoline β-scission outcompeting cyclopropane opening

probe_reagents.name: 3-bromo-isoxazoline 12
probe_reagents.observation: Gave product 9 in 33% with 66% recovered SM, showing a good anionic leaving group at C3 is required

probe_reagents.name: 3-ester isoxazoline 13 and 3-alkyl isoxazoline 14
probe_reagents.observation: No fragmentation product formed; confirms need for an expellable anionic LG (ArSO2-, Br) at C3

probe_reagents.name: C3-carboxylate 15 (anodic decarboxylation under alternating polarity)
probe_reagents.observation: Delivered 9 in 9% NMR yield, showing pendant redox handle can also trigger ring-opening

probe_reagents.name: NHPI ester 16 (cathodic activation)
probe_reagents.observation: Gave syn-hydroxy nitrile 9 in complex mixture, supporting analogous radical fragmentation

probe_reagents.name: C5-trimethylsilyl cycloadduct 23 (Brook probe)
probe_reagents.observation: Furnished silyl ether 24 (64%) via radical Brook rearrangement of oxygen-centered radical VII to α-oxy radical VIII, confirming oxygen-centered radical intermediate

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of cycloadduct 8 and analogs 12–16
probe_reagents.observation: Two reduction peaks at Epc = -1.01 V (oxime C=N) and -2.10 V (aryl sulfone); first reduction at oxime triggers fragmentation

probe_reagents.name: 1,4-cyclohexadiene (sacrificial reductant at anode)
probe_reagents.observation: Serves as competent anodic sacrificial reductant, easing purification by avoiding nonvolatile byproducts

10.1021_jacs.4c13682.notes: Three pathways were considered for cycloadduct fragmentation: path A (cathodic N–O cleavage to distonic radical anion III) was ruled out by absence of β-hydroxy-imine/amine; path C (cyclopropane ring-opening dominant) was ruled out by absence of stabilized tertiary-radical products 21/22; favored path B involves SET to C=N then sulfinate loss and β-scission. Minor side pathway: with C5-silyl substrates a radical Brook rearrangement diverts the oxygen-centered radical to an α-oxy radical, giving silyl ether instead of the cyano-hydroxylation product. Furoxan 11 (nitrile oxide dimer) is a stoichiometric byproduct consumed during the first ~1 F/mol of electrolysis.

10.1021_jacs.3c10585.rxn_class_summary: Membrane-free electrochemical epoxidation of chloropropene (and other alkenes) to epichlorohydrin via bromide-mediated bromohydrin intermediate over NiCo2O4 nanotip anode
10.1021_jacs.3c10585.electrochemical_role: Anode oxidizes Br- to Br radical (one-electron) which adds to alkene; anode also generates *OH from water oxidation; cathode reduces water to H2 generating OH- that deprotonates/eliminates HBr from bromohydrin
10.1021_jacs.3c10585.electron_flow: At the NiCo2O4 nanotip anode, Br- is oxidized by SET to Br•, which adds to the C=C of chloropropene to give a carbon radical; coupling with *OH (from anodic water oxidation) yields a bromohydrin. OH- generated at the cathode (from H2O reduction to H2) eliminates HBr from the bromohydrin to deliver the epoxide and regenerate Br-.
10.1021_jacs.3c10585.rate_determining_step: Anodic one-electron oxidation of Br- to Br• and its addition to the alkene (Br•/alkene radical addition step) over the nanotip anode

key_intermediates.name: beta-bromo carbon radical from Br• addition to chloropropene (ClCH2-CHBr-CH2• dominant; also ClCH2-CH•-CH2Br)
surface hydroxyl radical (*OH) from water oxidation

probe_reagents.name: BHT (butylated hydroxytoluene)
probe_reagents.observation: ECH yield in 0.1 M KBr dropped by >42%; yield in KCl essentially unchanged, supporting Br radical pathway

probe_reagents.name: 1,1-diphenylethylene (DPE)
probe_reagents.observation: ECH yield in KBr fell by >50%; DPE-Br adduct detected by HR-MS (m/z 259.0112/261.0091)

probe_reagents.name: N,N-diallyl-4-methylbenzenesulfonamide
probe_reagents.observation: Br•-trapped cyclized adduct detected by HR-MS (m/z 332.0137/334.0116), confirming Br radical

probe_reagents.name: DMPO (5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide)
probe_reagents.observation: EPR signals of DMPO-OH and DMPO-C adducts observed; HR-MS confirms DMPO-OH (m/z 130.0876) and DMPO-C with both [ClCH2C•(H)CH2Br] and [ClCH2CHBrCH2•] (m/z 270.1363/271.9733)

probe_reagents.name: H2-18O
probe_reagents.observation: Only 18O-labeled ECH detected by HR-MS, confirming water as sole oxygen source

10.1021_jacs.3c10585.notes: Alternative previously reported mechanism (Br/O redox synergistic, involving Br2/HBrO) is ruled out here: UV-vis shows no BrO- signal in KBr electrolyte, while ClO- is observed in KCl (consistent with Cl2/HOCl pathway). High-curvature NiCo2O4 nanotips enhance the local electric field to concentrate Br- and OH- at the anode; isolated Br adsorption sites suppress Br• coupling to Br2 (suppresses BER) and a high barrier for *OH further oxidation suppresses OER. NMR of bromohydrin intermediate shows ClCH2CHBrCH2OH : ClCH2CH(OH)CH2Br ≈ 2:1, indicating the ClCH2CHBrCH2• carbon radical is dominant.

10.1021_jacs.8b12965.rxn_class_summary: Visible-light-promoted intramolecular [2+2] cycloaddition of indole-tethered terminal olefins via energy transfer to give cyclobutane-fused angular tetracyclic spiroindolines.
10.1021_jacs.8b12965.electron_flow: Photoexcited Ir-photosensitizer V transfers triplet energy to indole substrate 1a, populating its T1 state (single electrons at C2/C3 of indole); the C3 (or C2) radical attacks the tethered terminal olefin in a tail-to-tail fashion to give a triplet 1,4-biradical, which crosses to an open-shell singlet via MECP and closes by radical-radical combination to the cyclobutane-fused spiroindoline.
10.1021_jacs.8b12965.rate_determining_step: Tail-to-tail C–C bond formation via TS1 (12.7 kcal/mol barrier relative to 1a-T1).

key_intermediates.name: Triplet excited state of indole substrate (1a-T1, 56.0 kcal/mol)

probe_reagents.name: 2,5-dimethylhexa-2,4-diene
probe_reagents.observation: Triplet quencher; standard reaction significantly inhibited (2a obtained in only 26% yield), supporting triplet intermediates.

probe_reagents.name: Stern–Volmer quenching with substrate 1a
probe_reagents.observation: Linear quenching of photoexcited V luminescence by 1a (R^2 = 0.95), confirming excited-state interaction.

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of 1a
probe_reagents.observation: E1/2(ox) = +1.16 V, E1/2(red) = -0.44 V vs Ag/AgCl; no correlation between sensitizer redox potentials and reactivity, disfavoring SET mechanism.
