10.1021_jacs.8b12965.notes: Favored pathway is triplet energy transfer with tail-to-tail cyclization via TS1 (68.7 kcal/mol) giving spiro[4.4] biradical INT1, then ISC through MECP1 to open-shell singlet INT1-OSS, then radical–radical combination (TS2, 59.1 kcal/mol) to product 2a. Alternative head-to-head pathway via TS1' (72.9 kcal/mol) leading to bridged [3.1.1] iso-2a (52.3 kcal/mol) is computed but not observed; head-to-tail TS could not be located. SET (photoinduced single-electron transfer) mechanism was considered but ruled out because reactivity does not correlate with photosensitizer redox potentials. Thermal [2+2] (TS3, 80.7 kcal/mol) is prohibited. ΔG(T1–S0) of substrate vs sensitizer correlates with yields, further supporting energy-transfer mechanism. X-ray structures of derivatives 6 and 8 confirm product connectivity.

10.1021_ja501093m.rxn_class_summary: Electrooxidative direct C-N coupling of N-protected imidazoles with aromatic and benzylic C-H compounds to give N-aryl/N-benzyl imidazoles after deprotection
10.1021_ja501093m.electrochemical_role: Anodic single-electron oxidation of the aromatic (or benzylic) substrate to its radical cation, which is then trapped by the N-protected imidazole nucleophile; the protecting group renders the resulting imidazolium electrooxidatively inactive, preventing overoxidation
10.1021_ja501093m.electron_flow: The anode removes one electron from the arene or benzylic substrate to give a radical cation; the N-protected imidazole adds to this electrophilic intermediate, and a second oxidation/deprotonation gives the N-substituted imidazolium ion, which is electroinactive and thus avoids overoxidation. Subsequent nonoxidative removal of the protecting group affords the N-substituted imidazole.

key_intermediates.name: N-protected (PG) imidazolium intermediate after C-N bond formation

10.1021_ja501093m.notes: Key design principle: use of an N-protecting group on the imidazole so the C-N coupled product is an imidazolium (electrooxidatively inactive), preventing the overoxidation that plagues the conventional approach with unprotected imidazoles (Figure 2a vs 2b). Naphthalene (1a) chosen as model arene because its anodically generated radical cation is well-documented. Mechanism is proposed/illustrated in Figure 2 rather than supported by radical traps or KIE on this page.

10.1021_jacs.4c10431.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical TEMPO-mediated dioxygenation of aryl-substituted allenes to give vinyl-TEMPO allylic alcohols (and minor diene), enabling trifunctionalization of the allene framework
10.1021_jacs.4c10431.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of TEMPO to TEMPO+, which acts as an outer-sphere single-electron mediator to oxidize the allene to a vinyl radical cation (INT1); cathode generates -OH (and 1/2 H2) that hydroxylates the resulting TEMPO-allyl cation INT2
10.1021_jacs.4c10431.electron_flow: TEMPO is oxidized at the anode to TEMPO+, which outer-sphere oxidizes the allene to vinyl radical cation INT1; TEMPO radical traps INT1 to give the allyl cation INT2, which is hydroxylated at the benzylic carbon by cathodically generated -OH (with base) to yield allylic alcohol 2, while a competing deprotonation by lutidine/base gives diene 3.
10.1021_jacs.4c10431.rate_determining_step: TEMPO+-mediated outer-sphere SET oxidation of the allene to INT1 (substrate-dependent; selectivity between 2 vs 3 controlled at the TS where TS_OH-benzyl is 2.4 kcal/mol below TS_diene)

key_intermediates.name: Allene vinyl/allyl radical cation INT1 (from 1, Ph-substituted)
TEMPO-trapped allyl cation INT2 (alpha-(TEMPO-oxy) allyl cation)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Serves as mediator and radical trap; vinyl-TEMPO adduct (INT2-derived products 2 and 3) isolated, demonstrating trapping of vinyl radical/cation

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO+ (oxoammonium salt, preoxidized TEMPO)
probe_reagents.observation: Substitution of TEMPO+ for TEMPO without electrolysis gave product, confirming TEMPO+ is the active oxidant rather than direct anodic allene oxidation

probe_reagents.name: Acetamido-TEMPO / Bobbitt's salt (ACT+ BF4-)
probe_reagents.observation: Higher-oxidation-potential mediator enabled reaction of electron-poor allene 5f to give 11 in 28%, supporting mediated SET mechanism sensitive to substrate oxidation potential

probe_reagents.name: Hammett series of para-substituted allenes (5a-g, 12a-c)
probe_reagents.observation: Yields track with allene oxidation potential: electron-rich allenes (lower E_ox) react; electron-poor allenes fail, consistent with rate-limiting SET to substrate

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of 1, TEMPO, and mixture
probe_reagents.observation: No new oxidation peak for 1+TEMPO mixture (no EDA complex); TEMPO+ reduction wave diminishes when potential is sufficient to oxidize 1, indicating TEMPO+ consumption by allene (outer-sphere ET)

probe_reagents.name: Constant-potential electrolysis at 1.0 V
probe_reagents.observation: Only recovered SM after 1.25 h; 51% of 2 after 16 h, confirming TEMPO/TEMPO+ mediation rather than direct allene oxidation

probe_reagents.name: ReactNMR (operando 1H NMR)
probe_reagents.observation: Allylic alcohol 2 forms within 5 min; diene 3 appears only after 7 min, consistent with computed lower barrier for hydroxylation vs deprotonation

probe_reagents.name: Competition: allene 1 vs alpha-methylstyrene vs phenylpropyne
probe_reagents.observation: Only allene 1 reacted (52% of 2), reflecting its lowest oxidation potential (1.29 vs 1.43 vs 1.64 V) and supporting SET-controlled selectivity

probe_reagents.name: DFT (omegaB97XD/cc-pVTZ // M06-2X/def2-TZVP, SMD)
probe_reagents.observation: TS_OH-benzyl 11.3 vs TS_diene 13.7 kcal/mol; NPA charge at C3 = 0.37 vs C1 = 0.21 explains hydroxylation regioselectivity at benzylic carbon

10.1021_jacs.4c10431.notes: Two pathways for INT2 access were considered: (A) direct anodic oxidation of allene to INT1 then TEMPO trap, and (B) TEMPO-mediated oxidation via outer- or inner-sphere ET. CV (no shift on mixing 1+TEMPO; loss of TEMPO+ reduction wave only when 1 can be oxidized), TEMPO+ stoichiometric competence, and constant-potential studies favor the outer-sphere TEMPO+-mediated pathway (Scheme 2B, outer-sphere). Direct oxidation (Scheme 2A) and inner-sphere EDA pathway are disfavored. Chemoselectivity between allylic alcohol 2 (Path A, hydroxylation by cathodic -OH) and diene 3 (Path B, deprotonation by lutidine/base) is set kinetically (ΔΔG‡ = 2.4 kcal/mol). Over-oxidation of 2 by a second TEMPO equivalent gives ketone 4 at higher T. Postfunctionalization of 2 with electrophiles (NBS, NIS, Selectfluor, I2, TFA, BF3·OEt2, TEMPO+) achieves allene trifunctionalization.

10.1021_jacs.0c11214.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical reductive coupling of unactivated olefins with ketones to form tertiary homoallylic/saturated alcohols via cathodically generated ketyl radicals at a Sn cathode with a sacrificial Zn anode
10.1021_jacs.0c11214.electrochemical_role: Cathodic one-electron reduction of the ketone at a Sn cathode generates a strongly surface-adsorbed ketyl radical that adds to the olefin; a second cathodic reduction of the resulting C-radical to a carbanion is enabled by Zn2+ (from sacrificial Zn anode) acting as a thermodynamic sink and via ZnBr2 coordination to the ketone
10.1021_jacs.0c11214.electron_flow: At the Sn cathode the ketone undergoes one-electron reduction to a strongly adsorbed ketyl radical (E), which adds to the unactivated olefin (C); a second one-electron reduction of the resulting C-radical (second E), facilitated by Zn2+ from the sacrificial Zn anode, yields a carbanion that is protonated on workup (second C) — an overall ECEC sequence.
10.1021_jacs.0c11214.rate_determining_step: Electrochemical reduction of the ketone (current-limited; kinetics zero-order in all components except current)

key_intermediates.name: β-hydroxy carbon-centered radical (after addition to olefin)

probe_reagents.name: α-substituted ketone 60 (R = H or TBS)
probe_reagents.observation: Elimination of the α-substituent observed (product 62, 48%), consistent with a ketyl radical intermediate

probe_reagents.name: Allyl alcohol 64
probe_reagents.observation: Bis-addition adduct 65 (50%) formed, suggesting carbanion intermediate where ZnBr2 assists departure of OH and regenerates an olefin

probe_reagents.name: Acetone-d6
probe_reagents.observation: 80% D incorporation at the highlighted position of product 67-d (74%), supporting a carbanion intermediate protonated on workup

probe_reagents.name: d7-DMF (with non-deuterated acetone)
probe_reagents.observation: 0% D incorporation in product 68 (72%), ruling out DMF as the proton source

probe_reagents.name: Kinetic order analysis
probe_reagents.observation: Zero-order in all components except current — reduction is purely electrochemical/current-limited

probe_reagents.name: Acetophenone (CV/SWV probe)
probe_reagents.observation: Pre-peaks observed on Sn (not on GC) indicate strong adsorption of ketyl radical to Sn; addition of olefin 61 causes anodic shift in cathodic peak (EC behavior); SWV with ZnBr2 reveals three reduction peaks, the third assigned to ZnBr2-coordinated ketone, supporting ECEC

10.1021_jacs.0c11214.notes: The Sn cathode is critical: strong adsorption of the ketyl radical (evidenced by CV pre-peaks absent on GC) modulates its reactivity, enabling coupling with unactivated olefins rather than typical nucleophilic ketyl behavior. The role of the sacrificial Zn anode is dual: avoids competing anodic oxidation and supplies Zn2+/ZnBr2 that coordinates the ketone, lowering the second reduction potential to make the ECEC pathway feasible. Limitation: α-substituted ketones undergo elimination rather than productive coupling.

10.1021_jacs.4c06207.rxn_class_summary: Copper-electrocatalyzed vicinal 1,2-bis(difluoromethylation) of alkenes using Zn(CF2H)2(DMPU)2 as a dichotomous CF2H radical and anion source
10.1021_jacs.4c06207.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of Zn(CF2H)2(DMPU)2 (Eonset = 0.25 V vs Fc/Fc+) generates a CF2H radical; a second anodic oxidation (Eonset = 0.20 V) converts alkyl-Cu(II)(CF2H) to alkyl-Cu(III)(CF2H)X intermediate; cathode reduces Zn(II) to Zn(0) (Eonset = -1.8 V) to balance charge
10.1021_jacs.4c06207.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes Zn(CF2H)2(DMPU)2 to release a CF2H radical that adds to the alkene terminus, giving a secondary benzylic radical; this radical is captured by an in-situ-formed Cu(II)-CF2H species to form an alkyl-Cu(III)(CF2H)(X) intermediate that undergoes reductive elimination to install the second CF2H group. The resulting Cu(I)-X is re-transmetalated by Zn(CF2H)2(DMPU)2 (the resulting Cu(I)-CF2H is reoxidized anodically to Cu(II)-CF2H), while at the cathode Zn(II) is reduced to Zn(0).
10.1021_jacs.4c06207.rate_determining_step: Reductive elimination from alkyl-Cu(III)(CF2H)(X) intermediate (TS-RE, ΔG‡ ≈ 18.18 kcal/mol from DFT); radical combination with Cu(II) is barrierless

key_intermediates.name: secondary benzylic alkyl radical from alkene addition (11)
alkyl-Cu(III)(CF2H)(X) complex (14, X = OTf or ClO4)

probe_reagents.name: vinylcyclopropane radical clock (substrate 4)
probe_reagents.observation: Ring-opened product 6 obtained as major product (42%) vs cyclopropane-retained 7 (8%), confirming benzylic radical intermediate (k ~ 10^8 s^-1)

probe_reagents.name: unactivated terminal alkene 8
probe_reagents.observation: Gave bis(difluoromethylation) product 9 in 42%, ruling out direct alkene oxidation pathway (path B) since unactivated alkene has higher oxidation potential

probe_reagents.name: Zn(SO2CF2H)2
probe_reagents.observation: Conventional radical CF2H source — gave only hydroxydifluoromethylated 3a' (40%), no vicinal bis product, showing need for dichotomous reagent

probe_reagents.name: NaSO2CF2H
probe_reagents.observation: Only 3a' (42%); no bis(difluoromethylation)

probe_reagents.name: TMSCF2H
probe_reagents.observation: No product detected

probe_reagents.name: no current (control)
probe_reagents.observation: n.d./n.d. — verifies electrochemical input required

probe_reagents.name: no Cu catalyst (control)
probe_reagents.observation: Only 3a' (28%); no vicinal bis-CF2H — Cu essential for second CF2H delivery

probe_reagents.name: Zn(CF3)2(DMPU)2 / Zn(C3F7)2(DMPU)2 / ZnEt2
probe_reagents.observation: None gave vicinal bis(alkylation), highlighting unique dual role of Zn(CF2H)2(DMPU)2

10.1021_jacs.4c06207.notes: Alternative mechanism considered: direct anodic oxidation of alkene to radical cation followed by nucleophilic CF2H addition (path B) — disfavored because (i) unactivated alkene 8 still reacts, (ii) Ep of alkene (2.2 V) ≫ Ep of 2 (0.75 V). Off-pathway: in absence of Cu, benzylic radical is further oxidized to carbocation and trapped by trace water to give hydroxydifluoromethyl product 3a' (radical-polar crossover). DFT shows alkyl-Cu(III)(CF2H)(X) with X = ClO4/OTf has ΔΔG‡ 8–10 kcal/mol lower than bis-CF2H Cu(III), explaining role of counteranion. 19F NMR verified Cu-CF2H intermediates 13 and 16. Cathodic event is Zn(II)→Zn(0) deposition confirmed by EDX.

10.1039_D3GC02701E.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical 1,4-alkoxydimerization of aryl alkenes with aliphatic alcohols to afford symmetric and asymmetric 1,4-dialkoxybutanes
10.1039_D3GC02701E.electrochemical_role: Anodic single-electron oxidation of the styrene to a radical cation; cathodic reduction of protons to H2
10.1039_D3GC02701E.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes styrene to a radical cation (I), which adds to a second styrene giving distonic radical cation II; methanol traps the cation to give carbon radical III, which is further oxidized at the anode to benzyl cation IV; a second methanol addition affords the 1,4-dialkoxybutane while H+ is reduced to H2 at the cathode.

key_intermediates.name: distonic benzyl radical cation from radical addition to second styrene (II)
methoxy-substituted carbon radical (III) after MeOH addition
benzyl carbocation (IV) after second anodic oxidation

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction completely suppressed with 2 equiv BHT; HRMS detected BHT-adduct V capturing radical intermediate III, confirming radical involvement

probe_reagents.name: Styrene (cyclic voltammetry probe)
probe_reagents.observation: Onset oxidation potential ~+1.713 V vs reference, supporting feasibility of anodic SET to form a cationic carbon species

probe_reagents.name: 1-cyclopropylvinylbenzene (radical clock)
probe_reagents.observation: No cyclopropane ring opening observed; instead 1,2-dimethoxylation byproduct A formed (53%), suggesting the radical is oxidized to a cation before ring opening can occur

10.1039_D3GC02701E.notes: Heteroaryl alkenes bearing pyrrole, pyridine, furan (1a-3a) failed, giving complex byproducts. With electron-rich heteroaryl substrate 3a, a 1,2-dimethoxylation side product (B) was observed. Unsuccessful nucleophiles include aniline, benzotriazole, AcOH, TMSCN, and NH4CN. Cross-1,4-alkoxydimerization between two different styrenes is feasible but lower yielding due to homo-dimerization competition.

10.1039_d3gc02701e.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical 1,4-alkoxydimerization of aryl alkenes with alcohols to give symmetric and asymmetric 1,4-dialkoxybutanes
10.1039_d3gc02701e.electrochemical_role: Anodic single-electron oxidation of styrene to a radical cation that initiates dimerization; a second anodic oxidation converts a β-alkoxy carbon radical to a carbocation; cathode reduces H+ to H2
10.1039_d3gc02701e.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes styrene by SET to a radical cation (I), which adds to a second styrene to form distonic cation-radical II; methanol traps the cation end giving β-alkoxy radical III; a second anodic SET oxidizes III to carbocation IV, which is trapped by a second equivalent of methanol to deliver the 1,4-dialkoxybutane; protons are reduced to H2 at the Pt cathode.
10.1039_d3gc02701e.rate_determining_step: Initial anodic single-electron oxidation of styrene (onset ~+1.713 V)

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: 2 equiv BHT fully suppressed product formation; HRMS detected BHT adduct V of radical intermediate III

probe_reagents.name: (1-cyclopropylvinyl)benzene
probe_reagents.observation: No ring-opened product observed; instead 1,2-dimethoxylation byproduct A (53%) and dimer 21 (21%) formed, attributed to fast oxidation of the radical to a cation before cyclopropyl ring-opening

probe_reagents.name: styrene (cyclic voltammetry)
probe_reagents.observation: Onset oxidation potential ~+1.713 V vs reference, confirming feasibility of anodic SET to form a cation/radical cation

10.1039_d3gc02701e.notes: Pyrrole-, pyridine-, and furan-substituted alkenes failed, likely due to competing oxidation of the heteroarene. With the cyclopropyl probe, the absence of ring-opening is rationalized by rapid second SET (radical→cation) outcompeting the radical clock, which is itself mechanistic evidence for the radical-polar crossover step (III→IV). H2O as nucleophile gave only 12% yield; aniline, benzotriazole, AcOH, TMSCN, NH4CN were unsuccessful nucleophiles.

10.1039_D4GC00352G_sup_1.rxn_class_summary: Photoelectrochemical radical cyclization of N-alkenyl quinazolinones with diethyl malonate to form pyrroloquinazolinones via malonyl radical addition/cyclization
10.1039_D4GC00352G_sup_1.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation (assisted by photoexcited Ir(ppy)3) generates a malonyl radical from diethyl malonate, which adds to the pendant alkene; subsequent oxidation/cyclization furnishes the pyrroloquinazolinone
10.1039_D4GC00352G_sup_1.electron_flow: Photoexcited Ir(ppy)3 and/or the anode oxidizes deprotonated diethyl malonate to a malonyl radical, which adds to the alkene tether of 1a; the resulting carbon radical cyclizes onto the quinazolinone and is further oxidized/deprotonated to yield 3a. Both light and current are required.

key_intermediates.name: alkyl radical after malonyl addition to pendant alkene of 1a

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Product 3a not formed (0%), consistent with radical intermediate

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: 3a suppressed (0%)

probe_reagents.name: 1,1-diphenylethylene (DPE)
probe_reagents.observation: Trapping product 4 (diethyl 2-(2,2-diphenylvinyl)malonate) isolated in 66%, confirming malonyl radical generation

probe_reagents.name: p-dinitrobenzene (pDNB)
probe_reagents.observation: 3a not formed (0%)

probe_reagents.name: CuCl2
probe_reagents.observation: 3a not formed (0%)

probe_reagents.name: CCl4
probe_reagents.observation: 3a not formed (0%)

probe_reagents.name: DPE (1,1-diphenylethylene)
probe_reagents.observation: Radical trapping affords 4 in 66% yield

probe_reagents.name: no current control (light only, stoich. Ir(ppy)3)
probe_reagents.observation: 0% 3a — current required

probe_reagents.name: pre-electrolysis then dark/light without current
probe_reagents.observation: 0% 3a — continuous electrolysis required, rules out solely photoredox pathway

10.1039_D4GC00352G_sup_1.notes: Multiple radical scavengers (TEMPO, BHT, pDNB, CuCl2, CCl4) all shut down the reaction, and DPE intercepts the malonyl radical to give 4 (66%), supporting a radical pathway. Control experiments confirm that both blue light and electrolysis are required; neither photoredox alone (Eq 1, no current) nor pre-electrolyzed catalyst in absence of current (Eq 2) delivers product, suggesting cooperative photoelectrochemical generation of the malonyl radical.

10.1039_D5GC02920A_sup_1.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical imino-trifluoromethylation of styrenes with CF3SO2Na and benzophenone imine to give beta-CF3 alpha-imino products
10.1039_D5GC02920A_sup_1.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of CF3SO2- (Langlois reagent) generates CF3 radical via SO2 extrusion; CF3 radical adds to styrene to give a benzylic radical that is further oxidized to a benzylic cation, which is trapped by benzophenone imine
10.1039_D5GC02920A_sup_1.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes CF3SO2- to CF3SO2 radical which loses SO2 to give CF3 radical; CF3 radical adds to the styrene terminal carbon producing a benzylic radical, which is further oxidized at the anode to a benzylic cation; benzophenone imine traps the cation, with TBD/HFIP assisting deprotonation to yield the N-diphenylmethylene amine product 5a.

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Product 5a was not detected, indicating involvement of radical intermediates

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: Product 5a was not detected, supporting a radical pathway

10.1039_D5GC02920A_sup_1.notes: Derivatization of 5a with PPTS in THF/H2O hydrolyzed the diphenylmethylene imine to give the primary amine 6 (3,3,3-trifluoro-1-(4-methoxyphenyl)propan-1-aminium tosylate, 82%), confirming the imine connectivity. Only TEMPO/BHT trapping data are shown on these pages; no CV, KIE, or DFT evidence reported here.

10.1039_D3GC04109C_sup_1.rxn_class_summary: Ni/Fe dual-catalyzed three-component radical 1,2-acylalkylation of alkenes with acyl chlorides and C(sp3)-H alkanes via HAT and radical addition
10.1039_D3GC04109C_sup_1.electron_flow: Fe/Cl-based HAT abstracts a C-H from the alkane to give an alkyl radical, which adds to the acrylate; the resulting α-ester radical is captured by a Ni-acyl species (from oxidative addition of the acyl chloride) and reductive elimination delivers the 1,2-acylalkylated product.

key_intermediates.name: cyclohexyl radical (alkyl C-radical from HAT on cyclohexane)
acyl radical / Ni-acyl species from p-toluoyl chloride
alkyl-Ni intermediate after radical addition to acrylate

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Desired product 4 not detected (n.d.); TEMPO-cyclohexyl adduct 47 detected by GCMS, indicating a free cyclohexyl radical intermediate

10.1039_D3GC04109C_sup_1.notes: Page shows only the TEMPO trapping experiment from the Mechanistic Investigations section; the trapped alkyl radical (cyclohexyl) supports a HAT-generated C(sp3) radical pathway. Standard conditions employ NiBr2·DME / dtbbpy, FeCl3·6H2O, LiCl in acetone/MeCN. Further mechanistic details (e.g., role of Ni vs Fe, acyl radical generation pathway) not visible on this page.

10.1039_D3GC02701E_sup_1.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical methoxylation/dimerization of styrenes with methanol via anodic oxidation proceeding through a radical-cation/radical intermediate
10.1039_D3GC02701E_sup_1.electrochemical_role: anodic oxidation of styrene to a styryl radical cation (onset ~+1.713 V vs Ag/AgCl), which is intercepted by methanol to give a benzylic carbon radical that couples to deliver the 1,4-dimethoxy product
10.1039_D3GC02701E_sup_1.electron_flow: The anode performs single-electron oxidation of styrene (Eonset ≈ +1.713 V) to give a styryl radical cation; methanol adds to the radical cation generating a β-methoxy benzylic carbon radical, which is intercepted (radical coupling/further oxidation) en route to the dimethoxylated coupling product.
10.1039_D3GC02701E_sup_1.rate_determining_step: anodic SET oxidation of the styrene to its radical cation

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: Product 2 was not detected (N.D.); BHT-trapped adduct V containing the β-methoxy benzylic fragment was detected by HRMS, supporting a carbon-radical intermediate

probe_reagents.name: styrene (1)
probe_reagents.observation: CV showed an oxidation onset at +1.713 V vs Ag/AgCl, consistent with direct anodic oxidation of styrene

10.1039_D3GC02701E_sup_1.notes: Mechanism inferred from CV (Figure S3) and BHT trapping (Scheme S1) shown in the SI. The BHT adduct corresponds to the β-methoxy benzylic radical, indicating MeOH addition occurs before the radical-coupling step; no KIE, DFT, or EPR evidence is presented on these pages.

10.1039_D5GC03120F_sup_1.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical 1,4-dimethoxylation of aryl-substituted 1,3-dienes with methanol via a radical pathway, with subsequent conversion of the allylic dimethoxy product to an allylic carbamate using CSI.
10.1039_D5GC03120F_sup_1.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation generates methoxy radical (·OMe) from methanol and oxidizes the aryl diene/allyl intermediate; radical coupling delivers the 1,4-dimethoxy product.
10.1039_D5GC03120F_sup_1.electron_flow: Anodic oxidation of MeOH generates a methoxy radical that adds to the aryl 1,3-diene to form a delocalized allyl/benzyl radical (intermediate II); a second ·OMe couples to the remote (1,4) position, producing the 1,4-dimethoxy product 2a.
10.1039_D5GC03120F_sup_1.rate_determining_step: Coupling of intermediate II with ·OMe (vs. H-abstraction from MeOH); DFT shows pathway (2) is favored (ΔG = -50.85 kcal/mol) over pathway (1) via TS2 at +72.15 kcal/mol.

key_intermediates.name: allyl/benzyl radical intermediate II (methoxy-added diene radical)

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: BHT-OMe adduct (2,6-di-tert-butyl-4-(methoxymethyl)phenol) isolated in 39% yield; target product 2a not detected, indicating radical involvement.

10.1039_D5GC03120F_sup_1.notes: Two radical pathways considered from intermediate II to 2a: pathway (1) H-atom transfer from neutral MeOH via TS2 (+72.15 kcal/mol, releasing H·) and pathway (2) direct coupling with ·OMe (-50.85 kcal/mol). Pathway (2) is strongly favored. A relaxed PES scan of the C–O distance also shows the 1,4-selective intermediate is more stable than the 1,2-selective one (minimum near 1.9 Å for 1,2; lower-energy 1,4 region), rationalizing 1,4- over 1,2-selectivity. Compound 5a is a downstream derivatization (CSI/Na2CO3 then Na2SO3/KOH) of 2a, not part of the electrochemical mechanism.

10.1039_D2GC00960A_sup_1.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical trifluoromethylation/cyclization of N-allyl sulfonamides with CF3SO2Na proceeding via a CF3 radical addition pathway
10.1039_D2GC00960A_sup_1.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of CF3SO2- (Langlois reagent) generates CF3 radical (via loss of SO2) that adds to the allyl alkene of the N-allyl sulfonamide
10.1039_D2GC00960A_sup_1.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes CF3SO2- to CF3SO2 radical, which extrudes SO2 to give CF3 radical; CF3 radical adds to the terminal alkene of the N-allyl sulfonamide, generating a carbon radical that undergoes further cyclization/functionalization to product.

key_intermediates.name: alkyl C-radical from CF3 addition to allyl group

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction suppressed (no desired product); BHT-CF3 adduct 6 detected by GC-MS, supporting CF3 radical intermediate

probe_reagents.name: Crossover experiment with two distinct N-allyl sulfonamides (methyl ester thiophene vs chlorothiophene 4-oxopentanamide)
probe_reagents.observation: Only homo-products 3i (68%) and 3z4 (62%) formed; no crossover products, indicating intramolecular cyclization rather than intermolecular radical exchange

10.1039_D2GC00960A_sup_1.notes: BHT trap confirms CF3 radical involvement; crossover experiment confirms intramolecular nature of the cyclization step (the radical addition/cyclization sequence occurs within a single substrate without intermolecular scrambling).

10.1039_D5GC01259G.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical vicinal amidoselenation of unactivated aliphatic alkenes with diaryl diselenides and acetonitrile via a tandem radical selenation/oxidative Ritter reaction
10.1039_D5GC01259G.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of diphenyl diselenide to a diselenide radical cation that fragments to PhSe+ and PhSe•; the alkyl radical formed after PhSe• addition to the alkene is further oxidized at the anode to a selenonium cation, while protons are reduced at the cathode to H2
10.1039_D5GC01259G.electron_flow: At the anode, Ph2Se2 is oxidized to its radical cation which fragments into PhSe+ and PhSe•; PhSe• adds to cyclohexene to give a β-selenoalkyl radical, which is then oxidized at the anode to a selenonium cation. Acetonitrile attacks this cation to form a nitrilium ion that is trapped by water, deprotonates/isomerizes to the amide product, while H+ is reduced to H2 at the cathode.
10.1039_D5GC01259G.rate_determining_step: Nucleophilic attack of acetonitrile on the selenonium ion (TS4, ΔG‡ ≈ 21.12 kcal/mol) is the highest barrier on the productive pathway

probe_reagents.name: BHT (2,6-di-tert-butyl-4-methylphenol)
probe_reagents.observation: Yield suppressed to 55% (2 eq) and 20% (4 eq), indicating radical involvement

probe_reagents.name: 1,1-diphenylethylene (DPE)
probe_reagents.observation: Complete inhibition (trace product) with 2 eq; PhSe-DPE adduct detected by HRMS (m/z 337.0483)

probe_reagents.name: phenylselenenyl chloride (PhSeCl)
probe_reagents.observation: No product at 65 °C/5 h without oxidant; only 32% with (NH4)2S2O8; 15% under electrochemical conditions — suggests ionic PhSe+ pathway is inefficient and radical pathway dominates

probe_reagents.name: D2O / anhydrous conditions
probe_reagents.observation: Under dry MeCN/N2 only 43% yield obtained, confirming water is the oxygen source for the amide group

probe_reagents.name: selenonium cation 4a and PhSe-DPE adduct 4b
probe_reagents.observation: Both detected by HRMS (239.0332 and 337.0483), confirming selenonium and selenyl radical intermediates

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry
probe_reagents.observation: Cyclohexene is not oxidizable; Ph2Se2 shows Eox = 1.70 V; with TsOH the second oxidation peak of the mixture shifts from 2.36 to 2.10 V, showing acid promotes the electrochemical step

10.1039_D5GC01259G.notes: Authors explicitly rule out a purely ionic PhSe+ pathway (no reaction with PhSeCl alone) in favor of a radical addition by PhSe• followed by anodic oxidation to the selenonium cation (radical-polar crossover). DFT shows the singlet selenonium 1IM2 is strongly favored over triplet 3IM2 (−35.93 vs −1.66 kcal/mol). Hydrogen gas at the cathode confirmed by detection experiment. Water (adventitious from solvent/air) is the source of the amide oxygen via Ritter-type trapping.

10.1039_d5gc01259g.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical amidoselenation of unactivated alkenes with diaryl diselenides via tandem radical selenation/oxidative Ritter reaction to give 1,4-selenoamide products
10.1039_d5gc01259g.electrochemical_role: Anodic single-electron oxidation of diphenyl diselenide to its radical cation, which fragments into PhSe+ and PhSe•; PhSe+ is reduced at the cathode to a second PhSe• radical; after radical addition to alkene, the resulting C-radical is re-oxidized at the anode to a selenonium/carbocation; protons are reduced at the cathode to H2
10.1039_d5gc01259g.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes (PhSe)2 to its radical cation, which fragments to PhSe+ and PhSe•; PhSe+ is reduced at the cathode to a second PhSe•. PhSe• adds to cyclohexene to give a C-radical A, which the anode oxidizes to selenonium/cation B; MeCN attacks (Ritter) to give nitrilium C, water adds to D, and deprotonation/tautomerization yields the amide 3n while protons are reduced to H2 at the cathode.
10.1039_d5gc01259g.rate_determining_step: Water addition to nitrilium intermediate IM3 via TS4 (barrier ~21.12 kcal/mol), the highest barrier on the DFT pathway

key_intermediates.name: selenonium/cyclohexyl cation B (after anodic oxidation of A)
nitrilium ion intermediate C (Ritter adduct with MeCN)

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: Yield suppressed to 55% (2 eq) and 20% (4 eq), consistent with radical involvement

probe_reagents.name: 1,1-diphenylethylene (DPE)
probe_reagents.observation: 2 eq DPE fully inhibited the reaction (trace product); PhSe-DPE adduct detected by HRMS (m/z 337.0490)

probe_reagents.name: HRMS detection of selenonium cation
probe_reagents.observation: C12H15Se+ detected (m/z 239.0332), confirming selenonium ion formation

probe_reagents.name: anhydrous/dry MeCN under N2
probe_reagents.observation: Yield dropped to 43%, indicating moisture/water is the oxygen source for the amide

probe_reagents.name: PhSeCl substitution for (PhSe)2
probe_reagents.observation: No product at 65 °C/5 h; with (NH4)2S2O8 as oxidant 32%; under electrochemical conditions without oxidant 15% — supports radical (not ionic) pathway from diselenide

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry
probe_reagents.observation: Cyclohexene 1n not electroactive in window; (PhSe)2 oxidized at 1.70 V; second oxidation peak of 1n+2n shifts from 2.36 V to 2.10 V upon TsOH addition, showing acid promotes oxidation

probe_reagents.name: Cathodic H2 detection
probe_reagents.observation: H2 evolution confirmed at cathode, consistent with proton reduction completing the redox balance

10.1039_d5gc01259g.notes: Authors explicitly disfavor an ionic PhSe+ addition pathway based on PhSeCl control experiments (no reaction without oxidant); the favored pathway is radical addition of PhSe• followed by anodic oxidation of the C-radical (radical-polar crossover) and Ritter trapping by MeCN/H2O. DFT shows the singlet selenonium cation 1IM2 is much more stable than the triplet 3IM2 (-35.93 vs -1.66 kcal/mol), so the singlet cation is the operative species for nucleophilic capture.

10.1039_D5GC01259G_sup_1.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical amidoselenation of alkenes (e.g., cyclohexene) with diphenyl diselenide and acetonitrile to give trans-1,2-selenyl-amido (acetamide) products via a seleniranium/Ritter-type cation pathway
10.1039_D5GC01259G_sup_1.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of PhSeSePh (or PhSe•) generates an electrophilic PhSe+ species that adds to the alkene to form a seleniranium ion; acetonitrile then performs Ritter-type nucleophilic capture
10.1039_D5GC01259G_sup_1.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes PhSeSePh to PhSe• / PhSe+; PhSe+ adds to the alkene forming a seleniranium cation (IM1 → 1IM2), which is opened by acetonitrile to give a nitrilium ion (IM3); water addition and tautomerization (via TS4/TS5) deliver the trans-β-selenyl acetamide product.
10.1039_D5GC01259G_sup_1.rate_determining_step: Structural transition IM1 → 1IM2 (formation of the cationic seleniranium via single-electron oxidation), as supported by ADCH charge analysis and DFT

key_intermediates.name: 1IM2: cationic seleniranium ion (singlet, after SET oxidation)
IM3: nitrilium ion from MeCN attack on seleniranium
IM6: allylbenzene–PhSe+ seleniranium (branching point for Markovnikov vs anti-Markovnikov)

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: Yield of 3n dropped from 92% to 55% (0.2 mmol) and 20% (0.4 mmol), indicating involvement of radical intermediates

probe_reagents.name: 1,1-diphenylethylene (DPE)
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction suppressed to trace yield, consistent with a radical (PhSe•) intermediate

10.1039_D5GC01259G_sup_1.notes: DFT (PBE0(D3BJ)/Def2SVP, SMD/MeCN) maps both singlet (1IM2) and triplet (3IM2) seleniranium pathways; singlet cationic pathway is favored. For allylbenzene, anti-Markovnikov TS6 (16.45 kcal/mol) is slightly higher than Markovnikov TS7 (15.95 kcal/mol), and IM7 (–6.45) is more stable than IM8 (–4.63), rationalizing regioselectivity. ADCH atomic charge analysis on IM1→1IM2 for cyclopentene/cyclohexene/cycloheptene explains substrate-dependent yields: cyclohexene's negative C2 charge (–0.1664) best enables SET oxidation to the seleniranium cation, while cyclopentene's positive C2 charge blocks the conversion.

10.1039_D3GC02128A.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical oxidative 1,2-aryl migration coupling of allyl alcohols with sulfonyl hydrazides to access γ-keto sulfones via a radical-mediated semipinacol-type rearrangement
10.1039_D3GC02128A.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of sulfonyl hydrazide (2a) to a sulfonyl diazonium/radical species (loss of 3H+, 3e-, then N2 extrusion gives a sulfonyl radical); a later C-centered radical intermediate is further oxidized at the anode (-e-) to a cation that loses a proton to give the ketone product; protons are reduced at the cathode to H2
10.1039_D3GC02128A.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes the sulfonyl hydrazide (2a) with loss of 3H+/3e- to a diazonium species A, which extrudes N2 to give sulfonyl radical B; B adds to the C=C of allyl alcohol 1a, forming carbinol radical C, which undergoes 1,2-aryl migration to ketyl-type radical D; D is oxidized at the anode to cation E, deprotonation of which yields the γ-keto sulfone 3aa, while protons are reduced at the cathode to H2.

key_intermediates.name: sulfonyl diazonium intermediate A (ArSO2-N=N+) from anodic oxidation of TsNHNH2
sulfonyl radical B (ArSO2•) after N2 extrusion
C-centered tertiary carbinol radical C (sulfonyl radical added to C=C of allyl alcohol)
carbocation E (anodic oxidation of radical D)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Addition of 3.0 equiv TEMPO totally prevented the transformation (0% of 3aa), indicating a radical pathway

probe_reagents.name: BHT (butylated hydroxytoluene)
probe_reagents.observation: 3.0 equiv BHT greatly decreased conversion (3aa yield dropped to 36%), consistent with radical intermediates

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of 1a, 2a, and 1a+2a
probe_reagents.observation: 2a shows oxidation peaks at 1.50 V and 2.01 V vs Ag/AgCl while 1a oxidizes at 2.36 V; in the mixture the 1a peak shifts, indicating 2a is preferentially oxidized at the anode and interacts with 1a

probe_reagents.name: X-ray diffraction of product 4
probe_reagents.observation: Structure of spirocyclic sulfone 4 (no 1,2-migration product from rigid benzo-cycloheptane substrate) confirmed by single-crystal XRD (CCDC 2268921), supporting steric control of the migration pathway

10.1039_D3GC02128A.notes: For rigid benzo-cycloheptane-substituted allyl alcohols, no 1,2-aryl migration occurs and the sulfonyl radical instead adds to the C=C to give spirocyclic/epoxide-type sulfones (products 4 and 5), attributed to steric hindrance preventing the aryl shift; this represents a divergent radical pathway off the main rearrangement mechanism.

10.1039_D4GC00352G.rxn_class_summary: Photoelectrocatalytic dehydrogenative carbocyclization of unactivated alkene-tethered quinazolin-4-ones with diethyl malonate to give polycyclic pyrroloquinazolinones, with H2 as sole byproduct
10.1039_D4GC00352G.electrochemical_role: Anodic PCET oxidation of base-activated diethyl malonate generates a malonate radical; cathode reduces protons to H2; photoexcited Ir(ppy)3* (via oxidative quenching) reoxidizes the cyclized N-centered radical to a nitranion (electron shuttle anchoring an EC intermediate)
10.1039_D4GC00352G.electron_flow: Base-activated diethyl malonate is oxidized at the anode via PCET to a malonate radical that adds to the tethered alkene; the resulting alkyl radical cyclizes onto the quinazolinone amidine to give an N-centered radical C, which is oxidized by photoexcited Ir(ppy)3* to a nitranion D (regenerating Ir(III) at the cathode); protonation, anodic oxidation, and deprotonation deliver the aromatized product while protons are reduced to H2 at the cathode.

key_intermediates.name: malonate radical (A) from PCET of diethyl malonate
alkyl radical B from addition of malonate radical to tethered alkene
N-centered cyclohexadienyl-type radical C from intramolecular amidine radical trapping
nitranion D from SET reduction of C by excited Ir(ppy)3*
tetrahydropyrroloquinazolinone E (protonated D, detected by HRMS)
Ir(ppy)3 / Ir(ppy)3* / Ir(IV) photocatalyst states (oxidative quenching cycle)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction suppressed (0% 3a)

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction suppressed (0% 3a)

probe_reagents.name: 1,1-diphenylethylene (DPE)
probe_reagents.observation: Malonate–DPE adduct 5 isolated in 66%, implicating malonate radical

probe_reagents.name: p-dinitrobenzene (pDNB)
probe_reagents.observation: SET inhibitor suppresses the reaction

probe_reagents.name: CuCl2
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction suppressed (0%)

probe_reagents.name: CCl4
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction suppressed (0%)

probe_reagents.name: IrCl4 (as alternative oxidant)
probe_reagents.observation: No reaction without current, ruling out Ir(IV) as key oxidant of substrate (path c)

probe_reagents.name: Electrochemically generated Ir(IV) (no light control)
probe_reagents.observation: Failed to promote carbocyclization without electricity, ruling out oxidative quenching shuttle (path b)

probe_reagents.name: UVA LEDs (365/385 nm, no PC)
probe_reagents.observation: Only 35–38% (vs 36% dark) — no triplet sensitization of 1b operative (path d excluded)

10.1039_D4GC00352G.notes: Authors explicitly rule out four alternative pathways: (a) reductive quenching photocatalytic cycle alone, (b) Ir(IV) as electron shuttle between anode and substrate, (c) highly oxidized excited-state Ir(IV)* acting as photooxidant, and (d) energy-transfer triplet sensitization of the quinazolinone 1b. Favored proposal is anodic generation of malonate radical (paths f/g) with Ir(ppy)3 oxidative quenching cycle reoxidizing the cyclohexadienyl N-radical C to nitranion D. H2 evolution at cathode confirmed by detection experiments (Fig. S24). Intermediate E detected by HRMS.

10.1039_d4gc00352g.rxn_class_summary: Photoelectrocatalytic dehydrogenative carbocyclization of unactivated alkene-tethered quinazolin-4-ones with diethyl malonate to give polycyclic pyrroloquinazolinones, with H2 as the sole byproduct.
10.1039_d4gc00352g.electrochemical_role: Anodic PCET oxidation of base-activated diethyl malonate to the malonate radical; cathodic reduction of protons to H2; the Ir(ppy)3 oxidative quenching photocatalytic cycle anchors and oxidizes the cyclized carbon radical intermediate (IrIII* oxidizes C to nitranion D, regenerated IrII reduced back to IrIII at the anode/by anodic events).
10.1039_d4gc00352g.electron_flow: Base-activated diethyl malonate undergoes anodic PCET to give a malonate radical, which adds to the tethered alkene of 1a to form alkyl radical B; intramolecular cyclization onto the amidine N gives radical C, which is oxidized by photoexcited IrIII* to nitranion D (closing the oxidative-quenching photocycle, with IrII reoxidized to IrIII). Protonation of D yields E, anodically oxidized/deprotonated to product 3a, while protons are reduced at the cathode to H2.

key_intermediates.name: malonate radical (deprotonated diethyl malonate, one-electron oxidized) - intermediate A
alkyl radical B from addition of malonate radical onto tethered alkene of 1a
N-centered (amidinyl) cyclized radical C after intramolecular radical trapping by amidine N
nitranion intermediate D (from SET oxidation of C by excited IrIII*)
tetrahydropyrroloquinazolinone E (protonated D, detected by HRMS)
pyrroloquinazolinone product 3a (after anodic oxidation of E)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction suppressed (0% 3a), consistent with radical intermediates

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction suppressed (0% 3a)

probe_reagents.name: 1,1-diphenylethylene (DPE)
probe_reagents.observation: Malonate-DPE adduct 5 isolated in 66%, indicating involvement of malonate radical

probe_reagents.name: p-dinitrobenzene (pDNB)
probe_reagents.observation: SET inhibitor suppressed the reaction

probe_reagents.name: CuCl2
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction suppressed

probe_reagents.name: CCl4
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction suppressed

probe_reagents.name: Pre-generated IrIV / IrCl4 (stoichiometric oxidant, no current)
probe_reagents.observation: No 3a formed, ruling out a discrete IrIV oxidant pathway (path c)

probe_reagents.name: UVA irradiation without PC
probe_reagents.observation: No beneficial effect (35-38% vs 36% dark), excluding triplet energy transfer sensitization of 1b (path d)

probe_reagents.name: On/off light and current experiments
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction halts when either light or current is off, ruling out a radical chain

probe_reagents.name: Hydrogen detection
probe_reagents.observation: H2 confirmed at cathode, consistent with proposed cathodic proton reduction

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of 1a, 2a, 3a, Ir(ppy)3
probe_reagents.observation: E_p/2 values established: 2a-base 0.83 V, 1a 1.92 V, 3a -0.72 V vs SCE; IrIII* reduction potential 0.31 V; supports anodic malonate oxidation and unfavorable SET from C to IrIII* being thermodynamically uphill only when product 3a (-1.85 V vs SCE for IrII/IrIII).

10.1039_d4gc00352g.notes: Authors explicitly disfavor alternative paths: (path a) merged electrolysis + reductive quenching Ir cycle - excluded by Stern-Volmer (2a does not quench IrIII*) and failure of stoichiometric Ir(ppy)3 photooxidant without current; (path b) IrIV electron-shuttle - excluded by failure of electrogenerated IrIV and IrCl4 to drive the reaction without current; (path c) excited IrIV oxidative pathway and (path d) triplet sensitization of quinazolinone 1b - excluded by UVA experiments without PC; (path e) reductive-quenching SET from C to IrIII* - excluded as thermodynamically unfavorable. Favored mechanism is the oxidative-quenching photocatalytic cycle (paths f/g) that anchors and rapidly oxidizes the anodically generated radical intermediate C. Hydrogen evolution at cathode maintains electron balance.

10.1039_D1GC02914B.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical oxidative radical cascade cyclization of olefinic amides with thiophenols/diselenides to synthesize sulfurated/selenylated benzoxazines, oxazolines, and iminoisobenzofurans without a metal catalyst or external oxidant
10.1039_D1GC02914B.electrochemical_role: Anodic single-electron oxidation of thiophenol generates a sulfur radical (which dimerizes reversibly to disulfide); cathodic reduction of the disulfide back to thiyl radical/thiolate closes the loop, with cathodic proton reduction to H2
10.1039_D1GC02914B.electron_flow: At the anode, thiophenol is oxidized by SET to a thiyl radical (RS•) which reversibly dimerizes to RS-SR; the disulfide is reduced at the cathode to a radical anion that fragments to RS• + RS−, regenerating thiyl radicals. RS• adds to the C=C of the olefinic amide to give alkyl radical INT-I, which undergoes intramolecular cyclization onto the amide oxygen and a second anodic SET oxidation to cation INT-II, followed by deprotonation to the heterocyclic product; protons are reduced at the cathode to H2.

key_intermediates.name: alkyl radical INT-I (C-radical after thiyl addition to alkene, cyclized onto amide oxygen pending)
cyclized carbocation INT-II (after radical cyclization and anodic oxidation)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO/radical trap control (radical pathway probe, short-time reaction)
probe_reagents.observation: Stopping reaction at 1.5 h gave product 3a in 10% yield and disulfide in 80% yield, supporting thiyl radical intermediacy

probe_reagents.name: Disulfide 2q as substrate
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction of disulfide 2q with N-(2-(prop-1-en-2-yl)phenyl)benzamide 1a gave 3a in 73% yield, confirming disulfide as a competent intermediate

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of 1a and 4-chlorobenzenethiol 2e
probe_reagents.observation: Oxidation peaks: 1a at 1.80 V, 2e at 1.51 V — thiol is preferentially oxidized at the anode; reduction peak of diphenyl disulfide at −1.14 V indicates cathodic reduction of disulfide

10.1039_D1GC02914B.notes: Mechanism is paired: anodic oxidation of thiol initiates the radical, while cathodic reduction of disulfide regenerates thiyl radical and produces H2. Authors also performed derivatizations (mCPBA oxidation to sulfoxide/sulfone, HCl hydrolysis of iminoisobenzofuran and oxazoline) which are downstream functionalizations, not mechanistic probes.

10.1039_d2gc00960a_si1.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical radical trifluoromethylation/cyclization of N-allylsulfonamides using CF3SO2Na as the CF3 radical source
10.1039_d2gc00960a_si1.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of CF3SO2- (Langlois reagent) to generate CF3 radical via loss of SO2; the CF3 radical then adds to the allyl group of the N-allylsulfonamide
10.1039_d2gc00960a_si1.electron_flow: At the anode, CF3SO2Na is oxidized with loss of SO2 to give a CF3 radical, which adds to the alkene of the N-allylsulfonamide to form a carbon radical that undergoes intramolecular cyclization onto the sulfonamide-tethered arene/heteroarene to give the cyclized product after rearomatization.

key_intermediates.name: carbon-centered radical from CF3 addition to allyl group

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction fully suppressed (no desired product) and BHT-CF3 adduct 6 detected by GC-MS, supporting a CF3 radical intermediate

probe_reagents.name: crossover substrates (methyl 5-(N-acetyl-N-allylsulfamoyl)thiophene-2-carboxylate and N-allyl-N-((5-chlorothiophen-2-yl)sulfonyl)-4-oxopentanamide)
probe_reagents.observation: Products 3i (68%) and 3z4 (62%) were obtained with no crossover products, indicating the cyclization is intramolecular

10.1039_d2gc00960a_si1.notes: Mechanism inferred from the two control experiments shown (BHT radical quench and crossover). The intramolecular nature of the C-radical cyclization onto the (hetero)arene is supported by the absence of crossover products.

10.1039_d5gc03300d.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical oxidative synthesis of tetralones from styrenes (olefins) and water using a platinum nanoparticle anode, proceeding via radical cation intermediate and intramolecular Friedel-Crafts annulation
10.1039_d5gc03300d.electrochemical_role: Anodic single-electron oxidation of styrene to a styryl radical cation; second anodic oxidation of a benzylic radical to a benzylic carbocation; cathodic reduction of protons to H2
10.1039_d5gc03300d.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes styrene by SET to a radical cation, which couples with a second styrene to give a distal 1,4-radical cation; intramolecular Friedel-Crafts annulation and -H+ yields a benzylic radical, which is further oxidized at the anode to a carbocation that is trapped by water; subsequent oxidation/deprotonation gives the tetralone, while protons are reduced to H2 at the cathode.
10.1039_d5gc03300d.rate_determining_step: Initial anodic single-electron oxidation of the styrene to its radical cation (consistent with measured oxidation potential ~1.96 V vs SHE and DFT 1.922 V)

key_intermediates.name: Wheland-type cyclohexadienyl radical cation from Friedel-Crafts annulation (C)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction significantly inhibited; TEMPO-adduct 3v detected by HRMS, supporting a radical intermediate

probe_reagents.name: 1,1-diphenylethylene (DPE)
probe_reagents.observation: Radical addition product 3x detected by HRMS, indicating a styryl/benzylic radical species

probe_reagents.name: tetralin-1-ol intermediate 3a'
probe_reagents.observation: Isolated in 17% yield at half reaction time; resubjection under standard conditions delivered tetralone 3a in 95% yield, confirming it as on-pathway

probe_reagents.name: H2(18)O
probe_reagents.observation: 18O is incorporated into product 3a'' (HRMS), proving the carbonyl oxygen comes from water, not O2

10.1039_d5gc03300d.notes: CV shows two oxidative overpotentials (+1.74 V and +2.17 V vs Ag/AgCl), consistent with sequential SET steps (styrene -> radical cation; benzylic radical D -> carbocation E). DFT computes substrate oxidation potential (1.922 V vs SHE) closely matching the experimental 1.964 V, and shows the A->D pathway is energetically downhill with a low barrier (TS1 at +3.38 kcal/mol). Substrates with strong electron-withdrawing groups, beta-methylstyrene, pyridine, and thiophene fail, consistent with the requirement to form a stabilized styryl radical cation on an electron-rich arene.

10.1039_d4gc01127a.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical oxidative coupling of cyclic beta-keto esters with styrenes to form beta-keto spirolactones via double anodic oxidation and water trapping under green solvent conditions
10.1039_d4gc01127a.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of the deprotonated beta-keto ester enolate to an alpha-keto radical, followed by a second anodic oxidation of the benzylic radical adduct to a benzylic carbocation; cathode reduces protons/water to H2
10.1039_d4gc01127a.electron_flow: Base deprotonates the beta-keto ester; the anode oxidizes the enolate to an alpha-keto radical (I) which adds to the styrene to give a benzylic radical (II); a second anodic oxidation gives a benzylic carbocation (III), which is trapped intermolecularly by water to alcohol IV, and intramolecular lactonization affords the spirolactone, while the cathode reduces protons to H2.

key_intermediates.name: alpha-keto radical from beta-keto ester (intermediate I)
benzylic radical adduct after styrene addition (intermediate II)
benzylic alcohol after water trapping (intermediate IV)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: TEMPO adduct 6 isolated in 89% yield, supporting formation of benzylic radical II; TEMPO adduct 7 of the alpha-keto radical detected by mass spectrometry

probe_reagents.name: H2-18O (18O-labeled water)
probe_reagents.observation: 18O incorporated into the lactone oxygen of product 4 (48% overall yield), demonstrating that water (not the ester) supplies the lactone oxygen, consistent with mechanism A (intermolecular water attack on carbocation)

10.1039_d4gc01127a.notes: Two mechanistic pathways are considered after the carbocation III: Mechanism A (favored) involves intermolecular attack of water on the benzylic carbocation to give alcohol IV, followed by intramolecular lactonization; Mechanism B involves intramolecular ester oxygen attack to form oxocarbenium IV' followed by hydrolysis. 18O-labeling experiments support Mechanism A. Isolation of hemiketal byproducts 3s/3t is also consistent with water trapping of the carbocation. Reaction performed in acetone/water with LiClO4 electrolyte, 2,6-lutidine base, graphite anode and Ni-foam cathode, runs under batch and continuous flow.

10.1039_d2gc03879j.rxn_class_summary: Telescoped electrocatalytic coupling of aromatic alkenes with NH3 and CO2 (or CS2) via an iodide-mediated N-H aziridination followed by CO2/CS2 ring expansion to give 2-oxazolidinones (or 2-thiazolidinethiones).
10.1039_d2gc03879j.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of iodide generates an electrophilic NH2I species (from NH3) that mediates N-H aziridination of the styrene; H2 is evolved at the cathode. Iodide is regenerated and reused in the subsequent (non-electrochemical) ring expansion step.
10.1039_d2gc03879j.electron_flow: At the anode, iodide is oxidized and combines with NH3 to give NH2I, which adds across the styrene to form a vicinal iodoamine; base-assisted cyclization releases iodide and gives the N-H aziridine, while protons are reduced to H2 at the cathode. In the second (thermal) cycle, the aziridine nitrogen attacks CO2 to form a carbamate zwitterion; iodide opens the ring regioselectively at the benzylic carbon, and ring closure expels iodide to deliver the 2-oxazolidinone, regenerating the iodide mediator.

key_intermediates.name: zwitterionic aziridinium carbamate adduct (aziridine N + CO2)
ring-opened iodo-carbamate (iodide attack at benzylic carbon)

probe_reagents.name: LiI (variable loading: 0, 0.2, 2 equiv)
probe_reagents.observation: CO2 insertion yield drops from 92% (2 equiv LiI) to 9% (no halide) despite full aziridine consumption; higher LiI suppresses oligomerization and improves regioselectivity (2a:3a up to 18:1).

probe_reagents.name: LiBr
probe_reagents.observation: Similar trend to LiI but less pronounced, confirming halide mediator role; iodide is the better halide.

probe_reagents.name: trans-beta-methylstyrene
probe_reagents.observation: Gives cis/trans product mixture (59:41 cis-major), indicating loss of stereochemistry consistent with an SN1-like ring-opening at the benzylic carbon.

probe_reagents.name: Telescoped vs. salt-removed control
probe_reagents.observation: Removing salts after aziridination and re-adding controlled LiI amounts shows iodide is required for efficient ring expansion (double use of catalyst confirmed).

10.1039_d2gc03879j.notes: Regioselectivity for the 5-aryl isomer is rationalized by benzylic-cation stabilization favoring iodide attack at the more hindered benzylic carbon (SN1-like), supported by partial racemization with trans-beta-methylstyrene. An analogous mechanism is proposed for CS2 to give 2-thiazolidinethiones. Parasitic pathways (oligomerization/copolymerization of aziridine with CO2) compete when iodide is low.

10.1039_d0gc03890c_si1.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical anti-Markovnikov hydroboration of styrenes with HBpin using Pt electrodes, nBu4NBF4 electrolyte and DIEA in CH3CN/THF
10.1039_d0gc03890c_si1.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of CH3CN and/or HBpin generates radical cations that, after deprotonation, yield .CH2CN and .Bpin radicals; the .Bpin radical adds to the styrene, and the resulting benzylic C-radical is reduced at the cathode to a carbanion that is protonated by DIEA-H+ (or THF-H+) to give the hydroboration product
10.1039_d0gc03890c_si1.electron_flow: At the anode, CH3CN (and/or HBpin, or DIEA when present) is oxidized by SET to a radical cation; deprotonation/H-atom loss gives .CH2CN and .Bpin radicals. .Bpin adds to the terminal carbon of styrene to give a benzylic radical, which is reduced at the cathode to a carbanion and protonated by [DIEA-H]+/[THF-H]+ to deliver the anti-Markovnikov hydroboration product, while H+ reduction at the cathode releases H2.

probe_reagents.name: THF-d8 (solvent label)
probe_reagents.observation: No deuterium incorporation into 3a, indicating THF is not the proton source for the benzylic position under standard conditions

probe_reagents.name: CD3CN (solvent label)
probe_reagents.observation: Partial D incorporation at benzylic position, d1-3a:3a = 84:16 (47% yield), implicating CH3CN as the proton/H source for the benzylic C-H

probe_reagents.name: d8-styrene
probe_reagents.observation: 1H NMR shows a single new H at the benzylic methine (1.22 ppm), confirming the H delivered to benzylic carbon comes from an external proton source (CH3CN/DIEA), Bpin goes to terminal CH2

probe_reagents.name: galvinoxyl radical
probe_reagents.observation: Galvinoxyl-Bpin adduct (8) detected by MS, supporting formation of .Bpin radical

probe_reagents.name: DMPO
probe_reagents.observation: EPR shows DMPO-H adduct under standard conditions; in absence of HBpin or styrene, DMPO-CH2CN (10) is also observed, confirming .CH2CN radical formation by anodic oxidation of CH3CN

probe_reagents.name: H2 detection by GC
probe_reagents.observation: H2 evolution monitored by SHIMADZU GC-2014 in various omission experiments, consistent with cathodic proton reduction

10.1039_d0gc03890c_si1.notes: Two mechanistic cycles are proposed: (i) standard conditions with DIEA, where anodic SET on DIEA gives [DIEA]+. that mediates generation of .Bpin and .CH2CN; the benzylic radical from .Bpin addition to styrene is reduced cathodically and protonated by [DIEA-H]+; (ii) in the absence of DIEA, direct anodic oxidation of CH3CN and HBpin produces the same radicals, with THF/[THF2-H]+ acting as proton shuttle. A separate cycle for diboration (with 2-vinylnaphthalene, 7r) shows that a second .Bpin radical traps the benzylic C-radical before cathodic reduction; control experiments rule out B2pin2 involvement, so the diboronate ester is not formed from hydroboration product.

10.1039_d3gc04109c.rxn_class_summary: Photoelectrocatalytic nickel-catalyzed three-component carboacylation/silanoylation of alkenes using acyl chlorides and unactivated C(sp3)-H donors or hydrosilanes via Fe-mediated LMCT chlorine radical HAT
10.1039_d3gc04109c.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation regenerates [FeIIICl4]- from [FeIICl3]- (reduced after LMCT/HAT); cathodic reduction of Ni(II) acyl intermediate F to Ni(I) species G enables turnover of the nickel cycle
10.1039_d3gc04109c.electron_flow: Visible light excites [FeIIICl4]- which undergoes LMCT to release Cl• and [FeIICl3]-; Cl• abstracts a C(sp3)-H (or Si-H) to give a carbon (or silyl) radical D, while anodic oxidation of [FeIICl3]- with Cl- regenerates [FeIIICl4]-. In parallel, Ni(I) oxidatively adds to acyl chloride to give Ni(III)-acyl F, which is cathodically reduced to Ni(II)-acyl G; D adds to the alkene to give secondary radical E, which is captured by G to form Ni(III) alkyl-acyl H that reductively eliminates the ketone and regenerates Ni(I).
10.1039_d3gc04109c.rate_determining_step: Not the C-H cleavage step (KIE kH/kD = 1.15); turnover-limiting step not explicitly assigned

key_intermediates.name: [FeIICl3]- (C) after LMCT loss of Cl radical
secondary alkyl radical after radical addition to acrylate (E)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction fully suppressed with 3 equiv TEMPO; cyclohexyl-TEMPO adduct 47 detected by GCMS, supporting cyclohexyl radical intermediate

probe_reagents.name: TBABClO4 (replace LiCl electrolyte)
probe_reagents.observation: No product formed, showing LiCl/chloride is essential for in situ [FeCl4]- formation

probe_reagents.name: Cyclohexyl chloride (in place of cyclohexane)
probe_reagents.observation: No target product, ruling out alkyl chloride as a key intermediate

probe_reagents.name: Ni(COD)2
probe_reagents.observation: Only trace product, suggesting Ni(0) is not in the main catalytic cycle

probe_reagents.name: Preformed Ni(II) complex 48 (NiBr2·dtbbpy analog)
probe_reagents.observation: Stoichiometric: no product without photocatalyst; catalytic (10 mol%) gave 55% yield, supporting Ni(II) complex as a competent catalyst

probe_reagents.name: [D]-cyclohexane (d12-cyclohexane)
probe_reagents.observation: Parallel KIE kH/kD = 1.15, indicating C-H cleavage is not turnover-determining

10.1039_d3gc04109c.notes: Dual catalytic cycle (Fe photoredox/LMCT + Ni cross-coupling) coupled to electrochemistry: anode regenerates Fe(III), cathode reduces Ni(III) to Ni(II)/Ni(I). CV data: Ni(II)/Ni(I) at -1.38 V and Ni(I)/Ni(0) at -1.88 V vs Fc/Fc+; Fe(II)/Fe(III) at -0.09 V vs Fc/Fc+. Hydrosilane variant proceeds analogously with Si-H HAT by Cl• to give silyl radical, then addition to alkene and Ni-mediated acylation.

10.1039_d3gc00837a.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical regioselective 1,2-aminoselenation and oxyselenation of alkenes (arylalkenes, 1-aryl-1,3-dienes, and non-activated aliphatic alkenes) using diselenides with N–H nucleophiles (e.g., saccharin) or carboxylic acids in an undivided cell with C anode / Ni cathode.
10.1039_d3gc00837a.electrochemical_role: Anodic single-electron oxidation of the diaryl diselenide (R'SeSeR') generates a diselenide radical cation that dissociates into an electrophilic selenium cation (R'Se+) and a selenium radical (R'Se•); cathode reduces protons to H2.
10.1039_d3gc00837a.electron_flow: Path I (favored, ionic): anode oxidizes R'SeSeR' to its radical cation, which fragments to R'Se+ and R'Se•; R'Se+ adds to the alkene to give a cyclic seleniranium ion that is opened by the N- or O-nucleophile with deprotonation to deliver the 1,2-difunctionalized product, while protons are reduced to H2 at the Ni cathode. Path II (radical, not excluded): R'Se• adds to the alkene to give a β-selenyl C-radical, which is anodically oxidized to the corresponding carbocation and then trapped by the nucleophile.

key_intermediates.name: β-selenyl carbocation E (from NuH addition / ring opening)
β-selenyl carbocation I (from anodic oxidation of H)

probe_reagents.name: single-crystal X-ray of products 5y and 7m / 4d'
probe_reagents.observation: X-ray structures confirmed regio- and stereochemistry of the 1,2-aminoselenation/oxyselenation products

10.1039_d3gc00837a.notes: Authors propose two concurrent pathways: a favored ionic seleniranium pathway (Path I) and a non-excluded radical pathway (Path II) via R'Se•-addition followed by anodic oxidation of the C-radical to a cation. Mechanistic probe experiment details are relegated to the ESI and not shown explicitly on the main-text pages; no TEMPO/BHT trapping, KIE, CV, EPR, or DFT data are presented here. Reaction tolerates strongly deactivated substrates (e.g., -NO2) and works on non-activated aliphatic alkenes; scaled to gram scale and applied to late-stage modification of bioactive molecules (estrone, probenecid, ibuprofen, ciprofibrate, etc.). Conditions: C(+)/Ni(−), Et4NBr in CH3CN, 10 mA, r.t., 2 h, undivided cell.

10.1039_d2gc00960a.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical stereoselective migratory heteroaryltrifluoromethylation of allylic amines via anodic oxidation of Langlois reagent and radical Smiles-type N-to-C heteroaryl migration
10.1039_d2gc00960a.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of Langlois reagent (CF3SO2Na) at RVC anode to generate CF3SO2 radical, which loses SO2 to give CF3 radical; cathode evolves H2 (proton reduction) but does not directly reduce the organic substrate
10.1039_d2gc00960a.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes CF3SO2- to CF3SO2 radical which releases SO2 to give CF3 radical; CF3 radical adds to the terminal alkene of the allylic N-heteroarylsulfonamide, the resulting C-radical undergoes intramolecular ipso addition onto the heteroaryl group (5-membered TS) forming a spirocyclic intermediate that fragments with SO2 loss to an N-centered radical, which is then reduced by in-situ-generated H2SO3 (from SO2 + H2O) and protonated to give the heteroaryl-migrated β-heteroaryl-γ-CF3 amine; cathode reduces protons to H2.

key_intermediates.name: CF3 radical (from anodic oxidation of CF3SO2Na with SO2 loss)
Carbon-centered radical I from CF3 addition to terminal alkene of allylic sulfonamide
Spirocyclic cyclohexadienyl radical II from ipso addition onto heteroaryl (Smiles rearrangement TS)
Nitrogen-centered (amidyl/sulfonyl-N) radical III after SO2 extrusion and heteroaryl migration
H2SO3 / HSO3- (from SO2 + H2O) acting as chemical reductant of N-radical
Oxidative cyclization byproduct 3d' (competing pathway when H2SO3 is low)

probe_reagents.name: BHT (2,6-di-tert-butyl-4-methylphenol)
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction completely suppressed; BHT-CF3 adduct 6 detected by GC-MS, supporting CF3 radical intermediate

probe_reagents.name: Crossover substrates (two different N-heteroarylsulfonamides mixed)
probe_reagents.observation: No crossover products (3i and 3z4 obtained cleanly, 68% and 62%), indicating intramolecular Smiles migration

probe_reagents.name: CD3CN (deuterated solvent)
probe_reagents.observation: 0% D incorporation in 3a, excluding H-abstraction from solvent by N-radical

probe_reagents.name: Divided cell experiment
probe_reagents.observation: Product obtained in similar yield (70%), excluding paired electrolysis / cathodic reduction of substrate

probe_reagents.name: Current on/off experiment
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction only proceeds under applied current; ~41% Faradaic efficiency, arguing against a long radical-chain process

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of 1a, CF3SO2Na, 3a amide anion, and H2SO3
probe_reagents.observation: 1a inert 0-2 V; CF3SO2Na oxidation ~1.37 V; amidyl anion of 3a 0.70 V; H2SO3 0.62 V — consistent with anodic initiation at Langlois reagent and H2SO3 acting as chemical reductant of N-radical

probe_reagents.name: Added H2SO3 / N2 bubbling (varying SO2 content)
probe_reagents.observation: More H2SO3 favors migration product 3d over oxidative cyclization 3d' (3d:3d' from 2:1 anhydrous up to 8:1 with added H2SO3), supporting H2SO3 as reductant

probe_reagents.name: Ion chromatography for sulfate
probe_reagents.observation: SO4(2-) byproduct detected, consistent with H2SO3 reducing N-radical to amide

10.1039_d2gc00960a.notes: Formally redox-neutral electrosynthesis: anodically generated SO2 (from Langlois reagent decomposition and Smiles rearrangement) is converted with water to H2SO3, which serves as the chemical reductant of the nitrogen radical, so no net stoichiometric reductant is required. A competing direct oxidative cyclization pathway (giving 3d') operates when [H2SO3]/[SO2] is low (anhydrous CH3CN or N2 bubbling). Alternative reduction pathways considered and excluded: (i) paired electrolysis (excluded by divided-cell), (ii) chain reduction by CF3SO2Na (CV shows it is harder to reduce than H2SO3), and (iii) H-abstraction from CH3CN (excluded by CD3CN). Stereoselectivity is rationalized by a favored 5-membered cyclic migration transition state placing CF3 away from α-substituent (Scheme 3c).

10.1039_d2gc02134j.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical chlorosulfuration or chlorosulfoxidation of unactivated olefins with thiophenols and HCl, with selectivity switched by operating conditions
10.1039_d2gc02134j.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of thiophenol to thiyl radical and subsequent anodic oxidation of carbon radical to carbocation (and sulfide to sulfonium/oxonium for sulfoxide formation); cathode reduces protons to H2
10.1039_d2gc02134j.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes ArSH to thiyl radical I (−H+), which adds to cyclohexene to give C-radical II; II is further oxidized at the anode to carbocation III (≡ sulfonium IV), which is trapped by Cl− to give sulfide 3a. Under prolonged electrolysis, 3a is anodically oxidized to a sulfur-centered cation radical V, trapped by H2O to oxonium VI, then oxidized to VII and deprotonated to sulfoxide 4a; cathode reduces 2H+ to H2.

key_intermediates.name: thiyl radical (ArS•) from anodic oxidation of p-toluenethiol
carbon-centered radical from thiyl addition to cyclohexene
carbocation from anodic oxidation of carbon radical
sulfonium intermediate (IV, resonance form of III)
cationic sulfur radical V from anodic oxidation of sulfide
sulfonium VII after further anodic oxidation

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction shut down; TEMPO–sulfide adduct 5 detected by HRMS, indicating radical intermediates

probe_reagents.name: Bis(4-methylphenyl) disulfide (6)
probe_reagents.observation: Isolated in 60% after 3 h; when used in place of ArSH gave 4a in 82%, supporting thiyl radical formation via disulfide

probe_reagents.name: H2 18O
probe_reagents.observation: 18O-labeled sulfoxide 7 obtained (7:4a = 69:31, 76%), showing the sulfoxide oxygen comes from H2O (in concentrated HCl), not O2

10.1039_d2gc02134j.notes: Selectivity between chlorosulfuration (3) and chlorosulfoxidation (4) is controlled by electrolysis time/conditions: shorter time gives sulfide 3a, longer time leads to further anodic oxidation to sulfoxide 4a with H2O as the oxygen source. Carbocation III and sulfonium IV are presented as resonance forms of the same intermediate.

10.1039_d5gc03120f.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical 1,4-selective dialkoxylation of 1,3-dienes with alcohols in an undivided cell to give (E)-1,4-diether alkenes
10.1039_d5gc03120f.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of methoxide (MeO-) to methoxy radical (MeO•); subsequent anodic oxidation of allyl/benzyl radical intermediate II to allyl cation III
10.1039_d5gc03120f.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes MeO- to MeO•, which adds to the terminal carbon of the 1,3-diene to give an allyl radical that rearranges to the more stable (E)-benzylic radical II; II is further oxidized at the anode to allyl cation III, which is trapped by MeOH/MeO- to deliver the 1,4-diether (path A, favored over direct radical-radical coupling of II with MeO•, path B).
10.1039_d5gc03120f.rate_determining_step: Anodic ionization of radical II to cation III (computed ionization energy 107.21 kcal/mol); overall radical addition/rearrangement step is strongly exergonic (-31.11 kcal/mol)

key_intermediates.name: primary allyl radical I from MeO• addition to terminal alkene of diene
allyl/benzyl cation III (after one-electron oxidation of II)

probe_reagents.name: BHT (added at t=0, 3.0 eq)
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction completely inhibited; BHT-OMe adduct isolated in 39% yield, confirming generation of MeO• radical

probe_reagents.name: BHT (added after 2 h, 3.0 eq)
probe_reagents.observation: Mono-methoxylation BHT-trapped radical intermediate detected by HRMS, supporting carbon radical intermediate II

10.1039_d5gc03120f.notes: Two pathways considered: path A (oxidation of radical II to cation III, then MeO- capture; methoxylation step exergonic by 58.28 kcal/mol, favored) vs path B (direct radical-radical coupling of II with MeO•). Path A favored thermodynamically and by suppression of homocoupling via steric effects. DFT shows (E)-II more stable than (Z)-II by 3.82 kcal/mol and 1,4-radical II more stable than 1,2-radical II' by 2.80 kcal/mol in MeOH complex state, rationalizing E- and 1,4-selectivity.

10.1039_C8GC01337C.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical oxidative oxysulfuration of alkenes with thiophenols and oxygen nucleophiles (water/alcohols/carboxylic acids) to give β-hydroxy sulfides, β-alkoxy sulfides, and thio-substituted γ-lactones.
10.1039_C8GC01337C.electrochemical_role: Anodic single-electron oxidation of thiophenol to aryl sulfur radical, and a second anodic oxidation of the resulting benzylic C-radical to a carbocation; cathode reduces protons to H2 and regenerates aryl sulfide anion.
10.1039_C8GC01337C.electron_flow: At the anode, ArSH is oxidized by SET to ArS•, which adds to the alkene to give a benzylic radical B; B is further oxidized at the anode to benzylic cation C, which is trapped by the O-nucleophile (H2O, ROH, or carboxylate) to deliver the oxysulfuration product and a proton. At the cathode, protons are reduced to H2 while ArSH/ArS− is regenerated.

probe_reagents.name: 1,1-diphenylethylene (toluylene, 49)
probe_reagents.observation: Trapping product 50 isolated in 72% yield, consistent with involvement of radical intermediates (ArS• adding to alkene).

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of 4-chlorothiophenol (34)
probe_reagents.observation: Oxidation peak at 1.99 V and reduction peak at -1.33 V in MeCN; both fall within the applied cell potential, supporting thiophenol involvement in both anodic and cathodic processes.

10.1039_C8GC01337C.notes: Lower yields for styrenes (secondary benzyl radical) vs α-methylstyrenes (tertiary benzyl radical) are taken as additional evidence for a radical pathway. The mechanism is presented as a paired electrolysis: anodic oxidation generates ArS• and the cation, while cathodic proton reduction releases H2 and regenerates ArSH via ArS−.

10.1039_d4gc05119j.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical dehydrogenative annulation between N-vinylamides (enamides) and O-nucleophiles (water, alcohols, carboxylic acids) to form 4-oxo-oxazolines, mediated by anodically generated bromine from NaBr.
10.1039_d4gc05119j.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of Br- to Br2 (mediator); Br2 functionalizes the enamide N-H to an N-Br intermediate which homolyzes to an N-centered radical; a second anodic SET converts the resulting C-radical to a carbocation. Cathode reduces H2O to H2/OH-.
10.1039_d4gc05119j.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes Br- to Br2, which brominates the amide N-H of the enamide to give N-Br species A; homolysis gives amidyl radical B that tautomerizes to alpha-C radical C; a second anodic one-electron oxidation produces N-acyliminium/carbocation D, which undergoes O-nucleophile addition, intramolecular cyclization, and deprotonation to the 4-oxo-oxazoline. Cathode reduces water to H2 and hydroxide.

key_intermediates.name: O-nucleophile adduct (J, e.g., hydroxyethyl-amide from H2O addition)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction partially inhibited (2a yield drops to 30%); TEMPO-radical adduct E (C18H27N2O2+, m/z 303.2056) and its hydrolysates F (C7H8NO+, 122.0599) and G (C11H22NO2+, 200.1639) detected by HRMS, confirming carbon radical C.

probe_reagents.name: H2O (nucleophile trap on cation)
probe_reagents.observation: Open-chain hydroxyethyl amide H (C9H10NO2+, 164.0701) and hydrolysate I (C2H4O2+, 61.0283) detected by HRMS, supporting cationic intermediate D.

probe_reagents.name: NaBr / 1a CV
probe_reagents.observation: NaBr oxidation peak at 1.19 V; 1a shows no oxidation between 0–2 V, indicating reaction is initiated by anodic oxidation of bromide rather than direct substrate oxidation.

probe_reagents.name: benzamide (observed byproduct)
probe_reagents.observation: Trace benzamide observed as side product, consistent with hydrolysis of intermediates C/D.

10.1039_d4gc05119j.notes: Bromide serves as a redox mediator (in situ Br2 generation) rather than as an incorporated atom. The mechanism proceeds through a radical (C) → cation (D) sequence (radical-polar crossover). Authors note intermediate D is supported because nucleophilic water adduct H was also detected. Cathode half-reaction is H2O reduction (H2 + OH-).

10.1039_d0gc03890c.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical anti-Markovnikov hydroboration of styrenes with HBpin in CH3CN/THF using DIEA, proceeding via a radical pathway with CH3CN as the primary hydrogen donor to the benzyl position
10.1039_d0gc03890c.electrochemical_role: Anodic single-electron oxidation of DIEA to an N-centered radical cation, which then oxidizes CH3CN and HBpin to their radical cations; the resulting Bpin radical adds to styrene and the benzyl radical/boronate alkyl intermediate is reduced at the cathode, where H+ is also reduced to H2
10.1039_d0gc03890c.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes DIEA to a radical cation, which abstracts electrons from CH3CN and HBpin, generating •CH2CN and •Bpin after deprotonation by THF/DIEA. The boryl radical adds to styrene to give a benzyl radical, which is reduced at the cathode to a boronate alkyl anion that is protonated by H+ to deliver the product, with concomitant cathodic H2 evolution.

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Product yield decreased from 72% to 8% with increasing TEMPO loading; alkyl-TEMPO adduct 9 (m/z 387.3053/388.3030) isolated, confirming benzyl C-radical

probe_reagents.name: Galvinoxyl free radical
probe_reagents.observation: No product formed with 1 equiv; alkyl-galvinoxyl adduct 8 (m/z 548.4141/549.4119) detected by MS, confirming Bpin radical

probe_reagents.name: DMPO
probe_reagents.observation: EPR signal (g=2.0052, A_N=14.47 G, A_H=20.82 G) and MS detection of DMPO-CH2CN adduct 10 confirmed •CH2CN

probe_reagents.name: THF-d8 / CD3CN
probe_reagents.observation: No D incorporation with CH3CN/THF-d8 (THF not H source); with CD3CN/THF gave d1-3a:3a = 84:16 with D at benzyl position, identifying CH3CN as primary H donor

probe_reagents.name: d1-HBpin (DBpin)
probe_reagents.observation: DBpin + styrene gave d1-3a:3a = 16:84, indicating HBpin contributes only minor H at benzyl site

probe_reagents.name: d8-styrene
probe_reagents.observation: d8-styrene + HBpin gave d8-3a (65%), confirming Bpin originates from HBpin

probe_reagents.name: H2 evolution monitoring
probe_reagents.observation: H2 bubbles detected by GC; rate decays to zero at ~180 min matching reaction time; substrate-free runs show CH3CN+DIEA generate large H2, while THF is not a H+ source

10.1039_d0gc03890c.notes: Authors note that direct quantitative detection of free Bpin radical was not possible; it may be stabilized by DIEA/THF. The lifetime/migration of the persistent benzyl radical F between electrodes is acknowledged as an open question. THF likely assists proton transfer to substrate ([THF]2H+) rather than serving as H source. With 2-vinylnaphthalene and excess HBpin (4.4 equiv), diboronate ester 7r forms selectively (71%), suggesting the benzyl radical can be further borylated rather than reduced/protonated when an extended π-system stabilizes it.

10.1039_D2GC01909D.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical three-component coupling of alkenes (α-methyl styrenes), potassium metabisulfite (SO2 source), and alcohols to afford β-alkoxyl sulfonate esters
10.1039_D2GC01909D.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of potassium metabisulfite generates SO2 radical cation A; cathode reduces protons to H2
