10.1039_D2GC01909D.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes K2S2O5-derived SO2 to a SO2 radical cation (A), which adds to the terminal carbon of α-methyl styrene to give β-sulfonyl C-radical B; a second anodic oxidation converts B into a benzylic cation C, which is trapped by methanol (D→E) with proton release (reduced to H2 at cathode), and a second methanol addition/deprotonation furnishes the β-alkoxyl sulfonate ester.

key_intermediates.name: β-sulfonyl carbon radical from addition of A to α-methyl styrene terminal carbon (B)
benzylic cation bearing CH2-SO2 group (C) after second anodic oxidation
β-methoxy sulfonyl cation intermediate (E) after deprotonation
α,β-unsaturated sulfonate ester 4 (from bulky alcohol, evidence of cation at α-position)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: 5.0 equiv TEMPO fully suppressed formation of 3aa; starting alkene 1a largely recovered, indicating radical intermediates

probe_reagents.name: 1-adamantanemethanol (2g, bulky alcohol)
probe_reagents.observation: Gave α,β-unsaturated sulfonate ester 4 in 26% yield, indicating SO2 adds at less hindered terminal carbon and a carbocation forms at the α-position

10.1039_D2GC01909D.notes: Cyclic voltammetry showed oxidation peak of K2S2O5 at 1.97 V (lower than α-methyl styrene at 2.2 V), supporting initial oxidation of metabisulfite rather than the alkene. Mechanism postulated based on ref 11 analogous reports. Cathode role is proton reduction to H2 (paired electrolysis, no productive cathodic chemistry on substrate).

10.1039_d5gc00295h.rxn_class_summary: Electrocatalytic reductive head-to-head homo-coupling of styrenes to 1,4-diphenylbutanes via cathodically generated alkene radical anions
10.1039_d5gc00295h.electrochemical_role: Cathodic reduction of styrene (and/or of nBuI from nBu4NI decomposition) generates an alkene radical anion; the anode oxidizes iodide back to I2, closing the redox cycle
10.1039_d5gc00295h.electron_flow: At the cathode, styrene is directly reduced (or via SET from a cathodically generated nBuI radical anion) to a radical anion B; B adds to another styrene to give coupled radical anion C, which is protonated by trace water to a benzyl radical D, reduced again at the cathode to carbanion E, and finally protonated to give 1,4-diphenylbutane; iodide is reoxidized at the anode to I2.

key_intermediates.name: benzyl radical D (after protonation of C by H2O)
benzyl carbanion E (after second cathodic reduction)
nBu3N radical cation (HAT donor in path b)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction shut down (only trace product), supporting radical intermediate

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction shut down (trace product)

probe_reagents.name: CBr4
probe_reagents.observation: Yield dropped to 17%, consistent with radical pathway

probe_reagents.name: DMF-d7
probe_reagents.observation: No D incorporation in product (27% yield, 0% D); DMF is not the H source

probe_reagents.name: D2O (1 equiv) in anhydrous DMF
probe_reagents.observation: 63% D incorporation at benzylic positions, identifying water as a major proton source

probe_reagents.name: anhydrous DMF (no added H2O)
probe_reagents.observation: Yield decreased sharply to 25%, indicating water is needed as proton source; residual H must come from nBu4NI

probe_reagents.name: CH3I
probe_reagents.observation: Benzyl monomethylated product detected (56%), trapping a benzyl carbanion intermediate

probe_reagents.name: KI instead of nBu4NI
probe_reagents.observation: Only trace product; iodide alone insufficient — nBu3N component of nBu4NI is critical

probe_reagents.name: KI + nBu3N (0.5 equiv)
probe_reagents.observation: Restored 30% yield, confirming role of nBu3N (HAT pathway)

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of styrene / nBu4NI
probe_reagents.observation: nBu4NI reduces at -0.804 V; styrene + nBu4NI shows enhanced reduction current at -0.776 V with peak position essentially unchanged from styrene alone, indicating direct cathodic reduction of styrene promoted by nBu4NI

10.1039_d5gc00295h.notes: Two parallel cathodic initiation pathways are proposed: (i) main pathway — direct cathodic reduction of styrene to radical anion B (supported by CV); (ii) minor pathway — cathodic reduction of nBuI (from nBu4NI decomposition to nBuI + nBu3N, detected by LC-MS/1H NMR) to radical anion A, which then SETs to styrene. Final protonation can occur via H2O (path a, supported by D2O labeling) or via HAT from nBu3N radical cation to radical anion C (path b). At the anode, I- is oxidized to I2 (1/2 I2), making the process paired electrolysis without external oxidant/reductant or metal catalyst.

10.1039_d2gc04399h.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical 1,2-diesterification (diacyloxylation) of alkenes with carboxylic acids using nBu4NOAc as both electrolyte and electrocatalyst to give 1,2-diesters under undivided-cell, metal/oxidant-free conditions.
10.1039_d2gc04399h.electrochemical_role: Anodic single-electron oxidation of acetate (from nBu4NOAc) to acetoxy radical, which acts as a homogeneous mediator that performs SET on the alkene to give an alkene radical cation; further anodic oxidation of the β-acyloxy radical intermediate to a carbocation. At the cathode, protons are reduced to H2.
10.1039_d2gc04399h.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes AcO− to AcO•; AcO• performs SET on the alkene to regenerate AcO− and form an alkene radical cation (I); carboxylic acid adds to I giving β-acyloxy radical II, which is further oxidized at the anode to carbocation III; a second carboxylic acid attacks III to deliver the 1,2-diester, while H+ is reduced to H2 at the cathode.

key_intermediates.name: acetoxy radical (AcO•) — mediator generated at anode
β-acyloxy carbon radical (II) after nucleophilic addition of R4COOH to radical cation
β-acyloxy carbocation (III) after second SET oxidation
acetate anion (AcO−) regenerated after SET with alkene
hydroxy ester by-product (e.g., 32, 33) from water as competing nucleophile

probe_reagents.name: DMPO (spin trap)
probe_reagents.observation: EPR signal observed, simulated as a mixture of DMPO-R (alkyl radical adduct: g=2.00593, AN=14.8 G, AH=21.3 G) and DMPO-OR (alkoxy radical adduct: g=2.00609, AN=12.9 G, AH=7.9 G), supporting a radical process.

probe_reagents.name: nBu4NOAc / nBu4NClO4 (CV substrate scan)
probe_reagents.observation: Onset potentials: nBu4NOAc +0.715 V, 1a +1.155 V, 1b +1.998 V, product 1 +1.766 V — acetate is the easiest to oxidize, supporting AcO• as mediator; increasing [nBu4NOAc] suppresses the oxidation peak of product 1, indicating acetate protects the product from over-oxidation.

probe_reagents.name: Electricity on/off and current variation (6 vs 3 mA)
probe_reagents.observation: No reaction without current; yield drops from 72% to 60% at lower current — electricity is essential.

probe_reagents.name: Et4NOCOCF3 (electrolyte swap)
probe_reagents.observation: Only trace product, confirming acetate (not generic carboxylate/electrolyte) is the active mediator.

probe_reagents.name: 3 Å molecular sieves / dry solvent
probe_reagents.observation: Removing water suppresses hydroxy ester by-product, consistent with cationic intermediate III being trapped by competing H2O nucleophile.

10.1039_d2gc04399h.notes: nBu4NOAc plays a dual role as electrolyte and electrocatalyst (acetate mediator). Authors note that decarboxylation of AcO• is slower than SET with the alkene under these conditions. Hydroxy ester by-products (e.g., from 2a, 3a giving 30, 31) arise from water trapping the carbocation III, supporting a polar (cationic) step after radical–polar crossover rather than direct radical coupling. The DMPO experiment also detects an alkoxy radical, possibly from secondary reactions, so the EPR evidence is suggestive rather than definitive for a single radical.

10.1039_c9gc02665g.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical oxidative tandem cyclization of olefinic carbonyls (1,3-dicarbonyls and N-allylamides) with diselenides to form seleno-dihydrofurans and seleno-oxazolines, constructing C-Se and C-O bonds simultaneously
10.1039_c9gc02665g.electrochemical_role: Cathode reduces diaryl diselenide to a [RSeSeR]•- radical anion that fragments into ArSe• and ArSe-; the selenyl radical adds to the alkene, and the resulting C-radical is oxidized at the anode to a carbocation that is trapped intramolecularly by the enol oxygen
10.1039_c9gc02665g.electron_flow: At the cathode, ArSeSeAr accepts one electron to form a radical anion that dissociates into ArSe• and ArSe-; ArSe• adds across the alkene of the olefinic carbonyl giving a β-selenyl C-radical, which is oxidized at the anode to a cation, then intramolecular O-attack by the enol oxygen and deprotonation deliver the dihydrofuran (or oxazoline). Protons are reduced at the cathode to H2.

key_intermediates.name: alkyl C-radical from PhSe• addition to alkenyl carbonyl (intermediate II)
alkyl carbocation after anodic oxidation of II

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Addition of 0.6 mmol (2 equiv) TEMPO suppressed the reaction; only trace 3ba detected, supporting radical intermediates

probe_reagents.name: diphenyl diselenide (CV study)
probe_reagents.observation: Cyclic voltammetry showed oxidation at +1.8 V and reduction at -1.5 V vs Ag/AgCl, indicating PhSeSePh can participate in both anodic and cathodic processes

10.1039_c9gc02665g.notes: Authors propose two pathways: path (a, favored) involves cathodic reduction of PhSeSePh to PhSe• which adds to the alkene, followed by anodic oxidation of the resulting C-radical; path (b) cannot be fully ruled out — direct anodic oxidation of 1b first to an enol/α-carbonyl radical that is then attacked by PhSe•/PhSeSePh. H2 evolution at the cathode is invoked to balance the redox.

10.1039_d5gc02979a.rxn_class_summary: Electroreductive linear hydrodimerization of allylbenzenes and vinylarenes to 1,4-diarylbutanes via in situ generated vinylarene radical anions
10.1039_d5gc02979a.electrochemical_role: Cathodic single-electron reduction of the vinylarene (formed in situ by electrochemical isomerization of allylbenzene) to an alkene radical anion; Mg sacrificial anode is oxidized to Mg(II)
10.1039_d5gc02979a.electron_flow: Allylbenzene first undergoes electrochemical isomerization to a vinylarene, which is reduced at the cathode by SET to an alkene radical anion; protonation (from solvent) gives a benzylic radical that adds to another vinylarene, and the resulting benzylic radical is either further reduced to a carbanion and protonated or completes the product via HAT, while the Mg anode supplies electrons by oxidation to Mg(II).

key_intermediates.name: vinylarene (styrene-type) intermediate from electrochemical isomerization of allylbenzene
benzylic radical (II) after protonation of radical anion
benzylic radical adduct (III) from radical addition to second vinylarene
benzylic carbanion (IV) from second cathodic reduction of III

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction completely inhibited; BHT-radical adduct detected by HRMS (m/z 357.2424)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Product yield dropped to 10%; TEMPO-adduct detected by HRMS (m/z 262.2165)

probe_reagents.name: 1,2-diphenylcyclopropane (substrate 11)
probe_reagents.observation: Underwent radical anion-mediated ring-opening reductive hydrogenation to 1,2-diphenylethane (12, 95%) with no dimerization, indicating radical anion does not directly homocouple

probe_reagents.name: benzyl ether substrate 9 (allyl OBn vinylarene)
probe_reagents.observation: Conversion of 9 to isopropylbiphenyl (10, 9%) supports benzyl carbanion intermediate

probe_reagents.name: CD3CN (solvent)
probe_reagents.observation: Product 2l' obtained in 31% yield with 98% D incorporation - H comes from solvent

probe_reagents.name: DMSO-d6
probe_reagents.observation: 2l' in 66% yield with 120% D incorporation (suggests minor benzylic C-H/D exchange)

probe_reagents.name: D2O
probe_reagents.observation: With 20 eq D2O, reduced product 13 (67%, 30% D) and coupled 2l' (25%, 9% D); low D suggests competitive D2O electrolysis

probe_reagents.name: sterically hindered olefin 7
probe_reagents.observation: Afforded vinylarene 8 in 45% yield, confirming allylbenzene-to-vinylarene isomerization

10.1039_d5gc02979a.notes: Authors note that for sterically congested 1,1,2-trisubstituted alkenes, addition of a tertiary radical to C1 is unfavorable, so a radical-radical coupling mechanism may operate instead for these substrates. The H source in products is primarily the solvent (DMSO/MeCN), not added proton donors. Anode material was modified to accommodate electron-deficient styrenes (1n).

10.1039_D2GC01932A.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical thiocyanation of enamides with NH4SCN to afford E-configured vinyl thiocyanates
10.1039_D2GC01932A.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of SCN- (from NH4SCN) generates the thiocyanate radical (·SCN), which adds to the enamide; cathode reduces protons to H2
10.1039_D2GC01932A.electron_flow: SCN- is anodically oxidized to ·SCN (in equilibrium with NCS-SCN), which adds to the enamide alkene to give a nitrogen-stabilized radical/iminium intermediate A; loss of a proton and bond rotation gives isomers B/C, and a [1,5]-H sigmatropic rearrangement (path I) followed by deprotonation delivers the E-vinyl thiocyanate, while protons are reduced to H2 at the cathode.

key_intermediates.name: thiocyanate radical (·SCN, in equilibrium with NCS-SCN)
Intermediate A: N-centered radical cation from SCN addition to enamide alpha-carbon
Intermediate B: alpha-SCN iminium after H loss/tautomer (rotational isomer)
Intermediate C: rotational isomer of B (precursor to [1,5]-H shift)
Intermediate D: enol/iminium after [1,5]-H sigmatropic rearrangement

probe_reagents.name: 1,1-diphenylethylene (DPE)
probe_reagents.observation: DPE-SCN adduct 9 formed and only trace 3aa observed, supporting a free ·SCN radical intermediate

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic enamide 10 (mechanistic probe)
probe_reagents.observation: Thiocyanation of cyclic enamide 10 gave 11 as E/Z = 10:1 mixture (E confirmed by X-ray), consistent with [1,5]-H sigmatropic rearrangement (path I) controlling E-selectivity

probe_reagents.name: NH4SCN (2a) — cyclic voltammetry
probe_reagents.observation: Quasi-reversible oxidation at Epa = 1.12 V vs Ag/AgCl, much lower than enamide 1a (2.28 V), indicating SCN- is oxidized first to generate ·SCN

probe_reagents.name: Enamide 1a — cyclic voltammetry
probe_reagents.observation: Irreversible oxidation at 2.28 V vs Ag/AgCl, higher than NH4SCN, so 1a is not the species oxidized at the anode

10.1039_D2GC01932A.notes: Two pathways considered for E-selectivity: path I via [1,5]-H sigmatropic rearrangement of rotamer C (favored, consistent with E-selectivity of cyclic enamide 10 product 11), and path II via direct deprotonation of rotamer B where steric clash between N-benzyl and SCN would favor the E-isomer. Authors favor path I for acyclic enamides based on the cyclic substrate experiment.

10.1039_D3GC00837A_sup_1.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical vicinal aminoselenation and oxyselenation of alkenes (e.g., cyclohexene, styrene) using saccharin or carboxylic acids as N/O nucleophiles and diphenyl diselenide as the Se source
10.1039_D3GC00837A_sup_1.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of bromide (from VBImBr/Et4NBr) generates Br radical/Br2 which oxidizes PhSeSePh to PhSe radical/PhSe+; the seleniranium-type cation (alkene + PhSe+) is then opened by the N- or O-nucleophile
10.1039_D3GC00837A_sup_1.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes Br- to Br•/Br2, which oxidizes PhSeSePh to PhSe• and/or PhSe+; addition to the alkene gives a seleniranium (or β-selenyl carbon radical that is further oxidized to a cation), which is opened by saccharin N–H or carboxylic acid to give the vicinal aminoselenation/oxyselenation product. Cathode reduces protons to H2.

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: Little effect on yield (styrene 98%; cyclohexene aminoselenation 91%; oxyselenation 92%) — minor radical involvement in early steps

probe_reagents.name: activated carbon
probe_reagents.observation: Modest decrease (83% cyclohexene aminoselenation; 75% oxyselenation) — partial radical character

probe_reagents.name: 1,1-diphenylethylene
probe_reagents.observation: Yield drops to 87% (aminoselenation) and 84% (oxyselenation); HRMS detected vinyl selenide c' (C20H17Se, 337.0485), consistent with PhSe• addition to DPE

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Strong inhibition: styrene aminoselenation 79%, cyclohexene aminoselenation drops to 20% (0.4 mmol) and trace (0.8 mmol); oxyselenation trace. HRMS detected TEMPO–cyclohexyl–SePh adduct e', confirming a β-selenyl carbon radical intermediate

probe_reagents.name: HRMS detection of seleniranium b'/d'
probe_reagents.observation: Seleniranium cations b' (from styrene, m/z 261.017) and d' (from cyclohexene, C12H15Se+ calcd 239.0334, found 239.0329) directly observed by HRMS

10.1039_D3GC00837A_sup_1.notes: Two mechanistic regimes are supported: (i) a radical pathway in which PhSe• adds to the alkene to give a β-selenyl carbon radical (trapped by TEMPO as adduct e'), which is oxidized to a cation and captured by N/O nucleophile; (ii) an ionic pathway via a seleniranium cation (b', d' detected by HRMS) opened by the nucleophile. The strong TEMPO inhibition and isolation of e' favor the radical-then-cation (radical polar crossover) route, while seleniranium detection indicates the cationic pathway may operate in parallel. Bromide acts as a redox mediator.

10.1039_D2GC04399H_sup_1.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical 1,2-diacyloxylation of styrenes with carboxylic acids to give vicinal diesters in acetone with nBu4NOAc electrolyte
10.1039_D2GC04399H_sup_1.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation generates carboxyl/styrene-derived radical species; carboxylate is oxidized at the anode and the styrene is converted via radical addition/oxidation to a benzylic cation that is trapped by carboxylate
10.1039_D2GC04399H_sup_1.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes the carboxylate (1b) to an acyloxy radical that adds to the styrene (1a) giving a benzylic radical; further anodic oxidation yields a benzylic cation trapped by a second carboxylate to deliver the 1,2-diester, with cathodic H2 evolution balancing charge.

key_intermediates.name: styrene-derived benzylic radical/cation after carboxyl radical addition
acyloxy radical from anodic oxidation of carboxylate
hydroxy ester by-product (32, 33) from water/hydroxide trapping

10.1039_D2GC04399H_sup_1.notes: Page shows solvent/co-solvent screen (acetone best, 72%; MIBK, cyclopentanone, diethyl carbonate alone fail due to voltage overload or no reaction; aqueous co-solvent suppresses yield, consistent with hydroxy ester by-products 32 and 33). Mechanistic section indicates EPR experiments were performed but spectra/details are not on this page.

10.1039_d5gc02920a.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical three-component imino-trifluoromethylation of olefins using Langlois reagent (CF3SO2Na) and benzophenone imines as bis-nucleophilic CF3 and amine sources, providing trifluoromethylated primary amine precursors.
10.1039_d5gc02920a.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of CF3SO2- (Langlois reagent) generates CF3 radical with loss of SO2; the benzyl radical formed after CF3 addition to the olefin is further oxidized at the anode to a benzyl cation that is trapped by the imine nitrogen; cathode reduces protons to H2.
10.1039_d5gc02920a.electron_flow: CF3SO2- is preferentially oxidized at the anode (Eox ~1.39 V vs olefin ~1.63 V; mixture shows first peak at 1.27 V), extruding SO2 to give CF3•, which adds to the styrene to form a benzyl radical; this radical is oxidized at the anode to a benzyl cation captured by the imine nitrogen to form the C–N bond, with cathodic H+ reduction to H2.

key_intermediates.name: benzyl radical after CF3 addition to styrene
iminium / N-attack adduct leading to C=N product
α-amino C-radical (Path b alternative: benzyl radical adds to C=N of imine)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction completely inhibited; product 5a N.D.

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction completely inhibited

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of CF3SO2Na / styrene / imine combinations
probe_reagents.observation: Eox(CF3SO2Na)=1.39 V; Eox(olefin)≈1.63 V; mixture shows first oxidation at 1.27 V, indicating CF3SO2- is oxidized preferentially

probe_reagents.name: Control without electricity
probe_reagents.observation: No product (N.D.), confirming electrochemical initiation

probe_reagents.name: Hydrolysis derivatization (PPTS, THF/H2O)
probe_reagents.observation: Imine 5a hydrolyzed to β-CF3 primary amine tosylate 6 in 82% yield, supporting imine intermediate

probe_reagents.name: LiAlH4 reduction attempt
probe_reagents.observation: Failed to reduce C=N to secondary amine (steric hindrance), indirectly supporting imine structure

10.1039_d5gc02920a.notes: Authors favor Path a (radical–polar crossover: CF3• addition, anodic oxidation of benzyl radical to cation, then nucleophilic capture by imine). An alternative Path b is proposed but not excluded: the benzyl radical adds directly to the C=N bond of the imine to give an aminyl/α-amino radical, which is then oxidized at the anode with loss of an N–H proton to regenerate the C=N. Single-crystal X-ray (CCDC 2456921) confirms product structure.

10.1039_D3GC05126A.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical epoxidation of alkenes using water as the oxygen source, catalyzed by a sterically hindered manganese porphyrin (TMP)MnCl under non-halide-mediated conditions
10.1039_D3GC05126A.electrochemical_role: Anodic single-electron oxidation of (TMP)Mn(III)-OH to (TMP)Mn(IV)=O, which either undergoes a second anodic SET or disproportionates to generate the active [(TMP)Mn(V)=O]+ oxidant; protons are reduced to H2 at the Pt cathode
10.1039_D3GC05126A.electron_flow: At the anode, (TMP)Mn(III)-OH is oxidized by SET to (TMP)Mn(IV)=O; a second SET (or disproportionation of two Mn(IV)=O units) generates [(TMP)Mn(V)=O]+, which transfers its oxygen atom to the alkene to give the epoxide and regenerate (TMP)Mn(III)-OH, while H2O supplies the oxygen and protons are reduced to H2 at the Pt cathode.
10.1039_D3GC05126A.rate_determining_step: Generation of [(TMP)Mn(V)=O]+ via the second single-electron anodic oxidation / disproportionation of (TMP)Mn(IV)=O

key_intermediates.name: (TMP)Mn(III)(OH) resting/active catalyst formed with pyridine and H2O
epoxide product via O-atom transfer to alkene

probe_reagents.name: H2-18O isotope labeling
probe_reagents.observation: 18O incorporated into styrene oxide (2a'), confirming water as the oxygen source rather than O2

probe_reagents.name: N2 atmosphere control
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction proceeds under N2, ruling out molecular oxygen as the O source

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of (TMP)MnCl
probe_reagents.observation: Two redox peaks at 0.67 and 0.9 V vs Fc/Fc+ assigned to Mn(III)/Mn(IV) and Mn(IV)/Mn(V); reversible Mn(III)/Mn(IV) peak indicates stable tetravalent intermediate

probe_reagents.name: Constant potential electrolysis at 0.75 V vs Fc/Fc+
probe_reagents.observation: Styrene epoxidation gave 2a in 87% GC yield with 87% Faradaic efficiency despite potential being insufficient to oxidize Mn(IV)=O to Mn(V)=O directly, supporting disproportionation pathway

10.1039_D3GC05126A.notes: Two parallel pathways are proposed for forming [(TMP)Mn(V)=O]+: (a) two consecutive anodic SETs on (TMP)Mn(III)(OH), and (b) anodic oxidation of (TMP)Mn(III)(OH) to (TMP)Mn(IV)=O followed by disproportionation into (TMP)Mn(III)(OH) + [(TMP)Mn(V)=O]+; the constant-potential experiment at 0.75 V favors the disproportionation route. The use of bulky TMP ligand is critical to enable SET-based generation of Mn(V)=O rather than the typically observed unproductive electrophilic chlorination or radical-type pathways associated with Mn(IV)=O from salen catalysts; pyridine acts as an axial ligand/base.

10.1039_d3gc05126a.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical epoxidation of alkenes using water as the oxygen source, catalyzed by a sterically hindered manganese porphyrin (TMP)MnCl under undivided-cell constant-current electrolysis.
10.1039_d3gc05126a.electrochemical_role: Anodic single-electron oxidation of (TMP)Mn(III)(OH) to (TMP)Mn(IV)=O, which either disproportionates or is further oxidized at the anode to give the active [(TMP)Mn(V)=O]+ oxidant; cathode reduces protons to H2.
10.1039_d3gc05126a.electron_flow: At the anode, (TMP)Mn(III)(OH) is oxidized by sequential single-electron transfers (via (TMP)Mn(IV)=O) to [(TMP)Mn(V)=O]+, or two (TMP)Mn(IV)=O units disproportionate to give [(TMP)Mn(V)=O]+ and (TMP)Mn(III)(OH); the Mn(V)=O then transfers its oxygen (derived from water) to the alkene to deliver the epoxide and regenerate Mn(III)(OH), while protons are reduced to H2 at the Pt cathode.
10.1039_d3gc05126a.rate_determining_step: Generation of [(TMP)Mn(V)=O]+ via single-electron oxidation/disproportionation of Mn(IV)=O at the anode

key_intermediates.name: epoxide product via O-atom transfer to alkene

probe_reagents.name: H2 18-O isotope labeling (H2^18O as co-solvent)
probe_reagents.observation: MS of styrene oxide product 2a' showed ^18O incorporation, confirming that the epoxide oxygen originates from water rather than O2.

probe_reagents.name: N2 atmosphere control
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction performed under N2 excludes molecular oxygen as the O source.

probe_reagents.name: Constant-potential electrolysis at 0.75 V vs Fc+/Fc
probe_reagents.observation: At this potential Mn(III)-OH can be oxidized to Mn(IV)=O but Mn(IV)=O cannot be further oxidized; styrene still gave 2a in 87% GC yield (87% FE), supporting disproportionation of Mn(IV)=O to [(TMP)Mn(V)=O]+.

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of (TMP)MnCl
probe_reagents.observation: Two redox peaks at 0.67 and 0.9 V vs Fc/Fc+ assigned to Mn(III)/Mn(IV) and Mn(IV)/Mn(V); the Mn(III)/Mn(IV) wave is reversible, indicating a stable Mn(IV) intermediate.

10.1039_d3gc05126a.notes: Two pathways for forming [(TMP)Mn(V)=O]+ are proposed and not fully distinguished: (a) two consecutive 1e- anodic oxidations of (TMP)Mn(III)(OH); (b) anodic oxidation to (TMP)Mn(IV)=O followed by disproportionation to [(TMP)Mn(V)=O]+ + (TMP)Mn(III)(OH). The constant-potential experiment at 0.75 V (below the Mn(IV)/Mn(V) wave) supports the disproportionation pathway being operative. Contrast with prior salen-Mn electrochemistry, where SET gives (salen)Mn(IV)=O that does electrophilic chlorination/radical-type epoxidation; the bulky TMP ligand is invoked to suppress those off-pathway modes and favor Mn(V)=O formation. Pyridine (0.5 equiv) is required as an axial ligand/base.

10.1039_c9gc01152h.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical halogenation/semi-pinacol rearrangement of allylic alcohols using MgBr2·6H2O or MgCl2 as halide source to form β-halocarbonyls with all-carbon quaternary centers
10.1039_c9gc01152h.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of halide anion (Br- or Cl-) to molecular halogen (Br2 / Cl2), which then acts as the electrophilic halogen source; cathode evolves H2 from protons
10.1039_c9gc01152h.electron_flow: Halide anion is oxidized at the anode to molecular halogen (via Br2•- to Br2); the halogen forms a halonium ion with the allylic alcohol alkene, triggering a classic electrophilic semi-pinacol 1,2-migration of the aryl/alkyl group from the carbinol carbon to give a β-halocarbonyl, while protons are reduced to H2 at the cathode.

key_intermediates.name: halonium ion / bromiranium ion on alkene

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: Inhibited the reaction, suggesting radical species involvement (halogen radical/Br2•-)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Inhibited the reaction, consistent with radical halogen intermediate

probe_reagents.name: non-symmetric migration substrate 1v (p-MeO-aryl vs p-CF3-aryl)
probe_reagents.observation: Selective migration of electron-rich p-MeO-aryl group gave 2v in 95% yield, identical to product from NBS-mediated reaction, supporting a cationic semi-pinacol migration

probe_reagents.name: NBS control
probe_reagents.observation: NBS as electrophilic Br source gave the same product 2v, supporting electrophilic Br+ / cationic pathway

10.1039_c9gc01152h.notes: Authors propose that anodically generated Br2 (via Br2•-) initiates an electrophilic semi-pinacol rearrangement; the high diastereoselectivity (dr > 20:1 for 2i, 2j) and preferential migration of electron-rich aryl groups support a cationic (rather than radical) migration step. Radical inhibition by BHT/TEMPO is attributed to interception of the halogen radical intermediate during anodic Br- oxidation, not a C-centered radical on substrate. MgBr2·6H2O is preferred over MgCl2 due to lower oxidation potential of Br-.

10.1039_D3GC05126A_sup_1.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical epoxidation of styrenes and aliphatic alkenes using (TMP)MnCl catalyst with H2O as oxygen source under constant current electrolysis
10.1039_D3GC05126A_sup_1.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of Mn(III) catalyst to a high-valent Mn-oxo species using H2O as the oxygen atom source; the Mn-oxo then transfers oxygen to the alkene
10.1039_D3GC05126A_sup_1.electron_flow: At the anode, the (TMP)Mn(III) catalyst is oxidized in the presence of H2O (and pyridine base) to generate a high-valent Mn-oxo intermediate; this electrophilic oxo transfers its oxygen atom to the alkene to give the epoxide, regenerating the lower-valent Mn for re-oxidation at the anode. Protons are reduced at the Pt cathode.

key_intermediates.name: high-valent Mn-oxo (Mn(V)=O or Mn(IV)=O) species derived from H2O

probe_reagents.name: H2-18O (isotope-labeled water)
probe_reagents.observation: GC-MS analysis of epoxide product from electrolysis with H2-18O confirms that the oxygen atom incorporated into the epoxide originates from water (Figure S1)

10.1039_D3GC05126A_sup_1.notes: Only the isotope-labeling page is shown here; full mechanistic picture (e.g., CV, radical traps, DFT) may appear elsewhere in the SI. The scale-up (3 mmol, 71% yield) confirms practicality. Pyridine likely serves as an axial ligand/base assisting Mn-oxo formation.

10.1039_D4GC01127A_sup_1.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical oxidative [3+2] annulation of 1,3-dicarbonyl indanones with styrenes/alkenes to form spirocyclic dihydrofuranones via radical intermediates
10.1039_D4GC01127A_sup_1.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of the 1,3-dicarbonyl (methyl 1-oxo-2,3-dihydro-1H-indene-2-carboxylate) to generate an α-carbonyl C-centered radical that adds to the alkene
10.1039_D4GC01127A_sup_1.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes the enolizable 1,3-dicarbonyl substrate 1a to an α-carbonyl radical, which adds across the alkene to give a benzylic C-radical; further single-electron oxidation at the anode yields a benzylic cation that is trapped by H2-18O / carbonyl oxygen to close the spirofuran ring.

key_intermediates.name: benzylic C-radical after addition to styrene/diphenylethylene
spirocyclic dihydrofuranone 3a (cyclized product incorporating 18O from H2-18O)

probe_reagents.name: H2-18O
probe_reagents.observation: 18O incorporation into product 3a (and downstream 4-18O), confirming water as the oxygen source in ring closure

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: TEMPO-adduct 6 isolated in 89.6% yield from reaction of 1a with styrene + TEMPO, trapping the α-carbonyl radical (and a styrene-derived adduct 7 also observed), supporting a radical pathway

10.1039_D4GC01127A_sup_1.notes: Two TEMPO adducts (6 and 7) are described: 6 corresponds to trapping of the α-carbonyl radical from 1a directly, while 7 corresponds to trapping after addition to styrene — together consistent with stepwise radical addition followed by oxidation (radical-polar crossover) and water-assisted cyclization.

10.1039_d5gc02319j.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical Fe-catalyzed three-component radical difunctionalization of olefins with hydrazine carboxylates and heteroarenes (quinoxalin-2(1H)-ones) to give beta-heteroarylated esters
10.1039_d5gc02319j.electrochemical_role: Anode oxidizes Fe(II) to Fe(III); Fe(III) mediator oxidizes hydrazine carboxylate (3a) to generate a methoxycarbonyl radical (with loss of N2 and H+); cathode reduces protons to H2
10.1039_d5gc02319j.electron_flow: At the anode Fe(II) is oxidized to Fe(III), which oxidizes hydrazine carboxylate 3a to a methoxycarbonyl radical (releasing N2 and H+); this radical adds to the olefin to give alkyl radical B, which adds to quinoxalinone 2a to give N-radical C; Fe(III) then oxidizes C and deprotonation rearomatizes to product 4, while protons are reduced to H2 at the cathode.
10.1039_d5gc02319j.rate_determining_step: Oxidation of methoxycarbonyl-radical addition adduct B over TS_B-C (DFT barrier ~6.90 kcal/mol from B; initial olefin addition TS_A-B at ~12.81 kcal/mol is highest)

key_intermediates.name: alkyl C-radical B (methoxycarbonyl radical addition adduct on 1-pentene)
N-centered radical adduct C (after C-radical addition to quinoxalinone 2a)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction fully inhibited; TEMPO-methoxycarbonyl adduct 78 and TEMPO-alkyl adduct 80 detected by HRMS, confirming methoxycarbonyl and C-centered radicals

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction inhibited, supporting radical mechanism

probe_reagents.name: 1,1-diphenylethylene (DPE)
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction inhibited; DPE-radical adduct 79 detected by HRMS

probe_reagents.name: Fe(OAc)2 / substrates CV
probe_reagents.observation: E1/2(ox) Fe(OAc)2 = 1.0 V vs AgCl, lower than olefin (2.15 V), heteroarene (1.92 V) and hydrazine carboxylate (1.2 V); Fe(III)/Fe(II) reduction (-0.52 V) is less negative than 3a reduction (-0.65 V), so Fe(III) can oxidize 3a

probe_reagents.name: DTBP / AIBN / BPO (thermal oxidants)
probe_reagents.observation: Ineffective under heating, showing electrocatalysis is essential rather than a simple radical initiator

10.1039_d5gc02319j.notes: DFT profile (Fig. 5c) supports stepwise radical addition: A (methoxycarbonyl radical + olefin) -> B (-9.69 kcal/mol) -> C (-12.28) -> 4 (-60.24); proton reduction to H2 at cathode closes the redox balance. Control with common thermal radical initiators failed, highlighting the unique role of the electrochemical Fe(III)/Fe(II) cycle.

10.1016_j.chempr.2020.12.002.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical aziridination of electron-rich styrenes with primary alkyl amines in flow, via anodic oxidation of the alkene to a radical cation that is attacked by the amine, followed by deprotonation, second SET, and ring closure
10.1016_j.chempr.2020.12.002.electrochemical_role: Anodic single-electron oxidation of the electron-rich styrene generates an alkene radical cation; cathode reduces protons to H2 (which can be used in a downstream Pd/C hydrogenation for formal hydroamination)
10.1016_j.chempr.2020.12.002.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes the electron-rich styrene by SET to a radical cation, which is attacked by the primary alkyl amine to give a β-amino C-radical; deprotonation and a second anodic SET deliver a β-amino benzylic carbocation that undergoes barrier-less intramolecular ring closure with loss of a proton to the trans-aziridine, while protons are reduced to H2 at the stainless-steel cathode.
10.1016_j.chempr.2020.12.002.rate_determining_step: Initial anodic SET oxidation of the alkene to its radical cation (ΔG = +28.3 kcal/mol, +1.226 V vs SCE); subsequent C–N bond formation proceeds via a low (+5.4 kcal/mol) transition state

key_intermediates.name: β-amino-α-carbon radical (C, after amine addition to radical cation)
β-amino benzylic carbocation (E, after second SET)
Schiff base / imine from overoxidation of alkene
aminium radical cation from amine oxidation (alternative initiation)

probe_reagents.name: 1,4-cyclohexadiene (1,4-CHD)
probe_reagents.observation: 0.5 equiv suppressed overoxidation of the alkene; imine byproduct dropped from 15% to 6% and aziridine 9A rose from 78% to 92%

probe_reagents.name: γ-terpinene
probe_reagents.observation: Non-toxic H-atom/electron donor alternative to 1,4-CHD; suppressed alkene overoxidation (88% 9A, 8% imine) and attenuated the CV oxidation wave of anethole

probe_reagents.name: cis- vs trans-stilbene
probe_reagents.observation: Both stereoisomers converge to the same trans-aziridine (19-A), consistent with a stepwise (non-concerted) radical-cation pathway

probe_reagents.name: styrene / trans-β-methylstyrene (high oxidation-potential alkenes)
probe_reagents.observation: No aziridine formed; rationalized by competing amine oxidation before alkene oxidation

probe_reagents.name: anethole + cyclohexylamine (CV studies)
probe_reagents.observation: CV shows two oxidation events of the alkene (radical cation, then oxidative cleavage to Schiff base); amine oxidation requires +1.665 V vs SCE and is kinetically disfavored

10.1016_j.chempr.2020.12.002.notes: An alternative initiation via anodic oxidation of the alkyl amine to an aminium radical is computed to be uphill (+38.4 kcal/mol, +1.665 V vs SCE) and is therefore disfavored, but the authors do not exclude it for some substrate combinations. Aryl amines fail because they are not nucleophilic enough to add to radical cation B. Scope limits (electron-rich β-substituted styrenes only) are rationalized by accessible alkene oxidation potential plus steric protection of the radical cation. The cathodically generated H2 is productively used downstream over a Pd/C packed bed to give the formal hydroamination product.

10.24820_ark.5550190.p011.041.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical synthesis of cyclopropanes (including spirocyclopropanes) from C-H acids and alkylidenemalononitriles using pyridinium bromide as a redox mediator in an undivided cell.
10.24820_ark.5550190.p011.041.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of bromide to Br2 (mediator regeneration); cathodic reduction of pyridinium cation to pyridine (base generator) with concomitant H2 evolution.
10.24820_ark.5550190.p011.041.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes 2 Br- to Br2 while the cathode reduces 2 PyH+ to 2 Py + H2; Py deprotonates the C-H acid whose anion is brominated by Br2 to give A; a second equivalent of Py displaces bromide forming pyridinium ylide B, which undergoes Michael addition to the alkylidenemalononitrile followed by intramolecular SN2 displacement of pyridine to close the cyclopropane 3.

key_intermediates.name: N-substituted pyridinium salt (alpha-pyridinio C-H acid, pre-ylide)

10.24820_ark.5550190.p011.041.notes: Mechanism is proposed by analogy (last cyclization step postulated following Abaszadeh's work on pyridinium ylide + benzylidenemalononitrile cyclopropanation). Authors note NaBr in MeOH is a more effective mediator than PyHBr in MeCN, attributed to mechanistic particularities but not probed experimentally. Bromine formation at the anode is supported by visual observation of its color; H2 evolution at the cathode is observed especially under unstirred conditions.

10.1080_00397911.2022.2028843.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical bromolactonization of 4-aryl-4-pentenoic acids with CBr4 as bromine radical source to give bromomethylated γ-butyrolactones
10.1080_00397911.2022.2028843.electrochemical_role: Cathodic reduction of CBr4 generates Br- and •CBr3; bromide is then anodically oxidized to Br• (and/or Br+), which adds across the alkene; benzylic radical is further oxidized at the anode to a benzylic cation
10.1080_00397911.2022.2028843.electron_flow: At the cathode CBr4 is reduced to Br- and •CBr3 (trapped as CHBr3, with H+ reduced to ½H2); at the anode Br- is oxidized to Br• which adds to the alkene of 1a to give benzylic radical I, which is further oxidized at the anode to benzylic cation II (alternatively Br+ adds directly to 1a giving II); intramolecular nucleophilic cyclization by the carboxylic acid then delivers γ-lactone 3a with loss of H+.

key_intermediates.name: bromine radical (Br•) from anodic oxidation of Br-
benzylic carbon radical intermediate I after Br• addition to alkene

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction still afforded 3a in 42% yield (radical pathway implicated but not fully suppressed)

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: 3a obtained in 44% yield under BHT (radical intermediates implicated)

probe_reagents.name: molecular Br2 (control without current)
probe_reagents.observation: Only trace 3a formed, indicating Br2 is not the active brominating intermediate

10.1080_00397911.2022.2028843.notes: Authors present two converging anodic pathways to the benzylic cation II: (a) Br• addition giving radical I followed by anodic oxidation, or (b) direct Br+ addition to the alkene. CV showed oxidation peaks of 1a at 1.74 V and CBr4 at 1.03 V, and CBr4 reduction at -0.78 V, consistent with cathodic activation of CBr4 and anodic oxidation of bromide/substrate. Radical traps only partially suppress the reaction, suggesting both radical and cationic routes may operate.

10.1039_d3cc04920e.rxn_class_summary: Galvanostatic electroreductive cyclodimerization of chalcones to densely functionalized 1,3-diol cyclopentanes with five contiguous stereocenters
10.1039_d3cc04920e.electrochemical_role: Cathodic single-electron reduction of chalcone to a radical anion at the Ag cathode (Epc = -1.52 V vs SCE), followed by a second cathodic reduction event of the dimerized enolate intermediate and a final more demanding reduction of the aldol ketone (E1/2 = -1.86 V); graphite anode oxidizes TEOA as sacrificial reductant/proton source
10.1039_d3cc04920e.electron_flow: Cathode reduces chalcone 1a to radical anion A, which adds in tail-to-tail Giese fashion to a second 1a giving radical B; a second cathodic event plus protonation (or radical-radical coupling of two A) produces 1,6-diketone monoenolate C, which undergoes non-diastereoselective intramolecular aldol to 2a/2a'; an irreversible cathodic ketone reduction of the more reactive H-bonded isomer 2a delivers diol 3a (dynamic kinetic resolution via retro-aldol equilibration of 2a').
10.1039_d3cc04920e.rate_determining_step: Final cathodic reduction of the aldol ketone 2 to the 1,3-diol 3 is the most energetically demanding step (occurs at -1.86 V vs the -1.52/-1.58 V for chalcone reduction), enabled only by galvanostatic self-adaptation

probe_reagents.name: isolated aldol intermediates 2a and 2a'
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction interrupted at 1 F/mol gave 68% combined 2a + 2a' (~1:1); resubjecting either to optimal conditions delivered 3a in >20:1 dr with no 3a' formed, confirming both as productive intermediates and revealing dynamic kinetic resolution

probe_reagents.name: pure 2a' + TEOA (control)
probe_reagents.observation: Exposure to TEOA (2 equiv) furnished a 1:1 2a:2a' mixture, supporting retro-aldol equilibration

probe_reagents.name: Hantzsch ester (HE)
probe_reagents.observation: Replacing TEOA with HE as reductant gave no 3a, only aldol mixture 18/17/— (entry 6), showing TEOA's dual role as reductant and proton source

probe_reagents.name: Zn sacrificial anode (no TEOA)
probe_reagents.observation: No 3a formed; only partial reduction of 1a and aldols (entry 7)

probe_reagents.name: controlled H2O addition
probe_reagents.observation: Made the second CV peak fully reversible at E1/2 = -1.86 V, supporting proton-coupled formation of C and aldol cyclization

10.1039_d3cc04920e.notes: Two pathways are proposed for formation of monoenolate C: (a) favored — Giese addition of radical anion A to neutral 1a giving B, then second cathodic reduction/protonation; (b) alternative — direct radical-radical coupling of two A followed by protonation, consistent with the half-height of the second CV wave and the fact that B→C reduction would occur at less negative potential than 1a→A. Diastereoselectivity arises from dynamic kinetic resolution: only the H-bond-activated isomer 2a is reduced rapidly, while 2a' equilibrates back via retro-aldol. X-ray structures of 3a and 2a' were obtained.

10.1039_D3CC05770D.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical Wacker-type aerobic oxygenation of triaryl-substituted alkenes to 1,2,2-triarylethanones using O2 as oxygen source in an undivided cell with LiClO4/CH3CN
10.1039_D3CC05770D.electrochemical_role: Anodic single-electron oxidation of the triaryl alkene to a radical cation (Eox ~2.1 V vs SCE); simultaneous cathodic reduction of O2 to superoxide (Ered ~ -0.91 V vs SCE)
10.1039_D3CC05770D.electron_flow: At the anode, the triaryl alkene undergoes single-electron oxidation to a radical cation; at the cathode, O2 is reduced to superoxide. Superoxide couples with the radical cation to give a zwitterion that cyclizes to a 1,2-dioxetane, which fragments with a 1,2-aryl shift to deliver the 1,2,2-triarylethanone (and an epoxide that further rearranges to the same ketone).

key_intermediates.name: zwitterionic peroxide adduct from radical cation + superoxide (4)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Only trace product 2a formed, indicating involvement of a radical pathway

probe_reagents.name: BHT (2,6-di-tert-butyl-4-methylphenol)
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction strongly inhibited; only trace 2a

probe_reagents.name: no-current control
probe_reagents.observation: No 2a formed without applied current, confirming electrochemical activation is required

probe_reagents.name: ultra-dry conditions
probe_reagents.observation: Yield of 2a unchanged (71%), excluding H2O as oxygen source

probe_reagents.name: 18O2
probe_reagents.observation: HRMS shows 18O-labeled 2a, confirming O2 as the oxygen source

probe_reagents.name: H2 18O under Ar
probe_reagents.observation: No product formed, excluding water as oxygen source

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry
probe_reagents.observation: O2 reduced at -0.91 V vs SCE; 1a oxidized at ~2.1 V vs SCE, supporting paired anodic/cathodic events

10.1039_D3CC05770D.notes: Authors invoke a 1,2-dioxetane intermediate that fragments with 1,2-aryl migration of the electron-rich aryl group to rationalize the 1,2,2-triarylethanone connectivity; an alternative pathway via an epoxide intermediate (6) that rearranges (Meinwald-type) is proposed as a parallel route to the same ketone. No DFT or KIE reported.

10.1039_d3cc00231d.rxn_class_summary: Mediator-free electrochemical trifluoromethylation/cyclization of β,γ-unsaturated oximes with Langlois reagent (CF3SO2Na) to give trifluoromethylated isoxazolines
10.1039_d3cc00231d.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of CF3SO2- to CF3SO2 radical (which loses SO2 to give CF3 radical); subsequent anodic single-electron oxidation of the alkyl radical intermediate A to carbocation B; cathode reduces protons to H2
10.1039_d3cc00231d.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes CF3SO2- to CF3SO2 radical which extrudes SO2 to give CF3 radical; CF3 radical adds to the terminal alkene of the β,γ-unsaturated oxime forming carbon radical A; a second anodic SET oxidizes A to carbocation B, which is trapped intramolecularly by the oxime hydroxyl with loss of H+ to give the isoxazoline, while the cathode reduces protons to H2.

key_intermediates.name: Carbon-centered radical A from CF3 addition to terminal alkene of β,γ-unsaturated oxime
Benzylic/alkyl carbocation B after anodic SET of A

probe_reagents.name: Argon atmosphere control
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction proceeds with comparable efficacy under Ar, ruling out a significant role for aerial O2

probe_reagents.name: Divided cell with substrate+CF3SO2Na in anolyte
probe_reagents.observation: 2a formed in 49% yield, confirming anodic chemistry

probe_reagents.name: Divided cell with substrate+CF3SO2Na in catholyte
probe_reagents.observation: <5% product, confirming oxidation (not reduction) is required

probe_reagents.name: OBz-protected oxime 5 (no free OH)
probe_reagents.observation: Hydroxy- or methoxy-trifluoromethylation products 6/7 detected by HRMS (water/MeOH attacks B), supporting radical addition then cationic trapping

probe_reagents.name: MeOH co-solvent on 5
probe_reagents.observation: Methoxy-trifluoromethylation product formed, consistent with carbocation intermediate B

probe_reagents.name: Competition between terminal alkene 1a and styryl alkene 1r
probe_reagents.observation: Exclusive formation of phenyl-substituted isoxazoline 2r, indicating better stabilization of radical/cation by aryl group

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of CF3SO2Na and 1a
probe_reagents.observation: Half-peak potentials 1.17 V (CF3SO2Na) vs 1.39 V (1a); CF3SO2Na preferentially oxidized; product 2a shows no oxidation in operating window

10.1039_d3cc00231d.notes: Mediator-free, undivided cell with graphite electrodes, LiClO4/MeCN-H2O electrolyte, Langlois reagent as CF3 source. Divided-cell experiments unambiguously localize the productive chemistry to the anode; OBz-protected substrate diverts B to nucleophilic trapping by H2O/MeOH, providing strong support for the radical-polar crossover pathway over a purely radical cyclization.

10.1039_d2cc03301a_si1.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical radical cyclization of N-aryl acrylamides with polyhalomethanes (e.g., BrCHCl2, CH2I2) to give 3,3-disubstituted oxindoles bearing haloalkyl groups
10.1039_d2cc03301a_si1.electrochemical_role: Cathodic reduction of the polyhalomethane (e.g., Br-CHCl2) generates a haloalkyl radical (·CHCl2) via C-Br bond cleavage; this radical adds to the acrylamide alkene, and the resulting C-centered radical cyclizes onto the arene to form the oxindole after rearomatization
10.1039_d2cc03301a_si1.electron_flow: Cathode reduces the C-X bond of the polyhalomethane to release a haloalkyl radical; this radical adds across the acrylamide C=C, the resulting α-amido carbon radical undergoes intramolecular homolytic aromatic substitution onto the N-aryl ring, and anodic oxidation/deprotonation rearomatizes the cyclohexadienyl radical to deliver the oxindole.

key_intermediates.name: tertiary C-radical from radical addition to acrylamide alkene
cyclohexadienyl radical after ipso/ortho cyclization onto arene

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction yield drops from 78% to <2% in the presence of 5 equiv TEMPO, consistent with a radical pathway

10.1039_d2cc03301a_si1.notes: Radical trapping with TEMPO essentially shuts down the reaction, supporting radical intermediates. Cyclization products bearing CH2I (compound 39) and CHCl2 (compound 3) were isolated, and a deuterated analogue (compound 48) was prepared en route to deuterated Lansai B, providing isotope-labeling support for the proposed radical addition/cyclization sequence.

10.1039_d2cc02958h.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical azidocyanation of alkene-tethered cyanohydrins via Mn-mediated azide radical addition and 1,4-nitrile migration to deliver 1,2-azidonitriles.
10.1039_d2cc02958h.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of an in situ formed [Mn(II)X2N3]- complex to a Mn(III)-azide species that delivers an azidyl radical to the alkene; cathode reduces protons to H2.
10.1039_d2cc02958h.electron_flow: At the anode, Mn(II)/azide ate complex is oxidized to Mn(III)-N3, which transfers an azidyl radical to the alkene of the cyanohydrin to give a C-centered radical; intramolecular addition onto the pendant nitrile forms an iminyl radical that undergoes beta-scission to a ketyl-type alkyl radical, which is anodically oxidized and loses H+ to give the 1,2-azidonitrile, while protons are reduced to H2 at the Pt cathode.

key_intermediates.name: secondary alkyl C-radical from azide addition to alkene (36)
cyclic iminyl radical from intramolecular nitrile cyclization (37)

probe_reagents.name: cyclopropyl-substituted cyanohydrin (radical clock 34)
probe_reagents.observation: Ring-opened azido-nitrile 35 obtained as sole product in 56% NMR yield, consistent with a carbon-radical intermediate after azide addition.

probe_reagents.name: Mn(OTf)2 / TBAN3 cyclic voltammetry
probe_reagents.observation: TBAN3 alone shows irreversible oxidation at ~0.97 V vs Fc/Fc+; with Mn(OTf)2 a new irreversible event appears at ~0.50 V vs Fc/Fc+, indicating formation of an oxidizable [Mn(II)X2N3]- species (Mn(II)->Mn(III)).

10.1039_d2cc02958h.notes: Authors note azidyl radicals could in principle be generated directly by anodic oxidation of azide, but CV shows Mn(OTf)2 lowers the oxidation potential substantially, supporting a Mn-mediated inner-sphere azide-transfer pathway rather than free N3 radical from direct azide oxidation. Methodology extends to sulfonylcyanation (using NaSO2Ph) via the same radical/migration manifold and to a malononitrile-derived substrate (30 -> 31).

10.1039_D2CC03922B_sup_1.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical radical cyclization of 1,6-enynes with sulfonimides to construct benzofuran derivatives bearing aminomethyl groups
10.1039_D2CC03922B_sup_1.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of sulfonimide (or its anion) to generate a nitrogen-centered sulfonamidyl radical that adds to the enyne
10.1039_D2CC03922B_sup_1.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes the sulfonimide anion (generated with nBu4NOAc base) to a N-centered radical, which adds to the terminal alkene of the 1,6-enyne; the resulting C-radical cyclizes onto the tethered alkyne, and the vinyl radical is further oxidized to a cation that is trapped by methanol to give the dimethyl ketal/benzofuran product.

key_intermediates.name: vinyl/alkenyl radical from N-radical addition to alkene of 1,6-enyne
cyclized aryl/vinyl radical after intramolecular addition onto alkyne

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Product 3a not detected (0% yield) under standard conditions with TEMPO added, consistent with a radical intermediate

probe_reagents.name: no-current control
probe_reagents.observation: Without electric current, product 3a was not formed (0% yield), confirming the electrochemical nature of the transformation

10.1039_D2CC03922B_sup_1.notes: Page also shows downstream synthetic applications: Ti(OiPr)4/Mg/Me3SiCl mediated N-S bond cleavage of 3a to monosulfonamide 5a (78%), and H2SO4-promoted hydrolysis/rearrangement of ketal 4a to ketone 6a (70%). These are derivatization steps, not part of the catalytic cycle.

10.1039_D5CC03812J_sup_1.rxn_class_summary: Photoelectrochemical radical tandem cyclization of N-aryl acrylamides with alkyl bromides using (TMS)3SiOH as halogen-atom transfer (XAT) reagent to access 3,3-disubstituted oxindoles bearing a CF3 group.
10.1039_D5CC03812J_sup_1.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation regenerates the excited-state photocatalyst (PQ); the photocatalyst oxidizes (TMS)3SiOH to (TMS)3SiO•, which rearranges to a silyl radical (TMS)2(TMSO)Si• that performs XAT on the alkyl bromide to generate the key alkyl radical.
10.1039_D5CC03812J_sup_1.electron_flow: Visible-light-excited PQ* oxidizes (TMS)3SiOH to give (TMS)3SiO•, which rearranges to a silyl radical that abstracts Br from R-Br (XAT) to generate an alkyl radical; this radical adds to the acrylamide alkene, the resulting C-radical cyclizes onto the arene, and rearomatization/oxidation delivers the oxindole while the reduced PQ is reoxidized at the anode.

key_intermediates.name: carbon radical after addition to acrylamide alkene
cyclohexadienyl radical from intramolecular aryl cyclization

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction completely inhibited; TEMPO–(2-hydroxyethyl) adduct 61 (C11H24NO2+, m/z 202.1803) detected by HRMS, indicating intermediacy of the alkyl radical derived from 2-bromoethanol.

probe_reagents.name: (bromomethyl)cyclopropane (63)
probe_reagents.observation: Ring-opened cyclization product 64 obtained in 48% yield, confirming a freely diffusing alkyl radical.

probe_reagents.name: 6-bromohex-1-ene (65)
probe_reagents.observation: Radical dicyclization product 66 (with cyclopentylmethyl pendant) obtained in 51% yield, consistent with 5-exo-trig cyclization of the hexenyl radical.

probe_reagents.name: (TMS)3SiOH (no R-Br control)
probe_reagents.observation: In the absence of alkyl bromide, silyl-incorporated oxindole 67 was isolated in 37% yield, supporting formation of (TMS)3SiO• and its rearrangement to a silyl radical that adds to the acrylamide.

10.1039_D5CC03812J_sup_1.notes: The silyl-trapping experiment in the absence of R-Br (product 67) is key evidence for the proposed Brook-type rearrangement of (TMS)3SiO• to (TMS)2(TMSO)Si• as the active XAT reagent. No DFT, CV, or KIE data presented on these pages.

10.1039_D4CC00843J_sup_1.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical oxidative ring-opening/oxidation of arylmethylenecyclopropanes with methanol to give methyl 4-methoxy-4-arylbutanoates via radical cation and cyclopropane ring-opening
10.1039_D4CC00843J_sup_1.electrochemical_role: Anodic single-electron oxidation of the electron-rich arylidenecyclopropane alkene to a radical cation; cathode reduces methanol to generate methoxide and H2
10.1039_D4CC00843J_sup_1.electron_flow: Anode performs SET oxidation of the styryl-cyclopropane 1b to a radical cation, which is trapped by cathodically generated methoxide; the resulting cyclopropylcarbinyl radical undergoes ring-opening, then a second anodic oxidation and trapping by hydroxide (from adventitious water/D2O) gives a primary alcohol intermediate, which is oxidized to the aldehyde and finally to an acylium that is captured by MeOH to deliver the methyl ester 2b.

key_intermediates.name: benzylic alpha-methoxy cyclopropylcarbinyl radical (II) after MeO- addition
ring-opened primary carbon radical / homoallyl-type radical (III) after cyclopropane C-C homolysis
acylium cation (VI) from oxidation of aldehyde
BHT-adduct of mono-methoxylated cyclopropyl benzylic species (off-cycle trap)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Complete shutdown of reaction; starting material unconsumed, indicating a radical pathway

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction shut down; HRMS detected BHT adduct (M+Na 433.2724) corresponding to BHT trapping of mono-methoxylated cyclopropyl benzylic radical/cation, supporting initial SET oxidation of the alkene to a radical cation

probe_reagents.name: D2O (MeOH/D2O mixed solvent)
probe_reagents.observation: Product 2b formed in 58%; intermediate III' bearing -OD on the terminal CH2 detected by GC-MS (base peak 211), confirming that ring-opening of cyclopropane is terminated by hydroxide/OD- from water

probe_reagents.name: 2-(cyclopropylmethyl)-1,3,5-trimethylbenzene (1v)
probe_reagents.observation: Electrochemical control experiment to probe cyclopropyl radical-clock behavior

probe_reagents.name: (cyclopropylidenemethyl)cyclohexane (1w)
probe_reagents.observation: Used to test substrate scope of radical ring-opening with non-aryl methylenecyclopropane

probe_reagents.name: Isolated intermediate III (4-aryl-4-methoxybutan-1-ol)
probe_reagents.observation: Subjected to standard electrochemical conditions and converted to product 2b, confirming it as an on-cycle intermediate

10.1039_D4CC00843J_sup_1.notes: Authors present two mechanistic proposals; favored Pathway-2 is depicted here, in which methoxide adds first to the radical cation and the cyclopropylcarbinyl radical then undergoes homolytic ring opening; the alternative Pathway-1 (not shown in detail) likely invokes cyclopropane ring opening before MeO- attack. A divided-cell experiment, current on/off, cyclic voltammetry, and Faradaic efficiency calculation further support the electrochemical radical-cation pathway. BHT adduct supports an initial alkene radical-cation oxidation step.

10.1039_D4CC05092D_sup_1.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical cyclopropanation of cinnamaldehydes with dimethyl malonate via iminium/enamine organocatalysis combined with iodide electrocatalytic mediation
10.1039_D4CC05092D_sup_1.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of iodide to iodine radical (E-cat cycle) and anodic oxidation of the enamine intermediate (ii) to a radical cation that captures the iodine radical; cathode reduces protons to H2
10.1039_D4CC05092D_sup_1.electron_flow: At the anode, iodide is oxidized to I• (E-cat cycle); in parallel the enamine intermediate (ii) is anodically oxidized to a radical cation that couples with I• to give an α-iodo iminium (iii). Intramolecular displacement of iodide by the malonate carbon closes the cyclopropane (iv); hydrolysis releases product 3a and regenerates the pyrrolidine catalyst, while the cathode reduces protons to H2.

key_intermediates.name: iminium ion from condensation of pyrrolidine catalyst with cinnamaldehyde 1a (i)
enamine adduct after Michael addition of dimethyl malonate (ii)
enamine-derived radical cation captured by I• (ii-a/iii)
cyclopropane-fused iminium (iv) after intramolecular SN2 displacement of iodide
iodine radical (I•) generated at the anode

10.1039_D4CC05092D_sup_1.notes: This scheme (Scheme S2) is presented as an ALTERNATIVE mechanism to the main proposal: instead of α-iodination of the enamine by I• directly, the enamine is first oxidized anodically to a radical cation which then captures I•. Both pathways converge on the α-iodo iminium (iii) and subsequent intramolecular cyclopropanation. No probe experiments (TEMPO, KIE, CV, DFT) are shown on this page to distinguish the two.

10.1039_c7cc03669h.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical synthesis of 3-alkyl phthalides via anodic oxidation of 2-vinylbenzoic acids in the presence of aliphatic carboxylic acids as alkyl radical precursors (Kolbe-type co-electrolysis).
10.1039_c7cc03669h.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of the aromatic carboxylate (2-vinylbenzoate) to an aroyloxy radical and concurrent Kolbe decarboxylation of the aliphatic carboxylate to an alkyl radical; cathodic reduction of the cyclized benzylic radical to an anion that is protonated to give the non-alkylated lactone byproduct.
10.1039_c7cc03669h.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes the 2-vinylbenzoate to an aroyloxy radical that undergoes 5-exo-trig cyclization onto the pendant alkene, giving a benzylic C-radical; this radical is trapped by an alkyl radical produced by simultaneous Kolbe decarboxylation of the aliphatic co-acid, or is alternatively reduced at the cathode to a carbanion that protonates to the unalkylated lactone.

key_intermediates.name: alkyl radical from Kolbe decarboxylation of aliphatic acid (11a, e.g. ethyl radical 5a)
ethyl benzoate (6), recombination byproduct of aroyloxy radical 4a + ethyl radical 5a
non-alkylated phthalide (3) from H-atom abstraction or protonation of 12

probe_reagents.name: benzoic acid alone (no co-acid)
probe_reagents.observation: Quantitative recovery of starting acid; no oxidation observed, suggesting aromatic carboxylates are not directly oxidized under standard Kolbe conditions.

probe_reagents.name: heptanoic acid alone in MeOH
probe_reagents.observation: Electrolysis gave dodecane (Kolbe dimer) with no methoxyhexane, indicating alkyl radical (not cation) intermediates.

probe_reagents.name: ethyl cinnamate (7)
probe_reagents.observation: Fully recovered after 30 min electrolysis, ruling out direct anodic oxidation of the styryl olefin to a radical cation captured by carboxylate.

probe_reagents.name: N,N-diallyl 2-vinylbenzamide (1g) with excess AcOH
probe_reagents.observation: Double 5-exo-trig cyclization product 8a was detected by 1H NMR alongside non-cyclized 8b/8c, supporting a radical cyclization mechanism.

probe_reagents.name: secondary/tertiary aliphatic co-acids
probe_reagents.observation: Only the non-alkylated phthalide 3 was obtained, consistent with overoxidation of secondary/tertiary radicals to carbocations under Kolbe conditions and H-atom abstraction by radical 10.

probe_reagents.name: allyl ester substrate (2j)
probe_reagents.observation: Remained untouched due to preferred s-cis conformation preventing intramolecular cyclization.

10.1039_c7cc03669h.notes: Two pathways are considered for forming the aroyloxy radical: (I) direct anodic oxidation of the aromatic carboxylate (disfavored, since benzoic acid alone is fully recovered) and (II) a redox/SET mechanism in which the Kolbe-generated alkyl radical oxidizes the aromatic carboxylate (favored, pathway 2 in Scheme 4). An alkyl carbocation route capturing benzoate is excluded by the heptanoic acid control (dodecane, no methoxyhexane). Ester byproduct 6 (ethyl benzoate) arises from radical-radical recombination of 4a and 5a. The non-alkylated phthalide 3 arises either by HAT from solvent/medium by radical 10 or by cathodic reduction of 10 to anion 12 followed by protonation.

10.1039_D1CC03389A.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical trifluoromethylation/cyclization of N-aryl methacrylamides with IMDN-SO2CF3 to synthesize isoquinoline-1,3-diones and oxindoles via cathodically generated CF3 radicals.
10.1039_D1CC03389A.electrochemical_role: Cathodic single-electron reduction of the IMDN-SO2CF3 reagent generates a CF3 radical (and releases 1-methyl-2-phenyl-1H-benzo[d]imidazole); the final cyclohexadienyl-type radical intermediate is then re-aromatized via anodic oxidation/deprotonation.
10.1039_D1CC03389A.electron_flow: Cathode reduces IMDN-SO2CF3 by single-electron transfer to release •CF3, which adds to the methacrylamide alkene to give carbon radical A; A cyclizes intramolecularly onto the aryl/amide ring to give cyclohexadienyl radical B, which is then oxidized at the anode and deprotonated to restore aromaticity and afford the isoquinoline-1,3-dione or oxindole.

key_intermediates.name: alkyl radical A (CF3-addition adduct on methacrylamide alkene)
cyclohexadienyl radical B (post-ipso cyclization onto arene)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction completely inhibited; only trace 5a detected.

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction completely inhibited; only trace 5a detected.

probe_reagents.name: 1,1-diphenylethylene (DPE)
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction suppressed; HRMS detected CF3–DPE adduct [M]+ 248.0812 (found 248.0807), confirming •CF3 formation from reagent 1.

probe_reagents.name: CF3SO2Na (Langlois reagent control)
probe_reagents.observation: No product 5a formed, indicating cathodic generation of •CF3 from Langlois reagent does not occur under these conditions.

probe_reagents.name: 1-methyl-3-phenyl-benzimidazolium BF4 (1b, lacking SO2CF3)
probe_reagents.observation: 5a obtained in 63%; demonstrates CF3 originates from the SO2CF3 portion of reagent 1 (the benzimidazolium is the redox-active carrier).

probe_reagents.name: N-methoxy methacrylamide substrate 3u
probe_reagents.observation: Desired cyclized 5u not formed; instead 5ua (34%) and 5ub (3%) observed, indicating that an anodic oxidation step is required to close the catalytic cycle/aromatize.

10.1039_D1CC03389A.notes: Mechanism is supported by radical trapping (TEMPO, BHT, DPE adduct by HRMS) and by the N-OMe substrate experiment indicating an anodic re-aromatization step is operative (paired electrolysis). No KIE, CV, or DFT studies reported. Control with CF3SO2Na vs IMDN-SO2CF3 confirms the benzimidazolium-sulfonyl reagent is uniquely suited for cathodic •CF3 generation under these mild conditions.

10.1039_D5CC03899E.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical ferrocene-mediated anodic phosphorylation/intramolecular cyclization of N-allylamides with diarylphosphine oxides to give phosphorylated oxazolines
10.1039_D5CC03899E.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of ferrocene to ferrocenium, which mediates single-electron oxidation of the deprotonated phosphine oxide (P-anion) to a P-centered radical; cathode reduces protons to H2
10.1039_D5CC03899E.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes ferrocene to ferrocenium, which performs SET on the Et3N-deprotonated Ph2P(O)- to give a phosphoryl radical; this adds to the alkene of the N-allylamide to give a C-radical, which is further oxidized at the anode (via Fc+) to a carbocation that undergoes intramolecular cyclization with the amide oxygen to form the oxazoline; the cathode reduces 2 H+ to H2.

key_intermediates.name: Anionic P-species (Ph2P(O)- from deprotonation of Ph2P(O)H by Et3N) - intermediate A
C-centered radical from P-radical addition to alkene of N-allylamide - intermediate C
Benzylic/secondary carbocation after anodic oxidation of C - intermediate D

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Product 3aa formation completely inhibited; TEMPO–P adduct 4 detected by HRMS ([M+H]+ = 375.2213)

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction inhibited; BHT–phosphoryl adduct 5 detected by HRMS ([M+NH4]+ = 438.2526)

probe_reagents.name: (1-cyclopropylvinyl)benzene
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction inhibited; ring-opened phosphorylated adduct 6 detected by HRMS ([M+K]+ = 385.1117), consistent with phosphoryl radical addition followed by cyclopropyl ring opening

probe_reagents.name: Ferrocene (CV study)
probe_reagents.observation: Fc oxidation at 0.07 V vs Ag/AgNO3, low enough to mediate further electrochemical events; supports redox mediator role

10.1039_D5CC03899E.notes: Mechanism is a ferrocene-mediated anodic radical/cation cascade (radical–polar crossover): P-radical addition to alkene then anodic oxidation to cation and cyclization by the amide oxygen. The X-ray structure of 3aa (CCDC 2466394) confirms regio/connectivity. Failure with trisubstituted/1,2-disubstituted alkenes (1v, 1w) and N-allylthioamide (1x) is rationalized by steric hindrance, regioselectivity issues, or reduced stability of the secondary carbocation intermediate D. Heteroaryl dithiophenylphosphine oxide 2f and diethyl phosphonate 2g failed, attributed to lower stability of the requisite P-centered anion.

10.1039_D5CC03082J_sup_1.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical Ni-catalyzed allylation/alkenylation of β-ketoesters with 1,1-diphenylethylene (and allyl-TMS) via anodic oxidation generating carbon radical intermediates
10.1039_D5CC03082J_sup_1.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of Ni(II)-enolate complex generates an α-carbonyl radical (radical cation intermediate); cathode reduces protons to H2 and assists desilylation/turnover
10.1039_D5CC03082J_sup_1.electron_flow: Ni(II) binds the β-ketoester enolate and is oxidized at the anode to a Ni-stabilized α-carbonyl radical/radical cation; this carbon radical adds to the alkene (1,1-diphenylethylene or allyl-TMS), and a second anodic oxidation yields a β-silyl carbocation that loses TMS+ (as TMSOH/TMS2O) to give the alkenylated product, while the cathode reduces protons to H2.

key_intermediates.name: Anodically oxidized Ni-bound α-carbonyl radical cation (B')
Ni(II)-bound C-C coupled alkyl intermediate after addition to allyl-TMS (C')
β-silyl carbocation (D') after second anodic oxidation

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Product 3 not formed; TEMPO-adduct 50 isolated in 82% yield (HRMS [M+Na]+ 368.1831), supporting a carbon radical intermediate

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: Product 3 not formed; BHT-adduct 51 isolated in 75% yield (HRMS [M+Na]+ 431.2183), consistent with radical trapping

probe_reagents.name: No current control
probe_reagents.observation: Without electrolysis under otherwise standard conditions, product 3 was not detected (0% yield), confirming requirement for anodic oxidation

10.1039_D5CC03082J_sup_1.notes: Mechanism (Scheme S3) is specifically proposed for the allyl-TMS variant forming product 31, invoking a radical-polar crossover where a β-silyl cation D' is intercepted by desilylation. For the 1,1-diphenylethylene case, only radical trapping evidence is shown; no DFT, CV, or KIE data provided. Cathodic chemistry includes H2 evolution and assistance in TMS removal via TMSOH/TMS2O.

10.1039_D1CC04813A.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical vicinal fluorosulfonylation of styrenes using arylsulfonyl hydrazides and Et3N·3HF via a radical-polar crossover pathway
10.1039_D1CC04813A.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of arylsulfonyl hydrazide to arylsulfonyl radical (with loss of N2, 3H+, 3e-); subsequent anodic oxidation of the benzyl radical adduct to a benzyl cation
10.1039_D1CC04813A.electron_flow: The anode oxidizes the arylsulfonyl hydrazide through consecutive SET (losing 3H+, 3e-, N2) to form an arylsulfonyl radical, which adds to the styrene to give a benzyl radical; this radical is further oxidized at the anode to a benzyl cation that is captured by fluoride from Et3N·3HF, while protons are reduced to H2 at the cathode.

probe_reagents.name: (1-(2-phenylcyclopropyl)-vinyl)benzene (radical clock)
probe_reagents.observation: Ring-opened fluorosulfonylation product (40) obtained in 15% yield, supporting a radical intermediate

probe_reagents.name: N-methyl-N-phenylmethacrylamide
probe_reagents.observation: Radical cyclization product (42) obtained in 42% yield in absence of Et3N·3HF, supporting arylsulfonyl radical generation

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of ArSO2NHNH2 and styrene
probe_reagents.observation: Oxidation peak of arylsulfonyl hydrazide at 1.70 V (vs Ag/AgCl); styrene at 2.52 V (shifted to 2.36 V with (NH4)2HPO4), indicating sulfonyl hydrazide is preferentially oxidized

10.1039_D1CC04813A.notes: Authors propose that anode-adsorbed HPO4^2- may H-bond with hydrofluoride to enhance nucleophilic fluorination, accounting for the beneficial additive effect of (NH4)2HPO4. Unactivated olefins (cyclohexene, 1-octene) fail, attributed to instability of the corresponding carbocation, further supporting the radical-polar crossover (cation) pathway over a pure radical chain.

10.1039_D4CC00843J.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical oxidative C-C bond cleavage/ring-opening of arylmethylenecyclopropanes with alcohols to give alkyl 4-alkoxy-4-arylbutanoates without external oxidant or metal catalyst
10.1039_D4CC00843J.electrochemical_role: Anodic single-electron oxidation of the styryl C=C of the methylenecyclopropane to a radical cation; cathode reduces methanol/protons to H2 and generates methoxide that serves as the nucleophile
10.1039_D4CC00843J.electron_flow: Anode removes one electron from the styryl double bond of the MCP to give a radical cation I; methoxide (generated at cathode) attacks, and a second anodic oxidation gives benzylic cation II which opens the cyclopropane to give carbocation II'; water traps it to alcohol III, which undergoes sequential two-electron anodic oxidation/loss of 2 H2O to aldehyde IV; further SET oxidation and H-loss yields acylium V, trapped by methoxide to give ester 2b.
10.1039_D4CC00843J.rate_determining_step: Initial anodic single-electron oxidation of the styryl C=C (Eox ≈ 1.55 V vs Ag/AgCl)

key_intermediates.name: β-methoxy benzylic carbocation after ring opening (II/II')
ring-opened γ-methoxy alcohol intermediate III (detected by GC-MS and HRMS)
aldehyde intermediate IV (γ-methoxy-γ-aryl aldehyde, detected by GC-MS/HRMS)
acylium cation V from SET oxidation and H-loss of aldehyde IV

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction completely shut down with 2 equiv TEMPO; only trace 2b

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: BHT-adduct 2b' detected by HRMS (M+Na 433.2719 calc / 433.2724 found), supporting radical pathway

probe_reagents.name: divided cell experiment
probe_reagents.observation: Product forms only when substrate is in the anodic chamber (50% yield); no reaction in cathodic chamber, confirming anodic oxidation is key

probe_reagents.name: substrate 1y (no C=C, only aryl ring)
probe_reagents.observation: No reaction, confirming the double bond is required for initiation

probe_reagents.name: substrate 1z (alkyl MCP without aryl ring)
probe_reagents.observation: No reaction, showing aryl ring is needed to lower oxidation potential of the alkene

probe_reagents.name: dicyclopropyl substrate giving 2u
probe_reagents.observation: Only one cyclopropane opens; the other remains intact, supporting ring opening via alkene oxidation rather than radical at cyclopropyl

probe_reagents.name: independently prepared intermediate III
probe_reagents.observation: Subjecting III to standard conditions delivers 2b, confirming III is on-pathway

probe_reagents.name: CH3OH/D2O labeling
probe_reagents.observation: Deuterated intermediate III' detected by GC-MS, confirming H2O incorporation in the ring-opening step

probe_reagents.name: electricity on/off
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction halts when current is off; continuous current required (ESI Fig. S1)

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry
probe_reagents.observation: Eox(1b) = 1.55 V; Eox(1v, no C=C) = 2.2 V vs Ag/AgCl, assigning the 1.55 V wave to the styryl alkene

10.1039_D4CC00843J.notes: An alternative pathway (Scheme S1) was considered in which ring opening occurs directly from radical cation I before the second oxidation; the authors favor the cation-mediated ring opening of II→II' due to β-silyl/β-methoxy stabilization. Cathodic methoxide generation and H2 evolution make this effectively a paired electrolysis.

10.1039_d2cc06246a_si_1.rxn_class_summary: Synthesis of vicinal azidoacetamides from alkenes using TMSN3, probed by a radical clock experiment with a vinylcyclopropane substrate.
10.1039_d2cc06246a_si_1.electron_flow: Addition to the styryl alkene generates a benzylic radical that triggers ring-opening of the adjacent cyclopropane to give a distal homoallyl radical; subsequent oxidation to a cation is intercepted by MeCN (Ritter) and azide delivery from TMSN3 gives the linear 1,4-azidoacetamide 31.

key_intermediates.name: carbon-centered radical at benzylic position of vinylcyclopropane (pre ring-opening)
ring-opened homoallyl/benzyl radical after cyclopropane fragmentation
ring-opened carbocation trapped by acetonitrile (Ritter-type) en route to acetamide

probe_reagents.name: 1-(2-phenylcyclopropyl)vinyl)benzene (30)
probe_reagents.observation: Ring-opened acyclic product 31 (N-(4-azido-1,4-diphenylbut-3-en-1-yl)acetamide) isolated in 17% yield, 4.4:1 dr, consistent with a radical intermediate that opens the cyclopropane.

10.1039_d2cc06246a_si_1.notes: Only the radical clock experiment is shown on this page; the ring-opened product implicates a carbon radical intermediate, but a radical-polar crossover is suggested by formation of the acetamide moiety (Ritter-type trapping of a cation by acetonitrile).

10.1039_D1CC03389A_sup_1.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical trifluoromethylation/cyclization of N-aryl methacrylamides using an N-triflyl benzimidazolium salt as the CF3 radical source to form CF3-bearing oxindoles/isoquinolinediones
10.1039_D1CC03389A_sup_1.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation generates the trifluoromethyl radical from the N-triflyl benzimidazolium reagent (1); the C-centered radical formed after CF3 addition to the acrylamide undergoes further anodic oxidation to a cation that is trapped by intramolecular arene cyclization
10.1039_D1CC03389A_sup_1.electron_flow: At the anode, the N-triflyl benzimidazolium cation (1) is oxidized/fragmented to release a CF3 radical, which adds to the alkene of the methacrylamide; the resulting carbon radical is further oxidized at the anode to a cation that undergoes intramolecular Friedel–Crafts-type cyclization onto the N-aryl ring, followed by rearomatization to give the CF3-substituted heterocycle.

key_intermediates.name: trifluoromethyl radical (CF3•) released from N-triflyl benzimidazolium salt
alkyl radical from CF3 addition to methacrylamide alkene
benzylic/alkyl cation after anodic oxidation of alkyl radical
benzimidazole byproduct from reagent 1 after CF3 loss

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Only trace 5a formed, indicating radical intermediate involvement

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: Only trace 5a formed; reaction suppressed
