probe_reagents.name: 1,1-diphenylethylene (DPE)
probe_reagents.observation: Only trace 3a; DPE-CF3 adduct 4 isolated in 86%, confirming CF3• generation

probe_reagents.name: p-dinitrobenzene (pDNB)
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction suppressed (0%), implicating single-electron transfer

probe_reagents.name: CuCl2
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction shut down (0%), single-electron scavenger

probe_reagents.name: CCl4
probe_reagents.observation: Only trace product, consistent with radical pathway

10.1021_acs.joc.3c00790.notes: Electricity on/off experiments show reaction stops without current, arguing against a long radical chain. CV shows 2a (1.20 V) is oxidized preferentially over 1a (1.81 V). H2 evolution at cathode confirmed by gas detection. Photoelectrochemical variant uses Mes-Acr+/Mes-Acr• as homogeneous mediator (excited-state Ered = 1.78 V) for sensitive substrates; this is a parallel mechanism rather than an alternative for the standard reaction.

10.1021_jo049889i.rxn_class_summary: Anodic electrooxidative intramolecular coupling of a silyl enol ether with a tethered furan to construct cis-fused annulated (six-membered ring) furans
10.1021_jo049889i.electrochemical_role: Anodic single-electron oxidation of the silyl enol ether to a radical cation, which is then attacked by the pendant furan; a second anodic oxidation and loss of TMS deliver the annulated furan
10.1021_jo049889i.electron_flow: The anode removes one electron from the silyl enol ether to form a radical cation; the tethered furan attacks intramolecularly to give a cyclized oxocarbenium/radical; 2-propanol traps the oxocarbenium and a second anodic SET generates a furyl cation that loses TMS to give the dihydrofuran intermediate, which aromatizes (loss of iPrOH) to the annulated furan.
10.1021_jo049889i.rate_determining_step: Initial anodic single-electron oxidation of the silyl enol ether (lowest oxidation potential component)

key_intermediates.name: silyl enol ether radical cation (from oxidation of TMS enol ether)
cyclized furyl oxocarbenium/oxonium radical (after furan attack on enol ether radical cation)
alpha-furyl radical after iPrOH trapping of oxonium

probe_reagents.name: model silyl enol ether of cyclohexanone (29)
probe_reagents.observation: E1/2 = 0.87 V vs Fc; comparable to substrate 14 (0.83 V), identifying enol ether as oxidation site

probe_reagents.name: 3-substituted silyl furan (30, TBS-furan)
probe_reagents.observation: E1/2 = 1.31 V vs Fc; furan harder to oxidize than enol ether, supporting path a

probe_reagents.name: cyclopropyl radical clock at C1 (substrate 35 from enone 34)
probe_reagents.observation: Cyclized to 37 in 69% with no ring-opened products; either intermediate is stabilized (siloxy) or oxidation/cyclization is faster than ring-opening

probe_reagents.name: acyclic cyclopropyl ketone probe (39 from enone 38)
probe_reagents.observation: Cyclized to 40 in 60% with no fragmentation, ruling out poor orbital overlap as the reason for lack of clock ring-opening

probe_reagents.name: C2-cyclopropyl probe (42 from enone 41)
probe_reagents.observation: Cyclopropane fully opened; ketone 44 (and alcohol 45 with BAHA) obtained in low yield, consistent with cyclopropylcarbinyl radical/cation at C2 lacking siloxy stabilization

probe_reagents.name: tris(4-bromophenyl)aminium hexachloroantimonate (BAHA)
probe_reagents.observation: Chemical one-electron oxidant gave the same ring-opened products (44 + 45), supporting a radical-cation intermediate

probe_reagents.name: 2-propanol (electrolyte cosolvent)
probe_reagents.observation: Traps furyl oxocarbenium to give isolable dihydrofuran acetal 46 when acidic workup is omitted

probe_reagents.name: byproduct enone 31 from substrate 16h
probe_reagents.observation: ~5% enone byproduct from deprotonation of the enol ether radical cation supports initial SET at the silyl enol ether

10.1021_jo049889i.notes: Alternative path b (initial SET from furan to give furan radical cation 26, attacked by silyl enol ether to give furyl radical 27) was considered but disfavored by CV data showing the silyl enol ether is more easily oxidized than the furan. Cis-fused ring junction stereochemistry is rationalized by a cis-exo transition state (TS-1) where the furan closes on the radical cation in an exo orientation. The C1 cyclopropyl clock failed to ring-open, attributed to siloxy stabilization of the adjacent radical cation; C2 substitution lacking such stabilization did ring-open.

10.1021_acs.joc.3c02777.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical trifluoromethylation/cyclization cascade of N-cyanamide alkenes with CF3SO2Na to construct trifluoromethylated nonaromatic fused azaheterobicyclic cyclic amidines.
10.1021_acs.joc.3c02777.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of CF3SO2- (Langlois reagent anion) to generate CF3 radical via loss of SO2; final benzhydryl radical intermediate is also oxidized at the anode to a benzhydryl cation that aromatizes by deprotonation.
10.1021_acs.joc.3c02777.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes CF3SO2- to CF3SO2 radical, which loses SO2 to give CF3 radical; CF3 radical adds to the styryl alkene of the N-cyanamide (Ia), which undergoes intramolecular cyclization onto the cyano group to give an iminyl radical (IIa), then radical cyclization onto the diphenyl alkene affords benzhydryl radical IIIa. The anode further oxidizes IIIa to benzhydryl cation IVa, and loss of H+ delivers the aromatic imidazoline 3b; H+ is reduced to H2 at the cathode.

key_intermediates.name: CF3SO2 radical (sulfonyl radical from anodic SET on CF3SO2-)
Ia: carbon-centered radical from CF3 addition to alkene of N-cyanamide 1b
IIa: iminyl radical from intramolecular cyclization onto cyano group
IIIa: benzhydryl (diphenylmethyl) radical after cyclization onto electron-deficient alkene
IVa: benzhydryl cation from anodic oxidation of IIIa

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Yield of 3b drops to 24%; reaction partly suppressed, indicating radical intermediates.

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: No 3b detected (nd); reaction fully suppressed, supporting radical pathway.

probe_reagents.name: Prolonged reaction time control (overoxidation probe)
probe_reagents.observation: After 4.5 h: trace 3b, 17% of byproduct 13, and 40% benzophenone, indicating anodic overoxidation of 3b.

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of 1b, 2a, 3b and mixtures
probe_reagents.observation: No oxidation peak for 1b in the window; 2a oxidizes at 1.53 V and 3b at 1.62 V (vs SCE), consistent with CF3SO2- being the species oxidized and with overoxidation of 3b.

10.1021_acs.joc.3c02777.notes: Overoxidation of product 3b at the anode accounts for benzophenone and byproduct 13 formation at prolonged times. Substrates with acyclic internal alkene give hydrotrifluoromethylation product 5h' instead of cyclization; phenyl-substituted linker analogue gives 5b' because the benzyl radical is directly oxidized at the anode rather than cyclizing onto the cyano group, consistent with the radical-polar crossover step in the proposed cycle.

10.1021_acs.joc.3c02934.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical intramolecular 6-endo-trig cyclization/selenylation of 2-aminochalcones (and 2-hydroxychalcones) with diaryl diselenides to give 3-selenyl azaflavanones and flavanones
10.1021_acs.joc.3c02934.electrochemical_role: Cathodic reduction of (PhSe)2 generates the [PhSeSePh]•– radical anion that fragments to PhSe• (and PhSe–, which is reoxidized at the graphite anode to PhSe•); the resulting PhSe radical adds to the chalcone, and the benzyl radical adduct is then anodically oxidized (SET) to a cation that cyclizes
10.1021_acs.joc.3c02934.electron_flow: Cathode reduces (PhSe)2 to its radical anion which cleaves to PhSe• and PhSe–; PhSe– is reoxidized at the anode to PhSe•. PhSe• adds to the chalcone C=C giving a stabilized benzyl radical, which is anodically oxidized to a cation that tautomerizes to a seleniranium ion and is opened by intramolecular anti-nucleophilic attack of the N–H (or O–H), followed by deprotonation to give the trans product.

key_intermediates.name: α-selenyl benzyl C-radical adduct (IV) on chalcone β-carbon
N-cyclized cationic intermediate (VII) prior to deprotonation

probe_reagents.name: BHT (butylated hydroxytoluene)
probe_reagents.observation: 2.0 equiv BHT completely inhibited formation of 3aa, indicating radical intermediates

probe_reagents.name: PhSeCl (no current)
probe_reagents.observation: Without current, 1a + PhSeCl gave 3aa in 88%, showing an ionic (PhSe+) pathway is also viable

probe_reagents.name: PhSeH (no current)
probe_reagents.observation: 0% 3aa without current

probe_reagents.name: PhSeH (with current)
probe_reagents.observation: 72% 3aa under current, consistent with PhSe• being the active species generated electrochemically

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of (PhSe)2 and 1a
probe_reagents.observation: (PhSe)2 reduction at 0.06 V and oxidation at 0.885 V (vs Ag/AgCl); 1a has higher oxidation/reduction potentials, so 2a is preferentially reduced at the cathode initiating the process

10.1021_acs.joc.3c02934.notes: Authors propose a dominant radical pathway (cathodic reduction of (PhSe)2 → PhSe• addition → anodic oxidation of benzyl radical → seleniranium → anti cyclization gives trans-product, dr > 20:1). A parallel ionic pathway via direct anodic generation of PhSe+ (II-2) leading to seleniranium VI is acknowledged as occurring simultaneously but is slower; this is supported by the PhSeCl-without-current control.

10.1021_acs.joc.5c01733.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical trifluoromethylation/iminyl-radical-mediated 1,5-HAT cyclization of N-cyanamide alkenes with CF3SO2Na to give trifluoromethylated bicyclic amidines and N-acryloylpyrrolidin-2-one derivatives
10.1021_acs.joc.5c01733.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of CF3SO2Na (2a, Eox = 1.13 V vs SCE) to generate CF3 radical (after loss of SO2); a second anodic oxidation converts the post-1,5-HAT benzylic radical IIIa to the benzylic carbocation IVa, which cyclizes to product. Cathode reduces protons to H2.
10.1021_acs.joc.5c01733.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes CF3SO2- to CF3SO2• which extrudes SO2 to give CF3•; CF3• adds to the N-cyanamide alkene to form C-radical Ia that cyclizes onto the cyanamide N≡C to give iminyl radical IIa; 1,5-HAT generates benzylic radical IIIa, which is further oxidized at the anode to benzylic cation IVa; intramolecular cyclization of the iminyl nitrogen onto the cation and deprotonation furnishes amidine 3a, while H+ is reduced to H2 at the cathode.

key_intermediates.name: carbon-centered radical Ia from CF3• addition to N-cyanamide alkene 1a
iminyl radical IIa from intramolecular cyclization of Ia
benzylic radical IIIa formed by 1,5-HAT from iminyl radical
benzylic carbocation IVa from anodic oxidation of IIIa

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: Product 3a not detected, consistent with radical intermediates

probe_reagents.name: 1,1-diphenylethylene (DPE)
probe_reagents.observation: 3a not detected; CF3-DPE adduct 8 isolated in 7% yield, trapping CF3 radical

probe_reagents.name: water (acetone/H2O 5/1)
probe_reagents.observation: Isolation of α-hydroxy pyrrolidinone 4 (21%) supports benzylic cation intermediate IVa

probe_reagents.name: HFIP cosolvent (acetone/HFIP/H2O 10/1/1)
probe_reagents.observation: α,α-Diphenylacetyl-pyrrolidin-2-one 5 (18%) isolated, supporting iminyl radical intermediate IIa

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of 2a/1a/3a/6c/7c
probe_reagents.observation: 2a oxidizes at 1.13 V, well below 1a (2.43 V), 6c (2.32 V), 3a (2.50 V), and 7c (1.46 V), indicating preferential anodic oxidation of CF3SO2Na and explaining overoxidation susceptibility of 7

10.1021_acs.joc.5c01733.notes: Two parallel product branches from common radical intermediates: bicyclic amidine 3a (from cation IVa cyclization) and N-acryloylpyrrolidinone 7a (from elimination/hydrolysis of cation IVb derived from substrate 6a). Side product 4 (water trap of IVa) and 5 (from iminyl radical IIa pathway) serve as diagnostic intermediates rather than productive intermediates. No oxidant or transition metal required; two-step anodic oxidation distinguishes this from Cu-catalyzed oxidative addition/reductive elimination pathway.

10.1021_acs.joc.1c03134.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical acylcyanation and aminocyanation of alkenes using α-oxocarboxylic acids (or (PhSO2)2NH) and a cyanobenziodoxole (CBX) as cyano source under undivided-cell constant-current conditions
10.1021_acs.joc.1c03134.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation/decarboxylation of α-oxocarboxylate to an acyl radical; cathodic reduction regenerates iodoarene from the iodine(III)-derived intermediate after CN transfer
10.1021_acs.joc.1c03134.electron_flow: At the Pt anode, the α-oxocarboxylate is oxidized by 1 e- and loses CO2 to give an acyl radical, which adds to the styrene to form a β-keto C-radical; this radical abstracts CN from CBX (3a) to deliver product 4 plus an iodoaryl carboxylate, which is reduced at the carbon cathode to close the redox balance. Without CBX, the C-radical is further oxidized anodically to a cation that eliminates to an enone byproduct.

key_intermediates.name: α-oxocarboxylate radical (A, after 1 e- anodic oxidation)
acyl (benzoyl) radical B after loss of CO2
β-keto carbon-centered radical C (acyl radical addition to styrene)
β-keto carbocation D (further anodic oxidation of C in absence of CBX)
CN-trapped product 4 and iodoaryl carboxylate F (from CBX after CN transfer to C)
homocoupling dimer 8 (PhC(O)C(O)Ph, benzil-type) from acyl radical dimerization

probe_reagents.name: α-oxocarboxylic acid 1b (PhCOCO2H) under standard conditions without alkene
probe_reagents.observation: Homocoupling product 8 (benzil-type) isolated in 37%, supporting facile acyl radical formation

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of CBX (3a)
probe_reagents.observation: CBX shows no redox activity between -2.0 and +2.5 V vs Ag/AgCl, ruling out direct CBX reduction/oxidation as the initiation step

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of PhCOCO2H 1b with/without NaOH and styrene
probe_reagents.observation: Weak oxidation at 1.26 V for 1b alone; strong peak at 1.26 V upon addition of 2 equiv NaOH (carboxylate easier to oxidize); catalytic current 1.0–2.0 V when 1b/NaOH/styrene combined, consistent with acyl radical addition to alkene

10.1021_acs.joc.1c03134.notes: Alternative off-cycle pathway: in the absence of CBX, the β-keto C-radical C is anodically oxidized to cation D, which deprotonates to enone 7. The mechanism is supported by CV and a homocoupling trapping experiment but lacks radical traps (TEMPO/BHT), KIE, or DFT, hence medium strength.

10.1021_acs.joc.5c00510.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical nitroindolylation bifunctionalization of arylalkenes using NaNO2 as nitro radical source, LiBr as mediator, and indoles to install -NO2 and indolyl groups across styrenes
10.1021_acs.joc.5c00510.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of nitrite (NO2-) to nitro free radical (NO2•), facilitated by Cu(I)/Cu(II) and B(C6F5)3; bromide is also oxidized to Br• which traps the benzylic radical; cathode reduces protons to H2
10.1021_acs.joc.5c00510.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes NO2- to •NO2 (mediated by Cu(I)→Cu(II) and B(C6F5)3); •NO2 adds to styrene 1a giving benzylic radical Int I, which Cu(II) further oxidizes to benzylic cation Int II; Br- captures Int II to form bromonitro intermediate 5, which undergoes SN2 by N-methylindole at C3 to deliver 4aa, while the cathode reduces H+ to H2.
10.1021_acs.joc.5c00510.rate_determining_step: SN2 displacement of bromide in intermediate 5 by C3 of N-methylindole to form the C-C bond

key_intermediates.name: benzylic carbon radical Int I from NO2 addition to styrene
benzylic carbocation Int II from Cu(II) oxidation of Int I
nitrostyrene side product (path d, H elimination)

probe_reagents.name: step-by-step loading (1a + NaNO2 first, then indole 3a)
probe_reagents.observation: Product 4aa obtained in 82% yield, supporting stepwise formation of a bromonitro intermediate before indole addition

probe_reagents.name: independently prepared bromonitro compound 5
probe_reagents.observation: 5 + indole 3a with B(C6F5)3 gave 4aa in 97% yield, confirming 5 as on-cycle intermediate; structure verified by 1H/13C NMR

probe_reagents.name: NaNO3 additive
probe_reagents.observation: CV shows strong oxidation peak at 0.62 V for NaNO2+NaNO3 vs 0.64 V for NaNO2 alone, indicating nitrate enhances Faradaic efficiency of nitrite oxidation

probe_reagents.name: B(C6F5)3
probe_reagents.observation: Lewis acid promotes nitrite oxidation to •NO2 (synergistic effect seen in CV); 0.5 equiv used in standard conditions

10.1021_acs.joc.5c00510.notes: EIS (impedance spectroscopy) data also support the role of NaNO3 in lowering charge-transfer resistance. Two competing side pathways are noted: path b giving 3-nitroindole (direct nitration of indole) and path d giving β-nitrostyrene via H-elimination from Int II; these must be suppressed. No TEMPO/BHT radical trap or KIE experiments were reported; mechanism is supported mainly by CV/EIS, stepwise loading, and isolation of bromonitro intermediate 5.

10.1021_acs.joc.3c00817.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical [2+2] cycloaddition of two alpha,beta-unsaturated carbonyl compounds (chalcones) to give tetrasubstituted cyclobutanes using NaI as redox mediator in an undivided cell
10.1021_acs.joc.3c00817.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of iodide (2 I- -> I2) generates I2 which iodinates the enolate/enone; cathode reduces water to H2 and HO-, regenerating base/closing the loop in a paired-electrolysis scenario
10.1021_acs.joc.3c00817.electron_flow: At the anode, 2 I- are oxidized (loss of 2 e-) to I2, which iodinates the chalcone enolate to give an alpha-iodo ketone; this intermediate undergoes Michael addition with a second chalcone enolate, and intramolecular displacement of iodide closes the cyclobutane ring. At the cathode, 2 H2O are reduced to H2 + 2 HO-, which regenerates the enolate base.

key_intermediates.name: iodine (I2) generated at the anode from 2 I-
open-chain bis-iodo / Michael adduct 2a' (precursor to cyclobutane via intramolecular SN2/displacement of I-)

10.1021_acs.joc.3c00817.notes: Mechanism in Scheme 4 is presented as 'plausible' without explicit radical traps, KIE, or CV data on these pages. NaI acts as both supporting electrolyte and redox mediator (I-/I2 shuttle). The chalcone precursors 1 are prepared by classical Claisen-Schmidt condensation (NaOH, EtOH/H2O) prior to electrolysis. Reaction is run in an undivided cell with PGE anode and stainless-steel cathode at 20 mA, MeCN-MeOH (3:1), 15 h (11.19 F/mol).

10.1021_acs.joc.4c00991.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical oxidative selenocyclization of N-allyl ureas with diaryl diselenides to give selenylated cyclic isoureas, with subsequent acid-catalyzed skeletal rearrangement to cyclic ureas
10.1021_acs.joc.4c00991.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of diaryl diselenide (ArSe-SeAr) to aryl selenium radical cations / ArSe+ cation; cathodic reduction of protons to H2 at platinum
10.1021_acs.joc.4c00991.electron_flow: At the graphite anode, diaryl diselenide B is oxidized (lower onset, 1.49 V vs 1.57 V for substrate) to generate ArSe+, which electrophilically adds to the allyl alkene of urea A to give a seleniranium ion I; intramolecular 5-exo-tet attack by the urea oxygen yields cyclic isourea C, while the two released protons are reduced to H2 at the Pt cathode. Under HClO4/heat, C is protonated at the exocyclic N (II) and undergoes reversible NGP back to I followed by irreversible N-attack to give the thermodynamic cyclic urea D.
10.1021_acs.joc.4c00991.rate_determining_step: Anodic oxidation of diaryl diselenide to generate the ArSe+ electrophile (lowest-potential, kinetically gating event)

key_intermediates.name: diaryl diselenide radical cation / ArSe+ cation (B -> ArSe+)
cyclic seleniranium ion (I) from ArSe+ addition to allyl alkene
kinetic 5-exo-tet cyclic isourea product C (O-cyclized, selenylated oxazoline-type)
N-protonated isourea II (after protonation of exocyclic N of C)
regenerated seleniranium I via reversible neighboring group participation (NGP) of Se

probe_reagents.name: argon atmosphere (O2 exclusion)
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction proceeds with similar yield (75%) under Ar, ruling out aerial O2 involvement

probe_reagents.name: divided cell with A1+B1 in anolyte
probe_reagents.observation: C1 formed in 55% yield, confirming oxidative (anodic) pathway

probe_reagents.name: divided cell with A1+B1 in catholyte
probe_reagents.observation: No product formed, confirming anode is the working electrode

probe_reagents.name: competition: (4-MeC6H4Se)2 (B2) vs (4-FC6H4Se)2 (B6)
probe_reagents.observation: Slight preference for electron-deficient 4-F aryl selenide product C27 (35%) over 4-Me C23 (27%), consistent with easier oxidation/transfer of more electron-poor diselenide

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of (PhSe)2 and A12
probe_reagents.observation: (PhSe)2 oxidizes at Ep=1.49 V with strong current; substrate A12 oxidizes at 1.57 V with much weaker current, indicating diselenide is preferentially oxidized

10.1021_acs.joc.4c00991.notes: Two-stage mechanism: (1) electrochemical selenocyclization gives kinetic O-cyclized isourea C; (2) separate HClO4/dioxane acid-catalyzed skeletal rearrangement converts C to thermodynamic N-cyclized urea D via reversible seleniranium reformation (NGP) then irreversible N-attack. Authors invoke H2 evolution at Pt cathode as the counter-reaction. No radical traps (TEMPO/BHT), KIE, or DFT reported; mechanism supported mainly by CV, divided-cell, and control experiments.

10.1021_acs.joc.5c00049_sup_1.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical bromocyclization of N-tosyl alkynyl alkenes mediated by an aryl iodide catalyst to form bromo-substituted pyrrolidine/heterocyclic products.
10.1021_acs.joc.5c00049_sup_1.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of [C2MPr]Br (ammonium bromide electrolyte) occurs first (Eox ≈ 1.21 V) to generate Br radical species; the aryl iodide (p-Tol-I) likely acts as a redox mediator. Cathode reduces protons/solvent.
10.1021_acs.joc.5c00049_sup_1.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes bromide (and/or aryl iodide mediator) to generate a Br-centered radical, which adds to the alkyne/alkene of substrate 1a; the resulting carbon radical undergoes intramolecular cyclization and is terminated by a second bromine atom transfer to give the dibrominated heterocycle.

key_intermediates.name: bromine radical (Br•) from anodic oxidation of bromide
carbon-centered radical from Br• addition to alkene/alkyne of substrate 1a
hypervalent aryl-iodine species from p-Tol-I oxidation (proposed mediator)

probe_reagents.name: 1,1-diphenylethylene (DPE)
probe_reagents.observation: Addition of 10 equiv DPE under standard conditions completely suppressed formation of product 2a (N.R.), consistent with a free-radical pathway.

10.1021_acs.joc.5c00049_sup_1.notes: CV shows oxidation of [C2MPr]Br (~1.21 V) is easier than that of 4-iodotoluene (~2.09 V) and substrate 1a, indicating bromide is oxidized first at the anode; the aryl iodide may still act as a redox mediator under turnover conditions. Radical trapping with DPE is suggestive but, as the authors note, inconclusive on its own.

10.1021_acs.joc.5c00049.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical oxidative dibrominative cyclization of 1,n-enynes using bromide ionic liquid as Br source and 4-iodotoluene as redox mediator to give vinyl/alkyl dibrominated heterocycles.
10.1021_acs.joc.5c00049.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of bromide (Br-) to bromine radical (Br•), which combines with 4-iodotoluene to form a hypervalent ArIBr2 (aryl iodine I/III) mediator; cathode reduces HFIP/H+ to H2.
10.1021_acs.joc.5c00049.electron_flow: At the anode, Br- is oxidized to Br•, which reacts with 4-iodotoluene to generate the active ArIBr2 hypervalent iodine(III) species; ArIBr2 engages the enyne to deliver two Br atoms via cyclization and C-Br bond formation, regenerating ArI. At the cathode, protons from HFIP are reduced to H2.

key_intermediates.name: vinyl bromide / alkyl bromide cyclized product 2a

probe_reagents.name: radical trap control experiments
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction inhibited, supporting a free-radical pathway.

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of Br- and 4-iodotoluene
probe_reagents.observation: Br- oxidizes prior to Br• formation; reduction peak for Br- disappears upon addition of 4-iodotoluene, indicating reaction between Br• and 4-iodotoluene to form ArIBr2.

10.1021_acs.joc.5c00049.notes: Recyclability demonstrated: [C2MPr]Br ionic liquid recovered (91%) via liquid-liquid extraction with bromoethane and reused for 5 cycles. Downstream derivatization (Suzuki with ArB(OH)2, SN2 with NaN3 and KI) selectively replaces the alkyl bromide while leaving the vinyl bromide intact, consistent with two electronically distinct C-Br bonds in the product.

10.1021_acs.joc.2c02309.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical PhI/TsOH-catalyzed oxidative cyclization of N-allylamides to dihydrooxazoles (and α-tosyloxylation of ketones) via an electrogenerated hypervalent iodine(III) Koser's reagent
10.1021_acs.joc.2c02309.electrochemical_role: Anodic 2e- oxidation of PhI (iodobenzene) in HFIP generates a hypervalent iodine(III) species [PhI(OCH(CF3)2)2] that acts as the active oxidant; iodine cycles between I(I) and I(III) at the anode
10.1021_acs.joc.2c02309.electron_flow: Anode removes 2e- from PhI in HFIP to form a PhI(III) bis-alkoxide; ligand exchange with TsOH·H2O gives Koser's reagent, which electrophilically activates the allyl double bond of the N-allylamide; the amide oxygen performs intramolecular 5-exo cyclization onto the iodonium-activated carbon, and reductive elimination from the resulting C-I(III) intermediate delivers OTs to the methylene and regenerates PhI.

key_intermediates.name: PhI(OCH(CF3)2)2 (anodically generated hypervalent iodine(III) bis-HFIP-oxy species)
Koser's reagent PhI(OH)(OTs) after ligand exchange with TsOH·H2O
Iodonium-activated alkene adduct (III): tosyloxy-iodane bound to alkene with amide oxygen poised for cyclization

probe_reagents.name: control without acid (TsOH·H2O omitted)
probe_reagents.observation: No reaction; starting material recovered, showing acid is required to form/stabilize Koser-type I(III) species

probe_reagents.name: control without PhI catalyst or without electricity
probe_reagents.observation: No reaction in α-tosyloxylation; confirms iodoarene mediator and anodic current are both essential

probe_reagents.name: TfOH as acid
probe_reagents.observation: No product formed (entry 1), attributed to instability of generated PhI(III) species

probe_reagents.name: BF3·Et2O as acid
probe_reagents.observation: Degradation of starting material, no desired product (entry 2)

probe_reagents.name: Gram-scale and catalyst recovery experiment
probe_reagents.observation: PhI recovered after 10 mmol scale-up (87% yield of 2a), supporting catalytic I(I)/I(III) turnover

10.1021_acs.joc.2c02309.notes: Mechanism (Scheme 4) is proposed by analogy to established PhI/Koser's-reagent chemistry and prior electrochemical hypervalent-iodine reports; no radical traps, KIE, EPR, CV, or DFT presented. The same catalytic system also performs α-tosyloxylation of ketones (Scheme 3) and 6-exo cyclization of homoallyl amides to dihydro-2H-pyrans, consistent with a generalized electrogenerated ArI(III) oxidant rather than a substrate-specific pathway. HFIP is implicated both as solvent stabilizing the cationic I(III) intermediate and as the initial alkoxide ligand on iodine.

10.1021_acs.joc.3c01310.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical radical cascade trifluoromethylation/sulfonylation-cyclization of N-allylamides with CF3SO2Na or arylsulfonyl hydrazines to access trifluoromethylated or sulfonylated 4,5-dihydrooxazoles.
10.1021_acs.joc.3c01310.electrochemical_role: Anodic single-electron oxidation of CF3SO2- to CF3SO2 radical (which loses SO2 to give CF3 radical); subsequent second anodic SET oxidizes the alkyl radical intermediate I to cation II. At the cathode, water is reduced to H2 and OH-.
10.1021_acs.joc.3c01310.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes CF3SO2- to CF3SO2 radical, which loses SO2 to give CF3 radical; CF3 radical adds regioselectively to the alkene of the N-allylamide to form C-radical I, which is oxidized at the anode a second time to carbocation II; K2CO3-assisted intramolecular attack of the amide oxygen on II with loss of H+ delivers the dihydrooxazole 3a. The cathode reduces water to H2 and hydroxide.

key_intermediates.name: CF3SO2 radical (from anodic oxidation of triflinate)
Carbon-centered radical I (CF3 addition adduct to allylamide alkene)
Benzylic/tertiary carbocation II (after second anodic SET)
Dihydrooxazole 3a (product after intramolecular O-cyclization and deprotonation)

probe_reagents.name: 1,1-diphenylethylene (DPE)
probe_reagents.observation: With CF3SO2Na: 3a only trace, CF3-DPE-OH adduct 6 detected by GC-MS. With sulfonyl source 4a: 5a 18%, Ts-DPE adduct 8 detected by GC-MS; confirms CF3 and sulfonyl radicals.

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: With 2a: 3a yield reduced to 23%, BHT-CF3 adduct 7 detected by GC-MS. With 4a: 5a 25%, BHT-Ts adduct 9 detected; supports radical pathway.

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of 1a, 2a, 4a
probe_reagents.observation: E_ox(2a) = 1.16 V and E_ox(4a) = 1.33 V vs SCE, both lower than E_ox(1a) = 1.89–1.90 V vs SCE, indicating triflinate/sulfinate is oxidized first at the anode.

probe_reagents.name: No-electricity control (entry 15)
probe_reagents.observation: No reaction without applied current, confirming electrochemical initiation.

10.1021_acs.joc.3c01310.notes: Mechanism is supported by radical trapping with BHT and DPE plus CV showing preferential oxidation of the sulfinate/triflinate over the N-allylamide. No DFT or KIE studies were performed. The cyclization step (II → 3a) is depicted as a polar intramolecular electrophilic O-cyclization promoted by K2CO3.

10.1021_acs.joc.2c03020.rxn_class_summary: electrochemical oxidative radical 1,2-halofunctionalization (iodo-, bromo-, chloro-) of alkenes with carboxylic acids, water, or alcohols as nucleophiles using nBu4NX as the halogen source
10.1021_acs.joc.2c03020.electrochemical_role: anodic oxidation of halide (X-) from nBu4NX to halogen radical (X•), which adds to the alkene; the resulting carbon radical is further anodically oxidized to a carbocation that is trapped by the nucleophile. An alternate path involves anodic oxidation of X• to X+ which forms a haliranium ion intermediate.
10.1021_acs.joc.2c03020.electron_flow: Halide anion X- is preferentially oxidized at the anode (lower Eox than the alkene) to give X•, which adds to the alkene to form a benzylic C-radical; this radical is further oxidized at the anode to a benzylic cation that is captured by the carboxylate/water/alcohol nucleophile. In parallel, X• can be further oxidized to X+ which forms a haliranium ion attacked by the nucleophile. At the cathode, H+ is reduced to H2.

key_intermediates.name: halogen radical X• (I•, Br•, Cl•) generated at anode
β-halo carbon radical (intermediate I) from X• addition to alkene
β-halo benzylic carbocation (intermediate II) from anodic oxidation of I
haliranium / halonium ion (intermediate III) from X+ addition to alkene

probe_reagents.name: DMPO (spin trap)
probe_reagents.observation: EPR signals consistent with iodine and bromine radicals detected during electrolysis of nBu4NI and nBu4NBr; DMPO adducts also observed in model reactions with 1a/1b

probe_reagents.name: vinylcyclopropane 2a (radical clock)
probe_reagents.observation: With nBu4NI only cyclopropyl-retained product 54 (56%) was obtained (radical oxidized to cation before ring opening, or I- oxidized to I+); with nBu4NBr both retained 55 (12%) and ring-opened 56 (40%) formed; with nBu4NCl retained 57 (20%) and ring-opened dichloro 58 (32%) formed, supporting radical intermediates Int-2 and Int-3

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of nBu4NI, nBu4NBr, nBu4NCl vs 1-methoxy-4-vinylbenzene and benzoic acid
probe_reagents.observation: Eox(nBu4NI)=+0.373 V, Eox(nBu4NBr)=+0.686 V, Eox(nBu4NCl)=+0.815 V vs SCE in MeCN/DCM, all much lower than 1a (+1.413 V); benzoic acid shows no oxidation up to +3.0 V — halides are preferentially oxidized

10.1021_acs.joc.2c03020.notes: Two parallel pathways are proposed in Scheme 4: (a) radical addition of X• to alkene then anodic oxidation of the C-radical to carbocation II (radical–polar crossover), and (b) anodic oxidation of X• to X+ followed by haliranium ion III formation; both are quenched by the nucleophile. The cyclopropyl-retained product with iodide could indicate either I- → I+ pathway or fast oxidation of the radical to a cation before ring opening, so the iodination case does not unambiguously distinguish the two routes.

10.1021_acs.joc.5c01984.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical three-component thio-perfluoroalkylation of alkenes with thiophenols and perfluoroalkyl iodides under FeCl2 catalysis to give vicinal thioether/perfluoroalkyl products.
10.1021_acs.joc.5c01984.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of an Fe(II)-thiolate complex (C) to an Fe(III) species (D), with concomitant cathodic reduction of D back to release product and regenerate Fe(II); perfluoroalkyl radical is generated via halogen-bonding activation of nC4F9I by TMEDA/H2O rather than direct electrolysis.
10.1021_acs.joc.5c01984.electron_flow: TMEDA/H2O activates nC4F9I via halogen bonding to release nC4F9 radical, which adds to the alkene giving benzylic radical B; in parallel, ArSH coordinates Fe(II) (C), is oxidized at the anode to Fe(III)-thiolate D, which couples with radical B and is reduced at the cathode to deliver the product while regenerating Fe(II).

key_intermediates.name: Halogen-bond (XB) complex of nC4F9I with TMEDA/H2O
Benzylic carbon radical B from nC4F9 addition to styrene
Fe(III)-thiolate species D after anodic oxidation
Perfluoroalkyl iodide adduct C (alkyl iodide on styrene)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Product 4aa only in trace amounts; TEMPO–nC4F9-alkyl adduct B detected by LCMS, supporting perfluoroalkyl/benzyl radical intermediates.

probe_reagents.name: Diaryl disulfide 2a' (4-MeO-C6H4-S-S-C6H4-4-OMe)
probe_reagents.observation: Using disulfide instead of thiol gives only trace 4aa, indicating disulfide is not the productive intermediate.

probe_reagents.name: nC4F9I + alkene without thiol (control for iodide adduct C)
probe_reagents.observation: Iodo-perfluoroalkylated alkene C not detected (n.d.), ruling out ATRA-type alkyl iodide as the intermediate.

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry (with/without FeCl2)
probe_reagents.observation: Eox decreases from 3.43 V to 3.23 V upon addition of FeCl2, indicating FeCl2 lowers the oxidation potential and mediates anodic oxidation.

10.1021_acs.joc.5c01984.notes: Authors explicitly exclude two alternative pathways: (i) disulfide-mediated thiyl radical pathway and (ii) ATRA-type iodoperfluoroalkylation followed by SN2 with thiolate, since both surrogate intermediates fail to give product under standard conditions. The perfluoroalkyl radical is proposed to form via halogen-bonding activation rather than direct cathodic reduction of C-I.

10.1021_acs.joc.4c00707.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical 1,2-dithiocyanation of alkynes and alkenes with NH4SCN to give vicinal bis(thiocyanato) vinyl/alkyl arenes
10.1021_acs.joc.4c00707.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of SCN- to thiocyanogen radical (•SCN, in equilibrium with (SCN)2); cathodic reduction of TFA protons to H2
10.1021_acs.joc.4c00707.electron_flow: SCN- is oxidized at the anode to •SCN, which adds to the alkyne/alkene to give a carbon-centered radical (A); Path A: a second •SCN couples with A to give the bis-thiocyanate; Path B: A is further oxidized at the anode to a vinyl/alkyl cation (B) that is trapped by SCN-. At the cathode, TFA is reduced to H2.

key_intermediates.name: β-thiocyanato vinyl/alkyl carbon radical (intermediate A from alkene/alkyne addition of •SCN)
β-thiocyanato vinyl/alkyl cation (intermediate B, from further anodic oxidation of A)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction of 1a suppressed to trace 2a; TEMPO-SCN/vinyl adduct detected by HRMS [M+H]+ = 317.1682. For styrene 3a, 4a yield dropped to 16%.

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: Yield of 2a reduced to 23%; 4a only trace, supporting radical pathway

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry (NH4SCN, 1a, TFA in MeCN/DCM)
probe_reagents.observation: Oxidation wave observed for NH4SCN; mixture with 1a+NH4SCN+TFA+MeCN+DCM shows enhanced current consistent with anodic SCN- oxidation initiating the reaction

10.1021_acs.joc.4c00707.notes: Authors propose two parallel pathways from radical intermediate A: Path A (radical-radical coupling with a second •SCN) is presented alongside Path B (radical-polar crossover via further anodic oxidation to vinyl cation B, then SCN- attack). The paper does not definitively distinguish A vs B. A derivatization experiment (2a + Ph2P(O)H/DBU → 6a) demonstrates synthetic utility but is not mechanistically diagnostic.

10.1021_acs.joc.1c02557.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical oxidative oxysulfonylation of o-vinyl benzoic acids with sulfonyl hydrazides to form γ-sulfonylated phthalide lactones
10.1021_acs.joc.1c02557.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of sulfonyl hydrazide to sulfonyl radical (via diazo intermediate, loss of N2); second anodic oxidation of benzylic C-radical adduct to a benzylic carbocation; cathodic reduction of protons to H2
10.1021_acs.joc.1c02557.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes sulfonyl hydrazide (−3e−, −3H+) to a diazo species that extrudes N2 to give a sulfonyl radical; this radical adds to the vinyl group of the o-vinyl benzoic acid to form a benzylic C-radical, which is further oxidized at the anode to a benzylic cation that is captured intramolecularly by the ortho-carboxyl oxygen, with cathodic H+ reduction to H2 closing the circuit.

key_intermediates.name: Benzylic carbon radical C from sulfonyl radical addition to o-vinyl benzoic acid
Benzylic carbocation D (after anodic oxidation of C)
γ-Sulfonylated phthalide product 3 (after intramolecular carboxylate cyclization and deprotonation)

probe_reagents.name: 1,1-Diphenylethylene (DPE)
probe_reagents.observation: Only trace 3a formed; radical trapping adduct 5 (Ph2C=CH-Ts type) detected by LC-MS, supporting a sulfonyl radical intermediate

probe_reagents.name: Triethyl phosphite P(OEt)3
probe_reagents.observation: Yield of 3a reduced from 96% to 66%, consistent with a radical pathway

probe_reagents.name: No-current control
probe_reagents.observation: No product 3a formed without electric current, confirming electrochemical activation is essential

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of 1a and 2a
probe_reagents.observation: Oxidation peaks at 1.78 V (1a) and 1.39 V (2a) vs Ag/AgCl, indicating sulfonyl hydrazide 2a is preferentially oxidized at the anode to generate the sulfonyl radical

10.1021_acs.joc.1c02557.notes: Mechanism is consistent with literature precedent (refs 18–20). Radical–polar crossover (oxidation of C-radical to cation D) is invoked rather than direct radical cyclization; ortho-substituted substrates (4n, 4o) failed, attributed to steric hindrance rather than electronic effects.

10.1021_acs.joc.5c01037.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical tandem radical addition/cyclization of para-quinone methides with haloacetonitriles to construct spiro[cyclohexane-1,2'-indene]-2,5-dien-4-one derivatives
10.1021_acs.joc.5c01037.electrochemical_role: Cathodic reduction of chloroacetonitrile generates a cyanomethyl radical and chloride anion; the chloride anion is then oxidized at the anode to a chlorine radical that couples with the carbon-centered intermediate
10.1021_acs.joc.5c01037.electron_flow: Chloroacetonitrile is reduced at the cathode to give a cyanomethyl radical plus chloride; the cyanomethyl radical adds to the alkyne of the p-quinone methide and undergoes 5-endo-dig cyclization to vinyl radical B; meanwhile chloride is oxidized at the anode to a chlorine radical, which couples with B to deliver the (Z)-spiro product 3aa.

key_intermediates.name: cyanomethyl radical (·CH2CN) from cathodic reduction of ClCH2CN
Radical intermediate A from addition of ·CH2CN to alkyne of p-QM 1a
Indene-type C-centered radical B after 5-endo-dig cyclization (favored)
Alternative radical B' (disfavored due to steric hindrance)
Chlorine radical from anodic oxidation of Cl-

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO (2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-1-piperidinyloxy)
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction completely inhibited with 2.0 equiv TEMPO, indicating radical intermediates

probe_reagents.name: BHT (2,6-di-tert-butyl-4-methylphenol)
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction completely inhibited with 2.0 equiv BHT, supporting radical pathway

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of chloroacetonitrile 2a
probe_reagents.observation: Cathodic reduction peak at -0.992 V shows 2a can accept electrons at the cathode

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of NaCl
probe_reagents.observation: Anodic oxidation peak at +1.23 V shows chloride can lose electrons at the anode

10.1021_acs.joc.5c01037.notes: Z-selectivity rationalized by preferred formation of intermediate B over sterically disfavored B'; the (E)-isomer pathway via B' is suppressed by steric hindrance. X-ray of product 3af confirms structure. Scaled-up reaction (8 mmol) gave 3ca in 62% yield; product can be elaborated by Sonogashira coupling.

10.1021_acs.joc.4c02530_sup_1.rxn_class_summary: Photoelectrochemical radical thiocyanation/cyclization of N-allyl pyrazoles with NaSCN to afford SCN-functionalized pyrazolo-fused heterocycles via radical pathway
10.1021_acs.joc.4c02530_sup_1.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of SCN- (assisted by photoexcited TPPT acting as single-electron oxidant) generates thiocyanate radical (and/or (SCN)2); the radical adds to the alkene, triggering radical cyclization onto the arene followed by rearomatization
10.1021_acs.joc.4c02530_sup_1.electron_flow: Photoexcited TPPT* oxidizes SCN- to SCN• (TPPT is regenerated at the anode); SCN• adds to the terminal alkene of 1a to give a tertiary C-radical that cyclizes onto the pendant arene, and rearomatization (H loss/oxidation) delivers product 3a.

key_intermediates.name: carbon-centered radical from SCN addition to alkene
cyclohexadienyl radical after intramolecular arene attack

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Yield of 3a dropped to 0% (vs 70% without quencher)

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: Yield <1%, reaction suppressed

probe_reagents.name: 1,1-diphenylethylene (DPE)
probe_reagents.observation: Yield <1%; (2-thiocyanatoethene-1,1-diyl)dibenzene 6 isolated in 75%, confirming SCN• generation

probe_reagents.name: p-dinitrobenzene (pDNB)
probe_reagents.observation: Yield 0%, consistent with SET inhibition

probe_reagents.name: CuCl2
probe_reagents.observation: Yield 0%, single electron pathway blocked

probe_reagents.name: CCl4
probe_reagents.observation: Yield 50%, partial inhibition

probe_reagents.name: (SCN)2 (NCS-SCN, thiocyanogen)
probe_reagents.observation: Used in place of NaSCN gave 3a in 64% NMR yield, indicating (SCN)2 can serve as a competent but less reactive intermediate; alternative pathways not via (SCN)2 may also operate

10.1021_acs.joc.4c02530_sup_1.notes: On-off experiments show both light AND current are required for sustained reactivity (light-off with continuous electrolysis stalls; electricity-off with continuous irradiation also stalls), consistent with a photoelectrochemical cycle where TPPT must be both photoexcited and electrochemically regenerated. (SCN)2 is shown to be a competent intermediate but is less reactive than NaSCN, so an alternative (non-(SCN)2) pathway involving direct anodic/photoredox oxidation of SCN- to SCN• is likely operative in parallel.

10.1021_acs.joc.5c00401.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical azidohydroxylation and azidoazidation (diazidation) of α-trifluoromethyl alkenes with TMSN3 via a radical-polar crossover mechanism affording β-CF3 azido alcohols or 1,2-diazides.
10.1021_acs.joc.5c00401.electrochemical_role: Anodic single-electron oxidation of azide (from TMSN3) to azidyl radical; subsequent second anodic oxidation of the β-CF3 alkyl radical adduct to a benzylic/α-CF3 carbocation. Cathode reduces HOAc to H2 and acetate.
10.1021_acs.joc.5c00401.electron_flow: At the anode, TMSN3 is oxidized by SET to an azidyl radical, which adds to the electron-poor α-CF3 alkene to give a benzylic C-radical; a second anodic oxidation converts this radical to a stabilized α-CF3 benzylic cation, which is trapped by H2O (path 1) to give the azido alcohol or by TMSN3/azide (path 2) to give the 1,2-diazide. The cathode reduces HOAc to H2 and acetate.

probe_reagents.name: 1,1-diphenylethylene (DPE)
probe_reagents.observation: Trapping adduct 3a' detected by HRMS, supporting involvement of an azidyl/C-radical.

probe_reagents.name: methanol (as solvent)
probe_reagents.observation: Azidomethoxylation product 4i obtained in 42% yield, supporting a carbocation intermediate trapped by MeOH.

probe_reagents.name: TMSN3 (CV)
probe_reagents.observation: Oxidation peak at +0.93 V vs Ag+/Ag, lower than alkene 1a (+2.10 V), indicating TMSN3 is preferentially oxidized at the anode.

probe_reagents.name: trifluoromethyl alkene 1a (CV)
probe_reagents.observation: Oxidation peak at +2.10 V vs Ag+/Ag, higher than TMSN3, ruling out direct alkene oxidation as initiation.

10.1021_acs.joc.5c00401.notes: Chemodivergence between azidohydroxylation (3) and diazidation (4) is controlled by electrode (Ni vs Pt cathode; graphite rod vs graphite felt anode) and solvent (DMSO/HOAc vs MeCN/HOAc); MeOH solvent diverts the cation to azidomethoxylation product, consistent with a radical-polar crossover via a carbocation rather than a closed-shell radical pathway.

10.1021_acs.joc.3c01310_sup_1.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical radical sulfonylation/cyclization of N-allylamides with sulfonyl hydrazides (or CF3SO2Na) to form trifluoromethylated/sulfonylated oxazolines via radical-polar crossover
10.1021_acs.joc.3c01310_sup_1.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of TsNHNH2 (4a) via stepwise -3H+/-3e- to generate sulfonyl diazo radical that loses N2 to give sulfonyl radical IV; a second anodic SET oxidizes the alkyl radical adduct V to cation VI; cathode reduces protons to H2
10.1021_acs.joc.3c01310_sup_1.electron_flow: At the anode, TsNHNH2 is oxidized (-3e-, -3H+) and extrudes N2 to give a sulfonyl radical, which adds regioselectively to the alkene of the N-allylamide forming a benzylic radical; a second anodic SET oxidizes this radical to a cation that is trapped intramolecularly by the amide oxygen to give the oxazoline after deprotonation. The cathode reduces protons to H2.

key_intermediates.name: carbon-centered benzylic radical adduct V (after sulfonyl addition to alkene of 1a)
benzylic carbocation VI (after second anodic SET)

probe_reagents.name: 1,1-diphenylethylene (DPE)
probe_reagents.observation: With CF3SO2Na: 3a only trace and Ph2C(OH)CF3-type adduct detected by GC-MS, indicating CF3 radical intercepted by DPE; with TsNHNH2: 5a obtained in 18% and Ts-CH=CPh2-type styrene adduct detected by GC-MS, indicating sulfonyl radical trapped by DPE

probe_reagents.name: BHT (2,6-di-tert-butyl-4-methylphenol)
probe_reagents.observation: With CF3SO2Na: 3a obtained in 23% with BHT-CF3 adduct (cyclohexadienone) detected by GC-MS; with TsNHNH2: 5a in 25% with BHT-Ts adduct detected by GC-MS, supporting CF3• and Ts• intermediates

probe_reagents.name: H-type divided cell experiment
probe_reagents.observation: Substrate + 2a/K2CO3 (or 4a) only in anodic chamber gave 3a (57%) and 5a (39%), confirming the key bond-forming events occur at the anode

10.1021_acs.joc.3c01310_sup_1.notes: Two coupled systems are described: (i) CF3SO2Na (2a) with K2CO3 in MeCN/H2O giving 3a (CF3-oxazoline), and (ii) TsNHNH2 (4a) with Me4NBF4 in MeCN giving 5a (Ts-oxazoline). The scheme S3 explicitly depicts only the sulfonyl hydrazide pathway, but the CF3SO2Na variant is proposed to proceed analogously via CF3• addition and radical-polar crossover. Divided-cell experiments locate the productive chemistry at the anode; DPE and BHT trapping experiments support discrete sulfonyl/CF3 radical intermediates.

10.1021_acs.joc.4c02363.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical oxidative intramolecular cyclization of alkenyl alcohols with external nucleophiles (diarylmethanimines or carboxylic acids) to access amino- and hydroxy-functionalized tetrahydrofurans/tetrahydropyrans
10.1021_acs.joc.4c02363.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of the alkene moiety to a radical cation, which is intercepted by the tethered alcohol; the resulting C-radical is further oxidized at the anode to a cation that is trapped by the external nucleophile (imine N or carboxylate O)
10.1021_acs.joc.4c02363.electron_flow: The anode oxidizes the alkene to a radical cation; the pendant hydroxyl cyclizes (5-endo-trig for n=1, 5-exo-trig for n=2) generating a C-centered radical, which is further oxidized at the anode to a carbocation; the external nucleophile (Ar2CNH or RCOOH) traps this cation, while protons are reduced to H2 at the Pt cathode.
10.1021_acs.joc.4c02363.rate_determining_step: Anodic single-electron oxidation of the alkene to the radical cation (initial SET)

key_intermediates.name: alkene-derived radical cation (Ia for n=1, Ib for n=2)
tetrahydrofuranyl C-centered radical II (n=1, 5-endo-trig from Ia)
tetrahydropyranyl/THF C-centered radical V (n=2, more stable than III)
oxocarbenium/benzylic cation from anodic oxidation of II/V
ester product Pb (with RCOOH nucleophile, 5-exo-trig pathway for n=2)

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of alkene S2, diphenylmethanimine, and acetic acid
probe_reagents.observation: Oxidation peaks of diphenylmethanimine (~2.60 V) and acetic acid (~2.80 V) are higher than that of the alkene C=C (~1.6 V using methoxy analogue S2-I/S2-II), indicating the alkene is oxidized first; cyclization proceeds via the alkene radical cation rather than free iminyl or acyloxy radicals

probe_reagents.name: O2 / air atmosphere control
probe_reagents.observation: Presence of O2 or air significantly inhibited the reaction, consistent with a radical-cation/radical pathway

probe_reagents.name: Current and time variation
probe_reagents.observation: Both decrease and increase in current/time lowered yield, consistent with insufficient turnover or overoxidation

10.1021_acs.joc.4c02363.notes: Two cyclization modes are proposed from the alkene radical cation: Path A (5-endo-trig, favored for n=1 via more stable radical II) gives product Pa; Path B (5-exo-trig, favored for n=2 via more stable radical V) gives Pb. For n=1, 5-exo radical IV is unstable; for n=2, 5-endo radical III is less stable than V. No TEMPO/BHT radical-trap or KIE experiments are reported on these pages; mechanism is supported primarily by CV and product selectivity.

10.1021_acs.joc.4c00297.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical oxidative sulfonylation-azidation of alkenes using arylsulfonyl hydrazides and TMSN3 to give β-azidoarylsulfones via radical-polar crossover
10.1021_acs.joc.4c00297.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of arylsulfonyl hydrazide to sulfonyl radical (with -3H+, -3e-, -N2); subsequent anodic oxidation of benzyl radical intermediate to benzyl cation; cathodic reduction of protons to H2
10.1021_acs.joc.4c00297.electron_flow: Anode oxidizes the arylsulfonyl hydrazide (losing 3e-, 3H+, N2) to generate a sulfonyl radical A, which adds to the styrene to give benzyl radical B; B is further oxidized at the anode to benzyl cation C, which is trapped by azide from TMSN3, while protons are reduced to H2 at the cathode.
10.1021_acs.joc.4c00297.rate_determining_step: Anodic oxidation of the sulfonyl hydrazide 1a (CV shows 1a oxidizes at lower potential than 2a; TMSN3 shows no oxidation peak)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction completely suppressed (4aa, 0%); TEMPO-sulfonyl adduct 5 detected by HRMS ([M+H]+ 430.2392)

probe_reagents.name: (1-cyclopropylvinyl)benzene
probe_reagents.observation: Ring-opened product 4ay isolated in 6% along with normal product 4ax (62%), confirming radical intermediate

probe_reagents.name: alkene-tethered diol substrate 6
probe_reagents.observation: Without TMSN3, intramolecular OH nucleophile traps the cation to give ring-formed tetrahydrofuran 7 in 51%, supporting carbocation intermediate

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of 1a, 2a, 3
probe_reagents.observation: 1a oxidation peak much lower than 2a; TMSN3 shows almost no oxidation peak, indicating 1a is oxidized first at anode

10.1021_acs.joc.4c00297.notes: Carbocation intermediate C is supported by trapping with internal hydroxyl nucleophile (Scheme 2d). Strongly electron-donating (p-OMe) and electron-withdrawing (p-NO2) styrenes failed, and p-CF3 styrene gave only trace product, consistent with the need for a moderately stabilizable benzylic radical/cation. α-substituted styrenes give higher yields due to more stable tertiary benzyl cation/radical.

10.1021_acs.joc.3c00475_sup_1.rxn_class_summary: Photo/electrochemical radical selenocyclization of N-tosyl allyl-(2-vinylaryl)amines with diphenyl diselenide to form selenated heterocycles
10.1021_acs.joc.3c00475_sup_1.electrochemical_role: Anodic/photoexcited oxidation enables single-electron transfer from substrate (alkene 2a) to the excited acridinium photocatalyst, generating a PhSe radical from (PhSe)2 that adds to the styrenyl alkene
10.1021_acs.joc.3c00475_sup_1.electron_flow: Photoexcited [Acr-Mes]+* is quenched by substrate 2a (Stern-Volmer linear, Ksv ~115 M^-1), generating an oxidized intermediate; (PhSe)2 provides PhSe radical that adds to the styrene, and cyclization onto the tethered N-allyl group followed by further SET/oxidation yields the selenated heterocycle 3a. Continuous irradiation is required (light on/off experiment).

key_intermediates.name: benzylic C-radical after PhSe addition to styrene

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Complete suppression of product (0% 3a) with both starting materials recovered; no TEMPO-SePh adduct detected by HRMS; authors suggest TEMPO interferes with electron transfer to diselenide

10.1021_acs.joc.3c00475_sup_1.notes: Fluorescence quenching is by substrate 2a (the alkene), not by (PhSe)2, indicating the photocatalyst initiates by oxidizing the alkene rather than the diselenide. TEMPO trap was inconclusive for identifying the PhSe radical since no adduct was observed; authors propose TEMPO instead blocks SET to diselenide, which is an alternative interpretation.

10.1021_acs.joc.4c02481.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical reductive bimolecular cycloaddition of 2-arylidene-1,3-indanediones to give spirocyclopentanol indane-1,3-diones with five contiguous stereocenters.
10.1021_acs.joc.4c02481.electrochemical_role: Cathodic single-electron reduction of 2-arylidene-1,3-indanedione (1a) to a radical anion (A) on the Pt cathode; the anode performs counter oxidation of hydroxide to O2/H2O.
10.1021_acs.joc.4c02481.electron_flow: At the Pt cathode, 1a is reduced by one electron to radical anion A (Ered = -1.06 V vs Ag/AgCl in the combined electrolyte, lower than with either electrolyte alone); A tautomerizes to A' which adds to a second 1a to give radical B; a second cathodic SET reduces B to anion C/C', which undergoes intramolecular aldol closure to D and is protonated by water to deliver 2a, while hydroxide is oxidized at the carbon anode to O2 + H2O.

key_intermediates.name: radical anion A of 2-benzylidene-1,3-indanedione (ketyl-type, radical on benzylic C)
tautomerized radical anion A' (radical on the indanedione enolate carbon)
radical adduct B from C-C coupling of A' with a second molecule of 1a
anion C / C' from second cathodic reduction of B
alkoxide/enolate D after intramolecular aldol cyclization

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction completely shut down (0% of 2a), indicating radical intermediates.

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: Yield of 2a dramatically decreased to 27%.

probe_reagents.name: 1,1-diphenylethylene (DPE)
probe_reagents.observation: Yield of 2a decreased to 19%.

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of 1a
probe_reagents.observation: Reduction peak at -1.06 V under standard conditions (Bu4NBF4 + Bu4NOTs), versus -1.25 V with only Bu4NBF4 and -1.38 V with only Bu4NOTs, showing combined electrolytes lower the reduction potential of 1a.

10.1021_acs.joc.4c02481.notes: Control experiments confirm both current and electrolyte are required (0% without either). Water modestly improves yield (60% without water vs 72% standard), attributed to conductivity/solubility and serving as proton source for D -> 2a. X-ray (CCDC 2383755) confirms the spirocyclic product structure. No DFT or KIE studies were reported; mechanism is proposed by analogy to prior electroreductive radical-radical/radical-anion couplings (refs 12,13).

10.1038_s41929-023-01050-y_sup_1.rxn_class_summary: Enantioselective electrochemical Rh-catalyzed dehydrogenation/conjugate addition of imidazolyl alkyl ketones with dienes (and Michael acceptors) via an α,β-unsaturated ketone (alkene) intermediate and Rh-enolate.
10.1038_s41929-023-01050-y_sup_1.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation (mediated by ferrocene) of the Rh-bound enolate/substrate drives α,β-dehydrogenation of the alkyl ketone to generate an electrophilic enone intermediate that is subsequently captured by a nucleophile (diene or enolate addition) under chiral Rh control.
10.1038_s41929-023-01050-y_sup_1.electron_flow: Λ-RhS coordinates the imidazolyl ketone and forms a Rh-enolate (assisted by DABCO); ferrocene is oxidized at the RVC anode to ferrocenium, which performs SET on the Rh-enolate to give an α-carbon radical that loses a second electron/proton to deliver the α,β-unsaturated ketone (52). This electrophilic enone is then trapped enantioselectively by a Rh-enolate of a second ketone or by a diene/Michael acceptor; protons are reduced to H2 at the Pt cathode.
10.1038_s41929-023-01050-y_sup_1.rate_determining_step: Anodic α,β-dehydrogenation of the Rh-enolate to give the enone intermediate (formation of 52)

probe_reagents.name: 2,3-dimethyl-1,3-butadiene (2a)
probe_reagents.observation: Omitted under standard conditions: the alkene intermediate 52 was isolated in 72% yield, confirming enone forms before diene engagement.

probe_reagents.name: no light control (without irradiation)
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction without light still delivers 52 in 71% yield, indicating the dehydrogenation step is electrochemical, not photochemical.

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: α-aminoxy adduct 53 isolated in 8% (23% ee) along with 86% of enone 52, supporting a transient α-carbon radical intermediate during dehydrogenation.

probe_reagents.name: phenyl vinyl ketone (Michael acceptor)
probe_reagents.observation: Under non-electrochemical conditions with Λ-RhS/DABCO, conjugate addition product 55 forms in 96% yield, 97% ee, demonstrating that the Rh-enolate is the nucleophilic species responsible for enantiocontrol.

10.1038_s41929-023-01050-y_sup_1.notes: Two key mechanistic conclusions: (i) the catalytic cycle proceeds through an isolable α,β-unsaturated ketone (52) generated by anodic dehydrogenation rather than by direct radical coupling, and (ii) enantioselectivity arises in the Rh-enolate Michael addition step (demonstrated by the non-electrochemical enolate trapping with phenyl vinyl ketone giving 97% ee). The low ee (23%) of the TEMPO adduct 53 suggests TEMPO traps a relatively free α-radical off the chiral Rh, consistent with an off-pathway/secondary capture rather than the productive enantiodetermining step.

10.1038_s41929-023-01050-y.rxn_class_summary: Photoelectrochemical asymmetric dehydrogenative [2+2] cycloaddition between alkyl 2-acyl imidazole ketones and alkenes, forging chiral cyclobutanes by activating two C(sp3)-H bonds and a C=C double bond under chiral-at-Rh Lewis acid / ferrocene-mediated electrolysis.
10.1038_s41929-023-01050-y.electrochemical_role: Ferrocene (Fc) acts as an anodic redox mediator: Fc is oxidized to Fc+ at the RVC anode, which then performs single-electron oxidation of the Rh-bound enolate intermediate II to a carbon radical cation III; the cathode reduces alcohol to H2 and alkoxide, completing the dehydrogenative cycle.
10.1038_s41929-023-01050-y.electron_flow: At the anode, Fc is oxidized to Fc+, which performs SET on the Rh-enolate II to give radical cation III; deprotonation yields allylic radical IV, which is further oxidized (by Fc+) to V/VI, the Rh-bound enone. Visible light excites VI to triplet biradical VII, which adds to the alkene to give 1,4-diradical VIII, followed by ISC and C-C bond closure to cyclobutane IX; protons released are reduced at the Pt cathode to H2, achieving overall dehydrogenation.
10.1038_s41929-023-01050-y.rate_determining_step: Electrochemical dehydrogenative oxidation of the alkyl ketone to the enone intermediate VI (1 -> 52, ~10 h) is markedly slower than the subsequent photochemical [2+2] cycloaddition (52 -> 3, ~3 h).

key_intermediates.name: Rh-bound alpha-carbonyl radical cation III (SET oxidation by Fc+)
Rh-bound allylic/alpha-carbonyl radical IV (after proton loss)
Rh-dienolate radical cation V (tautomer of VI after second SET)
Triplet 1,4-diradical VIII (after C-C bond formation with alkene)
Rh-coordinated cyclobutane IX (after ISC and ring closure)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: TEMPO-adduct 53 isolated (8%, 23% e.e.) at the alpha-carbonyl position; cyclobutane formation suppressed, supporting radical intermediates III/IV.

probe_reagents.name: Phenyl vinyl ketone (Michael acceptor)
probe_reagents.observation: Gave Michael adduct 55 (28%, 96% e.e.) plus mono-addition 54, confirming the enolate intermediate II.

probe_reagents.name: Reaction without diene 2
probe_reagents.observation: Enone 52 isolated in 72% yield, confirming the dehydrogenative pathway generates VI before cycloaddition.

probe_reagents.name: Stoichiometric Fc+PF6- (no current)
probe_reagents.observation: With 10 equiv Fc+PF6-, product 3 obtained in 25% (97% e.e.) plus enone 52 (35%), demonstrating Fc+ is the kinetically competent oxidant.

probe_reagents.name: Light/current on-off (sequential)
probe_reagents.observation: Electrolysis without light gives only enone 52; subsequent irradiation without current converts 52 + diene to 3 (77%, 14:1 d.r., 96% e.e.), decoupling the EC dehydrogenation from the photo-[2+2] step.

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of Fc / Fc + RhS-enolate
probe_reagents.observation: Catalytic current observed (oxidation peak enhanced, reduction peak vanishes) only when 1 + Rh + DABCO are present, confirming Fc+ oxidizes enolate II (Ep/2 ~0.50 V vs ~0.44 V for Fc+/Fc).

10.1038_s41929-023-01050-y.notes: Authors explicitly exclude an alternative pathway in which an electro-oxidized electron-rich olefin radical cation couples with another olefin: no cyclobutane forms in absence of diene, and the isolated enone 52 cleanly converts to product upon photolysis, supporting the 'dehydrogenation-then-photo-[2+2]' sequence over a radical-cation [2+2]. The chiral-at-Rh complex serves a dual role as Lewis acid and triplet photosensitizer, enabling enantiocontrol via the triplet biradical VII/VIII.

10.1038_s41929-018-0198-y.rxn_class_summary: Electricity-driven asymmetric rhodium-catalyzed oxidative cross-coupling of 2-acyl imidazoles with silyl enol ethers to give non-racemic 1,4-dicarbonyl compounds.
10.1038_s41929-018-0198-y.electrochemical_role: Anodic single-electron oxidation of the chiral Rh-bound enolate (Rh2-1a, E_p^ox = +0.52 V vs Ag/AgCl) generates a Rh-coordinated alpha-carbon radical; a second anodic SET after silyl enol ether addition gives an oxocarbenium that is trapped by MeOH; cathode reduces protons to H2.
10.1038_s41929-018-0198-y.electron_flow: The chiral Rh-Lewis acid binds the acyl imidazole and deprotonation gives a Rh-enolate that is anodically oxidized (1.5 V lower potential than free enol ether) to a Rh-bound alpha-radical; this radical adds to the silyl enol ether, the resulting beta-OTMS ketyl radical is oxidized again at the anode to an oxocarbenium, which is desilylated/trapped by MeOH to release product, while protons are reduced at the cathode to H2.

key_intermediates.name: Rh-bound secondary ketyl-type radical after silyl enol ether addition (IV)
Rh-bound oxocarbenium intermediate (V) after second SET

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Electrolysis of 1a with TEMPO (without 2a) gave TEMPO-adduct 7 in 92% yield with 92% current efficiency; the 2a-derived TEMPO adduct 8 was not observed, supporting trapping of the Rh-bound alpha-radical [Rh2-1a]·+ rather than a silyl-enol-ether-derived radical.

probe_reagents.name: 1,1-diphenylethylene (DPE)
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction of 1a with DPE in absence of 2a gave three-component product 9 (51%, 99% e.e.) via [Rh2-1a]·+ addition to DPE, further oxidation and MeOH trapping, supporting the proposed cationic intermediate V.

probe_reagents.name: Rh2-1a (isolated Rh-enolate complex)
probe_reagents.observation: Synthesized, crystallographically characterized (shielding of re face by bulky TMS); cyclic voltammetry shows reversible-type oxidation at +0.52 V vs Ag/AgCl, ~1.5 V lower than the silyl enol ether 2a (+1.72 V) and the free acyl imidazole 1a (>2 V), identifying it as the species actually oxidized.

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of 1a + 2,6-lutidine, 2a, Rh2, and Rh2-1a
probe_reagents.observation: Only Rh2-1a shows a low-potential oxidation wave at +0.52 V; free 1a and 2a require much higher potentials, demonstrating that catalyst-bound enolate formation enables the mild anodic oxidation.

10.1038_s41929-018-0198-y.notes: Chiral Lewis acid catalysis lowers oxidation potential of the substrate by ~1.5 V via enolate complexation, simultaneously preventing homocoupling by sequestering the radical and enforcing enantioselectivity (re-face shielded by bulky TMS group on the benzothiazole ligand). Stereochemistry of C-C bond formation occurs on the Rh-bound radical III; the second oxidation (III to V via IV) is proposed without direct kinetic evidence.

10.1038_s41586-021-03717-7.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical aziridination of unactivated alkenes via thianthrene-mediated alkene activation to dicationic adducts, intercepted by primary amines
10.1038_s41586-021-03717-7.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of thianthrene (TT) to its radical cation TT•+ at an RVC anode (~0.8 V vs Fc/Fc+); disproportionation of 2 TT•+ generates the thianthrene dication TT2+ in situ, which is the active electrophile toward the alkene
10.1038_s41586-021-03717-7.electron_flow: Anode removes one electron from TT to give TT•+; at high [TT], TT•+ adds across the alkene to give a distonic C-radical/sulfonium cation that is trapped by a second TT•+ to form bis adduct 2. Once TT is largely consumed, 2 TT•+ disproportionate to TT and TT2+, and TT2+ undergoes formal [3+2]-type cycloaddition with the alkene to give mono adduct 1, which is opened by amine/base to the aziridine.
10.1038_s41586-021-03717-7.rate_determining_step: Generation of TT2+ via disproportionation of TT•+ becomes rate-limiting in the second (accelerated) regime; in the first regime, radical addition of TT•+ to alkene controls the pseudo-zero-order rate

key_intermediates.name: bis adduct 2 (distonic bis-thianthrenium dication from TT•+ radical addition then radical-radical coupling)
β-thianthrenium carbon radical (from TT•+ addition across alkene)

probe_reagents.name: increased thianthrene loading (1.5 vs 2.5 equiv)
probe_reagents.observation: Higher [TT] delays the sharp rate inflection but does not change the initial product-forming regime, consistent with TT•+ addition dominating until TT is consumed

probe_reagents.name: constant-potential electrolysis at 1.3 V vs Fc/Fc+
probe_reagents.observation: Direct anodic generation of TT2+ (bypassing disproportionation) accelerates reaction and gives exclusively mono adduct 1 (>20:1 vs <1:20 under 3.7 mA constant current at 30% conv.)

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of TT/TT•+/TT2+
probe_reagents.observation: E1/2(TT) = 0.8 V and E1/2(TT•+) = 1.4 V vs Fc/Fc+; CV consistent with kinetically relevant disproportionation of TT•+ to TT and TT2+

probe_reagents.name: time-course product distribution (1 vs 2)
probe_reagents.observation: Bis adduct 2 forms exclusively at early times; mono adduct 1 grows in sharply after ~1 F/mol of TT has been passed, indicating a switch in mechanism as TT is consumed

probe_reagents.name: halide salts / KCN / no base controls
probe_reagents.observation: Adduct can be diverted to dihalogenation (40, 41), diamination (39), or vinyl nitrile (42), supporting a common electrophilic dicationic intermediate

10.1038_s41586-021-03717-7.notes: Two parallel pathways operate at different stages of the same reaction: (i) at high [TT], radical addition of TT•+ to alkene followed by radical-radical coupling with a second TT•+ gives the bis adduct 2; (ii) at low [TT], disproportionation of TT•+ generates TT2+, which undergoes formal cycloaddition with alkene to give mono adduct 1. The authors note this is an unusual kinetic scenario in which two distinct products each form in high selectivity but at different time regimes of one reaction. Direct reaction of TT•+ with alkene to give mono adduct (without disproportionation) was considered but disfavored based on constant-potential experiments.

10.1021_acssuschemeng.1c04473.rxn_class_summary: Iodide-mediated electrocatalytic aziridination of simple aromatic alkenes (styrenes) with NH3 to give unprotected N-H aziridines and H2
10.1021_acssuschemeng.1c04473.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of I- to I2 (I+ equivalent), which reacts with NH3 to form the electrophilic nitrogen transfer reagent NH2I; H+/NH4+ is reduced at the Ni cathode to H2
10.1021_acssuschemeng.1c04473.electron_flow: At the carbon anode 2 I- are oxidized to I2, which reacts with NH3 to generate NH2I (plus NH4+ and I-); NH2I aminohalogenates styrene to a vicinal iodoamine that undergoes base-assisted anti cyclization to the N-H aziridine, releasing I-, while NH4+/H2O is reduced at the Ni cathode to H2, closing the EC' cycle.
10.1021_acssuschemeng.1c04473.rate_determining_step: Anodic oxidation of I- to I+/I2 coupled to its chemical reaction with NH3 (EC' step generating NH2I); supported by quasilinear CV response only when both iodide and ammonia are present

probe_reagents.name: no iodide source (control)
probe_reagents.observation: 0% aziridine — iodide mediator is essential

probe_reagents.name: no current (control)
probe_reagents.observation: 0% aziridine — electrolysis required

probe_reagents.name: I2 (1 equiv) without current
probe_reagents.observation: 12% aziridine, indicating I2 is (one of) the active species generated by anodic oxidation

probe_reagents.name: reaction in the dark
probe_reagents.observation: 84% yield — light/photoinduction not significant

probe_reagents.name: Na2S2O3 quench
probe_reagents.observation: Dark red/brown I2 color disappears, regenerating I-, confirming presence of I2/I+ species

probe_reagents.name: 15NH4OH labeling
probe_reagents.observation: 1H/15N HMBC shows aziridine 15N at ~16 ppm; new 15N peaks at 103 and 256 ppm assigned to NH2I and NHI2

probe_reagents.name: UV-Vis spectrophotometry on reaction without styrene
probe_reagents.observation: Spectrum matches triiodide, confirming I2 generation at the anode

10.1021_acssuschemeng.1c04473.notes: An alternative cathodic half-reaction is direct reduction of water to H2 + OH- (equivalent to NH4+ reduction). The authors note steric and electronic substrate effects: α-substituted styrenes give very high yields (stabilization of partial positive charge at α-carbon during NH2I attack), while β-stilbenes give only traces due to sterics; this supports an electrophilic aminohalogenation pathway via NH2I rather than a free aminyl radical.

10.1039_d0sc01694b.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical tin-free radical addition of alkyl halides (iodides/bromides) to electron-deficient alkenes and alkynes, mediated by a catalytic O2-derived redox relay generating hydroxyl radicals.
10.1039_d0sc01694b.electrochemical_role: Cathodic reduction of O2 (around -0.7 to -0.8 V vs Ag QRE) generates superoxide; O2/ROS act as a redox mediator that activates the C-X bond rather than direct SET to the alkyl halide (which is not reduced within the solvent window).
10.1039_d0sc01694b.electron_flow: Cathode reduces O2 to superoxide/HOO•, which via Haber-Weiss-type chemistry (with trace H2O2 or I-) yields •OH; •OH abstracts/activates the halogen of R-X to give IOH and R•, which adds to the alkene; the resulting α-EWG radical is further reduced at the cathode to a carbanion that is protonated by H2O (or, for unstabilised vinyl radicals from alkynes, abstracts I from R-I in an ATRA pathway). O2 is regenerated (catalytic, ~0.57 mol%) and only ~2 e- per R-I are consumed overall.

key_intermediates.name: hydroperoxyl radical (HOO•, pKa 4.88, dominant under acidic pH)
hypervalent alkyl iodine(II) species R-I(II)-OH (proposed transient activated halide)
adduct radical after addition to alkene (R-CH2-CHR'• with EWG)
carbanion from cathodic reduction of adduct radical
alkyl iodane R-I(III) (mCPBA-generated; tested and ruled out as major intermediate)
vinyl radical intermediate (from alkyne addition, leads to ATRA)

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of all components
probe_reagents.observation: Only O2 is redox-active in the applied window (reduced at -0.7 to -0.8 V); alkyl iodide is not reduced, ruling out direct SET / superoxide-mediated reduction of C-I.

probe_reagents.name: charge integration during electrolysis
probe_reagents.observation: ~300 C passed for 1.44 mmol R-I (≈2 F/mol), demonstrating high Faradaic efficiency and catalytic role of O2.

probe_reagents.name: inert (Ar) atmosphere control
probe_reagents.observation: No reaction without O2; injection of ~0.2 mL O2 (~0.57 mol%) restored full conversion, showing O2 is a sub-stoichiometric mediator.

probe_reagents.name: methanol as solvent (•OH scavenger)
probe_reagents.observation: No reaction in pure MeOH; partial recovery only on adding water, supporting involvement of •OH.

probe_reagents.name: H2O2 substitution for O2
probe_reagents.observation: No product formed, arguing H2O2 is not the principal active oxidant (though traces may initiate).

probe_reagents.name: FeSO4 (Fenton catalyst) addition
probe_reagents.observation: No rate enhancement, further disfavoring H2O2 as main reservoir of •OH.

probe_reagents.name: D2O-MeCN solvent
probe_reagents.observation: Deuterium incorporated α to sulfone, consistent with carbanion intermediate protonated by water (no exchange of product under reaction conditions for 72 h).

probe_reagents.name: mCPBA pre-oxidation of iPr-I to iodane in Ar
probe_reagents.observation: Only 9% product on subsequent electrolysis with PhSO2-vinyl, indicating I(III) iodane is not a major on-cycle intermediate (low yield also rationalised by peracid generating •OH).

probe_reagents.name: ethyl propiolate (alkyne acceptor)
probe_reagents.observation: Gave α-iodo alkene (E/Z 1.2:1) — ATRA product — because unstabilised vinyl radical abstracts I faster than it is reduced.

probe_reagents.name: 4-methylphenyl ethynyl sulfone
probe_reagents.observation: Mixture of ATRA and reduction products, reflecting partial n→σ* stabilisation of vinyl radical/anion.

probe_reagents.name: 1-(4-iodobutyl)-1H-indole-3-carbaldehyde (intramolecular probe)
probe_reagents.observation: Smooth redox-neutral oxidative cyclisation (89%) without excess O2, consistent with a self-propagating pseudo-SRN1 cycle in which the cyclised radical activates another R-I.

10.1039_d0sc01694b.notes: Authors explicitly contrast two proposals: Pathway 1 (superoxide directly reduces C-I via SET, O2 as electron shuttle) is disfavored because CV shows R-I is not reduced in the window and water is essential. Favored Pathway 2 (Scheme 4) is a redox-relay where superoxide/HOO• + H2O2/I- generate •OH that activates R-I to a transient hypervalent R-I(II)-OH, fragmenting to R• and IOH; IOH + HOO• regenerates •OH. Initiation likely via trace H2O2 (Haber-Weiss) or trace iodide. For aryl/intramolecular cyclisations, a Bowman/Moody-type pseudo-SRN1 chain (cyclohexadienyl-type radical reduces another R-I) is invoked to explain redox-neutral oxidative cyclisation without stoichiometric O2.

10.1039_c9sc05729c.rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical intermolecular [3+2] annulation of N-aryl sulfonamides (anilines) with alkenes (α-methylstyrenes) to give 2,2-disubstituted indolines under undivided-cell constant-current electrolysis with DDQ co-mediator and AcOH
10.1039_c9sc05729c.electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of the N-aryl sulfonamide (aniline 1a) generates an N-centered radical after deprotonation; DDQ acts as a co-mediator/oxidant to re-oxidize the cyclohexadienyl-type intermediate III; cathode reduces protons (from AcOH) to H2
10.1039_c9sc05729c.electron_flow: The anode oxidizes aniline 1a to a radical cation which loses a proton to give an N-radical (I); resonance delivers a C-radical (II) that adds to alkene 2a giving benzylic radical III; III is further oxidized (anode or DDQ) to a cation that undergoes intramolecular C–N cyclization to indoline 3aa, while the cathode reduces protons from AcOH to H2.
10.1039_c9sc05729c.rate_determining_step: Anodic oxidation of aniline 1a (first-order in 1a, zero-order in alkene 2b)

key_intermediates.name: N-centered aminyl radical (after anodic oxidation and deprotonation of 1a)
C-centered (para-carbon) aryl radical from resonance of N-radical (species II)
benzylic radical adduct (III) from radical addition of II to α-methylstyrene 2a
cyclohexadienyl/iminium cation after oxidation of III by anode or DDQ

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction of 1a + 2a with 1 equiv TEMPO under standard conditions gave no desired product 3aa, consistent with a radical pathway

probe_reagents.name: triethyl phosphite P(OEt)3
probe_reagents.observation: With 5 equiv P(OEt)3 added, phosphorylation product 4c was isolated in 50%, indicating generation of a carbon radical

probe_reagents.name: alkene-free control (no 2a, no DDQ)
probe_reagents.observation: Homocoupling product 4d (3%) and 4e (53%) formed, indicating N-radical generation that converts to C-radical

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry
probe_reagents.observation: 1a oxidizes at 1.49 V vs Ag/AgCl; 2a shows no oxidation 0–2.0 V; catalytic current appears upon adding 2a (radical addition); HOAc does not change 1a oxidation (serves as proton source); DDQ only slightly increases current (does not directly oxidize 1a)

probe_reagents.name: UV-vis (DDQ + 1a)
probe_reagents.observation: No interaction between DDQ and 1a observed by UV

probe_reagents.name: EPR (electrolysis 30 min without 2a and DDQ)
probe_reagents.observation: Organic conjugated radical signal observed, supporting radical intermediate

probe_reagents.name: kinetic order analysis (HPLC initial rates)
probe_reagents.observation: First order in 1a, zero order in 2b; anodic oxidation of 1a is rate-determining
